topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT612 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Modern Automotive Technologies** refers to the integrated suite of advanced mechanical, electrical, electronic, computer-controlled, and information-based engineering systems deployed in contemporary road vehicles to enhance propulsion efficiency, occupant safety, driving comfort, environmental sustainability, and connectivity. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (OEMUT612) syllabus, this domain encompasses the study of conventional Internal Combustion (IC) engine refinements, hybrid-electric architectures, Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs), Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs), Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS), Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication, autonomous driving stacks, and lightweight material science.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The introductory module lays the conceptual foundation by mapping the historical evolution of the automobile, classifying present-day vehicle architectures, identifying global mega-trends (electrification, autonomy, connectivity, shared mobility — collectively termed **"CASE"**), and defining key engineering metrics such as **fuel economy (km/l or mpge)**, **tailpipe emissions (g CO₂/km)**, and **powertrain efficiency (% thermal / electrical conversion)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a modern car as a **"smartphone on wheels."** Just as a smartphone evolved from a simple calling device into a multi-sensor computing platform (camera, GPS, internet, AI), the automobile has evolved from Karl Benz's 1886 single-cylinder three-wheeled Motorwagen — essentially a horseless carriage — into a sophisticated **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** running millions of lines of code, equipped with LiDAR, radar, cameras, and electric motors. The chassis is the "body," the powertrain is the "heart," the ECU network is the "nervous system," and the driver (or the AI) is the "brain." Every decade has added new layers of intelligence, mirroring Moore's Law but adapted for vehicular hardware.

## 1.3 Scope of Modern Automotive Technologies

The discipline is best understood through its three primary engineering pillars:

| Pillar | Engineering Focus | Representative Technologies |
|---|---|---|
| **Propulsion** | Energy conversion & management | Gasoline / Diesel IC, Atkinson / Miller cycle, HEV, PHEV, BEV, FCEV, Hydrogen ICE |
| **Chassis & Safety** | Vehicle dynamics & occupant protection | ABS, ESP, Airbags (SRS), ADAS (AEB, LKA), Crumple zones, Reinforced passenger cell |
| **Connectivity & Autonomy** | Sensing, computation & networking | CAN / LIN / Ethernet buses, V2X (V2V, V2I, V2G), OTA updates, Level 1–5 autonomy |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiners' Perspective:** When a question asks *"Discuss the scope of modern automotive technologies,"* always structure your answer using the **CASE framework** — **C**onnected, **A**utonomous, **S**hared, **E**lectric. This single acronym carries maximum content density for minimum word count, which KTU valuators reward.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following benchmark constants are routinely referenced in KTU 2024 question papers:

- **1 mile ≈ 1.609 km**
- **1 gallon (US) ≈ 3.785 litres**
- **mpge (miles per gallon equivalent)** — energy parity metric where **33.7 kWh = 1 gallon of gasoline** (lower heating value)
- **Tailpipe CO₂ emission benchmark (India, BS-VI): 130 g CO₂/km** (passenger cars, MIDC cycle)
- **Battery specific energy (current Li-ion): 150–250 Wh/kg**
- **Hydrogen lower heating value (LHV): 120 MJ/kg**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Historical Evolution of the Automobile

The trajectory of automotive engineering can be partitioned into **six distinct eras**. KTU frequently tests chronological ordering and key innovation years.

| Era | Approximate Period | Defining Innovation | Key Milestone |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Mechanical Pioneering** | 1886–1900 | First practical IC automobile | Karl Benz, **Patent-Motorwagen No. 37435**, 1886 |
| **2. Mass Production Era** | 1908–1945 | Assembly line manufacturing | Henry Ford, **Model T**, 1908 |
| **3. Post-War Refinement** | 1946–1970 | V8 engines, automatic transmission, power steering | Hydraulic controls, **1950s** |
| **4. Electronics Revolution** | 1971–1995 | Fuel injection, ABS, airbags | Bosch **D-Jetronic** EFI (1967), Bosch ABS (1978) |
| **5. Computerisation** | 1996–2010 | CAN bus, OBD-II, drive-by-wire | Mandated OBD-II in USA (1996) |
| **6. Electrification & Autonomy** | 2011–Present | EV mainstream, ADAS, Level 3 autonomy | Tesla Model S (2012), Nissan Leaf (2010), Waymo (2016–) |

## 2.2 Classification of Modern Vehicle Powertrains

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly requires classifying vehicles by their energy source and drivetrain architecture:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Core Definition:** A **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)** combines two or more energy-conversion devices (e.g., an ICE and an electric motor) with an energy storage system (typically a battery) such that **at least one** of the energy converters can deliver traction power independently.

### 2.2.1 Vehicle Architecture Taxonomy

- **Conventional ICE Vehicle** — Single propulsion source, fossil fuel, mechanical transmission.
- **Micro Hybrid (Start-Stop)** — Adds a reinforced starter to shut down the ICE at traffic stops. **No electric traction.**
- **Mild Hybrid (MHEV)** — 48 V belt-driven starter-generator; ICE remains primary; **electrical assist only**; cannot drive electrically alone.
- **Full Hybrid (HEV)** — Toyota-style series-parallel architecture (e.g., THS-II). ICE + MG1 + MG2 + planetary gearset. **Short electric-only driving possible.**
- **Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV)** — Larger battery (8–18 kWh), grid-chargeable, 30–80 km electric range.
- **Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)** — Battery only (typically 30–100 kWh). **Zero tailpipe emissions.**
- **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV)** — Hydrogen tank + PEM fuel cell stack + buffer battery. **Refuelling in ~5 minutes, range ~500–650 km.**

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every equation a KTU 2024 candidate must memorise for Module 1 derivations and numerical sub-parts.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$ | Thermal efficiency of any heat engine | $\eta_{th}$ dimensionless; $W$ in J; $Q$ in J |
| 2 | $\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | Ideal Otto (petrol) cycle efficiency | $r$ = compression ratio; $\gamma \approx 1.4$ |
| 3 | $\eta_{Diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma (\rho - 1)}$ | Ideal Diesel cycle efficiency | $\rho$ = cut-off ratio |
| 4 | $\eta_{Brayton} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_{p}^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}$ | Brayton cycle (gas turbine / fuel cell air supply) | $r_{p}$ = pressure ratio |
| 5 | $E_{bat} = V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}$ | Stored battery energy | $V_{nom}$ in V; $C_{Ah}$ in A·h; $E$ in Wh |
| 6 | $E_{bat, kWh} = \dfrac{V \times Ah}{1000}$ | Battery energy in kWh | Output in kWh |
| 7 | $\rho_{air} = \dfrac{p}{R_{specific} \cdot T}$ | Air density at inlet (relevant for drag & ICE) | $p$ in Pa; $T$ in K; $R_{air} = 287$ J/(kg·K) |
| 8 | $F_{drag} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_{air} \cdot C_{d} \cdot A_{f} \cdot v^{2}$ | Aerodynamic drag force | $C_{d}$ drag coeff.; $A_{f}$ frontal area; $v$ velocity |
| 9 | $F_{roll} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$ | Rolling resistance | $\mu_{rr}$ coeff.; $m$ kg; $g = 9.81$ m/s² |
| 10 | $F_{grade} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ | Grade-climbing resistance | $\theta$ slope angle |
| 11 | $P_{tractive} = F_{total} \cdot v = \left(F_{drag} + F_{roll} + F_{grade} + m a \right) \cdot v$ | Tractive power at wheels | $P$ in W; $v$ in m/s |
| 12 | $CO_{2}\ (g/km) = \dfrac{FC \times \rho_{fuel} \times 3.169}{D}$ | Tailpipe CO₂ from fuel consumption | $FC$ l/100km; $\rho_{fuel} \approx 0.75$ kg/L for petrol |
| 13 | $\eta_{propulsion} = \dfrac{F_{tractive} \cdot v}{\dot{m}_{fuel} \times LHV_{fuel}}$ | Tank-to-wheel propulsion efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 14 | $E_{vehicle, kWh/100km} = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{D}$ | BEV specific energy consumption | $E_{bat}$ usable kWh; $D$ range km |
| 15 | $R_{range, BEV} = \dfrac{E_{bat, usable}}{\bar{P}_{aux} + \bar{P}_{drive}/1000}$ | BEV range estimate | Use averaged power over driving cycle |

> [!WARNING]
> **Formula Pitfall (KTU 2024 Valuation):** When calculating $CO_2$ emissions, students frequently forget that **3.169** is the grams of $CO_2$ released per gram of petrol combusted (carbon mass fraction × 44/12). Do **not** use 3.0 or 2.3; the examiners expect **3.169** for petrol and **3.169** to **3.206** for diesel.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

The propulsion-resistance equation (Row 11) is **the single most important formula** in any KTU Module 1 numerical. It ties together the four forces opposing a moving vehicle:

$$F_{total} = F_{drag} + F_{roll} + F_{grade} + m \cdot a$$

Where $m \cdot a$ is the linear-inertia term (relevant during acceleration, e.g., 0–100 km/h tests). Recognising that **velocity $v$ appears quadratically** in $F_{drag}$ explains why aerodynamic refinement (lowering $C_{d}$) yields dramatic fuel-economy gains at highway speeds, and why EV designers obsess over frontal area $A_f$ more than ICE designers do (since EVs cannot rely on waste-heat to warm the cabin).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 derivation mandate, **every algebraic transition is expanded** below. There are no skipped steps.

## 3.1 Derivation: Tractive Power Required by a Vehicle

### Step 1 — Identify the four resistive forces

A road vehicle must overcome four resistive loads at its wheels:

1. **Aerodynamic drag** — $F_{drag}$
2. **Rolling resistance** — $F_{roll}$
3. **Grade resistance** — $F_{grade}$
4. **Linear inertia** — $F_{acc} = m \cdot a$

### Step 2 — Express each force explicitly

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{drag} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho_{air}\, C_{d}\, A_{f}\, v^{2} \quad \text{(Newton, from fluid mechanics)} \\
F_{roll} &= \mu_{rr}\, m\, g\, \cos\theta \quad \text{(empirical, tyre deformation losses)} \\
F_{grade} &= m\, g\, \sin\theta \quad \text{(gravitational component along slope)} \\
F_{acc} &= m\, a \quad \text{(Newton's 2nd law on a flat slope)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Sum the forces

$$
F_{total} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho_{air}\, C_{d}\, A_{f}\, v^{2} + \mu_{rr}\, m\, g\, \cos\theta + m\, g\, \sin\theta + m\, a
$$

### Step 4 — Multiply by velocity to get instantaneous tractive power

Multiplying $F_{total}$ by $v$ yields the power that the wheels must deliver to the road:

$$
P_{tractive} = F_{total} \cdot v
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{tractive} = \;& \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho_{air}\, C_{d}\, A_{f}\, v^{3} + \mu_{rr}\, m\, g\, \cos\theta \cdot v \\
& + m\, g\, \sin\theta \cdot v + m\, a \cdot v
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Observe the velocity exponents

Notice that:

- **Aerodynamic term** scales as $v^{3}$ → dominant above ~80 km/h.
- **Rolling term** scales as $v$ → dominant below ~50 km/h.
- **Grade term** scales as $v$ → independent of speed (energy = $mgh$).
- **Inertia term** scales as $v \cdot a$ → dominant during transient accelerations.

This explains the textbook **"N-shaped" or "bathtub" fuel-consumption curve** seen on ICE engines, which is why modern CVT and dual-clutch transmissions target low engine speed at cruising velocity.

## 3.2 Derivation: Tailpipe CO₂ Emission per km

### Step 1 — Define fuel consumption

Let $FC$ = fuel consumption in **L per 100 km** (standard MIDC reporting unit in India).

### Step 2 — Convert volume to mass

$$
m_{fuel} = FC \times \rho_{fuel} \quad \text{(in grams per 100 km)}
$$

For petrol, $\rho_{fuel} \approx 0.75 \text{ kg/L} = 750 \text{ g/L}$.

### Step 3 — Carbon content by mass fraction

Petrol is approximately **86.4 % carbon** by mass; diesel is approximately **86.6 %**.

$$
m_{C} = 0.864 \times m_{fuel}
$$

### Step 4 — Stoichiometric mass of CO₂ per mass of carbon

Each 12 g of C becomes 44 g of CO₂ upon complete combustion:

$$
m_{CO_2} = m_C \times \frac{44}{12} = 0.864 \times m_{fuel} \times 3.667
$$

$$
m_{CO_2} = 3.169 \times m_{fuel}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert from "per 100 km" to "per km"

$$
CO_2 \left(\frac{g}{km}\right) = \frac{3.169 \times m_{fuel}}{100} = \frac{3.169 \times FC \times 750}{100}
$$

$$
CO_2 = 23.77 \times FC
$$

### Step 6 — Simplified final formula

$$
CO_2\, (g/km) = \dfrac{FC \times \rho_{fuel} \times 3.169}{D}
$$

where $D$ is distance, often set to 100 km.

### Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem (Model KTU 2024 Style):** A Maruti Suzuki Swift returns **18.4 km/L** under MIDC cycle. Calculate the tailpipe CO₂ emission in g/km.

**Solution:**

**Step A** — Compute fuel consumption per 100 km:

$$
FC = \frac{100}{18.4} = 5.435 \text{ L/100 km}
$$

**Step B** — Compute fuel mass per 100 km:

$$
m_{fuel} = 5.435 \times 0.75 = 4.076 \text{ kg} = 4076 \text{ g}
$$

**Step C** — Compute CO₂ per 100 km:

$$
m_{CO_2} = 3.169 \times 4076 = 12{,}917 \text{ g}
$$

**Step D** — Convert to g/km:

$$
CO_2 = \frac{12{,}917}{100} = \boxed{129.2 \text{ g/km}}
$$

This matches the Indian BS-VI corporate average and validates the empirical rule of thumb: **1 km/L ≈ 23.7 g CO₂/km** for petrol cars.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Powertrain Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is a high-density exam-ready reference. Reproducing this table in your answer booklet scores well in 14-mark questions.

| Parameter | ICE (Petrol) | HEV | BEV | FCEV |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy source** | Petrol | Petrol + Battery | Grid electricity | Hydrogen (H₂) |
| **Tank-to-Wheel $\eta$** | 20–35 % | 35–45 % | 60–75 % | 35–45 % |
| **Well-to-Wheel $\eta$** | 15–25 % | 25–35 % | 35–55 % | 22–32 % |
| **Refuelling time** | ~3 min | ~3 min (fuel) + hrs (AC charge) | 30 min (DC fast) to 8 h (AC) | ~5 min |
| **Range (typical)** | 600–900 km | 700–1000 km | 200–600 km | 500–650 km |
| **Tailpipe emissions** | Yes (CO₂, NOₓ, PM) | Yes (reduced) | **Zero** | **Zero** (water vapour only) |
| **Key limitation** | Fossil depletion, emissions | Complexity, dual-system cost | Charging infra, range anxiety | H₂ storage, production emissions |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part A carries 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks. Two are provided for practice.

---

### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]

**Define Modern Automotive Technology. List any four current mega-trends shaping the global automotive industry.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Modern Automotive Technology refers to the application of advanced **mechanical, electronic, electrical, computer-controlled, and information-based engineering systems** in road vehicles to improve performance, efficiency, safety, comfort, and environmental compatibility.

The four current mega-trends (collectively termed the **CASE** framework) are:

1. **C — Connected:** V2X communication, telematics, OTA updates.
2. **A — Autonomous:** Driver-assistance and self-driving systems (SAE Levels 1–5).
3. **S — Shared:** Mobility-as-a-Service (MaaS), ride-hailing.
4. **E — Electric:** BEVs, PHEVs, FCEVs replacing pure ICE vehicles.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing four trends with brief clarity.)*

---

### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]

**Differentiate between a Mild Hybrid (MHEV), a Full Hybrid (HEV), and a Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV) vehicle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | MHEV | HEV (Full) | PHEV |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Voltage bus** | 48 V | 200–650 V | 200–650 V |
| **Electric-only drive** | Not possible | Short distances (<5 km) | 30–80 km |
| **External charging** | No | No | Yes (AC or DC) |
| **Battery size** | <1 kWh | 1–2 kWh | 8–18 kWh |

*(3 marks for any tabular comparison with correct voltage and battery-size order.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark ESE-Style Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, candidates answer **ONE full question of 14 marks**, choosing between an **OR** pair. Sub-parts are **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a)** [7 marks]

**With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the architectural classification of modern vehicle powertrains. Compare the tank-to-wheel efficiency of an ICE, HEV, BEV, and FCEV.** *(Module 1, Course Outcome CO1, Bloom Level: Understand)*

**Model Answer:**

The classification of vehicle powertrains depends on the **energy source, storage mechanism, and propulsion interface** with the road.

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.4):**

A vehicle powertrain consists of:
- **Energy source** (fuel tank / battery / hydrogen tank).
- **Energy converter** (ICE / fuel cell / motor).
- **Transmission** (gearbox / planetary set / single-speed reducer).
- **Final drive** (differential or direct).

**Architectural Classification:**

1. **Conventional ICE:** Single energy source (petrol/diesel) → ICE → transmission → wheels.
2. **Hybrid Electric:** Two energy sources (fuel + battery) with **two energy converters** (ICE + electric motor). Series, parallel, or series-parallel topologies.
3. **Battery Electric:** Single source (battery) → inverter → traction motor → reducer → wheels. **No ICE, no gearbox**, single-speed.
4. **Fuel Cell Electric:** Hydrogen tank → PEM fuel cell stack → DC-DC converter → buffer battery + traction motor → wheels.

**Efficiency Comparison:**

| Powertrain | Tank-to-Wheel $\eta$ |
|---|---|
| ICE (petrol, Otto) | **20–25 %** |
| ICE (diesel) | **30–35 %** |
| HEV (Toyota THS-II) | **35–45 %** |
| BEV | **60–75 %** |
| FCEV | **35–45 %** |

The BEV is most efficient because it bypasses the thermodynamic Carnot ceiling entirely (electric motors are >90 % efficient). FCEVs are limited by the **fuel-cell system efficiency (~50–60 %) plus motor losses**, while ICEs are bounded by the **Otto / Diesel cycle efficiency** and mechanical friction.

*(Award 7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for architecture, 2 for efficiency table.)*

---

#### **Part (b)** [7 marks]

**Derive the tractive power equation for a road vehicle moving at velocity $v$ on a slope $\theta$ with acceleration $a$. Indicate which term dominates at (i) 30 km/h city driving and (ii) 120 km/h highway driving.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Identify forces at the wheel:

$$
F_{total} = F_{drag} + F_{roll} + F_{grade} + F_{inertia}
$$

**Step 2:** Express explicitly:

$$
F_{total} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^{2} + \mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta + m g \sin\theta + m a
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by $v$:

$$
P_{tractive} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^{3} + \mu_{rr} m g v \cos\theta + m g v \sin\theta + m a v
$$

**Step 4 — Dominance analysis:**

- **(i) City driving, 30 km/h ≈ 8.33 m/s:**
  $v$ is small, so $v^{3}$ is negligible. **Rolling resistance $F_{roll}$ dominates**, followed by inertia during frequent accelerations and stops. **Implication:** Tyre-pressure management and regenerative braking are the most effective efficiency interventions for urban duty cycles.

- **(ii) Highway driving, 120 km/h ≈ 33.33 m/s:**
  $v^{3}$ becomes dominant ($33.33^{3} \approx 37{,}000$ vs $8.33^{3} \approx 578$, a **64× increase**). **Aerodynamic drag dominates.** **Implication:** Reducing $C_d$ (e.g., Tesla Model S $C_d = 0.208$) or frontal area $A_f$ has outsized returns. This is why EV manufacturers obsess over streamlined silhouettes.

*(Award 7 marks: 3 for derivation, 2 for numerical substitution discussion, 2 for engineering implication.)*

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) [OR Choice]**

#### **Part (a)** [7 marks]

**Explain the SAE J3016 levels of driving automation (Level 0 to Level 5) with one example application per level.**

**Model Answer:**

The **SAE J3016 standard** provides a taxonomy with six levels of driving automation:

| Level | Name | Steering / Acceleration | Driving Monitoring | Fallback | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | No Automation | Human | Human | Human | Conventional 2010 car (e.g., Maruti Alto) |
| **1** | Driver Assistance | System (one axis) | Human | Human | Adaptive Cruise Control OR Lane Keep Assist |
| **2** | Partial Automation | System (both axes) | Human | Human | Tesla Autopilot, GM Super Cruise |
| **3** | Conditional Automation | System | System (limited) | **Human (must be ready)** | Audi A8 Traffic Jam Pilot |
| **4** | High Automation | System | System | System (no driver needed in ODD) | Waymo Robotaxi (geo-fenced) |
| **5** | Full Automation | System | System | System (all conditions) | Theoretical — no production examples yet |

**Note for KTU answer:** Levels 0–2 require the human driver to be perpetually engaged. Level 3 is the *transition point* where the **system, not the human, drives** but the human must remain ready to take back control. Levels 4 and 5 remove this requirement.

*(Award 7 marks: 5 for level table, 2 for transition comment about Level 3.)*

---

#### **Part (b)** [7 marks]

**A petrol car delivers 15 km/L. Density of petrol is 0.75 kg/L. Estimate the tailpipe CO₂ emission in g/km. Comment on its compliance with BS-VI norms.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute fuel consumed per 100 km:**

$$
FC = \frac{100}{15} = 6.667 \text{ L/100 km}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute fuel mass per 100 km:**

$$
m_{fuel} = 6.667 \times 0.75 = 5.0 \text{ kg} = 5000 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute carbon content:**

$$
m_{C} = 0.864 \times 5000 = 4320 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert C to CO₂ using 44/12 ratio:**

$$
m_{CO_2} = 4320 \times \frac{44}{12} = 15{,}840 \text{ g per 100 km}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute per km:**

$$
CO_2 = \frac{15{,}840}{100} = \boxed{158.4 \text{ g/km}}
$$

**Step 6 — Compliance comment:**

- The Indian BS-VI corporate-average emission target for passenger cars is approximately **130 g CO₂/km** (MIDC cycle).
- This vehicle emits **158.4 g/km**, exceeding the BS-VI target by **~22 %**.
- It would still legally comply with current BS-VI *type-approval limits* (which are tailpipe-pollutant based — CO, HC, NOₓ, PM — and do **not** impose a single-vehicle CO₂ cap), but it would **fail CAFC (Corporate Average Fuel Consumption)** norms if sold as part of a manufacturer's fleet without offset credits.

*(Award 7 marks: 4 for calculation, 2 for the numerical answer, 1 for compliance comment.)*

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU 2024 Pitfalls on "Introduction" questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing HEV with BEV:** Many students claim a HEV produces "zero emissions" — this is wrong. A HEV still has an ICE that combusts fuel and produces tailpipe CO₂.
> 2. **Wrong SAE level description:** Students often misplace Level 3. The defining feature of Level 3 is that **the system drives, but the human remains the legal fallback**. Levels 4 and 5 remove the human from the loop within their operational design domain (ODD).
> 3. **Carbon-to-CO₂ conversion error:** Using the multiplier **3.0 instead of 3.169** is the single most common arithmetic mistake in the CO₂ derivation problem. Examiners explicitly check this multiplier.
> 4. **Mixing mpge with kWh/100 km:** mpge is an **American fleet-economy metric** and is rarely tested in KTU, but if it appears, remember **33.7 kWh ≡ 1 gallon of gasoline (energy equivalent)**.
> 5. **Forgetting the "Grade" term in the tractive force equation:** When $\theta = 0$, students sometimes drop $F_{grade}$ correctly but then **also forget to set $\cos 0 = 1$ and $\sin 0 = 0$** explicitly in the substitution step. Always write the substitution explicitly to score full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for Module 1 — Introduction to Modern Automotive Technologies**

- **Definition:** Modern Automotive Technology = integrated mechanical + electronic + software + communication systems in road vehicles.
- **Historical milestones:** Benz 1886, Ford Model T 1908, D-Jetronic 1967, ABS 1978, OBD-II 1996, Tesla Model S 2012, BS-VI India 2020.
- **CASE framework:** Connected, Autonomous, Shared, Electric — the four mega-trends.
- **Vehicle classification:** ICE → MHEV → HEV → PHEV → BEV → FCEV, ordered by degree of electrification.
- **Key propulsion-resistance equation:** $P_{tractive} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^{3} + \mu_{rr} m g v \cos\theta + m g v \sin\theta + m a v$.
- **Drag dominates at high speed** (∝ $v^{3}$); **rolling resistance dominates at low speed** (∝ $v$); **inertia dominates during transients**.
- **CO₂ emission rule of thumb:** 1 km/L ≈ **23.7 g CO₂/km** (petrol, MIDC).
- **Efficiency ordering (TTW):** ICE 20–35 % < FCEV ≈ HEV 35–45 % < BEV 60–75 %.
- **SAE J3016 levels:** 0 (none) → 5 (full). Level 3 is the *human-fallback boundary*.
- **Critical constants:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³ at sea level, $R_{air} = 287$ J/(kg·K), $LHV_{H_2} = 120$ MJ/kg, $LHV_{gasoline} \approx 44$ MJ/kg, **33.7 kWh = 1 gallon gasoline (mpge)**.
- **Indian context:** BS-VI (equivalent to Euro 6) implemented April 2020; CAFC norms mandate fleet-level CO₂ reduction.
- **Engineering mantra:** *"Aerodynamics for the highway, rolling resistance for the city, and energy storage for both."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ULV & ZEV
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMUT612 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** ULV & ZEV --- ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is ULV? (Ultra-Low Emission Vehicle) **ULV (Ultra-Low Emission Vehicle)** is an emission standard classification originally established by the **California Air Resources Board (CARB)** and adopted globally, including in Indian and European automotive regulatory frameworks. A ULV is a vehicle whose tailpipe emissions of non-methane organic gases (NMOG), carbon monoxide (CO), oxides of nitrogen (NOx), and particulate matter (PM) are drastically reduced — typically **50% lower** than the baseline conventional gasoline vehicle of the same model year. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** > An *Ultra-Low Emission Vehicle (ULV)* is a motor vehicle that satisfies a pre-defined set of stringent tailpipe emission limits (NMOG + NOx ≤ a specified threshold) over its **full useful life (150,000 miles / 241,000 km)**, without any zero-emission powertrain requirement. It is an *intermediate clean-vehicle category* between conventional ICE vehicles and ZEVs. > [!NOTE] > **ULV is an EMISSION-CONTROLLED ICE vehicle** — it still has an internal combustion engine and a tailpipe, but the after-treatment and engine management are sophisticated enough that tailpipe output approaches near-zero levels of harmful pollutants. ### 1.2 What is ZEV? (Zero Emission Vehicle) **ZEV (Zero Emission Vehicle)** is the strictest emission category in the regulatory spectrum. A ZEV produces **no tailpipe emissions** of regulated pollutants (CO, NOx, NMOG, PM) and often no evaporative emissions either. By definition, ZEVs cannot rely on an internal combustion engine for propulsion. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** > A *Zero Emission Vehicle (ZEV)* is a vehicle that, during its operation, emits **zero grams per mile (g/mi)** of any criteria pollutant from its tailpipe, as certified under standardized test cycles (FTP-75, WLTP). ZEVs achieve propulsion exclusively through electric or hydrogen fuel cell powertrains. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Purity" Metaphor Imagine the air coming out of a car's tailpipe as a glass of dirty water: | Vehicle Type | Analogy | |---|---| | **Conventional Vehicle** | Murky, brown river water — full of sediments, pollutants, and contaminants | | **ULEV (Ultra-Low Emission)** | Filtered tap water — visibly clean, but a chemist's test still detects trace impurities | | **SULEV (Super-Ultra-Low Emission)** | Distilled water — extremely pure, but a microscopic particle analysis can still find something | | **PZEV (Partial ZEV)** | Triple-distilled, laboratory-grade water — practically pure | | **ZEV (Zero Emission Vehicle)** | **No water at all** — the tap simply does not exist. The "dirty water" problem is eliminated at the source | > [!TIP] > **The Core Insight:** ULVs *clean the exhaust*; ZEVs *eliminate the exhaust altogether*. This is the philosophical difference every KTU examiner loves to test. ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Per Protocol) - **FTP-75 Test Cycle:** Federal Test Procedure, 7.5 miles, used for US certification - **WLTP:** Worldwide Harmonised Light Vehicles Test Procedure (used in India/EU) - **Full Useful Life:** **150,000 miles (≈ 241,000 km)** - **SULEV NMOG+NOx limit:** **0.01 g/mi** — the lowest non-zero certified value - **ZEV tailpipe emission:** **0.00 g/mi** of all criteria pollutants --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The CARB Emission Classification Hierarchy (Step-by-Step Logic) The California Air Resources Board (CARB) created a **pyramid of emission cleanliness**, with the conventional vehicle at the base and the ZEV at the apex: **Step 1 — Conventional Baseline Vehicle** - Standard ICE vehicle, no special emission controls beyond the prevailing regulation of its year. - Emits the "100%" benchmark level of pollutants. **Step 2 — TLEV (Transitional Low-Emission Vehicle)** - Approximately 30% cleaner than baseline. - Largely obsolete in 2024+ regulatory contexts. **Step 3 — LEV (Low-Emission Vehicle)** - Around 50% cleaner than baseline. - Mandatory minimum for all new cars sold in California. **Step 4 — ULEV (Ultra-Low Emission Vehicle)** - 50% cleaner than the average new vehicle of its model year. - Achieved through advanced three-way catalysts, precise engine calibration, and low-sulfur fuels. **Step 5 — SULEV (Super-Ultra-Low Emission Vehicle)** - **90% cleaner** than the average new vehicle. - Requires premium after-treatment: close-coupled catalyst, secondary air injection, advanced electronic control. **Step 6 — PZEV (Partial Zero-Emission Vehicle)** - SULEV tailpipe standards + **zero evaporative emissions**. - Has ICE but no fuel-vapor leakage — essentially a "near-zero" vehicle. **Step 7 — ZEV (Zero-Emission Vehicle)** - Zero tailpipe emissions, zero evaporative emissions (typically). - BEVs, FCEVs qualify; ICE vehicles with after-treatment cannot. **Step 8 — AT-PZEV (Advanced Technology PZEV)** - Hybrid drivetrain + SULEV tailpipe + zero evaporative emissions. - A transitional technology bridging PZEV and ZEV. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Critical Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains the **non-methane organic gas (NMOG) + NOx** combined emission limits in grams per mile (g/mi) for each CARB category, applicable over the **150,000-mile full useful life**. These are the values the KTU examiner expects you to memorize. | Category | NMOG + NOx (g/mi) | CO (g/mi) | Formaldehyde (g/mi) | Tailpipe? | Powertrain | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **LEV (Baseline)** | 0.090 | 4.2 | 0.018 | Yes | ICE | | **ULEV** | 0.050 | 2.1 | 0.011 | Yes | ICE + Advanced Catalyst | | **SULEV** | 0.010 | 1.0 | 0.004 | Yes | ICE + Premium After-treatment | | **PZEV** | 0.010 | 1.0 | 0.004 | Yes (but zero evap.) | ICE + Sealed Fuel System | | **AT-PZEV** | 0.010 | 1.0 | 0.004 | Yes | Hybrid + SULEV + Sealed System | | **ZEV** | 0.000 | 0.0 | 0.000 | **No** | BEV / FCEV only | ### 2.3 The "Why" Behind ULEV → SULEV Engineering To go from ULEV (0.050 g/mi) to SULEV (0.010 g/mi) — a **5× improvement** — engineers deploy: 1. **High-cell-density substrates** in the catalytic converter (e.g., 900 cells/in² vs 400 cells/in² standard). 2. **Close-coupled pre-catalyst** mounted within 30 cm of the exhaust port — reaches light-off temperature in **<10 seconds**. 3. **Heated oxygen sensor (HO2S)** + wide-band UEGO sensor for stoichiometric air-fuel ratio control to within ±0.5%. 4. **Palladium-rhodium washcoat formulations** with ceria-based oxygen storage. 5. **Secondary air injection** pump to supply oxygen during cold-start, oxidizing HC during the richest phase. ### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility > [!NOTE] > ULVs are essential in **urban airshed management**. In cities like Delhi, Beijing, and Los Angeles, ULEV/SULEV mandates have reduced ground-level ozone precursors by measurable percentages. ZEVs are the long-term climate-change solution, while ULVs serve as the **bridge technology** during the global transition away from fossil-fuel mobility. ### 2.5 Comparative Engineering Trade-offs | Parameter | ULEV / SULEV | ZEV (BEV) | ZEV (FCEV) | |---|---|---|---| | **Energy Source** | Petrol/Diesel | Grid Electricity | Hydrogen | | **Refuel Time** | 3–5 minutes | 30 min – 8 hours | 5–10 minutes | | **Range** | 500–800 km | 200–600 km | 500–700 km | | **Tailpipe Emissions** | Trace | 0 | 0 (only H₂O vapor) | | **Well-to-Wheel CO₂** | High | Low–Medium (grid-dependent) | Low (if H₂ is green) | | **Infrastructure Cost** | Existing | High (charging) | Very High (H₂ stations) | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Percentage Emission Reduction Between Categories A frequently asked KTU 2024 numerical is: *"If a ULEV emits 0.050 g/mi NMOG+NOx and a SULEV emits 0.010 g/mi, what is the percentage reduction achieved by moving from ULEV to SULEV?"* **Step 1 — State the formula for percentage reduction:** $$ \text{Percentage Reduction} = \frac{E_{\text{baseline}} - E_{\text{new}}}{E_{\text{baseline}}} \times 100\% $$ **Step 2 — Identify the values:** $$ E_{\text{baseline}} = 0.050 \text{ g/mi (ULEV)} $$ $$ E_{\text{new}} = 0.010 \text{ g/mi (SULEV)} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the formula:** $$ \text{Percentage Reduction} = \frac{0.050 - 0.010}{0.050} \times 100\% $$ **Step 4 — Compute the numerator:** $$ 0.050 - 0.010 = 0.040 \text{ g/mi} $$ **Step 5 — Compute the ratio:** $$ \frac{0.040}{0.050} = 0.8 $$ **Step 6 — Convert to percentage:** $$ 0.8 \times 100\% = 80\% $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer:** A SULEV achieves an **80% reduction** in NMOG + NOx emissions compared to a ULEV. The examiner expects this exact calculation chain — show all six steps to score full marks. ### 3.2 Derivation: Total Emissions Over Full Useful Life **Problem:** A SULEV is driven for its full useful life of 150,000 miles. What is the total mass of NMOG+NOx emitted over its lifetime? **Step 1 — Identify the emission rate:** $$ E_{\text{SULEV}} = 0.010 \text{ g/mi} $$ **Step 2 — Identify the distance:** $$ d = 150{,}000 \text{ miles} $$ **Step 3 — Use the total-emission formula:** $$ M_{\text{total}} = E_{\text{rate}} \times d $$ **Step 4 — Substitute the values:** $$ M_{\text{total}} = 0.010 \text{ g/mi} \times 150{,}000 \text{ mi} $$ **Step 5 — Compute the product:** $$ M_{\text{total}} = 1{,}500 \text{ grams} $$ **Step 6 — Convert to kilograms (for engineering clarity):** $$ 1{,}500 \text{ g} = 1.5 \text{ kg} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** A SULEV, despite being "super ultra-low," still emits **1.5 kg of smog-forming pollutants** over its full life. A ZEV emits **0 kg**. This is the quantitative foundation of the regulatory push toward electrification. ### 3.3 Comparative Emission Mass Table (Analytical Matrix) | Vehicle Type | NMOG+NOx Rate (g/mi) | Total over 150,000 mi (g) | Total over 150,000 mi (kg) | Reduction vs Baseline | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | LEV Baseline | 0.090 | 13,500 | 13.500 | 0% | | ULEV | 0.050 | 7,500 | 7.500 | 44.4% | | SULEV | 0.010 | 1,500 | 1.500 | 88.9% | | PZEV | 0.010 | 1,500 | 1.500 | 88.9% | | **ZEV** | **0.000** | **0** | **0.000** | **100.0%** | ### 3.4 Derivation: Air-Fuel Ratio for Stoichiometric Combustion For an ICE vehicle to meet ULEV standards, precise control of the air-fuel ratio (AFR) is critical. The stoichiometric AFR for gasoline (octane C₈H₁₈) is derived from the balanced combustion equation: **Step 1 — Write the unbalanced equation:** $$ \text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ **Step 2 — Balance carbon (8 on left → 8 CO₂):** $$ \text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 8\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ **Step 3 — Balance hydrogen (18 H → 9 H₂O):** $$ \text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 8\text{CO}_2 + 9\text{H}_2\text{O} $$ **Step 4 — Balance oxygen (right side has 16 + 9 = 25 O atoms → 12.5 O₂):** $$ \text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + 12.5\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 8\text{CO}_2 + 9\text{H}_2\text{O} $$ **Step 5 — Convert to air (air = 21% O₂ by mole, 23.2% by mass):** By mass, oxygen is 23.2% of air; by mole, it is 21%. $$ \text{O}_2 \text{ required} = 12.5 \text{ moles} $$ $$ \text{Air required} = \frac{12.5}{0.21} = 59.52 \text{ moles of air} $$ **Step 6 — Compute the mass-based stoichiometric AFR:** Molecular weight of C₈H₁₈ = (8 × 12) + (18 × 1) = 114 g/mol. Molecular weight of air ≈ 28.97 g/mol. $$ \text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{59.52 \times 28.97}{114} \approx 14.7:1 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The universally accepted stoichiometric AFR for gasoline is 14.7:1.** ULEV/SULEV vehicles maintain this ratio within ±0.5% to ensure the three-way catalyst can simultaneously reduce NOx, oxidize CO, and oxidize unburnt hydrocarbons. ### 3.5 Catalyst Light-Off Time Calculation (Approximate) Modern SULEV catalytic converters must reach their light-off temperature (typically 250–300 °C) within seconds of engine start. The heating rate can be approximated: **Step 1 — Light-off temperature:** $$ T_{\text{light-off}} = 300^\circ\text{C} $$ **Step 2 — Ambient temperature (cold start):** $$ T_{\text{ambient}} = 25^\circ\text{C} $$ **Step 3 — Temperature rise required:** $$ \Delta T = 300 - 25 = 275^\circ\text{C} $$ **Step 4 — Typical heating rate with close-coupled catalyst:** $$ \frac{dT}{dt} \approx 50^\circ\text{C/s} $$ **Step 5 — Time to light-off:** $$ t_{\text{light-off}} = \frac{\Delta T}{dT/dt} = \frac{275}{50} = 5.5 \text{ seconds} $$ > [!TIP] > **Engineering Implication:** Close-coupled catalysts (mounted within 30 cm of the exhaust manifold) achieve light-off in 5–10 seconds — compared to 60+ seconds for traditional underbody catalysts — which is why they are mandatory for SULEV certification. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: CARB Emission Classification Pyramid This block-level architecture flow maps the hierarchy of emission categories from the dirtiest (conventional) to the cleanest (ZEV), along with the underlying powertrain technology and the regulatory thresholds. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Conventional ICE Vehicle
NMOG plus NOx: 0.160 g per mi
Baseline 100 percent] --> B[LEV Low Emission Vehicle
NMOG plus NOx: 0.090 g per mi
About 50 percent cleaner] B --> C[ULEV Ultra Low Emission Vehicle
NMOG plus NOx: 0.050 g per mi
50 percent cleaner than average] C --> D[SULEV Super Ultra Low Emission Vehicle
NMOG plus NOx: 0.010 g per mi
90 percent cleaner than average] D --> E[PZEV Partial Zero Emission Vehicle
SULEV tailpipe plus zero evaporative
Sealed fuel system required] E --> F[AT-PZEV Advanced Technology PZEV
Hybrid powertrain plus SULEV plus zero evap
Bridge technology to full ZEV] F --> G[ZEV Zero Emission Vehicle
NMOG plus NOx: 0.000 g per mi
Tailpipe eliminated entirely] G --> H[Powertrain: BEV Battery Electric Vehicle
Energy source: Grid electricity] G --> I[Powertrain: FCEV Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle
Energy source: Hydrogen] G --> J[Powertrain: Solar or Other Renewables
Energy source: Photovoltaic] style A fill:#3a0000,color:#ffffff style B fill:#5a1a1a,color:#ffffff style C fill:#7a3a3a,color:#ffffff style D fill:#1a5a1a,color:#ffffff style E fill:#1a7a3a,color:#ffffff style F fill:#1a9a5a,color:#ffffff style G fill:#003a7a,color:#ffffff style H fill:#001f4a,color:#ffffff style I fill:#001f4a,color:#ffffff style J fill:#001f4a,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: SULEV After-Treatment Functional Architecture A block-level functional architecture flow mapping the interactions within a SULEV-grade after-treatment system, illustrating how raw engine exhaust is progressively cleaned before exiting the (almost-zero-emission) tailpipe. ```mermaid flowchart LR ENG[Internal Combustion Engine
Stoichiometric AFR 14.7 to 1] --> EXH[Exhaust Manifold
Temp 700 to 900 C] EXH --> CC[Close Coupled Pre Catalyst
Light off within 10 seconds] CC --> SEC[Secondary Air Injection
For cold start HC oxidation] CC --> UB[Underbody Three Way Catalyst
CO plus HC plus NOx reduction] UB --> TWC[Main TWC
900 cells per square inch substrate] TWC --> HOU[Heated Oxygen Sensor UEGO
Closed loop lambda control] HOU --> ECU[Engine Control Unit ECU
AFR held within plus or minus 0.5 percent] ECU --> ENG TWC --> TAIL[Reduced Tailpipe Output
NMOG plus NOx 0.010 g per mi] TAIL --> ATM[Atmosphere
SuLEV Certified] style ENG fill:#5a1a1a,color:#ffffff style EXH fill:#7a3a3a,color:#ffffff style CC fill:#1a5a1a,color:#ffffff style SEC fill:#1a5a1a,color:#ffffff style UB fill:#1a7a3a,color:#ffffff style TWC fill:#1a9a5a,color:#ffffff style HOU fill:#005a9a,color:#ffffff style ECU fill:#005a9a,color:#ffffff style TAIL fill:#1a9a5a,color:#ffffff style ATM fill:#003a7a,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: ZEV Powertrain Functional Architecture A block-level functional architecture flow mapping the interactions within a Battery Electric Vehicle (ZEV) powertrain — the most extreme form of clean mobility, where the tailpipe is replaced by an electric motor. ```mermaid flowchart LR GRID[Electrical Grid or Solar] --> CHG[Onboard Charger or DC Fast Charger] CHG --> BAT[High Voltage Battery Pack
400 to 800 Volts DC] BAT --> INV[Power Inverter
DC to AC conversion] INV --> MOT[AC Traction Motor
Permanent magnet or induction] MOT --> WHLS[Drive Wheels
Mechanical propulsion] WHLS --> REGEN[Regenerative Braking
Kinetic energy recovery] REGEN --> BAT BAT --> AUX[12 Volt Auxiliary Battery
For lights and electronics] MOT --> THERM[Battery Thermal Management
Liquid cooling loop] THERM --> BAT style GRID fill:#1a3a7a,color:#ffffff style CHG fill:#1a5a9a,color:#ffffff style BAT fill:#1a7a3a,color:#ffffff style INV fill:#005a9a,color:#ffffff style MOT fill:#005a9a,color:#ffffff style WHLS fill:#003a5a,color:#ffffff style REGEN fill:#1a9a5a,color:#ffffff style AUX fill:#5a1a1a,color:#ffffff style THERM fill:#7a3a1a,color:#ffffff ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **"Differentiate between ULEV and ZEV with respect to tailpipe emissions and powertrain type."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | ULEV (Ultra-Low Emission Vehicle) | ZEV (Zero Emission Vehicle) | |---|---|---| | **Tailpipe Emissions** | Trace emissions; NMOG+NOx ≤ **0.050 g/mi** | **Zero** tailpipe emissions (0.000 g/mi) | | **Powertrain** | Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) with advanced after-treatment | Battery Electric (BEV) or Fuel Cell Electric (FCEV); no ICE | | **Emission Philosophy** | Cleans the exhaust stream | Eliminates the exhaust entirely | **Marking Key:** 1 mark for the tailpipe distinction, 1 mark for the powertrain distinction, 1 mark for the philosophical distinction. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **"List the three main pollutants regulated under CARB's ULEV standard and state the combined NMOG+NOx limit for ULEV certification."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Non-Methane Organic Gases (NMOG)** — 1 mark 2. **Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx)** — 1 mark 3. **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** — 1 mark (Formaldehyde is the fourth, but only three are core to KTU) **Combined NMOG+NOx limit for ULEV: 0.050 g/mi** — *(mentioned in passing, partial credit if quoted in numerical part)* --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper] **(a)** Explain the CARB emission classification hierarchy from conventional vehicles to ZEVs, with the NMOG+NOx emission limits for each category. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A SULEV-certified vehicle is driven for its full useful life of 150,000 miles. Calculate the total mass of NMOG+NOx emissions released and compare it with a LEV baseline vehicle over the same distance. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:** 1. **Conventional Vehicle (1 mark):** Baseline ICE vehicle with no special emission control; emits the 100% benchmark. 2. **LEV — Low Emission Vehicle (1 mark):** NMOG+NOx limit = 0.090 g/mi; approximately 50% cleaner than baseline. 3. **ULEV — Ultra-Low Emission Vehicle (1 mark):** NMOG+NOx limit = 0.050 g/mi; achieved through advanced three-way catalysts and electronic engine management. 4. **SULEV — Super Ultra-Low Emission Vehicle (1 mark):** NMOG+NOx limit = 0.010 g/mi; requires close-coupled catalysts and secondary air injection. 5. **PZEV — Partial Zero-Emission Vehicle (1 mark):** SULEV tailpipe standards + zero evaporative emissions; sealed fuel system. 6. **AT-PZEV — Advanced Technology PZEV (1 mark):** Hybrid drivetrain combined with SULEV tailpipe and zero evaporative emissions. 7. **ZEV — Zero Emission Vehicle (1 mark):** 0.000 g/mi; BEV or FCEV only; tailpipe is eliminated. **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 (1 mark):** State the formula: $$ M_{\text{total}} = E_{\text{rate}} \times d $$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** For SULEV: $$ M_{\text{SULEV}} = 0.010 \text{ g/mi} \times 150{,}000 \text{ mi} = 1{,}500 \text{ g} = 1.5 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** For LEV baseline: $$ M_{\text{LEV}} = 0.090 \text{ g/mi} \times 150{,}000 \text{ mi} = 13{,}500 \text{ g} = 13.5 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute the difference: $$ \Delta M = 13.5 - 1.5 = 12.0 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute the percentage reduction: $$ \text{Reduction} = \frac{12.0}{13.5} \times 100\% = 88.89\% $$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** State the conclusion: > A SULEV releases **1.5 kg** of NMOG+NOx over its full life — an **88.89% reduction** compared to the LEV baseline's 13.5 kg. **Step 7 (1 mark):** Engineering implication — even SULEV is not zero; only ZEV achieves 100% elimination. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper] **(a)** Describe the after-treatment technologies required to achieve SULEV certification. Explain why a ZEV cannot use these same technologies to reach true zero emissions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for gasoline (C₈H₁₈) being 14.7:1, calculate the mass of air required to completely combust 1 kg of gasoline. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:** 1. **Close-coupled pre-catalyst (1 mark):** Mounted within 30 cm of the exhaust manifold; reaches light-off temperature in 5–10 seconds, critical for cold-start HC reduction. 2. **High-cell-density three-way catalyst (1 mark):** 900 cells/in² substrate with Pd-Rh washcoat and ceria-based oxygen storage; simultaneously reduces NOx, oxidizes CO and HC. 3. **Secondary air injection (1 mark):** Injects atmospheric O₂ during cold-start to oxidize the rich mixture of unburnt hydrocarbons. 4. **Wide-band UEGO sensor (1 mark):** Controls stoichiometric AFR within ±0.5% to maintain three-way catalyst efficiency. 5. **PZEV-grade sealed fuel system (1 mark):** For PZEV, multi-layer fuel tanks, zero-permeation hoses, and activated carbon canisters eliminate evaporative emissions. 6. **Why ZEV cannot use these (1 mark):** All these technologies assume an *exhaust stream exists* — they clean combustion byproducts. A ZEV eliminates combustion, so there is no exhaust to clean. 7. **Fundamental difference (1 mark):** ULVs manage pollution; ZEVs prevent pollution at the molecular/chemical level. **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 (1 mark):** State the balanced combustion equation: $$ \text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + 12.5\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 8\text{CO}_2 + 9\text{H}_2\text{O} $$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Calculate molecular weight of C₈H₁₈: $$ M_{\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18}} = (8 \times 12) + (18 \times 1) = 114 \text{ g/mol} $$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Calculate moles of fuel in 1 kg: $$ n = \frac{1000 \text{ g}}{114 \text{ g/mol}} = 8.772 \text{ mol} $$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Calculate moles of O₂ required: $$ n_{O_2} = 8.772 \times 12.5 = 109.65 \text{ mol} $$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Convert O₂ moles to air moles (air = 21% O₂ by volume): $$ n_{\text{air}} = \frac{109.65}{0.21} = 522.14 \text{ mol of air} $$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** Convert to mass using air molecular weight (28.97 g/mol): $$ m_{\text{air}} = 522.14 \times 28.97 = 15{,}127.2 \text{ g} \approx 15.13 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 7 (1 mark):** Verification using the AFR ratio: $$ \text{AFR} = 14.7 \Rightarrow m_{\text{air}} = 1 \times 14.7 = 14.7 \text{ kg} $$ > [!NOTE] > The two methods differ slightly because the AFR of 14.7:1 is a mass-based engineering convention, while the stoichiometric calculation above is mole-based. The KTU-accepted answer is **14.7 kg of air per 1 kg of gasoline** (using the mass-based AFR directly) for simplicity. Either answer with proper justification earns full marks. --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing ULEV with ULEV-II** — the latter is an obsolete Indian term. Always use the CARB-defined 0.050 g/mi limit. > 2. **Writing "ZEV has no emissions at all"** — this is wrong! ZEV has **zero tailpipe emissions** but can have upstream (well-to-wheel) emissions from electricity generation. Be precise. > 3. **Forgetting to state units** — every emission number MUST carry the unit (g/mi or g/km). Marks are deducted silently. > 4. **Mixing up PZEV and ZEV** — PZEV has an ICE with zero *evaporative* emissions; ZEV has zero *tailpipe* emissions. They are not the same. > 5. **Skipping calculation steps** — KTU examiners follow a strict step-marking scheme. Each line in your derivation is a potential mark; do not combine steps. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **ULV & ZEV — 60-Second Rapid Revision:** > > - [x] **ULV (ULEV)** = Ultra-Low Emission Vehicle, **0.050 g/mi** NMOG+NOx, still uses ICE > - [x] **SULEV** = Super Ultra-Low, **0.010 g/mi**, requires close-coupled catalyst + secondary air injection > - [x] **PZEV** = Partial Zero Emission, SULEV tailpipe + **zero evaporative emissions** > - [x] **ZEV** = Zero tailpipe emissions, **0.000 g/mi**, BEV or FCEV only > - [x] **AT-PZEV** = Advanced Technology PZEV = Hybrid + SULEV + zero evap > - [x] **Test Cycle:** FTP-75 (US), WLTP (EU/India) > - [x] **Full Useful Life:** 150,000 miles ≈ 241,000 km > - [x] **Stoichiometric AFR for gasoline:** 14.7:1 > - [x] **Core pollutants:** NMOG, NOx, CO, Formaldehyde > - [x] **Hierarchy (lowest to highest cleanliness):** Conventional → LEV → ULEV → SULEV → PZEV → AT-PZEV → ZEV > - [x] **Light-off temperature of TWC:** ~300 °C, achieved in 5–10 s with close-coupled design > - [x] **Reduction from ULEV to SULEV:** **80%** (use the formula 0.050 → 0.010) > - [x] **Reduction from LEV to SULEV:** **88.9%** (use 0.090 → 0.010) > - [x] **CARB = California Air Resources Board** — origin of all these standards > - [x] **ZEV philosophy:** *Eliminate* the exhaust, not *clean* it ---

topics.title: Basic Components of Electric Vehicles - Inverters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMUT612 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Basic Components of Electric Vehicles - Inverters # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) An **automotive traction inverter** is a high-power, bidirectional DC-to-AC power electronic converter that forms the *electrical heart* of an Electric Vehicle (EV) powertrain. It interfaces the high-voltage DC battery pack (typically **300 V – 800 V**) with the AC traction motor (Induction Motor / PMSM / Reluctance Motor), controlling both **motoring torque** and **regenerative braking** by precisely modulating the frequency, amplitude, and phase of the output voltage using **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** strategies. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the inverter is classified as a **Vehicle Electrification Component** under the broader umbrella of **Power Electronics Converters (PECs)** — the bridge between the *energy source* (battery / fuel cell) and the *electromechanical load* (traction motor). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Definition to Memorize:** > *"An EV inverter is a solid-state, forced-commutated power electronic converter that uses fully controllable semiconductor switches (IGBT / SiC-MOSFET) to synthesize a variable-voltage, variable-frequency (VVVF) three-phase AC output from a fixed DC link, enabling bidirectional energy flow between the battery and the traction motor."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "DJ of the Powertrain" Imagine the EV battery as a **stationary water tank** (energy stored at one pressure) and the traction motor as a **spinning water wheel** (needs oscillating flow to turn smoothly). The inverter is the **bilingual translator at the door**: - It takes the **steady DC "language"** of the battery and converts it into the **3-phase AC "dialects"** the motor understands. - A **DJ's mixing console** is an even better analogy: the DJ has fixed music tracks (the DC bus) but uses sliders, knobs, and crossfaders (PWM duty cycles) to produce a completely new, variable rhythm (variable V and f for the motor). - Press the accelerator → DJ speeds up the beat (frequency ↑) and turns up the volume (voltage ↑). - Lift off the accelerator → the DJ *rewinds the same signal backward* — this is **regenerative braking**, where the inverter operates as a **boost rectifier**, pumping kinetic energy back into the DC battery. > [!NOTE] > **Key Performance Metrics (Board Must-Know):** > - **Switching frequency:** 5 kHz – 20 kHz (silicon IGBT), up to **100 kHz** (SiC/GaN wide-bandgap devices). > - **Efficiency target:** **> 95 %** at rated load; peak inverter efficiencies of **99 %** are achieved in modern Tesla / Lucid inverters using **SiC MOSFETs**. > - **Power density:** **30 – 50 kW / L** in state-of-the-art automotive traction inverters. > - **DC-link voltage:** **400 V** (主流) or **800 V** (premium / fast-charging platforms: Porsche Taycan, Hyundai E-GMP, Lucid Air). ## 1.3 Why the Inverter is the "Brain-Muscle" of an EV | Function | Role in EV | |---|---| | **DC → AC conversion** | Drives AC traction motor | | **Variable V/f control** | Matches motor torque-speed envelope (constant torque region, constant power region) | | **Bidirectional power flow** | Motoring + regenerative braking | | **Fast dynamic response** | Torque vectoring, stability control in < 1 ms | | **Galvanic isolation (via HVLV DC-DC)** | Safety barrier between high-voltage and low-voltage systems | | **EMI/EMC shaping** | Spread-spectrum switching to meet CISPR 25 automotive EMC norms | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of EV Inverters ### 2.1.1 By Power Stage Topology **A) Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** - DC link is a **stiff voltage source** (large capacitor $C_{dc}$). - Output is a **PWM voltage waveform** with controlled magnitude and frequency. - **Industry standard for EV traction** — used in **> 95 % of production EVs** (Tesla, Nissan Leaf, Chevy Bolt, Tata Nexon EV). **B) Current Source Inverter (CSI)** - DC link is a **stiff current source** (large inductor $L_{dc}$). - Output is a **PWM current waveform**; requires a series diode per switch. - Used in niche ultra-high-power applications; **rarely used in mass-market EVs** due to bulky inductors and slower dynamic response. ### 2.1.2 By Number of Voltage Levels | Topology | Levels | THD | dv/dt stress | EV Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 2-level VSI | 2 ($+V_{dc}, -V_{dc}$) | High | Severe | Legacy / low-cost EVs | | 3-level NPC (Neutral Point Clamped) | 3 | Lower | Halved | Mid-range EVs (Renault, Nissan) | | 3-level T-type | 3 | Lower | Halved | Compact EVs | | Multi-level (5/7-level) | $\geq 5$ | Very low | Very low | Heavy EVs, buses, trucks | ### 2.1.3 By Semiconductor Switch Technology | Switch | Voltage Rating | Frequency | Loss | EV Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | IGBT | up to 6.5 kV | ≤ 20 kHz | Moderate | Mainstream 400 V EVs | | SiC MOSFET | up to 1.7 kV | 50 – 100 kHz | **Low** | 800 V premium EVs (Tesla Model 3, Lucid) | | GaN HEMT | up to 650 V | > 100 kHz | **Very low** | Low-voltage auxiliary drives | | Si IGBT + SiC Diode | Hybrid | 15 – 20 kHz | Low | Cost-optimized 400 V | ## 2.2 Operating Principle — Six-Step / Square-Wave Mode A 3-phase 2-level VSI has **6 switches** ($S_1$ to $S_6$). Each leg produces $+V_{dc}$ when upper switch ON, and $-V_{dc}$ when lower switch ON. In the **six-step (180° conduction)** mode, each switch conducts for **180°** of the fundamental cycle, producing a quasi-square output. The phase displacement between legs is **120°**, generating a 3-phase balanced system. ## 2.3 Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) Strategies ### 2.3.1 Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM) A **sinusoidal reference** $v_c(t) = V_c \sin(\omega t)$ is compared with a **high-frequency triangular carrier** $v_{tri}(t)$ of amplitude $V_{tri}$ and frequency $f_{tri}$. The comparator output drives the switches. **Modulation Index:** $$m_a = \frac{V_c}{V_{tri}} \quad (0 \leq m_a \leq 1)$$ **Frequency Modulation Ratio:** $$m_f = \frac{f_{tri}}{f_{ref}}$$ For **linear modulation** ($m_a \leq 1$): peak of fundamental phase voltage $= m_a \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2}$. ### 2.3.2 Space Vector PWM (SVPWM) — *Industry Standard for EVs* - Treats the 3-phase system as a single **rotating space vector** in the $\alpha\beta$ plane. - **8 switching states** → 6 active + 2 zero vectors forming a hexagon. - Achieves **15.5 % higher DC-link utilization** than SPWM (fundamental peak phase voltage = $V_{dc}/\sqrt{3}$ instead of $V_{dc}/2$). - Lower harmonic current ripple → smoother torque, less battery stress. - Implemented in **all modern EV motor control units (MCUs)** — e.g., Infineon AURIX™, TI C2000, NXP S32K3. > [!NOTE] > **Why SVPWM Dominates EV Inverters:** > 1. Higher linear modulation range ($m_a$ up to $1.154$ before overmodulation). > 2. Reduced switching losses through optimized zero-vector placement. > 3. Direct digital implementation in fixed-point DSPs. > 4. Better DC-bus utilization → smaller battery for same torque. ## 2.4 Bidirectional Operation & Regenerative Braking During **motoring**: DC battery → Inverter (DC→AC) → Motor (acts as motor). During **regeneration**: Motor (acts as generator) → Inverter (AC→DC, **rectifier mode**) → DC battery. The same IGBT/SiC switches are used; only the **PWM logic** and **gate timing** are reversed. The anti-parallel diodes conduct naturally during freewheeling. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Pitfall:** Many students forget that the **anti-parallel body diode** of an IGBT/MOSFET is the *primary current path* during regenerative mode. Do not write "extra diode bridges" — the inverter is intrinsically bidirectional by virtue of its **MOSFET/IGBT body diodes + controlled turn-on**. ## 2.5 Thermal Management — The Hidden Hero Power loss per switch: $$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{dc} \cdot I_{load} \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off}) \cdot f_{sw}$$ Conduction loss per IGBT: $$P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{avg} + r_{CE} \cdot I_{rms}^{2}$$ Total inverter losses typically **2 – 8 %** of rated power → rejected as heat → removed via: - **Liquid cooling** (cold-plate integrated into inverter housing — Tesla, Lucid approach). - **Oil-cooled** modules (Toyota Prius family). ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready) | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $m_a = V_c / V_{tri}$ | Amplitude Modulation Index (SPWM) | dimensionless | | 2 | $m_f = f_{tri} / f_{ref}$ | Frequency Modulation Ratio | dimensionless | | 3 | $V_{1,phase(rms)} = \frac{m_a \cdot V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}}$ | SPWM fundamental phase RMS voltage (linear) | V | | 4 | $V_{1,LL(rms)} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{m_a \cdot V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}}$ | SPWM fundamental line-to-line RMS voltage | V | | 5 | $V_{1,LL(peak)} = \frac{m_a \cdot V_{dc}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | SVPWM fundamental line voltage (linear) | V | | 6 | $V_{LL,rms}^{(sq)} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \cdot V_{dc} \approx 0.816 \cdot V_{dc}$ | Six-step / square-wave line RMS voltage | V | | 7 | $THD = \frac{\sqrt{V_{rms}^{2} - V_{1}^{2}}}{V_{1}}$ | Total Harmonic Distortion | % | | 8 | $P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} V_{dc} I (t_{on} + t_{off}) f_{sw}$ | Switching loss per device | W | | 9 | $P_{cond} = V_{CE} I_{avg} + r_{CE} I_{rms}^{2}$ | Conduction loss per IGBT | W | | 10 | $\eta_{inv} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{loss}}$ | Inverter efficiency | % | | 11 | $f_{sw,min} > 20 \cdot f_{max,ref}$ | Minimum switching frequency (audio noise avoidance) | Hz | | 12 | $T_{em} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot (\lambda_{pm} I_q + (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q)$ | PMSM electromagnetic torque (inverter-fed) | N·m | | 13 | $C_{dc} \geq \frac{I_{rms}}{2 \cdot \Delta V_{dc} \cdot f_{sw}}$ | DC-link capacitor sizing | F | | 14 | $V_{LL,rms}^{(SVPWM,max)} = \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Max SVPWM linear-region line RMS | V | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world utility:** These formulas are used daily by EV design engineers at Tata Motors, Ather Energy, and Ola Electric to size the DC-link capacitor ($C_{dc}$), select the IGBT current rating, design the LC filter, and program the SVPWM lookup table in the motor controller firmware. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: RMS Value of Single-Phase Full-Bridge Square-Wave Inverter Output **Problem statement:** A single-phase full-bridge VSI is fed from a DC source $V_{dc}$. Each switch conducts for $180°$ (square-wave output). Derive the RMS value of the output voltage and the magnitude of the fundamental component using Fourier series. ### Step 1: Define the output waveform mathematically The output voltage $v_o(t)$ is an **odd, half-wave symmetric** square wave alternating between $+V_{dc}$ and $-V_{dc}$ every half-cycle. Therefore, only **odd harmonics** exist, and their Fourier coefficients are: $$v_o(t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,...}^{\infty} b_n \sin(n \omega t)$$ ### Step 2: Compute the Fourier coefficient $b_n$ $$b_n = \frac{2}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} V_{dc} \sin(n \omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$ Performing the integration: $$b_n = \frac{2 V_{dc}}{\pi} \left[ \frac{-\cos(n \omega t)}{n} \right]_{0}^{\pi}$$ $$b_n = \frac{2 V_{dc}}{n \pi} \left[ -\cos(n \pi) + \cos(0) \right]$$ $$b_n = \frac{2 V_{dc}}{n \pi} \left[ 1 - \cos(n \pi) \right]$$ ### Step 3: Apply parity conditions - For $n$ **even**: $\cos(n \pi) = +1 \Rightarrow b_n = 0$ → no even harmonics (confirmed). - For $n$ **odd**: $\cos(n \pi) = -1 \Rightarrow b_n = \frac{4 V_{dc}}{n \pi}$ ### Step 4: Final Fourier series expression $$v_o(t) = \frac{4 V_{dc}}{\pi} \left[ \sin(\omega t) + \frac{1}{3}\sin(3 \omega t) + \frac{1}{5}\sin(5 \omega t) + \frac{1}{7}\sin(7 \omega t) + ... \right]$$ ### Step 5: Magnitude of the fundamental component (n = 1) $$V_{1,peak} = \frac{4 V_{dc}}{\pi}$$ $$V_{1,rms} = \frac{4 V_{dc}}{\pi \sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\sqrt{2} \cdot V_{dc}}{\pi} \approx 0.9003 \cdot V_{dc}$$ ### Step 6: Total RMS value of the entire square wave (all harmonics included) By Parseval's theorem for a square wave oscillating between $\pm V_{dc}$: $$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o^{2}(t) \, d(\omega t)} = V_{dc}$$ ### Step 7: Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_{rms}^{2} - V_{1,rms}^{2}}}{V_{1,rms}}$$ $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_{dc}^{2} - \left(\frac{4V_{dc}}{\pi\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}}}{\frac{4V_{dc}}{\pi\sqrt{2}}}$$ $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{1 - \frac{8}{\pi^{2}}}}{\frac{4}{\pi\sqrt{2}}} = \frac{\sqrt{1 - 0.8106}}{0.9003} = \frac{0.4359}{0.9003}$$ $$\boxed{\text{THD}_{sq} \approx 0.4842 \text{ or } 48.42\%}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is why PWM is mandatory in EV inverters.** A pure square wave has **48.42 % THD** — disastrous for motor torque ripple, audible noise, and battery losses. PWM reduces THD to **< 5 %** in modern EV drives. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: SVPWM DC-Link Utilization vs. SPWM **Problem statement:** Prove that SVPWM gives **15.47 % higher** fundamental voltage than SPWM in the linear modulation region. ### Step 1: Maximum fundamental phase voltage in SPWM (linear) In SPWM, when $m_a = 1$ (max linear), the peak of the fundamental phase voltage is: $$V_{1,peak}^{SPWM} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2}$$ ### Step 2: Maximum fundamental phase voltage in SVPWM (linear) The SVPWM technique uses the inscribed circle of the hexagon. The maximum radius (modulation vector) of the inscribed circle in the hexagon of side $\frac{2 V_{dc}}{3}$ is: $$V_{1,peak}^{SVPWM} = \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}$$ ### Step 3: Ratio $$\frac{V_{1,peak}^{SVPWM}}{V_{1,peak}^{SPWM}} = \frac{V_{dc}/\sqrt{3}}{V_{dc}/2} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} = 1.1547$$ ### Step 4: Percentage increase $$\Delta\% = \left( \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} - 1 \right) \times 100\% = 15.47\%$$ $$\boxed{\text{SVPWM delivers } 15.47\% \text{ more fundamental voltage at } m_a = 1}$$ **Engineering interpretation:** For a given DC-link voltage $V_{dc}$, SVPWM-extracted motors produce **15.47 % more torque** at the same current — this is the principal reason every production EV uses SVPWM. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: DC-Link Capacitor Sizing for an EV Inverter **Problem statement:** An EV inverter draws 200 A (RMS) from a 400 V DC link at a switching frequency of 10 kHz. Maximum allowable peak-to-peak ripple voltage is 5 % of $V_{dc}$. Compute the minimum DC-link capacitance. ### Step 1: Allowed ripple voltage $$\Delta V_{dc} = 0.05 \times 400 = 20 \text{ V}$$ ### Step 2: Charge transferred per switching half-cycle (worst case) For a single-phase equivalent, the ripple current charges/discharges the capacitor at each switching transition: $$\Delta Q = \frac{I_{rms}}{2 \cdot f_{sw}}$$ $$\Delta Q = \frac{200}{2 \times 10{,}000} = 0.01 \text{ C}$$ ### Step 3: Capacitance $$C_{dc} = \frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta V_{dc}} = \frac{0.01}{20} = 500 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F}$$ $$\boxed{C_{dc} \geq 500 \ \mu F}$$ **Practical EV design:** A 400 V EV inverter uses multiple **film capacitors** in parallel (e.g., 10 × 50 μF) directly soldered onto the bus-bar. Total volume ≈ 1 – 2 litres, with ripple current rating of **> 300 A RMS at 10 kHz** and **lifetime > 15 years** at 105 °C ambient. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 EV Powertrain Block Diagram — Position of the Inverter ```mermaid flowchart LR BAT[HV Battery Pack
300V to 800V DC] -->|DC Link| INV[Traction Inverter
DC to AC VVVF] INV -->|3-phase AC| MOT[AC Traction Motor
PMSM or IM] MOT -->|Mechanical| GB[Single Speed
Gearbox] GB --> WH[Drive Wheels] MOT -.->|Back EMF| INV INV -.->|Charging DC| BAT BMS[BMS + VCU] -->|CAN Bus| INV INV -->|Resolver / Encoder| MOT INV --> THM[Cold Plate
Liquid Cooling] classDef power fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef ctrl fill:#065f46,stroke:#34d399,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef mech fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fcd34d,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff class BAT,INV,MOT,GB,WH power class BMS,THM ctrl ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** > - **Solid arrows** = power flow (motoring mode: Battery → Inverter → Motor → Wheels). > - **Dashed arrows** = feedback / regenerative flow. > - The **VCU (Vehicle Control Unit)** issues torque commands over CAN-FD to the inverter; the inverter uses a **rotary resolver** for rotor-position feedback to the PMSM/IM. --- ## 4.2 3-Phase 2-Level Voltage Source Inverter — Power Stage Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DC_LINK["DC Link Capacitor Bank"] C1[CDC] end VDC[(VDC
400V or 800V)] --> C1 C1 --> PHASEA C1 --> PHASEB C1 --> PHASEC subgraph PHASEA["Phase A Leg"] S1A[S1 IGBT/MOSFET] --- S2A[S2 IGBT/MOSFET] end subgraph PHASEB["Phase B Leg"] S1B[S3 IGBT/MOSFET] --- S2B[S4 IGBT/MOSFET] end subgraph PHASEC["Phase C Leg"] S1C[S5 IGBT/MOSFET] --- S2C[S6 IGBT/MOSFET] end PHASEA --> MA[(PMSM Stator
Phase A)] PHASEB --> MB[(PMSM Stator
Phase B)] PHASEC --> MC[(PMSM Stator
Phase C)] G1[Gate Driver
+ Isolated DC-DC] --> S1A G1 --> S2A G1 --> S1B G1 --> S2B G1 --> S1C G1 --> S2C classDef power fill:#0f172a,stroke:#22d3ee,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef ctrl fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fcd34d,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef motor fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff class VDC,C1,PHASEA,PHASEB,PHASEC power class G1 ctrl class MA,MB,MC motor ``` --- ## 4.3 SVPWM Switching States and Hexagon Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB START[SVPWM Algorithm in MCU] --> SENSE[Read Ia Ib Ic and Rotor Angle theta_e] SENSE --> SECTOR[Determine Sector N from theta_e
1 to 6] SECTOR --> TIME[Compute T1 T2 T0
Active and Zero Vector Durations] TIME --> LUT[Lookup Switching Table
from SVPWM ROM] LUT --> DEAD[Insert Dead Time
Td approx 1 to 2 microsec] DEAD --> GD[Drive Gate Signals
PWM1H to PWM3L] GD --> INV[Update Power Stage
2-Level VSI] classDef proc fill:#064e3b,stroke:#10b981,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef io fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fcd34d,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef hw fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff class START,SECTOR,TIME,LUT proc class SENSE,GD io class DEAD,INV hw ``` > [!NOTE] > **SVPWM Hexagon Geometry (8 switching states):** > - **6 active vectors** $V_1$ to $V_6$ at hexagon vertices → correspond to switching states **100, 110, 010, 011, 001, 101**. > - **2 zero vectors** $V_0 = V_7 = 000$ or $111$ → no energy transfer, used to fill the remaining dwell time. > - Any reference vector $V_{ref}$ within the hexagon is synthesized as a **time-weighted average** of two adjacent active vectors and a zero vector. --- ## 4.4 Bidirectional Power Flow (Motoring vs. Regeneration) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph M1[MOTORING MODE] M1A[Battery DC] -->|Discharge| M1B[Inverter acts as
DC to AC Converter] M1B -->|Variable V and f| M1C[Motor produces
Positive Torque] end subgraph M2[REGENERATIVE BRAKING] M2A[Motor acts as
Generator] -->|AC produced| M2B[Inverter acts as
AC to DC Boost Rectifier] M2B -->|Charging Current| M2C[Battery accepts
Charge] end M1 -.->|Driver lifts
accelerator| M2 M2 -.->|Driver presses
accelerator| M1 classDef mode1 fill:#065f46,stroke:#10b981,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef mode2 fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#fb923c,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff class M1A,M1B,M1C mode1 class M2A,M2B,M2C mode2 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define an automotive traction inverter. List any three functional requirements it must satisfy in an EV.** **Model Answer:** An automotive traction inverter is a bidirectional DC-to-AC power electronic converter using fully controllable semiconductor switches (IGBT / SiC MOSFET) that converts the fixed DC voltage of the high-voltage battery into a variable-voltage, variable-frequency (VVVF) three-phase AC output for driving the AC traction motor. **Three functional requirements:** 1. **Bidirectional power flow** — must support motoring and regenerative braking without additional hardware. 2. **High efficiency** — ≥ 95 % at rated load to maximise driving range. 3. **Wide operating speed range** — typically **0 – 18,000 rpm** for a passenger EV, requiring fundamental frequencies from near 0 Hz to ~ 1.2 kHz at maximum speed. 4. *(Bonus)* Robust thermal management and compliance with **ISO 26262 ASIL-D** functional safety. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Compare Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) and Current Source Inverter (CSI) for EV applications.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | VSI | CSI | |---|---|---| | DC-link element | Capacitor | Inductor | | Output controlled | Voltage waveform | Current waveform | | Dynamic response | Fast (preferred for EVs) | Slow (large L) | | Bidirectional capability | Intrinsic (body diode) | Requires series diode | | Efficiency | **> 96 %** | ~ 92 – 94 % | | Size / Weight | Compact | Bulky inductor | | EV adoption | **> 95 % of EVs** | Niche only | **Conclusion:** VSI is the *de facto* standard for mass-market EVs. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format) ### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Attempt either A or B* **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1 Choice Question]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of a 3-phase, 2-level Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) with a neat circuit diagram. Discuss the **180° conduction mode** and obtain the expressions for the **RMS value of the line voltage** and the **fundamental component of the phase voltage** in the six-step mode. **(b) [7 Marks]** A 3-phase VSI is fed from a 600 V DC link and supplies a star-connected PMSM. Using SPWM with modulation index $m_a = 0.85$ and switching frequency 10 kHz, calculate: - (i) Peak and RMS value of the fundamental phase voltage. - (ii) RMS value of the fundamental line-to-line voltage. - (iii) The THD of the line current assuming the dominant 5th and 7th harmonics contribute 8 % and 6 % of the fundamental respectively. --- ### ✅ Complete Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a) — Working Principle of 3-Phase 2-Level VSI (180° Conduction) A 3-phase, 2-level VSI consists of **three legs** (one per phase) with **two complementary switches per leg** (S1–S2, S3–S4, S5–S6). The upper and lower switches of a leg are **never ON simultaneously** (shoot-through protection via dead-time). **180° Conduction Sequence (one full cycle 360°):** | Interval | Conducting Switches | Phase Voltages (wrt DC mid-point) | |---|---|---| | 0° – 60° | S1, S6, S5 | $V_A = +V_{dc}/2$, $V_B = -V_{dc}/2$, $V_C = +V_{dc}/2$ | | 60° – 120° | S1, S6, S4 | $V_A = +V_{dc}/2$, $V_B = -V_{dc}/2$, $V_C = -V_{dc}/2$ | | 120° – 180° | S1, S3, S4 | $V_A = +V_{dc}/2$, $V_B = +V_{dc}/2$, $V_C = -V_{dc}/2$ | | 180° – 240° | S2, S3, S4 | $V_A = -V_{dc}/2$, $V_B = +V_{dc}/2$, $V_C = -V_{dc}/2$ | | 240° – 300° | S2, S3, S6 | $V_A = -V_{dc}/2$, $V_B = +V_{dc}/2$, $V_C = +V_{dc}/2$ | | 300° – 360° | S2, S4, S6 | $V_A = -V_{dc}/2$, $V_B = -V_{dc}/2$, $V_C = +V_{dc}/2$ | Each switch conducts for **180°** → six discrete steps per cycle → **six-step (quasi-square) operation**. **RMS Line Voltage (Six-Step):** $$V_{LL,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi/3} V_{dc}^{2} \, d\theta} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \cdot V_{dc} \approx 0.8165 \cdot V_{dc}$$ **Fundamental Peak Phase Voltage (Fourier analysis of quasi-square):** $$V_{1,peak}^{(phase)} = \frac{2 V_{dc}}{\pi} \approx 0.6366 \cdot V_{dc}$$ $$V_{1,rms}^{(phase)} = \frac{\sqrt{2} V_{dc}}{\pi} \approx 0.4502 \cdot V_{dc}$$ $$\boxed{V_{LL,rms}^{(sq)} \approx 0.8165 \cdot V_{dc}, \quad V_{1,peak}^{(phase)} = \frac{2 V_{dc}}{\pi}}$$ --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **Given:** $V_{dc} = 600$ V, $m_a = 0.85$, SPWM in linear region. **(i) Peak and RMS fundamental phase voltage:** $$V_{1,peak}^{(phase)} = \frac{m_a \cdot V_{dc}}{2} = \frac{0.85 \times 600}{2} = 255 \text{ V}$$ $$V_{1,rms}^{(phase)} = \frac{255}{\sqrt{2}} = 180.31 \text{ V}$$ **(ii) RMS fundamental line-to-line voltage:** $$V_{1,LL,rms} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{1,rms}^{(phase)} = \sqrt{3} \times 180.31 = 312.31 \text{ V}$$ **(iii) Approximate current THD** (with 5th harmonic at 8 % and 7th harmonic at 6 % of fundamental): For a stiff DC link and inductive load, the line current harmonics are: $$\text{THD}_I \approx \sqrt{(0.08)^2 + (0.06)^2} = \sqrt{0.0064 + 0.0036} = \sqrt{0.01} = 0.10$$ $$\boxed{\text{THD}_I \approx 10\%}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > 1. Do not confuse **phase voltage** and **line voltage** in SPWM derivations — KTU examiners specifically check whether the $\sqrt{3}$ factor has been correctly applied. > 2. In **linear modulation region**, the formula $V_{1,peak} = m_a \cdot V_{dc}/2$ is **only valid for SPWM**. For SVPWM, the formula is $V_{1,peak} = m_a \cdot V_{dc}/\sqrt{3}$. Mixing them up costs 2 marks. > 3. **THD for current ≠ THD for voltage** in PWM inverters. The inductive load attenuates high-frequency voltage harmonics, so the current THD is much lower than the voltage THD. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1 Choice Question]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With a block diagram, describe the **Space Vector PWM (SVPWM)** technique for a 3-phase VSI. Explain the **8 switching states**, the **hexagon diagram**, and clearly show how SVPWM achieves **15.47 % higher DC-link utilisation** than SPWM. **(b) [7 Marks]** An EV traction inverter uses a 400 V DC battery, SiC MOSFET switches with switching frequency 50 kHz, driving a PMSM that draws 250 A RMS at 200 Hz fundamental frequency. Calculate: - (i) The maximum fundamental line-to-line RMS voltage (SVPWM, linear region). - (ii) The switching loss per device if $t_{on} + t_{off} = 80$ ns. - (iii) The total inverter switching loss for 6 switches and compare it with the rated output power to estimate efficiency. --- ### ✅ Complete Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a) — Space Vector PWM (SVPWM) Block Diagram & Theory **Definition:** SVPWM is a digital PWM technique that represents the 3-phase reference voltages as a single rotating space vector $\vec{V_{ref}}$ in the stationary $\alpha\beta$ reference frame, synthesised using the 6 active and 2 zero switching states of the 2-level VSI. **8 Switching States of the 2-Level VSI:** | State | $S_A$ | $S_B$ | $S_C$ | Vector | Magnitude | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | $V_0$ (zero) | 0 | | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | $V_1$ | $\frac{2 V_{dc}}{3}$ | | 2 | 1 | 1 | 0 | $V_2$ | $\frac{2 V_{dc}}{3}$ | | 3 | 0 | 1 | 0 | $V_3$ | $\frac{2 V_{dc}}{3}$ | | 4 | 0 | 1 | 1 | $V_4$ | $\frac{2 V_{dc}}{3}$ | | 5 | 0 | 0 | 1 | $V_5$ | $\frac{2 V_{dc}}{3}$ | | 6 | 1 | 0 | 1 | $V_6$ | $\frac{2 V_{dc}}{3}$ | | 7 | 1 | 1 | 1 | $V_7$ (zero) | 0 | **Hexagon Construction:** The 6 active vectors $V_1$ to $V_6$ (each of magnitude $\frac{2 V_{dc}}{3}$) form the vertices of a regular hexagon. The hexagon is divided into **6 sectors** of 60° each. **Synthesis Principle:** In any sector, the reference vector $\vec{V_{ref}}$ is realised as: $$\vec{V_{ref}} \cdot T_s = \vec{V_k} \cdot T_k + \vec{V_{k+1}} \cdot T_{k+1} + \vec{V_0} \cdot T_0$$ where $T_k$ and $T_{k+1}$ are the dwell times of the two adjacent active vectors, and $T_0$ is the zero-vector time such that $T_k + T_{k+1} + T_0 = T_s$. **Mathematical Dwell-Time Equations (Sector 1):** $$T_1 = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{\vert \vec{V_{ref}} \vert}{V_{dc}} \cdot T_s \cdot \sin(60° - \theta)$$ $$T_2 = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{\vert \vec{V_{ref}} \vert}{V_{dc}} \cdot T_s \cdot \sin(\theta)$$ $$T_0 = T_s - T_1 - T_2$$ **Proof of 15.47 % Higher DC-Link Utilization:** Maximum magnitude of $\vec{V_{ref}}$ that fits inside the hexagon = radius of inscribed circle = $\frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}$.
