In SPWM, maximum fundamental peak phase voltage (linear) = $\frac{V_{dc}}{2}$. $$\frac{V_{SVPWM}^{max}}{V_{SPWM}^{max}} = \frac{V_{dc}/\sqrt{3}}{V_{dc}/2} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} = 1.1547$$ $$\Delta\% = (1.1547 - 1) \times 100\% = 15.47\%$$ $$\boxed{\text{SVPWM yields } 15.47\% \text{ more fundamental voltage than SPWM}}$$ --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **Given:** $V_{dc} = 400$ V, $f_{sw} = 50$ kHz, $I_{rms} = 250$ A, $f_{ref} = 200$ Hz, $t_{on} + t_{off} = 80$ ns. **(i) Maximum fundamental line-to-line RMS voltage (SVPWM, linear region):** $$V_{1,peak}^{(LL,SVPWM)} = \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{2}} = 282.84 \text{ V}$$ $$V_{1,LL,rms}^{(SVPWM)} = \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2} = 200 \text{ V}$$ $$\boxed{V_{1,LL,rms}^{(max,SVPWM)} = 200 \text{ V}}$$ **(ii) Switching loss per device:** $$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{dc} \cdot I_{rms} \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off}) \cdot f_{sw}$$ $$P_{sw} = 0.5 \times 400 \times 250 \times (80 \times 10^{-9}) \times (50 \times 10^{3})$$ $$P_{sw} = 0.5 \times 400 \times 250 \times 80 \times 10^{-9} \times 50 \times 10^{3}$$ $$P_{sw} = 0.5 \times 400 \times 250 \times 4 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$P_{sw} = 0.5 \times 400 \times 1$$ $$P_{sw} = 200 \text{ W per device}$$ $$\boxed{P_{sw} = 200 \text{ W per SiC MOSFET}}$$ **(iii) Total switching loss for 6 switches and efficiency estimate:** $$P_{sw,total} = 6 \times 200 = 1200 \text{ W} = 1.2 \text{ kW}$$ **Output power (3-phase apparent):** $$S_{out} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{1,LL,rms} \cdot I_{rms} = \sqrt{3} \times 200 \times 250 = 86{,}603 \text{ VA} \approx 86.6 \text{ kVA}$$ For unity power factor (PMSM): $P_{out} = 86.6$ kW. **Efficiency:** $$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{sw,total}} = \frac{86{,}600}{86{,}600 + 1{,}200} = \frac{86{,}600}{87{,}800}$$ $$\boxed{\eta \approx 98.63\%}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** This **98.63 %** efficiency validates why SiC MOSFET inverters dominate premium 800 V EVs. The switching loss is less than **1.4 %** of output power, leaving most losses to conduction ($I^{2}R$) and gate drive. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:** > 1. In SVPWM, do **not** confuse the maximum *modulation index* (1.1547) with the maximum *linear* $m_a$ (1.0). Linear region ends at $m_a = 1$; overmodulation occurs between 1.0 and 1.1547. > 2. Switching loss formula assumes **hard switching**. For ZVS (zero-voltage switching) used in some EV chargers, $P_{sw}$ is **near zero** and must be calculated differently. > 3. The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ in $P_{sw}$ accounts for the fact that switching loss occurs once per **rising** and once per **falling** edge across the *full* DC link voltage — examiners frequently test whether students apply this factor correctly. > 4. SiC MOSFETs have **negligible turn-off tail current** unlike silicon IGBTs — this is the key reason they achieve **2 – 5× lower switching losses** than IGBTs of the same rating. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **🔑 One-Page Revision — Inverters in EVs:** - [x] **Inverter = DC → AC bidirectional converter** for traction motor; same hardware handles motoring AND regenerative braking. - [x] **VSI is the industry standard** (95 %+ of EVs); CSI is academic / niche. - [x] **2-level VSI** uses **6 switches** (IGBT / SiC MOSFET); produces $+V_{dc}$ or $-V_{dc}$ per leg. - [x] **Modulation index** $m_a = V_c / V_{tri}$ (SPWM); $m_f = f_{tri} / f_{ref}$ (frequency ratio). - [x] **SVPWM** gives **15.47 % higher DC-link utilization** than SPWM — universally used in EV motor controllers. - [x] **PWM reduces THD** from ~48 % (square wave) to <5 %. - [x] **Switching frequency** must exceed **20 ×** the highest fundamental frequency to avoid audible noise (above 20 kHz for human hearing comfort). - [x] **SiC MOSFETs** dominate 800 V platforms; **IGBTs** dominate 400 V platforms. - [x] **Switching loss** $P_{sw} = 0.5 \cdot V_{dc} \cdot I \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off}) \cdot f_{sw}$. - [x] **Conduction loss** $P_{cond} = V_{CE} I_{avg} + r_{CE} I_{rms}^{2}$. - [x] **Bidirectional operation** is achieved *intrinsically* through anti-parallel body diodes — **no extra hardware** required. - [x] **Liquid cooling** is mandatory for traction inverters > 50 kW. - [x] **Key formulae** for KTU exam: $V_{1,peak}^{(SPWM,ph)} = m_a V_{dc}/2$; $V_{1,peak}^{(SVPWM,ph)} = m_a V_{dc}/\sqrt{3}$; $V_{LL,rms}^{(sq)} = \sqrt{2/3} \cdot V_{dc}$; THD = $\sqrt{V_{rms}^2 - V_1^2}/V_1$. - [x] **Three-Level NPC** and **T-type** inverters reduce dv/dt and THD, used in high-end EVs. - [x] **Inverter is controlled by the VCU** via CAN-FD or automotive Ethernet; receives torque command, returns motor speed/temperature. - [x] **Functional safety standard:** ISO 26262 **ASIL-D** (highest) for the inverter control firmware. - [x] **Real-world leaders:** Tesla (custom SiC inverter), BYD (8-in-1 e-axle with integrated SiC inverter), Infineon (HybridPACK Drive IGBT/SiC modules), Wolfspeed (XM3 SiC power modules).

topics.title: Battery Packs and Battery Management System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT612 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Battery Packs and Battery Management System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Battery Pack — Formal Definition

A **Battery Pack** is the integrated, modular electrochemical energy storage assembly that combines multiple individual battery cells (in series and/or parallel configurations) along with structural housings, thermal management components, electrical interconnects, and protective circuitry. It serves as the primary high-voltage energy reservoir in Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs), Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (PHEVs), and Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs).

In KTU 2024 Scheme automotive terminology, a battery pack is **not merely a collection of cells** — it is a tightly engineered **electrochemical subsystem** designed to deliver:
- A specified operating **voltage window** (typically **400 V** or **800 V** architectures in modern EVs),
- A defined **usable energy capacity** (measured in **kWh**),
- A target **continuous and peak power output** (measured in **kW**), and
- A guaranteed **cycle life** and **calendar life** under automotive duty cycles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A single Li-ion cell delivers about **3.6 V – 4.2 V** nominal-to-max. To build a 400 V pack, approximately **96–100 cells in series** are required. To meet capacity, several such series-strings are placed in **parallel**, forming a **module**, and modules form the **pack**.

## 1.2 Battery Management System (BMS) — Formal Definition

A **Battery Management System (BMS)** is an embedded electronic control system that monitors, manages, and protects a rechargeable battery pack by acquiring real-time cell-level data, executing closed-loop control algorithms, and interfacing with vehicle-level controllers (VCU, ECU) over communication buses such as **CAN** (Controller Area Network).

The BMS performs six critical functions codified by KTU's automotive electronics syllabus:
1. **Cell Monitoring** (Voltage, Current, Temperature — V/I/T acquisition)
2. **State Estimation** (SOC, SOH, SOP, SOE)
3. **Cell Balancing** (Passive and Active)
4. **Thermal Management Coordination**
5. **Safety Protection** (Over/under-voltage, over-current, over-temperature, isolation fault)
6. **Communication & Diagnostics** (UDS, OBD-II, CAN, ISO 26262 compliance)

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Banking Locker" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a battery pack as a **large commercial bank vault** containing many individual safety lockers (the cells), each holding a portion of the total "wealth" (energy).
> - The **Bank Manager** = the **BMS**, constantly checking each locker for tampering (voltage faults), overheating (thermal runaway), theft (over-current drain), and uneven deposits (cell imbalance).
> - The **Vault's Central Locking System** = the **Contactors & Pre-charge Circuit**, which safely connects/disconnects the high-voltage bus.
> - The **Air Conditioning** = the **Thermal Management System (TMS)**, keeping lockers at optimal temperature.
> - The **Account Book** = the **State-of-Charge (SOC) Estimator**, tracking how much "wealth" remains in real time.
> - The **Periodic Audit** = the **State-of-Health (SOH) Algorithm**, projecting long-term degradation.

Without the BMS, the cells would drift apart in voltage, overheat catastrophically, and degrade rapidly — just as a vault without a manager would lose money to mismanagement.

## 1.4 Key Automotive Battery Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Standard Unit | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------------|---------|
| Nominal Voltage | $V_{nom}$ | V (Volt) | Operating reference voltage |
| Capacity | $Q$ | Ah (Ampere-hour) | Total charge stored |
| Energy | $E$ | Wh or kWh | $V \times Q$ |
| C-rate | $C$ | $\text{h}^{-1}$ | Charge/discharge current relative to capacity |
| State of Charge | SOC | % (0–100) | Remaining usable energy |
| State of Health | SOH | % (0–100) | Current capacity vs. rated |
| State of Power | SOP | kW | Instantaneous deliverable power |
| Depth of Discharge | DOD | % | Complement of SOC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics in Bold for KTU Recall:**
> - **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ** (mega-joules)
> - **Nominal cell voltage (NMC): 3.6 V – 3.7 V**
> - **Nominal cell voltage (LFP): 3.2 V**
> - **Operating temperature window: -20 °C to +60 °C** (most EV packs)
> - **Standard cycle life target: 1000–2000 cycles** at 80% DOD for automotive Li-ion

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Battery Pack Architecture — Layered Hierarchy

A modern automotive battery pack follows a **four-tier hierarchical structure**:

### Tier 1: Cell (Electrochemical Core)
- The smallest independently packaged unit.
- Available formats: **Cylindrical (18650, 21700, 4680)**, **Pouch**, **Prismatic**.
- Each cell has its own voltage, capacity, and internal resistance $R_0$.

### Tier 2: Module
- A mechanical and electrical sub-assembly of multiple cells.
- Typically **10–16 cells in series** per module.
- Module-level **BMU (Battery Monitoring Unit)** may exist for distributed architectures.

### Tier 3: Pack
- A collection of modules integrated into a structural enclosure.
- Includes the **high-voltage distribution unit (HVDU)**, **contactors**, **fuses**, **pre-charge resistor**, and **BMS master controller (BCU — Battery Control Unit)**.

### Tier 4: System Integration
- Pack is mounted in the vehicle, connected to the **TMS (Thermal Management System)**, **OBC (On-Board Charger)**, **Inverter**, and **VCU (Vehicle Control Unit)** via the **CAN bus**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rationale:** Why a hierarchy? It allows **fault isolation** (a single bad module can be bypassed or replaced), **manufacturing scalability**, and **distributed thermal management** with coolant channels between modules.

## 2.2 Electrical Configuration — Series (S) and Parallel (P)

- **Series connection** ($n_s$ cells): Voltages **add**, capacity stays constant. Used to **build up pack voltage**.
- **Parallel connection** ($n_p$ cells): Capacity **adds**, voltage stays constant. Used to **build up pack energy**.

**Pack-level expressions:**

$$V_{pack} = n_s \cdot V_{cell}$$

$$Q_{pack} = n_p \cdot Q_{cell}$$

$$E_{pack} = V_{pack} \cdot Q_{pack} = n_s \cdot n_p \cdot V_{cell} \cdot Q_{cell}$$

$$P_{pack,\max} = V_{pack} \cdot I_{cont,\max} = n_s \cdot n_p \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I_{cell,\max}$$

## 2.3 Cell Balancing — The "Why" Behind It

In a series string, every cell must stay within a tight voltage window (e.g., **2.5 V – 4.2 V** for NMC). However, due to manufacturing tolerances, temperature gradients, and aging, cells **drift apart in SOC**. If uncorrected:
- The strongest cell limits pack capacity (over-charged first or over-discharged first).
- **Over-charge of a Li-ion cell can cause thermal runaway** (fire/explosion).
- **Over-discharge** causes irreversible copper dissolution and capacity loss.

**Two balancing strategies:**

1. **Passive Balancing** — Excess energy in higher-SOC cells is **dissipated as heat** through a parallel resistor switched by a MOSFET. Simple, cheap, slow, energy-wasting.
2. **Active Balancing** — Energy is **redistributed** from higher-SOC cells to lower-SOC cells via DC-DC converters, capacitors, or inductors. Complex, expensive, fast, energy-efficient.

## 2.4 State Estimation Algorithms

| State | Definition | Typical Algorithm | KTU Relevance |
|-------|------------|-------------------|---------------|
| **SOC** | Remaining capacity / rated capacity | Coulomb Counting + OCV correction; EKF | High — every exam |
| **SOH** | Current max capacity / original capacity | Cycle counting, impedance rise tracking | High |
| **SOP** | Maximum power deliverable in next 2 s | $P_{\max} = V_{min} \cdot (V_{OCV} - V_{min})/R_0$ | Medium |
| **SOE** | Remaining energy | Integral of $V \cdot I$ | Medium |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Battery Packs and BMS**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $E = V \cdot Q$ | Stored electrical energy | Wh |
| 2 | $E_{pack} = n_s \cdot n_p \cdot E_{cell}$ | Pack energy from cell-level | Wh or kWh |
| 3 | $V_{pack} = n_s \cdot V_{cell}$ | Series voltage addition | V |
| 4 | $Q_{pack} = n_p \cdot Q_{cell}$ | Parallel capacity addition | Ah |
| 5 | $C\text{-rate} = I/Q$ | Normalized current | $\text{h}^{-1}$ |
| 6 | $\text{SOC}(t) = \text{SOC}_0 - \dfrac{1}{Q_{rated}} \int_0^t I(\tau) \, d\tau$ | Coulomb counting | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 7 | $P_{\max,disch} = V_{OCV} \cdot \left( V_{OCV} - \sqrt{V_{OCV}^2 - 4 R_0 P_{req}} \right)/(2 R_0)$ | Power capability | W |
| 8 | $R_{int,pack} = n_s \cdot R_0 / n_p$ | Internal resistance scaling | Ω |
| 9 | $T_{cell} = T_{amb} + P_{loss} \cdot R_{th}$ | Steady-state cell temp | °C |
| 10 | $\eta = E_{disch}/E_{ch}$ | Round-trip efficiency | dimensionless |

> **Real-world utility:** Formula 6 (Coulomb counting) is implemented in every commercial BMS IC (e.g., TI BQ76PL536, NXP MC33771). Formula 7 dictates **regenerative-braking limits** — a critical design constraint for EVs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Pack Voltage and Energy from Cell-Level Parameters

**Problem Statement:** A battery pack is built from **$n_s$ cells in series** and **$n_p$ such strings in parallel**. Each cell has nominal voltage $V_{cell}$ and capacity $Q_{cell}$. Derive the expressions for pack voltage, pack capacity, and pack energy.

**Step 1 — Identify the cell-level electrical model.**
A single Li-ion cell is approximated as an ideal voltage source $V_{cell}$ in series with internal resistance $R_0$. The terminal voltage under load is:

$$V_{term} = V_{cell} - I \cdot R_0$$

**Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) along the series string.**
When $n_s$ cells are placed in series, their terminal voltages sum (current $I$ is identical through each series cell). Assuming matched cells:

$$V_{pack} = \sum_{i=1}^{n_s} V_{cell,i} = n_s \cdot V_{cell}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the parallel node.**
When $n_p$ strings are connected in parallel, the total current delivered to the load is the sum of branch currents. Each branch can deliver $I_{cell,\max}$, so:

$$I_{pack,\max} = n_p \cdot I_{cell,\max}$$

The pack **capacity** (in Ah) — defined as current × discharge time — is preserved at the cell level and multiplies by the number of parallel strings:

$$Q_{pack} = n_p \cdot Q_{cell}$$

**Step 4 — Derive pack energy.**
Energy = Voltage × Capacity:

$$E_{pack} = V_{pack} \cdot Q_{pack} = (n_s \cdot V_{cell}) \cdot (n_p \cdot Q_{cell}) = n_s \cdot n_p \cdot V_{cell} \cdot Q_{cell}$$

Since $E_{cell} = V_{cell} \cdot Q_{cell}$, we can write:

$$E_{pack} = n_s \cdot n_p \cdot E_{cell}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to SI base unit (Joules) using $1 \text{ Wh} = 3600 \text{ J}$:**

$$E_{pack} \, [\text{J}] = 3600 \cdot n_s \cdot n_p \cdot V_{cell} \cdot Q_{cell}$$

> **Insight for KTU:** If asked for a **400 V, 50 kWh** pack using NMC cells ($V_{cell} = 3.6$ V, $Q_{cell} = 50$ Ah = 0.18 MJ = 0.18 kWh = 0.05 kWh ... 0.18 kWh):
> $n_s = 400/3.6 \approx 111$ cells, $E_{cell} = 3.6 \times 50 = 180$ Wh = 0.18 kWh
> $n_s \cdot n_p \cdot 0.18 = 50 \Rightarrow n_p = 50/(111 \times 0.18) \approx 2.5$
> Real systems use $n_s = 96$ ($V_{pack} = 345$ V) or $n_s = 108$ ($V_{pack} = 388$ V) with higher $n_p$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Coulomb-Counting SOC Estimation

**Problem Statement:** Derive the discrete-time SOC update equation used in real BMS firmware from the continuous integral form.

**Step 1 — Start with the continuous definition of SOC:**

$$\text{SOC}(t) = \text{SOC}(t_0) - \frac{1}{Q_{rated}} \int_{t_0}^{t} I_{bat}(\tau) \, d\tau$$

Here $I_{bat}$ is **positive during discharge** and **negative during charge** by convention (or vice-versa — KTU students must declare sign convention explicitly).

**Step 2 — Discretize the integral using the trapezoidal rule for BMS digital sampling.**
With sampling period $\Delta t = t_{k+1} - t_k$:

$$\int_{t_k}^{t_{k+1}} I_{bat}(\tau) \, d\tau \approx \frac{\Delta t}{2} \left( I_{bat,k} + I_{bat,k+1} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the SOC recursion:**

$$\text{SOC}_{k+1} = \text{SOC}_k - \frac{\Delta t}{2 Q_{rated}} \left( I_{bat,k} + I_{bat,k+1} \right)$$

**Step 4 — For real-time implementation, the simple forward-Euler approximation is used (lower computational load):**

$$\text{SOC}_{k+1} \approx \text{SOC}_k - \frac{\Delta t}{Q_{rated}} I_{bat,k}$$

**Step 5 — Include Coulombic efficiency $\eta_c$ (charging is less than 100% efficient):**

$$\text{SOC}_{k+1} = \text{SOC}_k - \frac{\eta_c \cdot \Delta t}{Q_{rated}} I_{bat,k}$$

where $\eta_c \approx 0.99$ for Li-ion during charge, and $\eta_c = 1$ during discharge.

> **Engineering insight:** This simple integrator **drifts** because of sensor offset, quantization, and $Q_{rated}$ change with aging. KTU expects you to mention that the **OCV (Open-Circuit Voltage) correction** at rest is mandatory: after a long rest (≥1 hr), $\text{SOC} = f(\text{OCV})$ from the cell's OCV-SOC curve (Nernst equation-based).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Cell Balancing Time (Passive)

**Problem Statement:** A passive balancing circuit dissipates excess charge from a single over-charged cell through a $R_b = 50 \, \Omega$ resistor activated by a MOSFET. The cell is at 4.20 V (over-charged) and must be brought to 4.15 V. Cell capacity = 5 Ah. Estimate the balancing time.

**Step 1 — Compute the current through the balancing resistor:**

$$I_b = \frac{V_{cell} - V_{target}}{R_b} = \frac{4.20 - 4.15}{50} = \frac{0.05}{50} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{A} = 1 \, \text{mA}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the excess charge to be removed.**
From the cell's dQ/dV curve near full charge, a 50 mV step typically corresponds to ~1% SOC. For a 5 Ah cell:

$$\Delta Q \approx 0.01 \times 5 \, \text{Ah} = 0.05 \, \text{Ah} = 0.05 \times 3600 \, \text{C} = 180 \, \text{C}$$

**Step 3 — Compute balancing time:**

$$t_{balance} = \frac{\Delta Q}{I_b} = \frac{180}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = 180{,}000 \, \text{s} = 50 \, \text{hours}$$

> **Insight:** This is why **passive balancing is slow** — it is unsuitable for fast-charging EVs (30 min) but is used in overnight AC charging where balancing completes in parallel. **Active balancing** using 1–2 A inductor/capacitor shuttles can complete in 30–60 minutes.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — BMS Function vs. Failure Mode

| BMS Function | Sensor/Actuator Used | Failure Mode if Disabled | KTU Mark Allocation |
|--------------|----------------------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| Voltage monitoring | ADC + cell-tap wires | Cell over-charge → fire | High |
| Current sensing | Hall-effect / shunt | Inability to compute SOC; over-current damage | High |
| Temperature sensing | NTC thermistor (10 kΩ) | Thermal runaway undetected | High |
| Contactor control | HV relay + pre-charge | In-rush current → arc welding of contacts | Medium |
| Isolation monitoring | Bridge impedance measurement | Galvanic shock hazard to occupants | Medium |
| Balancing | MOSFET + resistor (passive) or DC-DC (active) | Pack capacity derating, premature aging | High |
| CAN communication | CAN transceiver (e.g., TJA1051) | Vehicle cannot read pack status | Medium |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Q1]
**Define Battery Management System (BMS). List any four critical functions of BMS in modern electric vehicles.**

**Model Answer:**
A Battery Management System (BMS) is an embedded electronic control system that monitors, controls, and protects a rechargeable battery pack by continuously acquiring cell-level voltage, current, and temperature data and executing closed-loop algorithms to ensure safe, efficient, and reliable operation.

**Four critical functions of BMS:**
1. **Cell Monitoring** — Real-time measurement of individual cell voltage, pack current, and temperature using ADCs and NTC thermistors.
2. **State Estimation** — Real-time computation of SOC, SOH, SOP using Coulomb counting, OCV correction, and Kalman filters.
3. **Cell Balancing** — Equalization of cell SOC through passive (resistive dissipation) or active (energy redistribution) methods.
4. **Safety Protection** — Disconnection of contactors on detection of over-voltage, under-voltage, over-current, over-temperature, or isolation faults.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for four functions with one-line explanation each.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Q3]
**Explain the difference between passive and active cell balancing with one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Passive Balancing | Active Balancing |
|--------|------------------|------------------|
| **Working principle** | Excess energy in higher-SOC cell is **dissipated as heat** through a parallel resistor | Excess energy is **redistributed** from higher-SOC to lower-SOC cell via DC-DC converter |
| **Energy efficiency** | Wastes energy as heat | Energy-efficient (>85%) |
| **Cost & complexity** | Low cost, simple MOSFET + resistor | High cost, complex magnetics/capacitors |
| **Speed** | Slow (mA-level bleed current) | Fast (1–5 A transfer current) |

**Advantage (passive):** Simple, low-cost, reliable — preferred for low-power packs and AC slow charging.
**Advantage (active):** Faster balancing — essential for DC fast charging where dwell time is short.

*(2 marks for tabular comparison + 1 mark for advantage of each.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** When explaining SOC estimation, **never write only "Coulomb counting"** — the examiner expects the integral form, discretization, and a mention of **drift correction via OCV**. A 7-mark answer without OCV correction is capped at **4 marks**.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the hierarchical architecture of an electric vehicle battery pack, starting from the cell level up to system integration. Discuss the role of the BMS master controller (BCU) and module-level BMUs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An EV battery pack is built using NMC cells of nominal voltage **3.6 V** and capacity **50 Ah**. The pack configuration is **96S2P** (96 in series, 2 in parallel). Compute:
   (i) Pack nominal voltage
   (ii) Pack capacity in Ah
   (iii) Pack energy in kWh
   (iv) Pack internal resistance, given each cell has $R_0 = 25 \, \text{m}\Omega$
   (v) If the vehicle consumes an average of **15 kWh / 100 km**, what is the theoretical range? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Hierarchical Architecture (7 marks):**

The EV battery pack is organized in a four-tier hierarchy:

1. **Cell Level:** The smallest independently packaged electrochemical unit. Cylindrical (18650/21700/4680), pouch, or prismatic formats. Each cell has a nominal voltage (3.6 V for NMC), a capacity (e.g., 50 Ah), and an internal resistance $R_0$.

2. **Module Level:** A mechanical and electrical sub-assembly of typically **10–16 cells in series** mounted on a cold plate with module-level interconnects. Each module contains a **BMU (Battery Monitoring Unit)** — an analog-front-end IC (e.g., NXP MC33771, TI BQ76PL536) that measures cell voltages and temperatures and reports them via **SPI daisy chain** to the master.

3. **Pack Level:** Modules are bolted into a structural enclosure and connected in series. The pack contains:
   - The **BCU (Battery Control Unit)** — the BMS master with a microcontroller running the SOC/SOH/SOP algorithms.
   - The **HVDU (High-Voltage Distribution Unit)** — main contactors, pre-charge circuit, HV fuse, current shunt.
   - The **isolation monitor** (per IEC 60664 / ISO 6469-3).
   - The **TMS (Thermal Management System)** interface.

4. **System Integration:** The pack interfaces with the **VCU** (Vehicle Control Unit), **Inverter**, **OBC (On-Board Charger)**, and external **DC fast charger** via the **CAN bus (500 kbps)** following ISO 11898 and the **UDS (Unified Diagnostic Services)** protocol stack over CAN for fault diagnostics (per ISO 14229).

**Role of BCU:** Central supervisory controller — runs state estimation, executes protection logic, drives contactors, commands balancing, communicates with VCU, and stores Diagnostic Trouble Codes (DTCs).

**Role of Module BMUs:** Distributed slave monitoring — digitize analog cell voltages, perform local diagnostics, and relay data to the BCU. This architecture reduces wiring harness complexity by **96%** compared to central monitoring (only one SPI/isoSPI daisy chain replaces hundreds of analog wires).

*(Distribution: 1 mark each for four tiers, 1.5 marks for BCU role, 1.5 marks for BMU role — total 7.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V_{cell} = 3.6$ V, $Q_{cell} = 50$ Ah, $n_s = 96$, $n_p = 2$, $R_0 = 25$ mΩ = 0.025 Ω.

**(i) Pack nominal voltage:**

$$V_{pack} = n_s \cdot V_{cell} = 96 \times 3.6$$

$$\boxed{V_{pack} = 345.6 \, \text{V}}$$

**(ii) Pack capacity in Ah:**

$$Q_{pack} = n_p \cdot Q_{cell} = 2 \times 50$$

$$\boxed{Q_{pack} = 100 \, \text{Ah}}$$

**(iii) Pack energy in kWh:**

$$E_{pack} = V_{pack} \cdot Q_{pack} = 345.6 \times 100 = 34{,}560 \, \text{Wh}$$

$$\boxed{E_{pack} = 34.56 \, \text{kWh}}$$

**(iv) Pack internal resistance:**

For the $n_s$ series string of one parallel group, resistances **add** in series. Two such strings are in parallel, so resistances **halve**:

$$R_{pack} = \frac{n_s \cdot R_0}{n_p} = \frac{96 \times 0.025}{2} = \frac{2.4}{2}$$

$$\boxed{R_{pack} = 1.2 \, \Omega}$$

**(v) Theoretical range:**

$$\text{Range} = \frac{E_{pack}}{\text{Consumption rate}} = \frac{34.56 \, \text{kWh}}{15 \, \text{kWh}/100 \, \text{km}}$$

$$\text{Range} = \frac{34.56}{15} \times 100 = 2.304 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\text{Range} = 230.4 \, \text{km}}$$

> **Engineering reality check:** Real-world range is 70–80% of theoretical due to auxiliary loads (HVAC, TMS pump, electronics), drive cycle aggressiveness, and depth-of discharge limits. Hence **practical range ≈ 160–185 km**.

*(Mark distribution: (i) 1 mark, (ii) 1 mark, (iii) 1.5 marks, (iv) 2 marks, (v) 1.5 marks.)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** What is State of Charge (SOC)? Derive the Coulomb-counting equation for SOC estimation in discrete time and explain why OCV correction is necessary. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the major functional blocks of a BMS — measurement inputs, estimation algorithms, protection logic, and actuator outputs. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) SOC Definition and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Definition:** State of Charge (SOC) is the ratio of the **remaining capacity** in a battery to its **rated (nominal) capacity**, expressed as a percentage:

$$\text{SOC} \, [\%] = \frac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100$$

A fully charged cell is at **100% SOC**; a fully discharged cell is at **0% SOC**.

**Continuous-time Coulomb counting:**

$$\text{SOC}(t) = \text{SOC}(t_0) - \frac{1}{Q_{rated}} \int_{t_0}^{t} I_{bat}(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Discrete-time derivation (Forward Euler):** Discretize the integral over a single sampling interval $\Delta t = t_{k+1} - t_k$ assuming $I_{bat}$ is approximately constant:

$$\int_{t_k}^{t_{k+1}} I_{bat}(\tau) \, d\tau \approx I_{bat,k} \cdot \Delta t$$

Substituting into the continuous form:

$$\text{SOC}_{k+1} = \text{SOC}_k - \frac{I_{bat,k} \cdot \Delta t}{Q_{rated}}$$

Including Coulombic efficiency $\eta_c$:

$$\text{SOC}_{k+1} = \text{SOC}_k - \frac{\eta_c \cdot I_{bat,k} \cdot \Delta t}{Q_{rated}}$$

**Why OCV correction is necessary:**
Coulomb counting **accumulates error** due to (i) current sensor offset (typically ±0.1%), (ii) quantization in ADC, (iii) $Q_{rated}$ change with aging, and (iv) self-discharge. After sufficient time, the integrated error becomes large.

**OCV correction:** When the pack has been at rest for a long time (≥1 hour) and the cell voltage is stable, the **Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV)** is read and mapped to SOC through a pre-characterized OCV-SOC curve (Nernst-based, monotonic for Li-ion). The SOC estimate is **reset** to this OCV-derived value, eliminating drift.

*In production BMS, an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) combines both methods — Coulomb counting is the predictor, OCV is the measurement update — yielding drift-free, accurate SOC estimates within ±2%.*

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark definition, 3 marks derivation, 1.5 marks OCV need, 1.5 marks OCV method — total 7.)*

---

**(b) BMS Functional Block Diagram (7 marks):**

A BMS consists of four major functional layers:

1. **Measurement Input Layer (Sensors):**
   - **Voltage sensors** (analog multiplexer + 16-bit ADC) on every cell tap, one per cell, sampling at 10–100 Hz.
   - **Current sensor** — Hall-effect IC (e.g., LEM LA-100P) for galvanic isolation, or precision shunt (e.g., 500 A / 75 mV) for high accuracy.
   - **Temperature sensors** — NTC thermistors (10 kΩ at 25°C, B = 3950) bonded to cell surfaces, with at least 2 per module.
   - **Isolation monitor** — measures impedance between HV bus and chassis per IEC 61557-8.

2. **Estimation Algorithm Layer (Software):**
   - **Coulomb counter** for SOC integration.
   - **OCV-SOC lookup table** for SOC reset.
   - **EKF / UKF observer** for sensor-fused SOC.
   - **Capacity-fade tracker** for SOH.
   - **Power-prediction model** for SOP using the equation:
     $$P_{max} = \frac{V_{OCV} (V_{OCV} - \sqrt{V_{OCV}^2 - 4 R_0 P_{req}})}{2 R_0}$$

3. **Protection Logic Layer:**
   - Threshold comparators for **OV** (4.25 V/cell), **UV** (2.5 V/cell), **OC** (e.g., 1.5C continuous, 3C peak), **OT** (60°C cell), **UT** (-20°C cell), and **isolation fault** (<100 Ω/V per ISO 6469-3).
   - Latching logic to ensure contactors open and remain open until fault is cleared by diagnostic service.

4. **Actuator Output Layer:**
   - **Contactor drivers** — gate-drive circuits for HV relays (e.g., 400 A Gigavac GX14).
   - **Pre-charge control** — closes pre-charge contactor first to limit in-rush current to <50 A.
   - **Balancing switches** — MOSFET drivers for passive resistors or gate signals for active DC-DC.
   - **TMS interface** — PWM outputs to coolant pump and chiller compressor.
   - **CAN driver** — TJA1051 or similar, broadcasting SOC, SOH, voltage, current, temperature at 100 ms interval; responding to UDS requests for DTCs.

*(Mark distribution: 1.5 marks each for the four layers with sub-bullets — total 7.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rapid Revision Checklist — Battery Packs and BMS**

- **Pack Architecture:** Cell → Module → Pack → System. **4 tiers**, hierarchical.
- **Series-Parallel Rule:** $V_{pack} = n_s V_{cell}$; $Q_{pack} = n_p Q_{cell}$; **$E_{pack} = n_s n_p V_{cell} Q_{cell}$**.
- **Internal Resistance Scaling:** $R_{pack} = n_s R_0 / n_p$ (series adds, parallel averages).
- **C-rate definition:** $C = I / Q_{rated}$. A 50 Ah cell at 1C = 50 A. At 2C = 100 A.
- **Nominal Voltages to memorize:** NMC = 3.6–3.7 V, LFP = 3.2 V, NCA = 3.6 V.
- **SOC = remaining / rated × 100%**. **Coulomb counting** integrates $I \, dt$; drifts, needs **OCV correction** at rest.
- **OCV-SOC curve** is monotonic for Li-ion — unique lookup table per chemistry.
- **EKF** is the industry-standard sensor fusion for SOC, SOP, SOH.
- **Cell Balancing:** Two types — **Passive** (heat dissipation via resistor, slow, cheap) and **Active** (DC-DC energy transfer, fast, efficient, costly).
- **BMS Functions (6):** Monitor, Estimate, Balance, Protect, Thermal-manage, Communicate.
- **Protection Thresholds (NMC):** OV = 4.25 V, UV = 2.5 V, OT = 60°C, UT = -20°C.
- **Communication:** **CAN (500 kbps)** is industry standard; **UDS (ISO 14229)** for diagnostics; **ISO 26262** for functional safety; **ISO 6469-3** for EV-specific safety.
- **Active balancing time** (1 A shuttling) ≈ 30–60 min; **passive** (1 mA bleed) ≈ 50 hours for 1% imbalance.
- **Energy conversions to memorize:** $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$, $1 \text{ Ah} = 3600 \text{ C}$.
- **Theoretical range** = $E_{pack} / \text{consumption rate}$; **practical** ≈ 70–80% of theoretical.
- **Distributed BMS** (module BMUs + central BCU) reduces wiring harness by **~96%** vs. centralized.
- **Pre-charge circuit** is mandatory — limits in-rush current to <50 A to prevent contactor welding.
- **Current sensing** uses either Hall-effect (galvanic isolation, no contact) or shunt resistor (more accurate, ground-referenced).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Automotive Motor?

In the context of **Modern Automotive Technologies**, a **Motor** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that transforms **electrical energy into mechanical energy** (rotational motion) based on the principles of electromagnetic force generation. In modern vehicles, motors serve two critical functions:

1. **Auxiliary Motors** – Used in conventional ICE vehicles for non-propulsion tasks (e.g., starter motor, wiper motor, window motor, fuel pump, HVAC blower).
2. **Traction Motors** – Used in **Electric Vehicles (EVs)** and **Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs)** to deliver propulsion torque directly to the drivetrain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A traction motor is a specialized electric machine designed to deliver high starting torque, wide constant-power speed range, high efficiency across the drive cycle, robust overload capability, and regenerative braking compatibility.

The fundamental governing physics is given by **Lorentz Force Law**:

$$\vec{F} = q(\vec{E} + \vec{v} \times \vec{B})$$

For a current-carrying conductor of length $L$ carrying current $I$ in a magnetic field $B$:

$$\vec{F} = B I L \sin(\theta)$$

Where $\theta$ is the angle between current direction and magnetic field. This force, acting at a radius $r$ from the motor shaft, produces **torque** $T$ — the foundational mechanism of all electric motors.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Spinning Water Wheel"**

Imagine a **water wheel** in a flowing river:
- The **flowing water** is analogous to **electric current**.
- The **gravity & slope** that pull the wheel down is analogous to the **magnetic field** pushing current-carrying conductors.
- The **wheel itself** is the **rotor**, and the **axle** is the **shaft** delivering useful mechanical work.

When you increase the **water flow** (current) or the **steepness of the slope** (magnetic field strength), the wheel spins faster and pushes harder. The mechanical power delivered is conceptually equivalent to **electrical input power × efficiency**.

For a **vehicle**:
- The motor's "river" is the **high-voltage battery pack** (typically 400V–800V in modern EVs).
- The motor's "wheel" is the **rotor** connected to the drivetrain.
- The "useful work" is **wheel torque** that pushes the car forward.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Automotive Motors (Syllabus Snapshot)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification of Motors used in Modern Automobiles:**

| **Category** | **Motor Type** | **Typical Application** |
|--------------|----------------|--------------------------|
| Conventional ICE Auxiliaries | DC Series Motor | Starter Motor (cranking ICE) |
| Conventional ICE Auxiliaries | Permanent Magnet DC (PMDC) | Wipers, Windows, Fans |
| EV / HEV Traction | Brushless DC (BLDC) | 2-Wheelers, Compact EVs |
| EV / HEV Traction | Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM) | Tesla Model 3, Toyota Prius |
| EV / HEV Traction | Three-Phase Induction Motor (IM) | Tesla Model S/X (early), Audi e-tron |
| EV / HEV Traction | Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM) | Renault Fluence Z.E., Off-highway EVs |
| EV / HEV Traction | Synchronous Reluctance Motor (SynRM) | Premium EV platforms |

---

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bold Highlight)

- **Rated Power ($P$):** Expressed in **kW** (typically 50–400 kW for passenger EVs).
- **Peak Torque ($T_{peak}$):** Delivered at zero/low speed; up to **600 Nm** in mid-segment EVs.
- **Base Speed ($\omega_b$):** The speed below which the motor delivers **constant torque**.
- **Maximum Speed ($\omega_{max}$):** Up to **15,000–20,000 RPM** (some reach 25,000 RPM).
- **Specific Power (Power Density):** Expressed in **kW/kg** (modern traction motors: 3–6 kW/kg).
- **Efficiency ($\eta$):** Typically **90–97%** over a wide operating range.
- **Constant Power Speed Ratio (CPSR):** Ratio $\omega_{max}/\omega_b$ (target: $\geq 3$).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Universal Motor Law

Every electric motor — irrespective of type — obeys three fundamental equations derived from electromagnetic field theory:

### 2.1.1 EMF Equation (Faraday's Law)

When a rotor rotates at angular velocity $\omega$ in a magnetic flux $\Phi$, the induced back-EMF is:

$$E_b = k_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega$$

Where:
- $k_e$ = EMF constant (machine construction parameter)
- $\Phi$ = flux per pole (in Weber, Wb)
- $\omega$ = angular speed in rad/s

### 2.1.2 Torque Equation (Lorentz Force × Radius)

$$T = k_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$$

Where:
- $k_t$ = torque constant
- $I_a$ = armature current
- $T$ = electromagnetic torque (N·m)

### 2.1.3 Mechanical Power Output

$$P_{out} = T \cdot \omega$$

---

## 2.2 Operating Modes of a Traction Motor

A modern automotive traction motor must operate in **four quadrants** of the torque-speed plane:

| **Quadrant** | **Torque** | **Speed** | **Mode** | **Vehicle State** |
|--------------|------------|-----------|----------|-------------------|
| I (Forward Motoring) | + | + | Propulsion | Accelerating forward |
| II (Forward Braking) | – | + | Regenerative Braking | Decelerating forward |
| III (Reverse Motoring) | – | – | Reverse Drive | Accelerating backward |
| IV (Reverse Braking) | + | – | Regen Braking (Reverse) | Decelerating backward |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** True EV traction motors must deliver **full four-quadrant operation** with **smooth transition** between motoring and generating modes. This is why power electronics (inverters) are inseparable from the motor system.

---

## 2.3 Characteristic Curve — Constant Torque & Constant Power Regions

A traction motor exhibits **two distinct operating zones**:

1. **Constant Torque Region** ($\omega \leq \omega_b$):  
   The motor delivers maximum torque up to base speed. Achieved by maintaining constant flux and varying current.
   $$T = T_{max} = \text{const.}$$

2. **Constant Power Region** ($\omega_b < \omega \leq \omega_{max}$):  
   Power remains constant; torque decreases inversely with speed. Achieved through **field weakening** (reducing flux in PMSM) or **slip control** (in induction motors).
   $$P = T \cdot \omega = \text{const.} \implies T \propto \frac{1}{\omega}$$

3. **Natural Characteristic (Series Motor):**
   $$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \left(\frac{I_{a1}}{I_{a2}}\right)^2$$
   Torque is proportional to the **square** of armature current — ideal for starter motors.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **ready-revision** summary. All formulas are mathematically rigorous and aligned with KTU Board examination expectations.

| **S.No.** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|-----------|-------------|-----------------|-----------|
| 1 | $E_b = k_e \Phi \omega$ | Back-EMF | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $T = k_t \Phi I_a$ | Electromagnetic Torque | N·m |
| 3 | $P_{out} = T \omega$ | Mechanical Output Power | W |
| 4 | $P_{in} = V I_a$ | Electrical Input Power | W |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$ | Efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $n_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous Speed | RPM |
| 7 | $s = \dfrac{n_s - n_r}{n_s}$ | Slip (Induction Motor) | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $T \propto \dfrac{s \cdot E_2^2 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}$ | Induction Motor Torque (Thevenin) | N·m |
| 9 | $P_{developed} = T \cdot \omega_s$ | Air-Gap Power | W |
| 10 | $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{\left(\dfrac{R_r}{s}\right)^2 + X^2} \cdot \dfrac{R_r}{s}$ | Induction Motor Torque (Detailed) | N·m |
| 11 | $CPSR = \dfrac{\omega_{max}}{\omega_{base}}$ | Constant Power Speed Ratio | Dimensionless |
| 12 | $s_{max\,T} = \dfrac{R_r}{X}$ | Slip at Maximum Torque | Dimensionless |
| 13 | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 V^2}{2 \omega_s X}$ | Maximum Torque (Induction Motor) | N·m |
| 14 | $\omega_{mech} = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | Convert RPM to rad/s | rad/s |
| 15 | $E = \dfrac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot N \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A}$ | General EMF Equation | Volts |

**Where:**  
$V$ = phase voltage, $f$ = supply frequency, $P$ = number of poles, $n_s$ = synchronous speed, $n_r$ = rotor speed, $s$ = slip, $R_r$ = rotor resistance, $X$ = standstill rotor reactance, $\omega_s$ = synchronous angular speed, $N$ = RPM, $Z$ = total conductors, $A$ = parallel paths.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **BLDC Motors** in **2-wheeler EVs** (e.g., Ola S1, Ather 450X) deliver 85–95% efficiency with excellent torque density.
- **PMSM** in **Tesla Model 3** offers superior specific power (~5 kW/kg) and is paired with silicon-carbide (SiC) inverters.
- **Induction Motors** in **Tesla Model S** (legacy) offer ruggedness, no rare-earth dependency, and high reliability.
- **Switched Reluctance Motors** in **heavy EVs and mining equipment** offer fault tolerance and extremely high-temperature operation.

---

## 2.6 Motor Selection Criteria for EV Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six Pillars of Traction Motor Selection (KTU High-Yield):**

1. **High Power Density (kW/kg)** — for compact packaging.
2. **Wide Constant-Power Speed Range (CPSR $\geq 3$)** — to avoid multi-gear transmissions.
3. **High Efficiency across Drive Cycle** — minimizes battery drain (WLTP/ARAI cycle).
4. **Robustness & Reliability** — survives vibration, temperature extremes (–40°C to +150°C).
5. **Regenerative Braking Capability** — must function as a generator seamlessly.
6. **Cost & Rare-Earth Availability** — PMSM uses NdFeB magnets; strategic supply concerns.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Power, Torque, and Speed Relationship

### Objective:
Establish the fundamental relationship between mechanical output power, torque, and angular speed of a motor.

### Given:
- A motor delivers torque $T$ at angular speed $\omega$ (rad/s).

### Derivation:

**Step 1:** Consider the work done $W$ by a constant tangential force $F$ acting at a radius $r$ as the shaft rotates through angle $\theta$.

$$W = F \cdot s = F \cdot (r\theta)$$

**Step 2:** By definition, torque is the product of force and moment arm.

$$T = F \cdot r \implies F = \frac{T}{r}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the expression for force into the work equation.

$$W = \frac{T}{r} \cdot r \cdot \theta = T \cdot \theta$$

**Step 4:** Power is the rate of doing work. For a constant angular velocity $\omega = d\theta/dt$:

$$P = \frac{dW}{dt} = T \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = T \cdot \omega$$

**Final Result:**

$$P_{out} = T \cdot \omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This equation reveals a **trade-off**. At low speed (start-up), to deliver high power, the motor must produce high torque. At cruising speed, torque can be reduced while power remains useful.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: EMF Equation of a DC Machine

### Objective:
Derive the generalized EMF equation for a DC machine (applies to both motors and generators).

### Given:
- $P$ = number of poles  
- $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb)  
- $N$ = speed of armature (RPM)  
- $Z$ = total number of armature conductors  
- $A$ = number of parallel paths

### Derivation:

**Step 1:** Flux cut by one conductor in one revolution = $\Phi \cdot P$ (Webers).

**Step 2:** Number of revolutions per second = $N / 60$.

**Step 3:** Average EMF induced per conductor (Faraday's Law):

$$e_{avg} = \frac{\Phi P N}{60} \text{ Volts}$$

**Step 4:** Total number of conductors connected in series in one parallel path = $Z / A$.

**Step 5:** The total EMF of the armature is the EMF per conductor times the number of conductors in series:

$$E_b = e_{avg} \cdot \frac{Z}{A}$$

**Final Result:**

$$E_b = \frac{\Phi \cdot P \cdot N \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always substitute values with **SI units**. Flux in Wb, speed in RPM (then convert if needed).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Synchronous Speed and Slip Relationship

### Objective:
Establish the relationship between supply frequency, number of poles, and rotor speed in an induction motor.

### Derivation:

**Step 1:** In a 2-pole machine, the rotating magnetic field completes **1 revolution per cycle** of the AC supply.  
**Step 2:** In a $P$-pole machine, the magnetic field rotates at:

$$n_s = \frac{120 f}{P} \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 3:** The rotor of an induction motor always rotates **below** synchronous speed. The fractional difference is called **slip** $s$:

$$s = \frac{n_s - n_r}{n_s}$$

**Step 4:** The actual rotor speed is therefore:

$$n_r = n_s (1 - s) = \frac{120 f}{P}(1 - s)$$

**Step 5:** At starting, $n_r = 0$, so $s = 1$. At synchronous speed (theoretical), $s = 0$. Normal running slip is 2–5% for industrial induction motors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical:** Without slip, **no rotor current flows, no torque is produced**, and the motor cannot start or sustain rotation. Slip is the *life-blood* of induction motor operation.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Maximum Torque Condition of Induction Motor

### Objective:
Determine the slip at which the induction motor produces maximum torque.

### Starting Equation:
The torque of an induction motor (per phase) is given by:

$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V^2 \cdot (R_r / s)}{(R_r / s)^2 + X^2}$$

**Step 1:** To find maximum torque, differentiate $T$ with respect to slip $s$ and set $dT/ds = 0$.

$$\frac{dT}{ds} = \frac{3 V^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{(R_r / s)^2 + X^2 - (R_r / s) \cdot 2 (R_r / s) \cdot (-1/s)}{\left[(R_r / s)^2 + X^2\right]^2}$$

**Step 2:** Setting the numerator to zero:

$$\left(\frac{R_r}{s}\right)^2 + X^2 + \frac{2 R_r^2}{s^2} = 0 \quad \text{(after simplification)}$$

**Step 3:** Solving for $s$:

$$s_{max\,T} = \frac{R_r}{X}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting back to find maximum torque:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 V^2}{2 \omega_s X}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** $s_{max\,T}$ is **independent of supply voltage**. This means maximum torque is achieved at the same slip regardless of voltage — but the magnitude of $T_{max}$ varies with $V^2$. This is why induction motors can briefly tolerate large voltage sags without losing peak torque capability.
