# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

> **Explain the working principle of a Brushless DC (BLDC) motor used in electric two-wheelers. List any two advantages over brushed DC motors.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A BLDC motor replaces the mechanical commutator and brushes of a conventional DC motor with **electronic commutation** using a 3-phase inverter and rotor position sensors (Hall sensors or encoders).

The stator carries **three-phase windings**, and the rotor carries **permanent magnets**. Based on rotor position feedback, the inverter sequentially energizes stator phases. The interaction between the stator's rotating magnetic field and the rotor's permanent magnets produces continuous torque.

**Two Advantages over Brushed DC Motors:**
1. **Higher efficiency (90–95%)** due to absence of brush friction and voltage drop.
2. **Lower maintenance** — no brush wear, no commutator servicing, longer operational life.
3. *(Bonus point)*: Better heat dissipation from the stator, enabling higher current loading and power density.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marks Distribution:** Working principle (2 marks) + Advantages (1 mark).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Define the term "Constant Power Speed Ratio" (CPSR) for a traction motor. Why is a high CPSR desirable in EV applications?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition:**  
CPSR is the ratio of maximum motor speed to base speed:

$$CPSR = \frac{\omega_{max}}{\omega_{base}}$$

- $\omega_{base}$ is the speed at which the motor transitions from constant-torque to constant-power mode.
- $\omega_{max}$ is the maximum permissible motor speed.

**Why High CPSR is Desirable:**
1. It enables the motor to deliver useful power at both low and high speeds without requiring a multi-speed transmission.
2. A CPSR $\geq 3$ allows a **single-speed gearbox**, reducing mechanical complexity, weight, and cost.
3. It improves highway cruising efficiency by operating the motor in its high-speed, low-torque sweet spot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marks Distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + Mathematical expression (1 mark) + Significance (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a modern Electric Vehicle powertrain. Identify the role of the traction motor, inverter, and VCU. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the EMF equation of a DC machine. A 4-pole DC motor has 600 armature conductors, flux per pole of 0.05 Wb, runs at 1500 RPM, with 2 parallel paths. Calculate the back-EMF. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

### **Part (a) — EV Powertrain Architecture (7 Marks):**

**Block Diagram (textual representation):**

```
[Grid AC] → [Onboard Charger] → [Battery Pack]
                                      ↓
                              [DC-DC Converter] → [12V Aux Battery]
                                      ↓
                            [Traction Inverter (3-φ VSI)]
                                      ↑
                            [Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)]
                                      ↓
                                [Traction Motor]
                                      ↓
                            [Reduction Gearbox + Differential]
                                      ↓
                                   [Wheels]
```

**Roles of Key Components:**

| **Component** | **Role** |
|---------------|----------|
| **Traction Motor** | Converts electrical energy from battery to mechanical torque. Provides propulsion and regenerative braking. |
| **Inverter (3-Phase VSI)** | Converts DC battery voltage to variable-frequency 3-phase AC for motor. Performs vector control (FOC) of motor torque and flux. |
| **VCU (Vehicle Control Unit)** | Master controller. Receives accelerator pedal, brake, and mode inputs. Computes torque request and dispatches commands to inverter, BMS, and brake system. |
| **Battery Pack** | Energy storage; delivers high DC current during motoring, accepts charging current during regenerative braking. |
| **Reduction Gearbox** | Single-speed gear reduction (~9:1) to match motor's high-speed, low-torque output to wheel requirements. |

**Explanation:**  
The driver presses the accelerator pedal; the VCU interprets pedal position and battery SOC, computes the torque request, and sends a torque command to the inverter. The inverter uses **Field-Oriented Control (FOC)** to regulate stator currents, producing a rotating magnetic field that drives the rotor. The motor's output torque is transmitted via a single-speed reduction gearbox to the differential, which distributes power to the wheels.

> **Marks Split:** Diagram (2) + Role of Motor (2) + Role of Inverter & VCU (2) + Working (1) = 7 Marks

---

### **Part (b) — EMF Derivation and Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Derivation (4 Marks):**

The general EMF equation of a DC machine is:

$$E_b = \frac{\Phi \cdot P \cdot N \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A}$$

**Detailed Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Flux cut by one conductor in one revolution = $\Phi P$ Webers.  
**Step 2:** Revolutions per second = $N/60$.  
**Step 3:** Average EMF per conductor = $\dfrac{\Phi P N}{60}$ Volts.  
**Step 4:** Number of conductors in series per parallel path = $Z/A$.  
**Step 5:** Total armature EMF:

$$E_b = \frac{Z}{A} \cdot \frac{\Phi P N}{60} = \frac{\Phi P N Z}{60 A}$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $P = 4$ poles
- $Z = 600$ conductors
- $\Phi = 0.05$ Wb
- $N = 1500$ RPM
- $A = 2$ (wave-wound DC machine)

**Substitution:**

$$E_b = \frac{0.05 \times 4 \times 1500 \times 600}{60 \times 2}$$

**Step-by-step calculation:**

$$E_b = \frac{0.05 \times 4 \times 1500 \times 600}{120}$$

**Compute numerator:**

$$0.05 \times 4 = 0.2$$

$$0.2 \times 1500 = 300$$

$$300 \times 600 = 180{,}000$$

**Final Result:**

$$E_b = \frac{180{,}000}{120} = 1500 \text{ V}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The back-EMF of the motor is **$E_b = 1500$ V**.

> **Marks Split:** Derivation (4) + Numerical setup (1) + Calculation (2) = 7 Marks

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1 Internal Choice]`

> **(a)** Explain the construction and working of a Three-Phase Induction Motor. How does it function as a traction motor in EVs? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 6-pole induction motor runs at 960 RPM. Calculate: (i) Synchronous speed, (ii) Slip, (iii) Frequency of rotor EMF, (iv) Speed of the rotor magnetic field relative to the stator magnetic field. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

### **Part (a) — Three-Phase Induction Motor (7 Marks):**

**Construction:**

A 3-phase induction motor consists of two main parts:

1. **Stator:** Three-phase balanced windings distributed in slots around a laminated silicon-steel core. The windings are spatially displaced by 120° electrical.
2. **Rotor:** Two types:
   - **Squirrel Cage Rotor:** Conducting bars (aluminum) embedded in slots, short-circuited by end rings. Robust, low-cost, no brushes.
   - **Wound Rotor:** Three-phase insulated windings connected to slip rings for external resistance control.

**Working Principle:**

When a balanced 3-phase AC supply is given to the stator windings, a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** is produced at synchronous speed:

$$n_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$$

This RMF cuts the stationary rotor conductors, inducing an EMF (Faraday's Law). Since the rotor circuit is closed, current flows. By Lenz's Law, the rotor current-carrying conductors experience a torque that drives the rotor in the **same direction** as the RMF. The rotor can never reach $n_s$ (otherwise, no relative motion, no induced EMF, no torque). Hence, slip is essential.

**Use as EV Traction Motor:**

In EVs (e.g., Tesla Model S), induction motors are used because:
1. **High reliability** — no permanent magnets, no brushes, robust rotor.
2. **Cost-effective** — squirrel cage is cheap; no rare-earth dependency.
3. **Good high-speed performance** — suitable for single-speed transmission EVs.
4. **Regenerative braking** — controlled via variable frequency inverter.

> **Marks Split:** Construction (2) + Working (3) + EV relevance (2) = 7 Marks

---

### **Part (b) — Numerical on Induction Motor (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Frequency $f = 50$ Hz
- Number of poles $P = 6$
- Rotor speed $n_r = 960$ RPM

### **(i) Synchronous Speed:**

$$n_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ RPM}$$

### **(ii) Slip:**

$$s = \frac{n_s - n_r}{n_s} = \frac{1000 - 960}{1000} = \frac{40}{1000} = 0.04$$

Slip $s = 0.04$ or **4%**.

### **(iii) Frequency of Rotor EMF:**

$$f_r = s \cdot f = 0.04 \times 50 = 2 \text{ Hz}$$

### **(iv) Speed of Rotor Magnetic Field Relative to Stator Magnetic Field:**

The relative speed is the **slip speed**:

$$n_{slip} = n_s - n_r = 1000 - 960 = 40 \text{ RPM}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The rotor's magnetic field always rotates at **synchronous speed** relative to the stator, but the rotor itself rotates at $n_r$. The *relative motion* between stator RMF and rotor is exactly the slip speed, which is responsible for EMF induction.

> **Marks Split:** Each sub-part = ~1.75 marks; precise values essential.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing **synchronous speed $n_s$** with **rotor speed $n_r$** in induction motor problems. Always compute $n_s$ first.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Using $A = 2$ in the EMF equation for **wave-wound** machines and $A = P$ for **lap-wound** machines. KTU Board Examiners explicitly check this.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to convert units — **Flux in Weber**, **Speed in RPM** (or convert to rev/s for some formulas). Unit errors lead to direct loss of 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Writing $T \propto I_a^2$ for induction motor (incorrect). This is true only for DC series motors. Induction motor torque is **non-linear** with slip — refer to the Thevenin torque equation.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Not mentioning **four-quadrant operation** when discussing traction motors. KTU 2024 scheme has high-weightage on this concept.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Motors (Module 1):**

- ✅ **Definition:** An automotive motor is an electromechanical energy converter; traction motors are specialized for propulsion in EVs.
- ✅ **Universal Equations:** $E_b = k_e \Phi \omega$ and $T = k_t \Phi I_a$ are the foundation for all motor analysis.
- ✅ **Power Equation:** $P = T \omega$ — the most important relationship for sizing motors.
- ✅ **Synchronous Speed:** $n_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ — applies to induction and synchronous motors.
- ✅ **Slip:** $s = \dfrac{n_s - n_r}{n_s}$ — must exist for induction motor torque production.
- ✅ **Max Torque Slip:** $s_{max\,T} = \dfrac{R_r}{X}$ — independent of supply voltage.
- ✅ **Max Torque Value:** $T_{max} = \dfrac{3V^2}{2\omega_s X}$ — depends on $V^2$ and rotor reactance.
- ✅ **CPSR:** $\dfrac{\omega_{max}}{\omega_{base}} \geq 3$ for modern EVs (single-speed transmission).
- ✅ **Four-Quadrant Operation:** Essential for EV traction motors — forward/reverse motoring and regen braking.
- ✅ **DC Series Motor:** Used as **starter motor** in ICE vehicles (high starting torque, $T \propto I^2$).
- ✅ **BLDC:** Preferred in **2-wheeler EVs**; electronically commutated; high efficiency.
- ✅ **PMSM:** Preferred in **premium EVs**; highest power density; uses NdFeB magnets.
- ✅ **Induction Motor:** Used in **Tesla (legacy) and mid-segment EVs**; rugged, no rare-earth.
- ✅ **SRM:** Used in **heavy-duty EVs**; no magnets, fault-tolerant, high torque ripple.
- ✅ **EMF Equation (DC):** $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi P N Z}{60 A}$ — must memorize for numerical problems.
- ✅ **Power Density Target:** Modern traction motors aim for **3–6 kW/kg**.
- ✅ **Efficiency Target:** 90–97% across drive cycle.
- ✅ **Cooling:** Critical for traction motors — typically **water-glycol jacket** or **oil spray** cooling.
- ✅ **Regenerative Braking:** Motor acts as a generator; energy returned to battery through bidirectional inverter.

---

> **End of Notes — Module 1: Motors**  
> **Prepared for KTU 2024 Scheme — OEMUT612 Modern Automotive Technologies**  
> **For Academic Use — Aligned with NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electronic Power Control Unit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT612 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electronic Power Control Unit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electronic Power Control Unit (EPCU)** — A high-voltage, high-power electronic subsystem in modern (electric and hybrid) automobiles that intelligently processes, converts, and distributes electrical energy between the traction battery, electric traction motor(s), generator, auxiliary loads, and the 12V low-voltage network. It is the *power-electronic heart* of any electrified powertrain, governing voltage levels (DC-DC conversion), AC-DC/AC-AC conversion (inversion/rectification), torque command execution, regenerative braking energy recovery, and system-level protection.

The EPCU is **not** the same as the VCU (Vehicle Control Unit) or the low-level ECU (Engine/Electronic Control Unit). In KTU 2024 nomenclature:

- **VCU** = *strategic brain* (driver intent, mode selection, torque arbitration)
- **EPCU** = *power-handling muscle* (actual switching of kW-class power)
- **BMS** = *battery guardian* (cell-level monitoring, SoC/SoH)
- **MCU** = *motor control unit* (often a sub-module of EPCU in compact designs)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The EPCU is the "Smart Power Junction Box" of an electric car.**
>
> Imagine your home has three incompatible power sources — a **high-voltage battery** (240V DC, like a giant UPS), a **solar panel** (variable DC), and the **mains grid** (230V AC) — and it must feed an **induction motor** (which wants variable-frequency AC). A naive approach would simply burn out the motor. The EPCU is the **intelligent switchboard** that:
>
> 1. Takes the raw battery DC and *chops* it into variable-frequency AC (via an inverter) to spin the motor at the exact speed/torque the driver demands.
> 2. Takes back the motor's generated AC during braking and *converts* it back to DC to recharge the battery (regenerative braking).
> 3. Steps down the 400V battery to 12V to keep headlights, ECU, and infotainment alive.
> 4. Protects every wire and chip from overcurrent, overvoltage, and short circuits — like a *circuit breaker with a PhD*.

In short: **the EPCU is what makes the battery's chaotic DC electricity behave like a polite, controllable, multi-purpose power supply.**

## 1.3 Position in the Modern Automotive Powertrain

The EPCU sits between the **High-Voltage (HV) bus** and all the **electromechanical actuators**. Its physical location is typically:

- Inside the **engine bay** in a HEV (e.g., Toyota THS, Hyundai TMED)
- Integrated with the **transmission housing** in some dedicated EV platforms (e.g., Tesla drive units, Hyundai E-GMP)
- A standalone **power electronics box** bolted to the chassis in commercial EVs

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU 2024 Mandate)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **bold** metrics are standard KTU assessment values for an EPCU in a typical B-segment passenger EV (≈ 150 kW peak):
>
> - **Nominal DC Bus Voltage:** 400 V (some modern platforms: 800 V)
> - **Peak Power Rating:** 100 kW – 250 kW (passenger) / up to 500 kW (performance)
> - **Continuous Power Rating:** 60 – 80 % of peak
> - **Peak Inverter Efficiency:** 96 – 98 % (silicon IGBT) / 99 % (SiC MOSFET)
> - **DC-DC Converter Efficiency:** 93 – 95 %
> - **Switching Frequency:** 5 kHz – 20 kHz (IGBT) / up to 100 kHz (SiC/GaN)
> - **Coolant Inlet Temperature:** 65 °C – 75 °C (liquid cooled)
> - **IP Rating of Housing:** IP67 (dust-tight, submersible to 1 m)
> - **Response Time (Torque Command):** ≤ 5 ms (VCU to wheel)
> - **Operating Ambient:** –40 °C to +85 °C

## 1.5 Key Subsystems inside an EPCU

A typical KTU-mapped EPCU contains **four** functionally distinct sub-blocks:

| # | Sub-Block | Primary Function | KTU-Synonym |
|---|-----------|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Inverter (INV)** | HV DC → Variable-freq. AC for traction motor | Motor Control Module (MCM) |
| 2 | **LDC (Low-Voltage DC-DC)** | HV 400V → 12V/48V auxiliary | Auxiliary Power Module (APM) |
| 3 | **OBC (On-Board Charger)** | AC grid → HV DC battery (AC Level 1/2) | — |
| 4 | **PDU (Power Distribution Unit)** | HV bus switching, contactors, fusing | HV Junction Box |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Convention:** In some textbooks (and in Hyundai's official literature), the *entire* power-electronic assembly — inverter + LDC + OBC + VCU (sometimes) — is collectively called the **EPCU**. The VCU is *not* always bundled. Confirm with your module-specific lecture notes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How the EPCU Actually Works

The EPCU executes a closed-loop sequence in **< 5 ms** every time the driver presses the accelerator:

1. **Driver Command Acquisition** — Accelerator pedal position sensor (APP) sends 0–5 V to the VCU.
2. **Torque Arbitration** — VCU subtracts torque limits (battery SoC, motor thermal, regen-cap) and publishes a **torque request** over **CAN FD** (typically 500 kbps, 2 Mbps in next-gen).
3. **Inverter Current Command Generation** — A Field-Oriented Control (FOC) algorithm computes the required *d-axis* and *q-axis* stator currents:
   $$ i_d^* = 0 \quad \text{(MTPA boundary)} \qquad i_q^* = \frac{2 \cdot T^*}{3 \cdot p \cdot \psi_{PM}} $$
   where $T^*$ is the commanded torque, $p$ is pole-pairs, $\psi_{PM}$ is the permanent-magnet flux linkage.
4. **PWM Modulation** — A **Space Vector PWM (SVPWM)** block converts the $i_d^*, i_q^*$ voltage references into switching pulses for the 6 IGBTs of the 3-phase inverter.
5. **DC Bus Regulation** — The LDC maintains 12V (or 48V) rail at ±0.5 % tolerance despite load transients (headlights + defogger + HVAC).
6. **Telemetry & Protection** — Every 10 ms, EPCU broadcasts state (motor speed, DC bus current, IGBT junction temp) over CAN; thermal derating kicks in at **T_j > 150 °C** for IGBTs and **T_j > 175 °C** for SiC MOSFETs.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Block

- **Why an inverter?** — A traction PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor) needs variable-frequency, variable-amplitude 3-phase AC. The battery only supplies DC. The inverter is the *translator*.
- **Why SVPWM and not sinusoidal PWM?** — SVPWM provides **15.5 % higher DC-bus utilization** (max line-to-line fundamental = $\frac{V_{DC}}{\sqrt{3}}$) and lower harmonic distortion (THD ≈ 5 % vs. 12 %).
- **Why a separate LDC?** — Isolating the 12V domain protects low-voltage electronics from HV transients and allows the auxiliary battery to *not* be a giant Li-ion pack.
- **Why IGBT over MOSFET in 400V systems?** — IGBTs handle **> 100 A** and **> 600 V** in a single die; equivalent MOSFETs would need paralleling (cost, gate-driver complexity).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the master table for any numerical problem the examiner sets on EPCU. Memorize the right column.**

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Symbols / Units | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Inverter DC-bus utilization (SVPWM) | $V_{LL,rms} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \dfrac{V_{DC}}{2} \cdot m_a$ | $m_a \in [0, 1.15]$ | $m_a > 1$ ⇒ overmodulation (non-linear) |
| 2 | Modulation index | $m_a = \dfrac{\hat{V}_{ref}}{V_{DC}/\sqrt{3}}$ | dimensionless | Linear region: $m_a \le 1$ |
| 3 | AC-side RMS fundamental (SVPWM) | $V_{1,rms} = \dfrac{m_a \cdot V_{DC}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | V | $m_a = 1$ ⇒ $V_{1,rms} \approx 0.707\,V_{DC}$ |
| 4 | PMSM torque | $T = \dfrac{3}{2}\,p\,\left[\psi_{PM}\,i_q + (L_d - L_q)\,i_d\,i_q\right]$ | N·m | Surface PM: $L_d = L_q$ |
| 5 | PMSM back-EMF | $E = \sqrt{2}\,\pi\,f\,N_{ph}\,\Phi_m$ | V | Linear vs. $\omega$ |
| 6 | DC-DC LDC power balance | $P_{HV} = \dfrac{P_{LV}}{\eta_{LDC}}$ | W | $\eta_{LDC} \approx 0.94$ |
| 7 | Switching loss per IGBT | $P_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,V_{DC}\,I_{load}\,(t_{on}+t_{off})\,f_{sw}$ | W | Dominant at $f_{sw} > 5$ kHz |
| 8 | Conduction loss (IGBT) | $P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{avg} + r_{CE} \cdot I_{rms}^2$ | W | Use junction temp T_j |
| 9 | Inverter efficiency | $\eta_{inv} = \dfrac{P_{out,mech}}{P_{in,DC}} = 1 - \dfrac{P_{sw}+P_{cond}+P_{gate}}{P_{in,DC}}$ | % | Typical 96–98 % |
| 10 | Regenerative braking power | $P_{regen} = T_{regen}\,\omega_m \le P_{bat,max,charge}$ | W | Limited by battery SoC & I²R |
| 11 | Energy recovered per stop | $E_{regen} = \int T_{regen}\,\omega_m\,dt$ | J | Typically 30–60 % of kinetic energy |
| 12 | Thermal resistance (junction → coolant) | $R_{th,j-c} = \dfrac{T_j - T_c}{P_{loss}}$ | K/W | Liquid cold-plate: 0.05–0.15 K/W |
| 13 | HV contactor drop-out time | $t_{drop} \le 20$ ms (ISO 6469-3) | ms | Pyro-fuse: < 5 ms |
| 14 | Insulation monitoring (resistance) | $R_{iso} \ge 100\ \Omega/V$ (DC) | Ω | For 400 V: $R_{iso} \ge 40$ kΩ |

> **IMPORTANT — LATEX SAFETY:** In the markdown table above, absolute value/size symbols are rendered as plain numerals (e.g., "$V_{DC}$" instead of "$\vert V_{DC}\vert$") to avoid breaking the pipe-delimited table syntax. In free-flowing equations below, the original notation is preserved.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Range extension:** A 2 % efficiency gain in the inverter translates to **~5 km** of additional range in a 250 km-rating EV.
- **Thermal cascading:** Inadequate cooling of EPCU forces motor derating → performance loss in summer traffic.
- **Functional Safety:** The EPCU is the **single point of failure** for the drive system. ASIL-D decomposition (ISO 26262) mandates dual-core lockstep processors and cross-checked gate drivers.
- **Cybersecurity:** Modern EPCUs (with OTA) are CAN-injectable targets — KTU 2024 now includes a sub-topic on securing CAN-FD against replay attacks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Achievable Line-to-Line RMS Voltage from a DC Bus (SVPWM)

> This is the *most-asked* KTU derivation. Show every line.

**Problem Statement:** A 3-phase 2-level voltage-source inverter is fed from a DC bus of magnitude $V_{DC}$. Using Space-Vector PWM, derive the *maximum* line-to-line RMS voltage that the load can see in the linear modulation region.

**Step 1 — Identify the 6 active voltage vectors and 2 zero vectors of a 2-level VSI.**

The 8 possible switching states $(S_a, S_b, S_c)$ produce 6 active vectors of magnitude $\tfrac{2}{3}V_{DC}$ (e.g., $V_1 = \tfrac{2}{3}V_{DC} \angle 0°$) and 2 zero vectors at the origin.

**Step 2 — Express the inscribed-circle radius of the hexagon.**

The locus of the reference vector $\vec{V}_{ref}$ in the linear region is the inscribed circle of the hexagon:

$$
R_{inscribed} = \frac{2}{3}V_{DC} \cdot \cos(30°) = \frac{2}{3}V_{DC} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Set $R_{inscribed}$ as the peak of the reference vector.**

$$
\hat{V}_{ref,max} = \frac{V_{DC}}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert peak line-to-neutral to line-to-line magnitude.**

The line-to-line voltage is $\sqrt{3}$ times the line-to-neutral voltage:

$$
\hat{V}_{LL,max} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \hat{V}_{ref,max} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{V_{DC}}{\sqrt{3}} = V_{DC}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert peak to RMS.**

$$
V_{LL,rms,max} = \frac{V_{DC}}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071 \, V_{DC}
$$

**Step 6 — Comparison with SPWM (for examiner's gold-star comment).**

For pure sinusoidal PWM, $V_{LL,rms,max} = \dfrac{V_{DC}}{2}$ (i.e., 0.5 $V_{DC}$). SVPWM therefore gives a gain of:

$$
G = \frac{0.7071 \, V_{DC}}{0.5 \, V_{DC}} = 1.1547 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 15.47\,\% \text{ higher}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final boxed result (write this in the exam):**
> $$\boxed{V_{LL,rms,max}^{SVPWM} = \dfrac{V_{DC}}{\sqrt{2}}, \quad \text{gain over SPWM} = 15.47\,\%}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Inverter Loss (IGBT-based, 3-Phase)

**Problem:** A 3-phase inverter uses 6 identical IGBTs. Each device sees half the load current on average. Compute total switching + conduction loss.

**Step 1 — Switching loss per device per cycle.**

Energy dissipated per turn-on + turn-off event:

$$
E_{sw} = \frac{1}{2}\,V_{DC}\,I_{load}\,(t_{on}+t_{off})
$$

Loss per device:

$$
P_{sw,1} = E_{sw} \cdot f_{sw} = \frac{1}{2}\,V_{DC}\,I_{load}\,(t_{on}+t_{off})\,f_{sw}
$$

**Step 2 — Switching loss for 6 devices.**

Each device switches once per fundamental cycle of the *opposite* leg, so per electrical cycle each IGBT switches $f_{sw}/f_1$ times. Sum over 6 devices:

$$
P_{sw,total} = 6 \cdot \frac{1}{2}\,V_{DC}\,I_{load}\,(t_{on}+t_{off})\,f_{sw}
$$

**Step 3 — Conduction loss per device.**

For sinusoidal modulation, average current per IGBT = $I_{peak}/\pi$, RMS = $I_{peak}/2$:

$$
P_{cond,1} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot \frac{I_{peak}}{\pi} + r_{CE}\cdot \left(\frac{I_{peak}}{2}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Conduction loss for 6 devices (assume identical duty cycles).**

$$
P_{cond,total} = 6\left[V_{CE(sat)}\cdot \frac{I_{peak}}{\pi} + r_{CE}\cdot \frac{I_{peak}^{2}}{4}\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Total inverter loss.**

$$
P_{loss,inv} = P_{sw,total} + P_{cond,total}
$$

**Step 6 — Inverter efficiency.**

$$
\eta_{inv} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{loss,inv}} = 1 - \frac{P_{sw,total} + P_{cond,total}}{P_{out} + P_{sw,total} + P_{cond,total}}
$$

> **Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
>
> Given: $V_{DC} = 400$ V, $I_{load} = 300$ A, $f_{sw} = 10$ kHz, $t_{on} = t_{off} = 200$ ns, $V_{CE(sat)} = 1.7$ V, $r_{CE} = 4$ mΩ.
>
> $$P_{sw,1} = \tfrac{1}{2}(400)(300)(0.4\times 10^{-6})(10^4) = 240\,\text{W}$$
> $$P_{sw,total} = 6 \times 240 = 1440\,\text{W}$$
> $$I_{peak} \approx 300 \text{ A (sinusoidal, }\hat I \approx 1.414\,I_{rms}\text{, but given as 300 A peak)}$$
> $$P_{cond,1} = 1.7 \cdot \frac{300}{\pi} + 0.004 \cdot \frac{300^2}{4} = 162.3 + 90 = 252.3\,\text{W}$$
> $$P_{cond,total} = 6 \times 252.3 \approx 1514\,\text{W}$$
> $$P_{loss,inv} \approx 2954\,\text{W} \approx 2.95 \text{ kW}$$
> $$\text{Output at 100 kW: } \eta_{inv} = \frac{100}{102.95} \approx 97.1\,\%$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Block-Wise Responsibilities

| Functional Block | Input Signal | Output Signal | Control Algorithm | Failure Mode (ASIL) |
|------------------|-------------|---------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Inverter | CAN torque req. (Nm) + resolver pos. (°) | 3-phase PWM gate pulses | FOC / DTC | Short-circuit → ASIL-D |
| LDC | 400 V HV bus | 12–14 V LV bus | Voltage-mode hysteretic | Open circuit → ASIL-B |
| OBC | 230 V AC (PFC input) | 400 V DC (CC/CV to battery) | PFC + LLC resonant | Over-voltage → ASIL-B |
| PDU | HVIL signal + contactor cmd | HV bus enable/disable | Hardwired logic | Welded contactor → ASIL-D |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Cue:** When asked *"Compare OBC, LDC, and Inverter on the basis of power flow direction"*, always tabulate: OBC = AC→DC, LDC = HV-DC→LV-DC (uni-directional in older designs, *bi-directional* in 48V mild-hybrids), Inverter = DC→AC *and* AC→DC (regen).

---

## 3.4 Communication Architecture Inside the EPCU

The EPCU talks to the rest of the car via:

$$
\text{VCU} \xrightarrow{\text{CAN FD, 2 Mbps}} \text{EPCU} \xrightarrow{\text{Internal SPI / CAN} } \text{Sub-modules}
$$

Some modern EPCUs (e.g., Tesla, Lucid) use **Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1)** to offload high-bandwidth telemetry like IGBT-junction temperature traces.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an EPCU

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HVBus[HV Battery 400V DC] --> Contactor1[HV Contactor 1]
    Contactor1 --> Precharge[Pre-charge Resistor and Relay]
    Precharge --> Contactor2[HV Contactor 2]
    Contactor2 --> DCBus[Internal DC Bus Capacitor Bank]
    DCBus --> INV[3-phase Inverter IGBT Bridge]
    DCBus --> LDC[Low-Voltage DC-DC Converter]
    DCBus --> OBC[On-Board Charger AC-DC PFC plus LLC]
    INV --> PMSM[Traction Motor PMSM]
    PMSM -. Regen AC .-> INV
    INV --> Resolver[Resolver and Position Sensor]
    Resolver --> INV
    LDC --> LBus[12V LV Bus]
    LBus --> LVBat[12V Lead-acid or Li-ion Aux]
    OBC --> ACPlug[AC Mains Plug Type 2]
    VCU[Vehicle Control Unit] -->|CAN FD torque request| INV
    VCU -->|CAN FD enable disable| Contactor1
    VCU -->|CAN FD charge cmd| OBC
    BMS[Battery Management System] -->|CAN SoC SoH limits| VCU
    INV -->|Telemetry motor speed torque temp| VCU
    LDC -->|LV current voltage| VCU
```

## 4.2 EPCU Signal & Power Flow Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SensingLayer["Sensing Layer"]
        APP[Accelerator Pedal Position]
        BRK[Brake Pedal Sensor]
        SOC[Battery SoC from BMS]
        TMOT[Motor Temperature NTC]
        TJ[IGBT Junction Temp NTC]
    end
    subgraph DecisionLayer["Decision Layer - VCU"]
        TQ[Torque Arbitration Block]
        MODE[Drive Mode Selector Eco Normal Sport]
    end
    subgraph PowerLayer["Power Layer - EPCU"]
        SVPWM[SVPWM Modulator]
        GDRV[Gate Driver and Desat Protection]
        IPM[IGBT or SiC Power Module]
        DCDC[Buck-Boost LDC]
        PDU2[HV Contactor PDU]
    end
    subgraph ActuatorLayer["Actuator Layer"]
        PMSM2[PMSM Stator Windings]
        LVNet[12V Loads]
        Wheel[Driven Wheel]
    end
    APP --> TQ
    BRK --> TQ
    SOC --> TQ
    TMOT --> TQ
    TQ --> SVPWM
    MODE --> SVPWM
    SVPWM --> GDRV
    GDRV --> IPM
    IPM --> PMSM2
    PMSM2 --> Wheel
    PDU2 --> IPM
    TJ --> GDRV
    IPM --> DCDC
    DCDC --> LVNet
```

## 4.3 Closed-Loop Torque Control (FOC) inside the Inverter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Tref[Torque Reference N.m] --> IQref[Iq Ref Generator MTPA]
    IDref[Id Ref equals 0] --> Park1[Park Transform abc to dq]
    IQref --> Park1
    Iabc[Measured Phase Currents ia ib ic] --> Clarke[Clarke Transform abc to alpha beta]
    Clarke --> Park1
    Theta[Rotor Angle from Resolver] --> Park1
    Park1 --> IdMeas[Id Measured]
    Park1 --> IqMeas[Iq Measured]
    IdMeas --> PI_D[PI Controller for D axis]
    IqMeas --> PI_Q[PI Controller for Q axis]
    VdRef[Id Ref equals 0] --> PI_D
    VqRef --> PI_Q
    PI_D --> InvPark[Inverse Park Transform dq to alpha beta]
    PI_Q --> InvPark
    Theta --> InvPark
    InvPark --> SVM[Space Vector Modulator]
    SVM --> GateDrv[6 Gate Driver Signals]
    GateDrv --> Inv[3 phase Inverter IGBTs]
    Inv --> PMSM3[PMSM Stator]
    PMSM3 -. Back EMF .-> Iabc
```

## 4.4 Inside One IGBT Power Module (3-Phase, 6-Pack)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Positive DC Terminal] --> D1[Diode 1]
    P --> D3[Diode 3]
    P --> D5[Diode 5]
    D1 --> T1[IGBT T1 Upper Phase A]
    T1 --> PA[Phase A Output]
    D3 --> T3[IGBT T3 Upper Phase B]
    T3 --> PB[Phase B Output]
    D5 --> T5[IGBT T5 Upper Phase C]
    T5 --> PC[Phase C Output]
    PA --> T2[IGBT T2 Lower Phase A]
    T2 --> N[Negative DC Terminal]
    PB --> T4[IGBT T4 Lower Phase B]
    T4 --> N
    PC --> T6[IGBT T6 Lower Phase C]
    T6 --> N
    N --> D2[Diode 2]
    N --> D4[Diode 4]
    N --> D6[Diode 6]
    D2 --> T1
    D4 --> T3
    D6 --> T5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a diode anti-parallel to each IGBT?** The motor is *inductive* — when a phase is switched off, the trapped inductor current has nowhere to go. The freewheeling diode provides a path, and during **regenerative braking** it actually *conducts the return current* back to the DC bus, charging the battery.

## 4.5 Power Flow Modes in an EV (How the EPCU Reconfigures)

| Mode | Driver Action | INV Direction | LDC Direction | OBC Direction |
|------|---------------|---------------|---------------|---------------|
| **1. Parked Charging** | Plugged in, ignition off | Idle | Idle (LV supplied by 12V aux) | **AC → DC** to HV battery |
| **2. Cranking / Ready** | Key ON | Idle / low preheat | **HV → 12V** | Idle |
| **3. Acceleration** | Accelerator pressed | **DC → AC** to motor | **HV → 12V** | Idle |
| **4. Cruise (Steady)** | Constant pedal | **DC → AC** (low torque) | **HV → 12V** | Idle |
| **5. Coasting / Lift-off** | Accelerator released | **AC → DC** (low regen) | **HV → 12V** | Idle |
| **6. Hard Braking** | Brake pedal hard | **AC → DC** (high regen) | **HV → 12V** | Idle |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1.** *Define Electronic Power Control Unit (EPCU) and list its four major sub-blocks.*

**Model Answer:**
The Electronic Power Control Unit (EPCU) is a high-power electronic subsystem in an electric or hybrid vehicle that processes and distributes electrical energy between the high-voltage battery, traction motor, and low-voltage auxiliaries. Its four major sub-blocks are:
1. **Inverter (INV)** — converts HV DC to variable-frequency 3-phase AC for the traction motor.
2. **Low-Voltage DC-DC Converter (LDC)** — steps down 400 V to 12 V for auxiliary loads.
3. **On-Board Charger (OBC)** — converts grid AC to HV DC for battery charging.
4. **Power Distribution Unit (PDU)** — performs HV bus switching, contactor control, and fusing.

---

**Q2.** *Why is Space Vector PWM (SVPWM) preferred over sinusoidal PWM in an automotive inverter?*

**Model Answer:**
SVPWM is preferred because:
1. It offers **15.47 % higher DC-bus utilization** (maximum line-to-line RMS = $V_{DC}/\sqrt{2}$ vs. $V_{DC}/2$ in SPWM).
2. It produces lower current THD (≈ 5 % vs. ≈ 12 %).
3. It is easier to implement in digital processors (only sector look-up + dwell-time calculation).
4. It enables smooth transition into overmodulation (six-step mode) for extended speed range.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — *Inverter, PWM & Power Loss***

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a 3-phase Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) used in the EPCU of an electric vehicle. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Block diagram** — DC bus → 3 legs of IGBTs (T1–T6) with anti-parallel diodes → 3-phase output (A, B, C) → PMSM stator.
2. **Switching logic** — 8 states: 6 active vectors ($V_1$–$V_6$) + 2 zero vectors ($V_0$, $V_7$). Each active vector is 60° apart, magnitude $\tfrac{2}{3}V_{DC}$.
3. **Switching sequence** — In a sector (e.g., Sector I: $V_1$, $V_2$), the inverter applies $V_1$ for $T_1$, $V_2$ for $T_2$, and $V_0$ for $T_0$ per switching period, with $T_1 + T_2 + T_0 = T_s$.
4. **Output waveform** — Quasi-square line-to-line voltage with fundamental sinusoidal component. For a 400 V bus: $V_{LL,rms} \approx 282$ V at $m_a = 1$.
5. **Role in EPCU** — The inverter executes the VCU's torque request by varying *frequency* (motor speed) and *amplitude* (motor torque).
6. **Protection** — Desaturation detection, short-circuit lockout, gate-supply under-voltage lockout.
7. **Conclusion** — The inverter is the *single most critical* sub-block of the EPCU; its efficiency directly determines vehicle range.

**(b)** *A 3-phase VSI is fed by a 400 V DC bus and supplies a PMSM at modulation index $m_a = 0.9$. The total switching loss is 1.2 kW and the conduction loss is 1.5 kW. Compute (i) the line-to-line RMS output voltage, (ii) the inverter efficiency when the output mechanical power is 90 kW. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Line-to-line RMS voltage:**

$$
V_{1,rms} = \frac{m_a \cdot V_{DC}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{0.9 \times 400}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{360}{1.4142} = 254.6 \text{ V}
$$

**(ii) Inverter efficiency:**

Total electrical input from DC bus = Output + Total losses:

$$
P_{in,DC} = 90 + (1.2 + 1.5) = 92.7 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\eta_{inv} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in,DC}} = \frac{90}{92.7} = 0.9709 = 97.09\,\%
$$

> **Answer:** $V_{LL,rms} = 254.6$ V, $\eta_{inv} = 97.09\,\%$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — *EPCU Architecture, LDC, OBC & Modes***

**(a)** *Draw the functional block diagram of an EPCU and explain the role of (i) the inverter, (ii) the LDC, and (iii) the OBC. Discuss how the EPCU handles regenerative braking. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Block diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 above (re-draw neatly in the answer booklet).

**Role of (i) Inverter:** Converts HV DC battery (400 V) into variable-frequency, variable-amplitude 3-phase AC for the traction PMSM. Uses SVPWM and FOC. Handles bidirectional power flow (motoring + regen).

**Role of (ii) LDC (Low-Voltage DC-DC Converter):** Steps down 400 V → 12 V at ≈ 2–3 kW continuous to power ECU, lights, infotainment, and HVAC blower. Topology is usually an isolated full-bridge with synchronous rectification (≈ 94 % efficiency). Bi-directional in 48 V mild-hybrid systems.

**Role of (iii) OBC (On-Board Charger):** Converts 230 V/15 A single-phase AC (or 400 V 3-phase) from the grid to 400 V DC for the HV battery. Two stages: PFC (boost, unity PF) + LLC resonant (galvanically isolated, CC/CV charging). Power ratings: 3.3 kW (1-phase), 11 kW (3-phase), 22 kW (fast AC).

**Regenerative Braking Handling:**
- When the driver lifts the accelerator, the VCU reduces the torque request to a *negative* value.
- The PMSM, still spinning, now acts as a *generator* — its rotating PM flux induces a 3-phase AC voltage in the stator.
- The same 6 IGBTs, with their anti-parallel diodes, now act as a *rectifier*: the diodes conduct to feed DC back into the HV bus, charging the battery.
- The EPCU enforces a *negative torque limit* based on battery SoC, I²R heating, and BMS headroom.
- Energy recovered per urban stop: 30–60 % of the original kinetic energy.

**(b)** *Compare SVPWM and SPWM with respect to DC-bus utilization, harmonic content, and implementation complexity. A 3-phase inverter operates in overmodulation with $m_a = 1.2$. Calculate the line-to-line RMS fundamental voltage for $V_{DC} = 400$ V. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion | SPWM | SVPWM |
|-----------|------|-------|
| Max DC-bus utilization (linear) | $0.5\,V_{DC}$ (RMS) | $0.577\,V_{DC} = V_{DC}/\sqrt{2}$ (RMS) |
| Gain over SPWM | 1.0 (reference) | **1.1547 (≈ 15.5 % more)** |
| Current THD at $m_a=1$ | ≈ 12 % | ≈ 5 % |
| Implementation | Compare triangle vs. sine (3 comparators) | Sector identification + dwell-time calc |
| Overmodulation range | None (saturates at $m_a=1$) | $1 < m_a \le 1.15$ linear, $1.15 < m_a \le 1.273$ six-step |
| Switching loss | Lower at low $f_{sw}$ | Comparable |

**Calculation for $m_a = 1.2$:**

In the linear SVPWM region ($m_a \le 1.1547$), the formula holds:
$$
V_{1,rms} = \frac{m_a \cdot V_{DC}}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

For $m_a = 1.2$ we are in **overmodulation** (non-linear). The effective modulation index saturates at 1.1547 for *linear* fundamental scaling. Using the practical overmodulation approximation (for $m_a = 1.2$, effective $m_a^{eff} \approx 1.137$):

$$
V_{1,rms}^{eff} \approx \frac{1.137 \times 400}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{454.8}{1.4142} = 321.6 \text{ V}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q.B Part b):**
> - Students often blindly apply $V = m_a \cdot V_{DC}/\sqrt{2}$ for $m_a = 1.2$, getting **339.4 V** — *wrong*, because the linear relationship breaks in overmodulation.
> - The correct approach: state that the relationship is *non-linear* above $m_a = 1.1547$, and either (i) quote the effective value, or (ii) state "this is overmodulation; the linear formula does not apply and the actual value lies between 282.8 V ($m_a=1$) and 360 V (six-step, $m_a = 1.273$)."
> - Marks lost for: (1) not mentioning the non-linear region, (2) not commenting on the resulting increase in low-order harmonics (5th, 7th), and (3) not mentioning the loss of smooth current waveform.

---

> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for ALL EPCU Questions:**

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students LOSE marks in EPCU questions:**
> 1. **Conflating VCU and EPCU.** VCU = *decision*, EPCU = *actuation*. Marks deducted for: "EPCU decides torque" — *wrong*; the VCU decides, the EPCU *executes*.
> 2. **Forgetting freewheeling diodes.** In any IGBT diagram, every switch MUST have an anti-parallel diode. Examiners do deduct 1 mark per missing diode (up to 6).
> 3. **Mixing up OBC and LDC.** OBC is **AC-from-grid → DC-to-battery**; LDC is **DC-HV → DC-LV**. Different power flow, different topology, different rating.
> 4. **Wrong unit on insulation resistance.** ISO 6469 mandates $R_{iso} \ge 100\ \Omega/V$ (i.e., proportional to system voltage), not a flat 500 kΩ.
> 5. **Ignoring ASIL rating.** When asked "what is the safety integrity of the EPCU?", students write "it has fuses." *Fuses are passive; the EPCU is ASIL-D with active monitoring, dual-core lockstep, and watchdogs.*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Pre-exam 60-second recap — read just before entering the hall:**

- ✅ EPCU = **power-electronic brain** = **Inverter + LDC + OBC + PDU**
- ✅ VCU is *separate* (decision-making); EPCU is *executive* (kW-class switching)
- ✅ Traction inverter uses **SVPWM** → 15.5 % more DC-bus utilization than SPWM
- ✅ Key formula: $V_{1,rms} = m_a \cdot V_{DC} / \sqrt{2}$ (linear region, $m_a \le 1.1547$)
- ✅ PMSM torque: $T = \tfrac{3}{2} p [\psi_{PM} i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q]$ → set $i_d^* = 0$ for MTPA
- ✅ IGBT conduction loss: $P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{avg} + r_{CE} \cdot I_{rms}^2$
- ✅ Switching loss: $P_{sw} = \tfrac{1}{2} V_{DC} I (t_{on} + t_{off}) f_{sw}$ — *dominates at high $f_{sw}$*
- ✅ Regenerative braking uses the **same 6 IGBTs + diodes** as a synchronous rectifier
- ✅ LDC = **HV (400 V) → LV (12 V)**, OBC = **AC (230/400 V) → HV DC (400 V)**
- ✅ Typical inverter efficiency: **96 – 98 %** (IGBT), **99 %** (SiC)
- ✅ Coolant inlet temperature: 65–75 °C; $R_{th,j-c} \approx 0.05$ K/W with cold-plate
- ✅ Functional safety: **ASIL-D** (ISO 26262), ISO 6469-3 for HV safety
- ✅ Insulation monitoring: $R_{iso} \ge 100\ \Omega/V$ (i.e., $\ge 40$ kΩ for 400 V)
- ✅ Communication: **CAN FD @ 2 Mbps** (next-gen: Automotive Ethernet 100BASE-T1)
- ✅ Switching frequencies: IGBT 5–20 kHz; SiC MOSFET up to 100 kHz; GaN HEMT up to 1 MHz (lab stage)
- ✅ Inverter response time: ≤ **5 ms** (VCU torque request → wheel torque)
- ✅ Freewheeling diodes are MANDATORY on every IGBT — *never draw a 3-phase inverter without them*
- ✅ When asked "compare SVPWM and SPWM", always lead with the **15.47 %** number — examiners *love* that figure
- ✅ "$m_a = 1.2$" in an exam means **overmodulation** — the linear formula fails; mention the non-linearity
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Wiring Harness - CAN Bus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT612 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electric Wiring Harness - CAN Bus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electric Wiring Harness (EWH)

### Formal KTU Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> An **Electric Wiring Harness (EWH)** is a systematic and integrated assembly of **electrical wires, cables, connectors, terminals, fuses, relays, conduits, and protective sleeves** that form the central nervous system of an automobile. It is engineered to transmit **electrical power** (for actuators, motors, lighting) and **electronic signals** (for sensors, ECUs, and communication buses) between various electrical and electronic subsystems in a vehicle, while withstanding the harsh automotive environment of heat, vibration, moisture, and chemical exposure.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Nervous System" 🧠
Think of a modern car as a human body. The **Engine, Brakes, Doors, Lights, and Infotainment** are like organs. Just as a human body needs nerves to send signals to the brain, an automobile needs a **Wiring Harness** to send power and data between components. Without the harness, a car would be a pile of disconnected metal and silicon — it is literally the **lifeline that carries both energy (blood)** and **information (nerve impulses)** to every subsystem.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Fact (Board-Exam Favorite):** A premium modern car like a BMW 7-Series or Mercedes S-Class contains over **1.5 km of wiring**, with more than **3000 individual wires** and **600+ connectors**, all bundled into a single complex harness.

### 1.2 Controller Area Network (CAN Bus)

### Formal KTU Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CAN (Controller Area Network) Bus** is a multi-master, message-oriented, **serial communication protocol** standardized under **ISO 11898**. Originally developed by **Bosch GmbH in 1986**, CAN Bus enables multiple **Electronic Control Units (ECUs)** to communicate with each other in real-time **without a central host computer**, using a **differential two-wire (CAN-H and CAN-L) bus topology**. It is the de-facto standard for in-vehicle networking (IVN) in the automotive industry.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Conference Call with Arbitration" 📞
Imagine **20 people in a room** (the ECUs) all sharing **one telephone line** (the bus). When Person A wants to speak, they raise their hand with a **priority tag** (the message ID). If two people try to speak at the same time, the one with the **higher priority (lower ID number)** gets to speak first, and the other politely waits. This is exactly how **CSMA/CD + AMP (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection and Arbitration on Message Priority)** works in CAN. No one needs a moderator — the protocol itself enforces order.

### 1.3 Why CAN Bus Replaced Point-to-Point Wiring

| Aspect | Old Point-to-Point Wiring | Modern CAN Bus |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of Wires** | **800–1500 wires** (heavy) | **2 wires** (CAN-H + CAN-L) |
| **Weight** | ~**50–80 kg** of copper | ~**2–4 kg** of twisted pair |
| **Cost** | Very high (labor + copper) | Reduced by **~30–40%** |
| **Data Sharing** | Each sensor needs own wire | Single bus shared by all ECUs |
| **Reliability** | Many failure points (connectors) | Robust differential signaling |
| **Speed** | Limited by mechanical switches | Up to **1 Mbit/s (Classical CAN)**, **5 Mbit/s (CAN FD)** |
| **Real-time** | No | Yes (deterministic latency) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Highlight:** The CAN protocol is governed by **ISO 11898-1** (Data Link Layer), **ISO 11898-2** (High-Speed Physical Layer up to 1 Mbit/s), and **ISO 11898-3** (Low-Speed Fault-Tolerant up to 125 kbit/s).

---

### 1.4 Major Components of a Wiring Harness

The following components are essential building blocks of every automotive wiring harness:

- **Wires & Cables** — Copper (or aluminum) conductors, often tin/silver-plated, with **PVC, XLPE, or Teflon** insulation. Typical gauges: **0.35 mm² to 16 mm²**.
- **Connectors** — Plastic-housed multi-pin plugs (e.g., Bosch, AMP, Sumitomo, Yazaki types) that mate male–female to join harness segments.
- **Terminals** — Metal contact pins (brass, phosphor-bronze) crimped or soldered onto wire ends.
- **Fuses & Fusible Links** — Overcurrent protection devices rated from **5 A to 60 A+**.
- **Relays** — Electromechanical or solid-state switches allowing low-current signals to control high-current loads.
- **Conduits & Sleeves** — Protective tubing (PVC conduit, corrugated loom, braided sleeve) shielding wires from abrasion, heat, and chemicals.
- **Grommets & Clips** — Rubber/plastic fixtures for routing through metal panels and securing the harness to the chassis.
- **Junction Boxes / Power Distribution Centers** — Centralized fuse/relay panels.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of the Electric Wiring Harness

A wiring harness is not just a random bunch of wires. It is **engineered** following strict design rules to ensure safety, EMI immunity, manufacturability, and serviceability.

### 2.1.1 Hierarchical Design Steps

1. **Load Analysis & Schematic Design** — Identify every electrical load (headlamps, ECU, motor) and determine current draw, voltage drop, and required wire cross-section.
2. **Wire Sizing using Voltage Drop Equation** — Critical to ensure less than **3% voltage drop** in power circuits and **< 0.5 V drop** in signal circuits.
3. **Circuit Protection Allocation** — Each circuit must have a dedicated fuse sized at **~125% of the steady-state load current**.
4. **Connector & Terminal Selection** — Based on current rating, mating cycles, and environmental sealing (**IP67 / IP69K** for engine bay).
5. **Bundling & Routing** — Group wires by function (sensor, power, communication) and route them through safe zones away from heat sources (exhaust, turbo) and moving parts.
6. **Shielding & Grounding** — Twisted/shielded pairs for CAN, sensor signals; star-ground topology for power returns.
7. **Testing & Validation** — Continuity, hi-pot (high voltage), insulation resistance, and EMI/EMC tests.

### 2.2 Why CAN Bus? The Underlying Engineering Need

Before CAN, every ECU (Engine, ABS, Transmission, Body Control, Instrument Cluster) communicated via **dedicated analog/digital lines** to every other ECU it needed to talk to. With **70+ ECUs** in modern cars, this required **thousands of wires**. CAN solves this with a **broadcast serial bus**.

### 2.3 CAN Bus Physical Layer — Differential Signaling

The physical layer consists of **two wires**: **CAN-H (High)** and **CAN-L (Low)**, terminated at each end with a **120 Ω resistor** (the characteristic impedance of a twisted pair).

| Bus State | CAN-H Voltage | CAN-L Voltage | Differential Voltage (V_diff) | Logic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Recessive (Logic '1')** | ~2.5 V | ~2.5 V | **0 V** | Bus idle / dominant bit logic-1 |
| **Dominant (Logic '0')** | ~3.5 V | ~1.5 V | **+2.0 V** | Active transmission / logic-0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Differential signaling** is the key to CAN's **EMI immunity**. Any external noise affects both CAN-H and CAN-L equally, so the *difference* remains unchanged. The receiver only looks at (V_CANH − V_CANL), not absolute voltages.

### 2.4 CAN Bus Data Link Layer — Frame Format

CAN has **four frame types**: **Data Frame, Remote Frame, Error Frame, Overload Frame**. The **Data Frame** is the most important.

#### Standard CAN Data Frame (11-bit ID)

| Field | Length (bits) | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **SOF (Start of Frame)** | 1 | Dominant bit — synchronizes all nodes |
| **Arbitration Field** | 12 | 11-bit ID + 1 RTR bit (dominant for data frame) |
| **Control Field** | 6 | IDE + r0 + 4-bit DLC (Data Length Code, 0–8 bytes) |
| **Data Field** | 0–64 | The actual payload (max 8 bytes in Classical CAN) |
| **CRC Field** | 16 | 15-bit CRC + 1 delimiter — error detection |
| **ACK Field** | 2 | Receiver acknowledges (1 bit + 1 delimiter) |
| **EOF (End of Frame)** | 7 | 7 recessive bits — marks frame end |
| **IFS (Inter-Frame Space)** | 3 | Required gap between frames |

> [!NOTE]
> **Extended CAN Frame** uses a **29-bit identifier** by inserting an **IDE (Identifier Extension)** recessive bit and an additional 18-bit ID segment, providing **2²⁹ = 536 million unique IDs** (vs. **2¹¹ = 2048** for standard).

### 2.5 Bit Stuffing

To maintain synchronization, after **5 consecutive identical bits**, the transmitter automatically inserts a **stuff bit of opposite polarity**. The receiver removes these bits. This guarantees a re-synchronization edge at least every **10 bit times**.

### 2.6 CSMA/CD + AMP Arbitration

When two nodes start transmitting simultaneously:
- Both nodes monitor the bus while transmitting.
- The node transmitting a **recessive bit** but reading a **dominant bit** loses arbitration and immediately backs off.
- The node with the **lower numerical ID** (more leading dominant 0s) wins, because **0 is dominant**.
- **No data is destroyed** — the loser simply retries after the current frame completes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **non-destructive bitwise arbitration** — the highest-priority message wins with **zero latency penalty**, and no part of its frame is corrupted.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Value | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Differential Voltage** | $V_{diff} = V_{CANH} - V_{CANL}$ | Volts (V) | +2 V = Dominant, 0 V = Recessive |
| 2 | **Recessive Bus Voltage** | $V_{CANH} = V_{CANL} = V_{CC}/2 = 2.5\,V$ | V | At 5 V supply |
| 3 | **Nominal Bit Time** | $t_{bit} = t_{SyncSeg} + t_{PropSeg} + t_{PhaseSeg1} + t_{PhaseSeg2}$ | seconds (s) | Sum of 4 segments |
| 4 | **Baud Rate** | $B = \dfrac{1}{t_{bit}}$ | bits/second (bps) | Inverse of bit time |
| 5 | **Maximum Bus Length vs Baud Rate** | Empirical (see Table 2.7.1) | meters (m) | Trade-off: speed ↔ distance |
| 6 | **Bus Utilization (Load)** | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{tx} \cdot L_{frame} \cdot 8}{B \cdot t_{window}} \times 100\%$ | % | Should be **< 70%** for headroom |
| 7 | **Bit Stuffing Overhead** | $S_{overhead} = \dfrac{D_{stuffed} - D_{original}}{D_{original}} \times 100\%$ | % | Worst-case ≈ **20%** |
| 8 | **Termination Resistance** | $R_{term} = 120\,\Omega$ | Ohms (Ω) | One at each end of bus |
| 9 | **Voltage Drop (Wiring)** | $V_{drop} = \dfrac{2 \cdot I \cdot \rho \cdot L}{A}$ | Volts (V) | Round-trip: 2L wire length |
| 10 | **Maximum CAN Nodes** | Up to **$2^{11}=2048$** (Standard) or **$2^{29}$** (Extended) | — | Limited by ID space, not bus |
| 11 | **CRC Polynomial (CAN)** | $x^{15} + x^{14} + x^{10} + x^{8} + x^{7} + x^{4} + x^{3} + 1$ | — | 15-bit Cyclic Redundancy Check |
| 12 | **Worst-Case Arbitration Latency** | $t_{arb,max} = (N-1) \cdot t_{frame,max}$ | seconds | N = nodes, t_frame = max frame time |

### Table 2.7.1 — Empirical CAN Baud Rate vs Maximum Bus Length

| Baud Rate | Max Bus Length | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **1 Mbit/s** | **40 m** | Powertrain (Engine, Transmission) |
| **500 kbit/s** | **100 m** | Chassis (ABS, ESP, Steering) |
| **250 kbit/s** | **250 m** | Body Electronics |
| **125 kbit/s** | **500 m** | Comfort & Infotainment |
| **50 kbit/s** | **1000 m** | Diagnostic & Industrial (LIN alternative) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** Higher baud rate → faster data → but shorter maximum bus length due to **propagation delay** (signal travel time ~5 ns/m).

### 2.8 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Reduced Complexity** — One 2-wire bus replaces thousands of dedicated lines.
- **Weight Reduction** — Critical for **EV range** (every kg of copper saved = more km of range).
- **Real-Time Determinism** — Essential for **brake-by-wire, steer-by-wire, ADAS** sensor fusion.
- **Diagnostic Standardization** — **UDS (Unified Diagnostic Services)** over CAN via **OBD-II port** (pins 6 and 14 in J1962 connector).
- **Scalability** — Adding a new ECU only requires tapping into the existing bus (with proper ID assignment).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation — Wire Cross-Section from Voltage Drop Constraint

A wiring harness designer must ensure that the voltage at the load terminal is not excessively reduced by the resistance of the wires. Given a maximum allowable voltage drop, we derive the **minimum required wire cross-sectional area**.

### Given
- Load current: $I$ (Amperes)
- One-way wire length from battery to load: $L$ (meters)
- Conductor resistivity: $\rho$ (Ohm·meters) — for copper, $\rho_{Cu} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega \cdot m$
- Maximum allowable voltage drop: $V_{drop,max}$ (Volts)

### Step 1 — Resistance of a Single Wire
The DC resistance of a single conductor of length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$ is:

$$
R_{wire} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{A}
$$

### Step 2 — Round-Trip Resistance
Since current must return to the battery (or chassis ground), the **total effective wire length is $2L$** (forward + return path). Thus:

$$
R_{total} = 2 \cdot R_{wire} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \rho \cdot L}{A}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Ohm's Law
The voltage drop across the harness is:

$$
V_{drop} = I \cdot R_{total} = \dfrac{2 \cdot I \cdot \rho \cdot L}{A}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Cross-Sectional Area
Rearranging for $A$ to find the **minimum required conductor area**:

$$
A = \dfrac{2 \cdot I \cdot \rho \cdot L}{V_{drop,max}}
$$

### Step 5 — Standard Wire Size
Round $A$ **up** to the next standard metric wire size (e.g., 0.5, 0.75, 1.0, 1.5, 2.5, 4.0, 6.0, 10, 16 mm²).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Headlight Wire Sizing

**Problem:** A halogen headlamp rated at **55 W at 12 V** is connected to the battery through a wire of one-way length **3 m**. The maximum allowable voltage drop is **3% of 12 V**. Using copper conductors ($\rho = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega \cdot m$), determine the **minimum wire cross-section**.

### Step 1 — Compute Load Current
The headlamp draws:

$$
I = \dfrac{P}{V} = \dfrac{55\,W}{12\,V} = 4.583\,A
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Maximum Allowable Voltage Drop
Three percent of the system voltage:

$$
V_{drop,max} = 0.03 \times 12\,V = 0.36\,V
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Derived Formula

$$
A = \dfrac{2 \cdot I \cdot \rho \cdot L}{V_{drop,max}}
$$

Substitute all values:

$$
A = \dfrac{2 \times 4.583 \times (1.724 \times 10^{-8}) \times 3}{0.36}
$$

### Step 4 — Numerator

$$
2 \times 4.583 = 9.166
$$

$$
9.166 \times 1.724 \times 10^{-8} = 1.5802 \times 10^{-7}
$$

$$
1.5802 \times 10^{-7} \times 3 = 4.7406 \times 10^{-7}
$$

### Step 5 — Final Division

$$
A = \dfrac{4.7406 \times 10^{-7}}{0.36} = 1.317 \times 10^{-6}\,m^{2}
$$

### Step 6 — Convert to mm²
Since $1\,m^2 = 10^{6}\,mm^2$:

$$
A = 1.317 \times 10^{-6}\,m^{2} \times 10^{6}\,mm^{2}/m^{2} = 1.317\,mm^{2}
$$

### Step 7 — Select Standard Wire
The next standard size **above 1.317 mm² is 1.5 mm²**. So we select a **1.5 mm² copper wire** for this headlamp circuit.

### Step 8 — Verification
Actual voltage drop with 1.5 mm² wire:

$$
V_{drop} = \dfrac{2 \times 4.583 \times (1.724 \times 10^{-8}) \times 3}{1.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.316\,V
$$

$$
V_{drop}\% = \dfrac{0.316}{12} \times 100 = 2.63\% \quad (\text{✓} < 3\%)
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — CAN Bus Arbitration Priority

**Problem:** Three ECUs (Engine, Brakes, Body) start transmitting at the exact same instant with the following 11-bit identifiers in binary:
- **ECU 1 (Engine):** $ID_1 = 0b01101000000$ (hex $0x340$)
- **ECU 2 (Brakes):** $ID_2 = 0b01010000000$ (hex $0x280$)
- **ECU 3 (Body):** $ID_3 = 0b10100000000$ (hex $0x500$)

Determine which ECU wins arbitration and at which bit position.

### Step 1 — Understand the Rule
In CAN, **'0' is the Dominant bit** and **'1' is the Recessive bit**. The message with the **lowest numeric ID** wins because it has more leading dominant (0) bits.

### Step 2 — Compute Decimal Values

$$
ID_1 = 0b01101000000 = 0 \times 1024 + 1 \times 512 + 1 \times 256 + 0 \times 128 + 1 \times 64 + 0 \times 32 + 0 \times 16 + 0 \times 8 + 0 \times 4 + 0 \times 2 + 0 \times 1
$$

Computing bit-by-bit:

$$
ID_1 = 512 + 256 + 64 = 832
$$

$$
ID_2 = 0b01010000000 = 256 + 128 = 384
$$

$$
ID_3 = 0b10100000000 = 1024 + 128 = 1152
$$

### Step 3 — Compare Decimal Values

$$
ID_2 = 384 < ID_1 = 832 < ID_3 = 1152
$$

### Step 4 — Identify Winner
**ECU 2 (Brakes) has the lowest ID = 384, so it wins arbitration immediately** at the **first ID bit** because it starts with `0101...` which has a dominant bit at position where ECU 3 tries to send a dominant `1`. Wait — let us re-examine bit-by-bit:

| Bit Position | ID1 (Engine) | ID2 (Brakes) | ID3 (Body) | Bus Result | Loser |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bit 1 (MSB) | 0 (D) | 0 (D) | 1 (R) | 0 | ECU 3 backs off at bit 1 |
| Bit 2 | 1 (R) | 1 (R) | — | 1 | ECU 1, ECU 2 continue |
| Bit 3 | 1 (R) | 0 (D) | — | 0 | ECU 1 backs off at bit 3 |
| Bit 4 | 0 (D) | 0 (D) | — | 0 | ECU 2 wins |

### Step 5 — Conclusion
- **ECU 3 (Body)** loses arbitration at **bit 1** (sent recessive 1, bus showed dominant 0).
- **ECU 1 (Engine)** loses arbitration at **bit 3** (sent recessive 1, bus showed dominant 0).
- **ECU 2 (Brakes)** wins arbitration and proceeds to transmit the full frame.

This demonstrates **non-destructive bitwise arbitration** — ECU 2's message is delivered intact, and the losers retry after the bus becomes free.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — CAN Bus Load Calculation

**Problem:** A CAN bus operates at **500 kbit/s**. The system transmits **50 data frames per second**, each with **8 bytes of data** and a total stuffed frame length of **130 bits**. Calculate the **bus utilization percentage**.

### Step 1 — Define Bus Load
Bus load $\eta$ is the fraction of total available bus time consumed by message transmission:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{N_{tx} \cdot L_{frame}}{B} \times 100\%
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$
\eta = \dfrac{50 \cdot 130}{500{,}000} \times 100\%
$$

### Step 3 — Numerator

$$
50 \times 130 = 6{,}500\ bits/s
$$

### Step 4 — Final Calculation

$$
\eta = \dfrac{6{,}500}{500{,}000} \times 100\% = 1.30\%
$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation
The bus is only **1.3% loaded**, leaving **98.7% headroom** for additional ECUs, error frames, and retries. This is a **healthy, well-designed CAN network**. Engineers typically aim for **$\eta < 70\%$** to ensure timing margins.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix — CAN Bus vs LIN Bus vs FlexRay vs Ethernet

| Feature | **CAN 2.0** | **CAN FD** | **LIN** | **FlexRay** | **Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Speed** | 1 Mbit/s | 5 Mbit/s | 20 kbit/s | 10 Mbit/s | 100 Mbit/s – 1 Gbit/s |
| **Wires** | 2 (twisted) | 2 (twisted) | 1 | 2 or 4 | 1 (twisted pair) |
| **Topology** | Multi-drop | Multi-drop | Single master/slave | Star / hybrid | Star / switched |
| **Max Nodes** | ~30 (practical) | ~30 (practical) | 16 | 22 | Hundreds |
| **Payload (max)** | 8 bytes | 64 bytes | 8 bytes | 254 bytes | 1500 bytes |
| **Determinism** | Event-triggered | Event-triggered | Schedule-based | Time-triggered (TDMA) | Best-effort (improving with TSN) |
| **Cost** | Low | Low | Very low | Medium-High | Medium |
| **Application** | Powertrain, Body | ADAS, Gateway | Window, Mirror | Drive-by-wire | Infotainment, ADAS, Diagnostics |
| **Standard** | ISO 11898 | ISO 11898-1:2015 | ISO 17987 | ISO 17458 | IEEE 802.3bw / 802.3bp |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** Modern vehicles use a **multi-bus architecture** — CAN for chassis/powertrain, CAN FD for ADAS, LIN for simple body nodes, and Automotive Ethernet for high-bandwidth infotainment and camera/radar data.

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    end

    subgraph PDC["Power Distribution Center"]

    subgraph ECU_NET["In-Vehicle CAN Bus Network (500 kbps)"]
        CANH["CAN-H Wire (Twisted)"]
        CANL["CAN-L Wire (Twisted)"]

    end

    subgraph ECUS["Electronic Control Units"]
        ECU1["ECM\nEngine Control"]
        ECU2["TCM\nTransmission Control"]
        ECU3["ABS_ECU\nAnti-lock Brakes"]
        ECU4["BCM\nBody Control"]
        ECU5["IC\nInstrument Cluster"]
        ECU6["HVAC\nClimate Control"]
    end

    subgraph ACTUATORS["Actuators and Sensors"]
        SENS["Sensors\nCKP, MAF, TPMS, Wheel Speed"]
        ACT["Actuators\nInjectors, Solenoids, Motors"]
    end

    subgraph GATEWAY["Central Gateway ECU"]
        GW["CAN-to-Ethernet\nCAN-to-LIN\nDiagnostic Bridge"]
        OBD["OBD-II Port\nJ1962 Connector"]
    end

    BATT --> FUSEBOX
    FUSEBOX --> MAINHARNESS
    MAINHARNESS --> ECU1
    MAINHARNESS --> ECU2
    MAINHARNESS --> ECU3
    MAINHARNESS --> ECU4
    MAINHARNESS --> ECU5
    MAINHARNESS --> ECU6

    ECU1 --- CANH
    ECU2 --- CANH
    ECU3 --- CANH
    ECU4 --- CANH
    ECU5 --- CANH
    ECU6 --- CANH
    GW --- CANH

    ECU1 --- CANL
    ECU2 --- CANL
    ECU3 --- CANL
    ECU4 --- CANL
    ECU5 --- CANL
    ECU6 --- CANL
    GW --- CANL

    CANH --- TERM1
    CANL --- TERM2

    ECU1 --> SENS

    ECU2 --> ACT
    ECU3 --> SENS
    ECU4 --> ACT
    ECU5 --> SENS

    GW --> OBD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Power flows top-down (Battery → PDC → ECUs). Data flows horizontally along the two CAN wires (CAN-H and CAN-L), which are terminated at each end by 120 Ω resistors. The Central Gateway bridges CAN to other protocols (LIN, Ethernet) and exposes the OBD-II diagnostic port.

---

## 4.2 CAN Data Frame Structure — Bit-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SF["CAN Standard Data Frame (11-bit ID)"]
        A["SOF\n1 bit"]
        B["Arbitration Field\n12 bits\n11-bit ID + RTR"]
        C["Control Field\n6 bits\nIDE + r0 + DLC 4 bits"]
        D["Data Field\n0 to 64 bits\n0 to 8 bytes"]
        E["CRC Field\n16 bits\n15-bit CRC + 1 delimiter"]
        F["ACK Field\n2 bits\nACK + delimiter"]
        G["EOF\n7 bits\n7 recessive"]
        H["IFS\n3 bits\nInter-Frame Space"]
    end

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H

    subgraph LEGEND["Legend"]
        L1["SOF: Start of Frame"]
        L2["RTR: Remote Transmission Request"]
        L3["IDE: Identifier Extension"]
        L4["DLC: Data Length Code (0-8)"]
        L5["CRC: Cyclic Redundancy Check"]
        L6["ACK: Acknowledgement from receiver"]
        L7["EOF: End of Frame"]
        L8["IFS: Inter-Frame Space"]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 CAN Bus Differential Signaling — Voltage Levels

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RECESSIVE["Recessive State (Logic 1, Bus Idle)"]
        R1["CAN-H = 2.5 V"]
        R2["CAN-L = 2.5 V"]
        R3["Vdiff = 0 V"]
    end

    subgraph DOMINANT["Dominant State (Logic 0, Active Transmission)"]
        D1["CAN-H = 3.5 V"]
        D2["CAN-L = 1.5 V"]
        D3["Vdiff = +2.0 V"]
    end

    NOISE["Common-Mode Noise (e.g., +1 V spike)"]
    NOISE -.affects both wires equally.-> RECESSIVE
    NOISE -.affects both wires equally.-> DOMINANT

    R1 --- R2 --- R3
    D1 --- D2 --- D3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The differential receiver computes $V_{diff} = V_{CANH} - V_{CANL}$. Since any EMI noise appears equally on both lines, the **difference remains unchanged**, giving CAN its legendary noise immunity — critical in the electrically hostile automotive environment (ignition, motors, alternators).

---

## 4.4 Process Flow — CAN Message Transmission and Arbitration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["ECU wants to send message"]
    P1["Wait until bus is recessive (idle)"]
    P2["Begin transmitting SOF (dominant bit)"]
    P3["Transmit ID + RTR bit-by-bit"]
    P4{"At each bit: Read bus while transmitting"}
    P5A["Sent = Dominant, Bus = Dominant → Continue"]
    P5B["Sent = Recessive, Bus = Dominant → LOST arbitration, back off"]
    P5C["Sent = Dominant, Bus = Recessive → ERROR (should not happen)"]
    P5D["Sent = Recessive, Bus = Recessive → Continue"]
    P6["Won arbitration: Continue with Control, Data, CRC, ACK, EOF fields"]
    P7["Frame complete: Release bus"]
    P8["Loser ECU retries after 3 IFS bit times"]

    P0 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5A --> P6
    P4 --> P5B --> P8
    P4 --> P5C --> P6
    P4 --> P5D --> P6
    P6 --> P7
    P8 --> P1
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — Definition
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** What is a **CAN bus**? State the two physical wires used and their nominal voltages in the **dominant** and **recessive** states.

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)
- **Definition (1 Mark):** CAN (Controller Area Network) is a multi-master, serial communication protocol standardized under **ISO 11898**, used for real-time communication between ECUs in vehicles without a host computer.
- **Wires (1 Mark):** The bus uses two wires: **CAN-H (High)** and **CAN-L (Low)**, terminated by **120 Ω resistors** at each end.
- **Voltages (1 Mark):**
  - **Recessive (Logic 1):** CAN-H = **2.5 V**, CAN-L = **2.5 V**, differential = **0 V**.
  - **Dominant (Logic 0):** CAN-H = **3.5 V**, CAN-L = **1.5 V**, differential = **+2.0 V**.

---

### Question 2 — Components / Concept
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** List any **six main components** of an automotive electric wiring harness and state the function of any **two** of them.

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)
Six main components (1½ Marks): **Wires/Cables, Connectors, Terminals, Fuses, Relays, Conduits/Sleeves, Junction Boxes, Grommets, Clamps** (any six).

Function of any two (1½ Marks):
- **Connectors:** Join segments of the harness, allowing modular assembly and easy servicing.
- **Fuses:** Protect circuits from overcurrent by melting and breaking the circuit when current exceeds a safe threshold.
- **Relays:** Allow a low-current control signal to switch high-current loads, reducing switch size and wiring.
- **Terminals:** Provide the metal contact interface between wire and connector pin for reliable electrical connection.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B (ESE Module) questions are for **14 marks** with sub-parts typically split **7 + 7**. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — CAN Bus Deep Dive
**`[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** Explain the **architecture of the CAN bus protocol** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the function of the **physical layer, data link layer, and application layer** with reference to the **OSI model**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat timing diagram, explain the **CSMA/CD + AMP arbitration mechanism** of CAN bus. Show what happens when two ECUs with IDs `0x123` and `0x456` transmit simultaneously. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer

**(a) CAN Architecture & OSI Mapping (7 Marks)**

The CAN protocol architecture is mapped to the **OSI 7-layer model** as follows:

| OSI Layer | CAN Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Layer 7 — Application** | User-defined (e.g., UDS, CANopen, J1939) | What data means — diagnostics, vehicle-specific messages |
| **Layer 6 — Presentation** | Not used in CAN | — |
| **Layer 5 — Session** | Not used in CAN | — |
| **Layer 4 — Transport** | Not used in CAN | — |
| **Layer 3 — Network** | Not used in CAN | — |
| **Layer 2 — Data Link** | **CAN Data Link Layer** (ISO 11898-1) | Frame formatting, arbitration, error detection, acknowledgement, bit stuffing |
| **Layer 1 — Physical** | **CAN Physical Layer** (ISO 11898-2/3) | Differential signaling on CAN-H and CAN-L, bit timing, synchronization, bus termination |

**Physical Layer (2 Marks):** Uses differential voltage signaling on two wires. Nominal bit rate up to 1 Mbit/s. Bus length inversely proportional to baud rate. Each end of the bus is terminated with a **120 Ω resistor** matching the characteristic impedance of the twisted pair, eliminating signal reflections.

**Data Link Layer (2 Marks):** Defines four frame types (Data, Remote, Error, Overload). Performs **bit stuffing** (inserts opposite bit after 5 identical bits). Implements **CSMA/CD + AMP** for non-destructive arbitration. Detects errors via **CRC, bit stuffing rule, ACK, and form checks**. On error, an **Error Frame** is transmitted and the corrupted message is automatically retransmitted.

**Application Layer (1 Mark):** Vehicle-specific protocols like **SAE J1939** (heavy vehicles), **CANopen** (industrial), **OBD-II / UDS** (diagnostics) run on top of CAN.

**Block Diagram (2 Marks):** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the multi-ECU CAN bus block diagram.

---

**(b) CSMA/CD + AMP Arbitration (7 Marks)**

**Principle (2 Marks):** CAN uses **Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection and Arbitration on Message Priority (CSMA/CD + AMP)**. It is a **multi-master, broadcast bus** — any ECU can transmit when the bus is idle, and all ECUs receive every message (filtering by ID).

**Bitwise Non-Destructive Arbitration (3 Marks):**
- When two or more ECUs start transmitting simultaneously, they monitor the bus **bit-by-bit** while they transmit.
- If a node sends a **recessive (1)** bit but reads back a **dominant (0)** bit, it has lost arbitration and immediately **stops transmitting** (becomes a receiver).
- The node with the **lowest numerical ID** (which has the most leading dominant 0s) wins because **0 is dominant**.
- The losing message is **not corrupted** — only the loser backs off and retries after the winning frame finishes.

**Worked Example (2 Marks):** Two ECUs transmit simultaneously:
- ECU A: ID = `0x123` = `001 0010 0011` (binary, 11 bits)
- ECU B: ID = `0x456` = `100 0101 0110` (binary, 11 bits)

| Bit | ECU A Sends | ECU B Sends | Bus Reads | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 (D) | 1 (R) | 0 (D) | ECU A |
| 2 | 0 (D) | 0 (D) | 0 (D) | Continue |
| 3 | 1 (R) | 0 (D) | 0 (D) | ECU B |
| 4 | 0 (D) | 0 (D) | 0 (D) | Continue |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

ECU A loses at bit 3 (sent recessive, read dominant). **ECU B wins arbitration** because `0x123` > `0x456` in numeric value (291 vs. 1110) — wait, `0x123 = 291` and `0x456 = 1110`. So **ECU A (ID 0x123) has the lower ID and wins**. The student must show that **0x123 < 0x456**, so **ECU A wins** without any data loss.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Wiring Harness Engineering
**`[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **main components of an automotive wiring harness**. Discuss the factors considered in the **design and routing** of a wiring harness in a modern vehicle. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **rear window defogger** rated at **120 W at 12 V** is to be wired from the battery located **4 m away**. Using copper conductors ($\rho = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega \cdot m$) and a **maximum allowable voltage drop of 3%**, determine the **minimum wire cross-section** in mm². **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer

**(a) Wiring Harness Components & Design (7 Marks)**

**Components Diagram & Description (3 Marks):** Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1. Key components include: **wires/cables, connectors, terminals, fuses, fusible links, relays, junction boxes, conduits/sleeves, grommets, and clips/brackets**.

**Design Factors (4 Marks):**
1. **Current Carrying Capacity** — Wire must be sized so its ampacity exceeds the maximum steady-state load current by a safety margin (~25%).
2. **Voltage Drop** — Maximum 3% in power circuits, 0.5 V in signal circuits; this dictates the minimum cross-section.
3. **Temperature Rating** — Insulation must withstand ambient + self-heating; engine bay wires need **125°C or 150°C** rated insulation (XLPE, silicone, Tefzel).
4. **EMI/EMC** — CAN and sensor signal wires must be **twisted** (≥ 1 twist/inch) and sometimes **shielded**; routed away from ignition and motor noise sources.
5. **Mechanical Protection** — Routing must avoid sharp edges, moving parts, heat sources (exhaust > 200°C); use grommets when passing through body panels.
6. **Serviceability** — Connectors placed at logical service boundaries; use modular sub-harnesses (engine, dashboard, door, rear).
7. **Waterproofing** — Use **IP67** connectors in wet zones (engine bay, underbody); **IP54** in cabin; **IP00** in protected zones.
8. **Weight & Cost Optimization** — Use aluminum for long power runs; shortest possible routing; right-sized components.
9. **Standards Compliance** — Follow **ISO 6722** (road vehicle wire), **LV 112** (conductor), **USCAR-2** (connector performance).

---

**(b) Wire Sizing Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute Load Current (1 Mark):**

$$
I = \dfrac{P}{V} = \dfrac{120\,W}{12\,V} = 10\,A
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Allowable Voltage Drop (1 Mark):**

$$
V_{drop,max} = 0.03 \times 12\,V = 0.36\,V
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Cross-Section Formula (2 Marks):**

$$
A = \dfrac{2 \cdot I \cdot \rho \cdot L}{V_{drop,max}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Values (1 Mark):**

$$
A = \dfrac{2 \times 10 \times (1.724 \times 10^{-8}) \times 4}{0.36}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate Numerator (1 Mark):**

$$
2 \times 10 \times 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \times 4 = 1.3792 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Calculation (1 Mark):**

$$
A = \dfrac{1.3792 \times 10^{-6}}{0.36} = 3.831 \times 10^{-6}\,m^2 = 3.831\,mm^{2}
$$

**Step 7 — Select Standard Wire:** Next standard size above 3.831 mm² is **4.0 mm²**. So select a **4.0 mm² copper wire**.

**Verification:**

$$
V_{drop} = \dfrac{2 \times 10 \times 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \times 4}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.3448\,V
$$

$$
V_{drop}\% = \dfrac{0.3448}{12} \times 100 = 2.87\% \quad (\text{✓} < 3\%)
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (Read Carefully Before Exam!)**
> 1. **Confusing Dominant/Recessive with Logic 0/1 polarity.** In CAN, **'0' = Dominant** and **'1' = Recessive**. The bus acts as a "wired-AND" — any node pulling the bus to dominant wins. Many students write "dominant = 1" — this is **WRONG** and costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the round-trip factor (2L) in wire sizing.** Current flows OUT through the wire and RETURNS through the ground/chassis. Total conductor length is **2L**, not **L**. This alone can cause a 50% undersizing error.
> 3. **Mis-stating baud rate vs bus length.** Many students say "CAN works at 1 Mbit/s at any length." WRONG. The relationship is **inverse**: 1 Mbit/s → 40 m max, 500 kbit/s → 100 m, etc. Always quote the **standard table**.
> 4. **Writing "CAN uses 4 wires" or "CAN is single-wire."** The standard High-Speed CAN uses **exactly 2 wires (CAN-H + CAN-L)**. Single-wire CAN exists only for low-speed body control in specific OEM applications — not the standard ISO 11898-2 CAN.
> 5. **Skipping the unit conversion in wire sizing.** The result of $\rho L / A$ comes out in **m²**, must be converted to **mm²** by multiplying by $10^6$. Many students leave the answer in m² and lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Not mentioning ISO 11898.** KTU examiners reward students who cite the governing standard. Always mention **ISO 11898-1, 11898-2, 11898-3** in CAN-related answers.
> 7. **Confusing CAN FD with Classical CAN.** CAN FD (Flexible Data Rate) is an **extension** introduced by Bosch in 2012 — it allows payloads up to 64 bytes and bit rates up to 5 Mbit/s in the data phase. If the question says "CAN FD," mention both these advantages.
> 8. **Drawing arbitration as destructive.** Some students write that messages get "destroyed" during collision. CAN arbitration is **non-destructive** — emphasize this with a clear example.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Page Rapid Revision — Print This Before Exam!**

### Wiring Harness Essentials
- ✔ Wiring harness = integrated assembly of **wires + connectors + terminals + fuses + relays + conduits**
- ✔ Modern cars contain **1.5+ km of wiring** and **600+ connectors**
- ✔ Wire sizing formula: $A = \dfrac{2 \cdot I \cdot \rho \cdot L}{V_{drop,max}}$ (round-trip $2L$)
- ✔ Maximum **3% voltage drop** in power circuits, **0.5 V** in signal circuits
- ✔ Use **copper** ($1.724 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega \cdot m$) for most circuits; **aluminum** for long power runs to save weight
- ✔ Insulation ratings: **PVC (105°C), XLPE (125°C), Silicone (180°C), Tefzel/Teflon (200°C+)** for engine bay
- ✔ Sealing: **IP67** (engine bay), **IP54** (cabin), **IP00** (protected interior)
