- ✔ Standard wire sizes: **0.35, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0, 1.5, 2.5, 4.0, 6.0, 10, 16, 25, 35, 50, 70 mm²**

### CAN Bus Essentials
- ✔ CAN = **Controller Area Network**, standardized as **ISO 11898**, developed by **Bosch in 1986**
- ✔ **Two-wire differential bus**: CAN-H + CAN-L, terminated with **120 Ω** at each end
- ✔ **Recessive (1):** CAN-H = 2.5 V, CAN-L = 2.5 V, V_diff = **0 V**
- ✔ **Dominant (0):** CAN-H = 3.5 V, CAN-L = 1.5 V, V_diff = **+2.0 V**
- ✔ **'0' is Dominant** — bus behaves as wired-AND
- ✔ **Multi-master, broadcast, message-oriented** — no host computer
- ✔ **Non-destructive bitwise arbitration** via CSMA/CD + AMP — lowest ID wins
- ✔ **Standard ID: 11 bits (2048 IDs)**, **Extended ID: 29 bits (536 million IDs)**
- ✔ **Bit stuffing:** After **5 identical bits**, a stuff bit of opposite polarity is inserted
- ✔ **Max payload:** Classical CAN = **8 bytes**; CAN FD = **64 bytes**
- ✔ **Baud rate vs length:** 1 Mbit/s → 40 m; 500 kbit/s → 100 m; 250 kbit/s → 250 m
- ✔ **Error detection:** 15-bit CRC + bit stuffing check + ACK check + form check
- ✔ **Error types:** Bit Error, Stuff Error, CRC Error, Form Error, ACK Error
- ✔ **Error handling:** Bit monitoring, Bit stuffing rule, Message validation, Automatic retransmission
- ✔ **Bus load formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{N_{tx} \cdot L_{frame}}{B} \times 100\%$, target **< 70%**
- ✔ **Five CAN frame types:** Data, Remote, Error, Overload, Interframe space

### Multi-Bus Vehicle Architecture (Modern Cars)
- ✔ **Powertrain/Chassis:** Classical CAN 2.0 (500 kbit/s)
- ✔ **ADAS / Gateway:** CAN FD (2–5 Mbit/s)
- ✔ **Body / Comfort:** LIN (20 kbit/s) for low-cost nodes
- ✔ **Infotainment / Camera / Radar:** Automotive Ethernet (100 Mbit/s – 1 Gbit/s)
- ✔ **Drive-by-Wire (x-by-wire):** FlexRay (10 Mbit/s, time-triggered)

### Key Acronyms to Memorize
- **ECU** — Electronic Control Unit
- **CAN** — Controller Area Network
- **EWH** — Electric Wiring Harness
- **OBD-II** — On-Board Diagnostics, Generation 2
- **CSMA/CD + AMP** — Carrier Sense Multiple Access / Collision Detection + Arbitration on Message Priority
- **CRC** — Cyclic Redundancy Check
- **DLC** — Data Length Code
- **SOF/EOF/IFS** — Start / End / Inter-Frame Space
- **EMI/EMC** — Electromagnetic Interference / Compatibility
- **UDS** — Unified Diagnostic Services (over CAN)
- **TDMA** — Time Division Multiple Access (used in FlexRay)
- **TSN** — Time-Sensitive Networking (Ethernet in cars)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiplex Wiring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT612 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Multiplex Wiring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Multiplex Wiring** (also called **Automotive Multiplexing** or **In-Vehicle Networking**) is a communication methodology in modern automobiles where multiple data signals from various sensors, actuators, and Electronic Control Units (ECUs) are transmitted over a **single shared communication channel** (bus) using time-division or frequency-division multiplexing techniques. It replaces the conventional point-to-point (star) wiring harness architecture with a **serial bus topology** governed by standardized protocols such as **CAN (Controller Area Network — ISO 11898)**, **LIN (Local Interconnect Network — ISO 17987)**, **FlexRay (ISO 17458)**, **MOST (Media Oriented Systems Transport)**, and **Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1 / 1000BASE-T1)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Multiplex wiring is the *enabling backbone* of all modern vehicle electronics. Without it, features like ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems), drive-by-wire, OTA (Over-The-Air) updates, and integrated infotainment would be physically and economically impossible to deploy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Conference Call" Model

Imagine an office where every employee needs to talk to every other employee. In the **traditional wiring model**, you would need a **separate dedicated phone line** between every pair of employees. If there are 50 employees, that is `50 × 49 / 2 = 1225` separate physical wires — a logistical nightmare.

In the **multiplexed model**, all employees share a **single conference call line**. Each person is assigned a **unique identifier (ID/address)** and only speaks when it is their turn (a technique called *Time-Division Multiple Access* — TDMA) or only speaks when the line is free and they raise their hand (*Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Resolution* — CSMA/CR, used by CAN).

| Aspect | Traditional Wiring (Star) | Multiplex Wiring (Bus) |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Wires | Very High (`n(n-1)/2` trend) | Just **2 wires** (twisted pair) |
| Weight | Heavy copper harness | ~**70% lighter** |
| Cost | High material + labor | Lower per node |
| Scalability | Poor | Excellent (plug-and-play ECUs) |
| Fault Diagnosis | Manual tracing | OBD-II self-diagnostics |

## 1.3 Standardized Multiplex Protocols — Quick Reference

- **CAN (Controller Area Network):** Dominant powertrain and chassis bus. Speed: **125 kbps to 1 Mbps**. Standard: **ISO 11898**.
- **LIN (Local Interconnect Network):** Low-cost sub-bus for mirrors, seats, windows. Speed: **up to 19.2 kbps**. Standard: **ISO 17987**.
- **FlexRay:** High-speed deterministic bus for X-by-wire. Speed: **up to 10 Mbps**. Standard: **ISO 17458**.
- **MOST:** Infotainment-oriented (audio/video streaming). Speed: **25 to 150 Mbps**.
- **Automotive Ethernet:** Backbone of next-gen vehicles. Speed: **100 Mbps to 1 Gbps**. Standards: **100BASE-T1, 1000BASE-T1 (IEEE 802.3bw/bp)**.

> [!NOTE]
> A modern premium vehicle (e.g., a BMW 7-Series or Mercedes S-Class) contains approximately **80–100 ECUs** connected via **5 to 7 different multiplex buses** running simultaneously across various domains (Powertrain, Chassis, Body, Infotainment, ADAS, Diagnostics).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

A multiplex wiring system operates through **four distinct functional layers**, mirroring the OSI 7-layer model but simplified for automotive use:

1. **Physical Layer (Layer 1):** The actual copper twisted pair, fiber optic cable, or differential copper pair. Voltage levels: **CAN-H = 3.5 V**, **CAN-L = 1.5 V** in dominant state; both ≈ **2.5 V** in recessive (idle) state. Termination resistors of **120 Ω** are placed at both ends of the bus.
2. **Data Link Layer (Layer 2):** Implements the **CSMA/CR + ACK** arbitration. Every message has an **11-bit (Standard CAN 2.0A)** or **29-bit (Extended CAN 2.0B) arbitration ID**. The ECU with the *lowest ID wins* arbitration and transmits.
3. **Network Layer (Layer 3):** Routing of messages between buses via a **Gateway ECU**. For example, the Powertrain CAN and Body CAN cannot talk directly — a central gateway filters, translates, and forwards relevant messages.
4. **Application Layer (Layer 7):** The actual sensor data, actuator commands, and diagnostic services (UDS — Unified Diagnostic Services over CAN, ISO 14229).

### 2.1.1 The "Why" Behind Multiplexing — The Wire Count Problem

In a modern mid-segment car:

$$N_{\text{traditional}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n_{\text{ECU}}} M_i$$

where $M_i$ is the number of sensors/actuators wired to ECU $i$. With **~80 ECUs** and an average of **5–10 wires per ECU**, the loom can exceed **1500 individual conductors**, weighing **> 60 kg** and measuring **> 3 km** in length.

With multiplexing, this reduces to **2 twisted-pair wires per bus segment** plus gateway connectors.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Parameter | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $R_{\text{term}}$ | $R_{\text{term}} = 120\ \Omega$ | CAN bus termination resistance at each end | Ω |
| $V_{\text{diff}}$ | $V_{\text{diff}} = V_{\text{CAN-H}} - V_{\text{CAN-L}}$ | Differential voltage (dominant state) | V |
| $V_{\text{diff,dom}}$ | $V_{\text{diff,dom}} = 3.5 - 1.5 = 2.0\ \text{V}$ | Differential voltage in dominant state | V |
| $V_{\text{diff,rec}}$ | $V_{\text{diff,rec}} \approx 0\ \text{V}$ | Differential voltage in recessive state | V |
| $f_{\text{bus}}$ | $f_{\text{bus}} = 1 / T_{\text{bit}}$ | Bus bit frequency | Hz |
| $T_{\text{bit}}$ | $T_{\text{bit}} = T_q \times N_{\text{bits/quanta}}$ | Bit time | s |
| $L_{\text{util}}$ | $L_{\text{util}} = \dfrac{\text{Bus Active Time}}{\text{Total Time}} \times 100\%$ | Bus load / utilization | % |
| $N_{\text{frame}}$ | $N_{\text{frame,bytes}} = 8$ (CAN), $N_{\text{frame,bytes}} = 254$ (CAN-FD) | Data bytes per frame | bytes |
| $R_{\text{eff,max}}$ | $R_{\text{eff,max}} = 60\ \text{m (CAN @ 1 Mbps)}$ | Maximum bus length | m |
| $\Delta v$ | $\Delta v = v_{\text{prop}} \times t_{\text{prop}}$ | Signal propagation distance | m |
| $BW_{\text{req}}$ | $BW_{\text{req}} = \sum (\text{frame\_len} \times \text{frame\_rate})$ | Aggregate bandwidth requirement | bps |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The **120 Ω termination** and the **2.0 V differential dominant voltage** are the two most frequently asked numerical values in KTU exams. Memorize them.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Weight & Emissions Reduction:** A 50 kg reduction in wiring harness saves approximately **6 g/km of CO₂** over the vehicle lifecycle — directly helping manufacturers meet **BS-VI / Euro 7** norms.
2. **Functional Safety (ISO 26262):** Multiplex buses enable redundant communication paths (e.g., dual-channel FlexRay) for ASIL-D safety functions like electric power steering and brake-by-wire.
3. **OTA Updates:** Modern software-defined vehicles (SDVs) push firmware updates over the CAN/Ethernet bus — impossible without multiplexing.
4. **Diagnostics:** **OBD-II (On-Board Diagnostics, ISO 15031)** port reads fault codes (DTCs) directly from the multiplex bus — a foundational tool for service technicians.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Multiplex wiring is fundamentally a *systems engineering* topic rather than a pure mathematics topic. Therefore, the "derivations" below are presented as **analytical derivations of design parameters** — bus load calculation, propagation delay, and frame timing — which are the calculations most likely to appear in KTU numerical questions.

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Bus Length vs Bit Rate

The maximum achievable bus length in a CAN network is **inversely proportional** to the bit rate, governed by the propagation delay of the signal across the physical medium.

**Step 1 — Define propagation velocity:**
Signal travels on a twisted-pair cable at approximately **$v_{\text{prop}} = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s** (about 2/3 the speed of light).

**Step 2 — Define bit time:**
$$T_{\text{bit}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{bus}}}$$

**Step 3 — Arbitration constraint:**
For arbitration to function correctly, the signal must propagate to the farthest node and back *within one bit time*. The round-trip constraint is:

$$2 \cdot t_{\text{prop}} \leq T_{\text{bit}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute propagation delay:**
$$2 \cdot \frac{L_{\text{max}}}{v_{\text{prop}}} \leq \frac{1}{f_{\text{bus}}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for maximum bus length:**
$$L_{\text{max}} \leq \frac{v_{\text{prop}}}{2 \cdot f_{\text{bus}}}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Example (KTU Style):**
Given $f_{\text{bus}} = 500\ \text{kbps}$ and $v_{\text{prop}} = 2 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$:

$$
L_{\text{max}} \leq \frac{2 \times 10^8}{2 \times 500 \times 10^3}
$$

$$
L_{\text{max}} \leq \frac{2 \times 10^8}{10^6} = 200\ \text{m}
$$

In practice, **CAN @ 500 kbps is limited to ~100 m** due to additional delays from transceivers and connectors (safety factor ≈ 2).

## 3.2 Derivation: Bus Load / Utilization Factor

**Step 1 — Count messages per second:**
Suppose a CAN bus carries the following messages:

| Message | ID (hex) | Length (bytes) | Rate (Hz) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Engine RPM | 0x100 | 8 | 100 |
| Vehicle Speed | 0x101 | 8 | 50 |
| Brake Pressure | 0x102 | 4 | 200 |
| Steering Angle | 0x103 | 6 | 100 |

**Step 2 — CAN frame structure (Standard CAN 2.0A):**
- SOF: 1 bit
- Arbitration field (11-bit ID + RTR): 12 bits
- Control field (IDE, r0, DLC): 6 bits
- Data field: $N \times 8$ bits
- CRC field (15 bits + delimiter): 16 bits
- ACK field (2 bits): 2 bits
- EOF (7 bits): 7 bits
- Inter-frame space (3 bits): 3 bits

$$\text{Total bits per frame} = 47 + (N \times 8)$$

where $N$ is the number of data bytes.

**Step 3 — Calculate bits per second for each message:**

$$
\text{Frame}_{\text{Engine RPM}} = 47 + (8 \times 8) = 111\ \text{bits}
$$

$$
\text{Bits/sec}_{\text{Engine RPM}} = 111 \times 100 = 11{,}100\ \text{bps}
$$

Applying this to all messages:

$$
\text{Bits/sec}_{\text{Speed}} = (47 + 64) \times 50 = 5550\ \text{bps}
$$

$$
\text{Bits/sec}_{\text{Brake}} = (47 + 32) \times 200 = 15{,}800\ \text{bps}
$$

$$
\text{Bits/sec}_{\text{Steering}} = (47 + 48) \times 100 = 9500\ \text{bps}
$$

**Step 4 — Aggregate bus traffic:**
$$
B_{\text{total}} = 11{,}100 + 5550 + 15{,}800 + 9500 = 41{,}950\ \text{bps}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute bus load:**
For a bus operating at $f_{\text{bus}} = 250\ \text{kbps} = 250{,}000\ \text{bps}$:

$$
L_{\text{util}} = \frac{B_{\text{total}}}{f_{\text{bus}}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
L_{\text{util}} = \frac{41{,}950}{250{,}000} \times 100\% = 16.78\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** Bus load should generally be kept **below 30–40%** to allow margin for error frames, retransmissions, and future feature additions. A load above **70%** indicates a serious design flaw.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Signal Voltage Levels on CAN Bus

| Bus State | $V_{\text{CAN-H}}$ | $V_{\text{CAN-L}}$ | $V_{\text{diff}}$ | Logic Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dominant (logic 0) | **3.5 V** | **1.5 V** | **2.0 V** | Active bit |
| Recessive (logic 1) | **2.5 V** | **2.5 V** | **0 V** | Idle bit |
| Fault / Shorted to GND | 0 V | 0 V | 0 V | Stuck recessive |
| Fault / Shorted to Vbat | 5 V (or 12 V) | 5 V (or 12 V) | 0 V | Stuck recessive |

This differential signaling is the key reason CAN is **highly immune to common-mode noise** — both wires pick up the same interference, but the *difference* between them cancels it out.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Automotive Multiplex Network Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[OBD2 Diagnostic Port] --> nodeGateway
    nodeB[Engine ECU] --> nodePowertrainCAN
    nodeC[Transmission ECU] --> nodePowertrainCAN
    nodeD[ABS ECU] --> nodeChassisCAN
    nodeE[Steering ECU] --> nodeChassisCAN
    nodeF[Body Control Module] --> nodeBodyCAN
    nodeG[Infotainment ECU] --> nodeMOSTBus
    nodeH[ADAS ECU] --> nodeEthernetBus
    nodePowertrainCAN[Powertrain CAN Bus 500 kbps] --> nodeGateway
    nodeChassisCAN[Chassis CAN Bus 500 kbps] --> nodeGateway
    nodeBodyCAN[Body CAN Bus 125 kbps] --> nodeGateway
    nodeMOSTBus[MOST Infotainment Bus] --> nodeGateway
    nodeEthernetBus[Automotive Ethernet 1 Gbps] --> nodeGateway
    nodeGateway[Central Gateway ECU] --> nodeNet
    nodeNet[In Vehicle Network Backbone]
    nodeTermA[120 Ohm Termination] --- nodePowertrainCAN
    nodeTermB[120 Ohm Termination] --- nodePowertrainCAN
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — CAN Message Arbitration Process

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant ECU1 as Engine ECU ID 0x100
    participant ECU2 as Brake ECU ID 0x050
    participant BUS as CAN Bus Twisted Pair
    Note over ECU1, ECU2: Both ECUs start transmission simultaneously
    ECU1->>BUS: SOF bit dominant
    ECU2->>BUS: SOF bit dominant
    Note over BUS: Bit 1 ID Recessive vs Dominant
    ECU1->>BUS: ID bit 1 recessive
    ECU2->>BUS: ID bit 1 dominant
    ECU2->>BUS: Wins arbitration lower ID
    ECU1->>BUS: Backs off and retries
    ECU2->>BUS: Completes full frame transmission
    ECU2->>BUS: ACK slot receivers acknowledge
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Multiplexed vs Traditional Wiring Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Vehicle Electronic Architecture Design]
    nodeStart --> nodeQ1{Number of ECUs greater than 10?}
    nodeQ1 --> nodeNo[Use traditional point to point wiring]
    nodeQ1 --> nodeYes[Adopt multiplex wiring]
    nodeYes --> nodeQ2{Safety critical functions present?}
    nodeQ2 --> nodeYes2[Use FlexRay or dual CAN redundancy]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeNo2[Use standard CAN bus]
    nodeYes2 --> nodeEth[Consider Ethernet backbone for ADAS]
    nodeNo2 --> nodeLin[Add LIN sub bus for low speed actuators]
    nodeEth --> nodeFinal[Optimized Multiplex Architecture]
    nodeLin --> nodeFinal
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**  
**Define multiplex wiring. List any two advantages over conventional wiring.**

**Model Answer:**
Multiplex wiring is a communication technique in which multiple electrical signals from different sensors and actuators are transmitted over a **single shared serial bus** using time-division multiplexing, eliminating the need for individual dedicated wires.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Significant weight and cost reduction** — A single twisted pair replaces hundreds of individual conductors, reducing harness weight by up to **70%**.
2. **Ease of diagnostics and scalability** — Faults can be read through the **OBD-II port**, and new ECUs can be added to the bus with minimal harness modification.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for two advantages = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**  
**State the function of a Gateway ECU in a multiplex network.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Gateway ECU** acts as a **router and protocol translator** between different multiplex buses (e.g., Powertrain CAN and Body CAN). It filters irrelevant messages, translates between protocols (CAN ↔ LIN ↔ Ethernet), and ensures that messages from one domain do not overload another. Without the gateway, buses operating at different speeds and protocols cannot interoperate.

*(2 marks for function + 1 mark for necessity = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture and working of a **CAN (Controller Area Network)** bus system used in modern automobiles. With a neat diagram, describe the dominant and recessive voltage levels. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture:**
The CAN bus consists of **two wires** — **CAN-High (CAN-H)** and **CAN-Low (CAN-L)** — terminated by **120 Ω resistors** at each end. Multiple ECUs are connected in parallel across the bus. Each ECU has a **transceiver**, **CAN controller**, and **host microprocessor**.

**Working Principle:**
1. Any ECU can initiate transmission when the bus is idle (recessive).
2. Each message carries an **arbitration ID** (11-bit or 29-bit).
3. If two ECUs transmit simultaneously, **CSMA/CR arbitration** ensures the ECU with the *lower ID wins*.
4. The winning ECU completes transmission; the losing ECU waits and retries.
5. All receiving ECUs perform **CRC checking** and send an **ACK** bit.

**Voltage Levels:**

$$
V_{\text{diff, dominant}} = 3.5\ \text{V} - 1.5\ \text{V} = 2.0\ \text{V}
$$

$$
V_{\text{diff, recessive}} = 2.5\ \text{V} - 2.5\ \text{V} \approx 0\ \text{V}
$$

**Advantages:**
- Differential signaling provides **excellent EMI immunity**.
- **Multi-master** architecture — no central controller needed.
- Built-in **error detection** (CRC, ACK, Bit stuffing).

*(Mark allocation: 3 marks architecture + 2 marks working + 2 marks diagram and voltage levels = 7 marks)*

---

**(b)** A CAN bus operates at **250 kbps**. Calculate (i) the bit time, (ii) the maximum theoretical bus length assuming a propagation velocity of $2 \times 10^8$ m/s, and (iii) the bus utilization if the bus carries 4 messages with the following specifications: Message 1 — 8 bytes at 100 Hz, Message 2 — 8 bytes at 50 Hz, Message 3 — 4 bytes at 200 Hz, Message 4 — 2 bytes at 100 Hz. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Bit Time:**
$$
T_{\text{bit}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{bus}}} = \frac{1}{250 \times 10^3} = 4 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s} = 4\ \mu\text{s}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Bus Length:**
$$
L_{\text{max}} = \frac{v_{\text{prop}}}{2 \cdot f_{\text{bus}}} = \frac{2 \times 10^8}{2 \times 250 \times 10^3} = \frac{2 \times 10^8}{5 \times 10^5} = 400\ \text{m}
$$

*(Note: Practical limit ≈ 200 m due to transceiver delays — 1 mark for the practical comment.)*

**(iii) Bus Utilization:**
Using the formula: Total bits per frame $= 47 + (N \times 8)$:

- Message 1: $(47 + 64) \times 100 = 11{,}100$ bps
- Message 2: $(47 + 64) \times 50 = 5550$ bps
- Message 3: $(47 + 32) \times 200 = 15{,}800$ bps
- Message 4: $(47 + 16) \times 100 = 6300$ bps

$$
B_{\text{total}} = 11{,}100 + 5550 + 15{,}800 + 6300 = 38{,}750\ \text{bps}
$$

$$
L_{\text{util}} = \frac{38{,}750}{250{,}000} \times 100\% = 15.5\%
$$

Since $L_{\text{util}} < 30\%$, the bus has healthy spare capacity.

*(1 mark per sub-part + 1 mark for verdict = 7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Compare **CAN, LIN, and FlexRay** multiplex protocols based on **speed, application, cost, and topology**. Mention at least four comparison points. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | CAN | LIN | FlexRay |
|---|---|---|---|
| Max Speed | **1 Mbps** | **19.2 kbps** | **10 Mbps** |
| Standard | ISO 11898 | ISO 17987 | ISO 17458 |
| Topology | Bus (multi-master) | Bus (single-master) | Bus or Star |
| Cost | Medium | **Very Low** | High |
| Application | Powertrain, Chassis | Windows, Mirrors, Seats | Drive-by-wire, ADAS |
| Synchronization | Event-triggered | Master-triggered | Time-triggered (TDMA) |
| Data Bytes/Frame | 8 | 8 | 254 |

**Conclusion:** LIN is used as a **sub-bus under CAN** for non-critical low-speed actuators, saving cost. CAN handles medium-criticality systems. FlexRay is reserved for safety-critical X-by-wire systems where deterministic timing is mandatory.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per major point × 4 + 1 mark each for additional rows)*

---

**(b)** Explain the role of **termination resistors (120 Ω)** in a CAN bus. What happens if they are (i) missing, (ii) placed only at one end, or (iii) of incorrect value (e.g., 60 Ω)? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of 120 Ω Termination:**
The CAN bus is a **transmission line**. Without termination, signal reflections at the cable ends cause **ringing**, corrupting the waveform and causing **bit errors**. The 120 Ω resistors at both ends **match the characteristic impedance** of the twisted-pair cable, absorbing reflections.

**Failure Mode Analysis:**

| Condition | Effect |
|---|---|
| **(i) Missing** | Severe reflections, intermittent communication failure, high error frame count |
| **(ii) Only one end** | Reflections still occur from the unterminated end; bus becomes unreliable beyond that point |
| **(iii) 60 Ω (incorrect)** | Partial impedance mismatch → reflections attenuated but not eliminated; signal integrity compromised, especially at high bit rates |

**Diagnostic Check:**
Measure resistance between CAN-H and CAN-L with the bus powered OFF. A healthy bus reads **≈ 60 Ω** (two 120 Ω resistors in parallel). Reading **120 Ω** indicates one termination is missing; **OL (open line)** indicates both missing.

*(2 marks for role + 1.5 marks per failure case × 3 + 0.5 mark for diagnostic = 7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing differential voltage with single-ended voltage.** CAN-H and CAN-L are *both* referenced to ground individually. The *logic* is in the *difference*, not the absolute level. Writing "CAN-H = 3.5 V is logic 1" will cost you a full mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert kbps to bps** in numerical problems. The bit time formula $T_{\text{bit}} = 1/f$ requires *consistent units*. Using 250 kbps as "250" in the denominator is a silent error.
> 3. **Not mentioning the Gateway ECU** in CAN architecture diagrams. A bare "ECUs connected to a bus" without the gateway *and* the 120 Ω terminators is incomplete.
> 4. **Skipping the "why"** in answers. KTU 2024 scheme rewards *application-level reasoning* (Bloom's Level 3+) over rote definitions (Bloom's Level 1). Always state **why** a feature exists — e.g., "120 Ω is used *because* it matches the twisted-pair characteristic impedance."
> 5. **Writing LIN as "Local Interconnect Network" but listing its speed as 1 Mbps.** Memorize: LIN = **≤ 19.2 kbps**, CAN = **≤ 1 Mbps**, FlexRay = **≤ 10 Mbps**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Multiplex wiring = one shared bus for many signals**, replacing the point-to-point wiring loom.
- **Primary protocols:** CAN (workhorse), LIN (low-cost sub-bus), FlexRay (safety-critical), MOST (infotainment), Automotive Ethernet (future backbone).
- **CAN voltage levels:** Dominant → $V_{\text{CAN-H}} = 3.5\ \text{V}$, $V_{\text{CAN-L}} = 1.5\ \text{V}$, $V_{\text{diff}} = 2.0\ \text{V}$. Recessive → both ≈ 2.5 V.
- **Termination:** Two **120 Ω resistors** in parallel give ≈ **60 Ω** measurable between CAN-H and CAN-L with power off.
- **Arbitration:** Non-destructive CSMA/CR — the **lower ID wins**, the loser retries without corrupting the winner's message.
- **Bit time:** $T_{\text{bit}} = 1/f_{\text{bus}}$. Maximum bus length: $L_{\text{max}} = v_{\text{prop}} / (2 \cdot f_{\text{bus}})$.
- **Bus load** should stay **below 30–40%** for design margin.
- **Gateway ECU** routes and translates between buses of different protocols and speeds.
- **OBD-II** is the standardized diagnostic access port to the multiplex bus.
- **Differential signaling** = the secret behind CAN's EMI immunity — common-mode noise cancels out.
- **Modern vehicle = ~80–100 ECUs** over **5–7 buses** → multiplex wiring is the *only* viable architecture for SDVs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regenerative Braking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT612 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Regenerative Braking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Regenerative Braking** is an energy recovery mechanism employed in hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs), battery electric vehicles (BEVs), and fuel cell vehicles (FCVs) in which the kinetic energy of the vehicle — which would otherwise be dissipated as heat through conventional friction brakes — is converted into electrical energy by operating the traction motor as a generator. This recovered electrical energy is then stored in the on-board energy storage system (battery pack or ultra-capacitor bank) for subsequent use during propulsion or auxiliary loads.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **OEMUT612 – Modern Automotive Technologies**, regenerative braking is studied under Module 1 as one of the foundational energy-management strategies that distinguish modern electrified powertrains from conventional internal combustion engine (ICE) vehicles. The principle rests on the reversibility of electromechanical energy conversion: any electric machine capable of functioning as a motor can, when mechanically driven above its no-load speed, function as a generator and deliver current back to the source.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Recharging Roller-Skater"**
> Imagine a person coasting downhill on roller-skates. In an ordinary vehicle, they would press a brake pad against the wheel and the rubbing friction would convert their motion into useless heat on the rim — exactly like a conventional disk brake. In a regenerative braking vehicle, however, the wheel is mechanically coupled to a small generator (the same motor that pushed the skater uphill earlier). As the skater rolls down, the wheel spins the generator, and the generator pushes current back into a rechargeable battery pack. The skater feels a smooth, controlled deceleration (because the generator resists being spun), and when they reach the bottom, the battery is partially recharged — ready to power the next uphill climb. Energy that was "wasted" as heat is instead "regenerated" as stored electricity.

### 1.3 Key System Metrics and Boundary Conditions

The standard KTU metrics used to characterise regenerative braking performance are:

- **Regenerative Braking Efficiency ($\eta_{rb}$)** — typically **0.30 to 0.70** for production BEVs.
- **Maximum Recoverable Braking Power ($P_{rb,max}$)** — often **60–80 kW** in mid-size EVs.
- **State of Charge (SOC) operating window** — usually between **20 % and 80 %** to avoid battery damage.
- **Vehicle Mass ($m$)** — typically **1200 kg – 2500 kg** for passenger BEVs.
- **Deceleration Limit ($a_{dec,max}$)** — usually **0.3g to 0.5g** (≈ **3 m/s² to 5 m/s²**) for purely regenerative braking; beyond this, friction brakes engage (blended braking).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** According to KTU Module 1 outcomes, students must be able to *(i)* describe the working principle of regenerative braking, *(ii)* compare it with conventional friction braking, and *(iii)* explain the energy flow in HEVs/BEVs during deceleration events.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

The regenerative braking process can be decomposed into the following engineering stages:

- **Step 1 — Brake Pedal / Deceleration Request Initiation**
  The driver depresses the brake pedal (or releases the accelerator pedal in one-pedal-driving mode). The vehicle control unit (VCU) interprets pedal travel as a *deceleration demand signal*.

- **Step 2 — Brake Force Distribution Arbitration**
  The brake controller computes the *total demanded braking force* $F_{brake,total}$ and decides what fraction can be supplied regeneratively versus what must be supplied by friction brakes. This arbitration respects the **maximum regeneration torque limit** of the motor and the **SOC ceiling** of the battery.

- **Step 3 — Motor Transition (Traction → Generator Mode)**
  The power electronics inverter commands the traction motor to operate in the **generator quadrant** of its torque–speed map. Field current is maintained, and the rotor is driven externally by the wheels. Back-EMF exceeds the battery terminal voltage, forcing current to flow *into* the battery.

- **Step 4 — Electrical Conditioning and Battery Charging**
  The induced AC voltage from the motor is rectified (DC machine case) or rectified via the inverter (AC machine case). A DC-DC buck-boost converter regulates the bus voltage to match the battery's safe charging profile (CC-CV — Constant Current then Constant Voltage).

- **Step 5 — SOC Monitoring and Cut-off Decision**
  The Battery Management System (BMS) continuously monitors SOC. Once SOC reaches the upper threshold (commonly **80–85 %**), regenerative torque is ramped down to zero to prevent over-charge, and the friction brake takes over fully.

- **Step 6 — Friction Brake Blending (Hydraulic or Electro-Mechanical)**
  Any deceleration demand *not* met by regeneration is delivered by the conventional friction brake. Smooth blending ensures the driver does not perceive a transition jerk.

### 2.2 Why Each Step Matters (Engineering Justification)

- **Step 1**: Without pedal sensing, the system cannot differentiate between *coast-down* (which is fully regenerative in many EVs) and *emergency stop* (which must use friction brakes for safety).
- **Step 2**: Distributing brake force protects the motor from overheating and protects the battery from excessive C-rate charging.
- **Step 3**: The *motor-as-generator* principle is governed by Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction: $e = N\frac{d\Phi}{dt}$.
- **Step 4**: Battery chemistry (Li-ion) cannot accept arbitrarily high charging currents; CC-CV regulation preserves cycle life.
- **Step 5**: Over-charging a Li-ion cell beyond 4.2 V/cell causes lithium plating, thermal runaway, and permanent capacity loss.
- **Step 6**: Regulatory standards (ECE R13-H, ISO 26262 functional safety) require *guaranteed* mechanical braking in all failure modes.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Kinetic Energy of Vehicle | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | Joules (J) | $m$ = vehicle mass (kg); $v$ = velocity (m/s) |
| Recoverable Energy | $E_{rec} = \eta_{rb} \cdot E_k$ | Joules (J) | $\eta_{rb}$ = regen efficiency (dimensionless) |
| Regen Braking Force | $F_{rb} = \tau_{gen} \cdot G / r_{wheel}$ | Newtons (N) | $\tau_{gen}$ = motor generator torque; $G$ = gear ratio; $r_{wheel}$ = wheel radius |
| Regen Power | $P_{rb} = F_{rb} \cdot v = \tau_{gen} \cdot \omega_{m}$ | Watts (W) | $\omega_{m}$ = motor angular speed (rad/s) |
| Motor Back-EMF | $E = K \Phi \omega_{m}$ | Volts (V) | $K$ = machine constant; $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb) |
| Charging Current | $I_{chg} = (E - V_{bat}) / R_{int}$ | Amperes (A) | $V_{bat}$ = battery terminal voltage; $R_{int}$ = circuit resistance |
| Deceleration | $a = F_{rb} / m$ | m/s² | When $F_{rb}$ is the only braking force |
| Energy Recovery Ratio | $R_{err} = E_{rec} / E_{consumed,total}$ | dimensionless | Used in ECE R101 test cycles |
| Battery SOC Change | $\Delta SOC = (E_{rec} / E_{bat,cap}) \cdot 100$ | % | $E_{bat,cap}$ = battery pack nominal capacity |
| Overall Vehicle Efficiency Gain | $\Delta\eta = \dfrac{\eta_{rb}}{1 - \eta_{rb}} \cdot \dfrac{E_k}{E_{fuel,eq}}$ | dimensionless | Approximate fuel-equivalent gain |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Regenerative braking is not merely a fuel-economy gimmick — it is a *thermal management and range-extending necessity*. The four practical applications that KTU examiners frequently emphasise are:

- **Urban Range Extension**: In stop-and-go city cycles (e.g., **WLTP Low**, **Indian Driving Cycle**), regenerative braking can extend EV range by **15–30 %**.
- **Brake Wear Reduction**: Friction pad replacement intervals extend from **30,000 km** (conventional) to over **100,000 km** (EVs with heavy regen).
- **Thermal Load Reduction**: Brake rotor temperatures can drop from **400 °C** (mountain descents on ICE vehicles) to below **150 °C**, mitigating fade.
- **Energy Audit Compliance**: Mandatory for OEMs under **UN ECE R101** and **Bharat Stage VI** (Real Driving Emissions) compliance testing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Recoverable Kinetic Energy During a Braking Event

**Given:** A vehicle of mass $m$ travelling at initial velocity $v_1$ decelerates to a final velocity $v_2$ using a regenerative brake with efficiency $\eta_{rb}$.

**Step 1 — Write the initial and final kinetic energies.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{k1} &= \tfrac{1}{2} m v_1^{2} \\
E_{k2} &= \tfrac{1}{2} m v_2^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Kinetic energy is a scalar that depends only on the square of velocity. At the start of braking the vehicle carries $E_{k1}$; at the end it carries $E_{k2}$.

**Step 2 — Compute the change in kinetic energy (= work done by brakes).**

$$
\Delta E_k = E_{k1} - E_{k2} = \tfrac{1}{2} m \left(v_1^{2} - v_2^{2}\right)
$$

*Logic:* By the Work–Energy Theorem, the net work done on the vehicle equals the change in kinetic energy. The brakes remove exactly $\Delta E_k$ of mechanical energy from the system.

**Step 3 — Apply regenerative efficiency to obtain recovered electrical energy.**

$$
E_{rec} = \eta_{rb} \cdot \Delta E_k = \eta_{rb} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} m \left(v_1^{2} - v_2^{2}\right)
$$

*Logic:* Of the total mechanical energy removed, only the fraction $\eta_{rb}$ emerges as useful electrical energy. The remainder is lost as motor copper/iron losses, inverter losses, and aerodynamic drag.

**Step 4 — Special case: full stop from velocity $v$.**

$$
E_{rec,full} = \eta_{rb} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}
$$

*Logic:* Setting $v_2 = 0$ recovers the classical textbook expression; this is the maximum energy recoverable from a single stop event.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Regenerative Braking Force and Deceleration Limit

**Given:** Traction motor rated torque $\tau_{max}$, fixed gear ratio $G$ (motor to wheel), wheel radius $r_w$, vehicle mass $m$, and rolling resistance coefficient $f_r$.

**Step 1 — Compute the maximum tangential braking force at the wheel–road contact patch.**

$$
F_{rb,max} = \frac{\tau_{max} \cdot G}{r_w}
$$

*Logic:* Torque multiplied by gear ratio gives the wheel torque; dividing by wheel radius converts this to a tangential road force.

**Step 2 — Compute the resulting maximum deceleration.**

$$
a_{dec,max} = \frac{F_{rb,max}}{m} = \frac{\tau_{max} \cdot G}{m \cdot r_w}
$$

*Logic:* Newton's Second Law ($F = m a$) gives deceleration as the ratio of net braking force to vehicle mass.

**Step 3 — Check road adhesion (no wheel lock-up constraint).**

$$
\mu \cdot m \cdot g \geq F_{rb,max}
$$

*Logic:* The available friction force between tyre and road is $\mu \cdot m g$ (where $\mu$ ≈ **0.7–1.0** on dry asphalt, ≈ **0.2–0.4** on wet road). If regenerative force exceeds this, the wheels will lock — so the controller must cap $F_{rb,max}$ to within adhesion limits.

**Step 4 — Including rolling resistance.**

$$
a_{dec,net} = \frac{F_{rb,max} - f_r \cdot m g \cdot \cos\theta}{m}
$$

*Logic:* On a slope of angle $\theta$, gravity assists or opposes deceleration; rolling resistance always opposes motion.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Flow During Regeneration

**Given:** Vehicle speed $v$, motor speed $\omega_m$ related by $\omega_m = G \cdot v / r_w$, and motor-generator back-EMF constant $K\Phi$.

**Step 1 — Express the back-EMF generated.**

$$
E_{emf} = K \Phi \cdot \omega_m = K \Phi \cdot \frac{G \cdot v}{r_w}
$$

*Logic:* As the wheel rotates the motor, the rotating armature in the magnetic field cuts flux lines, generating an EMF proportional to speed (Faraday's Law).

**Step 2 — Express the charging current supplied to the battery.**

$$
I_{chg} = \frac{E_{emf} - V_{bat}}{R_{int}}
$$

*Logic:* Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the generator–battery loop. The net driving voltage for current is the back-EMF minus the battery's terminal voltage; the circuit's internal resistance limits the current.

**Step 3 — Express the recovered electrical power.**

$$
P_{rec} = E_{emf} \cdot I_{chg} = \left(K \Phi \cdot \frac{G \cdot v}{r_w}\right) \cdot \frac{E_{emf} - V_{bat}}{R_{int}}
$$

*Logic:* Electrical power delivered to the battery equals the product of generated EMF and the current it drives through the closed loop.

**Step 4 — Equate mechanical input to electrical output (power balance).**

$$
P_{rec} = \eta_m \cdot \tau_{gen} \cdot \omega_m
$$

*Logic:* By conservation of energy, electrical power recovered equals mechanical power input multiplied by machine efficiency $\eta_m$. This identity is used by VCU to estimate state of charge without direct torque measurement in some architectures.

### 3.4 Numerical Example (Worked-Out)

> **Problem:** A **1500 kg** BEV decelerates from **90 km/h (25 m/s)** to a complete stop using regenerative braking. The motor's average regenerative efficiency is **0.65**. Compute (i) the kinetic energy dissipated, (ii) the energy recovered to the battery, and (iii) the equivalent petrol volume saved, assuming petrol's chemical energy density is **34 MJ/L**.

**Solution — Part (i):**

$$
E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1500 \cdot 25^{2} = 468{,}750 \text{ J} \approx 468.75 \text{ kJ}
$$

*Logic:* Direct substitution into $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$. The vehicle carries 468.75 kJ of kinetic energy at 90 km/h.

**Solution — Part (ii):**

$$
E_{rec} = 0.65 \cdot 468{,}750 = 304{,}687.5 \text{ J} \approx 304.7 \text{ kJ}
$$

*Logic:* Multiply total KE by regenerative efficiency. About 65 % of the braking energy is recovered.

**Solution — Part (iii):**

$$
V_{petrol} = \frac{E_{rec}}{\rho_E} = \frac{304{,}687.5}{34 \times 10^{6}} \approx 8.96 \times 10^{-3} \text{ L} \approx 9 \text{ mL}
$$

*Logic:* Dividing recovered energy by petrol's energy density gives the equivalent fuel volume — about 9 mL per single aggressive stop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Although 9 mL per stop seems trivial, an urban driver may execute **40–60 stops per hour**. Over a year of city driving, this regen capability can equate to **50–100 litres of fuel** equivalent — a substantial environmental and economic benefit.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Regenerative Braking System

The diagram below maps the functional flow of energy, signals, and control decisions from the moment the brake pedal is depressed to the moment electrical energy is deposited in the battery pack.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Input - Brake Pedal Travel Sensor] --> B[Vehicle Control Unit - VCU]
    A --> C[Accelerator Release - One Pedal Drive]
    B --> D{Brake Force Arbitration Logic}
    C --> D
    D --> E[SOC Check - Battery Management System BMS]
    E -->|SOC below ceiling| F[Regenerative Mode - Motor as Generator]
    E -->|SOC at ceiling| H[Friction Brake Only Mode]
    F --> G[Inverter Commands Generator Quadrant Torque]
    G --> I[Three Phase AC Machine - Traction Motor]
    I --> J[Mechanical Coupling - Wheels and Gearbox]
    J --> K[DC DC Converter - Bus Voltage Regulation]
    K --> L[High Voltage Battery Pack - Energy Storage]
    L --> M[SOC Update to BMS]
    D --> N[Friction Brake Calliper - Hydraulic or Electro Mechanical]
    N --> O[Wheel Rotors - Mechanical Heat Dissipation]
    H --> N
    M --> D
    style A fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style B fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style F fill:#b5e48c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style I fill:#ffadad,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style L fill:#9bf6ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style O fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.2 Energy Flow State Diagram (Traction vs Regeneration vs Coasting)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Propulsion
    Propulsion: PROPULSION MODE\nBattery to Motor\nTorque positive
    Propulsion --> Regeneration: Accelerator release or brake pedal pressed
    Regeneration: REGENERATION MODE\nMotor to Battery\nTorque negative
    Regeneration --> Coasting: Brake pedal released and pedal not pressed
    Regeneration --> FrictionOnly: SOC at ceiling or regen limit reached
    Coasting: COASTING MODE\nNo torque\nVehicle decelerates from drag
    FrictionOnly: FRICTION ONLY MODE\nNo regen permitted\nMechanical brake active
    FrictionOnly --> Propulsion: Accelerator pressed
    Coasting --> Propulsion: Accelerator pressed
    Coasting --> Regeneration: Brake pedal pressed
```

### 4.3 Component-Level Schematic Mapping

| Subsystem | Primary Component | Function During Regen |
|---|---|---|
| Driver Interface | Brake Pedal Position Sensor | Generates deceleration request signal |
| Central Controller | VCU / HCUs | Arbitrates regen vs friction torque split |
| Energy Storage | Li-ion Battery Pack (350–800 V) | Receives and stores recovered energy |
| Machine | 3-Phase PMSM / Induction Motor | Operates as generator |
| Power Electronics | Traction Inverter (IGBT/SiC) | Rectifies AC, controls field current |
| Aux Power Module | DC-DC Converter | Steps bus voltage for 12 V auxiliary |
| Thermal Loop | Cooling Pump + Radiator | Removes heat from inverter & motor |
| Mechanical | Friction Disc + Calliper | Supplies deficit braking force |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, OEMUT612]**
> **Q1.** Define regenerative braking. Why is it not employed in conventional petrol-only vehicles?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Regenerative braking is an energy recovery mechanism in which the traction motor of an electric or hybrid vehicle is operated as a generator during deceleration, converting the vehicle's kinetic energy into electrical energy that is stored in the battery. **(2 marks)**
> Conventional petrol-only vehicles do not employ this principle because they lack an electric traction motor and on-board rechargeable energy storage; their kinetic energy must therefore be dissipated as heat through friction brakes. **(1 mark)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, OEMUT612]**
> **Q2.** State any three factors that limit the amount of energy recoverable through regenerative braking.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
> 1. **Battery State of Charge (SOC) ceiling:** Once SOC approaches the upper limit (typically 80–85 %), regen is curtailed to prevent overcharging.
> 2. **Motor torque and power ratings:** The maximum regenerative torque is capped by the motor's thermal and electrical limits.
> 3. **Road adhesion (tyre–road friction coefficient $\mu$):** If regen torque would exceed the friction circle, wheels will lock; the controller must therefore limit regenerative force to within adhesion limits.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, OEMUT612 — Question A (14 Marks)]**
> **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working principle of a regenerative braking system in a battery electric vehicle. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 1200 kg electric car travelling at 108 km/h applies regenerative braking and decelerates uniformly to 36 km/h. If the average regenerative efficiency is 0.60, calculate (i) the kinetic energy lost during braking, (ii) the electrical energy recovered, and (iii) the average regenerative braking power if the braking event lasts 8 seconds. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Working Principle (7 marks):**

1. **(1 mark)** When the driver presses the brake pedal, the pedal position sensor sends a deceleration demand signal to the VCU.
2. **(1 mark)** The VCU checks the battery's SOC and the motor's available torque via the BMS and inverter respectively.
3. **(1 mark)** If regeneration is permitted, the VCU commands the inverter to operate the traction motor in its generator quadrant — i.e., the rotor is driven by the wheels and produces back-EMF.
4. **(1 mark)** The generated AC voltage is rectified by the inverter, and the DC-DC converter regulates the bus voltage to a level suitable for battery charging.
5. **(1 mark)** Current flows into the high-voltage battery pack, partially recharging it; the SOC is updated continuously.
6. **(1 mark)** If the demanded deceleration exceeds the regen system's capability, the friction brakes are blended in smoothly.
7. **(1 mark)** Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes for the complete signal and energy flow.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Convert speeds to SI units.

$$
v_1 = 108 \text{ km/h} = 30 \text{ m/s}, \quad v_2 = 36 \text{ km/h} = 10 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(1 mark)** *Logic:* 1 km/h = 1000/3600 = 5/18 m/s; hence 108 × (5/18) = 30 and 36 × (5/18) = 10.

**Step 2:** Compute kinetic energy lost.

$$
\Delta E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m (v_1^{2} - v_2^{2}) = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1200 \cdot (30^{2} - 10^{2})
$$

$$
\Delta E_k = 600 \cdot (900 - 100) = 600 \cdot 800 = 480{,}000 \text{ J} = 480 \text{ kJ}
$$

**(2 marks)** *Logic:* Substituting values gives the total mechanical energy removed from the vehicle.

**Step 3:** Compute recovered electrical energy.

$$
E_{rec} = 0.60 \cdot 480{,}000 = 288{,}000 \text{ J} = 288 \text{ kJ}
$$

**(2 marks)** *Logic:* 60 % of mechanical energy is converted to electrical energy by the motor-generator and stored in the battery.

**Step 4:** Compute average regenerative power.

$$
P_{rb,avg} = \frac{E_{rec}}{t} = \frac{288{,}000}{8} = 36{,}000 \text{ W} = 36 \text{ kW}
$$

**(2 marks)** *Logic:* Power is energy per unit time. Over 8 seconds, 288 kJ of energy yields an average power of 36 kW.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, OEMUT612 — Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative]**
> **(a)** Compare regenerative braking with conventional friction braking on the basis of (i) energy utilisation, (ii) brake wear, (iii) thermal management, and (iv) response time. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With the help of a torque-speed characteristic curve of a traction motor, explain the operating regions of propulsion, idling, and regeneration. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Comparative Analysis (7 marks — 1 mark per well-explained point, plus 1 mark for tabular clarity):**

| Parameter | Conventional Friction Brake | Regenerative Brake |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Energy utilisation | Energy dissipated as heat (100 % loss) | Energy recovered and stored (60–70 % recovery) |
| (ii) Brake wear | High pad and disc wear (replace every 30,000 km) | Minimal mechanical wear; pads last > 100,000 km |
| (iii) Thermal management | Rotor temperatures can exceed 400 °C, leading to brake fade | Low thermal load; friction brake only used in high-demand stops |
| (iv) Response time | Hydraulic: ~ 50–100 ms; Electric: ~ 10 ms | Motor torque response: ~ 1–5 ms (much faster) |

**(1 mark)** A concluding remark that regenerative braking is *complementary*, not *replacement*, to friction braking — both systems operate in blended mode in production EVs.

**Part (b) — Torque–Speed Characteristic (7 marks):**

1. **(1 mark)** Sketch the motor's torque–speed curve, showing the constant-torque region (low speed) and constant-power region (high speed), with motor speed $\omega_m$ on x-axis and torque $\tau_m$ on y-axis.
2. **(1 mark)** Mark **Quadrant I (positive torque, positive speed)** as the **Propulsion Region** — current flows from battery to motor, producing forward motion.
3. **(1 mark)** Mark **Quadrant II (negative torque, positive speed)** as the **Regenerative Braking Region** — back-EMF exceeds battery voltage, current flows back to battery.
4. **(1 mark)** Mark the **Zero Torque Line** as **Coasting / Idle** — no torque exchange, motor is electrically disconnected.
5. **(1 mark)** Show that maximum regen torque is highest at low speeds and falls off in the constant-power region (similar shape to propulsion torque curve, mirrored below the axis).
6. **(1 mark)** Indicate **base speed** $\omega_{base}$ separating the constant-torque and constant-power regions — this is also the speed below which field-weakening controls limit regen current.
7. **(1 mark)** Discuss the implication: at very high speeds, regenerative braking power is naturally capped by the motor's voltage and current limits — this is why friction brakes still engage above ~ 0.3g deceleration demand.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> - Students frequently **omit unit conversions** (km/h → m/s) in numericals — this costs 1–2 marks.
> - Many students incorrectly treat **regenerative efficiency** as the *motor* efficiency instead of the *overall system* efficiency (motor + inverter + battery + gearbox). Always clarify what $\eta_{rb}$ represents.
> - In block diagrams, students often forget to **label signal vs power flow arrows**. KTU examiners specifically deduct marks for unlabeled flow lines.
> - Do not confuse **back-EMF** with **supply voltage**. The back-EMF is generated *by* the spinning rotor; it is the source of energy in regen mode.
> - In the torque-speed curve, always label **all four quadrants** explicitly, even if the question focuses on Q1 and Q2 only.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Regenerative braking converts kinetic energy into electrical energy** by operating the traction motor as a generator.
- **Only electric and hybrid vehicles** can employ regenerative braking because they have reversible electromechanical energy converters.
- **Maximum recoverable energy** from a stop from velocity $v$ is $E_{rec,max} = \eta_{rb} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$.
- **SOC ceiling** (typically 80–85 %) prevents battery overcharge and is a hard limit on regeneration.
- **Regenerative braking is blended** with friction braking; it does not replace mechanical brakes entirely.
- **Motor back-EMF** ($E = K \Phi \omega_m$) drives current into the battery when it exceeds the battery's terminal voltage.
- **Brake force arbitration** by the VCU respects motor torque limits, SOC, road adhesion ($\mu m g$), and driver demand.
- **Real-world regen efficiency** is **30–70 %**; total losses include motor copper/iron losses, inverter switching losses, gearbox friction, and battery charge acceptance losses.
- **ECE R101 and Indian ARAI test cycles** mandate reporting of energy recovery ratio $R_{err}$ for type approval.
- **Brake-by-wire systems** (e.g., Bosch iBooster, Continental MK C1) are commonly used in modern EVs to enable precise blending of regen and friction torque.
- **One-pedal driving** in many modern BEVs (e.g., Nissan Leaf, Tesla Model 3) achieves near-100 % regen during normal deceleration by mapping accelerator pedal release to maximum regen torque.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Factors to be Considered for Converting Automobiles to Electric Vehicle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT612 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Factors to be Considered for Converting Automobiles to Electric Vehicle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 📘 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Vehicle Electrification / EV Conversion** is the systematic engineering process of replacing the conventional Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) powertrain — comprising the engine, fuel tank, exhaust system, and transmission — with an **electric powertrain** consisting of an **electric motor**, **battery pack**, **power electronic controller**, and **auxiliary systems**, while retaining or suitably modifying the existing vehicle chassis, suspension, and braking subsystems.

The conversion must satisfy the **KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) criterion** of preserving vehicle dynamic stability, safety, payload capacity, and intended operational range while meeting efficiency, cost, and emission-reduction targets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The basic factors encompass **vehicle weight, range, payload, performance (acceleration/top speed), battery sizing, motor selection, charging infrastructure, safety, cost, and regulatory compliance**. Each factor is interdependent and must be analyzed *before* physical conversion begins.

---

## 🧠 Intuitive Analogy — "The Heart Transplant of a Car"

Imagine a conventional car is a **human body**:
- The **ICE engine** is the **heart**, pumping energy.
- The **fuel tank** is the **stomach** (storing chemical energy).
- The **exhaust** is the **lungs** (releasing waste gases).
- The **transmission** is the **circulatory system**, distributing power.

**EV conversion is essentially performing a heart transplant** — replacing the ICE heart with a quieter, cleaner **electric motor heart**, and refilling the stomach with a **high-voltage battery meal** instead of petrol. But a heart transplant isn't just plug-and-play; doctors (engineers) must check:
- Will the new heart fit inside the chest cavity? → **Space & Packaging**
- Can the body handle the new weight? → **Weight Distribution & Payload**
- Will the new heart pump enough blood for daily activities? → **Power & Performance**
- Is the body's blood type compatible? → **Mechanical & Electrical Compatibility**
- How far can the body run on one meal? → **Range & Battery Capacity**

This is exactly the multi-factor engineering challenge of EV conversion.

---

## 🔑 The Seven Core Conversion Factors (Foundational List)

| # | Factor | Engineering Domain | Critical Question |
|---|--------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Vehicle Weight & Payload** | Mechanical / Structural | Will added battery mass exceed GVW? |
| 2 | **Range Requirement** | Energy Storage | How many km/day does the user need? |
| 3 | **Battery Sizing & Chemistry** | Electrochemistry | Li-ion vs Lead-acid — energy density trade-off |
| 4 | **Motor Power & Torque** | Electrical Machines | Will it match original ICE performance? |
| 5 | **Charging Infrastructure** | Power Electronics | AC Level 1/2 or DC fast charging? |
| 6 | **Cost & ROI** | Economics | Payback period vs fuel savings |
| 7 | **Safety & Regulations** | Compliance | AIS-156, IEC-62133, FAME-II norms |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Remember:**
> - Air density at sea level: **ρ = 1.225 kg/m³**
> - Gravitational acceleration: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Standard C_d (drag coefficient) for sedan: **0.25 – 0.35**
> - Rolling resistance coefficient (μ): **0.008 – 0.015** (for car tires on asphalt)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🔬 Factor 1: Vehicle Weight, Payload & Mass Distribution

### Why It Matters
Adding a battery pack (typically **200 – 600 kg** for a mid-size car conversion) drastically alters the **sprung mass**, **center of gravity (CG)**, and **unsprung mass distribution**. This affects:
- Suspension geometry & damping
- Braking distance (kinetic energy ∝ mass)
- Tire wear and steering response

### Key Engineering Equations

The total tractive force required at the wheels is the sum of four resistive forces:

$$F_{total} = F_{rolling} + F_{aero} + F_{climb} + F_{acceleration}$$

Where each component is defined as follows:

$$F_{rolling} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

$$F_{aero} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$$

$$F_{climb} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

$$F_{acceleration} = m \cdot a \cdot \delta$$

Where:
- $\mu_{rr}$ = coefficient of rolling resistance (dimensionless)
- $m$ = vehicle mass in **kg** (curb + payload)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration = **9.81 m/s²**
- $\theta$ = road gradient angle
- $\rho$ = air density = **1.225 kg/m³**
- $C_d$ = drag coefficient (dimensionless)
- $A_f$ = frontal area in **m²**
- $v$ = vehicle velocity in **m/s**
- $a$ = acceleration in **m/s²**
- $\delta$ = mass factor for rotational inertia (typically **1.05 – 1.10**)

### Why Each Force Exists
- **Rolling resistance**: Tires deform as they roll; energy is lost as heat.
- **Aerodynamic drag**: Air molecules are pushed aside; kinetic energy imparted to them.
- **Climbing force**: Gravitational potential energy must be overcome on inclines.
- **Acceleration force**: Newton's second law — $F = ma$ — to overcome inertia.

---

## 🔋 Factor 2: Range Requirement & Battery Sizing

### The Range Equation

$$R = \frac{E_{battery} \cdot \eta_{system}}{E_{consumption\_per\_km}}$$

Where:
- $R$ = range in **km**
- $E_{battery}$ = usable battery energy in **kWh**
- $\eta_{system}$ = drivetrain efficiency (typically **0.80 – 0.90**)
- $E_{consumption\_per\_km}$ = energy used per km in **kWh/km**

### Energy Consumption Per Kilometer

$$E_{consumption\_per\_km} = \frac{F_{total} \cdot v}{3600 \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{transmission}}$$

### Battery Pack Energy

$$E_{battery} = V_{pack} \cdot Q_{pack} = N_s \cdot V_{cell} \cdot N_p \cdot Q_{cell}$$

Where:
- $N_s$ = number of cells in series
- $N_p$ = number of cells in parallel
- $V_{cell}$ = nominal cell voltage
- $Q_{cell}$ = cell capacity in **Ah**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Depth of Discharge (DoD) Constraint:**
> Lithium-ion batteries should never be discharged below **20%** to prolong cycle life. Hence, *usable* capacity is only **80%** of the *rated* capacity. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests this.

---

## ⚙️ Factor 3: Motor Power & Torque Selection

### Power Required at Wheels

$$P_{wheel} = F_{total} \cdot v = \frac{F_{total} \cdot v}{1000} \quad \text{[in kW]}$$

### Motor Rated Power (accounting for drivetrain losses)

$$P_{motor} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{transmission}}$$

### Required Motor Torque

$$T_{motor} = \frac{F_{total} \cdot r_{wheel}}{G \cdot \eta_{gear}}$$

Where:
- $r_{wheel}$ = effective wheel radius in **m**
- $G$ = overall gear ratio
- $\eta_{gear}$ = gear efficiency

### Motor Speed (RPM)

$$N_{motor} = \frac{v \cdot 60 \cdot G}{2 \pi \cdot r_{wheel}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Common EV Motor Types:** BLDC (Brushless DC), PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor), Induction Motor, and SRM (Switched Reluctance Motor). BLDC is the most common in 2W/3W conversions due to **high efficiency (>90%)** and **simplicity of control**.

---

## 💰 Factor 4: Cost & Return on Investment (ROI)

### Simple Payback Period

$$\text{Payback Period} = \frac{\text{Conversion Cost} - \text{Government Subsidy}}{\text{Annual Fuel Savings}}$$

### Annual Fuel Savings

$$S_{annual} = D_{yearly} \cdot (C_{fuel} - C_{electric})$$

Where:
- $D_{yearly}$ = annual distance driven in **km**
- $C_{fuel}$ = cost per km of ICE operation in **₹/km**
- $C_{electric}$ = cost per km of EV operation in **₹/km**

---

## 📋 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units | Boundary / Limiting Value |
|---|---------|------------------|---------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | $F_{rolling} = \mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta$ | Resistance from tire deformation | Newtons (N) | $\mu_{rr} \in [0.008, 0.015]$ |
| 2 | $F_{aero} = 0.5 \rho C_d A_f v^2$ | Wind drag | N | $C_d \in [0.25, 0.45]$ |
| 3 | $F_{climb} = m g \sin\theta$ | Gravitational pull on inclines | N | $\theta \le 30°$ typical urban |
| 4 | $F_{accel} = m a \delta$ | Inertial resistance | N | $\delta \approx 1.05$ |
| 5 | $P = F \cdot v$ | Power needed at wheels | Watts (W) | Divide by 1000 for kW |
| 6 | $E_{batt} = V \cdot Q$ | Battery energy storage | kWh | DoD $\le$ 80% for Li-ion |
| 7 | $R = E_{batt} \cdot \eta / E_{km}$ | Vehicle range | km | $R \in [80, 500]$ km typical |
| 8 | $T = F \cdot r_{wheel} / G$ | Required motor torque | N·m | Peak $T \le 3 \times$ rated |
| 9 | $N = 60 \cdot v \cdot G / (2\pi r)$ | Motor speed | RPM | $N \in [1000, 10000]$ |
| 10 | $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{gear} \cdot \eta_{inv}$ | System efficiency | dimensionless | $\eta_{sys} \in [0.75, 0.90]$ |

---

## 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are the **backbone of EV design software** (like ADVISOR, MATLAB/Simulink Drivetrain Blockset, AVL CRUISE). Automotive OEMs in India (Tata Motors, Mahindra Electric, Ather Energy) and conversion startups (Etrio, Altigreen, Cell Propulsion) use these exact equations — sometimes with proprietary coefficients — to design, certify, and homologate their electric vehicles. Mastery of these formulas is **mandatory for any EV design engineer**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 📐 DERIVATION 1: Total Tractive Force Equation (From Newton's Second Law)

### Starting Principle
A vehicle moving at constant velocity experiences **force balance**:

$$\sum F = m \cdot a_{effective}$$

When accelerating, $a_{effective} = a$. When cruising, $a = 0$, so $\sum F = 0$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Identify all resistive forces acting on the vehicle at the wheel contact patch and frontal area.

The four resistances are:
1. Rolling resistance from tire-road contact
2. Aerodynamic drag from air displacement
3. Climbing resistance from gravity along incline
4. Inertial resistance during acceleration

**Step 2:** Write Newton's equation along the direction of motion.

$$m \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} = F_{tractive} - F_{rolling} - F_{aero} - F_{climb}$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to define $F_{tractive}$ as the sum of all opposing forces plus the inertial term.

$$F_{tractive} = F_{rolling} + F_{aero} + F_{climb} + m \cdot a$$

**Step 4:** Introduce the rotational inertia factor $\delta$ to account for rotating masses (wheels, motor rotor, gears).

$$F_{tractive} = F_{rolling} + F_{aero} + F_{climb} + \delta \cdot m \cdot a$$

**Step 5:** Expand each term using physical models.

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{rolling} &= \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta) \\
F_{aero} &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2 \\
F_{climb} &= m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta) \\
F_{accel} &= \delta \cdot m \cdot a
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6:** Combine into the master equation.

$$F_{tractive} = \mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta + \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^2 + m g \sin\theta + \delta m a$$

This is the **fundamental equation of vehicle dynamics** — used in every EV/ICE design textbook.

---

## 📐 DERIVATION 2: Battery Pack Sizing for a Given Range

### Problem Statement
A converted EV must travel **150 km** per charge on a city driving cycle with average speed **40 km/h** and average acceleration **0.5 m/s²**.

### Step 1: Estimate Average Energy Consumption per km

Using a typical mid-size car: $m = 1500$ kg, $C_d = 0.30$, $A_f = 2.2$ m², $\mu_{rr} = 0.012$, $\eta_{sys} = 0.85$.

**Step 1a:** Compute each resistance at $v = 40$ km/h = **11.11 m/s**.

$$F_{rolling} = 0.012 \cdot 1500 \cdot 9.81 = 176.58 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{aero} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2.2 \cdot (11.11)^2 = 49.91 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{climb} = 0 \text{ (assume flat road, } \theta = 0°)$$

$$F_{accel} = 1.05 \cdot 1500 \cdot 0.5 = 787.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 1b:** Sum tractive force.

$$F_{total} = 176.58 + 49.91 + 0 + 787.5 = 1013.99 \text{ N}$$

**Step 1c:** Compute power at wheels.

$$P_{wheel} = F_{total} \cdot v = 1013.99 \cdot 11.11 = 11265.4 \text{ W} \approx 11.27 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 1d:** Compute motor input power.

$$P_{motor} = \frac{11.27}{0.90} = 12.52 \text{ kW (peak, including 90\% motor efficiency)}$$

**Step 1e:** Average power consumption per km.

$$E_{km} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{v_{km/h} \cdot 1000} = \frac{11.27 \text{ kW}}{40 \text{ km/h}} = 0.282 \text{ kWh/km}$$

> Note: This is a **peak estimate**. For real-world cycle (with stop-start), average ≈ 0.12 – 0.18 kWh/km.

**Step 2: Required Battery Energy**

Using $R = E_{batt} \cdot \eta_{sys} / E_{km}$:

$$E_{batt} = \frac{R \cdot E_{km}}{\eta_{sys}} = \frac{150 \cdot 0.18}{0.85} = 31.76 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 3: Account for Depth of Discharge (DoD = 80%)**

$$E_{rated} = \frac{E_{batt}}{0.80} = 39.7 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 4: Convert to Battery Pack Configuration**

For a **96V** pack (typical for converted cars), using **3.7V nominal Li-ion cells**:

$$N_s = \frac{96}{3.7} = 26 \text{ cells in series}$$

If using **100 Ah** prismatic cells, $Q_{pack} = 100$ Ah.

$$E_{pack} = 96 \cdot 100 = 9600 \text{ Wh} = 9.6 \text{ kWh per string}$$

To achieve 39.7 kWh:

$$N_p = \frac{39.7}{9.6} = 4.14 \approx 4 \text{ parallel strings}$$

So a **96V, 400Ah** pack is required.

---

## 📐 DERIVATION 3: Motor Torque & Speed Selection

### Given
- Vehicle mass: 1500 kg
- Wheel radius: 0.3 m
- Gear ratio: 7:1 (single-speed)
- Maximum speed: 120 km/h = 33.33 m/s
- Maximum grade: 30%

### Step 1: Compute Maximum Torque Required (Starting from rest on 30% grade)

$$F_{climb, max} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\arctan(0.30)) = 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.287 = 4223.2 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{rolling, max} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\arctan(0.30)) \approx 0.012 \cdot 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.958 = 169.2 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{total, max} = 4223.2 + 169.2 = 4392.4 \text{ N}$$

### Step 2: Required Motor Torque

$$T_{motor, peak} = \frac{F_{total, max} \cdot r_{wheel}}{G \cdot \eta_{gear}} = \frac{4392.4 \cdot 0.3}{7 \cdot 0.95} = 198.1 \text{ N·m}$$

### Step 3: Required Motor Speed at 120 km/h

$$N_{motor, max} = \frac{33.33 \cdot 60 \cdot 7}{2 \pi \cdot 0.3} = 7425 \text{ RPM}$$

### Step 4: Select Motor Rating
A motor with **continuous rating 25 kW, peak 50 kW, 200 N·m peak torque, 7500 RPM** would be suitable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why over-rate continuous power?** EV motors are commonly allowed to deliver **2× to 3×** their continuous rating for short durations (typically **30-60 seconds**) during acceleration and hill climbing. This avoids oversizing and reduces cost.

---

## 📊 ANALYTICAL DECISION MATRIX — Selecting Conversion Strategy

| Factor | Low-Cost Conversion | Mid-Range Conversion | Premium Conversion |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| **Battery Chemistry** | Lead-Acid (LFP) | LFP / NMC | NMC 811 / Solid-State (future) |
| **Battery Capacity** | 10 – 15 kWh | 20 – 30 kWh | 40 – 60 kWh |
| **Range** | 60 – 80 km | 120 – 180 km | 250 – 400 km |
| **Motor Type** | BLDC Hub Motor | PMSM Mid-Drive | PMSM Dual-Motor AWD |
| **Peak Power** | 5 – 10 kW | 15 – 30 kW | 75 – 150 kW |
| **Charging** | AC Level 1 (3 kW) | AC Level 2 (7 kW) | DC Fast (50 kW+) |
| **Cost (India, 2024)** | ₹ 1.5 – 2.5 Lakh | ₹ 4 – 6 Lakh | ₹ 8 – 15 Lakh |
| **Target Vehicle** | 2W / 3W / Auto | Small Cars (Tata Nano class) | Sedan / SUV (Nexon EV class) |
| **Subsidy (FAME-II)** | Limited | Eligible | Eligible |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ✏️ PART A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**List any six basic factors to be considered when converting a conventional automobile into an electric vehicle.**

### ✅ Model Answer (6 points):

1. **Vehicle Weight & Payload** — Battery adds 200–600 kg, affecting GVW, suspension, and CG.
2. **Range Requirement** — Daily km of operation determines battery capacity.
3. **Battery Sizing & Chemistry** — Li-ion vs Lead-acid; DoD limits usable energy.
4. **Motor Power & Torque** — Must match or exceed original ICE performance.
5. **Charging Infrastructure** — AC Level 1/2 vs DC fast charging compatibility.
6. **Cost & Subsidy** — Conversion cost vs fuel savings; FAME-II eligibility.
7. (Bonus) **Safety & Regulations** — AIS-156, IEC standards compliance.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2023]**
**Define Depth of Discharge (DoD) and State of Charge (SoC) in the context of EV batteries. Why is DoD limited to 80% for lithium-ion batteries?**

### ✅ Model Answer (6 points):

- **State of Charge (SoC):** The *currently available* energy in the battery as a percentage of its *rated* capacity.
  $$SoC = \frac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$$

- **Depth of Discharge (DoD):** The *fraction of battery capacity* that has been *used*.
  $$DoD = 100\% - SoC$$

- **Why limit DoD to 80%?**
  1. **Cycle life protection** — Deep discharges below 20% SoC cause accelerated lithium plating and electrode degradation.
  2. **Voltage cutoff** — Cells drop below safe voltage threshold (~2.5V) and may become permanently damaged.
  3. **Thermal safety** — Deeply discharged cells can swell and pose fire hazards upon recharging.
  4. **Warranty compliance** — Manufacturers void warranty if DoD > 80% regularly.
  5. **Practical usable energy** — A "100 kWh" EV typically delivers only "80 kWh usable".

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> Students often confuse SoC and DoD. **Remember**: SoC + DoD = 100%. If SoC = 20%, then DoD = 80%. Limited DoD = 80% means you can *only use 80%* of the battery. Losing 1 mark for this confusion is very common.

---

## ✏️ PART B: 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**A small car of mass 1200 kg (including driver) is to be converted into an electric vehicle. The design requirements are:**
- **Maximum speed: 80 km/h on level road**
- **Maximum gradient: 20% (≈ 11.31°)**
- **Rolling resistance coefficient: 0.012**
- **Drag coefficient: 0.32, Frontal area: 2.1 m²**
- **Air density: 1.225 kg/m³, Rotational inertia factor: 1.08**

#### **(a) [7 Marks] Calculate the total tractive force required (i) at constant 80 km/h on level road, and (ii) at 30 km/h climbing a 20% gradient from rest. Also compute the corresponding power required at wheels.**

#### **(b) [7 Marks] If the converted vehicle must achieve a range of 120 km per charge, with an average energy consumption of 0.15 kWh/km and overall system efficiency of 85%, determine the required (i) usable battery energy, (ii) rated battery energy with 80% DoD limit, and (iii) battery pack configuration for a 96V system using 3.7V, 50Ah cells.**

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A

#### **Part (a): Tractive Force and Power Calculations**

**Case (i): Constant 80 km/h on level road**

Convert speed: $v = 80$ km/h $= 22.22$ m/s

Compute each resistive force:

$$F_{rolling} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta) = 0.012 \cdot 1200 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \cos(0°) = 141.26 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{aero} = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2 = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.32 \cdot 2.1 \cdot (22.22)^2 = 203.12 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{climb} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta) = 0 \text{ (level road, } \theta = 0°)$$

$$F_{accel} = 0 \text{ (constant speed, no acceleration)}$$

**Total tractive force:**

$$F_{total,i} = 141.26 + 203.12 + 0 + 0 = 344.38 \text{ N}$$

**Power required at wheels:**

$$P_{i} = F_{total,i} \cdot v = 344.38 \cdot 22.22 = 7651.7 \text{ W} \approx 7.65 \text{ kW}$$

---

**Case (ii): 30 km/h climbing 20% gradient from rest**

Convert: $v = 30$ km/h $= 8.33$ m/s, $\theta = \arctan(0.20) = 11.31°$

$$\cos(11.31°) = 0.9806, \quad \sin(11.31°) = 0.1961$$

$$F_{rolling} = 0.012 \cdot 1200 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.9806 = 138.51 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{aero} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.32 \cdot 2.1 \cdot (8.33)^2 = 28.55 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{climb} = 1200 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.1961 = 2308.5 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{accel} = \delta \cdot m \cdot a = 1.08 \cdot 1200 \cdot a$$

*(Since "from rest" but constant 30 km/h, $a = 0$)*

$$F_{accel} = 0$$

**Total tractive force:**

$$F_{total,ii} = 138.51 + 28.55 + 2308.5 + 0 = 2475.56 \text{ N}$$

**Power required at wheels:**

$$P_{ii} = 2475.56 \cdot 8.33 = 20621.4 \text{ W} \approx 20.62 \text{ kW}$$

> **Observations for KTU students:**
> - The hill-climbing force dominates (~93% of total force).
> - At 80 km/h flat, the motor needs only **7.65 kW** — a much smaller motor would suffice.
> - The **peak** requirement (hill climb) is **~2.7×** the cruise requirement → justifies a motor with **2-3× peak overload capability**.

---

#### **Part (b): Battery Sizing**

**Step 1: Required usable energy**

$$E_{usable} = R \cdot E_{km} / \eta_{sys} = \frac{120 \text{ km} \cdot 0.15 \text{ kWh/km}}{0.85} = 21.18 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2: Rated battery energy with 80% DoD**

$$E_{rated} = \frac{E_{usable}}{DoD} = \frac{21.18}{0.80} = 26.47 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 3: Pack configuration**

Number of cells in series:

$$N_s = \frac{V_{pack}}{V_{cell}} = \frac{96}{3.7} = 25.95 \approx 26 \text{ cells}$$

Per-string energy:

$$E_{string} = 96 \text{ V} \cdot 50 \text{ Ah} = 4800 \text{ Wh} = 4.8 \text{ kWh}$$

Number of parallel strings:

$$N_p = \frac{E_{rated}}{E_{string}} = \frac{26.47}{4.8} = 5.51 \approx 6 \text{ parallel strings}$$

**Pack configuration: 26S6P — Total 156 cells, 96V, 300Ah, 28.8 kWh**

**Verification:** $E_{actual} = 96 \cdot 300 = 28800$ Wh = 28.8 kWh, with 80% usable = 23.04 kWh ≥ 21.18 kWh ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks - Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam - June 2022]**

#### **(a) [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the major components of an electric vehicle powertrain. How does it differ from a conventional ICE powertrain?**

#### **(b) [7 Marks] A three-wheeler auto-rickshaw of mass 450 kg is converted to electric drive. The original ICE had peak power of 6 kW. If the rolling resistance is 0.015 and drag coefficient is 0.6 with frontal area 1.8 m², calculate the peak power required for the electric motor to match the ICE performance at 40 km/h on a level road. State your assumptions clearly.**

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B

#### **Part (a): EV Powertrain Components**

| Component | ICE Powertrain (Original) | EV Powertrain (Converted) |
|-----------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| Energy Source | Petrol/Diesel tank (15-50L) | Li-ion battery pack (10-60 kWh) |
| Energy Converter | Internal Combustion Engine | Electric Motor (BLDC/PMSM/IM) |
| Power Control | Carburetor/Injector + ECU | Motor Controller (VFD/Inverter) |
| Transmission | Multi-speed gearbox (4-6 gears) | Single-speed reduction gearbox |
| Exhaust | Catalytic converter + muffler | ❌ None required |
| Cooling | Radiator + water pump | Liquid/air cooling for motor & battery |
| Auxiliary | 12V battery + alternator | 12V aux battery + DC-DC converter |
| Fueling | Petrol pump | Charging port (AC Type 1/2 or DC CCS) |

**Key differences:**
1. EV has **fewer moving parts** (~20 vs 2000+ in ICE) → higher reliability.
2. **No transmission** needed due to motor's wide constant-power RPM range.
3. **Regenerative braking** captures kinetic energy (15-25% range extension).
4. **Zero tailpipe emissions** → direct environmental benefit.

#### **Part (b): Peak Power Calculation for E-Auto**

**Given data:**
- $m = 450$ kg, $v = 40$ km/h $= 11.11$ m/s, $\mu_{rr} = 0.015$, $C_d = 0.6$, $A_f = 1.8$ m², $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\theta = 0°$

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state cruising, no acceleration.
2. Level road, no wind.
3. No rotational inertia factor considered for simplicity.

**Compute forces:**

$$F_{rolling} = 0.015 \cdot 450 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \cos(0°) = 66.21 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{aero} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 1.8 \cdot (11.11)^2 = 81.65 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{climb} = 0, \quad F_{accel} = 0$$

$$F_{total} = 66.21 + 81.65 = 147.86 \text{ N}$$

**Power at wheels:**

$$P_{wheel} = F_{total} \cdot v = 147.86 \cdot 11.11 = 1642.7 \text{ W} = 1.64 \text{ kW}$$

**Including drivetrain efficiency** ($\eta_{motor} = 0.90$, $\eta_{gear} = 0.95$):

$$P_{motor,continuous} = \frac{1.64}{0.90 \cdot 0.95} = 1.92 \text{ kW}$$

**But peak requirement** (to match ICE's 6 kW during hill climbs / sudden loads):

$$P_{motor,peak} \approx 1.5 \times \text{ to } 2 \times P_{continuous} = 2.88 \text{ to } 3.84 \text{ kW}$$

**Recommended motor rating:** A **2 kW continuous, 5 kW peak** BLDC motor is sufficient to match and exceed the original 6 kW ICE for normal operation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s. **Loss: 1-2 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Not applying $\cos(\theta)$ to rolling resistance. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Computing power at *wheel* but not accounting for *drivetrain efficiency* to get *motor input power*. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to mention **assumptions** explicitly. KTU examiners award 1 mark for "clearly stated assumptions".
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Confusing **continuous** vs **peak** power ratings. EV motors are typically **2-3× peak** rated. **Loss: 1-2 marks.**

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and revise 5 minutes before exam:**

- ✅ **EV Conversion = Replace ICE + fuel tank + exhaust with motor + battery + controller**, while retaining chassis, suspension, brakes.
- ✅ **7 Critical Factors**: Weight, Range, Battery, Motor, Charging, Cost, Safety/Regulations.
- ✅ **Master Force Equation**: $F_{tract} = \mu m g \cos\theta + 0.5 \rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin\theta + \delta m a$
