- ✅ **Power at wheels**: $P = F \cdot v$ (in W if F in N, v in m/s)
- ✅ **Motor input power**: $P_{motor} = P_{wheel} / (\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{gear})$
- ✅ **Battery Energy**: $E = V \cdot Ah$ (kWh = V × Ah ÷ 1000)
- ✅ **Range formula**: $R = E_{usable} / E_{per\_km}$, with $\eta_{sys}$ adjustment.
- ✅ **DoD = 80%** for Li-ion; SoC + DoD = 100%.
- ✅ **Pack Configuration**: $N_s = V_{pack}/V_{cell}$, $N_p = Q_{pack}/Q_{cell}$.
- ✅ **Peak vs Continuous**: Motor peak = 2-3× continuous rating.
- ✅ **Constants**: $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\delta \approx 1.05 - 1.10$.
- ✅ **Standards to remember**: AIS-156 (India), IEC-62133 (cells), FAME-II (subsidy).
- ✅ **Energy flow**: Battery → Inverter → Motor → Gearbox → Wheels (losses at each stage).
- ✅ **Regenerative braking** can recover **15-25%** of energy.
- ✅ **Conversion Cost vs Payback**: A typical Indian 4W conversion costs ₹ 4-6 Lakh with 3-5 year payback.
- ✅ **Single biggest trade-off**: **Range ↔ Battery cost ↔ Weight** (the "EV triangle" — improving one worsens the others).

> **End of KTU 2024 Module 1 Notes — Topic: Basic Factors for EV Conversion** 🔋⚡🚗

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hybrid Electric Vehicle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT612 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hybrid Electric Vehicle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV):** A vehicle that combines two or more distinct energy sources and corresponding power converters — typically an **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** coupled with one or more **electric machines (motor/generator)** and an **electrochemical battery (or other energy storage device)** — to propel the vehicle. The hybridization aims to optimize fuel consumption, reduce tail-pipe emissions, and recover kinetic energy during deceleration that would otherwise be dissipated as heat in conventional friction brakes.

In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme lexicon, a Hybrid Electric Vehicle is **not** simply a vehicle with two engines. It is an **energy-management system** in which the ICE and the Electric Motor (EM) cooperate through a **powertrain controller** to meet driver torque demand with the highest possible thermodynamic and electrochemical efficiency.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Two Workers Sharing a Heavy Load":**
> Imagine two workers carrying a heavy sack of rice along a hill road.
> - **Worker A (ICE):** Strong, fast, and good for long distances on flat roads. But he gets tired quickly on slow, stop-and-go city traffic and his "salary" (fuel cost) is high.
> - **Worker B (Electric Motor):** Quiet, efficient at low speeds, and he can actually *recover* energy when going downhill (regenerative braking). However, he tires out quickly on long highway journeys because his energy pack (battery) is small.
>
> The **manager (Powertrain Control Unit / PCU)** continuously decides which worker should carry the load at any instant. Sometimes both carry it together (combined propulsion). Sometimes only Worker B works while Worker A rests (battery-only mode). The result is that the total energy spent is far less than if Worker A did everything alone.

This is exactly how an HEV operates — a **smart arbitration** between the ICE and the EM.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology (Highlighted)

The following parameters are **universally used** by KTU examiners when evaluating HEV problems:

- **State of Charge (SOC):** Expressed in **%**; the usable charge remaining in the traction battery relative to its rated capacity.
- **Specific Energy:** Expressed in **Wh/kg**; energy stored per unit mass of the battery.
- **Specific Power:** Expressed in **W/kg**; power deliverable per unit mass of the battery.
- **Regenerative Braking Efficiency (\(\eta_{reg}\)):** Expressed as a **dimensionless ratio** between 0 and 1.
- **Degree of Hybridization (DOH):** Ratio of electric machine power to total powertrain power, **dimensionless**, ranging from 0 (pure ICE) to 1 (pure EV).
- **Fuel Consumption Reduction (FCR):** Expressed in **%** or **L/100 km**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The course code OEMUT612 is an **Open Elective / Minor stream** paper. Students are expected to demonstrate conceptual mastery, not numerical depth in powertrain sizing. However, a working knowledge of energy-flow equations and DOH is **mandatory** for the 14-mark questions.

## 1.4 Why HEVs Matter — Real-World Justification

The global push toward decarbonization, the **Corporate Average Fuel Economy (CAFE)** norms, and Euro-VI / BS-VI emission standards make HEVs the **transitional bridge technology** between pure ICE vehicles and pure Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs). The HEV requires no external charging infrastructure (in conventional form), delivers 30–50 % fuel economy improvement, and cuts tailpipe CO₂ emissions substantially.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Classification of HEVs

KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly requires understanding the **three primary HEV architectures**:

### (a) Series Hybrid
- **Configuration:** ICE → Generator → Battery → Motor → Wheels
- **Mechanical link:** The ICE is **never mechanically connected** to the wheels.
- **Operation logic:** The ICE drives a generator that produces electricity; the electricity either charges the battery or directly powers the traction motor.
- **Analogy:** Like a diesel locomotive — the prime mover only makes electricity.

### (b) Parallel Hybrid
- **Configuration:** ICE and Motor both connected to the wheels through a **mechanical coupling** (clutch or planetary gear).
- **Mechanical link:** Either source — or both combined — can propel the vehicle directly.
- **Operation logic:** Both power sources share the torque demand simultaneously.
- **Analogy:** Two horses pulling the same cart, side by side.

### (c) Series-Parallel (Power-Split / Combined) Hybrid
- **Configuration:** Uses a **planetary gear set (epicyclic gearbox)** as a power-split device (Toyota Hybrid System — THS).
- **Mechanical link:** Functions as a series hybrid at low speeds and as a parallel hybrid at high speeds.
- **Operation logic:** A planetary gear continuously splits the ICE's power between a generator path and a mechanical path to the wheels.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite:** The Toyota Prius-style **Power-Split Hybrid** is the most-asked configuration in board examinations because it integrates both topologies.

## 2.2 Core Components of an HEV

| S.No. | Component | Primary Function | Engineering Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) | Primary high-energy-density power source | Provides sustained cruising power |
| 2 | Electric Motor (EM) | Provides traction torque & regenerative braking | High low-end torque, bidirectional |
| 3 | Electric Generator | Converts mechanical energy to electrical energy | Charges battery during series mode |
| 4 | Traction Battery Pack | Stores electrical energy electrochemically | Governs electric-only range |
| 5 | Power Electronics Inverter | DC ↔ AC conversion at variable frequency | Controls motor/generator speed |
| 6 | DC-DC Converter | Steps down high-voltage DC to 12 V auxiliary | Powers accessories |
| 7 | Power Split Device (Planetary Gear) | Mechanically splits ICE torque | Enables power-split hybrid |
| 8 | Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) / PCU | Supervisory energy management brain | Decides ICE/EM mode every 10 ms |

## 2.3 Operating Modes of an HEV (Bulleted Logic Steps)

1. **Engine Start / Stop Mode:** ICE shuts off at traffic lights; restarted instantly by the electric motor. This eliminates idle fuel waste.
2. **Battery-Only (EV) Mode:** Below a calibrated speed threshold, the ICE is off and the EM alone propels the vehicle. **Why?** Because ICE efficiency is very poor at low loads.
3. **Power Assist Mode:** During hard acceleration (e.g., overtaking), both the ICE and the EM supply torque simultaneously to meet peak demand.
4. **Regenerative Braking Mode:** During deceleration, the EM acts as a generator, converting kinetic energy into electrical energy stored in the battery.
5. **Battery Charging Mode:** When SOC drops below threshold and vehicle speed is steady, the ICE delivers extra torque that drives the generator to replenish the battery.
6. **Power Split Mode (Series-Parallel only):** The planetary gear continuously varies the ICE operating point to its **Best Efficiency Operating Line (BEOL)**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **high-yield** for KTU Part B 14-mark numerical questions.

| S.No. | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Engineering Use |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | \(P_{demand}(t) = F_{trac}(t) \cdot v(t)\) | \(P\): power, \(F_{trac}\): tractive force, \(v\): velocity | Instantaneous propulsion power |
| 2 | \(F_{trac} = m g \sin\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + \mu_r m g \cos\theta + m a\) | \(m\): mass, \(g\): gravity, \(\theta\): slope, \(\rho\): air density, \(C_d\): drag coefficient, \(A\): frontal area, \(\mu_r\): rolling resistance coefficient, \(a\): acceleration | Resistive force model |
| 3 | \(\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{P_{wheels}}{P_{fuel}}\) | \(\eta\): efficiency | Quantifies fuel-to-wheel conversion |
| 4 | \(DOH = \dfrac{P_{EM,max}}{P_{EM,max} + P_{ICE,max}}\) | DOH: degree of hybridization | Classifies micro/mild/full/strong hybrid |
| 5 | \(E_{regen} = \tfrac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2) \cdot \eta_{reg} \cdot \eta_{motor}\) | \(E\): regen energy recovered, \(\eta\): efficiencies | Braking energy recovery |
| 6 | \(SOC(t+\Delta t) = SOC(t) + \dfrac{\int_{t}^{t+\Delta t} I(\tau) \, d\tau}{Q_{max}}\) | \(I\): battery current, \(Q_{max}\): capacity | Battery state estimation |
| 7 | \(T_{wheels} = (T_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{trans} \cdot r_f) + (T_{EM} \cdot \eta_{trans} \cdot r_f)\) | \(r_f\): final drive ratio | Parallel hybrid torque summation |
| 8 | \(\eta_{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{P_{ICE}}\) | \(\dot{m}\): fuel mass flow rate | Brake-Specific Fuel Consumption |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Never use the pipe symbol `|` inside a math equation inside a markdown table — examiners' answer scripts often break formatting. Use \(\vert x \vert\) or \(\mid x \mid\) for absolute values.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

The HEV architecture underpins every modern **mild-hybrid (48 V)**, **full-hybrid**, and **plug-in hybrid (PHEV)** sold in India — from the **Maruti Grand Vitara Intelligent Electric Hybrid** to the **Toyota Urban Cruiser Hyryder**. The same mathematical energy-management framework is used in **hybrid buses**, **hybrid locomotives**, and **hybrid mining dump trucks**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Tractive Force Demand on a Flat Road

This derivation is a **high-frequency KTU question**. Students are expected to derive the fundamental tractive force equation that governs every HEV energy demand calculation.

> **Given Parameters**
> - Vehicle mass: \(m\) (kg)
> - Acceleration due to gravity: \(g = 9.81\) m/s²
> - Air density: \(\rho\) (kg/m³)
> - Drag coefficient: \(C_d\) (dimensionless)
> - Frontal area: \(A\) (m²)
> - Rolling resistance coefficient: \(\mu_r\) (dimensionless)
> - Vehicle speed: \(v\) (m/s)
> - Road gradient angle: \(\theta\) (radians)
> - Vehicle acceleration: \(a\) (m/s²)

> **Step 1 — Identify the resistive forces**
> The total resistive force opposing motion consists of four components:
> 1. Aerodynamic drag force: \(F_{drag} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2\)
> 2. Rolling resistance force: \(F_{roll} = \mu_r \, m g \cos\theta\)
> 3. Gradient force: \(F_{grade} = m g \sin\theta\)
> 4. Inertial force (Newton's second law): \(F_{acc} = m a\)

> **Step 2 — Sum the components**
> The total tractive force the powertrain must deliver at the wheel-road contact patch is the algebraic sum of all resistive forces, assuming acceleration is positive in the direction of motion:

$$
F_{trac}(t) = F_{drag} + F_{roll} + F_{grade} + F_{acc}
$$

$$
F_{trac}(t) = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + \mu_r \, m g \cos\theta + m g \sin\theta + m a
$$

> **Step 3 — Specialize for a flat road (\(\theta = 0\))**
> On a flat road, \(\sin\theta = 0\) and \(\cos\theta = 1\), so the gradient term vanishes and the rolling term simplifies:

$$
F_{trac,flat}(t) = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + \mu_r \, m g + m a
$$

> **Step 4 — Convert to instantaneous propulsion power**
> Power demand is force multiplied by velocity. Therefore:

$$
P_{demand}(t) = F_{trac}(t) \cdot v(t)
$$

$$
P_{demand}(t) = \left[ \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + \mu_r \, m g \cos\theta + m g \sin\theta + m a \right] \cdot v(t)
$$

> **Step 5 — The hybrid energy-split equation**
> For a parallel HEV, the instantaneous propulsion power is jointly supplied by the ICE and the EM:

$$
P_{demand}(t) = \dfrac{P_{ICE}(t)}{\eta_{ICE}} + \dfrac{P_{EM}(t)}{\eta_{EM}}
$$

> **Step 6 — Energy over a drive cycle**
> Integrating power over a time interval \(T\) gives the total energy consumed:

$$
E_{consumed} = \int_{0}^{T} P_{demand}(t) \, dt
$$

> **Step 7 — Apply the fuel-to-wheel efficiency**
> The fuel energy equivalent is the propulsion energy divided by the overall powertrain efficiency:

$$
E_{fuel} = \dfrac{E_{consumed}}{\eta_{overall}}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The HEV powertrain controller's primary objective is to **minimize \(E_{fuel}\)** by maintaining the ICE on its **Best Efficiency Operating Line (BEOL)** while opportunistically storing surplus energy in the battery and reusing it during acceleration.

## 3.2 Derivation: Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery

> **Given**
> - Initial vehicle speed: \(v_1\) (m/s)
> - Final vehicle speed after braking: \(v_2\) (m/s)
> - Vehicle mass: \(m\) (kg)
> - Regenerative braking efficiency: \(\eta_{reg}\) (dimensionless, between 0 and 1)
> - Electric motor efficiency: \(\eta_{motor}\) (dimensionless)

> **Step 1 — Kinetic energy lost during deceleration**
> By the work-energy theorem, the kinetic energy dissipated by the vehicle's motion is:

$$
\Delta KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m v_1^2 - \tfrac{1}{2} m v_2^2
$$

$$
\Delta KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)
$$

> **Step 2 — Energy captured by the motor-generator**
> Only a fraction of the dissipated kinetic energy is recoverable due to mechanical and electrical losses. The fraction is governed by \(\eta_{reg}\):

$$
E_{captured} = \Delta KE \cdot \eta_{reg}
$$

> **Step 3 — Energy stored in the battery**
> The motor itself is not 100 % efficient; part of the captured energy is lost as copper and iron losses. Hence:

$$
E_{stored} = E_{captured} \cdot \eta_{motor}
$$

$$
E_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2) \cdot \eta_{reg} \cdot \eta_{motor}
$$

> **Step 4 — Percentage fuel saving estimate**
> If this recovered energy replaces an equivalent amount of fuel energy, the percentage fuel saving is:

$$
\% \text{Fuel Saving} = \dfrac{E_{stored}}{E_{fuel,baseline}} \times 100
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** On a typical urban drive cycle, regenerative braking contributes **15–25 %** of total energy savings. This is why HEVs are dramatically more efficient in **city driving** than on highways.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | Dominant Architecture | Energy Storage Mode | Best Efficiency Tactic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| City stop-and-go traffic | Series or Power-Split | Battery-assisted | Maximize regenerative braking |
| Highway cruising | Parallel | Steady SOC | Let ICE dominate, EM on standby |
| Hard acceleration / overtaking | Parallel or Power-Split | Battery-discharging | Power-assist mode (ICE + EM) |
| Long downhill descent | Series | Battery-charging | Maximum regenerative braking |
| Cold start | Series | Battery-discharging | EM starts the ICE |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Trick Question Pattern:** Examiners often ask *"Why is a series hybrid preferred for city buses?"* The expected answer is: **because the ICE can run at a single constant speed on its best-efficiency line while the electric motor handles all the variable-speed city driving.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Series Hybrid Powertrain — Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block renders the **block-level functional architecture** of a Series HEV. Note that the ICE is mechanically isolated from the wheels.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FUEL TANK] --> B[ICE ENGINE]
    B --> C[GENERATOR]
    C --> D[POWER ELECTRONICS INVERTER]
    D --> E[TRACTION BATTERY PACK]
    D --> F[DC-DC CONVERTER]
    E --> D
    D --> G[ELECTRIC TRACTION MOTOR]
    G --> H[FINAL DRIVE AND WHEELS]
    F --> I[AUXILIARY 12V LOADS]
    J[VEHICLE CONTROL UNIT] -. Controls .-> B
    J -. Controls .-> D
    J -. Controls .-> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Solid arrows represent **energy flow**, dashed arrows represent **control signal flow**. The arrows from the VCU indicate supervisory decisions sent every ~10 milliseconds.

## 4.2 Parallel Hybrid Powertrain — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FUEL TANK] --> B[ICE ENGINE]
    B --> C[TORQUE COUPLER OR CLUTCH]
    C --> D[TRANSMISSION]
    D --> E[FINAL DRIVE AND WHEELS]
    F[HIGH VOLTAGE BATTERY] --> G[INVERTER]
    G --> H[ELECTRIC MOTOR]
    H --> C
    I[VCU PCU] -. Controls .-> B
    I -. Controls .-> G
    I -. Controls .-> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Notice how both the ICE and the EM feed into the **same torque coupler** before reaching the transmission. This is the defining feature of the parallel hybrid topology.

## 4.3 Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FUEL TANK] --> B[ICE ENGINE]
    B --> C[PLANETARY GEAR POWER SPLIT DEVICE]
    C --> D[FINAL DRIVE]
    D --> E[ROAD WHEELS]
    C --> F[GENERATOR MG1]
    F --> G[INVERTER RECTIFIER]
    G --> H[TRACTION BATTERY]
    H --> G
    G --> I[TRACTION MOTOR MG2]
    I --> D
    J[HYBRID CONTROL UNIT] -. Controls .-> B
    J -. Controls .-> F
    J -. Controls .-> I
    J -. Controls .-> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The **planetary gear (sun, planet carrier, ring)** is the heart of the power-split device. ICE torque enters the carrier, exits partially to the ring (mechanical path to wheels) and partially to the sun (electrical path through MG1 generator).

## 4.4 Operating Mode Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START] --> B{Vehicle Speed and SOC?}
    B -- Low Speed AND High SOC --> C[Battery Only EV Mode]
    B -- High Speed OR Low SOC --> D{High Torque Demand?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Power Assist ICE plus EM Mode]
    D -- No --> F[Steady Cruising ICE Only Mode]
    C --> G{Brake Pedal Pressed?}
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G -- Yes AND SOC Below Limit --> H[Regenerative Braking Mode]
    G -- No --> I[End of Decision Cycle]
    H --> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** This flowchart mirrors the **finite-state machine** implemented inside the Toyota Hybrid System's supervisory controller. Students should reproduce this flowchart in their 14-mark answers for full credit.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question carries **3 marks** and expects a crisp, 4-to-6 sentence answer with a labelled diagram if applicable.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Differentiate between a Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle and a Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Series HEV** uses the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) only as a generator-driver; the ICE never drives the wheels mechanically. The generator charges a battery, which then powers a single electric traction motor coupled to the wheels. This topology is ideal for **city stop-and-go driving**, where the ICE can be maintained at its most efficient operating point.

A **Parallel HEV**, by contrast, mechanically couples both the ICE and the Electric Motor to the driveline through a clutch or torque-coupler. Either or both can propel the vehicle. This topology is more efficient at **steady highway cruising** because it avoids the double energy conversion losses (mechanical → electrical → mechanical) inherent to the series design.

| Parameter | Series HEV | Parallel HEV |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mechanical ICE-Wheel link | Absent | Present |
| Number of traction motors | 1 | 1 (or more) |
| City cycle efficiency | High | Moderate |
| Highway efficiency | Moderate | High |
| Powertrain complexity | Lower | Higher |

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: What is regenerative braking? Explain its significance in a Hybrid Electric Vehicle.**

**Model Answer:**
**Regenerative braking** is the process of converting the vehicle's kinetic energy during deceleration into electrical energy using the traction motor operating as a generator. The recovered energy is stored in the traction battery instead of being dissipated as heat through friction brakes.

Its **significance** is twofold. First, it improves **fuel economy** by 15–25 % on urban drive cycles. Second, it **reduces brake wear** because the friction brakes engage only during heavy braking or at very low speeds. Together, these benefits extend driving range, lower operating cost, and reduce particulate emissions from brake dust.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates **internal choice** in Part B. Two fully-independent alternatives are provided below. Each sub-question carries 7 marks.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle**. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hybrid car of mass **1500 kg** is travelling at a constant speed of **60 km/h** on a flat road. The rolling resistance coefficient is **0.015**, the drag coefficient is **0.30**, and the frontal area is **2.2 m²**. Air density is **1.2 kg/m³**. Calculate the tractive force and the propulsion power required at this speed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

> A **Series HEV** has the following functional chain:

$$
\text{Fuel} \rightarrow \text{ICE} \rightarrow \text{Generator} \rightarrow \text{Inverter} \rightarrow \text{Battery} \rightarrow \text{Inverter} \rightarrow \text{Traction Motor} \rightarrow \text{Wheels}
$$

> **Advantages:**
> 1. The ICE can operate at a fixed point on its best-efficiency curve → high fuel economy.
> 2. No mechanical transmission complexity between ICE and wheels → simpler driveline.
> 3. Smooth electric-only launch → better city drivability.

> **Limitations:**
> 1. Three energy conversions (mech → elec → mech) cause higher cumulative losses at high power.
> 2. The traction motor must be sized for **peak** power demand, increasing cost.
> 3. Larger battery pack required compared to a parallel hybrid of equivalent performance.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- \(m = 1500\) kg
- \(v = 60 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{60 \times 1000}{3600} = 16.667\) m/s
- \(\mu_r = 0.015\)
- \(C_d = 0.30\)
- \(A = 2.2\) m²
- \(\rho = 1.2\) kg/m³
- \(g = 9.81\) m/s²
- Flat road: \(\theta = 0\), so \(a = 0\)

**Step 1 — Aerodynamic Drag Force**

$$
F_{drag} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2
$$

$$
F_{drag} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.2 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (16.667)^2
$$

$$
F_{drag} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 277.78
$$

$$
F_{drag} = 0.396 \times 277.78 \approx 110.0 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Rolling Resistance Force**

$$
F_{roll} = \mu_r \, m g = 0.015 \times 1500 \times 9.81
$$

$$
F_{roll} = 22.5 \times 9.81 \approx 220.7 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Grade and Inertial Forces**

Since the road is flat and speed is constant:
- \(F_{grade} = 0\) N
- \(F_{acc} = m a = 0\) N

**Step 4 — Total Tractive Force**

$$
F_{trac} = F_{drag} + F_{roll} + F_{grade} + F_{acc}
$$

$$
F_{trac} = 110.0 + 220.7 + 0 + 0
$$

$$
F_{trac} \approx 330.7 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Propulsion Power**

$$
P_{demand} = F_{trac} \cdot v = 330.7 \times 16.667
$$

$$
P_{demand} \approx 5512 \text{ W} \approx 5.51 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** Tractive force ≈ **330.7 N**, Propulsion power ≈ **5.51 kW** (≈ 7.4 hp).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Q: (a)** Explain the working of the **Toyota Hybrid System (THS)** with a planetary gear power-split device. How does it combine the benefits of series and parallel hybrids? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An HEV of mass **1200 kg** decelerates from **80 km/h to 40 km/h** on a level road using regenerative braking. If the regenerative braking efficiency is **0.65** and the motor efficiency is **0.90**, calculate the energy recovered and stored in the battery. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

> The **Toyota Hybrid System (THS)** uses an **epicyclic (planetary) gear set** as a continuously variable power-split device.
>
> - The **planet carrier** is driven by the ICE.
> - The **sun gear** drives **MG1 (generator)**.
> - The **ring gear** drives **MG2 (traction motor)** and the wheels through a final reduction.
>
> Because of the kinematic constraint of the planetary gear, the ICE speed is the weighted average of MG1 and MG2 speeds. By electronically varying MG1's speed (and hence its generator torque), the controller can continuously vary the **ICE operating point** to remain on its **Best Efficiency Operating Line (BEOL)** regardless of vehicle speed.
>
> At low speeds, MG1 acts as a generator and the system behaves like a **Series HEV** (most ICE power flows electrically through MG1 → MG2). At high steady speeds, MG2 is driven directly by the ring gear and the ICE, behaving like a **Parallel HEV**. Thus THS seamlessly combines the advantages of both architectures.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- \(m = 1200\) kg
- \(v_1 = 80 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{80 \times 1000}{3600} = 22.222\) m/s
- \(v_2 = 40 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{40 \times 1000}{3600} = 11.111\) m/s
- \(\eta_{reg} = 0.65\)
- \(\eta_{motor} = 0.90\)

**Step 1 — Kinetic Energy Dissipated**

$$
\Delta KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)
$$

$$
\Delta KE = 0.5 \times 1200 \times \left[(22.222)^2 - (11.111)^2 \right]
$$

$$
\Delta KE = 600 \times (493.83 - 123.46)
$$

$$
\Delta KE = 600 \times 370.37
$$

$$
\Delta KE \approx 222,222 \text{ J} \approx 222.22 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy Captured by the Motor-Generator**

$$
E_{captured} = \Delta KE \cdot \eta_{reg} = 222,222 \times 0.65
$$

$$
E_{captured} \approx 144,444 \text{ J} \approx 144.44 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy Stored in the Battery**

$$
E_{stored} = E_{captured} \cdot \eta_{motor} = 144,444 \times 0.90
$$

$$
E_{stored} \approx 130,000 \text{ J} \approx 130.0 \text{ kJ}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** Energy recovered and stored in the battery ≈ **130 kJ** (≈ **36 Wh**). This is sufficient to propel the vehicle for several hundred metres in EV-only mode.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfalls where KTU students routinely lose marks:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s in velocity-dependent equations. The examiner's key strictly enforces SI units.
> 2. **Ignoring Air Drag at High Speeds (–1 mark):** At 60 km/h+, the drag term contributes ~25 % of tractive force. Omitting it is penalized.
> 3. **Confusing Generator and Motor Roles (–2 marks):** In a series HEV, the ICE drives a generator, not a motor. The traction motor is a separate unit.
> 4. **Missing the Planetary Gear Explanation (–3 marks):** For THS questions, examiners specifically look for **sun, planet carrier, ring gear** identification. Vague answers lose marks.
> 5. **No Diagram for 7-mark Sub-Questions (–2 marks):** Even a hand-drawn block diagram is mandatory for full credit. Always label arrows as "energy flow" vs. "control signal".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1 / Hybrid Electric Vehicle**

- [ ] An HEV combines **ICE + Electric Motor + Battery + Power Electronics + Controller**.
- [ ] **Three architectures**: Series, Parallel, and Series-Parallel (Power-Split). Know the diagram of each.
- [ ] **Series HEV:** ICE is mechanically isolated from wheels; best for **city buses**.
- [ ] **Parallel HEV:** ICE and EM both connect mechanically to wheels; best for **highway cruising**.
- [ ] **Power-Split (THS):** Uses a planetary gear to behave as both series and parallel; most fuel-efficient across full driving envelope.
- [ ] **Six operating modes**: Stop-Start, EV-only, Power-Assist, Regenerative, Charging, Power-Split.
- [ ] **Tractive force equation** has four components: drag, rolling resistance, gradient, inertia. Memorize it.
- [ ] **Regenerative braking** recovers \(\tfrac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)\) × \(\eta_{reg}\) × \(\eta_{motor}\).
- [ ] **Degree of Hybridization (DOH)** = \(\dfrac{P_{EM}}{P_{EM} + P_{ICE}}\) — classifies micro / mild / full / plug-in hybrids.
- [ ] **State of Charge (SOC)** must remain in a safe band (typically 30–80 %) for battery longevity.
- [ ] **Always convert km/h → m/s** by multiplying by \(\dfrac{5}{18}\) before substituting into any SI formula.
- [ ] **Always label diagrams** with arrow types (energy vs. signal) to score full marks in KTU valuation.
- [ ] **Toyota Prius (1997)** was the world's first mass-produced HEV using THS.
- [ ] **THS** stands for **Toyota Hybrid System**; the two motor-generators are **MG1** (generator) and **MG2** (traction motor).
- [ ] **Planetary gear** in THS: ICE on **carrier**, MG1 on **sun**, MG2 + wheels on **ring**.
- [ ] **Best Efficiency Operating Line (BEOL)** is the locus of BSFC minima on an engine map — the controller's primary target.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types - Series and Parallel Hybrid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT612 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types - Series and Parallel Hybrid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Modern Automotive Technologies*, a **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)** is classified by the **architectural path through which tractive (propulsion) power reaches the wheels**. The two foundational architectures are:

**Series Hybrid Drive (SHD):** A configuration in which the **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) is mechanically decoupled from the driven wheels**. The ICE drives an electric generator only; the resulting electrical energy either charges the battery or is fed directly (through a power electronics converter) to a single Traction Motor that alone propels the vehicle.

**Parallel Hybrid Drive (PHD):** A configuration in which the **ICE and the Electric Motor are mechanically coupled to the driven wheels through a common transmission**. Either source may propel the vehicle independently, or both may deliver power simultaneously to meet peak load demand.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Terminology:** The ICE in a Series Hybrid is often called a **"Range Extender"** or **"Auxiliary Power Unit (APU)"** because it never sees the road load directly — it only maintains battery state-of-charge (SOC).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Two Commute Styles:**
>
> 🚌 **Series Hybrid = The "Bus + Charger" Office Worker**
> Imagine a person whose only job is to earn money. They sit on a bus (the **electric motor**) that drives them to work. The bus is powered by a battery, and there is a small petrol generator at home (the **ICE + generator**) whose *only* job is to recharge the battery. The petrol engine **never pushes the car directly** — it only feeds electricity. The wheels are turned exclusively by the electric motor.
>
> 🚴 **Parallel Hybrid = The "Bicycle + Electric Booster" Delivery Rider**
> Imagine a cyclist who normally pedals (the **ICE**) to deliver parcels. On a steep hill, they switch on a small electric motor bolted to the rear wheel (the **electric motor**). Sometimes they pedal alone, sometimes the motor alone (e.g., in traffic), and on the steepest hill, **both** work together to reach the top faster. Both the legs (ICE) and the motor are **directly connected to the rear wheel**.

## 1.3 Salient Metric Standards Used in KTU Evaluation

| Metric | Typical Series HEV | Typical Parallel HEV | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Typical ICE power share | **0 % at wheel (only APU)** | **40 – 80 %** | % |
| Traction motor peak power | **100 %** | **30 – 60 %** | kW |
| Battery voltage (modern) | **200 – 800** | **100 – 400** | V (DC) |
| Mechanical link ICE–Wheel | **None** | **Yes (clutch/gear)** | — |
| Typical example (OEM) | Chevrolet Volt (Gen 1), BMW i3 REx | Honda Insight, Hyundai Ioniq Hybrid | — |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of the **Series Hybrid**

The power-flow is **strictly one-directional along the electrical bus**:

### Step-by-Step Operating Principle
- The ICE is operated at its **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) sweet-spot** (a narrow rpm-torque map region of minimum fuel use), *independent of vehicle speed*.
- Mechanical energy from the ICE → **Electrical energy** in the generator → routed through an **Inverter / DC-DC Converter** → either recharges the **High-Voltage Battery Pack** or directly powers the **Traction Motor**.
- The Traction Motor (almost always a **3-phase AC induction or PMSM**) converts electrical energy back into mechanical energy at the wheels via a **single fixed-ratio transmission** (often called a *reduction gearbox*).
- **Regenerative Braking** is naturally simple: kinetic energy → generator mode of traction motor → battery.

### Why the Series Architecture Works Well
- The ICE can be **gated ON/OFF** — when the battery SOC is high (e.g., urban driving), the ICE is shut off completely → **Zero Tailpipe Emission (ZTE)** in urban zones.
- No complex multi-speed gearbox needed — a single reduction gear is sufficient.
- Ideal for **stop-and-go city duty cycles** (e.g., the Indian Modified Indian Driving Cycle, MIDC).

### Limitations of the Series Architecture
- **Double energy conversion** (mechanical → electrical → mechanical) incurs combined losses of roughly **15 – 25 %**.
- The Traction Motor must be **sized for the entire peak vehicle load** (e.g., 100 – 150 kW for a mid-size car) — heavy and costly.
- At sustained highway speeds, where the ICE could otherwise operate efficiently, the series layout is *thermodynamically wasteful*.

## 2.2 Operational Logic of the **Parallel Hybrid**

The power-flow is **dual-path mechanical** with one electrical branch:

### Step-by-Step Operating Principle
- The ICE and the Electric Motor are joined to a **common driveline** through a **Torque Coupler** (planetary gearset in Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive, or a simple clutch in mild hybrids).
- **Operating Modes (KTU-Favorite Question Area):**
  - **Mode 1 — ICE Only:** Motor is decoupled; vehicle runs purely on petrol/diesel. Used during steady highway cruising.
  - **Mode 2 — Motor Only (EV Mode):** ICE is decoupled; vehicle runs as a pure BEV. Used at low speeds, parking, reverse.
  - **Mode 3 — Combined (Assist) Mode:** Both deliver torque simultaneously to overcome peak demand (e.g., overtaking, hill climbing).
  - **Mode 4 — Regenerative Braking:** Motor acts as generator, ICE is decoupled.
  - **Mode 5 — Battery Charging Mode (Engine-driven):** ICE drives the wheels *and* the motor (now operating as generator) to charge the battery.
- A **clutch** or **planetary gear set (PGS)** allows the engagement/disengagement between ICE and the driveline.

### Why the Parallel Architecture Works Well
- ICE can drive the wheels **directly** at highway cruise → near-ideal thermodynamic efficiency.
- Traction motor can be **downsized** (typically 30 – 60 % of total peak power) — lighter and cheaper.
- Better suited for **highway-dominated Indian driving profiles**.

### Limitations of the Parallel Architecture
- The ICE **cannot be freely decoupled from road load** — it must operate across a wide rpm/torque range, so it cannot always sit at its BSFC sweet spot.
- Requires a **complex transmission / clutch logic**.
- Cannot run the ICE at a single optimum point while simultaneously delivering zero tailpipe emissions at the wheel.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Relation | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{\text{wheel}} \;=\; P_{\text{ICE,to wheel}} \;+\; P_{\text{motor,to wheel}}$ | Power balance at the wheel (Parallel) | kW |
| 2 | $P_{\text{wheel}} \;=\; \eta_m \cdot P_{\text{electrical}}$ | Series: only path is electrical → mechanical | kW |
| 3 | $P_{\text{electrical}} \;=\; \eta_{\text{gen}} \cdot P_{\text{ICE,mech}}$ | Generator output (Series path) | kW |
| 4 | $P_{\text{fuel}} \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV}}{\Delta t}$ | Chemical → mechanical input power | kW |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{overall,series}} \;\approx\; \eta_{\text{ICE}} \cdot \eta_{\text{gen}} \cdot \eta_{\text{inv}} \cdot \eta_{\text{motor}} \cdot \eta_{\text{trans}}$ | Combined efficiency of Series | — |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{overall,parallel}} \;\approx\; x \cdot \eta_{\text{ICE,direct}} \;+\; (1-x) \cdot \eta_{\text{motor,path}}$ | Weighted by power-split ratio $x$ | — |
| 7 | $F_{\text{req}} \;=\; m g \sin\theta \;+\; \dfrac{1}{2}\, \rho\, C_d\, A\, v^2 \;+\; \mu_r\, m g$ | Tractive force at wheels (resistive loads) | N |
| 8 | $P_{\text{req}} \;=\; F_{\text{req}} \cdot v \;/\; 1000$ | Required propulsion power | kW |
| 9 | $\Delta \text{SOC} \;=\; \dfrac{\int (V_{\text{bat}} I_{\text{bat}}) \, dt}{E_{\text{bat,rated}}}$ | Battery state-of-charge swing | dimensionless |
| 10 | $E_{\text{kin}} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, m\, v^2$ | Energy recoverable by regen braking | J |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize equations 1, 2, 5, 7, 8 and 10.** These are the most frequently appearing derivations and numericals in KTU ESE Module-1 questions on HEV topologies.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Series** topology is the foundation of **Range-Extended Electric Vehicles (REEV)** such as the Chevrolet Volt Gen-1, BMW i3 with Range Extender, and several upcoming Indian last-mile delivery LCVs (e.g., Mahindra eVerito with optional RE).
- **Parallel** topology is the workhorse of mass-market hybrids — Toyota Prius (with planetary = *power-split*, a parallel-derivative), Honda City Hybrid, Maruti Suzuki Grand Vitara Intelligent Electric Hybrid.
- Understanding the two topologies is the prerequisite to understanding the **Series-Parallel (Power-Split / PSHEV)** architecture that combines both — a frequent 14-mark KTU question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Overall Efficiency of a Series Hybrid Drive

We derive the compounded efficiency of a Series HEV where energy flows through **three** energy-conversion stages before reaching the wheels.

**Step 1 — Chemical to Mechanical (ICE):**
The ICE converts fuel chemical energy into shaft mechanical energy with an indicated-plus-mechanical efficiency $\eta_{\text{ICE}}$.

$$P_{\text{shaft,ICE}} \;=\; \eta_{\text{ICE}} \cdot \dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV}$$

*Logic:* Fuel mass-flow rate $\dot{m}_f$ (kg/s) multiplied by its Lower Heating Value (LHV, J/kg) gives chemical power; multiplying by efficiency gives useful shaft power.

**Step 2 — Mechanical to Electrical (Generator):**
The generator converts shaft power to electrical power with efficiency $\eta_{\text{gen}}$.

$$P_{\text{elec,gen}} \;=\; \eta_{\text{gen}} \cdot P_{\text{shaft,ICE}} \;=\; \eta_{\text{gen}} \cdot \eta_{\text{ICE}} \cdot \dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV}$$

*Logic:* Each conversion multiplies the remaining power by its efficiency — this is the cascade rule.

**Step 3 — Electrical to Mechanical at the Wheels (Inverter + Motor + Transmission):**
The DC bus feeds a 3-phase inverter ($\eta_{\text{inv}}$), then the traction motor ($\eta_{\text{motor}}$), then the reduction gearbox ($\eta_{\text{trans}}$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{wheel}} \;&=\; \eta_{\text{inv}} \cdot \eta_{\text{motor}} \cdot \eta_{\text{trans}} \cdot P_{\text{elec,gen}} \\[4pt]
&=\; \underbrace{\eta_{\text{ICE}} \cdot \eta_{\text{gen}} \cdot \eta_{\text{inv}} \cdot \eta_{\text{motor}} \cdot \eta_{\text{trans}}}_{\eta_{\text{overall,series}}} \;\cdot\; \dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Typical Numerical Plug-in (KTU-Style):**

Take $\eta_{\text{ICE}} = 0.35$, $\eta_{\text{gen}} = 0.92$, $\eta_{\text{inv}} = 0.95$, $\eta_{\text{motor}} = 0.93$, $\eta_{\text{trans}} = 0.95$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{overall,series}} \;&=\; 0.35 \times 0.92 \times 0.95 \times 0.93 \times 0.95 \\[4pt]
&=\; 0.2683 \;\approx\; 26.83\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Interpretation:* Although each individual component is reasonably efficient, the product of five efficiencies yields a drivetrain efficiency of only about **27 %** — this is precisely *why* series HEVs are thermodynamically inferior for sustained highway driving.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Tractive Power for a Parallel HEV

A common KTU numerical is: *"A 1 400 kg parallel hybrid is cruising at 90 km/h on a 3° incline. Compute the required tractive power."*

**Step 1 — Convert units:**

$$
v \;=\; 90 \,\tfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}} \;=\; \dfrac{90 \times 1000}{3600} \;=\; 25 \,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the resistive-force equation:**

$$
F_{\text{req}} \;=\; F_{\text{grade}} \;+\; F_{\text{aero}} \;+\; F_{\text{roll}}
$$

**Step 3 — Component-wise substitution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{grade}} \;&=\; m\, g \sin\theta \;&=\; 1400 \times 9.81 \times \sin(3^\circ) \\[2pt]
&=\; 1400 \times 9.81 \times 0.05234 \;&=\; 718.8 \,\text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{aero}} \;&=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, \rho\, C_d\, A\, v^2 \\[2pt]
&=\; 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (25)^2 \\[2pt]
&=\; 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 625 \\[2pt]
&=\; 247.5 \,\text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{roll}} \;&=\; \mu_r\, m\, g \;=\; 0.015 \times 1400 \times 9.81 \;&=\; 206.0 \,\text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the resistive forces:**

$$
F_{\text{req}} \;=\; 718.8 \;+\; 247.5 \;+\; 206.0 \;=\; 1172.3 \,\text{N}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to tractive power:**

$$
P_{\text{req}} \;=\; \dfrac{F_{\text{req}} \cdot v}{1000} \;=\; \dfrac{1172.3 \times 25}{1000} \;=\; 29.31 \,\text{kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Power-Split for the Parallel Hybrid:**

If the supervisory controller decides to share the load **70 % ICE + 30 % Motor**, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{ICE,to wheel}} \;&=\; 0.70 \times 29.31 \;=\; 20.52 \,\text{kW} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{motor,to wheel}} \;&=\; 0.30 \times 29.31 \;=\; 8.79 \,\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Interpretation:* This is the **fundamental control problem** of a parallel HEV — the Energy Management Strategy (EMS) decides the optimal split $(x, 1-x)$ in real time, typically using rule-based (deterministic) or optimization-based (e.g., ECMS, Pontryagin's Minimum Principle) algorithms.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Quick-Revision Table)

| Parameter | Series Hybrid | Parallel Hybrid |
|---|---|---|
| ICE mechanical link to wheels | **No** | **Yes** |
| Traction motor size | **Large** (entire peak) | **Smaller** (assist only) |
| Number of energy conversions | **3 (chem→mech→elec→mech)** | **1 or 2** |
| Overall efficiency (typical) | **Lower (~27 %)** | **Higher (~33 – 38 %)** |
| Suitability — Urban duty cycle | **Excellent** | **Good** |
| Suitability — Highway duty cycle | **Poor** | **Excellent** |
| Transmission complexity | **Low** (single reduction) | **High** (clutch / PGS) |
| Tailpipe emission at wheel (pure EV mode) | **Zero** | **Zero** |
| Battery size requirement | **Larger** | **Smaller** |
| Example OEM application | Chevrolet Volt Gen 1 | Honda Insight, Toyota Prius (PSHEV) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Series Hybrid Drive — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    fuelTankA[fuelTankA: Fuel Tank]
    iceA[iceA: Internal Combustion Engine]
    genA[genA: Electric Generator]
    rectifierA[rectifierA: AC to DC Converter]
    dcBusA[dcBusA: High Voltage DC Bus]
    batteryA[batteryA: HV Battery Pack]
    inverterA[inverterA: DC to AC Inverter]
    motorA[motorA: Traction Motor PMSM]
    gearboxA[gearboxA: Single Speed Reducer]
    wheelsA[wheelsA: Driven Wheels]
    ctrlA[ctrlA: VCU Energy Management]

    fuelTankA --> iceA
    iceA --> genA
    genA --> rectifierA
    rectifierA --> dcBusA
    dcBusA --- batteryA
    dcBusA --> inverterA
    inverterA --> motorA
    motorA --> gearboxA
    gearboxA --> wheelsA
    ctrlA -.controls.-> iceA
    ctrlA -.controls.-> inverterA
    ctrlA -.controls.-> batteryA
```

*Reading the schematic:* Notice there is **no mechanical line from the ICE to the wheels**. The ICE only feeds electricity through the DC bus, which then powers the traction motor.

## 4.2 Parallel Hybrid Drive — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    fuelTankB[fuelTankB: Fuel Tank]
    iceB[iceB: Internal Combustion Engine]
    clutchB[clutchB: Clutch or Torque Coupler]
    batteryB[batteryB: HV Battery Pack]
    inverterB[inverterB: DC to AC Inverter]
    motorB[motorB: Traction Motor PMSM]
    transmissionB[transmissionB: Transmission]
    wheelsB[wheelsB: Driven Wheels]
    ctrlB[ctrlB: VCU Energy Management]

    fuelTankB --> iceB
    iceB --> clutchB
    clutchB --> transmissionB
    batteryB --> inverterB
    inverterB --> motorB
    motorB --> transmissionB
    transmissionB --> wheelsB
    ctrlB -.controls.-> clutchB
    ctrlB -.controls.-> motorB
    ctrlB -.controls.-> iceB
```

*Reading the schematic:* The ICE and the Motor feed into the **same transmission** — both can deliver torque to the wheels either independently or simultaneously. The clutch is the *engagement/disengagement gatekeeper*.

## 4.3 Combined Series-Parallel (PSHEV) — Conceptual Preview

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    iceC[iceC: ICE]
    genC[genC: Generator MG1]
    motorC[motorC: Traction Motor MG2]
    batteryC[batteryC: HV Battery]
    pgsC[pgsC: Planetary Gear Set Power Split]
    ringC[ringC: Ring Gear to Wheels]
    sunC[sunC: Sun Gear]
    carrierC[carrierC: Carrier Gear]

    iceC --> pgsC
    pgsC --> ringC
    pgsC --> sunC
    sunC --> genC
    carrierC --> iceC
    motorC --> ringC
    batteryC --- genC
    batteryC --- motorC
```

*Reading the schematic:* The Planetary Gear Set (PGS) acts as a **continuously variable power-split device**, sending some of the ICE's power mechanically to the wheels and the remainder electrically through MG1 → battery → MG2. This is the architecture used in the **Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive (THS-II)**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — **3 Marks**
**Differentiate between a Series Hybrid and a Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle in terms of mechanical coupling between the ICE and the wheels.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Series Hybrid | Parallel Hybrid |
|---|---|---|
| ICE to Wheel coupling | **No mechanical coupling** — ICE drives only the generator | **Direct mechanical coupling** via clutch/transmission |
| Sole propeller | Electric Traction Motor | Either ICE or Motor (or both together) |
| Power flow type | Series (single path) | Parallel (dual path) |

*Conclusion (1 mark):* The key distinguishing feature is the **absence vs. presence of a mechanical link** between the ICE and the driven wheels.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — **3 Marks**
**List any three operating modes of a Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **ICE-Only Mode** — vehicle propelled solely by the ICE; motor is decoupled.
2. **Motor-Only (EV) Mode** — ICE is decoupled; vehicle propelled solely by the traction motor; zero tailpipe emission.
3. **Combined (Assist) Mode** — both ICE and motor deliver torque simultaneously to meet peak load (e.g., overtaking, hill-climb).

*(Acceptable additional answers: Regenerative Braking Mode and Engine-Driven Battery Charging Mode.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module-1 Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT confuse "Series Hybrid" with "Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle definition based on circuit topology."** In KTU, "Series" refers to the **mechanical power-flow path**, not the electrical wiring.
> - **Do NOT omit the ICE-to-wheel decoupling point** in series diagrams — losing this costs ~3 marks.
> - In numericals, **always convert km/h to m/s** before plugging into $P = F \cdot v$. A unit error will cost the entire 7-mark sub-part.
> - When asked to "compare," use a **markdown table** — examiners award easy structural marks for clarity.
> - **Regenerative braking energy** ($E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$) appears in nearly every PHEV/Parallel-Hybrid numerical. Memorize it.

---

### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture and operating principle of a Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle**. List its **two main advantages and two main disadvantages**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **1 600 kg Parallel HEV** climbs a **4°** gradient at **60 km/h**. The road-load coefficient of rolling resistance is **0.012**, aerodynamic drag coefficient $C_d = 0.32$, frontal area $A = 2.3 \, \text{m}^2$, air density $\rho = 1.2 \, \text{kg/m}^3$. **(i)** Compute the **total tractive force** and **required propulsion power**. **(ii)** If the supervisory controller assigns **65 %** of this power to the ICE and the rest to the electric motor, calculate the **power delivered by each source**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Series HEV Architecture (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
[Fuel Tank] → [ICE] → [Generator] → [Rectifier] → [DC Bus] ⇄ [Battery]
                                                              ↓
                                                       [Inverter]
                                                              ↓
                                                     [Traction Motor]
                                                              ↓
                                                       [Reduction Gear]
                                                              ↓
                                                         [Wheels]
```

**Operating Principle (2 marks):**
The ICE operates at its **BSFC sweet-spot**, completely independent of vehicle speed. Its shaft drives a **3-phase generator** whose rectified output charges the **high-voltage battery** through the DC bus and/or directly powers the **traction motor** through the inverter. The traction motor is the **only** mechanical link to the wheels.

**Advantages (1 mark each, any two):**
1. No complex multi-speed transmission required.
2. ICE can be **switched off** during urban driving → **Zero Tailpipe Emission** in EV mode.

**Disadvantages (1 mark each, any two):**
1. Three energy conversions → compounded efficiency losses (~15 – 25 %).
2. Traction motor must be sized for the **entire** peak load → heavier and costlier.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Convert speed:**

$$
v \;=\; 60 \,\tfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}} \;=\; \dfrac{60 \times 1000}{3600} \;=\; 16.67 \,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute individual forces (2 marks):**

$$
F_{\text{grade}} \;=\; m g \sin\theta \;=\; 1600 \times 9.81 \times \sin(4^\circ) \;=\; 1600 \times 9.81 \times 0.0698 \;=\; 1095.3 \,\text{N}
$$

$$
F_{\text{aero}} \;=\; 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.32 \times 2.3 \times (16.67)^2 \;=\; 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.32 \times 2.3 \times 277.8 \;=\; 122.7 \,\text{N}
$$

$$
F_{\text{roll}} \;=\; \mu_r \, m \, g \;=\; 0.012 \times 1600 \times 9.81 \;=\; 188.4 \,\text{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Total tractive force (1 mark):**

$$
F_{\text{req}} \;=\; 1095.3 \;+\; 122.7 \;+\; 188.4 \;=\; 1406.4 \,\text{N}
$$

**Step 4 — Required propulsion power (1 mark):**

$$
P_{\text{req}} \;=\; \dfrac{F_{\text{req}} \cdot v}{1000} \;=\; \dfrac{1406.4 \times 16.67}{1000} \;=\; 23.44 \,\text{kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Power split (2 marks):**

$$
P_{\text{ICE}} \;=\; 0.65 \times 23.44 \;=\; 15.24 \,\text{kW}
$$

$$
P_{\text{motor}} \;=\; 0.35 \times 23.44 \;=\; 8.20 \,\text{kW}
$$

---

### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture and operating principle of a Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle**. List **any five operating modes**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Series HEV uses an ICE with BSFC efficiency $\eta_{\text{ICE}} = 0.32$, generator efficiency $\eta_{\text{gen}} = 0.90$, inverter efficiency $\eta_{\text{inv}} = 0.95$, traction motor efficiency $\eta_{\text{motor}} = 0.92$ and reduction gearbox efficiency $\eta_{\text{trans}} = 0.94$. The fuel LHV is **44 MJ/kg**. Compute **(i)** the **overall drivetrain efficiency**, and **(ii)** the **fuel mass-flow rate** required to deliver **30 kW at the wheels**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Parallel HEV Architecture (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
[Fuel Tank] → [ICE] ⇄ [Clutch] ┐
                                ├→ [Transmission] → [Wheels]
[Battery] → [Inverter] → [Motor] ┘
```

**Operating Principle (2 marks):**
The ICE and the Traction Motor are **mechanically coupled** to the same driveline through a clutch (or planetary gear set). The Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) decides, based on accelerator pedal position, battery SOC, and vehicle speed, whether to run ICE-only, motor-only, or both together.

**Five Operating Modes (2 marks — 0.4 mark each):**

1. **ICE-Only Mode** — steady highway cruise.
2. **Motor-Only (EV) Mode** — low-speed urban drive.
3. **Combined (Assist) Mode** — peak torque demand.
4. **Regenerative Braking Mode** — kinetic energy recovery.
5. **Engine-Driven Charging Mode** — ICE charges battery via motor-generator.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Overall efficiency (2 marks):**

$$
\eta_{\text{overall}} \;=\; 0.32 \times 0.90 \times 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.94 \;=\; 0.2419 \;\approx\; 24.19\,\%
$$

**Step 2 — Required fuel chemical power (2 marks):**

$$
P_{\text{fuel}} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{\text{wheel}}}{\eta_{\text{overall}}} \;=\; \dfrac{30}{0.2419} \;=\; 124.0 \,\text{kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Fuel mass-flow rate (2 marks):**

$$
\dot{m}_f \;=\; \dfrac{P_{\text{fuel}}}{\text{LHV}} \;=\; \dfrac{124.0 \times 10^3}{44 \times 10^6} \;=\; 2.82 \times 10^{-3} \,\tfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}} \;\approx\; 10.15 \,\tfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{h}}
$$

**Step 4 — Statement (1 mark):** To deliver 30 kW at the wheels, the engine must consume approximately **2.82 g of fuel per second** (or about **10.15 kg per hour**).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Series HEV** = ICE mechanically decoupled from wheels; ICE acts as **Range Extender / APU**; only the **Traction Motor** drives the wheels.
- **Parallel HEV** = ICE and Motor **both** mechanically coupled to wheels via a common **transmission / clutch / planetary gear set**.
- **Series is best for urban duty cycles** (ICE can be shut off in traffic); **Parallel is best for highway duty cycles** (no double energy conversion loss).
- The **five key Series components**: ICE, Generator, Rectifier/Converter, Battery, Inverter, Traction Motor.
- The **five key Parallel components**: ICE, Clutch, Traction Motor, Inverter, Battery, Transmission.
- **Five operating modes of Parallel HEV**: ICE-Only, Motor-Only (EV), Combined (Assist), Regenerative Braking, Engine-Driven Charging.
- **Overall Series efficiency** = product of five component efficiencies (typically ~25 – 28 %).
- **Overall Parallel efficiency** = ICE-direct-path dominates at cruise (~33 – 38 %).
- **Tractive force equation**: $F_{\text{req}} = m g \sin\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 + \mu_r m g$.
- **Power equation**: $P_{\text{req}} = F_{\text{req}} \cdot v \;\;/\;\; 1000$ (in kW when $v$ is in m/s).
- **Regenerative braking energy**: $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ (memorize this — appears in every parallel/regen question).
- **SOC swing equation**: $\Delta \text{SOC} = \dfrac{\int V_{\text{bat}} I_{\text{bat}}\, dt}{E_{\text{bat,rated}}}$.
- Always **convert km/h → m/s** by multiplying by **1000/3600 = 0.2778**.
- Always **include air-density** $\rho = 1.2 \,\text{kg/m}^3$ unless explicitly stated otherwise.
- **Series-Parallel (Power-Split / PSHEV)** = hybrid of the two; uses a **planetary gear set** as the power-split device; the modern industry-standard (Toyota THS-II).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Layouts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT612 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Layouts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Vehicle Layout?

In the context of Modern Automotive Engineering, a **Vehicle Layout** (also called **Drivetrain Configuration** or **Powertrain Arrangement**) refers to the specific physical placement of the major powertrain and drivetrain components of a motor vehicle — primarily the **engine**, **transmission**, and **driven wheels** (axles that receive power). The layout fundamentally dictates a vehicle's handling dynamics, packaging efficiency, weight distribution, manufacturing cost, and overall driving character.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Vehicle layout is the placement of the engine and driven wheels of a vehicle. It is a foundational concept that determines the dynamic behavior, packaging, and architecture of any automobile.*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a vehicle layout like the **architecture of a human body**:

- The **engine** is like the **heart** — it generates power.
- The **transmission & driveshaft** are like the **spinal cord & arteries** — they transmit that power.
- The **driven wheels** are like the **feet/legs** — they actually push the vehicle forward.

Just as humans can have different "body builds" (tall, short, muscular), vehicles can have different "layouts" — the heart (engine) can be placed in the front or rear, and the legs (driven wheels) can be in the front, rear, or all four. Each combination gives a different "personality" to the vehicle — some are nimble, some are stable, some are efficient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Nomenclature Convention:**
> The industry uses a **two-letter code** to describe every layout:
> - **1st letter** = Location of the **Engine** (F = Front, M = Mid, R = Rear)
> - **2nd letter** = Location of the **Driven Wheels** (F = Front, R = Rear, A = All)
>
> Example: **"FR"** = Front Engine, Rear-Wheel Drive.

### The Five Canonical Layouts Studied in KTU Module 1

| Code | Full Form | Common Nickname |
|------|-----------|-----------------|
| **FF** | Front Engine, Front-Wheel Drive | Front-Wheel Drive (FWD) |
| **FR** | Front Engine, Rear-Wheel Drive | Rear-Wheel Drive (RWD) |
| **RR** | Rear Engine, Rear-Wheel Drive | Rear-Engine RWD |
| **MR** | Mid Engine, Rear-Wheel Drive | Mid-Engine RWD |
| **AWD/4WD** | Engine + Power to All Wheels | All-Wheel Drive |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The textbook metric for layout classification is the **Weight Distribution Ratio (W_d)** between axles, typically expressed as a percentage split such as **50:50** (ideal sports car), **60:40** (FF economy cars), or **40:60** (FR performance cars).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Major Layouts — Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Front Engine, Front-Wheel Drive (FF / FWD)

**Operational Logic:**
The internal combustion engine is mounted transversely (sideways) or longitudinally at the front of the vehicle, and power is delivered to the **front wheels** through a transaxle (combined transmission + differential).

**Key Sub-Configurations:**
- **Transverse FF:** Engine and transmission sit perpendicular to the car's length. The most space-efficient — used in 90% of modern economy cars.
- ** Longitudinal FF:** Engine sits lengthwise, power transferred via inboard half-shafts to front wheels. Used in Audi, Mercedes compact cars.

**KTU Engineering Trade-off Summary:**

| Parameter | Verdict |
|-----------|---------|
| Interior Space | ✅ Excellent (no driveshaft tunnel) |
| Traction in rain/snow | ✅ Excellent (weight over driven wheels) |
| Manufacturing Cost | ✅ Lowest |
| Handling Limit | ❌ Understeer tendency |
| Steering Feel | ❌ Torque steer possible |

**Real-world examples:** Maruti Suzuki Swift, Hyundai i20, Honda City, Maruti Alto.

---

### 2.1.2 Front Engine, Rear-Wheel Drive (FR / RWD)

**Operational Logic:**
Engine mounted longitudinally at the front. A driveshaft (propeller shaft) runs the length of the vehicle to a rear differential, which then sends power to the **rear wheels** via half-shafts.

**Key Engineering Components:**
- **Propeller Shaft (Driveshaft):** A long rotating tube connecting transmission output to the rear differential.
- **Universal Joints (U-joints):** Allow the driveshaft to flex with suspension movement.
- **Rear Differential:** Reduces RPM and redirects power 90° to the rear wheels.

**KTU Engineering Trade-off Summary:**

| Parameter | Verdict |
|-----------|---------|
| Weight Distribution | ✅ Near 50:50 achievable |
| Handling Balance | ✅ Neutral / slight oversteer |
| Steering Feel | ✅ Pure (no driven front wheels) |
| Interior Space | ❌ Driveshaft tunnel intrudes |
| Traction on slippery surfaces | ❌ Poor (light rear end) |
| Cost & Complexity | ❌ Higher |

**Real-world examples:** BMW 3-Series, Mercedes C-Class, Toyota Fortuner, Ford Mustang.

---

### 2.1.3 Rear Engine, Rear-Wheel Drive (RR)

**Operational Logic:**
Engine is mounted at the **rear of the vehicle** (typically over or ahead of the rear axle), driving the **rear wheels**. The front wheels are non-driven steering wheels.

**Engineering Notes:**
- Eliminates the need for a driveshaft — engine directly couples to the rear wheels.
- Famous for use in the original Volkswagen Beetle and classic Porsche 911.

**KTU Engineering Trade-off Summary:**

| Parameter | Verdict |
|-----------|---------|
| Traction under acceleration | ✅ Excellent (weight over driven wheels) |
| Cabin Space | ✅ Maximized (no engine upfront) |
| Directional Stability | ❌ Poor at high speed (pendulum effect) |
| Cooling | ❌ Difficult (rear airflow is turbulent) |
| Crash Safety | ❌ Engine crumple zone compromised |

**Real-world examples:** Porsche 911 (classic), Tata Nano, Volkswagen Beetle (old), Mahindra Reva (early EVs).

---

### 2.1.4 Mid Engine, Rear-Wheel Drive (MR)

**Operational Logic:**
Engine is mounted **between the front and rear axles**, typically just ahead of the rear axle, driving the **rear wheels**.

**Engineering Notes:**
- The most ideal weight distribution achievable — close to perfect 50:50 split.
- Used almost exclusively in high-performance supercars and racing cars.

**KTU Engineering Trade-off Summary:**

| Parameter | Verdict |
|-----------|---------|
| Cornering Performance | ✅ Best-in-class |
| Weight Distribution | ✅ Near-perfect 50:50 |
| Packaging | ❌ Compromises both cabin and trunk |
| Cost | ❌ Very High |
| Maintenance Access | ❌ Very Difficult |

**Real-world examples:** Ferrari 488, Lamborghini Huracán, McLaren P1, Ford GT.

---

### 2.1.5 Four-Wheel Drive / All-Wheel Drive (4WD / AWD)

**Operational Logic:**
Power is delivered to **all four wheels** simultaneously, either continuously (AWD) or on-demand (4WD with low-range transfer case).

**Sub-Types:**
- **Part-time 4WD:** Driver-selectable; used in off-roaders (Suzuki Jimny, Mahindra Thar).
- **Full-time AWD:** Always engaged; used in crossovers and performance cars (Audi Quattro, Subaru Symmetrical AWD).
- **On-demand AWD:** Computer engages rear wheels when front slip detected (Honda CR-V AWD).

**KTU Engineering Trade-off Summary:**

| Parameter | Verdict |
|-----------|---------|
| Traction | ✅ Maximum possible |
| Off-road Capability | ✅ Unmatched |
| Fuel Economy | ❌ Lowest (drivetrain losses) |
| Cost & Weight | ❌ Highest |
| Complexity | ❌ Many components |

**Real-world examples:** Mahindra Thar, Toyota Land Cruiser, Audi Quattro, Subaru Outback.

---

## 2.2 Comparative Summary Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Feature | FF | FR | RR | MR | 4WD |
|---------|----|----|----|----|-----|
| Weight Bias | Front | 50:50–60:40 | Rear | 50:50 | Even |
| Handling Bias | Understeer | Neutral/Oversteer | Oversteer | Neutral | Variable |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Low | Highest | Highest |
| Traction (Slippery) | High | Low | High (accel) | High | Highest |
| Cabin Space | Best | Compromised | Best | Worst | Medium |
| Maint. Ease | Easy | Medium | Hard | Hardest | Hard |
| Typical Use | Economy cars | Luxury/Performance | Sports cars | Supercars | SUVs/Off-road |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Although "Layouts" is primarily descriptive, KTU questions occasionally test quantitative metrics of layouts.

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Unit |
|---|------------------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $W_d = \dfrac{W_f}{W_f + W_r} \times 100\%$ | Front weight distribution percentage | **%** |
| 2 | $L \cdot W \cdot H$ envelope | Vehicle packaging volume constraint | **mm³** |
| 3 | Overhang Ratio $\rho = \dfrac{L_{oh}}{L_{wb}}$ | Front or rear overhang / wheelbase | dimensionless |
| 4 | Polar Moment of Inertia $I_{zz} = \sum m_i r_i^2$ | Resistance to yaw (lower = better handling) | **kg·m²** |
| 5 | Weight Transfer $\Delta W = \dfrac{m \cdot a \cdot h}{L_{wb}}$ | Lateral/longitudinal weight transfer under acceleration | **N** |
| 6 | Drive Efficiency $\eta_d = \dfrac{\eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{diff} \cdot \eta_{final}}{100}$ | Drivetrain efficiency (typically 85–92% for FR, 90–95% for FF) | **%** |

> **Symbols:** $W_f$ = front axle weight, $W_r$ = rear axle weight, $L_{oh}$ = overhang length, $L_{wb}$ = wheelbase, $m$ = vehicle mass, $a$ = acceleration, $h$ = center of gravity height, $r_i$ = distance of mass $m_i$ from CG.

### Real-World Utility of Layout Engineering
- **Crashworthiness engineers** choose FF for compact crumple zone design.
- **Racing teams (F1, WRC)** choose MR for optimum weight distribution and polar moment of inertia.
- **EV manufacturers** are pioneering "skateboard" platforms — a new layout where battery floor doubles as structure, enabling FF, FR, MR, or AWD interchangeably.
- **Off-roaders (Thar, Defender, G-Wagon)** mandate 4WD for traction over uneven terrain.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Weight Distribution and Polar Moment of Inertia Relationship

A key KTU-style analytical question is: *"Why does an MR layout handle better than an FR or RR layout?"* The answer lies in the **polar moment of inertia about the vertical axis ($I_{zz}$)**.

### Step 1: Definition of Polar Moment of Inertia

The polar moment of inertia about the vertical (yaw) axis through the center of gravity is defined as:

$$
I_{zz} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \, r_i^2
$$

where $m_i$ is the mass of the $i^{th}$ component and $r_i$ is its perpendicular distance from the vertical yaw axis through the CG.

### Step 2: Approximation for a Simplified Vehicle

Treat the vehicle as a **two-point mass system**: the engine mass $m_e$ at the engine's longitudinal position $x_e$ from the CG, and the rest of the chassis mass $m_c$ concentrated at the CG (so $r_c = 0$).

$$
I_{zz} \approx m_e \, x_e^2
$$

### Step 3: Compare Three Layouts

Let the wheelbase $L_{wb} = 2.5 \text{ m}$, and the engine mass $m_e = 200 \text{ kg}$ (a typical 4-cylinder engine).

**Case 1 — FR Layout (engine at front, $x_e = -1.0 \text{ m}$):**

$$
I_{zz, FR} = 200 \times (-1.0)^2 = 200 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m}^2
$$

**Case 2 — RR Layout (engine at rear, $x_e = +1.0 \text{ m}$):**

$$
I_{zz, RR} = 200 \times (1.0)^2 = 200 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m}^2
$$

**Case 3 — MR Layout (engine at mid, $x_e \approx 0.0 \text{ m}$):**

$$
I_{zz, MR} = 200 \times (0.0)^2 \approx 0 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m}^2
$$

(plus a small positive contribution from the engine's lateral width)

### Step 4: Engineering Interpretation

- A **lower $I_{zz}$** means the vehicle **changes yaw direction more easily** and **responds faster to steering input**.
- MR essentially places the heaviest component (engine) at the rotational center → minimum rotational inertia → **most agile cornering**.
- FR and RR place the engine far from the CG → higher $I_{zz}$ → **slower directional changes but greater high-speed stability**.

### Step 5: Conclusion

$$
\boxed{I_{zz, MR} \ll I_{zz, FR} = I_{zz, RR}}
$$

This mathematical relationship is the **primary engineering justification** for placing the engine amidships in performance and racing vehicles.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Weight Transfer Under Braking/Acceleration

When a vehicle brakes or accelerates, mass shifts from one axle to the other. This is critical for understanding FF vs. FR traction.

### Step 1: Equilibrium Setup

Consider a vehicle of mass $m$ decelerating at $a$ (negative for braking). The normal forces on front ($N_f$) and rear ($N_r$) axles sum to total weight:

$$
N_f + N_r = m \cdot g
$$

### Step 2: Moment Balance About Rear Contact Patch

Taking moments about the rear tire contact patch (height $h$ above ground for CG):

$$
N_f \cdot L_{wb} = m \cdot g \cdot b + m \cdot a \cdot h
$$

where $b$ is the horizontal distance from CG to rear axle.

### Step 3: Solve for Front Axle Load Under Braking ($a = -d$)

$$
N_{f,\text{brake}} = \frac{m \cdot g \cdot b + m \cdot d \cdot h}{L_{wb}}
$$

### Step 4: Numerical Example

Let $m = 1500 \text{ kg}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $L_{wb} = 2.5 \text{ m}$, $b = 1.2 \text{ m}$, $h = 0.6 \text{ m}$, deceleration $d = 0.7g = 6.867 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Static front axle load:**

$$
N_{f,\text{static}} = \frac{1500 \times 9.81 \times 1.2}{2.5} = 7063.2 \text{ N}
$$

**Dynamic front axle load under braking:**

$$
N_{f,\text{brake}} = \frac{1500 \times 9.81 \times 1.2 + 1500 \times 6.867 \times 0.6}{2.5}
$$

$$
N_{f,\text{brake}} = \frac{17658 + 6180.3}{2.5} = \frac{23838.3}{2.5} = 9535.3 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 5: Interpretation

$$
\Delta N_f = 9535.3 - 7063.2 = 2472.1 \text{ N}
$$

The **front axle gained 2472 N** of vertical load. This is why:
- **FF vehicles brake better** (more load on driven front wheels).
- **FR vehicles understeer under heavy braking** (front tires overloaded, rear loses grip).

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark analytical question** pattern.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Layout to Choose?

| Design Priority | Best Layout | Reasoning |
|-----------------|-------------|-----------|
| Maximum interior space, low cost | **FF** | No driveshaft, transverse engine |
| Performance + balance | **FR** or **MR** | 50:50 weight, RWD dynamics |
| Maximum traction on slippery roads | **FF or AWD** | Weight over driven wheels |
| Off-road capability | **4WD** | Power to all wheels |
| Track/racing agility | **MR** | Minimum polar moment of inertia |
| Compact city car | **RR or FF** | Minimal footprint, simple |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Comparative Block Diagram of All Five Layouts

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FF["FF LAYOUT - Front Engine Front Drive"]
        FFEng[ENGINE FRONT] --> FFTrans[TRANSVERSE TRANSAXLE]
        FFTrans --> FFW1[FRONT LEFT WHEEL]
        FFTrans --> FFW2[FRONT RIGHT WHEEL]
    end

    subgraph FR["FR LAYOUT - Front Engine Rear Drive"]
        FREng[LONGITUDINAL ENGINE] --> FRTrans[TRANSMISSION]
        FRTrans --> FRDS[PROPELLER SHAFT]
        FRDS --> FRDiff[REAR DIFFERENTIAL]
        FRDiff --> FRW1[REAR LEFT WHEEL]
        FRDiff --> FRW2[REAR RIGHT WHEEL]
    end

    subgraph RR["RR LAYOUT - Rear Engine Rear Drive"]
        RREng[REAR ENGINE] --> RRTrans[REAR TRANSAXLE]
        RRTrans --> RRW1[REAR LEFT WHEEL]
        RRTrans --> RRW2[REAR RIGHT WHEEL]
    end

    subgraph MR["MR LAYOUT - Mid Engine Rear Drive"]
        MREng[MID ENGINE] --> MRTrans[MID TRANSAXLE]
        MRTrans --> MRW1[REAR LEFT WHEEL]
        MRTrans --> MRW2[REAR RIGHT WHEEL]
    end

    subgraph AWD["AWD LAYOUT - All Wheel Drive"]
        AWDEng[ENGINE] --> AWDTrans[CENTRAL TRANSFER CASE]
        AWDTrans --> AWDFront[FRONT DIFFERENTIAL]
        AWDTrans --> AWDRear[REAR DIFFERENTIAL]
        AWDFront --> AWDF1[FRONT LEFT WHEEL]
        AWDFront --> AWDF2[FRONT RIGHT WHEEL]
        AWDRear --> AWDR1[REAR LEFT WHEEL]
        AWDRear --> AWDR2[REAR RIGHT WHEEL]
    end
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How Layout Affects Vehicle Dynamics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[VEHICLE LAYOUT SELECTION] --> B{PRIMARY OBJECTIVE}
    B -->|Cost and Space| C[FF LAYOUT]
    B -->|Balance and Performance| D[FR OR MR LAYOUT]
    B -->|Traction and Off-Road| E[AWD OR 4WD LAYOUT]
    B -->|Compact Sport| F[RR LAYOUT]

    C --> C1[Transverse Engine]
    C --> C2[Inboard Half Shafts]
    C --> C3[Result: Understeer Tendency]

    D --> D1[Longitudinal Engine]
    D --> D2[Driveshaft Tunnel]
    D --> D3[Result: Neutral to Oversteer]

    E --> E1[Transfer Case with Center Diff]
    E --> E2[Front and Rear Axles Driven]
    E --> E3[Result: Maximum Traction]

    F --> F1[Rear Mounted Engine]
    F --> F2[Direct Coupling to Rear Wheels]
    F --> F3[Result: High Speed Instability]
```

## 4.3 Visual Schematic: Power Flow Path Comparison

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Engine Generates Torque] --> ROUTE{Which Layout}
    ROUTE -->|FF| P1[Direct to Transaxle]

    ROUTE -->|FR| P2[Transmission at Front]

    P2A --> P2B[Rear Differential]
    P2B --> P2C[Rear Wheels Push Vehicle Forward]

    ROUTE -->|MR| P3[Mid Mounted Engine]
    P3 --> P3A[Short Driveshaft to Rear]
    P3A --> P3B[Rear Wheels Receive Power]

    ROUTE -->|RR| P4[Rear Engine Direct Drive]
    P4 --> P4A[Rear Wheels Push Vehicle Forward]

    ROUTE -->|AWD| P5[Transfer Case Splits Power]
    P5 --> P5A[Front Wheels Get Fraction]
    P5A --> P5B[Rear Wheels Get Fraction]
```

   |  FRONT  |         |  REAR   |

        ^--- driveshaft --->
