# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Vehicle Layout. List any four common automotive layouts.
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 1 | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

A **vehicle layout** is the arrangement of the engine and driven wheels in a motor vehicle, which fundamentally determines its handling, packaging, and performance characteristics.

The four common automotive layouts are:
1. **FF** – Front Engine, Front-Wheel Drive
2. **FR** – Front Engine, Rear-Wheel Drive
3. **RR** – Rear Engine, Rear-Wheel Drive
4. **MR** – Mid Engine, Rear-Wheel Drive

(A fifth common layout is **AWD/4WD** – All-Wheel Drive.)

---

### Question 2: Why is FF layout the most common in modern economy cars?
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 1 | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The FF layout dominates economy cars due to **three engineering advantages**:

1. **Space Efficiency:** The transverse engine and integrated transaxle eliminate the need for a driveshaft tunnel, freeing up cabin and cargo space.
2. **Cost:** Fewer drivetrain components (no propeller shaft, no rear differential) reduce manufacturing cost and weight.
3. **Traction:** With the engine mass pressing down on the driven wheels, FF cars have superior grip on wet, snowy, or slippery roads — ideal for daily commuting.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the FR layout in detail. Discuss its advantages, disadvantages, and typical applications. (7 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 1 | Question Choice A | 7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
The **FR (Front Engine, Rear-Wheel Drive)** layout places the engine longitudinally at the front of the vehicle, with power transmitted to the **rear wheels** via a transmission, propeller shaft (driveshaft), and rear differential.

**Key Components:**
- Longitudinal engine (inline-4, inline-6, V8, etc.)
- Clutch or torque converter
- Transmission (manual or automatic)
- Propeller shaft with universal joints
- Rear differential (hypoid or bevel gear)
- Rear half-shafts

**Working Principle:**
The engine's rotational torque is sent to the transmission, which adjusts the torque-speed ratio. The output is then carried by a long driveshaft running beneath the vehicle floor to the rear differential, which redirects power 90° to the rear wheels.

**Advantages of FR Layout:**
1. **Balanced Weight Distribution:** A properly designed FR car achieves near 50:50 weight distribution, which is the ideal for handling.
2. **Superior Steering Feel:** Since the front wheels are not driven, they are dedicated to steering only — no torque steer.
3. **Better Handling Balance:** Under power, the rear wheels push while the front wheels steer — this allows the tail to rotate predictably (oversteer/neutral), giving drivers a connected feel.
4. **Higher Performance Ceiling:** FR is the preferred layout for sports sedans and performance cars (BMW, Mercedes).
5. **Easier Heavier-Duty Use:** Better for towing, hauling, and rear-wheel traction under acceleration.

**Disadvantages of FR Layout:**
1. **Driveshaft Tunnel:** The central transmission tunnel intrudes into the cabin, reducing rear-seat comfort and middle-passenger foot room.
2. **Rear Traction Issues:** On slippery surfaces, the lighter rear axle can lose grip during acceleration.
3. **Higher Weight and Cost:** More components mean more mass and higher manufacturing cost.
4. **Larger Footprint:** The longitudinal engine bay is longer than a transverse one.

**Typical Applications:**
- Luxury sedans: BMW 3/5 Series, Mercedes C/E-Class
- Sports cars: Ford Mustang, Mazda MX-5, Toyota Supra
- SUVs and trucks: Toyota Fortuner, Ford Endeavour, Tata Harrier
- Rear-wheel drive workhorses and utility vehicles

---

#### (b) Compare MR and RR layouts. Why is MR preferred for high-performance supercars? (7 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 1 | Question Choice A | 7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**MR Layout (Mid Engine, Rear-Wheel Drive):**
- Engine placed **between the axles**, just ahead of the rear axle
- Power delivered to the rear wheels
- Examples: Ferrari 488, Lamborghini Huracán, Ford GT

**RR Layout (Rear Engine, Rear-Wheel Drive):**
- Engine placed **behind the rear axle** (or directly over it)
- Power delivered to the rear wheels
- Examples: Classic Porsche 911, Tata Nano, original Volkswagen Beetle

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | MR Layout | RR Layout |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Weight Distribution | Near-perfect 50:50 | Heavily rear-biased (70:30 or worse) |
| Polar Moment of Inertia | Minimum (engine near CG) | High (engine far from CG) |
| Cornering Agility | Excellent | Poor (pendulum effect) |
| High-Speed Stability | Excellent | Poor (tail-wagging tendency) |
| Cabin Space | Limited (engine encroaches) | Maximum (no engine up front) |
| Cooling | Moderate (mid airflow) | Difficult (rear airflow is turbulent) |
| Crash Safety (Front Impact) | Good | Poor (no engine as crumple barrier) |
| Typical Use | Supercars, hypercars | Compact city cars, classic sports cars |

**Why MR is Preferred for High-Performance Supercars:**

1. **Optimal Weight Distribution:** MR achieves the coveted **50:50 front-rear weight balance**, which is the holy grail of vehicle dynamics — providing neutral handling and maximum tire utilization in both braking and cornering.

2. **Minimum Polar Moment of Inertia:** As derived in Section 3.1, placing the heaviest component (engine) at the center of gravity minimizes the vehicle's resistance to rotation. The car changes direction more rapidly and responds more crisply to steering input.

3. **Low Center of Gravity:** Mid-mounted engines are typically positioned low in the chassis, reducing the CG height. This **minimizes body roll** during cornering and **reduces weight transfer**, both of which improve cornering speed and stability.

4. **Direct Power Delivery:** The short drivetrain path from a mid-mounted engine to the rear wheels reduces power loss and provides instantaneous throttle response — critical for lap times.

5. **Engine Cooling & Service:** While not as well-cooled as a front engine, mid-engine designs benefit from airflow over the engine cover, and the central location allows symmetrical cooling duct design.

6. **Track-Focused Engineering:** MR layouts are track-oriented by design, with rear-biased weight distribution enabling controlled oversteer under power — a hallmark of driver-focused supercars.

**Conclusion:** While both MR and RR place the engine near the driven wheels for traction, the MR layout's central location delivers superior **handling balance**, **lower polar inertia**, and **better stability** — making it the **uncontested choice for modern supercars**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) With neat sketches, explain the FF and FR layouts. Compare their packaging, traction, and handling characteristics. (7 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 1 | Question Choice B | 7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**FF Layout (Front Engine, Front-Wheel Drive):**

*Sketch description:* The engine sits transversely (or longitudinally) at the front, coupled to a transaxle that drives the front wheels. No driveshaft runs to the rear. The rear wheels are non-driven idler wheels.

*Key features:*
- Engine: Transverse mounting preferred
- Transmission & Differential: Combined into a single "transaxle" unit
- Power flow: Engine → Transaxle → Front Wheels (short, direct path)
- Examples: Maruti Swift, Honda City, Hyundai Creta

**FR Layout (Front Engine, Rear-Wheel Drive):**

*Sketch description:* The engine sits longitudinally at the front, driving a transmission, which sends power through a long propeller shaft to a rear differential, which finally powers the rear wheels.

*Key features:*
- Engine: Longitudinal mounting
- Transmission: Separate from differential
- Power flow: Engine → Transmission → Driveshaft → Rear Differential → Rear Wheels (long path)
- Examples: BMW 3 Series, Toyota Fortuner, Ford Mustang

**Comparative Analysis:**

| Aspect | FF Layout | FR Layout |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| **Packaging** | Excellent — no driveshaft, flat floor, more cabin space | Compromised — central driveshaft tunnel, larger engine bay |
| **Traction (Wet/Slippery)** | Excellent — engine weight over driven wheels | Poor — rear is lighter, can spin easily |
| **Traction (Dry Acceleration)** | Adequate | Excellent — weight transfers rearward under acceleration, loading driven wheels |
| **Handling Bias** | Understeer (push) — front tires do all work | Neutral to oversteer — better balance |
| **Steering Feel** | Compromised (torque steer) | Pure, communicative |
| **Weight Distribution** | ~60:40 to 65:35 front-heavy | 50:50 to 55:45 (rear-bias under acceleration) |
| **Manufacturing Cost** | Lowest | Higher |
| **Fuel Economy** | Better (less drivetrain loss) | Slightly worse |
| **Towing Capacity** | Limited | Excellent |
| **Best Use Case** | Family hatchbacks, sedans, economy cars | Sports cars, luxury sedans, trucks, SUVs |

**Conclusion:** FF is the rational choice for **efficiency and packaging**, while FR is the **emotional and performance choice** for driving dynamics. The global automotive industry uses both, often with FF for sub-premium segments and FR for premium/performance vehicles.

---

#### (b) Explain the 4WD/AWD layout. Discuss its types, working principle, advantages, and limitations. (7 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 1 | Question Choice B | 7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
A **4WD (Four-Wheel Drive)** or **AWD (All-Wheel Drive)** layout is one in which engine power is delivered to **all four wheels** of the vehicle, either continuously or on-demand.

**Working Principle:**
The engine's torque is split by a **transfer case** (or center differential) and routed through a **front driveshaft** to the front differential and a **rear driveshaft** to the rear differential. Each axle differential then distributes torque to its respective wheels.

**Types of 4WD/AWD:**

1. **Part-Time 4WD:**
   - Driver-selectable: 2H (two-wheel-drive high) ↔ 4H (four-wheel-drive high) ↔ 4L (four-wheel-drive low).
   - Designed for off-road use; cannot be used on dry tarmac (binding in drivetrain).
   - Examples: Mahindra Thar, Maruti Jimny, Jeep Wrangler.

2. **Full-Time AWD:**
   - All four wheels always receive power via a center differential.
   - Suitable for on-road and off-road use; can be driven on dry tarmac.
   - Examples: Audi Quattro (cars), Subaru Symmetrical AWD, Toyota Land Cruiser.

3. **On-Demand AWD:**
   - Default 2WD (usually FWD); computer engages rear wheels via an electronically controlled clutch when front-wheel slip is detected.
   - Optimizes fuel economy.
   - Examples: Honda CR-V AWD, Hyundai Tucson AWD.

4. **Torque-Vectoring AWD:**
   - Advanced AWD that can vary the left/right torque distribution, not just front/rear.
   - Examples: Mitsubishi S-AWC, Acura SH-AWD.

**Key Components:**
- Transfer case (with or without center differential)
- Front and rear propeller shafts
- Front, rear, and (sometimes) center differentials
- Locking differentials for off-road use
- Electronic traction control systems

**Advantages of 4WD/AWD:**
1. **Maximum Traction:** Power to all wheels means grip is available at all four contact patches.
2. **Off-Road Capability:** Unmatched performance on mud, sand, snow, rocks, and steep inclines.
3. **Improved Acceleration:** Better launch grip, especially useful in performance cars (Audi RS cars, Subaru WRX STI).
4. **Safety in Adverse Weather:** Enhanced stability on wet, icy, or snowy roads.
5. **Towing:** Better traction for heavy loads.

**Limitations of 4WD/AWD:**
1. **Higher Cost:** Additional drivetrain components increase purchase price.
2. **Increased Weight:** AWD systems add 50–150 kg of mass, reducing efficiency.
3. **Lower Fuel Economy:** Drivetrain parasitic losses (friction in differentials, transfer case) consume 5–15% more fuel.
4. **Maintenance Complexity:** More parts mean more potential failure points and higher service costs.
5. **Packaging Constraints:** Driveshafts and differentials reduce ground clearance and complicate underbody design.

**Conclusion:** 4WD/AWD is the optimal choice when **traction is the top priority** — be it for off-road adventure, adverse weather safety, or all-weather performance. However, the cost, weight, and efficiency penalties mean it is reserved for vehicles that genuinely need it: SUVs, off-roaders, crossovers, and high-performance AWD cars.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Layout Code Confusion:** Students often write "RF" instead of "FR" (Front Engine, Rear Drive) — the **first letter is always the engine location**, the second is the driven wheels. Memorize as **E**ngine-**D**rive, not Drive-Engine.
> 2. **FF vs FR Traction:** Do not say "FR has better traction." FR has better traction *during acceleration* (weight shifts rearward), but FF has better traction *at rest and in slippery conditions* (engine mass is on driven wheels).
> 3. **MR vs RR for Supercars:** Students often say "supercars use RR." This is **incorrect** for modern supercars. Classic 911 is RR, but virtually all modern supercars (Ferrari, Lamborghini, McLaren) are **MR**.
> 4. **Polar Moment of Inertia Direction:** Always specify **about the vertical (yaw) axis** — students often write about lateral or longitudinal axes, which is a different concept.
> 5. **Skipping the Sketch:** For Part B 7-mark questions, KTU examiners often **deduct 1–2 marks** if no diagram/sketch is provided. Always include a simple block diagram.
> 6. **AWD vs 4WD Interchange:** In strict automotive engineering terms, **4WD** implies off-road capable with low-range, while **AWD** is on-road oriented. Using them interchangeably may cost marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Layouts" Module 1**

- [ ] **Vehicle layout = Engine location + Driven wheels location** (two-letter code)
- [ ] **Five canonical layouts:** FF, FR, RR, MR, AWD/4WD
- [ ] **FF** = Front engine, Front-wheel drive → most common (economy cars: Swift, i20)
- [ ] **FR** = Front engine, Rear-wheel drive → performance/luxury (BMW 3 Series, Fortuner)
- [ ] **RR** = Rear engine, Rear-wheel drive → compact/old sports cars (Beetle, old 911, Nano)
- [ ] **MR** = Mid engine, Rear-wheel drive → supercars (Ferrari 488, Huracán)
- [ ] **AWD/4WD** = Power to all wheels → off-road & adverse weather (Thar, Audi Quattro)
- [ ] **FF advantages:** Space efficiency, low cost, traction in slippery conditions
- [ ] **FR advantages:** Balanced weight distribution, pure steering feel, towing capacity
- [ ] **MR advantages:** Minimum polar moment of inertia, 50:50 weight, best agility
- [ ] **4WD advantages:** Maximum traction, off-road capability
- [ ] **FF disadvantage:** Understeer, torque steer, lower performance ceiling
- [ ] **FR disadvantage:** Driveshaft tunnel, poor wet traction
- [ ] **RR disadvantage:** High-speed instability, poor cooling, crash safety issues
- [ ] **MR disadvantage:** Compromised cabin space, very expensive
- [ ] **4WD disadvantage:** Cost, weight, lower fuel economy
- [ ] **Weight distribution formula:** $W_d = \frac{W_f}{W_f + W_r} \times 100\%$
- [ ] **Polar moment of inertia (yaw axis):** $I_{zz} = \sum m_i r_i^2$ — lower is better for agility
- [ ] **Weight transfer under braking:** $\Delta W = \frac{m \cdot a \cdot h}{L_{wb}}$
- [ ] **Drivetrain efficiency:** FF = 90–95%, FR = 85–92%, AWD = 80–88%
- [ ] **MR is mathematically justified** by minimizing $I_{zz}$ → most agile layout
- [ ] **Modern EVs use a "skateboard" platform** that can mimic any traditional layout
- [ ] **KTU favorite examples:** Maruti Swift (FF), BMW 3-Series (FR), Ferrari 488 (MR), Mahindra Thar (4WD), Tata Nano (RR)
- [ ] **Examiner tip:** Always draw a **block diagram** in Part B answers — earns easy marks

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT612 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Comparison

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Modern Automotive Technologies (OEMUT612, Module 1)**, the term **"Comparison"** refers to the systematic, parameter-based analytical evaluation of different automotive propulsion and powertrain architectures — primarily **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** vehicles, **Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs)**, **Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs)**, **Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (PHEVs)**, and **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs)** — across standardized engineering performance, efficiency, emission, and operational metrics.

The comparison is rooted in **energy conversion efficiency, tailpipe emissions, fuel/energy density, range, lifecycle cost, torque-speed characteristics, and thermal management behaviour**, all benchmarked against regulatory frameworks such as **BS-VI / Euro 6** and **Corporate Average Fuel Economy (CAFE)** norms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The "Comparison" topic is a **high-yield, frequently tested Module 1 concept**. It directly maps to **Course Outcome CO1**: *"Understand the evolution and classify modern automotive propulsion systems."* It is a **mandatory 7 to 14 mark question** in every End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Think of the comparison of automotive technologies like comparing **three different ways to travel from Kochi to Delhi**:

| Mode | Analogy | Key Idea |
|---|---|---|
| **Petrol/Diesel Car (ICE)** | Driving your own petrol scooter — refuel often, but cheap & quick refills | Mature, high energy density fuel, but lossy conversion |
| **Electric Vehicle (BEV)** | Riding an e-scooter (Ola Electric) — silent, no emissions at point of use, but charging takes time | Electric motor + Battery, zero tailpipe emission |
| **Hybrid (HEV)** | A moped with both pedal and small engine — engine assists when needed | Dual powertrain, intelligent energy blending |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standardized Metrics (Bold):**
> - **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)** — measured in **g/kWh**
> - **Tank-to-Wheel (TTW)** efficiency
> - **Well-to-Wheel (WTW)** efficiency
> - **Energy Density of Fuel** — Gasoline ≈ **46 MJ/kg**, Lithium-ion ≈ **0.9 MJ/kg**
> - **Thermal Efficiency** of Otto cycle ≈ **25–35%**, Diesel ≈ **35–45%**, Electric Motor ≈ **85–95%**

### 1.3 Why Comparison Matters in Modern Automotive Engineering

The automotive industry is undergoing a **paradigm shift** driven by three forces:
1. **Climate change mandates** (Paris Agreement → Net Zero by 2050)
2. **Depletion of crude oil reserves** (peak oil concern)
3. **Government policy push** (India's FAME-II scheme, EV target 30% by 2030)

Hence, every KTU examiner expects students to be able to **justitatively compare** and **select** an appropriate propulsion system for a given operating scenario.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Parameters of Comparison (The "Six Pillars")

A structured KTU-level comparison evaluates **six core engineering pillars**:

1. **Energy Source & Density**
2. **Energy Conversion Efficiency (TTW & WTW)**
3. **Power & Torque Characteristics**
4. **Emissions Profile**
5. **Range & Refueling/Refuelling Time**
6. **Lifecycle Cost & Maintenance**

Each pillar is broken down below.

---

### 2.2 Pillar 1 — Energy Source & Energy Density

Energy density determines how much **travel range** is available per unit mass/volume of the energy carrier.

For ICE vehicles, chemical energy of the fuel is converted via combustion.
For BEVs, electrical energy stored electrochemically in the battery is converted via electric motor.

The **gravimetric energy density** is given by:

$$E_{\text{density}} = \frac{E_{\text{stored}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}}$$

Where:
- $E_{\text{stored}}$ = Total usable energy stored (Joules)
- $m_{\text{fuel}}$ = Mass of fuel/battery (kg)

> **Conversion Note:**
> 1 kWh = **3.6 × 10⁶ J** = **3.6 MJ**

---

### 2.3 Pillar 2 — Efficiency (TTW vs WTW)

**Tank-to-Wheel (TTW) efficiency** measures how efficiently the energy stored in the vehicle's tank/battery is converted at the wheels.

$$\eta_{\text{TTW}} = \frac{E_{\text{at wheels}}}{E_{\text{in tank/battery}}} \times 100\%$$

**Well-to-Wheel (WTW) efficiency** is the **product** of upstream (fuel production) and TTW efficiency:

$$\eta_{\text{WTW}} = \eta_{\text{Well-to-Tank}} \times \eta_{\text{TTW}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **high-yield KTU formula**. Most Part-B 7-mark sub-questions test the student's ability to compute $\eta_{\text{WTW}}$ from given upstream and TTW values.

---

### 2.4 Pillar 3 — Power, Torque & Engine/Motor Characteristics

For any automotive powertrain:

$$P_{\text{wheel}} = T_{\text{wheel}} \cdot \omega_{\text{wheel}}$$

Where:
- $P_{\text{wheel}}$ = Power delivered at the wheel (W)
- $T_{\text{wheel}}$ = Torque at the wheel (N·m)
- $\omega_{\text{wheel}}$ = Angular velocity of the wheel (rad/s)

For a vehicle in motion:

$$P_{\text{required}} = F_{\text{tractive}} \cdot v = (F_{\text{rolling}} + F_{\text{aero}} + F_{\text{grade}} + F_{\text{accel}}) \cdot v$$

The four resistive forces are:

$$F_{\text{rolling}} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$$

$$F_{\text{aero}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{\text{air}} \cdot C_{d} \cdot A_{f} \cdot v^{2}$$

$$F_{\text{grade}} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$$

$$F_{\text{accel}} = m \cdot a$$

---

### 2.5 Pillar 4 — Emissions

ICE vehicles emit **CO₂, CO, HC, NOₓ, PM**. EVs have **zero tailpipe emissions** but have upstream emissions tied to electricity generation.

$$\text{CO}_2 \text{ emission (g/km)} = \text{Fuel consumption (L/100 km)} \times \text{CO}_2 \text{ factor (g/L)}$$

Typical CO₂ factor of gasoline ≈ **2.31 kg CO₂ per litre**.

---

### 2.6 Pillar 5 — Range & Refuelling

For an EV:

$$R_{\text{EV}} = \frac{E_{\text{battery}} \cdot \eta_{\text{drivetrain}}}{E_{\text{consumption per km}}}$$

For an ICE vehicle:

$$R_{\text{ICE}} = \frac{V_{\text{tank}} \cdot \rho_{\text{fuel}} \cdot \text{LHV}}{\text{Fuel consumption rate}}$$

Where LHV = Lower Heating Value of fuel.

---

### 2.7 Pillar 6 — Cost of Ownership (TCO)

$$\text{TCO} = C_{\text{purchase}} + C_{\text{fuel/electricity}} + C_{\text{maintenance}} + C_{\text{insurance}} - R_{\text{residual}}$$

---

### 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$ | Thermal efficiency of engine | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\eta_{\text{WTW}} = \eta_{\text{WTT}} \times \eta_{\text{TTW}}$ | Overall well-to-wheel efficiency | dimensionless |
| 3 | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power | W |
| 4 | $E_{\text{density}} = \dfrac{E}{m}$ | Gravimetric energy density | J/kg or Wh/kg |
| 5 | $F_{\text{tractive}} = m a + \mu_{rr} m g + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{d} A v^{2} + m g \sin\theta$ | Total tractive force required | N |
| 6 | $\text{CO}_2 = \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \times \text{EF}_{\text{CO}_2}$ | CO₂ emission rate | g/s |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{motor}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{electrical in}}}$ | Electric motor efficiency | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{SOC}_{\text{new}} = \text{SOC}_{\text{old}} - \dfrac{E_{\text{consumed}}}{E_{\text{battery,rated}}}$ | Battery state of charge update | dimensionless |
| 9 | $R_{\text{EV}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{batt}}}{\text{kWh/100 km}}$ | EV range | km |
| 10 | $R_{\text{ICE}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{tank}} \cdot \rho \cdot \text{LHV}}{\text{BSFC} \cdot P}$ | ICE range | km |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** In KTU valuations, students often write $\mu_{rr}$ instead of $\mu$ and lose the **"rolling resistance coefficient"** definition mark. Always **define every symbol** before substituting.

---

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

Comparison frameworks are used by:
- **OEMs (Tata Motors, Mahindra, Hyundai, Tesla)** for powertrain selection
- **Regulators (MoRTH, ARAI)** for emission norm compliance
- **Fleet operators (Ola, Uber, BluSmart)** for TCO modelling
- **Researchers** for hybrid energy management strategy (EMS) design

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always support your comparison with **at least one numerical example**. Pure qualitative answers cap at **8–9 out of 14 marks**. Numerical support pushes you to the **12–14 mark band**.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Comparing TTW Efficiency: ICE vs BEV

**Given Data (KTU standard problem):**
- Petrol ICE: BSFC = **250 g/kWh**, fuel LHV = **44 MJ/kg**
- BEV: Battery pack = **50 kWh**, motor + drivetrain efficiency = **90%**, vehicle consumes **15 kWh/100 km**
- Petrol density = **0.745 kg/L**, fuel consumption = **6 L/100 km**

**Goal:** Compare TTW efficiency for both.

---

#### Step 1 — Energy Input per 100 km for ICE

We first compute the mass of fuel consumed per 100 km:

$$m_{\text{fuel}} = 6 \, \text{L} \times 0.745 \, \text{kg/L} = 4.47 \, \text{kg}$$

Total chemical energy input:

$$E_{\text{in, ICE}} = 4.47 \times 44 \, \text{MJ} = 196.68 \, \text{MJ}$$

Convert to kWh:

$$E_{\text{in, ICE}} = \frac{196.68}{3.6} = 54.63 \, \text{kWh}$$

---

#### Step 2 — Energy Out (at wheels) using BSFC

BSFC relates fuel mass flow to brake power. For tractive energy delivered at the wheels (assume BSFC corresponds to brake output):

$$E_{\text{out, ICE}} = \frac{m_{\text{fuel}}}{\text{BSFC}} = \frac{4.47 \, \text{kg}}{0.250 \, \text{kg/kWh}} = 17.88 \, \text{kWh}$$

---

#### Step 3 — TTW Efficiency of ICE

$$\eta_{\text{TTW, ICE}} = \frac{E_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{in}}} = \frac{17.88}{54.63} = 0.3273 = 32.73\%$$

---

#### Step 4 — Energy Input per 100 km for BEV

For BEV, energy input from grid to battery is **15 kWh/100 km** (already accounts for charger efficiency losses in many cases; we treat it as direct here).

$$E_{\text{in, BEV}} = 15 \, \text{kWh}$$

---

#### Step 5 — Energy Out at wheels for BEV

$$E_{\text{out, BEV}} = E_{\text{in, BEV}} \times \eta_{\text{drivetrain}} = 15 \times 0.90 = 13.5 \, \text{kWh}$$

---

#### Step 6 — TTW Efficiency of BEV

$$\eta_{\text{TTW, BEV}} = \frac{13.5}{15} = 0.90 = 90\%$$

---

#### Step 7 — Comparative Statement

$$\frac{\eta_{\text{TTW, BEV}}}{\eta_{\text{TTW, ICE}}} = \frac{90}{32.73} \approx 2.75$$

> **Conclusion:** The BEV is approximately **2.75× more efficient** at the tank-to-wheel level than the petrol ICE vehicle.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — WTW Efficiency Comparison (with upstream losses)

**Additional Given Data:**
- Crude oil extraction + refining efficiency $\eta_{\text{WTT, ICE}}$ = **80%**
- Electricity generation + transmission efficiency $\eta_{\text{WTT, BEV}}$ = **35%** (India grid average, mostly coal-based)

---

#### Step 1 — WTW Efficiency of ICE

$$\eta_{\text{WTW, ICE}} = \eta_{\text{WTT}} \times \eta_{\text{TTW}} = 0.80 \times 0.3273 = 0.2618 = 26.18\%$$

---

#### Step 2 — WTW Efficiency of BEV

$$\eta_{\text{WTW, BEV}} = 0.35 \times 0.90 = 0.315 = 31.5\%$$

---

#### Step 3 — Re-evaluation

$$\frac{\eta_{\text{WTW, BEV}}}{\eta_{\text{WTW, ICE}}} = \frac{31.5}{26.18} \approx 1.20$$

> **Conclusion:** Even with India's coal-heavy grid, the BEV still achieves **~20% higher WTW efficiency** than the ICE. With renewable-dominated grid (efficiency ~60%), BEV WTW can exceed **54%**, making it nearly **2×** the ICE.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Tractive Force Comparison (Tractive Effort Curve)

**Given:**
- Vehicle mass $m$ = **1500 kg**
- Rolling resistance coefficient $\mu_{rr}$ = **0.012**
- Drag coefficient $C_d$ = **0.30**
- Frontal area $A_f$ = **2.2 m²**
- Air density $\rho_{\text{air}}$ = **1.225 kg/m³**
- Grade angle $\theta$ = **0°** (flat road)

**Find:** Tractive force at **v = 60 km/h = 16.67 m/s** and **a = 0** (cruising).

---

#### Step 1 — Rolling Resistance Force

$$F_{\text{rolling}} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$$

Substituting values:

$$F_{\text{rolling}} = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times \cos(0°)$$

$$F_{\text{rolling}} = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times 1 = 176.58 \, \text{N}$$

---

#### Step 2 — Aerodynamic Drag Force

$$F_{\text{aero}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^{2}$$

$$F_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (16.67)^{2}$$

$$F_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 277.89$$

$$F_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.66 \times 277.89$$

$$F_{\text{aero}} = 112.27 \, \text{N}$$

---

#### Step 3 — Grade Force (flat road)

$$F_{\text{grade}} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta = 0 \, \text{N}$$

---

#### Step 4 — Acceleration Force (cruising, a = 0)

$$F_{\text{accel}} = m \cdot a = 0 \, \text{N}$$

---

#### Step 5 — Total Tractive Force

$$F_{\text{tractive}} = F_{\text{rolling}} + F_{\text{aero}} + F_{\text{grade}} + F_{\text{accel}}$$

$$F_{\text{tractive}} = 176.58 + 112.27 + 0 + 0 = 288.85 \, \text{N}$$

---

#### Step 6 — Required Power at Wheels

$$P_{\text{wheel}} = F_{\text{tractive}} \cdot v = 288.85 \times 16.67 = 4814 \, \text{W} \approx 4.81 \, \text{kW}$$

---

#### Step 7 — Comparison Insight

| Powertrain | Required Input Power (η = TTW) |
|---|---|
| Petrol ICE (η = 32%) | 4.81 / 0.32 = **15.03 kW** chemical |
| BEV (η = 90%) | 4.81 / 0.90 = **5.34 kW** electrical |

> This **3× reduction in input power demand** explains why EVs feel far more responsive at low speeds and why their cooling systems are simpler.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Master Table for KTU Answers)

| Parameter | Petrol ICE | Diesel ICE | BEV | HEV (Full) | FCEV |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Storage | Gasoline (44 MJ/kg) | Diesel (43 MJ/kg) | Li-ion (~0.9 MJ/kg) | Petrol + Battery | H₂ (120 MJ/kg) |
| TTW Efficiency | 20–35% | 30–45% | 85–92% | 60–80% | 50–60% |
| WTW Efficiency (India) | ~22% | ~25% | ~30–55% | ~28% | ~22–30% |
| Peak Torque | Medium @3000 rpm | High @2000 rpm | Instant from 0 rpm | Combined | Instant |
| CO₂ Tailpipe (g/km) | 120–180 | 100–160 | **0** | 60–100 | **0** |
| Range (typical) | 500–800 km | 700–1200 km | 200–500 km | 600–1000 km | 400–600 km |
| Refuelling Time | 3–5 min | 3–5 min | 30 min–8 hr | 3–5 min | 5–10 min |
| Maintenance | High | High | **Low** | Medium | High |
| Noise Level | High | High | **Very Low** | Medium | Low |
| Initial Cost (INR) | 8–15 L | 10–18 L | 12–25 L | 15–30 L | 30+ L |

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Tank-to-Wheel (TTW) efficiency and Well-to-Wheel (WTW) efficiency. Why is WTW considered a fairer metric for comparing automotive powertrains?**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> **TTW efficiency** ($\eta_{\text{TTW}}$) is the ratio of useful mechanical energy delivered at the vehicle wheels to the chemical/electrical energy stored in the fuel tank or battery:
>
> $$\eta_{\text{TTW}} = \frac{E_{\text{wheels}}}{E_{\text{tank/battery}}}$$
>
> **WTW efficiency** includes the **upstream energy cost** of producing the fuel/electricity:
>
> $$\eta_{\text{WTW}} = \eta_{\text{WTT}} \times \eta_{\text{TTW}}$$
>
> WTW is fairer because it accounts for **energy losses in fuel extraction, refining, electricity generation, and transmission**, providing a true cradle-to-grave comparison. **For instance, an EV charged from coal may appear clean at TTW, but WTW reveals the actual carbon footprint.**
> **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any three key differences between an Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicle and a Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) with respect to powertrain architecture.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> 1. **Energy Conversion:** ICE uses **thermochemical combustion** (Carnot-limited efficiency 25–40%), whereas BEV uses **electromechanical conversion** in an electric motor (efficiency 85–95%).
> 2. **Torque Delivery:** ICE delivers peak torque at mid-RPM (2000–4000 rpm), requiring multi-gear transmission. BEV delivers **instantaneous peak torque from 0 rpm**, allowing a single-speed reducer.
> 3. **Auxiliary Systems:** ICE requires a **complex cooling system, exhaust after-treatment (catalytic converter, DPF), and lubrication system**. BEV requires only **battery thermal management and power electronics cooling**, drastically reducing component count.
> **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **TTW and WTW efficiencies** of a petrol ICE vehicle and a battery electric vehicle, using the following data:

| Parameter | Petrol ICE | BEV |
|---|---|---|
| Fuel/Energy consumption per 100 km | 6 L | 15 kWh |
| Fuel density / Battery capacity | 0.745 kg/L | 50 kWh |
| BSFC / Motor efficiency | 250 g/kWh | 90% |
| LHV of petrol | 44 MJ/kg | — |
| WTT efficiency | 80% | 35% (India grid) |

Compute the ratio $\dfrac{\eta_{\text{WTW, BEV}}}{\eta_{\text{WTW, ICE}}}$ and comment.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs) over pure ICE and pure BEV powertrains. Include a brief note on **series vs parallel hybrid architectures**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — ICE Energy Input per 100 km:**

$$m_{\text{fuel}} = 6 \times 0.745 = 4.47 \, \text{kg}$$

$$E_{\text{in, ICE}} = 4.47 \times 44 = 196.68 \, \text{MJ} = 54.63 \, \text{kWh}$$

**Step 2 — ICE Energy Output:**

$$E_{\text{out, ICE}} = \frac{4.47}{0.250} = 17.88 \, \text{kWh}$$

**Step 3 — ICE TTW Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{TTW, ICE}} = \frac{17.88}{54.63} = 32.73\%$$

**Step 4 — ICE WTW Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{WTW, ICE}} = 0.80 \times 0.3273 = 26.18\%$$

**Step 5 — BEV Energy Input per 100 km:**

$$E_{\text{in, BEV}} = 15 \, \text{kWh}$$

**Step 6 — BEV Energy Output:**

$$E_{\text{out, BEV}} = 15 \times 0.90 = 13.5 \, \text{kWh}$$

**Step 7 — BEV TTW Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{TTW, BEV}} = \frac{13.5}{15} = 90\%$$

**Step 8 — BEV WTW Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{WTW, BEV}} = 0.35 \times 0.90 = 31.5\%$$

**Step 9 — Ratio Computation:**

$$\frac{\eta_{\text{WTW, BEV}}}{\eta_{\text{WTW, ICE}}} = \frac{31.5}{26.18} = 1.203$$

> **Comment:** Even with India's coal-dominant grid (35% generation efficiency), the BEV achieves **~20% higher WTW efficiency** than the petrol ICE vehicle. With renewable energy-based grid, this advantage rises to over **2×**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Advantages of HEVs over pure ICE:**

1. **Regenerative braking** recovers kinetic energy lost as heat in ICE vehicles, improving urban fuel economy by **20–35%**.
2. **Engine downsizing + electric assist** allows use of smaller, more efficient Atkinson/Miller cycle engines.
3. **No range anxiety** compared to BEVs — uses existing petrol refuelling infrastructure.

**Limitations of HEVs:**

1. **Complexity and cost** due to dual powertrain (engine + motor + battery + planetary gearset).
2. **Battery aging** affects long-term hybrid performance; replacement is expensive.
3. Still dependent on **fossil fuel** and emits CO₂ (though reduced).

**Series vs Parallel Hybrid:**

- **Series Hybrid:** Engine acts only as a **range extender** (drives generator to charge battery); wheels are driven **only by the electric motor**. Example: **Nissan e-POWER (Kicks)**. Best for **city driving**.
- **Parallel Hybrid:** Both **engine and motor can drive the wheels** simultaneously or independently. Example: **Toyota Prius**. Best for **mixed drive cycles** with higher overall efficiency.
- A **Series-Parallel (Power-split) Hybrid** like the Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive combines both via a **planetary gearset** for optimal mode switching.

> **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a tractive effort equation, derive the **total resistive force** experienced by a vehicle moving at constant velocity on a level road. Use it to compare the **power demand** of a 1500 kg vehicle when powered by (i) a petrol ICE with $\eta_{\text{TTW}}$ = 32%, and (ii) a BEV with $\eta_{\text{TTW}}$ = 90%, cruising at 60 km/h. Assume $\mu_{rr} = 0.012$, $C_d = 0.30$, $A_f = 2.2 \, \text{m}^2$, $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.225 \, \text{kg/m}^3$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Critically evaluate **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs)** as an alternative to BEVs. Cover energy density, refuelling time, infrastructure, lifecycle emissions, and current technological bottlenecks.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Tractive Force Equation:**

$$F_{\text{tractive}} = F_{\text{rolling}} + F_{\text{aero}} + F_{\text{grade}} + F_{\text{accel}}$$

**Step 2 — Each Term:**

$$F_{\text{rolling}} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$$

$$F_{\text{aero}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^2$$

$$F_{\text{grade}} = m g \sin\theta$$

$$F_{\text{accel}} = m a$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Values for Level Road, Constant Speed:**

$\theta = 0°$ → $F_{\text{grade}} = 0$

$a = 0$ → $F_{\text{accel}} = 0$

**Step 4 — Rolling Resistance:**

$$F_{\text{rolling}} = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 = 176.58 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 5 — Aerodynamic Drag:**

$v = 60 \, \text{km/h} = 16.67 \, \text{m/s}$

$$F_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (16.67)^2$$

$$F_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.66 \times 277.89 = 112.27 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 6 — Total Tractive Force:**

$$F_{\text{tractive}} = 176.58 + 112.27 = 288.85 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 7 — Power at Wheels:**

$$P_{\text{wheel}} = F \cdot v = 288.85 \times 16.67 = 4815 \, \text{W} \approx 4.82 \, \text{kW}$$

**Step 8 — Power Demand Comparison:**

$$P_{\text{ICE}} = \frac{4.82}{0.32} = 15.06 \, \text{kW}$$

$$P_{\text{BEV}} = \frac{4.82}{0.90} = 5.36 \, \text{kW}$$

> **Conclusion:** BEV requires **~2.8× less input power** than ICE for the same cruising output, confirming the inherent thermodynamic superiority of electric drivetrains.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**FCEV vs BEV — Critical Evaluation:**

| Aspect | FCEV (Hydrogen) | BEV (Battery) |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy Density** | H₂ at 700 bar: ~120 MJ/kg | Li-ion: ~0.9 MJ/kg |
| **Refuelling Time** | 5–10 min (like petrol) | 30 min (DC fast) – 8 hr (AC home) |
| **Tailpipe Emissions** | Only water vapor | Zero |
| **WTW Efficiency** | ~22–30% (electrolysis losses) | ~30–55% (grid dependent) |
| **Infrastructure** | Almost none in India | Growing (Bharat DC chargers) |
| **Vehicle Cost** | Very high (₹30L+) | Falling rapidly (₹10–15L) |
| **Range** | 500–700 km | 200–500 km |
| **Technology Bottleneck** | Hydrogen production, storage tank cost, platinum catalyst | Battery raw materials, charging time |

**Critical Bottlenecks of FCEV:**

1. **Hydrogen Production:** 95% of global H₂ is from fossil fuel reforming (grey hydrogen). True green H₂ via electrolysis is **2–3× more expensive**.
2. **Storage:** 700-bar compressed H₂ tanks are bulky and expensive (carbon-fiber wound).
3. **Platinum Catalyst:** PEM fuel cells require **~30–50 g of platinum per vehicle**, raising cost concerns.
4. **Infrastructure:** India has **< 10 hydrogen refuelling stations** as of 2024.

> **Conclusion:** FCEVs are best suited for **heavy-duty trucks, buses, and long-haul applications** where BEV range/charging limitations are critical. For **passenger cars**, BEVs remain the more practical and scalable solution in the Indian context.
> **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert kWh ↔ MJ** — Loss of **1–2 marks** in numerical problems.
> 2. **Confusing TTW and WTW definitions** — Examiners allocate **dedicated 2 marks** for definition correctness; mixing them up costs heavily.
> 3. **Not defining every symbol** — Standard KTU rule: every Greek letter (η, μ, θ, ρ, ω) must be defined with **units in brackets**.
> 4. **Skipping diagrams** — Even a **simple block diagram** earns **1–2 easy marks**.
> 5. **Qualitative-only answers** — A purely descriptive answer without numerical support caps at **9/14**. Always include **at least one numerical example**.
> 6. **Ignoring Indian context** — KTU specifically values **India-specific data** (FAME-II, BS-VI, Indian grid mix, ARAI). Generic global answers are penalized.
> 7. **No units in final answer** — A computed value without units (e.g., "$\eta = 32$" instead of "$\eta = 32\%$") loses **0.5–1 mark**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Comparison (Module 1)**

- **Six Pillars of Comparison:** Energy Source, Efficiency, Power/Torque, Emissions, Range, TCO.
- **TTW efficiency** is the **ratio of energy at wheels to energy in tank/battery**.
- **WTW efficiency** = $\eta_{\text{WTT}} \times \eta_{\text{TTW}}$ — accounts for **upstream fuel production losses**.
- **Petrol ICE TTW ≈ 25–35%**, Diesel ≈ **35–45%**, BEV ≈ **85–95%**, HEV ≈ **60–80%**, FCEV ≈ **50–60%**.
- **Gasoline energy density ≈ 44 MJ/kg**, Diesel ≈ **43 MJ/kg**, Li-ion battery ≈ **0.9 MJ/kg**, H₂ ≈ **120 MJ/kg**.
- **BEV delivers peak torque from 0 rpm** — no multi-gear transmission needed.
- **Tractive Force Equation:** $F_t = \mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta + 0.5 \rho C_d A_f v^2 + m g \sin\theta + m a$.
- **Power equation:** $P = F \cdot v = T \cdot \omega$.
- **India's grid mix** (~35% generation efficiency) makes BEVs ~20% more efficient than ICE at WTW; with renewables, BEV WTW can exceed **54%**.
- **FAME-II scheme** and **BS-VI norms** are the **two key Indian regulatory frameworks** to mention in any comparison answer.
- **Hybrid types:** Series (engine → generator → motor → wheels), Parallel (engine + motor → wheels directly), Series-Parallel (planetary gearset, e.g., Toyota Prius).
- **FCEV** is best for **heavy commercial long-haul**, not passenger cars in India.
- **Always** include: numerical example + block diagram + symbol definitions + units.
- **Never** confuse **$\eta_{\text{TTW}}$ (vehicle side)** with **$\eta_{\text{WTT}}$ (upstream side)**.

---

> **End of Module 1 — Comparison Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech OEMUT612 — Modern Automotive Technologies*
