**Step 3 — Substitute the values for Stratified Lean-Burn Mode:**

In a lean burn engine, the excess air dilutes the products of combustion, lowering the peak temperature and shifting the equilibrium. The effective $\gamma$ rises towards 1.4 (closer to pure air behavior):

$$
\gamma_{lean} \approx 1.38 \quad \text{(approaching but not reaching 1.4)}
$$

Thus:

$$
\eta_{i,strat} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{1.38 - 1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{0.38}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Evaluation for $r = 10$ (a typical modern direct-injection engine):**

Homogeneous mode:

$$
\eta_{i,hom} = 1 - \frac{1}{10^{0.35}}
$$

Let us compute $10^{0.35}$:

$$
\log_{10}(10^{0.35}) = 0.35 \implies 10^{0.35} \approx 2.239
$$

Therefore:

$$
\eta_{i,hom} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.239} = 1 - 0.4467 = 0.5533 = 55.33\%
$$

Stratified lean mode:

$$
\eta_{i,strat} = 1 - \frac{1}{10^{0.38}}
$$

Compute $10^{0.38}$:

$$
10^{0.38} \approx 2.399
$$

Therefore:

$$
\eta_{i,strat} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.399} = 1 - 0.4168 = 0.5832 = 58.32\%
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Percentage Improvement:**

$$
\Delta \eta = \eta_{i,strat} - \eta_{i,hom} = 0.5832 - 0.5533 = 0.0299
$$

In percentage terms:

$$
\Delta \eta \, (\%) = 0.0299 \times 100 = 2.99 \,\% \approx 3 \,\%
$$

**Step 6 — State the Engineering Conclusion:**

> The stratified lean-burn mode provides approximately **3 % higher indicated thermal efficiency** than the homogeneous stoichiometric mode at the same compression ratio. This 3 % theoretical gain, when combined with the elimination of throttling losses (typically 5–10 % in real engines) and the heat-release-rate improvements of lean combustion, explains the **real-world 15–25 % fuel-economy improvement** seen in TSI engines.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Lambda Calculation for a TSI Engine from Exhaust Gas Composition

A frequently asked Part-A (3-mark) question in KTU ESE asks the student to compute the air–fuel ratio and lambda from measured exhaust gas concentrations.

**Given (typical dry exhaust mole fractions for a TSI engine in stratified mode):**

- $CO_2 = 8.0\,\%$
- $CO = 0.4\,\%$
- $O_2 = 4.5\,\%$
- $H_2O$ is removed (dry analysis)
- $N_2 = 87.1\,\%$ (balance)

**Combustion Equation (per kg of fuel, gasoline approximated as $C_8H_{18}$):**

$$
C_8H_{18} + a\,(O_2 + 3.76\,N_2) \rightarrow b\,CO_2 + c\,CO + d\,H_2O + e\,O_2 + f\,N_2
$$

**Step 1 — Carbon Balance (C atoms in = C atoms out):**

$$
8 = b + c
$$

**Step 2 — Hydrogen Balance (H atoms in = H atoms out):**

$$
18 = 2d \implies d = 9
$$

**Step 3 — Oxygen Balance:**

$$
2a = 2b + c + d + 2e
$$

**Step 4 — Nitrogen Balance:**

$$
3.76\,a = f
$$

**Step 5 — Normalize on 100 moles of dry exhaust (since we have mole %):**

Total dry moles = 100 (since water is excluded). The dry mole fractions become equivalent to moles per 100 moles dry gas:

- $b = 8.0$
- $c = 0.4$
- $e = 4.5$
- $f = 87.1$

**Step 6 — Solve for $a$ (the moles of $O_2$ supplied per mole of fuel):**

From nitrogen balance:

$$
f = 3.76\,a \implies 87.1 = 3.76\,a \implies a = 23.16 \text{ moles of } O_2
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the actual air supplied per mole of fuel:**

$$
\text{Actual air} = a \times (1 + 3.76) = 23.16 \times 4.76 = 110.24 \text{ moles of air}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute the actual moles of fuel per 100 moles of dry exhaust:**

From carbon balance:

$$
b + c = 8.0 + 0.4 = 8.4 \text{ C atoms in dry gas}
$$

Since each fuel molecule has 8 C atoms, moles of fuel = 8.4 / 8 = 1.05. However, we must be careful — this number 1.05 represents the fuel molecules per **100 moles of dry exhaust**.

**Step 9 — Compute the moles of air per mole of fuel:**

$$
\text{Air per mole fuel} = \frac{110.24}{1.05} = 105.0 \text{ moles of air}
$$

**Step 10 — Compute the air–fuel ratio on molar basis and convert to mass basis:**

The molecular weight of air is 28.97 g/mol, and the molecular weight of $C_8H_{18}$ is 114 g/mol. So:

$$
AFR_{mass} = \frac{105.0 \times 28.97}{1 \times 114} = \frac{3041.85}{114} = 26.68
$$

**Step 11 — Compute the lambda value:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}} = \frac{26.68}{14.7} = 1.815
$$

**Step 12 — Verify with Equivalence Ratio:**

$$
\phi = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{1.815} = 0.551
$$

This $\phi < 1$ confirms lean operation, consistent with the **stratified mode** of the TSI engine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A lambda of 1.81 indicates the engine is operating in deep lean burn (stratified mode) — consistent with part-load cruising. Real-world TSI stratified operation is typically capped at λ ≈ 1.6–1.8 due to combustion stability limits (misfire at λ > ~1.8 for gasoline).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of TSI Engine Modes

| Criterion | Stratified (Lean) | Homogeneous (Stoich) | Homogeneous Lean |
|---|---|---|---|
| λ | 1.4 – 2.0 | 1.0 | 1.05 – 1.2 |
| Injection Timing | Late (compression stroke) | Early (intake stroke) | Early |
| Throttle Position | Wide open | Closed (moderate) | Partially open |
| Pumping Loss | **Minimal** | High | Moderate |
| NOx Emissions | Lower (low temp) | Standard (3-way cat) | Moderate |
| Particulate Matter | Higher (wall wetting risk) | Standard | Standard |
| BSFC (g/kWh) | **210 – 230** | 250 – 280 | 240 – 260 |
| Specific Power (kW/L) | Low–Medium | High | Medium |
| Operating Load Range | 5 – 30 % | 30 – 100 % | 30 – 70 % |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a TSI Engine System

The following block diagram maps the major sub-systems and their interaction paths in a typical Volkswagen/Audi TSI engine. This satisfies the KTU requirement for a "block-level functional architecture" where physical cross-section drawings are not required.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Driver Accelerator Pedal Position Sensor] --> ECU
    B[Crankshaft Position Sensor CKP] --> ECU
    C[Camshaft Position Sensor CMP] --> ECU
    D[Mass Air Flow Sensor MAF] --> ECU
    E[Manifold Absolute Pressure MAP] --> ECU
    F[Lambda Sensor UEGO Pre-Cat] --> ECU
    G[Lambda Sensor Post-Cat] --> ECU
    H[Knock Sensor Piezoelectric] --> ECU
    I[Coolant Temperature Sensor CTS] --> ECU

    ECU[Engine Control Unit Bosch MED17] --> J[Fuel High Pressure Pump]
    ECU --> K[Piezo Injectors 7 Hole]
    ECU --> L[Ignition Coils Pencil Type]
    ECU --> M[Throttle Body Electronic]
    ECU --> N[Turbocharger Wastegate Actuator]
    ECU --> O[Variable Valve Timing Solenoid]

    J --> K
    K --> P[Combustion Chamber Stratified or Homogeneous Cloud]
    L --> P
    M --> Q[Intake Manifold Boosted Air]
    P --> R[Exhaust Manifold to Turbo]
    R --> N
    N --> S[Turbocharger Compressor Inlet]
    S --> Q
    O --> T[Cam Phaser Inlet and Exhaust]
    P --> U[Three Way Catalyst TWC]
    F --> U
    U --> V[NOx Trap or SCR Optional]
    V --> W[Exhaust Tailpipe]

    style ECU fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
    style P fill:#c00000,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
    style U fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- The **left column** represents all input sensors feeding the ECU.
- The **ECU (central blue block)** is the decision-maker that selects between stratified, homogeneous, and homogeneous-lean modes in real time.
- The **middle column** represents actuators: fuel pump, injectors, ignition, throttle, wastegate, and VVT.
- The **right column** represents the physical airflow, combustion, and aftertreatment chain.
- The **red block (Combustion Chamber)** is where the actual stratified or homogeneous cloud is formed depending on ECU command.

---

### 4.2 Combustion Mode Selection Logic

The following flowchart explains the **decision algorithm** used inside the ECU to switch between stratified, homogeneous, and full-load modes. KTU students are expected to describe this logic in 2-mark to 7-mark theory questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Start Request] --> B{Engine Speed and Load}
    B -- Low Speed and Low Load --> C[Stratified Lean Mode]
    B -- Medium Speed and Medium Load --> D[Homogeneous Lean Mode]
    B -- Medium Speed and High Load --> E[Homogeneous Stoichiometric Mode]
    B -- High Speed and Full Load --> F[Homogeneous Rich Mode]
    C --> G{Temperature and Knock Sensor}
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G -- Knock Detected --> H[Retard Ignition Timing]
    G -- Lambda Drift Detected --> I[Adjust Injection Duration]
    H --> J[Recirculate to Operating Mode]
    I --> J
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- The ECU evaluates **pedal position, engine speed, gear, and boost pressure** to choose an operating mode.
- Real-time feedback from **knock and lambda sensors** continuously corrects ignition timing and injection duration.
- The closed-loop control cycle runs at **~100 Hz** (10 ms loop time) on the MED17 platform.

---

### 4.3 Stratified Charge Cloud Formation (Schematic Block View)

Since a physical drawing of a fuel spray is outside the scope of a Mermaid diagram, this block-level view shows how the air motion, spray pattern, and spark timing interact to create the stratified cloud.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Air Induction via Turbocharger Wide Open Throttle] --> B[Cylinder During Compression Stroke 60 to 90 percent CR]
    C[High Pressure Fuel Injector 200 bar] --> B
    B --> D[Stratified Cloud Forms Near Spark Plug]
    D --> E[Spark at 25 deg BTDC]
    E --> F[Local Rich Ignition at Spark Plug]
    F --> G[Flame Propagates into Lean Region]
    G --> H[Complete Combustion at Lambda around 1.5]
    style D fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    style H fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "draw and explain" the stratified charge formation, the student is expected to describe the sequence shown above. A hand-drawn diagram of the cylinder cross-section is acceptable, but the block-level logic above captures the *conceptual sequence* that examiners are looking for.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define stratified charge engine. How does it differ from a homogeneous charge engine?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **stratified charge engine** is an internal combustion engine in which the air–fuel mixture is **not uniform** throughout the combustion chamber. A rich mixture is concentrated near the spark plug, while the surrounding regions contain a lean mixture or pure air.

In contrast, a **homogeneous charge engine** has a **uniformly distributed** air–fuel mixture throughout the chamber (typically at λ = 1, i.e., stoichiometric).

| Parameter | Stratified | Homogeneous |
|---|---|---|
| Mixture uniformity | Non-uniform (layered) | Uniform |
| Overall λ | 1.4 – 2.0 (lean) | 1.0 (stoich) |
| Throttle | Wide open | Partially closed |
| Pumping loss | Low | High |
| Fuel economy | Better | Poorer |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for difference, 1 for comparison table or clear distinction.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is the meaning of the acronym "TSI"? List the three main operating modes of a TSI engine.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**TSI** stands for **Turbo Stratified Injection**, a registered technology of the Volkswagen Group:
- **T** – Turbocharger (exhaust gas-driven forced induction)
- **S** – Stratified (stratified charge combustion at part load)
- **I** – Injection (high-pressure direct injection of fuel)

The three primary operating modes of a TSI engine are:

1. **Stratified Lean Mode** — λ ≈ 1.4 – 2.0; late fuel injection; wide-open throttle. Used at low load, low speed (city driving).
2. **Homogeneous Stoichiometric Mode** — λ = 1.0; early fuel injection; partially closed throttle. Used at medium-to-high load.
3. **Homogeneous Lean Mode** — λ ≈ 1.05 – 1.2; early injection; partially open throttle. Used during steady cruise.

*(3 marks: 1 for acronym expansion, 2 for operating modes.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** with sub-parts of **7 + 7** marks. The student must answer **ONE full question (both sub-parts) out of two choices.** This is a true KTU internal-choice pattern.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model, Module 2, OEMUT612]**

**(a)** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the working principle of a stratified charge engine. Discuss the conditions required for reliable stratified operation and explain why fuel is injected during the **late compression stroke** and not during the intake stroke. (7 marks)

**(b)** A gasoline engine operates in **homogeneous mode** at a compression ratio of 10.5, with γ = 1.35 for products. The same engine, in **stratified lean mode**, operates at the same compression ratio but with effective γ = 1.38. Compute:
   1. The indicated thermal efficiency in homogeneous mode.
   2. The indicated thermal efficiency in stratified mode.
   3. The percentage improvement in efficiency.
   4. Comment on whether the stratified mode can be sustained at full load. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle of Stratified Charge Engine (7 marks):**

A stratified charge engine forms a **locally rich, globally lean** air–fuel mixture in the combustion chamber. The key features are:

- **Mixture preparation:** Fuel is injected **directly into the cylinder** at high pressure (100–200 bar) by a multi-hole injector. Injection is timed to occur during the **late compression stroke** (typically between 60 % and 90 % of the stroke).

- **Charge formation:** The in-cylinder air motion (tumble or swirl) carries and confines the fuel spray, forming a **conical rich cloud** around the spark plug. The remainder of the chamber is filled with air or a very lean mixture.

- **Ignition:** The spark plug ignites the locally rich mixture. The flame kernel then propagates outward into the surrounding lean region.

**Why late compression injection?**
- Early injection (intake stroke) would give the fuel time to mix with all the air → homogeneous mixture.
- Late injection (compression stroke) prevents mixing → the rich pocket stays localized.
- Late injection also **cools the charge** through the latent heat of fuel vaporization, allowing **higher compression ratios without knock**.

**Conditions for reliable stratified operation:**
1. Strong in-cylinder air motion (swirl ratio ≥ 2.0).
2. High-pressure, well-atomized fuel spray (Sauter Mean Diameter < 25 µm).
3. Spark plug located within the fuel-rich cloud.
4. Operating range: λ between **1.4 and 2.0** (leaner than this leads to misfire; richer than this loses the lean-burn advantage).

*(7 marks: 2 for diagram/description, 2 for late injection rationale, 2 for conditions, 1 for range.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Homogeneous Mode Efficiency:**

The Otto cycle formula for indicated thermal efficiency:

$$
\eta_{i,hom} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}
$$

Substituting $r = 10.5$ and $\gamma = 1.35$:

$$
\gamma - 1 = 0.35
$$

Compute $10.5^{0.35}$:

$$
\log_{10}(10.5^{0.35}) = 0.35 \times \log_{10}(10.5) = 0.35 \times 1.02119 = 0.35742
$$

Therefore:

$$
10.5^{0.35} = 10^{0.35742} \approx 2.277
$$

Thus:

$$
\eta_{i,hom} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.277} = 1 - 0.4392 = 0.5608 = 56.08\%
$$

**Step 2 — Stratified Mode Efficiency:**

Substituting $r = 10.5$ and $\gamma = 1.38$:

$$
\gamma - 1 = 0.38
$$

Compute $10.5^{0.38}$:

$$
\log_{10}(10.5^{0.38}) = 0.38 \times 1.02119 = 0.38805
$$

Therefore:

$$
10.5^{0.38} = 10^{0.38805} \approx 2.443
$$

Thus:

$$
\eta_{i,strat} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.443} = 1 - 0.4093 = 0.5907 = 59.07\%
$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Improvement:**

$$
\Delta \eta \, (\%) = \frac{0.5907 - 0.5608}{0.5608} \times 100 = \frac{0.0299}{0.5608} \times 100 = 5.33\%
$$

**Step 4 — Comment on Full-Load Operation:**

> The stratified mode **cannot be sustained at full load** because:
> 1. At full load, peak cylinder temperatures exceed **2200 K**, and lean burn (low flame speed) cannot deliver torque fast enough — knock resistance is poor.
> 2. The catalyst cannot operate efficiently at λ > 1 (three-way catalyst requires stoichiometric gases for NOx, CO, and HC reduction).
> 3. The driver demands maximum air–fuel charge, so λ must approach or fall below 1.0 — the engine switches to **homogeneous stoichiometric** or **rich** mode.
> 4. Misfire probability rises sharply at λ > 1.8 even at part load, so full load is impossible.

*(7 marks: 2 for step 1, 2 for step 2, 1 for step 3, 2 for step 4.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model, Module 2, OEMUT612]**

**(a)** Explain the **TSI engine system** in detail. Describe its major components, the role of the **turbocharger**, the **piezo injector**, and the **Engine Control Unit (ECU)**. Discuss the two primary advantages of TSI over conventional PFI (Port Fuel Injection) engines. (7 marks)

**(b)** A TSI engine operates in stratified mode with the following dry exhaust gas analysis (in mole %): $CO_2 = 8.5$, $CO = 0.3$, $O_2 = 4.0$, $N_2 = 87.2$. The fuel is gasoline approximated as $C_8H_{18}$ (M = 114 kg/kmol). Compute:
   1. The actual air–fuel ratio (mass basis).
   2. The lambda (λ) value.
   3. The equivalence ratio (φ).
   4. Identify whether the engine is operating in lean, stoichiometric, or rich mode. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) TSI Engine System (7 marks):**

A **TSI engine** (Turbo Stratified Injection) is a **turbocharged, direct-injection, stratified-charge gasoline engine**. Its major subsystems are:

1. **Turbocharger:** An exhaust-driven radial-flow turbine coupled to a centrifugal compressor. It pressurizes the intake air (boost pressure 0.3 to 1.5 bar gauge), increasing volumetric efficiency and specific power. A **variable geometry turbo (VGT)** or **electronic wastegate** modulates boost based on engine demand.

2. **Piezoelectric Injector:** A high-pressure (100–350 bar), multi-hole (typically 6–7 holes) injector using a piezo crystal stack. It delivers extremely fine atomization (SMD < 25 µm) and supports **multiple injection events per cycle** (pilot, main, post injections).

3. **Engine Control Unit (Bosch MED17 or equivalent):** Runs stratified–homogeneous mode-switching logic, controls injection timing, ignition timing, turbo wastegate position, throttle position, and VVT. Closed-loop lambda control updates injection duration ~100 times per second.

4. **Other components:** High-pressure fuel pump, charge air cooler, wide-band UEGO lambda sensor (fore and aft of three-way catalyst), knock sensor, electronic throttle body, variable valve timing (VVT) actuators, and three-way catalytic converter.

**Two primary advantages of TSI over conventional PFI:**

- **Higher fuel efficiency (15–25 % improvement):** Achieved by stratified lean burn at part load, eliminating throttling losses.
- **Higher specific output (kW/L):** Turbocharging and direct injection allow smaller displacement to produce the same power — engine "downsizing" — reducing vehicle weight and improving driveability.

*(7 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for major components, 2 for turbo/injector/ECU detail, 2 for two advantages.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Carbon Balance:**

Moles of $CO_2$ = 8.5; moles of $CO$ = 0.3. Total carbon atoms in dry exhaust per 100 moles:

$$
C_{atoms} = 8.5 + 0.3 = 8.8
$$

Moles of fuel per 100 moles of dry exhaust:

$$
n_{fuel} = \frac{8.8}{8} = 1.10 \text{ moles of } C_8H_{18}
$$

**Step 2 — Nitrogen Balance:**

Moles of $N_2$ = 87.2. Air contains $N_2/O_2$ ratio of 3.76:

$$
n_{O_2,supplied} = \frac{87.2}{3.76} = 23.19 \text{ moles of } O_2
$$

**Step 3 — Total Air Moles:**

$$
n_{air} = 23.19 \times (1 + 3.76) = 23.19 \times 4.76 = 110.38 \text{ moles of air}
$$

**Step 4 — Air–Fuel Ratio (Molar Basis):**

$$
AFR_{molar} = \frac{110.38}{1.10} = 100.35 \text{ moles of air per mole of fuel}
$$

**Step 5 — Air–Fuel Ratio (Mass Basis):**

Using molecular weights: air = 28.97 kg/kmol, $C_8H_{18}$ = 114 kg/kmol.

$$
AFR_{mass} = \frac{100.35 \times 28.97}{1 \times 114} = \frac{2907.14}{114} = 25.50
$$

**Step 6 — Compute λ:**

Stoichiometric AFR for gasoline = 14.7

$$
\lambda = \frac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}} = \frac{25.50}{14.7} = 1.735
$$

**Step 7 — Compute Equivalence Ratio φ:**

$$
\phi = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{1.735} = 0.576
$$

**Step 8 — Identify the Operating Mode:**

Since $\lambda = 1.735 > 1.0$ and $\phi = 0.576 < 1.0$, the engine is operating in **lean burn** mode. The value of λ = 1.735 falls within the **stratified lean-burn range** (1.4 – 2.0) typical of TSI engines at part-load cruise.

*(7 marks: 1 for carbon balance, 1 for nitrogen balance, 1 for air moles, 1 for AFR mass basis, 1 for λ, 1 for φ, 1 for mode identification.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks in Stratified Charge / TSI Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing λ with φ:** Many students write $\lambda = 14.7 / AFR$ instead of the correct $AFR / 14.7$. The correct definition is $\lambda = AFR_{actual} / AFR_{stoich}$. Always check before writing the formula.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract water from dry exhaust analysis:** When given "dry" mole percentages, students sometimes include $H_2O$ in the balance. In KTU 2024 scheme problems, the $H_2O$ content is **not measured**; balance the equation using dry-basis values and account for the 9 moles of $H_2O$ per mole of $C_8H_{18}$ *theoretically* but **do not include them in the dry mole balance**.
>
> 3. **Reporting BSFC without units:** Always write BSFC in **g/kWh**, not "kg/hr" or "L/hr". KTU examiners will deduct 1 mark for missing or wrong units.
>
> 4. **Not justifying the choice of γ:** In efficiency calculations, students frequently use $\gamma = 1.4$ for the entire cycle. This is the **cold cycle** value. For the **combustion / expansion** processes, use a lower γ (1.30 – 1.35) to account for dissociation. The 1-mark deduction for "no justification of γ value" is very common.
>
> 5. **Mixing up stratified with homogeneous in mode-switching diagrams:** When asked to draw a "mode-switching flowchart", students often forget to include the **knock sensor** and **lambda sensor** feedback loops. KTU 2024 scheme awards 1 full mark for the closed-loop control aspect.
>
> 6. **Omitting the aftertreatment discussion:** TSI engines require a **three-way catalyst** in stoichiometric mode and may need a **NOx trap** in lean mode. Forgetting the aftertreatment link will cost 1–2 marks in 7-mark questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Card — Pin This on Your Wall**

- [ ] **Stratified charge** = non-uniform air–fuel mixture; rich near spark plug, lean elsewhere.
- [ ] **Lean burn** = λ > 1; typical TSI range λ = 1.4 – 2.0.
- [ ] **TSI = Turbo + Stratified + Injection** (Volkswagen trademark).
- [ ] **Late compression injection** is essential to prevent mixture homogenization.
- [ ] **Stoichiometric AFR (gasoline) = 14.7 : 1**; *not 14.3, not 15.0.*
- [ ] **Lambda** = $AFR_{actual} / 14.7$; **φ** = $14.7 / AFR_{actual}$ = $1/\lambda$.
- [ ] **Three operating modes of TSI:** Stratified (λ = 1.4 – 2.0), Homogeneous (λ = 1.0), Homogeneous lean (λ = 1.05 – 1.2).
- [ ] **Throttle is wide open** in stratified mode → minimal pumping loss.
- [ ] **Turbocharger boost** = 0.3 – 1.5 bar gauge; improves specific output.
- [ ] **Piezo injectors** = 100 – 350 bar; SMD < 25 µm; multi-hole (6 – 7 holes).
- [ ] **Otto efficiency formula:** $\eta = 1 - (1 / r^{\gamma - 1})$.
- [ ] **Use γ = 1.4 for cold (isentropic) processes; γ = 1.30 – 1.35 for combustion / expansion products.**
- [ ] **BSFC of TSI stratified mode** = 210 – 230 g/kWh (vs 250 – 280 g/kWh for stoichiometric PFI).
- [ ] **Fuel economy gain of TSI vs PFI** = 15 – 25 % (real-world).
- [ ] **Stratified mode cannot be sustained at full load** — switch to homogeneous stoichiometric.
- [ ] **NOx is higher in lean burn** → requires EGR or NOx trap.
- [ ] **Particulate matter is higher in direct injection** → requires GPF (Gasoline Particulate Filter) since Euro 6d.
- [ ] **ECU control loop** runs at ~100 Hz on Bosch MED17 platform.
- [ ] **Misfire risk** rises sharply at λ > 1.8 for gasoline.
- [ ] **Key suppliers of TSI technology:** Bosch (injectors, ECU), IHI / Garrett (turbo), Continental (sensors).
- [ ] **Real-world examples:** VW 1.0 TSI, VW 1.5 TSI EVO, Audi 2.0 TFSI, Ford 1.0 EcoBoost, Hyundai 1.6 T-GDI.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RCCI Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT612 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** RCCI Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Reactivity Controlled Compression Ignition (RCCI)** is an advanced dual-fuel, low-temperature combustion (LTC) strategy in which **two fuels of differing auto-ignition reactivity** are introduced into the combustion chamber in a stratified manner. A **low-reactivity fuel (LRF)** — typically gasoline, ethanol, or natural gas — is premixed with the intake air to form a relatively homogeneous, low-reactivity charge. A **high-reactivity fuel (HRF)** — typically diesel, biodiesel, or a high-cetane distillate — is injected directly into the cylinder near top dead center (TDC), creating a **gradient of fuel reactivity** in the cylinder. Auto-ignition is initiated in the most reactive regions first, and the reaction front then propagates into progressively less reactive mixtures. This controlled, staged auto-ignition enables simultaneous control of **NOx and soot emissions** while operating over a high-efficiency, partially premixed combustion regime.

RCCI belongs to the family of **Advanced Combustion Concepts (ACC)** which also include HCCI, PCCI (Premixed Charged Compression Ignition), and PPC (Partially Premixed Combustion). The defining feature of RCCI compared to HCCI is the **temporal and spatial separation of fuel injection streams**, which allows a wider, more practical operating range.

### Key Terminology Table

| Term | Full Form | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **RCCI** | Reactivity Controlled Compression Ignition | Dual-fuel LTC strategy |
| **LRF** | Low Reactivity Fuel | Premixed with air (gasoline, ethanol, NG) |
| **HRF** | High Reactivity Fuel | Direct injected (diesel, biodiesel) |
| **AKI** | Anti-Knock Index | (RON + MON)/2 — measures LRF resistance to knock |
| **CN** | Cetane Number | Measures HRF ignition delay |
| **LTC** | Low Temperature Combustion | Operates below ~2000 K adiabatic flame temp |
| **TDC** | Top Dead Center | Reference crank angle for peak pressure |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Plain-English Intuition**
> Imagine you are cooking a thick **dal (lentil stew)** on a heavy iron tava. If you pour very hot dal (high reactivity) on one half of the tava and slightly warm dal (low reactivity) on the other half simultaneously, the hot side will start bubbling first (auto-ignition) and then the bubbling will gradually spread to the cooler side. You control the entire "boiling" by adjusting **where** and **how much** of the hot mixture you pour. This staged "boiling-over" is exactly what happens in an RCCI engine — the cylinder contains a controlled **reactivity gradient** that propagates combustion gradually, instead of an abrupt explosion (as in conventional diesel) or a flame-front burn (as in spark-ignition gasoline). The result is **controlled, fast, yet cool** combustion that gives high efficiency *and* low emissions simultaneously.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics**
> - **Premixed Equivalence Ratio ($\phi_{LRF}$):** Typically 0.3 – 0.7 (lean overall)
> - **Direct-Injected Fuel Mass Fraction:** ~10 % – 40 % of total energy
> - **Peak In-Cylinder Temperature:** **< 2000 K** (limits thermal NOx formation)
> - **Reactivity Gradient ($\Delta \text{Reactivity}$):** Difference between HRF and LRF reactivity — the *governing* parameter
> - **EGR Tolerance:** Up to **~60 %** (used to extend RCCI range)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics Behind RCCI Combustion

RCCI is built on three foundational phenomena in compression-ignition chemistry:

**1. Two-Stage Auto-Ignition Chemistry**
Hydrocarbon oxidation follows a **two-stage heat-release** mechanism. During the first stage (often called "cool flame"), intermediate species (formaldehydes, peroxides) are formed with mild heat release. The second stage is the main, high-temperature auto-ignition. RCCI exploits this by ensuring the cool-flame region occurs in *low-reactivity* zones (LRF-rich) and the main ignition in *high-reactivity* zones (HRF-rich), thereby **decoupling the two stages spatially and temporally**.

**2. Fuel Reactivity Stratification**
Instead of mixing both fuels uniformly (HCCI) or stratifying only *mass* (PPC), RCCI stratifies the **reactivity field** of the in-cylinder mixture. The Knocking Resistance Rating (Octane Number) of the LRF is high (RON ~ 85–100), while the Cetane Number of the HRF is high (CN ~ 45–55 for diesel). This **reactivity gradient** $\nabla R$ acts as a combustion-phasing controller.

**3. In-Cylinder Fuel Blending (ICFB)**
Modern RCCI engines employ a sophisticated **dual-injection** architecture:
- **Port Fuel Injection (PFI)** of LRF during the intake stroke
- **Direct Injection (DI)** of HRF late in the compression stroke (often multiple pulses)
This creates a non-uniform mixture where reactivity varies with location.

### 2.2 Operating Envelope of RCCI

RCCI typically operates successfully between:
- **BMEP:** 2 – 12 bar (low-to-mid load)
- **Speed:** 1000 – 3000 rpm
- **$\lambda$ (relative air–fuel ratio):** 1.4 – 2.5 (overall lean)
- **EGR Rate:** 0 % – 60 %

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{AKI} = \dfrac{\text{RON} + \text{MON}}{2}$ | Anti-Knock Index of LRF | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | Overall equivalence ratio (per zone) | — |
| 3 | $\dot{Q}_{\text{RCCI}} = \dot{Q}_{\text{cool flame}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{high-T ignition}}$ | Total HRR decomposition | J/s |
| 4 | $\text{IMEP} = \oint P \, dV / V_d$ | Indicated Mean Effective Pressure | bar |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{ind}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{ind}}}{m_f \cdot \text{LHV}}$ | Indicated thermal efficiency | — |
| 6 | $E_{\text{NO}_x} \propto \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-E_a}{R T_{\text{peak}}}\right)$ | Zeldovich thermal NOx dependence (Arrhenius) | ppm |
| 7 | $\text{Soot} \propto \phi_{\text{local}} \cdot T^{-1}$ | Soot formation (rich-zone dependence) | g/kWh |
| 8 | $\tau_{\text{ign}} = f\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\text{CN}}, T, P, \phi\right)$ | Ignition delay (HRF) | ms / CAD |
| 9 | $P_{\text{max}} \leq 200\,\text{bar}$ (RCCI limit) | Peak pressure cap to avoid mechanical stress | bar |
| 10 | $T_{\text{adiabatic,flame}} = T_u + \dfrac{Y_F \cdot \text{LHV}}{c_{p,\text{mix}}}$ | Adiabatic flame temperature | K |

> [!NOTE] **CRITICAL TABLE FORMATTING NOTE**
> For KTU exam answer sheets, always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead of the vertical pipe symbol in table entries, otherwise markdown rendering breaks.

### 2.4 Why RCCI Works — The Trade-Off Triangle

Conventional engines are constrained by the **Efficiency–Emissions–Cost triangle**:
- **Gasoline SI** → low NOx/soot, low efficiency at high load (knock-limited).
- **Diesel CI** → high efficiency, high NOx *and* soot (the classic **NOx-soot trade-off**).
- **HCCI** → very high efficiency & clean, but extremely narrow operating range (no control of combustion phasing).
- **RCCI** → best of all worlds: **high efficiency + low NOx + low soot + controllable phasing**, with moderate engineering complexity.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> RCCI is the leading combustion strategy for **heavy-duty on-road trucks (US 2027 NOx + GHG standards)**, **agricultural tractors**, and **hybrid powertrains** where the engine operates at a narrow, optimal load range. Companies like **Caterpillar, Cummins, Westport Innovations** and the **University of Wisconsin ERC** have demonstrated RCCI prototypes with **brake thermal efficiencies up to ~46 %** — about 8–10 percentage points higher than baseline diesel.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why RCCI Eliminates the NOx-Soot Trade-Off

The classic **NOx-soot trade-off** in conventional diesel engines can be derived from elementary combustion kinetics. Below is the complete, exam-ready derivation that a KTU 2024 answer must contain.

**Step 1 — Define local equivalence ratio in a stratified mixture**
Consider a small volume element $dV$ inside the cylinder. The local fuel–air equivalence ratio is

$$
\phi(x, \theta) = \frac{m_f(x, \theta)}{m_a(x, \theta)} \cdot \frac{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}}{1}
$$

where $x$ is the spatial coordinate and $\theta$ is crank angle. In a **diesel spray** without premixing, $\phi_{\text{local}}$ varies from 0 (pure air) to > 4 (in the spray core), so there are *always* rich zones.

**Step 2 — Soot formation rate in rich zones**
Soot is formed in fuel-rich, high-temperature regions by the **soot precursor (polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon) mechanism**:

$$
\dot{S} = A_s \, \phi_{\text{local}}^{n_s} \, P^{m_s} \, \exp\!\left(\frac{-E_s}{R T}\right)
$$

For diesel sprays, $n_s \approx 2\text{–}4$, meaning soot grows **very rapidly** with rich equivalence. The diesel spray core is the dominant soot source.

**Step 3 — Thermal NOx formation (Zeldovich mechanism)**
NOx (mainly NO) forms by the extended Zeldovich mechanism:

$$
\text{(a)}\;\; \text{O} + \text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{N}
$$
$$
\text{(b)}\;\; \text{N} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}
$$
$$
\text{(c)}\;\; \text{N} + \text{OH} \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{H}
$$

The net rate is highly temperature-sensitive:

$$
\frac{d[\text{NO}]}{dt} = k_1 [\text{O}][\text{N}_2] - k_{-1} [\text{NO}][\text{N}] + \cdots
$$

The **empirical approximation** widely used in engine textbooks is

$$
[\text{NO}_x] \approx A_{\text{NO}} \, \exp\!\left(\frac{-E_a}{R T_{\text{peak}}}\right)
$$

where $E_a / R \approx 38{,}000\,\text{K}$. This shows that **doubling peak temperature more than doubles NO concentration**.

**Step 4 — The trade-off in conventional diesel**
In a normal diesel engine:
- To **reduce soot**, you need *more* premixing (longer ignition delay → leaner spray core) → lower peak temperature.
- To **reduce NOx**, you need *less* premixing (retarded injection, EGR) → richer local zones → more soot.
- This produces the famous **banana curve** on a NOx–soot plot.

**Step 5 — How RCCI breaks the trade-off**
RCCI uses an LRF-rich premixture to **suppress soot formation** (no rich zones exist because LRF is fully premixed at $\phi < 0.7$). The HRF injection is **lean** enough that the local rich-equivalence zones are eliminated. Simultaneously, the LRF acts as a thermal *heat sink* (because of its lower energy density and lower combustion temperature per unit mass), keeping $T_{\text{peak}} < 2000\,\text{K}$, which kills thermal NOx.

Mathematically, the combined constraint is:

$$
\phi_{\text{local, max}} < 1 \quad \text{(to prevent soot)}
$$

$$
T_{\text{peak}} < 2000\,\text{K} \quad \text{(to prevent thermal NOx)}
$$

Both conditions are achievable *simultaneously* only in LTC strategies like RCCI, because the LRF is premixed *lean* and the HRF is injected *lean*.

### 3.2 Derivation: RCCI Combustion Phasing Control via Reactivity Gradient

**Step 1 — Define the Reactivity Gradient**
Let the local octane number of the premixed LRF be $\text{ON}(x)$ and the local cetane number contribution from the HRF be $\text{CN}(x)$. The *effective reactivity* $R$ is

$$
R(x, \theta) = \text{CN}_{\text{local}}(x,\theta) - \text{ON}_{\text{local}}(x,\theta)
$$

**Step 2 — Ignition Delay Model**
A simplified Arrhenius-type ignition delay used widely is

$$
\tau_{\text{ign}}(x) = A \, \phi^{-a} \, P^{-b} \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E}{R\,T(x, \theta)}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Combustion Phasing (CA50)**
CA50 is the crank angle at which 50 % of the cumulative heat release has occurred. For a reactivity-stratified charge, CA50 is approximately

$$
\text{CA50} \approx \theta_{\text{inj,HRF}} + \int_{\theta_{\text{inj,HRF}}}^{\text{CA50}} \frac{d\theta}{1 + k_r \cdot R(x,\theta)}
$$

where $k_r$ is a reactivity rate constant. **Higher $R$ (more HRF or higher CN) → earlier CA50.**

**Step 4 — Control Authority**
By independently controlling (a) **LRF mass** (PFI duration), (b) **HRF mass** (DI pulse), (c) **HRF timing** (SOI), and (d) **EGR rate**, the engine ECU can shift CA50 by up to **±20 CAD** — a much wider authority than HCCI can provide.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Exam Pattern)

**Problem:** An RCCI engine uses 70 % gasoline (PFI, AKI = 92) and 30 % diesel (DI, CN = 50) by energy. The overall equivalence ratio is 0.45, intake temperature 380 K, pressure 1.8 bar, and diesel SOI is 8° BTDC. Estimate the ignition delay of the diesel spray and identify the combustion mode.

**Given:**
- LRF (gasoline) AKI = 92, $\text{LHV} = 44\,\text{MJ/kg}$
- HRF (diesel) CN = 50, $\text{LHV} = 43\,\text{MJ/kg}$
- $P_{\text{inj}} = 1.8\,\text{bar} = 1.8 \times 10^5\,\text{Pa}$
- $T_{\text{cyl}} \approx 850\,\text{K}$ (compressed charge)
- $\phi = 0.45$

**Step 1 — Use the Hardenberg Hohenberg correlation:**

$$
\tau_{\text{ign}} = \left(0.36 + \frac{0.22}{\text{CN}}\right) \cdot \exp\!\left(E_a \left(\frac{1}{RT} - \frac{1}{17{,}190}\right)\right) \cdot \left(\frac{21}{P - 12.4}\right)^{1.0}
$$

with $E_a = 6188\,\text{bar}\cdot\text{ms}/\text{K}$ and $P$ in bar.

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$
\left(0.36 + \frac{0.22}{50}\right) = 0.3644
$$

$$
\frac{1}{RT} = \frac{1}{8.314 \times 850} = 1.415 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{17{,}190} = 5.82 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{K}^{-1}
$$

Exponent term:

$$
\exp\!\left(6188 \times (1.415 - 0.582) \times 10^{-4}\right) = \exp(0.515) = 1.674
$$

Pressure term:

$$
\left(\frac{21}{1.8 - 12.4}\right) = \left(\frac{21}{-10.6}\right)
$$

A negative pressure bracket indicates the formula is only valid for $P > 12.4$ bar (i.e., inside the cylinder, *not* at injection). Convert to in-cylinder pressure at SOI:

$$
P_{\text{cyl,SOI}} \approx 35\,\text{bar},\quad \left(\frac{21}{35 - 12.4}\right) = \left(\frac{21}{22.6}\right) = 0.929
$$

**Step 3 — Final delay**

$$
\tau_{\text{ign}} = 0.3644 \times 1.674 \times 0.929 \approx 0.567\,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to CAD at 1500 rpm**

$$
\omega = 1500 \cdot \frac{2\pi}{60} = 157.08\,\text{rad/s}
$$

$$
\text{CAD}_{\text{ign}} = \tau_{\text{ign}} \cdot \omega \cdot \frac{180}{\pi} = 0.567 \times 10^{-3} \times 157.08 \times 57.296 \approx 5.1^{\circ}
$$

**Result:** $\tau_{\text{ign}} \approx 0.57\,\text{ms}$ or **5.1 CAD**. This delay is long enough for the diesel to partially premix with the LRF–air mixture, creating a **mildly premixed low-temperature combustion**, which is the signature of RCCI.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 RCCI Fuel-Reactivity Stratification Map (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Intake Stroke: PFI of Low-Reactivity Fuel LRF] --> B[LRF fully premixes with air by IVC]
    B --> C[Compression Stroke: charge is moderately lean phi less than 0.7]
    C --> D{Near TDC}
    D --> E[Direct Injection of High-Reactivity Fuel HRF]
    E --> F[Spray creates local rich zones with high reactivity]
    F --> G[Auto-ignition begins in HRF rich zones first]
    G --> H[Cool flame region LRF rich low temp]
    G --> I[High temperature region HRF rich main heat release]
    H --> J[Controlled burn front propagates into LRF zones]
    I --> J
    J --> K[T peak less than 2000 K low NOx and low soot]
    K --> L[Expansion Stroke: high indicated efficiency]
```

### 4.2 RCCI vs HCCI vs PPC vs Diesel Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[RCCI Dual Fuel Reactivity Stratified] --> B1[HRF direct injected]
    A1 --> B2[LRF port injected]
    A1 --> B3[Wide operating range moderate complexity]

    C1[HCCI Single Fuel Homogeneous] --> D1[Single fuel premixed]
    C1 --> D2[No control of CA50 narrow range]

    E1[PPC Single Fuel Partially Premixed CI] --> F1[Single fuel late single injection]
    E1 --> F2[Long ignition delay premixing]

    G1[Conventional Diesel] --> H1[Single fuel early injection]
    G1 --> H2[Rich diffusion flame high NOx or high soot]
```

### 4.3 RCCI Control Authority Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[ECU Control Inputs] --> A1[LRF Mass Port Injection Pulse Width]
    A0 --> A2[HRF Mass Direct Injection Pulse Width]
    A0 --> A3[HRF Start of Injection Timing SOI]
    A0 --> A4[EGR Valve Position]
    A0 --> A5[Intake Pressure Boost]

    A1 --> B0[In-Cylinder Reactivity Field R of x theta]
    A2 --> B0
    A3 --> B0
    A4 --> B0
    A5 --> B0

    B0 --> C0[Local Equivalence Ratio phi of x theta]
    C0 --> D0[Local Temperature Field T of x theta]
    D0 --> E0[Local Ignition Delay tau of x theta]
    E0 --> F0[CA50 Combustion Phasing]
    F0 --> G0[Target CA50 approx 8 to 12 ATDC]
```

### 4.4 NOx-Soot Trade-off Map (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Conventional Diesel: high NOx, high soot] --> A1[Banana curve regime]
    B[RCCI: low NOx, low soot] --> B1[Inside the clean target box]
    C[HCCI: very low NOx, very low soot] --> C1[Very narrow operating window]
    D[Gasoline SI: low NOx, low soot] --> D1[Low efficiency at high load]

    A1 --> E[Target emission box]
    B1 --> E
    C1 --> E
    D1 -.Not in.-> E
```

> [!NOTE] **Diagram Compilation Note**
> All Mermaid node IDs above (e.g., `A1`, `B0`, `C0`) follow the alphanumeric-prefix rule. No reserved keywords (e.g., `end`, `subgraph`) are used as node IDs. All labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text without any markdown bold or italic formatting inside the double-quoted strings.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define RCCI. What is the role of low-reactivity and high-reactivity fuels?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Reactivity Controlled Compression Ignition (RCCI) is a dual-fuel, low-temperature combustion strategy in which a low-reactivity fuel (LRF) is premixed with the intake air and a high-reactivity fuel (HRF) is directly injected near TDC. The **LRF (e.g., gasoline, ethanol)** controls the overall reactivity and prevents premature auto-ignition in the bulk mixture, while the **HRF (e.g., diesel, biodiesel)** initiates the combustion front and determines the start of combustion. Together, they create a controlled **reactivity gradient** in the cylinder that allows simultaneous low NOx and low soot, with high indicated efficiency. (3 marks)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] List three advantages of RCCI over conventional diesel combustion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Simultaneous reduction of NOx and soot** (breaks the diesel NOx–soot trade-off).
2. **Higher indicated thermal efficiency** (up to 46 %) due to low-temperature, near-constant-volume combustion.
3. **Fuel flexibility** — the use of two fuels allows blending of biofuels (ethanol, biodiesel) with conventional fuels. (3 marks)

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU 2024 ESE Pattern: Internal Choice is given. **You must attempt ONE of the two.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]

**(a)** Explain the principle of Reactivity Controlled Compression Ignition (RCCI) with a neat schematic of in-cylinder reactivity stratification. Compare RCCI with HCCI on the basis of (i) fuel system, (ii) combustion control authority, and (iii) operating range. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between local equivalence ratio, peak flame temperature, and NOx formation in an RCCI engine. Show, with equations, why RCCI breaks the conventional diesel NOx–soot trade-off. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a)** RCCI operates on the principle of **in-cylinder fuel blending of two fuels with widely differing auto-ignition reactivity**. A low-reactivity fuel (gasoline, ethanol, or natural gas) is port-injected during the intake stroke and homogenizes with the air by the time the inlet valve closes. A high-reactivity fuel (diesel or biodiesel) is then directly injected into the cylinder late in the compression stroke, typically within 10–20° BTDC. The diesel spray creates local zones of high reactivity while the bulk mixture remains low in reactivity. Auto-ignition begins in the most reactive regions (diesel-rich) and propagates outward to the less reactive gasoline-rich regions in a controlled manner.

**Comparison with HCCI:**

| Parameter | **HCCI** | **RCCI** |
|---|---|---|
| Fuel system | Single fuel, premixed | Dual fuel, LRF premixed + HRF direct injected |
| Combustion control authority | Very limited (no SOI control) | Wide (LRF ratio, HRF mass, SOI, EGR all controllable) |
| Operating range | Very narrow (high-octane fuels limit low-load) | Moderate to wide (load 2–12 bar BMEP achievable) |

The schematic of the in-cylinder reactivity stratification is shown in **Section 4.1** above. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derivation:

Local equivalence ratio is

$$
\phi_{\text{local}}(x) = \frac{m_f(x)}{m_a(x)} \cdot \frac{1}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}}
$$

Peak local flame temperature in a lean charge follows the adiabatic flame temperature relation:

$$
T_{\text{peak}} = T_u + \frac{Y_F \cdot \text{LHV}}{c_{p,\text{mix}}} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\phi_{\text{local}}}{1 + \phi_{\text{local}}/\phi_{\text{opt}}}\right)
$$

For lean mixtures, $T_{\text{peak}}$ decreases nearly linearly with decreasing $\phi_{\text{local}}$. NOx formation follows the Zeldovich mechanism with Arrhenius temperature dependence:

$$
[\text{NO}_x] \approx A \exp\!\left(\frac{-38{,}000}{T_{\text{peak}}}\right)
$$

In RCCI, the LRF premixed charge is always lean ($\phi_{\text{LRF}} < 0.7$), so the *bulk* temperature stays below 2000 K, suppressing NOx. At the same time, the HRF injection is controlled such that even the spray core does not exceed $\phi = 1$, eliminating the rich stoichiometric pockets that produce soot. Hence RCCI breaks the trade-off:

$$
\phi_{\text{local,max}} < 1 \;\;\text{(no soot)} \quad \wedge \quad T_{\text{peak}} < 2000\,\text{K} \;\;\text{(no NOx)}
$$

Both conditions hold simultaneously, which is impossible in conventional diesel. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]

**(a)** Discuss the four key control parameters that govern the combustion phasing (CA50) in an RCCI engine. Show, with a block diagram, how each parameter influences the in-cylinder reactivity field. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A heavy-duty RCCI prototype uses 65 % gasoline (PFI) and 35 % diesel (DI) by energy. The overall equivalence ratio is 0.5. The premixed equivalence ratio of the gasoline–air mixture is 0.42 and the locally rich diesel zone reaches $\phi_{\text{local}} = 0.9$. Estimate the peak in-cylinder temperature and the relative NOx index (relative to baseline diesel $T = 2400\,\text{K}$). Use LHV of gasoline = 44 MJ/kg, $c_p$ of mixture = 1.05 kJ/kg·K, intake temperature 360 K. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a)** The four governing parameters of CA50 in RCCI are:

1. **LRF Port-Injection Mass** — Increases LRF mass → lower reactivity overall → retards CA50.
2. **HRF Direct-Injection Mass** — Increases HRF mass → higher reactivity → advances CA50.
3. **HRF Start of Injection (SOI)** — Earlier SOI → more premixing time → earlier but smoother combustion; later SOI → closer to diffusion-like.
4. **EGR Rate** — Higher EGR → lower temperature and lower $O_2$ → retards CA50 and reduces pressure-rise rate.

The block diagram showing how each parameter influences the in-cylinder reactivity field is presented in **Section 4.3** above. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Computation:

Step 1 — Effective energy fraction of diesel in local rich zone:
For the rich zone mass $m_{\text{zone}}$, total fuel energy $Q_{\text{zone}} = m_f \cdot \text{LHF}$. The fraction of HRF in the rich zone is approximated by the ratio of local to overall equivalence:

$$
f_{\text{HRF,zone}} = \frac{\phi_{\text{local}}}{\phi_{\text{overall}}} = \frac{0.9}{0.5} = 1.8
$$

But since the rich zone is locally only a fraction of the total charge, we take an *effective* local energy concentration factor of 1.0 (i.e., the rich zone does not exceed stoichiometric, hence no rich combustion).

Step 2 — Effective fuel–air ratio of rich zone:

$$
(F/A)_{\text{zone}} = 0.9 \times 0.0682 = 0.0614
$$

Step 3 — Peak temperature using energy balance (assumption: lean overall, complete combustion):

$$
T_{\text{peak}} = T_u + \frac{(F/A)_{\text{zone}} \cdot \text{LHV}_{\text{avg}}}{c_p \cdot (1 + 1/\phi_{\text{local}} \cdot 14.6)}
$$

where the stoichiometric mass ratio of air to gasoline is ~14.6.

Effective air–fuel ratio in zone:

$$
(A/F)_{\text{zone}} = \frac{1}{0.0614} = 16.3
$$

Total mixture mass per kg of fuel:

$$
m_{\text{mix}} = 1 + 16.3 = 17.3\,\text{kg}
$$

Energy released per kg of mixture:

$$
q = \frac{\text{LHV}}{m_{\text{mix}}} = \frac{44{,}000}{17.3} \approx 2543\,\text{kJ/kg}
$$

Step 4 — Peak temperature:

$$
T_{\text{peak}} = 360 + \frac{2543}{1.05} = 360 + 2422 = 2782\,\text{K}
$$

Step 5 — Correction: in real RCCI, the bulk mixture dominates peak temperature. The *effective* $T_{\text{peak}}$ of the reacting zone is moderated by the LRF cooling effect. A more realistic bulk estimate using the overall $\phi = 0.5$:

$$
(F/A)_{\text{overall}} = 0.5 \times 0.0682 = 0.0341
$$
$$
m_{\text{mix,overall}} = 1 + \frac{1}{0.0341} = 30.3\,\text{kg}
$$
$$
q_{\text{overall}} = \frac{44{,}000}{30.3} = 1452\,\text{kJ/kg}
$$
$$
T_{\text{peak,bulk}} = 360 + \frac{1452}{1.05} = 360 + 1383 = 1743\,\text{K}
$$

Step 6 — Relative NOx index (RNI):

$$
\text{RNI} = \frac{\exp(-38{,}000 / 1743)}{\exp(-38{,}000 / 2400)} = \frac{1.58 \times 10^{-10}}{9.84 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.6 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Result:** $T_{\text{peak, bulk}} \approx 1743\,\text{K}$, **RNI $\approx 1.6 \times 10^{-4}$** — meaning NOx is reduced by about **4 orders of magnitude** compared to baseline diesel. **(7 marks)**

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING] **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing RCCI with HCCI** — they are *not* the same. HCCI is single-fuel and homogeneous; RCCI is dual-fuel and reactivity-stratified. Examiners deduct **2 full marks** if you do not explicitly mention the *reactivity stratification*.
> 2. **Forgetting the Zeldovich equation** — at least one temperature-dependent NOx equation must be quoted for full marks in any derivation question.
> 3. **Confusing equivalence ratio notation** — use $\phi$ for local and $\phi_{\text{overall}}$ for global. Mixing them costs marks.
> 4. **Missing the role of EGR** — EGR is *not* optional in RCCI; it extends the operating range and controls pressure-rise rate.
> 5. **Neglecting cold-start limitations** — RCCI fails below ~80 °C coolant because fuel evaporation and reactivity control are inconsistent. Examiners reward students who mention this limitation.
> 6. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — always state $T$ in K, $\tau_{\text{ign}}$ in ms or CAD, and $\phi$ as dimensionless.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist**
> - **RCCI = Dual-Fuel + Reactivity Stratification + LTC.** Memorize this exact phrase.
> - **LRF** (gasoline/ethanol) is **port-injected**; **HRF** (diesel) is **directly injected** near TDC.
> - **Reactivity gradient** $\nabla R$ is the *governing control parameter*.
> - **CA50** is controlled by LRF mass, HRF mass, SOI, and EGR.
> - **Peak temperature < 2000 K** kills thermal NOx; **$\phi_{\text{local}} < 1$** kills soot.
> - **NOx–soot trade-off is broken** because both conditions hold simultaneously.
> - **NOx $\propto \exp(-E_a / RT_{\text{peak}})$** — must be quoted in any derivation.
> - **Hohenberg-Begen ignition delay** formula is the standard numerical tool.
> - **Hardenberg-Hohenberg** correlation is exam-acceptable for $\tau_{\text{ign}}$ calculations.
> - **Operating range:** 2 – 12 bar BMEP, 1000 – 3000 rpm, EGR 0 – 60 %.
> - **Efficiency gain:** 8 – 10 percentage points over baseline diesel (≈ 46 % BTE demonstrated).
> - **Limitation:** Poor cold-start; narrow fuel flexibility window; HC and CO emissions slightly higher than conventional diesel.
> - **Compared to HCCI:** Wider operating range; better transient control.
> - **Compared to PPC:** Higher efficiency ceiling; better load extension.
> - **Compared to Diesel:** Lower NOx AND lower soot simultaneously.
> - **Key historical milestone:** ERC University of Wisconsin (2011 – 2014) demonstrated > 45 % BTE on a multicylinder heavy-duty engine using gasoline–diesel RCCI.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrogen Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT612 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Hydrogen Engine** is an internal combustion engine (ICE) — either a modified spark-ignition (SI) or dual-fuel compression-ignition (CI) prime mover — that derives its motive power from the controlled oxidation (combustion) of molecular hydrogen (H₂) in place of, or in conjunction with, conventional petroleum-based hydrocarbon fuels. The reaction pathway is the exothermic recombination of hydrogen with atmospheric oxygen:

$$2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Energy}$$

Hydrogen engines are classified by KTU under the broader umbrella of **Alternative Fuels** and represent a near-zero-carbon powertrain solution, since the only regulated tailpipe emission is water vapour and trace quantities of NOₓ (formed thermally from the nitrogen in inducted air).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Hydrogen engines are studied under Module 2 (Alternative Fuels for IC Engines) alongside CNG, LPG, Biodiesel, and Electric/Hybrid systems. The focus is on combustion chemistry, performance, emissions (especially NOₓ), and on-engine modifications required.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a chef (the combustion chamber) trying to light a campfire. With wood (gasoline/diesel), the fire is slow to start, smoulders steadily, and produces a lot of soot and smoke. Now replace the wood with **hydrogen gas** — it lights almost instantly from the tiniest spark (like a flint stone click), burns with an almost invisible pale-blue flame, and leaves no soot behind — only water. However, that flame is so hot that it can "cook" the nitrogen in the surrounding air, creating a pollutant (NOₓ). Hydrogen is like an extremely energetic, clean-burning but very finicky fuel — brilliant in performance but temperamental in storage and prone to "running hot."

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Property | Hydrogen (H₂) | Gasoline (reference) |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular weight | **2.016 g/mol** | ≈ 110 g/mol |
| Lower Heating Value (LHV) | **120 MJ/kg** | 44 MJ/kg |
| Density (STP gas) | **0.0899 kg/m³** | 740 kg/m³ (liquid) |
| Stoichiometric A/F ratio (mass) | **34.3 : 1** | 14.7 : 1 |
| Auto-ignition temperature | **858 K (585 °C)** | ~620 K |
| Minimum ignition energy | **0.02 mJ** | 0.25 mJ |
| Laminar flame speed in air | **2.7–3.2 m/s** | 0.4 m/s |
| Flammability limits (φ) | **0.1 to 7.1** | 0.4 to 1.4 |
| Diffusivity in air | **0.61 cm²/s** | 0.05 cm²/s |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **3× higher flame speed** and **10× lower ignition energy** of hydrogen. These two properties together are what make hydrogen engines run smoothly in ultra-lean mixtures but also cause **backfire and pre-ignition** problems — a recurring KTU exam theme.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Combustion Chemistry — The Heart of a Hydrogen Engine

The combustion of hydrogen is fundamentally simpler than hydrocarbon combustion because there is **no carbon atom** to deal with. This means:

- **No CO, CO₂, or unburnt HC** are produced in the ideal reaction.
- The entire chemical energy is released as sensible heat + water vapour enthalpy.
- Pollutant formation is restricted to **thermal NOₓ** (from N₂ + O₂ ↔ 2NO at flame temperatures above ~1800 K).

The stoichiometric equation per mole:

$$\text{H}_2 + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \frac{1}{2}(3.76)\text{N}_2 \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + 1.88\,\text{N}_2$$

When run **lean** (excess air), which is the preferred hydrogen-engine strategy, additional O₂ and N₂ dilute the burned gases, lowering peak flame temperature and suppressing NOₓ.

## 2.2 Why Hydrogen Engines? — The Five Engineering Drivers

1. **Renewable source pathway:** H₂ can be produced from water electrolysis using solar/wind electricity (green hydrogen).
2. **Highest mass energy density** of any chemical fuel: 120 MJ/kg vs 44 MJ/kg for gasoline — a near 3× advantage per kg.
3. **Ultra-lean combustion capability:** Hydrogen's wide flammability range (φ = 0.1 to 7.1) allows stable burning at equivalence ratios as low as 0.2 — improving efficiency.
4. **Zero carbon emissions** at tailpipe (only H₂O and trace NOₓ).
5. **High diffusivity:** H₂ mixes with air ~12× faster than gasoline vapour, enabling more homogeneous, complete combustion.

## 2.3 Operational Challenges — Why Hydrogen Engines Aren't Everywhere

- **Pre-ignition and backfire:** Residual H₂ in the induction manifold can ignite prematurely, sending a flame into the intake.
- **Abnormal combustion:** Knocking, pre-ignition, and rapid pressure rise rates (dP/dθ up to 10 bar/°CA) are common.
- **Storage on-board:** H₂'s very low density demands high-pressure tanks (350–700 bar) or cryogenic liquid H₂ (–253 °C).
- **Material compatibility:** Hydrogen embrittlement damages steels and copper; aluminium and composites are preferred.
- **Reduced volumetric energy density:** Even at 700 bar, H₂ occupies ~4× the volume of gasoline delivering the same energy.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{A/F}_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{8}{0.232} = 34.48$ | Stoichiometric air-to-fuel mass ratio for H₂ | – |
| 2 | $\phi = \dfrac{(\text{A/F})_{\text{actual}}}{(\text{A/F})_{\text{stoich}}}$ | Equivalence ratio definition | – |
| 3 | $\eta_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{m_f \cdot \text{LHV}}$ | Brake thermal efficiency | – |
| 4 | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b}$ | Brake specific fuel consumption | kg/kWh |
| 5 | $\rho_{\text{H}_2,\,700\,\text{bar}} \approx 40\,\text{kg/m}^3$ | Compressed H₂ density | kg/m³ |
| 6 | $V_{\text{equiv}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{gas}}}{120 \times \rho_{\text{H}_2}}$ | Equivalent H₂ volume for given energy | m³ |
| 7 | $\text{NO}_x \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R T_{\text{flame}}}\right)$ | Zeldovich thermal NOₓ (Arrhenius form) | ppm |
| 8 | $\dot{Q}_{\text{HV}} = \dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV}$ | Heat release rate (Hesselbarth–Heywood) | W |
| 9 | $\text{BSEC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV}}{P_b}$ | Brake specific energy consumption | MJ/kWh |
| 10 | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\phi}$ | Excess air ratio (lambda) | – |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorise the **8 kg O₂ / kg H₂** requirement — it is the single most-tested number in this module. From this, dividing by 0.232 (mass fraction of O₂ in air) yields the stoichiometric A/F ratio of ~34.3:1.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hydrogen engines are not a theoretical curiosity. Major OEMs have built operational prototypes:
- **BMW Hydrogen 7** (V12, 200 km range on liquid H₂)
- **Toyota Hydrogen Combustion Engine** (1.6 L turbo, 2024 Le Mans entrant)
- **Hyundai Nexo FCEV** (uses H₂ *fuel cell*, not engine — important distinction!)
- **Cummins B6.7H** hydrogen engine for heavy-duty trucks

These vehicles validate that hydrogen can be retrofitted into existing ICE platforms, providing a transitional technology before full fuel-cell commercialisation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Hydrogen

**Given:** Combustion reaction: $2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$

**Step 1 — Molecular masses.**
- Molecular mass of H₂ = $2 \times 1.008 = 2.016\,\text{g/mol}$
- Molecular mass of O₂ = $2 \times 16 = 32\,\text{g/mol}$

**Step 2 — Mass of O₂ required per kg of H₂.**
From stoichiometry, 2 moles of H₂ react with 1 mole of O₂. Therefore:

$$\dfrac{m_{\text{O}_2}}{m_{\text{H}_2}} = \dfrac{1 \times 32}{2 \times 2.016} = \dfrac{32}{4.032} = 7.937\,\text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg H}_2$$

**Step 3 — Convert O₂ requirement to air requirement.**
Dry air is taken as **23.2 % O₂ by mass** in KTU-notation (precise: 23.16 %).

$$\dfrac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_{\text{H}_2}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{O}_2}/0.232}{m_{\text{H}_2}} = \dfrac{7.937}{0.232}$$

$$\boxed{(\text{A/F})_{\text{stoich, H}_2} \approx 34.21 \approx 34.3}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Equivalence Between 1 L Gasoline and Compressed H₂

**Given:**
- Density of gasoline = 740 kg/m³
- LHV of gasoline = 44 MJ/kg
- LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg
- Compressed H₂ density at 700 bar ≈ 40 kg/m³

**Step 1 — Energy content of 1 L gasoline.**

$$E_{\text{gasoline}} = V \cdot \rho \cdot \text{LHV} = (1 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 740 \cdot 44 = 32.56\,\text{MJ}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of H₂ required to match this energy.**

$$m_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{E}{\text{LHV}_{\text{H}_2}} = \dfrac{32.56}{120} = 0.2713\,\text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Volume occupied by this H₂ at 700 bar.**

$$V_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{m_{\text{H}_2}}{\rho_{\text{H}_2}} = \dfrac{0.2713}{40} = 6.78 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3 \approx 6.78\,\text{L}$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric penalty factor.**

$$\boxed{\dfrac{V_{\text{H}_2}}{V_{\text{gasoline}}} = \dfrac{6.78}{1.0} \approx 6.8\times\text{ larger}}$$

This explains why hydrogen vehicles require bulky tanks despite their clean combustion.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Indicated Power and BSFC for a Hydrogen Engine

A four-cylinder, four-stroke hydrogen engine operates at 3000 rpm with a measured BSFC of 0.10 kg/kWh and brake thermal efficiency of 42 %.

**Step 1 — Convert engine speed to power strokes per second.**
For a 4-stroke engine: cycles per second = N/2 = 3000/2 = **1500 cycles/s**.

**Step 2 — Heat input rate from BSFC.**

For each kWh of brake work, 0.10 kg H₂ is burned.

$$\dot{m}_f = \text{BSFC} \times P_b$$

Let us assume rated brake power $P_b = 75\,\text{kW}$ (a typical KTU textbook value for an H₂ engine):

$$\dot{m}_f = 0.10 \times 75 = 7.5\,\text{kg/h} = 2.083 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{kg/s}$$

**Step 3 — Chemical power input.**

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV} = 2.083 \times 10^{-3} \times 120 \times 10^{6} = 250\,\text{kW}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check thermal efficiency.**

$$\eta_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{P_b}{\dot{Q}_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{75}{250} = 0.30 = 30\,\%$$

**Step 5 — Volumetric efficiency check.**
Swept volume $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L \cdot n$ with $D = 0.08\,\text{m},\ L = 0.09\,\text{m},\ n = 4$:

$$V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.08)^2(0.09)(4) = 1.81 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3 = 1.81\,\text{L}$$

Volumetric flow rate of air at this speed:

$$\dot{V}_{\text{air}} = \eta_v \cdot V_s \cdot \dfrac{N}{2} \cdot 60$$

For $\eta_v = 0.85$, $N = 3000$:

$$\dot{V}_{\text{air}} = 0.85 \times 1.81 \times 10^{-3} \times 1500 \times 60 = 138.5\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

Air mass flow: $\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = 138.5 \times 1.2 = 166.2\,\text{kg/h}$.

Fuel flow at λ = 2 (lean burn): $\dot{m}_f = \dot{m}_{\text{air}}/(2 \times 34.3) = 166.2/68.6 = 2.42\,\text{kg/h}$.

**Step 6 — Compute BSFC again.**

$$\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b} = \dfrac{2.42}{75} = 0.0323\,\text{kg/kWh}$$

This very low BSFC reflects hydrogen's high LHV — **a key KTU exam takeaway**.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Hydrogen vs Gasoline

| Performance / Emission Metric | Hydrogen Engine | Gasoline Engine | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| BSFC (kg/kWh) | **0.07–0.12** | 0.25–0.35 | H₂ looks "low" because of high LHV |
| BSEC (MJ/kWh) | **8.4–14.4** | 11–15 | True efficiency metric — H₂ is comparable |
| Brake thermal efficiency | **38–45 %** | 25–35 % | Higher due to lean burn and fast flame |
| Tailpipe CO₂ | **0 g/km** | 150–250 g/km | Zero carbon at point of use |
| Tailpipe NOₓ | **Low (lean burn)**, else high | Moderate | Critical design lever |
| Volumetric energy density | **4800 MJ/m³ @ 700 bar** | 32 000 MJ/m³ | Major storage challenge |
| Cold start capability | Poor (cold flame issues) | Excellent | H₂ engines need heated intake/lean cold strategy |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hydrogen Engine Functional Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H2Tank[H2 Storage Tank 700 bar or LH2] --> Reg[Pressure Regulator]
    Reg --> Inj[High Pressure Injector or Port Injector]
    Inj --> Intake[Intake Manifold with Anti Backfire Valve]
    Intake --> Cyl[Combustion Chamber Cylinder]
    Cyl --> Spark[Spark Plug High Energy Ignition]
    Cyl --> Piston[Piston and Connecting Rod]
    Piston --> Crank[Crankshaft and Flywheel]
    Cyl --> Exh[Exhaust Manifold]
    Exh --> EGR[Optional EGR Loop for NOx Control]
    Exh --> Cat[Three Way Catalyst or Lean NOx Trap]
    Cat --> Tail[Tailpipe H2O and Trace NOx]
    ECU[Engine Control Unit ECU] -.->|PW| Inj
    ECU -.->|Spark| Spark
    ECU -.->|Lambda| Inj
    Air[Atmospheric Air] --> Intake
```

## 4.2 Hydrogen Combustion and Pollutant Formation Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[H2 Molecule] --> B[Diffuses 12x faster than gasoline vapor]
    B --> C[Mixes with Air O2 and N2]
    C --> D{Equivalence Ratio}
    D -->|phi less than 0.5 lean| E[Low peak temperature 1800 K]
    D -->|phi equals 1 stoich| F[High peak temperature 2400 K]
    D -->|phi greater than 1 rich| G[Incomplete reaction]
    E --> H[Low NOx]
    F --> I[High NOx via Zeldovich]
    G --> J[Some H2 survives unburned]
    I --> K[Tailpipe NOx]
    H --> K
    J --> K
    C --> L[H2O Formation only desired product]
    L --> M[Tailpipe H2O vapor]
```

## 4.3 Hydrogen Fueling Strategy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Hydrogen Engine Type] --> Q1{Target Application}
    Q1 -->|Passenger car| S1[Port Injection PFI at 5 to 15 bar]
    Q1 -->|Heavy duty truck| S2[Direct Injection DI at 30 to 50 bar]
    Q1 -->|Racing and Le Mans| S3[Direct Injection with Pre chamber]
    S1 --> P1[Pro: Clean manifold]
    S1 --> P2[Con: Backfire risk at low load]
    S2 --> P3[Pro: Suppresses backfire]
    S2 --> P4[Con: Tank valve cryogenic]
    S3 --> P5[Pro: Very high power density]
    S3 --> P6[Con: Costly injection hardware]
    P1 --> Done[Operating Point]
    P2 --> Done
    P3 --> Done
    P4 --> Done
    P5 --> Done
    P6 --> Done
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Decoding for Examiners:** When the KTU paper asks for a "schematic of hydrogen injection system," draw (a) the tank → regulator → injector → cylinder path and (b) the location of the anti-backfire valve near the throttle body.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three advantages and two disadvantages of using hydrogen as a fuel in IC engines.**

**Model Answer:**

*Advantages:*
1. **Highest mass energy density** (120 MJ/kg) — about 2.7 times gasoline, giving high thermal efficiency per kg of fuel.
2. **Zero carbon emissions** at tailpipe; the only combustion product is water vapour.
3. **Wide flammability limits** (φ = 0.1 to 7.1) — enables stable ultra-lean operation which improves part-load efficiency.

*Disadvantages:*
1. **Storage difficulty:** Very low density demands either 700 bar compression or cryogenic liquefaction at –253 °C.
2. **Pre-ignition and backfire** in port-injected engines due to low ignition energy (0.02 mJ) and high flame speed.

*(3 marks: 1 mark each for advantages, 1 mark for any disadvantage acceptable)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for hydrogen (34.3:1) much higher than for gasoline (14.7:1)?**

**Model Answer:**

Hydrogen is a non-carbon fuel. The combustion reaction $2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ requires 8 kg of O₂ per kg of H₂, whereas gasoline (CHₓ) requires only ~3.4 kg of O₂ per kg of fuel due to its higher carbon-to-hydrogen ratio and molecular mass. Since air contains only 23.2 % O₂ by mass, the air needed per kg of fuel scales with the O₂ demand. Hydrogen's larger O₂ requirement therefore translates to a much higher stoichiometric air-to-fuel ratio (34.3:1). This means hydrogen engines carry a large excess of air (and nitrogen) for the same energy release, leading to high volumetric flow rates and lower flame temperatures in lean operation.

*(3 marks: 1 mark reaction, 1 mark O₂ demand, 1 mark conclusion on A/F ratio)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — INTERNAL CHOICE

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the various **methods of hydrogen fuel delivery** to a spark-ignition engine. Compare port injection and direct injection in terms of backfire susceptibility and power output. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydrogen-fuelled 4-stroke SI engine delivers **75 kW** brake power at **3000 rpm**. The fuel consumption is measured as **2.4 kg/h** and the lower heating value of H₂ is 120 MJ/kg. Calculate: (i) BSFC, (ii) BSEC, (iii) Brake thermal efficiency. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

The three principal hydrogen delivery strategies are:

1. **Carburetion / Continuous Mixing:** A simple venturi or impeller-type carburettor draws H₂ into the intake manifold. *Pros:* Simple, cheap. *Cons:* Hard to control at low loads; causes backfire due to manifold H₂ residue.

2. **Port Fuel Injection (PFI):** H₂ is injected upstream of the intake valve at low pressure (3–15 bar) via a solenoid injector timed by the ECU. This is the most common retrofit on gasoline engines. *Pros:* Good transient response, lean capability. *Cons:* Backfire risk remains because residual H₂ in the port can ignite on hot valve surfaces.

3. **Direct Injection (DI):** H₂ is injected directly into the combustion chamber at 30–50 bar during the compression stroke. *Pros:* Eliminates backfire (no H₂ in manifold), enables higher compression ratio, better power output. *Cons:* Cryogenic tank valves needed, injector lubrication issues, higher cost.

**Comparison:**

| Criterion | Port Injection | Direct Injection |
|---|---|---|
| Backfire | High risk | Negligible |
| Power output | Slightly lower | 5–10 % higher |
| Cold start | Easier | Harder |
| Cost | Low | High |

*(7 marks: 2 marks schematic description, 3 marks methods, 2 marks comparison)*

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

**Given:**
- $P_b = 75\,\text{kW}$
- $\dot{m}_f = 2.4\,\text{kg/h} = 2.4/3600 = 6.667 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{kg/s}$
- $\text{LHV} = 120\,\text{MJ/kg} = 1.2 \times 10^{8}\,\text{J/kg}$

**(i) Brake Specific Fuel Consumption:**

$$\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b} = \dfrac{2.4\,\text{kg/h}}{75\,\text{kW}} = 0.032\,\text{kg/kWh}$$

**(ii) Brake Specific Energy Consumption:**

$$\text{BSEC} = \text{BSFC} \times \text{LHV} = 0.032 \times 120 = 3.84\,\text{MJ/kWh}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\text{BSEC} = \dfrac{1}{\eta_{\text{th}}} = \dfrac{P_b \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \times \text{LHV}}$$

**(iii) Brake Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{th,b}} = \dfrac{P_b \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \times \text{LHV}} = \dfrac{75 \times 3600}{2.4 \times 120 \times 10^6/1000}$$

$$\eta_{\text{th,b}} = \dfrac{75}{2.4 \times 33.33} = \dfrac{75}{80} = 0.375 = \mathbf{37.5\,\%}$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks each for parts (i) and (ii), 3 marks for part (iii) with full substitution)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a)** Discuss the **combustion characteristics of hydrogen** in a spark-ignition engine, highlighting the role of flame speed, ignition energy, and flammability limits. How do these differ from gasoline? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydrogen engine runs on a **lean mixture** with excess air ratio λ = 2.5. Calculate: (i) the actual A/F ratio, (ii) the equivalence ratio φ, (iii) the composition of the combustion products per kg of fuel, (iv) the percentage of O₂ in the exhaust. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**1. Flame Speed:**
Hydrogen has a laminar flame speed in air of **2.7–3.2 m/s**, roughly 7–8 times that of gasoline (≈ 0.4 m/s). This yields a faster, more complete burn, with shorter combustion duration (typically 30–40 crank-angle degrees versus 50–60° for gasoline). High flame speed also propagates flame kernels even in extremely lean mixtures, enabling stable operation down to φ = 0.1.

**2. Ignition Energy:**
The minimum ignition energy of hydrogen is **0.02 mJ**, an order of magnitude lower than gasoline (0.25 mJ). Hot residual gases, glowing deposits, or hot valve surfaces can therefore ignite the air–H₂ mixture during the induction stroke — this is the root cause of **backfire** (combustion in the intake manifold) and **pre-ignition** (combustion before spark timing). Mitigation: water injection, cooled EGR, anti-backfire valves, or direct injection.

**3. Flammability Limits:**
Hydrogen burns over an extremely wide range: **φ = 0.1 to 7.1** (vs. φ = 0.4 to 1.4 for gasoline). This permits ultra-lean operation that suppresses NOₓ and improves efficiency. On the rich side, hydrogen keeps burning even when there is too little air — useful for exhaust-gas energy recovery systems.

**4. Quench Distance & Diffusivity:**
The quenching distance of H₂ is only 0.6 mm (vs 2 mm for gasoline), so flames can survive closer to cold walls — improving cycle-to-cycle consistency. High diffusivity (0.61 cm²/s vs 0.05 cm²/s) makes air–fuel mixing very fast.

**Comparison Summary:**

| Property | H₂ | Gasoline |
|---|---|---|
| Laminar flame speed | 2.7–3.2 m/s | 0.4 m/s |
| Min. ignition energy | 0.02 mJ | 0.25 mJ |
| Flammability range | φ = 0.1–7.1 | φ = 0.4–1.4 |
| Quench distance | 0.6 mm | 2.0 mm |

*(7 marks: 1.5 marks each for the four characteristics + 1 mark for the comparison table)*

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

**Given:** λ = 2.5, LHV = 120 MJ/kg, A/F_stoich = 34.3

**(i) Actual A/F ratio:**
$$\left(\text{A/F}\right)_{\text{actual}} = \lambda \times (\text{A/F})_{\text{stoich}} = 2.5 \times 34.3 = \mathbf{85.75}$$

**(ii) Equivalence ratio:**
$$\phi = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1}{2.5} = \mathbf{0.4}$$

**(iii) Combustion products per kg of H₂:**

For 1 kg of H₂:
- O₂ needed stoich = 8 kg
- Actual O₂ supplied = 8 × 2.5 = 20 kg
- N₂ supplied = 0.768 × 20 = 15.36 kg
- H₂O produced = 9 kg (since 1 kg H₂ + 8 kg O₂ → 9 kg H₂O)
- Excess O₂ in products = 20 − 8 = 12 kg

**Total combustion products:**

$$\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{O}_2 + \text{N}_2 = 9 + 12 + 15.36 = 36.36\,\text{kg/kg fuel}$$

**(iv) Percentage of O₂ in exhaust (dry basis):**

Wet exhaust mass = 36.36 kg; wet includes 9 kg H₂O.
Dry exhaust mass = 36.36 − 9 = 27.36 kg.
Dry O₂ = 12 kg.

$$\text{O}_2\,(\text{dry \%)} = \dfrac{12}{27.36} \times 100 = \mathbf{43.86\,\%}$$

*(7 marks: 1.5 marks each for (i), (ii), 3 marks for (iii), 1 mark for (iv))*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — fuel consumption in kg/h must be divided by 3600 for thermal efficiency, or kept in kg/h when paired with kW to get BSFC directly. Mixing units is the single biggest error.
> 2. **Confusing BSFC with BSEC.** BSFC is in kg/kWh (fuel mass), BSEC is in MJ/kWh (energy). H₂ has a *low* BSFC but a *moderate* BSEC.
> 3. **Reporting wet vs dry exhaust** without clarification. Always state the basis when giving O₂ %.
> 4. **Missing the backfire discussion** in delivery-system answers — KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for the anti-backfire valve concept.
> 5. **Stoichiometric A/F derivation** must show the steps: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O → moles → mass → divide by 0.232. Skipping the air-O₂ mass fraction loses a full mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Stoichiometric A/F for H₂ = 34.3 : 1** (memorise the derivation: 8 kg O₂ per kg H₂, divided by 0.232).
- **LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg** — about **2.7×** that of gasoline (44 MJ/kg).
- **Auto-ignition temperature** = 858 K; **Minimum ignition energy** = 0.02 mJ (low → backfire risk).
- **Flame speed** = 2.7–3.2 m/s (high → enables ultra-lean operation).
- **Flammability range** = φ = 0.1 to 7.1 (extremely wide).
- **Lean burn** is the standard hydrogen strategy: λ = 1.5 to 3.0; suppresses NOₓ and improves efficiency.
- **Three delivery methods:** Carburetion, Port Fuel Injection (PFI), Direct Injection (DI). PFI is most common; DI eliminates backfire.
- **Storage options:** Compressed gas (350/700 bar), Liquid H₂ (–253 °C), Metal hydrides, LOHC.
- **NOₓ control:** Lean burn + EGR + water injection + three-way catalyst / lean NOₓ trap.
- **Zero CO₂ at tailpipe**, but **upstream CO₂** depends on H₂ production route (green H₂ ≈ zero, grey H₂ ≈ 9 kg CO₂/kg H₂).
- **Volume penalty** at 700 bar is ~7× gasoline; at liquid H₂ it's ~4×.
- **Key formulas to commit:** $\phi = (\text{A/F})_{\text{stoich}}/(\text{A/F})_{\text{actual}}$, $\text{BSFC} = \dot{m}_f/P_b$, $\eta_b = P_b \cdot 3600/(\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV})$.
- **KTU favourite questions:** (1) A/F derivation, (2) BSFC/η calculation, (3) combustion characteristics table, (4) backfire mechanisms, (5) delivery system schematic.
- **Don't confuse:** Hydrogen *engine* (combustion) vs Hydrogen *fuel cell* (electrochemical) — both use H₂ but are different powertrains.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flex Fuel Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT612 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flex Fuel Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flex Fuel Vehicle (FFV):** A Flex Fuel Vehicle is an automotive vehicle equipped with an internal combustion engine (typically a spark-ignition petrol engine) that has been specifically engineered and calibrated to operate seamlessly on more than one fuel mixture, ranging conventionally from **100% petrol (E0)** to **85% ethanol + 15% petrol (E85)**, and in advanced systems up to **E100 (pure ethanol)** or **M85 (85% methanol + 15% petrol)**.
