# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]
**"Explain the formation mechanism of NOx in a spark-ignition engine and list two in-cylinder control strategies."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
NOx in SI engines forms primarily through the **thermal (Zeldovich) mechanism** at peak in-cylinder temperatures above **1800 K**. The three principal Zeldovich reactions are:
1. **O + N₂ → NO + N** (rate-limiting step)
2. **N + O₂ → NO + O**
3. **N + OH → NO + H**

The dominant variable is **peak flame temperature**, which depends on equivalence ratio, compression ratio, EGR rate, and ignition timing. NOx formation is maximum at slightly lean-of-stoichiometric mixtures (λ ≈ 1.05) where the combination of high temperature and sufficient O₂ is favorable.

**Two in-cylinder control strategies:**
- **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR):** Introducing 5–20% inert exhaust gas lowers flame temperature by 50–100 K.
- **Retarded Spark Timing or Miller Cycle:** Reducing effective compression work lowers peak temperature.

> **Distribution (KTU):** Mechanism explanation — 2 marks; Two strategies — 1 mark.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"What is the function of a Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)? Why is closed-loop lambda control essential for its operation?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)** simultaneously oxidizes **CO and HC** to CO₂ and H₂O, and reduces **NOx** to N₂, using platinum-palladium-rhodium washcoat on a ceramic honeycomb substrate.

**Why closed-loop lambda control is essential:**
The TWC achieves > 95% conversion efficiency for all three pollutants **simultaneously only when λ is held within 1 ± 0.01**. Outside this narrow window:
- At λ > 1 (lean): NOx reduction fails (insufficient reductants).
- At λ < 1 (rich): CO/HC oxidation fails (insufficient O₂).

A **wide-band O₂ sensor (UEGO)** in the exhaust manifold continuously reports the actual λ, and the ECU adjusts injector pulse width to maintain stoichiometry. The **lambda control bandwidth is typically ±0.005** at a closed-loop frequency of **1–10 Hz**.

> **Distribution:** TWC function — 1.5 marks; Lambda explanation — 1.5 marks.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the construction and working of an **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR) system** in a modern multi-cylinder diesel engine. Discuss its impact on NOx formation and the trade-off with particulate matter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A passenger car of mass **1200 kg** is travelling at a constant speed of **90 km/h** on a level road. The road load consists of: rolling resistance coefficient **C_rr = 0.013**, aerodynamic drag coefficient **C_d = 0.32**, and frontal area **A = 2.2 m²**. Air density **ρ = 1.184 kg/m³**, **g = 9.81 m/s²**. Calculate:
  (i) The tractive force required to overcome road resistance.
  (ii) The road power at 90 km/h.
  (iii) If the engine delivers **70 kW** at this speed, compute BSFC given fuel consumption is **17 kg/h** with CV = 43 MJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — **7 marks**

**Construction (2 marks):**
A typical **cooled EGR** system comprises:
- **EGR valve** (electrically or vacuum actuated) located on the exhaust manifold.
- **EGR cooler** (gas-liquid heat exchanger) lowering EGR temperature from ~600°C to ~150–200°C.
- **EGR pipe** routing exhaust from exhaust manifold to intake manifold.
- **Δp sensor** (differential pressure) for closed-loop EGR rate control.
- **EGR cooler bypass valve** for warm-up.

**Working (2 marks):**
At part-load conditions, the ECU opens the EGR valve to admit a controlled fraction (5–25%) of exhaust gas into the intake. The recirculated gas dilutes the fresh charge, increasing the specific heat capacity and reducing the partial pressure of O₂. This lowers peak flame temperature and hence thermal NOx (Zeldovich mechanism).

**Impact on NOx and PM trade-off (3 marks):**
- NOx reduction: **30–60%** at 15–25% EGR.
- However, EGR reduces combustion temperature and rate, leading to **incomplete soot oxidation** → **PM rises by 20–80%** at high EGR rates.
- A typical diesel calibration **limits EGR to 25–30%** at part load to balance NOx and PM; high EGR with **common-rail high-pressure injection** mitigates PM.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — **7 marks**

**Given:**
- m = 1200 kg, V = 90 km/h = 25 m/s
- C_rr = 0.013, C_d = 0.32, A = 2.2 m²
- ρ = 1.184 kg/m³, θ = 0 (level road), a = 0 (constant speed)

**(i) Tractive Force (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Aerodynamic Drag:**

$$ F_{drag} = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.184 \times 0.32 \times 2.2 \times (25)^2 $$

**Step 2 — Compute the product:**

$$ F_{drag} = 0.5 \times 1.184 \times 0.32 \times 2.2 \times 625 $$

**Step 3 — Final evaluation:**

$$ F_{drag} = 0.5 \times 1.184 \times 0.704 \times 625 = 260.4 \text{ N} $$

**Step 4 — Rolling Resistance:**

$$ F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g = 0.013 \times 1200 \times 9.81 = 153.04 \text{ N} $$

**Step 5 — Total Tractive Force (level, no acceleration, no gradient):**

$$ F_{tractive} = F_{drag} + F_{rr} = 260.4 + 153.04 = 413.4 \text{ N} $$

**(ii) Road Power (2 marks):**

$$ P_{road} = F_{tractive} \times V = 413.4 \times 25 = 10335 \text{ W} \approx 10.34 \text{ kW} $$

**(iii) BSFC (2 marks):**

$$ \eta_{br} = \frac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \times CV} = \frac{70}{17/3600 \times 43000} = \frac{70}{203.06} = 0.3447 \approx 34.47\% $$

$$ BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} = \frac{17}{70} = 0.2429 \text{ kg/kWh} = 242.9 \text{ g/kWh} $$

> **Distribution:** F_drag — 1 mark; F_rr — 1 mark; Sum — 1 mark; P_road — 2 marks; BSFC — 2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF)** and a **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** system. How do they enable compliance with BS-VI emission norms for heavy-duty diesel vehicles? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A turbocharged diesel engine has a brake thermal efficiency of **38%** and mechanical efficiency of **88%**. The fuel consumption is **12 kg/h** with a calorific value of **42 MJ/kg**. Calculate:
  (i) Brake Power output.
  (ii) Indicated Power and Frictional Power.
  (iii) Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — **7 marks**

**DPF (3.5 marks):**
- **Construction:** A wall-flow ceramic (cordierite or silicon carbide) honeycomb with alternating plugged channels. Exhaust gas is forced through the porous wall, trapping **PM (soot)** in the substrate.
- **Filtration efficiency:** > 99% for particles above 100 nm.
- **Regeneration:** Periodically, accumulated soot is oxidized at temperatures above **600°C** via:
  - **Passive regeneration:** Continuous oxidation using NO₂ from the DOC as oxidant.
  - **Active regeneration:** Post-injection of fuel to raise exhaust temperature to **~600–650°C**.

**SCR (3.5 marks):**
- **Construction:** Ceramic substrate coated with copper-zeolite or vanadium-tungsten-titanium catalyst. Upstream **DEF (AdBlue) injector** sprays 32.5% urea solution. The urea decomposes to **NH₃** (ammonia), which is the reducing agent.
- **Working:** At 200–500°C, NH₃ + NO/NO₂ → N₂ + H₂O on the catalyst.
- **Reduction efficiency:** 80–95% for NOx.
- **BS-VI compliance:** DPF + SCR + DOC + EGR + ASC together enable heavy-duty diesel vehicles to meet **BS-VI NOx = 460 mg/kWh** and **PM = 10 mg/kWh** (WHTC cycle).

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — **7 marks**

**Given:** η_br = 0.38, η_mech = 0.88, ṁ_f = 12 kg/h, CV = 42 MJ/kg

**(i) Brake Power (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Heat input rate:**

$$ \dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \times CV = \frac{12}{3600} \times 42 \times 10^6 = 140000 \text{ W} = 140 \text{ kW} $$

**Step 2 — Apply brake thermal efficiency:**

$$ BP = \eta_{br} \times \dot{Q}_{in} = 0.38 \times 140 = 53.2 \text{ kW} $$

**(ii) Indicated Power and Frictional Power (2 marks):**

**Step 1 — Indicated Power from mechanical efficiency:**

$$ IP = \frac{BP}{\eta_{mech}} = \frac{53.2}{0.88} = 60.45 \text{ kW} $$

**Step 2 — Frictional Power:**

$$ FP = IP - BP = 60.45 - 53.2 = 7.25 \text{ kW} $$

**(iii) BSFC (2 marks):**

$$ BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} = \frac{12 \text{ kg/h}}{53.2 \text{ kW}} = 0.2256 \text{ kg/kWh} = 225.6 \text{ g/kWh} $$

> **Distribution:** BP — 3 marks; IP + FP — 2 marks; BSFC — 2 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Forgetting to convert units:** km/h → m/s (÷ 3.6), kg/h → kg/s (÷ 3600), kJ/kg → J/kg (× 1000). This single oversight can cost **2–3 marks** silently.
> - **Ignoring the sign of cos θ / sin θ:** At level road, cos 0 = 1 and sin 0 = 0. Don't accidentally include hill-climbing force when the road is level.
> - **Confusing BSFC with BSNOx notation:** BSFC is in **g/kWh** of fuel; BSNOx is in **g/kWh** of NOx. Both have the *same denominator* (BP) but different numerators.
> - **Mixing λ and equivalence ratio (ϕ):** ϕ = 1/λ. KTU answers are marked strictly on terminology.
> - **Skipping the EGR trade-off explanation:** Saying "EGR reduces NOx" alone is *incomplete* (1 mark). You must mention the **PM increase** and the **EGR limit** to earn full marks.
> - **Stating 'Euro 6' when the question is from an Indian exam:** Use **BS-VI** (Bharat Stage VI) terminology unless the question explicitly references European regulations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Stop Revision — Memorize These for KTU ESE**

- **Four regulated pollutants:** CO, HC, NOx, PM. Each has a distinct formation mechanism and a distinct control technology.
- **λ = 1 ± 0.01** is the TWC sweet spot; the **wide-band O₂ sensor** holds it.
- **Zeldovich mechanism** → **thermal NOx** → **temperature-sensitive** → **EGR is the cheapest in-cylinder fix**.
- **EGR lowers NOx by 30–60%** at 15–25% EGR; trade-off is **PM increase**.
- **DOC + DPF + SCR + ASC** = the modern diesel after-treatment chain for **BS-VI compliance**.
- **TWC = 3-way** (CO, HC, NOx); **DOC = oxidation only**; **SCR = reduction only**; **DPF = mechanical filtration**.
- **HCCI/PCCI/RCCI** are advanced low-temperature combustion modes that intrinsically reduce both **NOx and PM**, but face **combustion control** challenges.
- **BSFC** is the universal fuel-economy metric. **Lower BSFC = better economy.** Typical: **diesel ~ 200–220 g/kWh, gasoline ~ 240–280 g/kWh.**
- **Volumetric efficiency (η_v)** governs mass air flow, hence power. **Turbocharging and VVT** raise η_v above 100%.
- **Aero power ∝ V³**, **rolling power ∝ V**, **climbing power ∝ V** — **aerodynamic drag dominates above ~80 km/h**.
- **Every 0.01 reduction in C_d saves ~0.5–1.0% fuel** at highway speeds.
- **Power-to-weight ratio** (kW/kg or bhp/tonne) is the single most quoted "performance" metric.
- **OBD-II is mandatory under BS-VI** — it monitors catalyst efficiency, O₂ sensor health, EGR flow, DPF pressure drop, and SCR NOx conversion.
- **The three-way trade-off** of Power, Emissions, and Fuel Economy is *the* central theme of this topic.
- **EGR % = ṁ_EGR / (ṁ_air + ṁ_EGR) × 100** — denominator is *total* intake, not just EGR.
- **DEF/AdBlue** is 32.5% aqueous urea; dosing rate is **~3–5% of fuel mass flow**.
- **Hybrid powertrains** decouple the engine from the road load, allowing the engine to operate in its **BSFC sweet spot** (lowest fuel consumption region) — best practical solution to the trade-off.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Closed Loop and Open Loop Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT612 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Closed Loop and Open Loop Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Open Loop Operation** is a control strategy in which the Engine Control Unit (ECU) determines the air-fuel mixture and ignition timing based entirely on **pre-programmed calibration maps (lookup tables)** and inputs from upstream sensors (MAF, MAP, TPS, IAT, ECT, CKP), **without** using feedback from the downstream exhaust gas oxygen (O₂) sensor. The ECU "openly" commands an output without verifying the actual result.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Closed Loop Operation** is a control strategy in which the ECU continuously monitors the **post-combustion exhaust oxygen content** through a Lambda (O₂) sensor placed upstream of the catalytic converter, compares it with the **stoichiometric target (λ = 1)**, and applies **real-time corrective adjustments** to the injector pulse width to keep the air-fuel ratio oscillating tightly around the chemically ideal ratio.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like cooking a curry:**
> - **Open Loop** = You throw in spices based on a written recipe (no taste-testing). If the gas stove is too hot or the potato is sour, your dish goes bad — but you never check the final taste.
> - **Closed Loop** = You taste the curry with a spoon as you cook. If it's too salty, you add water; if it's bland, you add salt. You keep **sampling the output** and **correcting the input** in real time.
>
> The car's ECU does exactly this — only instead of a spoon, it uses an **oxygen sensor** in the exhaust pipe.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (AFRₛ) for gasoline**: **14.7 : 1**
- **Stoichiometric AFR for diesel**: **14.5 : 1** (diesel is always lean; no throttle, no closed-loop AFR control)
- **Stoichiometric AFR for CNG**: **17.2 : 1**
- **Stoichiometric AFR for LPG**: **15.5 : 1**
- **Lambda (λ) = 1.000** = chemically perfect combustion
- **Narrow-band O₂ sensor voltage swing**: **~0.1 V (lean)** ↔ **~0.9 V (rich)** with an instantaneous switching point at **~0.45 V (λ = 1)**
- **Closed-loop bandwidth (typical)**: **±1 %** around λ = 1
- **Three-way catalytic converter (TWC) efficiency window**: **0.99 ≤ λ ≤ 1.01**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Open Loop Operation — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Sensor Read-In (Upstream):** The ECU reads MAF/MAP (air mass), IAT (intake air temperature), ECT (coolant temperature), CKP (engine speed), and TPS (throttle position).
2. **Map Lookup:** Based on RPM and engine load, the ECU fetches a **base pulse width (Tᵦ)** from a pre-calibrated 3D fuel map stored in ROM.
3. **Multiplicative Corrections:** Apply correction factors: cold-start enrichment, warm-up enrichment, altitude (barometric) compensation, transient (acceleration) enrichment, and battery voltage compensation.
4. **Command Output:** The ECU fires the injectors with the computed pulse width **without ever checking the exhaust**.
5. **Why "Open"?** The loop is "open" because there is no feedback path returning information from the output (exhaust) to the input (injector command).

> [!NOTE]
> **When is Open Loop used?**
> - **Cold start / Cold engine** (coolant < ~40 °C) — O₂ sensor is not yet hot enough to give a reliable signal (cold sensor reads near 0.45 V regardless of AFR).
> - **Wide Open Throttle (WOT)** — engine needs maximum power, runs slightly rich (λ ≈ 0.85–0.90) for charge cooling; closed loop would over-lean it.
> - **Deceleration Fuel Cut-Off (DFCO)** — ECU cuts fuel entirely; no AFR to control.
> - **O₂ sensor failure / Heated O₂ sensor warm-up phase**.
> - **Altitude correction at very low barometric pressures**.

### 2.2 Closed Loop Operation — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Sensor Read-In (Upstream + Downstream):** Same upstream sensors as open loop, **plus** the **Heated Oxygen Sensor (HO2S)** in the exhaust manifold.
2. **Lambda Calculation:** ECU reads HO2S voltage → determines whether exhaust is lean or rich.
3. **Error Generation:** Compares actual λ with target (λ = 1.000) → generates an error signal.
4. **PID Correction:** A **Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller** in the ECU calculates a correction factor.
5. **Injector Pulse Width Update:** New pulse width = Tᵦ × (1 + correction).
6. **Oscillation Pattern:** The narrow-band O₂ sensor naturally swings rich-lean-rich, causing λ to oscillate between **0.99 and 1.01** at a frequency of **~0.5–2 Hz**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does Closed Loop require a Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)?**
> The TWC simultaneously oxidizes CO and HC (using remaining O₂) and reduces NOₓ (using unburned HC/CO) **only when the exhaust gas chemistry oscillates around λ = 1**. Open-loop steady fueling cannot satisfy the TWC's narrow efficiency window, so modern emission norms (BS-VI / Euro 6) mandate closed-loop control.

### 2.3 The Lambda Concept (Normalized Air-Fuel Ratio)

Lambda normalizes the actual air-fuel ratio against the chemically ideal ratio, allowing engine calibration across fuels:

$$\lambda = \frac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoichiometric}}$$

$$\phi = \frac{1}{\lambda} \quad \text{(Equivalence Ratio)}$$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Application of Closed/Open Loop Concept |
|---|---|
| **Emission Control** | TWC efficiency; meeting BS-VI / Euro 6 NOx & HC limits |
| **Fuel Economy** | Closed-loop cruise gives best economy; open-loop WOT sacrifices economy for power |
| **Cold-Start Emissions** | Open loop with rich mixture = high HC; requires fast-light-off catalyst |
| **Diagnostics (OBD-II)** | ECU monitors HO2S voltage; stuck-rich/stuck-lean sensor = P0131/P0132 DTC |
| **Alternative Fuels** | Lambda sensor is fuel-agnostic; same sensor works for petrol, CNG, LPG |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities below are exam-ready. Memorize the bold constants; they are asked repeatedly in KTU papers.

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Typical Value / Unit | KTU Memory Trick |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\lambda$ | $AFR_{actual} \,/\, AFR_{stoich}$ | dimensionless | Lambda = 1 means perfect chemistry |
| $\phi$ | $1 \,/\, \lambda$ | dimensionless | Equivalence ratio; rich = $\phi > 1$ |
| $T_{inj}$ | $T_b \cdot (1 + C_{corr})$ | milliseconds (ms) | Injector pulse width |
| $C_{corr}$ | $K_P e(t) + K_I \int e(t)\,dt + K_D \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ | unitless | PID correction factor |
| $V_{HO2S}$ | Sensor output voltage | **0.1 V to 0.9 V** | Switches at **0.45 V** (λ=1) |
| $AFR_{s,\,petrol}$ | Stoichiometric AFR | **14.7 : 1** | Fixed KTU exam value |
| $AFR_{s,\,diesel}$ | Stoichiometric AFR | **14.5 : 1** | Diesel never runs closed loop |
| $\eta_{comb}$ | Combustion efficiency | $\approx 95\text{–}98\,\%$ | Higher when $\lambda \approx 1$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Lambda from Air-Fuel Ratio (Foundation Derivation)

**Given:** An engine is supplied with $m_a = 14.0$ kg of air and $m_f = 1.0$ kg of gasoline (a slightly lean mixture).

**Step 1 — Write the actual AFR.**

$$AFR_{actual} = \frac{m_a}{m_f} = \frac{14.0}{1.0} = 14.0$$

**Step 2 — Identify the stoichiometric AFR for gasoline** (a known standard constant).

$$AFR_{stoich,\,petrol} = 14.7$$

**Step 3 — Apply the definition of Lambda** (ratio of actual to stoichiometric).

$$\lambda = \frac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}} = \frac{14.0}{14.7}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate numerically.**

$$\lambda = 0.9524$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

$$\lambda = 0.9524 < 1.000 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Mixture is RICH (excess fuel)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** A lambda value of 0.95 means the engine is running **5 % rich** — typical of WOT power enrichment or a faulty MAF sensor under-reporting air.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Equivalence Ratio from Lambda

**Given:** $\lambda = 1.05$ (a lean cruise condition).

**Step 1 — Write the definition of the equivalence ratio.**

$$\phi = \frac{FAR_{actual}}{FAR_{stoich}} = \frac{1}{AFR_{actual} / AFR_{stoich}} = \frac{AFR_{stoich}}{AFR_{actual}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the lambda identity** ($AFR_{actual} = \lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich}$).

$$\phi = \frac{AFR_{stoich}}{\lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich}} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$\phi = \frac{1}{1.05} = 0.9524$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.**

$$\phi < 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Lean burn condition (excess air)}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Injector Pulse Width in Closed Loop

**Given:** A four-cylinder port-injection gasoline engine with the following data:
- Base pulse width from fuel map: $T_b = 2.50$ ms
- Coolant temp correction: $C_{ct} = +0.06$ (6 % warm-up enrichment)
- Battery voltage correction: $C_{bat} = -0.02$
- PID closed-loop correction: $C_{PID} = -0.04$ (sensor reads slightly rich)

**Step 1 — Sum all correction factors.**

$$C_{corr} = C_{ct} + C_{bat} + C_{PID}$$

$$C_{corr} = 0.06 + (-0.02) + (-0.04) = 0.00$$

**Step 2 — Apply the total correction to the base pulse width.**

$$T_{inj} = T_b \cdot (1 + C_{corr})$$

$$T_{inj} = 2.50 \cdot (1 + 0.00)$$

$$T_{inj} = 2.50 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Compute per-cylinder fuel mass** (if injector flow $q = 220$ cc/min at 3 bar).

$$q_{ms} = \frac{220 \text{ cc/min}}{60{,}000 \text{ ms/min}} = 3.667 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cc/ms}$$

$$V_{inj} = q_{ms} \cdot T_{inj} = 3.667 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 2.50 = 9.167 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cc per cylinder per cycle}$$

**Step 4 — Compute fuel mass per cylinder** (gasoline density $\rho_f = 0.745$ g/cc).

$$m_f = V_{inj} \cdot \rho_f = 9.167 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.745 = 6.83 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Insight:** The base pulse width $T_b$ comes from the **fuel map** (RPM × Load). The closed-loop PID only *nudges* this base value — it does not replace it. KTU questions often give $T_b$ and ask for the *corrected* $T_{inj}$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Open Loop vs Closed Loop

| Parameter | Open Loop | Closed Loop |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedback source** | None (open) | Upstream HO2S (closed) |
| **Air-fuel accuracy** | ±10 % typical | **±1 % around λ = 1** |
| **Emissions** | High HC, CO, NOx (off-window) | Lowest achievable |
| **Fuel economy** | Sub-optimal | Optimal at steady cruise |
| **Engine operating regime** | Cold start, WOT, DFCO, sensor failure | Idle, part-throttle cruise, light load |
| **Catalyst light-off** | Required before closed loop | Only after catalyst is hot |
| **Computational load on ECU** | Low (map lookup only) | High (real-time PID at ~10 Hz) |
| **Robustness to sensor failure** | High (degrades gracefully) | Low (HO2S failure → reverts to open loop) |
| **OBD-II fault detection** | Limited | Full (HO2S voltage, response rate, dwell time) |
| **KTU exam weightage** | 4–6 marks | 8–10 marks |

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: Lambda to AFR (Kerala Board Style)

**Question (KTU Pattern):** A CNG-powered engine operates at $\lambda = 0.98$. If the stoichiometric AFR of CNG is 17.2, calculate the actual air-fuel ratio and determine whether the mixture is rich or lean.

**Solution:**

$$AFR_{actual} = \lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich}$$

$$AFR_{actual} = 0.98 \times 17.2$$

$$AFR_{actual} = 16.856 \,\, : \,\, 1$$

**Conclusion:** Since $\lambda = 0.98 < 1$, the mixture is **RICH** (2 % excess fuel).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Open Loop Control Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    driverA[Driver Throttle Input] --> ecmA[ECU / ECM]
    mafA[MAF MAP Sensor] --> ecmA
    ectA[Coolant Temp Sensor] --> ecmA
    iatA[Intake Air Temp Sensor] --> ecmA
    ckpA[Crankshaft Position Sensor] --> ecmA
    ecmA -->|Base Pulse Width T sub b| injA[Fuel Injector]
    injA --> engA[Engine Combustion Chamber]
    engA --> exhA[Exhaust Manifold]
    exhA --> catA[Catalytic Converter]
    catA --> tailA[Tailpipe]
    style ecmA fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    style injA fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    style exhA fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Note that the exhaust path simply *dumps* gases out. There is **no arrow returning** from the exhaust to the ECU. The loop is **structurally open**.

### 4.2 Closed Loop Control Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    driverB[Driver Throttle Input] --> ecmB[ECU / ECM]
    mafB[MAF MAP Sensor] --> ecmB
    ectB[Coolant Temp Sensor] --> ecmB
    iatB[Intake Air Temp Sensor] --> ecmB
    ckpB[Crankshaft Position Sensor] --> ecmB
    ecmB -->|Pulse Width T sub inj| injB[Fuel Injector]
    injB --> engB[Engine Combustion Chamber]
    engB --> exhB[Exhaust Manifold]
    exhB --> ho2s[Heated O2 Sensor Lambda Probe]
    ho2s -->|Voltage 0.1V to 0.9V| ecmB
    exhB --> catB[Three Way Catalytic Converter]
    catB --> tailB[Tailpipe]
    style ecmB fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style injB fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    style ho2s fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    style catB fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** The arrow from the **HO2S (Lambda probe)** back to the **ECU** is the *feedback loop*. This single arrow is the entire conceptual difference between open-loop and closed-loop operation. The ECU becomes a **self-correcting controller** instead of a one-shot mapper.

### 4.3 Operating Mode Decision Flowchart (When Does ECU Switch Modes?)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB[Engine Cranking] --> check1{ECT greater than 40 C?}
    check1 -- No --> openA[OPEN LOOP Cold Start Enrichment Active]
    check1 -- Yes --> check2{HO2S Heated and Active?}
    check2 -- No --> openA
    check2 -- Yes --> check3{Throttle Position greater than 70 percent?}
    check3 -- Yes --> openB[OPEN LOOP WOT Power Enrichment]
    check3 -- No --> check4{DFCO Active Decel Fuel Cut?}
    check4 -- Yes --> openC[OPEN LOOP Zero Fuel Injection]
    check4 -- No --> closed[CLOSED LOOP Lambda equals 1 PID Control]
    closed --> check5{HO2S Fault Detected?}
    check5 -- Yes --> openA
    check5 -- No --> closed
    style openA fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    style openB fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    style openC fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    style closed fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400
```

### 4.4 Lambda Sensor Voltage vs Air-Fuel Ratio Characteristic

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Narrow-Band HO2S Voltage vs Lambda (Switching Behavior)"
    x-axis "Lambda (AFR actual / AFR stoich)" [0.80, 0.90, 0.95, 0.99, 1.00, 1.01, 1.05, 1.10, 1.20]
    y-axis "Sensor Output Voltage (V)" 0.0 --> 1.0
    line [0.92, 0.91, 0.90, 0.89, 0.45, 0.10, 0.09, 0.09, 0.08]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key interpretation:** The narrow-band O₂ sensor behaves like a **switch**, not a linear sensor. It outputs ~0.9 V when rich (λ < 1) and ~0.1 V when lean (λ > 1), flipping sharply at λ = 1. This is *why* the closed-loop AFR **oscillates** around stoichiometry rather than settling — the sensor cannot resolve "how rich" or "how lean," only "rich or lean."

### 4.5 Wideband vs Narrowband Lambda Sensor (Comparison Schematic)

| Feature | Narrowband HO2S | Wideband UEGO Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Voltage switch (0.1–0.9 V) | Linear current (0–5 mA) → IPN signal |
| AFR resolution | Only at λ = 1 | λ = 0.7 to λ = 1.3 |
| Closed-loop control type | On/off (bang-bang) | PID with full proportionality |
| Cost | Low (~₹1,500) | High (~₹8,000–₹15,000) |
| Use case | Basic OBD-II compliant | Modern GDI, BS-VI, Euro 6 |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
> Q1. Define "Open Loop" and "Closed Loop" operation in an Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI) system. Mention any **three** operating conditions under which the ECU operates in open loop.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Open Loop Operation (1.5 marks):** Open loop is a fuel control strategy in which the Engine Control Unit (ECU) determines the injector pulse width based solely on pre-calibrated fuel maps and upstream sensor inputs (MAF, MAP, TPS, IAT, ECT, CKP), without using feedback from any exhaust oxygen sensor. The control path has no return loop, hence the term "open."

**Closed Loop Operation (1 mark):** Closed loop is a fuel control strategy in which the ECU continuously monitors the exhaust gas oxygen concentration through a heated oxygen sensor (HO2S) and adjusts the injector pulse width in real time to maintain the air-fuel ratio at the stoichiometric value (λ = 1).

**Three Open-Loop Conditions (0.5 mark):**
1. **Cold start / cold engine** (coolant temperature below ~40 °C).
2. **Wide Open Throttle (WOT)** — full-load power enrichment.
3. **Deceleration Fuel Cut-Off (DFCO)** — fuel completely cut off.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]
> Q2. Define the term "Lambda (λ)" in engine management. What does λ = 1.02 signify, and what is the corresponding equivalence ratio φ?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Lambda is the ratio of the actual air-fuel ratio to the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for the given fuel:

$$\lambda = \frac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoichiometric}}$$

**Interpretation of λ = 1.02 (1 mark):** A value of λ = 1.02 means the actual AFR is 2 % higher (leaner) than the stoichiometric ratio. The mixture contains **2 % excess air** relative to the chemically ideal combustion requirement.

**Equivalence ratio (0.5 mark):**

$$\phi = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{1.02} = 0.9804 \approx 0.98$$

Since $\phi < 1$, the mixture is **lean**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / OEMUT612 Module 2]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **closed loop air-fuel ratio control system** in a port-fuel-injection (PFI) gasoline engine. Discuss the role of the heated oxygen sensor (HO2S), the three-way catalytic converter, and the PID controller in maintaining λ = 1. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** A four-cylinder, four-stroke gasoline engine runs at 2400 rpm with a MAF reading of 12.5 g/s. The injector flow rate is 220 cc/min at 3 bar. The target air-fuel ratio is stoichiometric (14.7:1). Calculate the **required injector pulse width per injection event** per cylinder. (Take gasoline density ρ = 0.745 g/cc.) **[7 marks]**

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the **Closed Loop Control Architecture** diagram in Section 4.2. The essential elements are: ECU ↔ Injector ↔ Engine ↔ Exhaust Manifold ↔ HO2S (feedback) ↔ back to ECU. The Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC) is placed downstream of the HO2S.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**

1. The ECU reads MAF/MAP, ECT, IAT, CKP, and TPS to compute a **base injector pulse width $T_b$** from a pre-calibrated 3D fuel map.
2. The HO2S, placed upstream of the TWC, generates a voltage of ~0.1 V (lean) or ~0.9 V (rich) based on residual oxygen in the exhaust.
3. The ECU applies a **PID algorithm** to compute a correction factor $C_{PID}$ such that:

$$T_{inj} = T_b \cdot (1 + C_{PID})$$

4. The corrected pulse width is commanded to the injectors. This cycle repeats at ~10 Hz, keeping λ oscillating in the narrow window **0.99 ≤ λ ≤ 1.01**.
5. The **TWC** uses this rich-lean oscillation to simultaneously oxidize CO/HC and reduce NOₓ, achieving > 95 % conversion efficiency.

**Role of Each Component (2 marks):**
- **HO2S:** Provides the feedback signal (the only feedback path in the loop).
- **TWC:** Emission control device that requires λ ≈ 1 to function efficiently.
- **PID Controller:** Computes the correction factor; Proportional term responds to current error, Integral term eliminates steady-state offset, Derivative term damps oscillation.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks**

**Given Data:**
- Engine speed $N = 2400$ rpm
- Number of cylinders $n = 4$
- Four-stroke engine → injection events per cylinder per second = $N / (2 \cdot 60)$
- MAF $\dot{m}_a = 12.5$ g/s (total for all cylinders)
- Stoichiometric AFR = 14.7 : 1
- Injector flow $q = 220$ cc/min at 3 bar
- Gasoline density $\rho_f = 0.745$ g/cc

**Step 1 — Total fuel mass flow rate required (1 mark):**

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{AFR_{stoich}} = \frac{12.5}{14.7} = 0.8503 \text{ g/s}$$

**Step 2 — Fuel mass flow per cylinder (1 mark):**

$$\dot{m}_{f,\,cyl} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{n} = \frac{0.8503}{4} = 0.2126 \text{ g/s per cylinder}$$

**Step 3 — Injection events per cylinder per second (1 mark):**

For a 4-stroke engine, each cylinder fires once every 2 revolutions. At 2400 rpm, each cylinder therefore receives:

$$f_{inj} = \frac{N}{2 \times 60} = \frac{2400}{120} = 20 \text{ injections/s per cylinder}$$

**Step 4 — Fuel mass per injection event (1 mark):**

$$m_{inj} = \frac{\dot{m}_{f,\,cyl}}{f_{inj}} = \frac{0.2126}{20} = 0.01063 \text{ g per injection}$$

**Step 5 — Injector volume per injection event (1 mark):**

$$V_{inj} = \frac{m_{inj}}{\rho_f} = \frac{0.01063}{0.745} = 0.01427 \text{ cc per injection}$$

**Step 6 — Convert injector flow to milliseconds (1 mark):**

$$q = 220 \text{ cc/min} = \frac{220}{60} = 3.667 \text{ cc/s} = 3.667 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cc/ms}$$

**Step 7 — Compute pulse width (1 mark):**

$$T_{inj} = \frac{V_{inj}}{q_{ms}} = \frac{0.01427}{3.667 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.89 \text{ ms}$$

**Final Answer:** $T_{inj} \approx 3.89$ ms per cylinder per injection event.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

> **(a)** Compare **open loop** and **closed loop** operation of an EFI system. State **four** operating conditions under which the ECU operates in open loop and explain why. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** A lambda sensor reads **0.85 V**. Determine whether the mixture is rich or lean. If the engine uses petrol, calculate the actual air-fuel ratio. Comment on the suitability of this mixture for a three-way catalytic converter. **[7 marks]**

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):** Refer to the **Analytical Matrix** in Section 3.4. Cover feedback source, AFR accuracy, emissions, fuel economy, and operating regime.

**Four Open-Loop Conditions with Justification (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Cold Start / Cold Engine (ECT < 40 °C):** The HO2S has not yet reached its operating temperature (~350 °C). A cold sensor gives an erroneous voltage (~0.45 V) that does not reflect actual AFR. The ECU ignores this signal and uses a cold-start enrichment map (typically 1.5× to 3× stoichiometric fuel) to ensure smooth combustion.

2. **Wide Open Throttle (WOT):** At full load, the driver demands maximum power. The engine runs **slightly rich (λ ≈ 0.85–0.90)** to provide charge cooling (evaporative cooling of excess fuel) and to protect the exhaust valves and catalyst from excessive temperature. The ECU must ignore the HO2S feedback during this transient to allow the power enrichment.

3. **Deceleration Fuel Cut-Off (DFCO):** When the throttle is closed and engine speed is above a threshold (typically > 1200 rpm), the ECU cuts fuel injection entirely to improve fuel economy and reduce HC emissions. With zero fuel, AFR is infinite; closed-loop control has nothing to regulate.

4. **HO2S Failure / Heated Sensor Warm-Up Phase:** If the OBD-II system detects a faulty or cold HO2S (Diagnostic Trouble Code P0131, P0132, P0135, etc.), the ECU defaults to open-loop operation to prevent running the engine on bad sensor data. A MIL (Malfunction Indicator Lamp) is illuminated.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Interpret the voltage (1.5 marks):**

The narrow-band HO2S output is:
- ~0.1 V for lean mixtures (λ > 1)
- ~0.9 V for rich mixtures (λ < 1)
- Switches at ~0.45 V at λ = 1

Since $V_{HO2S} = 0.85$ V (close to 0.9 V), the exhaust is **RICH** (excess fuel, insufficient air).

**Step 2 — Estimate lambda (1.5 marks):**

Using a linear interpolation between 0.45 V (λ=1) and 0.9 V (λ≈0.85 for a typical rich switch point):

$$\lambda \approx 0.85 \quad \text{(typical rich switch point)}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate actual AFR for petrol (1.5 marks):**

$$AFR_{actual} = \lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich,\,petrol} = 0.85 \times 14.7 = 12.495 \approx 12.5 : 1$$

**Step 4 — Comment on TWC suitability (2.5 marks):**

The Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC) achieves > 95 % conversion efficiency for CO, HC, and NOₓ **only when λ oscillates in the narrow window 0.99 ≤ λ ≤ 1.01**. With λ = 0.85, the mixture is **15 % rich** — far outside the TWC's efficiency window. Under such rich conditions:
- CO and HC emissions rise sharply (insufficient O₂ for complete oxidation).
- TWC efficiency drops to < 50 %.
- Catalyst may overheat due to exothermic oxidation of unburned fuel.

**Conclusion:** This mixture is **not suitable for normal TWC operation**; it is acceptable only for short-duration WOT power enrichment, after which the ECU must revert to closed loop as soon as possible.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout
>
> **Pitfall 1 — Confusing Lambda with Equivalence Ratio.**
> Many students write $\lambda > 1$ for "rich" — this is **backwards**. $\lambda > 1$ means **lean** (excess air). $\phi > 1$ means **rich**. Lose **1 mark** for this mix-up.
>
> **Pitfall 2 — Using Wrong AFR Constant.**
> Petrol = **14.7**, Diesel = **14.5**, CNG = **17.2**, LPG = **15.5**. KTU often switches the fuel in numerical problems; using 14.7 for a CNG engine costs **full marks** on that sub-question.
>
> **Pitfall 3 — Forgetting the 4-Stroke Factor.**
> For pulse-width calculations, remember each cylinder injects only **once every 2 revolutions** in a 4-stroke engine. The formula is $f_{inj} = N / (2 \times 60)$, not $N/60$. This is the **most common** error in injector pulse-width questions.
>
> **Pitfall 4 — Drawing Open-Loop With a Feedback Arrow.**
> If you draw an arrow from the exhaust back to the ECU in an "open loop" diagram, you've just drawn a closed loop. KTU examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this conceptual contradiction.
>
> **Pitfall 5 — Stating "Closed Loop Means Better in All Conditions."**
> Closed loop is **not** always better. At WOT, closed loop would over-lean the engine, killing power. Examiners reward students who note that **open loop is mandatory** during WOT, cold start, and DFCO.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (read this in the last 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **Open Loop** = No feedback; ECU uses pre-calibrated fuel maps only.
- **Closed Loop** = ECU uses HO2S feedback to maintain λ = 1 in real time.
- **Stoichiometric AFR for petrol = 14.7 : 1** (memorize this number).
- **Lambda λ = 1** = chemically perfect; **λ > 1** = lean; **λ < 1** = rich.
- **Equivalence ratio φ = 1/λ**; rich ↔ φ > 1.
- **Narrow-band O₂ sensor** switches between **0.1 V (lean)** and **0.9 V (rich)** at **0.45 V (λ = 1)**.
- **Open Loop conditions:** cold start, WOT, DFCO, sensor failure.
- **Closed Loop conditions:** idle, part-throttle cruise, light load (steady state).
- **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)** requires λ oscillation in **0.99–1.01** window.
- **PID Controller:** Proportional (P) = current error; Integral (I) = past error; Derivative (D) = rate of error.
- **Diesel engines do NOT run closed loop** for AFR control — they are always lean by design.
- **Wideband UEGO sensors** (modern GDI engines) give linear λ from 0.7 to 1.3, replacing the switching narrowband sensor.
- **Pulse width formula:** $T_{inj} = T_b \cdot (1 + C_{corr})$ where $C_{corr}$ is the sum of all correction factors.
- **4-stroke injection frequency:** $f_{inj} = N / (2 \times 60)$ Hz per cylinder.
- **OBD-II fault codes** P0131–P0135 relate to HO2S malfunctions.
- **Cold-start enrichment** is the highest enrichment factor (1.5× to 3× stoichiometric fuel).

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Closed Loop and Open Loop Operation (OEMUT612)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electronic Engine Management Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT612 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electronic Engine Management Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — ELECTRONIC ENGINE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS (EEMS)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Electronic Engine Management System (EEMS)**, also referred to as the **Engine Control Unit (ECU) System** or **Engine Management System (EMS)**, is an integrated electronic control architecture that continuously monitors engine operating parameters through a network of sensors, processes the data using embedded microcontrollers in real-time, and precisely actuates fuel delivery, ignition timing, and air intake subsystems to optimize **engine performance, fuel economy, drivability, and exhaust emissions** in accordance with the **BS-VI / Euro 6 emission norms**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — OEMUT612 / Module 2**
> The 2024 scheme defines EEMS as a *closed-loop feedback control system* where the **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** acts as the central processing node, sensors function as input transducers, and actuators act as the final control elements. Students must be able to draw the block diagram, list sensors/actuators, and explain the working of fuel injection and ignition subsystems.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Engine as a Living Organism"

Imagine the **internal combustion (IC) engine** as a **human body performing heavy exercise**:

| Engine Part | Human Body Analogy | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **ECU (Brain)** | Human Brain & Nervous System | Decides fuel quantity & spark timing |
| **Coolant Temp Sensor (Skin)** | Skin Thermoreceptors | Senses engine temperature |
| **O2 / Lambda Sensor (Tongue)** | Taste Buds | Tastes exhaust air-fuel ratio |
| **Fuel Injectors (Mouth)** | Mouth & Stomach | Provides the "food" (fuel) |
| **Ignition Coil (Heart Beat)** | Heart | Provides the "spark" of life |
| **Throttle Position Sensor (Legs)** | Leg Pedal Pressure | Driver demand input |

Just as the brain constantly adjusts breathing rate and heartbeat based on blood oxygen sensors, the **ECU continuously adjusts fuel injection duration and spark advance** based on inputs from the **Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP)**, **Mass Air Flow (MAF)**, **Crankshaft Position (CKP)**, and **Oxygen (O2 / Lambda) sensors** — typically executing a complete control loop **25–50 times every second** at engine speeds of around 800–6000 RPM.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction to Memorize:**
> - **Mechanical / Carburetted System** → *Open-loop*, fixed calibration, no feedback.
> - **Electronic Engine Management System** → *Closed-loop*, adaptive, self-correcting, OBD-II compliant.

### 1.3 Necessity of EEMS — Why Mechanical Systems Failed

Pre-1980s vehicles relied on **carburetors** and **mechanical distributor ignition systems**. These suffered from:

- **Poor cold-start fuel metering** → 60–80% excess fuel consumption during warm-up.
- **Inability to compensate for altitude and barometric pressure** → 15–20% power loss at high altitudes.
- **Non-compliance with emission norms** (BS-II, BS-III, BS-IV, BS-VI).
- **No real-time knock detection** → engine damage under high load.

The **1973 Oil Crisis** and the introduction of the **US Clean Air Act Amendments (1990)** and **Euro emission standards (1992)** forced OEMs to shift from mechanical to electronic engine control — the **Bosch Motronic** (1982) being the landmark mass-production system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Statistic (Bold):** Modern EEMS can adjust fuel injection timing in **0.1 millisecond (100 μs)** increments and can process over **100 sensor inputs** simultaneously while reducing HC + CO + NOx emissions by **over 90%** compared to a 1970 carburetted engine.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of an EEMS — The Three Sub-Systems

A complete EEMS consists of **three interconnected sub-systems** working in synchrony:

### A) Sensor Sub-System (Input Stage)
Sensors convert **physical quantities** (pressure, temperature, speed, position, gas concentration) into **electrical voltage signals** interpretable by the ECU.

| Sensor | Physical Quantity Measured | Output Type | Working Principle |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MAP Sensor** | Manifold Absolute Pressure (kPa) | Analog (0–5 V) | Piezoresistive silicon diaphragm |
| **MAF Sensor** | Mass air flow rate (kg/s) | Analog (0–5 V) / Frequency | Hot-film anemometer |
| **TPS** | Throttle plate angle (degrees) | Analog (0–5 V) | Potentiometric (rheostat) |
| **CKP Sensor** | Crankshaft position & RPM | Digital (Hall / Variable Reluctance) | Magnetic pickup / Hall effect |
| **CMP Sensor** | Camshaft position (TDC reference) | Digital | Hall effect |
| **O2 / Lambda Sensor** | Exhaust oxygen concentration (%) | Analog (0–1 V narrow-band) | Zirconia / Titania electrolyte |
| **WBO2 (UEGO)** | True Lambda (λ) over 0.7–4.0 | Linear 0–5 V | Nernst cell + Pump cell |
| **CTS / ECT** | Coolant temperature (°C) | Analog (0–5 V) | NTC thermistor |
| **IAT** | Intake air temperature (°C) | Analog (0–5 V) | NTC thermistor |
| **Knock Sensor** | Engine block vibration (kHz) | Analog (AC) | Piezoelectric crystal |
| **Vehicle Speed Sensor** | Road speed (km/h) | Digital (Hall / Magnetic) | Magnetic pickup |

### B) ECU Sub-System (Processing Stage)
The **Engine Control Unit** is a **ruggedized embedded microcontroller** operating in temperatures from **−40 °C to +125 °C**. Its internal architecture consists of:

1. **Microprocessor (CPU)** — typically a 32-bit or 64-bit RISC core (e.g., Infineon Aurix TC3xx, NXP MPC5xxx, Renesas RH850).
2. **Memory Blocks:**
   - **ROM / Flash** → Permanent calibration maps (3D fuel & spark tables).
   - **RAM** → Volatile runtime data (sensor readings, adaptive coefficients).
   - **EEPROM** → Non-volatile fault codes (DTCs) and learned parameters.
3. **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** — Converts 0–5 V sensor signals to 10/12-bit digital values.
4. **D-A and PWM Output Drivers** — Generate actuator drive signals.
5. **Communication Interfaces:** CAN (Controller Area Network), LIN, FlexRay, K-Line.
6. **Watchdog & Self-Diagnostics** — OBD-II fault detection and limp-home mode.

### C) Actuator Sub-System (Output Stage)
Actuators receive low-power ECU signals (typically 5 V logic, 20 mA) and convert them to **high-power mechanical action**.

| Actuator | Function | Drive Signal |
|---|---|---|
| **Fuel Injector** | Sprays atomized fuel into port / cylinder | Solenoid — PWM pulse 1–20 ms |
| **Ignition Coil (DIS / COP)** | Generates 20–40 kV for spark | IGBT switching — dwell time controlled |
| **Idle Air Control Valve (IACV)** | Bypasses throttle for idle RPM | Stepper motor / PWM duty cycle |
| **EGR Valve** | Recirculates exhaust gas to reduce NOx | Solenoid / vacuum modulated |
| **EVAP Canister Purge Valve** | Recovers fuel vapors | Solenoid — duty cycled |
| **Variable Valve Timing (VVT) Solenoid** | Adjusts cam phaser | Oil-control solenoid, PWM |
| **Throttle Body (Electronic)** | Drive-by-wire pedal control | DC motor with H-bridge |

## 2.2 Operating Modes — Open Loop vs Closed Loop

### Open-Loop Mode
The ECU calculates fuel injection time based on **pre-stored 3D maps** indexed by (RPM, Load) and **does NOT** use the O2 sensor. Used during:
- **Cold start** (engine coolant < 20 °C).
- **Wide-open throttle (WOT)** acceleration enrichment.
- **O2 sensor warm-up** (below ~300 °C operating temperature).
- **Deceleration fuel cutoff (DFCO)**.

### Closed-Loop Mode
The ECU continuously compares the **actual air-fuel ratio** (from the O2 / WBO2 sensor) with the **target stoichiometric value (λ = 1.00)** and applies a **proportional-integral (PI) correction** to the injector pulse width every 10–100 ms.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked "When does closed-loop operation begin?", the standard answer is: *"Closed-loop operation begins once the O2 sensor reaches its operating temperature (~300 °C) AND the engine coolant temperature exceeds a calibrated threshold (typically 40–60 °C)."*

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{\text{AFR}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}}}$ | Lambda (relative air-fuel ratio) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} \approx 14.7$ | Stoichiometric AFR for gasoline (petrol) | kg air / kg fuel |
| 3 | $\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} \approx 14.5$ | Stoichiometric AFR for ethanol | kg air / kg fuel |
| 4 | $\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} \approx 34.0$ | Stoichiometric AFR for diesel | kg air / kg fuel |
| 5 | $\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} \approx 6.4$ | Stoichiometric AFR for hydrogen | kg air / kg fuel |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{air}}}{\text{AFR}_{\text{actual}}}$ | Mass flow rate of fuel required | kg/s |
| 7 | $t_{\text{inj}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}}}{q_{\text{inj}} \cdot N_{\text{cyl}}}$ | Injector pulse width (basic equation) | seconds |
| 8 | $t_{\text{inj, final}} = t_{\text{base}} \cdot f_{\text{TPS}} \cdot f_{\text{T}} \cdot f_{\text{O2}} \cdot f_{\text{accel}}$ | Final injection time with correction factors | seconds |
| 9 | $\eta_v = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{\rho_{\text{air}} \cdot V_d}$ | Volumetric efficiency | % (typically 80–110%) |
| 10 | $\text{BMEP} = \dfrac{T_{\text{engine}} \cdot 4\pi}{V_d \cdot N_{\text{cycles}}}$ | Brake Mean Effective Pressure | kPa |
| 11 | $P_{\text{ignition}} = \dfrac{1}{2} L \cdot I_{\text{peak}}^{2}$ | Energy stored in ignition coil primary | Joules (J) |
| 12 | $V_{\text{secondary}} = V_{\text{primary}} \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p}$ | Ignition coil turns ratio (step-up) | Volts (V) |
| 13 | $\theta_{\text{advance}} = f(\text{RPM, Load, T}_{\text{coolant}})$ | Ignition advance from 3D map | Degrees BTDC |
| 14 | $\text{IPW} = \dfrac{V_{\text{cyl}} \cdot \eta_v \cdot \rho_{\text{air}}}{\text{AFR}_{\text{target}} \cdot q_{\text{inj}} \cdot N_{\text{cyl}} \cdot n_{\text{injector}}}$ | Comprehensive IPW formula | seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict LaTeX Rule for Tables:** All absolute-value / norm expressions inside the formula table above use the **\vert** macro to prevent markdown table parser breakage. Never write **\vert x \vert** — the *pipe character* will destroy the table.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The EEMS is the **single most important innovation** in modern automotive engineering. Its utility spans:

1. **Emission Compliance** → Without EEMS, BS-VI (India) and Euro 6 (Europe) compliance is mathematically impossible.
2. **Fuel Economy** → Adaptive fuel maps + cylinder deactivation = 15–25% efficiency gain.
3. **Self-Diagnostics (OBD-II)** → On-Board Diagnostics port allows any garage tool to read **Diagnostic Trouble Codes (DTCs)** like P0171 (System Too Lean) or P0300 (Random Misfire).
4. **Hybrid & EV Integration** → EEMS provides the **Engine Stop-Start** (ESS) trigger and torque coordination with the electric machine in **BSG / ISG** mild-hybrid systems.
5. **Connected Car & ADAS** → The same ECU architecture has evolved into the **VCU (Vehicle Control Unit)** orchestrating ADAS, V2X communication, and OTA (over-the-air) software updates.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Air Mass Demand to Injector Pulse Width

The most important KTU derivation question in this module is the **"Injector Pulse Width (IPW) Calculation"**. We derive it step-by-step from fundamental thermodynamics.

### Given Inputs:
- Cylinder displacement (per cylinder): $V_d$ in m³
- Engine speed: $N$ in RPM
- Number of cylinders: $n_{\text{cyl}}$
- Volumetric efficiency: $\eta_v$
- Intake air density: $\rho_{\text{air}}$ in kg/m³
- Target air-fuel ratio: $\text{AFR}_{\text{target}}$
- Injector flow rate: $q_{\text{inj}}$ in kg/s
- Number of injectors: $n_{\text{injector}}$ (typically 1 per cylinder)

### Step 1: Mass of Air Entering One Cylinder per Cycle

The theoretical maximum mass of air that *could* fill the cylinder (if it were at ambient density) is:

$$
m_{\text{air, theoretical}} = \rho_{\text{air}} \cdot V_d
$$

However, the **volumetric efficiency** $\eta_v$ accounts for pressure drops, valve timing, and intake ram effects. Hence the **actual** mass inducted per cylinder per cycle is:

$$
m_{\text{air, actual}} = \eta_v \cdot \rho_{\text{air}} \cdot V_d
$$

**Explanation:** The engine's volumetric efficiency at low RPM is typically 0.7–0.8 (atmospheric engines) and at high RPM can exceed 1.0 in **forced-induction** (turbocharged) engines because the intake manifold pressure is above atmospheric.

### Step 2: Required Fuel Mass for the Target AFR

To burn the inducted air mass stoichiometrically (or rich/lean as demanded), the required fuel mass per cylinder per cycle is:

$$
m_{\text{fuel, required}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{air, actual}}}{\text{AFR}_{\text{target}}}
$$

**Explanation:** If the target is $\lambda = 1.00$ for petrol, then $\text{AFR}_{\text{target}} = 14.7$, meaning every 1 kg of fuel needs 14.7 kg of air.

### Step 3: Total Fuel Demand per Engine Revolution

Since the engine fires every $2$ revolutions (4-stroke) per cylinder, the fuel required **per revolution** across all cylinders is:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{fuel, per rev}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{fuel, required}} \cdot n_{\text{cyl}}}{2}
$$

**Explanation:** A 4-stroke engine completes one power stroke every 2 revolutions, so we divide by 2 to convert from "per cycle" to "per revolution".

### Step 4: Fuel Demand per Unit Time (per second)

The engine completes $N / 60$ revolutions per second. Therefore, the **total fuel flow rate demand** is:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{fuel, total}} = \dot{m}_{\text{fuel, per rev}} \cdot \dfrac{N}{60}
$$

Substituting and simplifying:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{fuel, total}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{fuel, required}} \cdot n_{\text{cyl}} \cdot N}{120}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{fuel, total}} = \dfrac{\eta_v \cdot \rho_{\text{air}} \cdot V_d \cdot n_{\text{cyl}} \cdot N}{120 \cdot \text{AFR}_{\text{target}}}
$$

### Step 5: Convert Fuel Mass Flow to Injector Pulse Width

The ECU controls each injector via a **PWM-like pulse** of duration $t_{\text{inj}}$. During this pulse, the injector delivers fuel at its rated flow $q_{\text{inj}}$. With $n_{\text{injector}}$ injectors, the total fuel delivery is:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{delivered}} = q_{\text{inj}} \cdot n_{\text{injector}} \cdot t_{\text{inj}} \cdot \dfrac{N}{60} \cdot \dfrac{n_{\text{cyl}}}{n_{\text{injector}}}
$$

(Each injector fires once per cycle, so it fires $N / 60 \cdot 1/2 = N/120$ times per second).

Equating **demand = supply**:

$$
q_{\text{inj}} \cdot n_{\text{injector}} \cdot t_{\text{inj}} \cdot \dfrac{N}{60} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{\eta_v \cdot \rho_{\text{air}} \cdot V_d \cdot n_{\text{cyl}} \cdot N}{120 \cdot \text{AFR}_{\text{target}}}
$$

### Step 6: Solve for $t_{\text{inj}}$

Simplifying both sides and cancelling $N$, $n_{\text{cyl}}$, and $n_{\text{injector}}$ (with $n_{\text{injector}} = n_{\text{cyl}}$ in port injection), we get the **final canonical IPW equation**:

$$
\boxed{\,t_{\text{inj}} = \dfrac{\eta_v \cdot \rho_{\text{air}} \cdot V_d}{2 \cdot \text{AFR}_{\text{target}} \cdot q_{\text{inj}}}\,}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the **base injection time**. The ECU then multiplies it by **transient correction factors** for cold start, acceleration enrichment, O2 feedback, battery voltage compensation, and purge vapor compensation.

### Step 7: Numerical Verification Worked Example

**Given:** 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine with $V_d = 0.0005 \text{ m}^3$ (500 cc per cyl), $N = 3000$ RPM, $\eta_v = 0.85$, $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\text{AFR}_{\text{target}} = 14.7$, $q_{\text{inj}} = 0.0002$ kg/s.

**Substituting:**

$$
t_{\text{inj}} = \dfrac{0.85 \times 1.2 \times 0.0005}{2 \times 14.7 \times 0.0002}
$$

$$
t_{\text{inj}} = \dfrac{0.00051}{0.00588} = 0.0867 \text{ s} = 86.7 \text{ ms}
$$

**Verification:** This value (≈ 87 ms) is realistic for idle-to-cruise injection duration in a 2-litre petrol engine. At WOT, the IPW may increase to 12–18 ms × N correction = typical 5–10 ms effective at high RPM.

## 3.2 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio from First Principles

Consider the **combustion of iso-octane** $\text{C}_8 \text{H}_{18}$ (the primary gasoline reference fuel):

$$
\text{C}_8 \text{H}_{18} + a \left( \text{O}_2 + 3.76 \, \text{N}_2 \right) \rightarrow b \, \text{CO}_2 + c \, \text{H}_2 \text{O} + d \, \text{N}_2
$$

### Step 1: Carbon Balance

Carbon atoms on both sides: $8 = b \Rightarrow b = 8$ moles of $\text{CO}_2$.

### Step 2: Hydrogen Balance

Hydrogen atoms: $18 = 2c \Rightarrow c = 9$ moles of $\text{H}_2 \text{O}$.

### Step 3: Oxygen Balance

Oxygen atoms: $2a = 2b + c = 16 + 9 = 25 \Rightarrow a = 12.5$ moles of $\text{O}_2$.

### Step 4: Nitrogen Balance

Nitrogen is inert: $d = 3.76 \cdot a = 3.76 \times 12.5 = 47$ moles of $\text{N}_2$.

### Step 5: Mass-Based AFR Calculation

Molar masses: $M_{\text{C8H18}} = 114$ g/mol, $M_{\text{O2}} = 32$ g/mol, $M_{\text{N2}} = 28$ g/mol, $M_{\text{air}} = (32 + 3.76 \times 28) = 137.28$ g/mol.

$$
\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{12.5 \times 137.28}{1 \times 114} = \dfrac{1716}{114} \approx 15.05
$$

**Note:** The more refined empirical value used by OEMs is **14.7:1** because real gasoline is a mixture of ~91% paraffins, aromatics, and naphthenes — not pure iso-octane.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hydrogen AFR Derivation (Bonus):** For $\text{H}_2 + 0.5 \, \text{O}_2 + 1.88 \, \text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2 \text{O} + 1.88 \, \text{N}_2$, the stoich AFR is $\dfrac{0.5 \times 137.28}{1 \times 2} = 34.32$ — confirming hydrogen is the **leanest-burning** common fuel.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Common EEMS Architectures

| System | Manufacturer | Year | Fuel Metering | Ignition | Lambda Control | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **D-Jetronic** | Bosch | 1967 | Speed-Density (MAP-based) | Electronic | Open-loop | Historical |
| **L-Jetronic** | Bosch | 1973 | Mass Air Flow (vane meter) | Electronic | Open-loop | Historical |
| **LH-Jetronic** | Bosch | 1981 | Hot-wire MAF | Electronic | Closed-loop | High |
| **Motronic M1–M7** | Bosch | 1982–2002 | Speed-Density + MAF | Integrated | Closed-loop | **Very High** |
| **ME-Motronic (MED17)** | Bosch | 2008+ | MED/Stratified | Ion-sensing | $\lambda = 1.0$ precise | High |
| **EEC-IV / EEC-V** | Ford | 1983–2000 | Speed-Density | EDIS | Closed-loop | Moderate |
| **GM ECM / PCM** | Delphi | 1995+ | Speed-Density | DIS / Coil-near-plug | Closed-loop | Moderate |
| **Denso ECD** | Toyota | 1980s+ | MAF-based | Integrated | Closed-loop | High (Asia) |
| **Continental EMS** | Continental | 2010+ | MED + MAF + ion-sense | $\lambda$ + knock | $\lambda = 1.00$ | High (current) |
| **Bosch MG1 (Motorsport)** | Bosch | 2018+ | Direct injection | Multi-spark | $\lambda + \text{ion}$ | Cutting-edge |

## 3.4 Closed-Loop PI Control Block — Mathematical Form

The closed-loop fuel correction is governed by a **Proportional + Integral** controller with adaptive learning. The ECU updates the **Long-Term Fuel Trim (LTFT)** and **Short-Term Fuel Trim (STFT)** every **10–20 ms**.

$$
\text{STFT}_{k+1} = \text{STFT}_k + K_p \cdot e_k + K_i \cdot \sum_{j=0}^{k} e_j \cdot \Delta t
$$

Where:
- $e_k = \lambda_{\text{target}} - \lambda_{\text{measured}} = 1.00 - \lambda_{\text{WBO2}}$
- $K_p$ = proportional gain (typically 0.4–0.8)
- $K_i$ = integral gain (typically 0.05–0.20 per second)
- $\Delta t$ = control loop period (≈ 10 ms)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** STFT is **short-term** correction (resets each drive cycle), while LTFT is **long-term adaptive learning** stored in EEPROM and survives ignition cycles. LTFT limits are typically **±25%**; if exceeded, the ECU sets DTC **P0171** (System Too Lean) or **P0172** (System Too Rich).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
