# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the term "Engine Management System". List any four sensors and four actuators used in a modern EMS. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

An **Engine Management System (EMS)** is an electronic control system that uses sensors, an **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)**, and actuators to monitor and control the engine's fuel injection, ignition timing, idle speed, and emission levels in real time.

**Four Sensors (1/2 mark each):**
1. **Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP) Sensor** — measures intake manifold pressure.
2. **Mass Air Flow (MAF) Sensor** — measures incoming air mass.
3. **Crankshaft Position (CKP) Sensor** — measures RPM and piston position.
4. **Oxygen (O2 / Lambda) Sensor** — measures residual oxygen in exhaust.

**Four Actuators (1/2 mark each):**
1. **Fuel Injectors** — spray atomized fuel.
2. **Ignition Coil** — generates high-voltage spark.
3. **Idle Air Control Valve (IACV)** — bypasses throttle for idle control.
4. **Electronic Throttle Body** — drive-by-wire throttle control.

### Q2. Differentiate between **Open-Loop** and **Closed-Loop** modes of operation in an EMS. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Open-Loop Mode | Closed-Loop Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedback** | No feedback from O2 sensor | Uses O2 / WBO2 sensor feedback |
| **Operation** | Pre-stored 3D map based | Real-time adaptive PI control |
| **Conditions** | Cold start, WOT, DFCO, O2 not warm | Normal cruising, steady load |
| **Accuracy** | Approximate (±10%) | Precise ($\lambda = 1.00 \pm 0.02$) |
| **STFT / LTFT** | Disabled / frozen | Active correction |

**Key Trigger:** Closed-loop begins only after O2 sensor reaches ~300 °C AND coolant > 40 °C.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (Option 1):**

**Q A (a)** Explain the **working principle of an Electronic Engine Management System** with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the ECU and list any **six sensors** and **six actuators** with their functions. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

The EEMS consists of three sub-systems: **Input Sensors → ECU → Output Actuators**, arranged in a closed feedback loop with the **O2 sensor**.

**(i) Working Principle (3 marks):**
The ECU continuously polls input sensors (MAP, MAF, CKP, CMP, TPS, O2, CTS, IAT, knock sensor) at sampling rates of **1–10 kHz**. These analog signals are **signal-conditioned** (amplified, filtered) and **digitized** by the **12-bit ADC**. The **microcontroller** consults pre-calibrated **3D fuel and spark maps** stored in **Flash ROM**, computes the required **injector pulse width (IPW)** and **ignition advance angle**, and drives the actuators through **MOSFET / IGBT power stages**. The **O2 sensor** in the exhaust provides real-time feedback, enabling the ECU to maintain **$\lambda = 1.00 \pm 0.02$** (stoichiometric) — the precise ratio required for optimal **Three-Way Catalyst (TWC)** conversion of HC, CO, and NOx.

**(ii) Six Sensors (2 marks):**
1. **MAP** — manifold pressure → speed-density calculation.
2. **MAF** — air mass → precise fuel calculation.
3. **CKP / CMP** — engine speed and firing order.
4. **O2 / Lambda** — exhaust AFR feedback.
5. **TPS** — driver demand input.
6. **Knock Sensor** — detects engine knock (pre-ignition).

**(iii) Six Actuators (2 marks):**
1. **Fuel Injectors** — solenoid-controlled fuel delivery.
2. **Ignition Coils** — 25–40 kV spark generation.
3. **IACV** — bypass air control for idle RPM.
4. **EGR Valve** — exhaust gas recirculation for NOx reduction.
5. **EVAP Purge** — fuel vapor recovery.
6. **Electronic Throttle** — drive-by-wire pedal response.

**Q A (b)** With the help of neat diagrams, explain the **working of a port fuel injection (PFI) system** and a **direct injection (DI) system**. Compare their advantages and disadvantages. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Port Fuel Injection (PFI) — Multipoint Injection (MPI):**
Fuel is injected by an **electromagnetic solenoid injector** into the **intake port** just upstream of the **intake valve**. The injector is triggered by the ECU with a pulse of **1.5–20 ms** duration, **once per engine cycle per cylinder**. The fuel spray mixes with incoming air in the port, forming a homogeneous mixture that enters the combustion chamber when the intake valve opens. The PFI system typically operates at **3.0–4.5 bar** fuel rail pressure.

**Direct Injection (DI) — Gasoline Direct Injection (GDI):**
Fuel is injected by a high-pressure **piezoelectric or solenoid injector** **directly into the combustion chamber**, after the intake valve has closed. The fuel rail pressure is much higher — typically **100–350 bar** in current GDI systems (Bosch MED17 / Continental EMS3). DI enables **stratified-charge operation** at light load (fuel concentrated near spark plug) and **homogeneous stoichiometric** at high load, yielding 10–20% better fuel economy.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | PFI (MPI) | GDI (DI) |
|---|---|---|
| Injection Location | Intake port | Combustion chamber |
| Fuel Rail Pressure | 3–5 bar | 100–350 bar |
| Mixing Time | Long (port + valve) | Very short (in-cylinder) |
| Fuel Economy | Baseline | +10–20% |
| Emissions (HC, CO) | Lower | Higher (particulate ↑) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (high-pressure pump + injectors) |
| Knock Margin | Better | Reduced (carbon deposits) |

---

### **Question B (Option 2):**

**Q B (a)** Derive the **Injector Pulse Width (IPW) formula** for a 4-stroke, multi-cylinder petrol engine from first principles. A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke engine with $V_d = 0.0005$ m³/cyl operates at 3000 RPM. Given $\eta_v = 0.85$, $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.2$ kg/m³, target AFR = 14.7, and injector flow $q_{\text{inj}} = 0.0002$ kg/s. Calculate the base IPW. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

**(i)** Air mass per cylinder per cycle: $m_{\text{air}} = \eta_v \cdot \rho_{\text{air}} \cdot V_d$

**(ii)** Fuel mass per cycle: $m_{\text{fuel}} = m_{\text{air}} / \text{AFR}_{\text{target}}$

**(iii)** Total fuel flow rate (per second) = $m_{\text{fuel}} \cdot n_{\text{cyl}} \cdot (N / 120)$ (since 4-stroke: 1 cycle per 2 revs)

**(iv)** Total fuel delivered by $n_{\text{inj}}$ injectors, each firing $N / 120$ times/sec at flow $q_{\text{inj}}$ for $t_{\text{inj}}$ seconds:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{delivered}} = n_{\text{inj}} \cdot q_{\text{inj}} \cdot t_{\text{inj}} \cdot \dfrac{N}{60} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}
$$

**(v)** Equating demand = supply and solving:

$$
\boxed{\,t_{\text{inj}} = \dfrac{\eta_v \cdot \rho_{\text{air}} \cdot V_d}{2 \cdot \text{AFR}_{\text{target}} \cdot q_{\text{inj}}}\,}
$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Substituting:
- $\eta_v = 0.85$, $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.2$ kg/m³, $V_d = 0.0005$ m³
- $\text{AFR}_{\text{target}} = 14.7$, $q_{\text{inj}} = 0.0002$ kg/s

$$
t_{\text{inj}} = \dfrac{0.85 \times 1.2 \times 0.0005}{2 \times 14.7 \times 0.0002} = \dfrac{0.00051}{0.00588}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t_{\text{inj}} = 0.0867 \text{ s} = 86.7 \text{ ms}\,}
$$

**Note:** This is the *base* IPW. The ECU multiplies by transient correction factors: cold-start enrichment ($\times 1.5$–$2.0$), acceleration enrichment ($\times 1.1$–$1.3$), battery voltage compensation ($\times 1.05$–$1.15$), and STFT/LTFT correction ($\times 0.85$–$1.15$).

**Q B (b)** Explain the **closed-loop air-fuel ratio control** using a Wide-Band Oxygen (WBO2 / UEGO) sensor. With a neat block diagram, describe the **PI controller** operation and the concept of **Short-Term and Long-Term Fuel Trims (STFT, LTFT)**. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**WBO2 / UEGO Sensor (2 marks):**
A **Wide-Band Oxygen Sensor** (also called **Universal Exhaust Gas Oxygen sensor, UEGO**) measures the **true Lambda ($\lambda$)** value over a range of **0.7 to infinity**, unlike a narrow-band sensor that only indicates "rich" or "lean" near $\lambda = 1.0$. It uses a **Nernst cell** (concentration cell) plus a **pump cell** to actively pump oxygen ions across a **zirconium dioxide ($\text{ZrO}_2$)** electrolyte, maintaining a reference voltage of **0.45 V**. The pump current required is directly proportional to the actual $\lambda$ value. WBO2 enables precise control in **lean-burn GDI**, **homogeneous charge**, and **stratified-charge** modes.

**PI Controller Operation (2 marks):**
The ECU executes a **discrete-time Proportional-Integral (PI) controller** every **10–20 ms**:
- **Error:** $e_k = \lambda_{\text{target}} - \lambda_{\text{measured}}$
- **Control output:** $\Delta t_{\text{inj}} = K_p \cdot e_k + K_i \cdot \sum e_k \cdot \Delta t$
- $K_p \approx 0.5$ (proportional) and $K_i \approx 0.1$/s (integral) are tuned for stability.
- The PI output adjusts the **injector pulse width** in real time, minimizing steady-state error to near zero.

**STFT and LTFT (3 marks):**
- **Short-Term Fuel Trim (STFT)** — fast, **resets to 0%** at every ignition-off. Range: **±10%**. Reflects immediate corrections.
- **Long-Term Fuel Trim (LTFT)** — slow, **stored in EEPROM**. Learns the *average* STFT over hours of operation. Range: **±25%**.
- If $|\text{LTFT}| > 25\%$, the ECU sets **DTC P0171** (lean) or **P0172** (rich) and may trigger **limp-home mode**.
- The **total fuel trim** = $\text{STFT} + \text{LTFT}$, applied multiplicatively to the base IPW.

**Final IPW formula:**

$$
t_{\text{inj, final}} = t_{\text{base}} \cdot (1 + \text{STFT}/100) \cdot (1 + \text{LTFT}/100) \cdot f_{\text{cold}} \cdot f_{\text{accel}} \cdot f_{\text{battery}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in 4-stroke engines.** Many students divide by $N/60$ instead of $N/120$ — losing 2 marks in derivation.
> 2. **Confusing AFR with Lambda.** $\text{AFR} = 14.7$ is **stoichiometric for gasoline**; $\lambda = 1.0$ is **stoichiometric for any fuel**. Always clarify which is which.
> 3. **Not mentioning the trigger conditions for closed-loop** (O2 sensor at 300 °C AND coolant > 40 °C) — a 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Drawing the block diagram without a feedback path** — examiners specifically check the closed-loop arrow returning from O2 sensor to ECU.
> 5. **Mixing up IPW units** (ms vs s) — always quote the answer in **ms** when discussing injector pulse widths in modern context.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **EEMS = Sensors + ECU + Actuators** arranged in a **closed-loop** configuration.
- [x] **ECU** uses 12-bit ADC, 32-bit RISC core, Flash ROM, EEPROM, RAM.
- [x] **MAP + CKP** form the **speed-density** calculation; **MAF** provides direct air mass.
- [x] **Stoichiometric AFR for petrol = 14.7**; for diesel ≈ 34; for hydrogen ≈ 6.4.
- [x] **Lambda ($\lambda$)** is a *normalized* AFR, fuel-independent: $\lambda = 1.0$ at stoich.
- [x] **Open-loop** during cold start, WOT, DFCO; **closed-loop** during normal cruise and idle.
- [x] **IPW formula:** $t_{\text{inj}} = \dfrac{\eta_v \cdot \rho_{\text{air}} \cdot V_d}{2 \cdot \text{AFR} \cdot q_{\text{inj}}}$ — memorize for KTU.
- [x] **STFT** (fast, resets) ±10%; **LTFT** (slow, adaptive) ±25%; triggers DTCs P0171/P0172.
- [x] **O2 sensor** requires ~300 °C to function; modern sensors have **electric heaters**.
- [x] **WBO2 (UEGO)** measures true $\lambda$ over 0.7–4.0; narrow-band only indicates rich/lean.
- [x] **Knock sensor** retards ignition timing by 2–5° when vibration > threshold frequency (typically 7–8 kHz).
- [x] **EGR** reduces peak combustion temperature → lower NOx.
- [x] **EVAP** captures fuel vapors to prevent HC emissions.
- [x] **OBD-II** standard (since 1996 US / 2013 BS-IV India) provides 16-pin diagnostics port.
- [x] **BS-VI norms** (India, 2020+) demand **$\lambda = 1.00 \pm 0.01$** control with **GPF (Gasoline Particulate Filter)** on GDI engines.
- [x] **Future EEMS** integrates with **VCU (Vehicle Control Unit)** for hybrid/ADAS coordination, OTA updates, and AI-based predictive calibration.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electronic Cruise Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT612 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electronic Cruise Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electronic Cruise Control (ECC)** is a closed-loop, feedback-controlled automotive driver-assistance system designed to automatically maintain a driver-prescribed vehicle speed without continuous accelerator pedal input. The system is formally classified as a **Longitudinal Vehicle Dynamics Control System** operating under the *Negative Feedback Control* paradigm, where the **Actual Vehicle Speed** (measured by a speed sensor) is continuously compared against the **Desired Set Speed** (operator input), and the resulting **Error Signal** drives an actuator that modulates the throttle body opening to nullify the speed deviation.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Electronic Cruise Control is positioned within the broader umbrella of **Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)** and serves as the foundational building block for more sophisticated systems such as **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)**, **Adaptive Cruise Control with Stop & Go**, and **Cooperative Adaptive Cruise Control (CACC)**.

### Key Terminology Anchored to KTU Syllabus

| Term | Standard KTU Definition |
|---|---|
| **Set Speed** | The target vehicle velocity commanded by the driver via the SET switch |
| **Cruise Module** | The electronic control unit (ECU) that processes sensor inputs and generates actuator commands |
| **Throttle Actuator | The electromechanical (or electro-pneumatic) device that physically regulates airflow into the engine |
| **Speed Sensor** | Typically a Hall-effect or magneto-resistive sensor mounted on the transmission output shaft |
| **Error Signal (e)** | The mathematical difference: $e(t) = v_{set} - v_{actual}(t)$ |
| **Servo Loop** | The closed control loop comprising sensor → controller → actuator → plant (vehicle) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Auto-Pilot" for Your Right Foot**

Imagine you are driving on a perfectly straight, flat Kerala highway from Kochi to Thrissur. You set your speed at **80 km/h** using the cruise control stalk. From that moment, **your right foot can rest on the armrest**. The car will:

1. **Sense** its current speed 100 times every second.
2. **Compare** it with the 80 km/h you commanded.
3. If the car is going **too slow** (e.g., 78 km/h on a slight incline), it will **automatically open the throttle slightly** to regain 80 km/h.
4. If the car is going **too fast** (e.g., 82 km/h on a downhill), it will **automatically close the throttle** to slow back to 80 km/h.

The driver becomes the **supervisor**, not the **operator**. The system performs the tedious, continuous micro-corrections that a human driver would otherwise make with their foot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's View:** A textbook-perfect answer must explicitly mention that cruise control is a **closed-loop** (not open-loop) system and that it operates on the principle of **negative feedback**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following numerical standards are conventionally used in cruise control system design and should be memorized for KTU examinations:

- **Standard Cruise Operating Speed Range:** **40 km/h to 200 km/h** (varies by OEM)
- **Typical Controller Sampling Frequency:** **10 Hz to 100 Hz** (modern ECUs)
- **Throttle Actuator Response Time:** **< 50 ms**
- **Speed Sensor Resolution:** **1 pulse per revolution** of transmission output shaft (with 60-tooth tone wheels being common)
- **Standard Control Law:** **PID (Proportional–Integral–Derivative)** with anti-windup
- **Engine Torque Response Bandwidth:** Approximately **2 Hz to 5 Hz** (plant bandwidth limitation)

## 1.4 Historical Context & Evolution Path

```
Mechanical Governors (1900s)
        ↓
Vacuum-Based Cruise Control (Chrysler Imperial, 1958)
        ↓
Electronic Cruise Control (1970s onwards)
        ↓
Adaptive Cruise Control with Radar/Lidar (1990s onwards)
        ↓
Predictive / Cooperative ACC (2010s onwards)
```

This evolution shows that Electronic Cruise Control is the **technological parent** of every modern ACC system. KTU 2024 examiners often test this lineage.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Architecture — Functional Decomposition

Electronic Cruise Control is composed of **three major functional subsystems**, each containing multiple components:

### Subsystem A: **Input / Sensing Subsystem**

- **Speed Sensor (VSS — Vehicle Speed Sensor):** Located on the transmission output shaft. Generates a **frequency-modulated pulse train** whose frequency is proportional to wheel/ driveshaft rotational speed. The ECU counts pulses over a fixed time window to compute instantaneous vehicle speed.
- **Driver Interface Switches:** SET, RESUME, ACCEL (+), COAST (−), CANCEL, ON/OFF. These are typically multiplexed through a **stalk-mounted switch pod** wired to the ECU via the CAN (Controller Area Network) bus.
- **Brake Pedal Switch (Normally Closed):** A safety-critical interlock. When the brake pedal is depressed, the switch **immediately disengages** cruise control and restores manual throttle authority.
- **Clutch Pedal Switch (Manual Transmission):** Same safety function as the brake switch.
- **Throttle Position Sensor (TPS):** Provides feedback on the actual throttle plate angle (the controlled variable in some architectures).
- **Engine RPM Sensor:** Used for redundant speed estimation in some ECUs.

### Subsystem B: **Processing / Control Subsystem**

- **Cruise Control ECU (often integrated into the PCM/ECM):** A microcontroller running the closed-loop control algorithm at a periodic interrupt (typically every **10 ms to 100 ms**).
- **Control Algorithm:** Almost universally a **PID controller** with saturation limits and anti-windup compensation.
- **Mode Manager:** A finite-state machine that determines whether the system is in DISENGAGED, ENGAGED, OVERRIDE, or FAULT state.

### Subsystem C: **Actuation Subsystem**

- **Drive-by-Wire Throttle Body:** In modern vehicles, an electric motor (DC or stepper) directly actuates the throttle plate.
- **Vacuum Actuator / Servomotor (legacy):** Older systems used engine manifold vacuum modulated by a solenoid to pull a diaphragm connected to the throttle.
- **Electronic Throttle Body (ETB) with integrated TPS:** The standard in post-2000 vehicles.

## 2.2 The Core Control Philosophy — Why Negative Feedback?

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Engineering Principle:** In the presence of **disturbances** (road gradient, wind, payload, tire pressure variation), an open-loop throttle command cannot maintain constant speed. A **closed-loop feedback** system automatically compensates for these unknown disturbances because it measures the *effect* of the disturbance (speed error) rather than trying to model the disturbance itself.

This is the **single most important conceptual point** in cruise control theory. Without feedback, the system would require a perfect mathematical model of the vehicle, the road, the wind, and the driver — an impossible task.

## 2.3 PID Control Law — The Heart of the System

The standard cruise control law is the **Parallel PID Form**:

$$
u(t) = K_p \, e(t) \; + \; K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau \; + \; K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

Where:

- $u(t)$ = Control output (throttle command, % opening or PWM duty cycle)
- $e(t) = v_{set} - v_{actual}(t)$ = Speed error (km/h or m/s)
- $K_p$ = Proportional gain (immediate response)
- $K_i$ = Integral gain (eliminates steady-state error)
- $K_d$ = Derivative gain (predicts future error, damps overshoot)

### Role of Each Term in Plain English

| Term | Plain English Meaning | Real-World Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Proportional (P)** | "React **now** to the current error" | If you are 5 km/h below set speed, open the throttle a moderate amount. |
| **Integral (I)** | "React based on **accumulated past** error" | If a long hill keeps you 2 km/h slow for 30 seconds, gradually open the throttle more. |
| **Derivative (D)** | "React based on **how fast** the error is changing" | If you are suddenly decelerating rapidly, preemptively close the throttle to avoid overshoot. |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Exams)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every equation you must memorize** for the Electronic Cruise Control topic. These are the most commonly asked derivations and numerical problems.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e(t) = v_{set} - v_{actual}(t)$ | Speed error signal | km/h or m/s |
| 2 | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$ | PID control law (continuous) | % or PWM |
| 3 | $u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i T_s \sum_{i=0}^{k} e[i] + K_d \frac{e[k]-e[k-1]}{T_s}$ | PID control law (discrete / digital ECU) | % or PWM |
| 4 | $F_{tract} = m \frac{dv}{dt} + F_{aero} + F_{roll} + F_{grade}$ | Vehicle longitudinal dynamics (Newton's 2nd Law) | N |
| 5 | $F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$ | Aerodynamic drag force | N |
| 6 | $F_{roll} = \mu_r \, m \, g \cos\theta$ | Rolling resistance force | N |
| 7 | $F_{grade} = m \, g \sin\theta$ | Grade (slope) resistance force | N |
| 8 | $G(s) = \frac{K_p s^2 + K_p K_i s + K_p K_d s^3}{s(s+\alpha)(s+\beta)}$ | Open-loop transfer function with PID | — |
| 9 | $\tau = \frac{J_{eff}}{B_{eff}}$ | Effective mechanical time constant | s |
| 10 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K}{m}}$ | Natural frequency of vehicle speed response | rad/s |
| 11 | $\zeta = \frac{B}{2\sqrt{Km}}$ | Damping ratio | dimensionless |
| 12 | $v_{max} = \frac{\pi r_{wheel} f_{sensor}}{N_{teeth}} \times 3.6$ | Speed from sensor frequency | km/h |

**Where:**
- $m$ = vehicle mass (kg)
- $C_d$ = drag coefficient (~0.3 for sedans)
- $A$ = frontal area (m²)
- $\rho$ = air density (≈ **1.225 kg/m³** at sea level)
- $\mu_r$ = rolling resistance coefficient (~0.015 for passenger car tires)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (≈ **9.81 m/s²**)
- $\theta$ = road slope angle
- $J_{eff}$ = effective inertia, $B_{eff}$ = effective damping
- $T_s$ = digital sampling period (s)

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not use the `|` symbol (vertical bar) inside markdown tables** — it breaks the table parser. In formulas like $K \vert s \vert$, write it as $K \cdot s$ or use the LaTeX `\vert` command.

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind Cruise Control — Engineering Justification

Cruise control is not a luxury gimmick. It serves **four legitimate engineering purposes**:

1. **Fuel Economy Optimization:** On flat highways, maintaining constant speed eliminates the fuel-wasting acceleration-deceleration cycles of human driving. Studies show **5–14% fuel savings** on long highway trips.
2. **Driver Fatigue Reduction:** Long-distance truck and car drivers experience reduced cognitive load, leading to **fewer accidents**.
3. **Emissions Compliance:** Constant speed operation allows the catalytic converter to maintain optimal temperature and the engine to operate in its cleanest fuel map region.
4. **Legal Speed Compliance:** Removes the risk of inadvertent speeding.

## 2.6 Safety Architecture — Why It Cannot Fail

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Cruise control must be **fail-safe** by design. The system must disengage instantly on any of the following events:
> - Brake pedal application
> - Clutch pedal application (manual transmission)
> - Driver pressing CANCEL
> - Vehicle speed falls below minimum threshold (typically 40 km/h)
> - Speed sensor signal loss (plausibility check failure)
> - ECU diagnostic trouble code (DTC) flag

This multi-channel disengagement strategy uses **redundant, diverse sensors** — a standard automotive functional safety (ISO 26262) practice.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady-State Speed Error Analysis for a P-Only Controller

**Problem Setup:** A vehicle of mass $m = 1500$ kg is cruising on a flat road ($\theta = 0$) at a set speed of $v_{set} = 100$ km/h. The powertrain applies a tractive force $F_t$ proportional to the throttle command $u$ with gain $K_t = 300$ N/%. A proportional-only controller with gain $K_p = 5$ %/(km/h) regulates the throttle. The road has a rolling resistance coefficient $\mu_r = 0.012$ and aerodynamic drag coefficient $C_d A = 0.7$ m². Determine the **steady-state speed error**.

**Step 1 — State the longitudinal force balance equation:**

At steady state, acceleration is zero, so Newton's Second Law gives:

$$
F_{tract} = F_{aero} + F_{roll}
$$

**Step 2 — Express each force in terms of speed:**

The tractive force from the controller is:

$$
F_{tract} = K_t \cdot u_{ss} = K_t \cdot K_p \cdot e_{ss}
$$

The aerodynamic drag is:

$$
F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2
$$

The rolling resistance is:

$$
F_{roll} = \mu_r \, m \, g
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and solve for steady-state error:**

At steady state, $v_{actual} \approx v_{set}$ (small error assumption), so we can linearize drag around $v_{set}$:

$$
F_{aero}(v) \approx F_{aero}(v_{set}) + \underbrace{\rho C_d A v_{set}}_{B_{aero}}(v - v_{set})
$$

The force balance becomes:

$$
K_t K_p e_{ss} = F_{aero}(v_{set}) - B_{aero} e_{ss} + \mu_r m g
$$

Grouping the error terms:

$$
e_{ss}(K_t K_p + B_{aero}) = F_{aero}(v_{set}) + \mu_r m g
$$

Solving explicitly:

$$
e_{ss} = \frac{F_{aero}(v_{set}) + \mu_r m g}{K_t K_p + B_{aero}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation:**

Convert $v_{set} = 100$ km/h $= 27.78$ m/s.

Aerodynamic drag at set speed:

$$
F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.7 \times (27.78)^2
$$

$$
F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.7 \times 771.6
$$

$$
F_{aero} = 330.8 \text{ N}
$$

Aerodynamic damping coefficient:

$$
B_{aero} = 1.225 \times 0.7 \times 27.78 = 23.82 \text{ N·s/m}
$$

Rolling resistance force:

$$
F_{roll} = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 = 176.6 \text{ N}
$$

Total disturbance force:

$$
F_{dist} = 330.8 + 176.6 = 507.4 \text{ N}
$$

Loop gain:

$$
K_t K_p = 300 \times 5 = 1500 \text{ N/(km/h)}
$$

Converting to consistent units: $1500 \text{ N/(km/h)} \times \frac{1 \text{ km/h}}{0.2778 \text{ m/s}} = 5400 \text{ N/(m/s)}$

Total denominator:

$$
K_t K_p + B_{aero} = 5400 + 23.82 = 5423.82 \text{ N/(m/s)}
$$

Steady-state speed error:

$$
e_{ss} = \frac{507.4}{5423.82} = 0.0935 \text{ m/s} = 0.337 \text{ km/h}
$$

**Result:** With a P-only controller, the vehicle settles at **99.66 km/h** instead of the commanded 100 km/h — a small but **non-zero** steady-state error. **This is why integral action is added.**

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Why a P-Only Controller Has a Steady-State Error, and a PI Controller Eliminates It

**The Core Reason — A Linear Systems Proof:**

Consider the open-loop transfer function of the vehicle-plus-controller with P control only:

$$
L(s) = K_p \cdot \frac{K_t}{m s + B_{eff}}
$$

The closed-loop transfer function from $v_{set}$ to $v_{actual}$ is:

$$
T(s) = \frac{L(s)}{1 + L(s)} = \frac{K_p K_t}{m s + B_{eff} + K_p K_t}
$$

Applying the **Final Value Theorem** for a step input of magnitude $V$:

$$
v_{actual}(\infty) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{V}{s} \cdot T(s) = V \cdot \frac{K_p K_t}{B_{eff} + K_p K_t}
$$

For this to equal $V$ (zero error), the denominator must approach infinity or the numerator must equal the denominator — neither happens with finite $K_p$. The steady-state error is:

$$
e_{ss} = V - v_{actual}(\infty) = V \cdot \frac{B_{eff}}{B_{eff} + K_p K_t}
$$

This is **non-zero for any finite $K_p$**. To eliminate it, we need a controller with **infinite low-frequency gain** — exactly what an integrator provides.

**With PI control**, the open-loop transfer function becomes:

$$
L(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}\right) \cdot \frac{K_t}{m s + B_{eff}} = \frac{K_t(K_p s + K_i)}{s(m s + B_{eff})}
$$

Note the **pole at the origin** (from the integrator). Applying the Final Value Theorem again:

$$
v_{actual}(\infty) = V \cdot \frac{K_t K_i}{0 + 0} \rightarrow V
$$

The infinite DC gain forces the steady-state error to **exactly zero**. This is the **theoretical justification for integral action** in cruise control.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Discrete-Time PID for Digital ECUs

Modern ECUs are digital, so the continuous PID must be discretized. The most common form is the **Velocity (Incremental) Form**:

**Step 1 — Write the position-form discrete PID:**

$$
u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i T_s \sum_{i=0}^{k} e[i] + \frac{K_d}{T_s}(e[k] - e[k-1])
$$

**Step 2 — Write the same equation for the previous step:**

$$
u[k-1] = K_p e[k-1] + K_i T_s \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} e[i] + \frac{K_d}{T_s}(e[k-1] - e[k-2])
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract to get the velocity form (delta-u):**

$$
\Delta u[k] = u[k] - u[k-1]
$$

$$
\Delta u[k] = K_p(e[k] - e[k-1]) + K_i T_s e[k] + \frac{K_d}{T_s}(e[k] - 2e[k] + e[k-2])
$$

**Step 4 — Why use velocity form?** 

The velocity form has **two practical advantages** in automotive ECUs:

- **Anti-windup is automatic** — if $u[k]$ saturates, you simply stop integrating $\Delta u$.
- **Bumpless transfer** — when switching from manual to cruise control, you set $\Delta u = 0$ for the first iteration to avoid a step change.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Stability Analysis via Routh-Hurwitz

**Plant model (linearized vehicle):**

$$
G_p(s) = \frac{K_t}{m s + B_{eff}} = \frac{300}{1500 s + 200}
$$

**PI Controller:**

$$
G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} = \frac{5 s + 1}{s}
$$

**Closed-loop characteristic equation:**

$$
1 + G_c(s) G_p(s) = 0
$$

$$
1 + \frac{(5s + 1)}{s} \cdot \frac{300}{1500s + 200} = 0
$$

$$
s(1500s + 200) + 300(5s + 1) = 0
$$

$$
1500s^2 + 200s + 1500s + 300 = 0
$$

$$
1500s^2 + 1700s + 300 = 0
$$

**Routh Array:**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|---|---|---|
| $s^2$ | 1500 | 300 |
| $s^1$ | 1700 | 0 |
| $s^0$ | 300 | — |

**Routh-Hurwitz Stability Criterion:** For a second-order polynomial $as^2 + bs + c$, stability requires $a > 0$, $b > 0$, $c > 0$. All three conditions are satisfied ($1500 > 0$, $1700 > 0$, $300 > 0$), so **the system is stable**.

**Natural frequency and damping ratio:**

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{300}{1500}} = \sqrt{0.2} = 0.447 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
\zeta = \frac{1700}{2 \sqrt{1500 \times 300}} = \frac{1700}{2 \times 670.8} = 1.267
$$

Since $\zeta > 1$, the system is **overdamped** — no overshoot, slow response. Reducing $K_p$ would speed up the response but increase steady-state error. This is the classic **speed-vs-accuracy trade-off** in control engineering.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Cruise Control System Characteristics

| Design Parameter | Effect of Increase | Trade-Off |
|---|---|---|
| $K_p \uparrow$ | Faster response, less steady-state error | Increased overshoot, possible oscillation |
| $K_i \uparrow$ | Eliminates steady-state error faster | Increased overshoot, integrator windup risk |
| $K_d \uparrow$ | Better damping, less overshoot | Amplifies sensor noise |
| $T_s \uparrow$ | Less CPU load | Slower control loop, potential aliasing |
| Throttle authority | Better disturbance rejection | Reduced ride comfort (jerky corrections) |
| Sampling frequency | More accurate control | Higher hardware cost |

    ENGAGED --> STANDBY : Driver presses CANCEL
    ENGAGED --> STANDBY : Driver presses BRAKE
    ENGAGED --> STANDBY : Driver presses CLUTCH

    ENGAGED --> FAULT : Sensor plausibility check fails
    OVERRIDE --> ENGAGED : Driver releases accelerator and presses RESUME
    FAULT --> OFF : System requires service

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Electronic Cruise Control. State any two advantages. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Electronic Cruise Control (ECC)** is a closed-loop driver-assistance system that automatically maintains a driver-selected vehicle speed by continuously comparing the actual vehicle speed (from a speed sensor) with the desired set speed and adjusting the throttle opening to eliminate any speed error, **without requiring accelerator pedal input**.

**Advantages (any two):**
1. **Improved fuel efficiency** on highways due to elimination of unnecessary acceleration/deceleration cycles.
2. **Reduced driver fatigue** during long-distance travel.
3. **Better speed limit compliance** — prevents inadvertent over-speeding.
4. **Lower emissions** due to engine operation in the optimal fuel map region.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Open-Loop and Closed-Loop Cruise Control. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Open-Loop Cruise | Closed-Loop Cruise (Electronic) |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | None | Continuous speed feedback |
| Accuracy | Low — affected by grade, wind | High — self-correcting |
| Complexity | Simple throttle map lookup | PID algorithm with ECU |
| Disturbance rejection | Poor | Excellent |
| Modern usage | Not used | Standard in all vehicles |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Adapted for Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an **Electronic Cruise Control system**. Label all sensors, the controller, and the actuator clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **mathematical model** of the longitudinal vehicle dynamics as a first-order plant suitable for PID tuning, and explain why an **integral term is essential** in the cruise control law. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a):

**Block Diagram (must be drawn in the answer book):**

Refer to the ASCII schematic in Section 4.4 of these notes. The expected diagram has the following blocks in a closed loop:

1. **Set-point input** ($v_{set}$)
2. **Summing junction** with negative feedback
3. **PID Controller** block
4. **Throttle Actuator** block
5. **Vehicle Plant** block (engine + drivetrain + vehicle mass)
6. **Speed Sensor** in the feedback path
7. **Disturbance input** entering the plant

**Working Principle (Step-by-step explanation):**

- The driver accelerates the vehicle to the desired cruising speed (e.g., 80 km/h) using the accelerator pedal.
- The driver presses the **SET** switch on the cruise stalk. The ECU reads the current speed from the **VSS** and stores it as $v_{set}$.
- The ECU enters the **ENGAGED** state. The PID algorithm begins executing at the sampling period $T_s$.
- On each cycle, the ECU: (i) reads the VSS pulse count, (ii) computes $v_{actual}$, (iii) computes the error $e = v_{set} - v_{actual}$, (iv) computes the throttle command $u$ using the PID law, (v) outputs a PWM signal to the **Electronic Throttle Body**.
- The throttle body adjusts airflow into the engine, which changes the tractive force.
- The **negative feedback** ensures any deviation from $v_{set}$ is corrected within a few sampling periods.
- The system **disengages** automatically on brake application, clutch application, CANCEL switch, or speed dropping below threshold.

**Mark Distribution (as per KTU pattern):**
- Block diagram: 3 marks
- Working principle explanation: 3 marks
- Component identification: 1 mark

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b):

**Derivation of First-Order Vehicle Plant Model:**

**Step 1 — Newton's Second Law in the longitudinal direction:**

$$
m \frac{dv}{dt} = F_{tractive} - F_{aero} - F_{roll} - F_{grade}
$$

**Step 2 — Express each force:**

- $F_{tractive} = K_t \cdot u$ (throttle-to-torque-to-force linearization)
- $F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 \approx B_{aero} v$ (linearized around operating point)
- $F_{roll} = \mu_r m g$ (constant for flat road)
- $F_{grade} = m g \sin\theta$ (constant for fixed grade)

**Step 3 — Combine and linearize:**

$$
m \frac{dv}{dt} + B_{aero} v = K_t u - F_{roll} - F_{grade}
$$

**Step 4 — Define effective damping $B_{eff} = B_{aero}$ and effective disturbance $d$:**

$$
m \frac{dv}{dt} + B_{eff} v = K_t u - d
$$

**Step 5 — Take the Laplace transform (zero initial conditions):**

$$
m s V(s) + B_{eff} V(s) = K_t U(s) - D(s)
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the transfer function $G_p(s) = V(s)/U(s)$:**

$$
G_p(s) = \frac{V(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{K_t}{m s + B_{eff}}
$$

This is a **first-order plant** with DC gain $K_t/B_{eff}$ and time constant $\tau = m/B_{eff}$.

**Why Integral Action is Essential:**

Consider the steady-state response to a step input with **P-only control**. Using the Final Value Theorem:

$$
v_{actual}(\infty) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{K_p K_t}{m s + B_{eff} + K_p K_t} \cdot \frac{V_{set}}{s} = \frac{K_p K_t}{B_{eff} + K_p K_t} V_{set}
$$

The steady-state error is:

$$
e_{ss} = \frac{B_{eff}}{B_{eff} + K_p K_t} V_{set} \neq 0
$$

With a **PI controller** $G_c(s) = K_p + K_i/s$, the open-loop transfer function gains a pole at the origin:

$$
L(s) = \frac{K_t(K_p s + K_i)}{s(m s + B_{eff})}
$$

The new steady-state value becomes:

$$
v_{actual}(\infty) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{L(s)}{1 + L(s)} \cdot \frac{V_{set}}{s} = V_{set}
$$

Hence the steady-state error becomes **exactly zero**. The integral term provides **infinite DC gain**, which is mathematically necessary to track a constant set-point with zero error in the presence of constant disturbances (like rolling resistance on flat ground or a constant grade).

**Mark Distribution:**
- Vehicle dynamics equation: 2 marks
- Linearization and Laplace transform: 2 marks
- Transfer function: 1 mark
- PI eliminates steady-state error proof: 2 marks

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Adapted for Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **PID control law** as applied to an Electronic Cruise Control system. With suitable expressions, describe the contribution of the **Proportional, Integral, and Derivative** terms. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle of mass **1500 kg** cruises on a flat road with a desired speed of **90 km/h**. The aerodynamic drag coefficient is $C_d A = 0.65$ m², rolling resistance coefficient $\mu_r = 0.013$, and air density $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³. Calculate the **total resistance force**, the **required tractive force**, and the **steady-state speed error** if a P-controller with $K_p = 3$ %/(km/h) and throttle gain $K_t = 250$ N/% is used. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a):

**The PID Control Law for Cruise Control:**

The standard parallel-form PID controller output is:

$$
u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

In the discrete domain (executed in the ECU):

$$
u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i T_s \sum_{i=0}^{k} e[i] + \frac{K_d}{T_s}\big(e[k] - e[k-1]\big)
$$

**Role of Each Term:**

**Proportional Term ($K_p e$):**
- Provides **immediate corrective action** proportional to the current error.
- If the vehicle is 4 km/h below set speed, the P-term commands $4 \times K_p$ percent throttle.
- **Limitation:** Cannot eliminate steady-state error; high $K_p$ causes oscillation.

**Integral Term ($K_i \int e \, dt$):**
- Accumulates **all past errors**.
- If a long incline keeps the vehicle 2 km/h below set speed for an extended time, the integral builds up and progressively increases the throttle.
- **Benefit:** Guarantees **zero steady-state error** in steady-state.
- **Risk:** Integrator windup during large transients; requires anti-windup logic.

**Derivative Term ($K_d \, de/dt$):**
- Reacts to the **rate of change** of error, not the error itself.
- Acts as a **predictor** — if the error is rapidly increasing (vehicle decelerating), the D-term preemptively opens the throttle.
- **Benefit:** Improves damping, reduces overshoot, enhances transient response.
- **Risk:** Amplifies high-frequency sensor noise; usually requires a low-pass filter.

**Tuning Notes for KTU:**

Common Ziegler-Nichols or Tyreus-Luyben tuning gives starting values like $K_p \approx 5$, $K_i \approx 0.5$, $K_d \approx 0.1$ for a typical sedan. Final values are tuned on a test track.

**Mark Distribution:**
- PID equation (both forms): 2 marks
- P-term explanation: 1.5 marks
- I-term explanation: 1.5 marks
- D-term explanation: 1.5 marks
- Tuning context: 0.5 marks

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b):

**Given:**
- $m = 1500$ kg
- $v_{set} = 90$ km/h $= 25$ m/s
- $C_d A = 0.65$ m²
- $\mu_r = 0.013$
- $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³
- $K_p = 3$ %/(km/h)
- $K_t = 250$ N/%

**Step 1 — Aerodynamic Drag Force:**

$$
F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2
$$

$$
F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.65 \times (25)^2
$$

$$
F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.65 \times 625
$$

$$
F_{aero} = 243.75 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Rolling Resistance Force:**

$$
F_{roll} = \mu_r \, m \, g = 0.013 \times 1500 \times 9.81
$$

$$
F_{roll} = 191.3 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Resistance Force:**

$$
F_{resist} = F_{aero} + F_{roll} = 243.75 + 191.3 = 435.05 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Required Tractive Force (steady state on flat road):**

At steady state, $\frac{dv}{dt} = 0$, so:

$$
F_{tractive} = F_{resist} = 435.05 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Steady-State Speed Error:**

Using the formula derived in Section 3.1:

$$
e_{ss} = \frac{F_{resist}}{K_t K_p + B_{aero}}
$$

Compute aerodynamic damping:

$$
B_{aero} = \rho C_d A v_{set} = 1.2 \times 0.65 \times 25 = 19.5 \text{ N·s/m}
$$

Compute loop gain (convert $K_p$ to consistent units: 1 km/h = 0.2778 m/s):

$$
K_t K_p = 250 \times 3 = 750 \text{ N/(km/h)} = \frac{750}{0.2778} = 2700 \text{ N/(m/s)}
$$

Total denominator:

$$
K_t K_p + B_{aero} = 2700 + 19.5 = 2719.5 \text{ N/(m/s)}
$$

Steady-state error:

$$
e_{ss} = \frac{435.05}{2719.5} = 0.160 \text{ m/s}
$$

Converting to km/h:

$$
e_{ss} = 0.160 \times 3.6 = 0.576 \text{ km/h}
$$

**Result Summary:**
| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Aerodynamic drag | 243.75 N |
| Rolling resistance | 191.3 N |
| Total resistance | 435.05 N |
| Required tractive force | 435.05 N |
| Steady-state speed error | **0.576 km/h** |

**Mark Distribution:**
- Correct force calculations: 3 marks
- Required tractive force: 1 mark
- Steady-state error derivation: 2 marks
- Final numerical answer with units: 1 mark

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in Cruise Control Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to mention "closed-loop" or "negative feedback"** in the definition. Examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for these keywords. A generic definition like "a system that maintains constant speed" is incomplete and will lose marks.
>
> 2. **Confusing "speed error" with "speed"**. The error is a *difference*, not an absolute quantity. Writing $e(t) = v$ instead of $e(t) = v_{set} - v_{actual}$ loses 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Mixing up the sign of feedback**. The summing junction in the block diagram **must** show a negative sign on the feedback path. Drawing a positive feedback loop will be marked wrong.
>
> 4. **Skipping units in numerical problems**. Always write **N** for force, **km/h** or **m/s** for speed, and **kg** for mass. A correct number with wrong or missing units loses 0.5–1 mark per instance.
>
> 5. **Not mentioning safety interlocks**. Any cruise control explanation that omits the brake/clutch disengagement logic is considered an incomplete industrial answer and loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 6. **Using Python or code in a non-CS paper**. KTU valuation strictly prohibits code snippets in Mechanical/Automobile engineering papers. Show all derivations in mathematical form.
>
> 7. **Confusing PID with P-only**. A common trap: examiners ask "Why is integral action needed?" and students repeat the formula without the **Final Value Theorem proof**. The proof is what carries the marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullet List — Read Before the Exam**
>
> ✅ Electronic Cruise Control is a **closed-loop, negative-feedback** driver assistance system.
>
> ✅ The core function is to maintain a **set speed** commanded by the driver without accelerator pedal input.
>
> ✅ Main subsystems: **Input (sensors + switches) → Processing (ECU + PID) → Actuation (throttle) → Plant (vehicle)**.
>
> ✅ The **speed error** is defined as $e(t) = v_{set} - v_{actual}(t)$.
>
> ✅ The standard control law is **PID**: $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$.
>
> ✅ The **P-term** provides immediate response but cannot eliminate steady-state error.
>
> ✅ The **I-term** eliminates steady-state error by providing infinite DC gain; risk of **integrator windup** requires anti-windup logic.
>
> ✅ The **D-term** predicts future error and damps overshoot; sensitive to sensor noise.
>
> ✅ Vehicle plant (linearized) is a **first-order system**: $G_p(s) = \frac{K_t}{ms + B_{eff}}$.
>
> ✅ Key resistive forces: **Aerodynamic drag** $F_{aero} = \frac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$, **Rolling resistance** $F_{roll} = \mu_r m g$, **Grade resistance** $F_{grade} = mg\sin\theta$.
>
> ✅ Steady-state error with P-only: $e_{ss} = \frac{B_{eff}}{B_{eff} + K_p K_t} V_{set} \neq 0$.
>
> ✅ Steady-state error with PI: **exactly zero** (because of the pole at the origin).
>
> ✅ **Safety interlocks**: brake pedal, clutch pedal, CANCEL switch, low-speed cutoff, sensor plausibility check.
>
> ✅ Typical operating speed range: **40–200 km/h**.
>
> ✅ Sampling frequency: **10–100 Hz** in modern ECUs.
>
> ✅ The system uses **drive-by-wire throttle** in modern implementations.
>
> ✅ Cruise control is the **precursor** to Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC), which adds radar/lidar sensing.
>
> ✅ Benefits: **fuel economy (5–14% savings)**, **driver fatigue reduction**, **emissions reduction**, **speed compliance**.
>
> ✅ The cruise control state machine has states: **OFF, STANDBY, ENGAGED, OVERRIDE, FAULT**.
>
> ✅ Discrete PID uses **velocity (incremental) form** for automatic anti-windup and bumpless transfer.
>
> ✅ **Routh-Hurwitz criterion** confirms stability when all first-column entries of the Routh array are positive.
>
> ✅ Damping ratio $\zeta > 1$ implies overdamped (no overshoot); $\zeta = 1$ critically damped; $\zeta < 1$ underdamped (oscillatory).
>
> ✅ **Exam mantra:** Always end any cruise control answer with the phrase *"ensuring driver safety through fail-safe disengagement logic."* This single sentence can recover 1–2 borderline marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chassis Control System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT612 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Chassis Control System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Chassis Control System — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Chassis Control System** in a modern automobile refers to an integrated network of electro-mechanical, hydraulic, and electronic subsystems that actively or passively regulate the vehicle's longitudinal (braking/traction), lateral (steering/stability), and vertical (suspension/ride) dynamics to enhance **safety, comfort, handling, and performance**. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (OEMUT612), this umbrella domain encompasses the coordinated functioning of the **braking, steering, suspension, and powertrain-tire interaction** sub-systems, governed by an electronic control unit (ECU) that processes signals from sensors and actuates corrective torques/forces in real time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A Chassis Control System is *not* a single component — it is a **system-of-systems**. The KTU examiner expects you to treat it as the integration of ABS, TCS, ESC, Active Suspension, EPS/EHPS, and Steer-by-Wire under a common supervisory architecture (often called Vehicle Dynamics Control — VDC).

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context:** Traditional vehicles used **Passive Chassis Systems** where components (springs, dampers, brake lines) had fixed characteristics. Modern vehicles use **Active/Semi-Active Chassis Control** where the ECU continuously modifies system behavior based on driver inputs and road conditions.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of the chassis control system as the "Reflexes and Balance Centre" of the human body.**

When you walk on a slippery floor, three things happen automatically:
- Your **legs (suspension)** adjust stiffness to keep you upright (vertical control).
- Your **arms (steering)** make quick corrective gestures to maintain direction (lateral control).
- Your **feet (braking/traction)** modulate grip to prevent slipping (longitudinal control).

All of this is coordinated by your **cerebellum (the VDC/ESC ECU)** without you consciously thinking. Similarly, a Chassis Control System monitors the **driver's intent** (steering angle, brake pedal, throttle), the **vehicle's state** (yaw rate, lateral acceleration, wheel speed), and the **road's response** (slip, friction) — then commands the brakes, steering, suspension, and engine to keep the car stable.

## 1.3 The Three Axes of Vehicle Dynamics Control

| Axis | Vehicle Motion | Primary Subsystems | Goal |
|------|---------------|--------------------|------|
| **Longitudinal (X-axis)** | Forward/Backward motion, braking | ABS, TCS, EBD, AEB | Prevent wheel lock, maintain traction |
| **Lateral (Y-axis)** | Cornering, side-slip | ESC/ESP, Active Steering, AFS | Prevent skid, maintain intended path |
| **Vertical (Z-axis)** | Bounce, pitch, roll | Active/Semi-Active Suspension | Ride comfort + handling balance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric for KTU:** The integration is typically expressed in terms of the **friction circle (or "g-g" diagram)** which shows the limit of available tire force. KTU questions often test whether you can map a particular subsystem (ABS, ESC, etc.) to the correct region of the friction circle.

## 1.4 High-Yield Subsystem Inventory (KTU Examiner's Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize this list — KTU Part A questions (3 marks) are frequently drawn directly from this taxonomy.

1. **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)** — prevents wheel lock during hard braking.
2. **Electronic Brake-force Distribution (EBD)** — varies brake force between wheels.
3. **Traction Control System (TCS / ASR)** — prevents wheel spin during acceleration.
4. **Electronic Stability Program (ESP / ESC / VDC)** — corrects understeer/oversteer.
5. **Brake Assist System (BAS)** — detects emergency braking and amplifies pedal force.
6. **Hill-Start Assist Control (HAC)** — prevents rollback on inclines.
7. **Electronic Power Steering (EPS) / Electro-Hydraulic Power Steering (EHPS)** — assists steering effort.
8. **Active/Semi-Active Suspension** — variable damping/spring rate.
9. **Active Front Steering (AFS) / Rear-Wheel Steering (RWS)** — variable steering ratio.
10. **Steer-by-Wire (SbW) / Drive-by-Wire / Brake-by-Wire / Throttle-by-Wire** — X-by-Wire technologies.
11. **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) / Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB)** — longitudinal automation.
12. **Roll Stability Control (RSC)** — prevents rollover in tall vehicles.
13. **Tyre Pressure Monitoring System (TPMS)** — indirect chassis input.
14. **Active Damping System (ADS)** — real-time shock absorber control.
15. **Continuously Controlled Damping (CCD)** — semi-active suspension variant.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Chassis Control Systems

### 2.1.1 By Mode of Intervention

| Category | Description | Energy Source | KTU Example |
|----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| **Passive** | Fixed characteristics, no external energy | None (mechanical) | Conventional spring-damper |
| **Semi-Active** | Adjustable damping/steering, no major external energy | Small (solenoid/motor) | CDC (Continuously Controlled Damping) |
| **Active** | Force-generating actuators add energy | Significant (hydraulic/electric) | Active Suspension, AFS, SbW |
| **By-Wire** | Mechanical linkage replaced by electrical signals | ECU + actuator | Steer-by-Wire, Brake-by-Wire |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "compare" or "distinguish," always present a **table with at least 3 differentiating parameters** (response time, energy, cost, complexity).

## 2.2 The Tire-Road Interaction — Foundation of Chassis Control

Every chassis control command ultimately manipulates the **tire force**. The tire-road friction coefficient $\mu$ is the binding constraint.

### 2.2.1 Slip Ratio (Longitudinal)

For braking (negative slip) and traction (positive slip):

$$\lambda = \frac{v_{\text{wheel}} - v_{\text{vehicle}}}{v_{\text{vehicle}}}$$

where:
- $v_{\text{wheel}} = \omega_{\text{wheel}} \cdot r_{\text{eff}}$ (linear velocity of wheel circumference)
- $v_{\text{vehicle}}$ = true vehicle speed measured at the wheel that is not slipping
- $r_{\text{eff}}$ = effective tire rolling radius
- $\omega_{\text{wheel}}$ = angular velocity of the wheel

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention:** In many KTU textbooks, slip ratio is defined as $\lambda = \frac{\omega r - v}{v}$ for traction and $\lambda = \frac{v - \omega r}{v}$ for braking — both forms are accepted; the magnitude is what matters.

**Optimal slip ratio for maximum tire force:** typically $\lambda_{\text{opt}} \approx 0.08$ to $0.15$ for dry asphalt.

### 2.2.2 Slip Angle (Lateral)

$$\alpha = \delta - \theta_v$$

where $\delta$ is the steering angle and $\theta_v$ is the vehicle heading (velocity vector angle). For small angles:

$$\alpha \approx \frac{v_y}{v_x} - \delta$$

where $v_y$ and $v_x$ are lateral and longitudinal velocities at the tire contact patch.

### 2.2.3 Tire Force Generation (Linear Region)

In the linear region of tire behavior:

$$F_x = C_x \cdot \lambda, \qquad F_y = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha$$

where:
- $C_x$ = longitudinal tire stiffness (N per unit slip) $\approx 100{,}000 \text{ N}$
- $C_\alpha$ = cornering stiffness (N/rad) $\approx 80{,}000 \text{ N/rad}$

In the **saturated (nonlinear) region**, force is bounded by the friction circle:

$$F_x^2 + F_y^2 \le (\mu F_z)^2$$

where $F_z$ is the vertical load on the tire.

## 2.3 ABS — Anti-lock Braking System

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle

The ABS controller runs a closed-loop algorithm (typically a **slip-cycle controller** or **threshold controller**):
1. **Pressure Hold** — when slip exceeds optimal band.
2. **Pressure Release** — when slip becomes too high (wheel about to lock).
3. **Pressure Re-apply** — when wheel re-accelerates.

**Cycle frequency:** typically **15–25 Hz** (modern systems reach 100 Hz).

### 2.3.2 Critical Equations

The wheel dynamics are governed by:

$$J_w \cdot \dot{\omega}_w = T_{\text{drive}} - T_{\text{brake}} - F_x \cdot r_{\text{eff}}$$

The vehicle deceleration:

$$m \cdot \dot{v} = -F_{x,\text{total}} = -F_{\text{brake}} - F_{\text{aero}} - F_{\text{roll}}$$

## 2.4 Electronic Stability Control (ESC / ESP / VDC)

ESC uses a **reference model** approach. It compares the driver's intended path (from steering angle $\delta$) with the actual vehicle response (from yaw rate $\dot\psi$ and lateral acceleration $a_y$).

**Bicycle Model Equations (2-DOF):**

$$m v (\dot{\beta} + \dot{\psi}) = F_{yf} \cos\delta + F_{yr}$$

$$I_z \ddot{\psi} = a F_{yf} \cos\delta - b F_{yr}$$

where:
- $m$ = vehicle mass
- $v$ = longitudinal velocity
- $\beta$ = sideslip angle at CG
- $\psi$ = yaw angle
- $I_z$ = yaw moment of inertia
- $a, b$ = distances from CG to front and rear axles
- $F_{yf}, F_{yr}$ = front/rear lateral tire forces
- $\delta$ = steering angle

**Yaw rate error:**

$$\Delta\dot{\psi} = \dot{\psi}_{\text{measured}} - \dot{\psi}_{\text{desired}}$$

If $\vert \Delta\dot{\psi} \vert > \epsilon$ (threshold ~3–5°/s), ESC applies **selective braking** to generate a correcting yaw moment.

## 2.5 Active Suspension — Quarter Car Model

The classic **quarter-car model** (2-DOF) is essential for KTU derivations.

Equations of motion (sprung mass $m_s$ and unsprung mass $m_u$):

$$m_s \ddot{x}_s = -k_s (x_s - x_u) - c_s (\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u) + F_{\text{active}}$$

$$m_u \ddot{x}_u = k_s (x_s - x_u) + c_s (\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u) - k_t (x_u - x_r) - F_{\text{active}}$$

where:
- $x_s$ = sprung mass displacement
- $x_u$ = unsprung mass displacement
- $x_r$ = road input
- $k_s$ = suspension spring stiffness
- $c_s$ = suspension damping coefficient
- $k_t$ = tire stiffness
- $F_{\text{active}}$ = active force actuator

**Natural frequency (heave mode):**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k_s}{m_s}}$$

**Damping ratio:**

$$\zeta = \frac{c_s}{2\sqrt{k_s m_s}}$$

**Critical damping:**

$$c_{c} = 2 \sqrt{k_s m_s}$$

## 2.6 Steer-by-Wire (SbW)

Replaces the mechanical column with:
1. **Steering Angle Sensor** at the handwheel.
2. **ECU** that computes desired road-wheel angle.
3. **Feedback Actuator** at the handwheel (variable ratio + haptic feedback).
4. **Road-Wheel Actuator** (motor + steering rack).

**Variable steering ratio:**

$$\delta_{fw} = i_s \cdot \delta_{hw} + f(v)$$

where $i_s$ is the speed-dependent ratio and $f(v)$ is the variable correction function (typically decreases $i_s$ at high speed for stability, increases at low speed for maneuverability).

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | KTU Application |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega r - v}{v}$ | Longitudinal slip ratio | dimensionless | ABS, TCS |
| 2 | $\alpha = \delta - \beta - \dfrac{a \dot\psi}{v}$ | Tire slip angle (linear bicycle) | rad | ESC, AFS |
| 3 | $F_x = C_x \lambda$, $F_y = C_\alpha \alpha$ | Linear tire forces | N | All subsystems |
| 4 | $F_x^2 + F_y^2 \le (\mu F_z)^2$ | Friction circle constraint | N² | Stability limits |
| 5 | $J_w \dot\omega = T_{\text{engine}} - T_{\text{brake}} - F_x r$ | Wheel rotational dynamics | N·m | ABS, TCS |
| 6 | $m v (\dot\beta + \dot\psi) = F_{yf} \cos\delta + F_{yr}$ | Lateral force balance | N | ESC, AFS |
| 7 | $I_z \ddot\psi = a F_{yf} \cos\delta - b F_{yr} + M_z$ | Yaw moment balance | N·m | ESC, RSC |
| 8 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_s}{m_s}}$ | Suspension natural frequency | rad/s | Ride analysis |
| 9 | $\zeta = \dfrac{c_s}{2\sqrt{k_s m_s}}$ | Damping ratio | dimensionless | Ride comfort |
| 10 | $\delta_{fw} = i_s(v) \cdot \delta_{hw}$ | Variable steering ratio | rad | AFS, SbW |
| 11 | $\dot\psi_{\text{desired}} = \dfrac{v \cdot \delta}{L + K v^2}$ | Steady-state cornering yaw rate | rad/s | ESC reference model |
| 12 | $P_{\text{brake,max}} = \mu m g$ | Maximum brake deceleration | m/s² | ABS threshold |
| 13 | $\Delta\dot\psi = \dot\psi_{\text{meas}} - \dot\psi_{\text{des}}$ | Yaw rate error | rad/s | ESC control law |
| 14 | $H_{\infty}$ or LQR cost: $J = \int (x^T Q x + u^T R u) dt$ | Optimal control index | varies | Active suspension, SbW |
| 15 | $K_{us} = \dfrac{v^2}{g R} - 1$ | Understeer gradient (K) | rad/g | ESC tuning, AFS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** In the formula table above, the slip ratio and slip angle equations are the most frequently asked. Always write them with proper sign convention. For the bicycle model, the 2-DOF form (lateral translation + yaw rotation) is the *expected* form. Do not use full 7-DOF models unless the question specifically asks for them.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Subsystem | Industrial Application | Modern Vehicle Segment |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| ABS | Mandatory in most countries (post-2004 EU, 2013 India) | All cars, two-wheelers (>125cc) |
| ESC | Mandatory in EU (2014), USA (2012), India (2022–2024) | All passenger vehicles |
| Active Suspension | Luxury segment | Mercedes ABC, Audi Adaptive Air, Bose |
| Steer-by-Wire | Premium + EV segment | Infiniti Q50, Tesla Cybertruck (RWS) |
| TPMS | Mandatory in many countries | All modern vehicles |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Quarter-Car Suspension Natural Frequency and Damping Ratio

### 3.1.1 Free-Body Setup

Consider a single corner of the vehicle (a "quarter car") modeled as two lumped masses connected by a spring-damper, with the road as the input.

**Assumptions (standard for KTU):**
- The vehicle travels at a constant forward speed over a smooth road.
- The sprung mass $m_s$ represents the body (1/4 of the total).
- The unsprung mass $m_u$ represents the wheel + tire + brake assembly.
- The tire is modeled as a stiff spring $k_t$ with no damping.
- The suspension has linear spring $k_s$ and viscous damper $c_s$ in parallel.

### 3.1.2 Equations of Motion (Derivation Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Free body of the sprung mass:**

Forces on $m_s$:
- Spring force pulling down (when $x_s > x_u$): $-k_s(x_s - x_u)$
- Damper force: $-c_s(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u)$
- Inertia: $m_s \ddot{x}_s$

Newton's second law:

$$m_s \ddot{x}_s = -k_s (x_s - x_u) - c_s (\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u)$$

Rearranged:

$$m_s \ddot{x}_s + c_s \dot{x}_s - c_s \dot{x}_u + k_s x_s - k_s x_u = 0$$

**Step 2 — Free body of the unsprung mass:**

Forces on $m_u$:
- Reaction from suspension spring: $+k_s (x_s - x_u)$
- Reaction from suspension damper: $+c_s (\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u)$
- Tire spring force (road contact): $-k_t (x_u - x_r)$
- Inertia: $m_u \ddot{x}_u$

Newton's second law:

$$m_u \ddot{x}_u = k_s (x_s - x_u) + c_s (\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u) - k_t (x_u - x_r)$$

Rearranged:

$$m_u \ddot{x}_u - c_s \dot{x}_s + c_s \dot{x}_u - k_s x_s + (k_s + k_t) x_u = k_t x_r$$

**Step 3 — State-space matrix form:**

Define the state vector:

$$\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} x_s \\ x_u \\ \dot{x}_s \\ \dot{x}_u \end{bmatrix}$$

The system in matrix form (using $x_r = 0$ for natural frequency analysis):

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_s \\ \dot{x}_u \\ \ddot{x}_s \\ \ddot{x}_u \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -\frac{k_s}{m_s} & \frac{k_s}{m_s} & -\frac{c_s}{m_s} & \frac{c_s}{m_s} \\ \frac{k_s}{m_u} & -\frac{k_s + k_t}{m_u} & \frac{c_s}{m_u} & -\frac{c_s}{m_u} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_s \\ x_u \\ \dot{x}_s \\ \dot{x}_u \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ \frac{k_t}{m_u} \end{bmatrix} x_r
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Characteristic equation (no road input, sprung mass only, $m_u \to 0$ simplification):**

For a simplified body-bounce mode (single-DOF, ignoring unsprung mass):

$$m_s \ddot{x} + c_s \dot{x} + k_s x = 0$$

The characteristic equation is:

$$m_s s^2 + c_s s + k_s = 0$$

Dividing by $m_s$:

$$s^2 + \frac{c_s}{m_s} s + \frac{k_s}{m_s} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with the standard second-order form:**

Standard form: $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$

By direct comparison:

$$\omega_n^2 = \frac{k_s}{m_s} \implies \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k_s}{m_s}}$$

$$2\zeta\omega_n = \frac{c_s}{m_s} \implies \zeta = \frac{c_s}{2\sqrt{k_s m_s}}$$

**Step 6 — Typical numerical example for a passenger car:**

Let $m_s = 350 \text{ kg}$ (quarter car), $k_s = 18{,}000 \text{ N/m}$, $c_s = 1{,}200 \text{ N·s/m}$.

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{18000}{350}} = \sqrt{51.43} \approx 7.17 \text{ rad/s}$$

Frequency in Hz:

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{7.17}{6.283} \approx 1.14 \text{ Hz}$$

Damping ratio:

$$\zeta = \frac{1200}{2 \sqrt{18000 \times 350}} = \frac{1200}{2 \times 2510} \approx 0.239$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment:** $\zeta \approx 0.2$ to $0.4$ is the typical target for a passenger car — this balances ride comfort (low $\zeta$ preferred for isolation) and road holding (high $\zeta$ preferred to prevent resonant overshoot). Active systems tune this in real time.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Slip Ratio and the "Magic" of ABS

### 3.2.1 Setup

Consider a single wheel of mass $J_w$ and effective rolling radius $r$. The vehicle of mass $m$ is moving at $v$. The brake applies a torque $T_b$.

**Step 1 — Velocity definitions:**

Wheel angular velocity: $\omega_w$ (rad/s)
Linear velocity at tire circumference: $v_w = \omega_w r$
True vehicle speed (assumed equal to a non-braked reference wheel): $v$

**Step 2 — Slip ratio definition:**

For a braking maneuver, the wheel slows down, so $v_w < v$:

$$\lambda = \frac{v - v_w}{v} = \frac{v - \omega_w r}{v}$$

Alternatively, in KTU textbook form, the "slip" $s$ is defined as:

$$s = 1 - \frac{\omega_w r}{v}$$

For pure rolling, $\omega_w r = v$, so $s = 0$.
For full lock, $\omega_w = 0$, so $s = 1$.

**Step 3 — Tire friction force vs. slip curve:**

Empirically, the friction coefficient $\mu$ vs. slip $\lambda$ follows:

$$\mu(\lambda) = \mu_{\text{peak}} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\lambda_{\text{opt}}} \quad \text{for } \lambda < \lambda_{\text{opt}}$$

$$\mu(\lambda) = \mu_{\text{peak}} \cdot \left[ 1 - \frac{(\lambda - \lambda_{\text{opt}})(1 - \mu_{\text{peak}}/\mu_{\text{slide}})}{\lambda_{\text{max}} - \lambda_{\text{opt}}} \right] \quad \text{for } \lambda \ge \lambda_{\text{opt}}$$

The longitudinal tire force is:

$$F_x = \mu(\lambda) \cdot F_z$$

**Step 4 — Equation of motion for the wheel:**

Torque balance on the wheel:

$$J_w \dot{\omega}_w = -T_b - F_x \cdot r$$

(negative because $F_x$ opposes motion during braking)

**Step 5 — Equation of motion for the vehicle:**

$$m \dot{v} = -F_x$$

**Step 6 — Combining:**

Taking the derivative of slip:

$$\dot{s} = -\frac{r \dot{\omega}_w}{v} + \frac{\omega_w r \dot{v}}{v^2} \approx -\frac{r \dot{\omega}_w}{v} \quad (\text{assuming } \dot{v} \text{ is small})$$

Substituting the wheel equation:

$$\dot{s} = -\frac{r}{J_w v} \left( -T_b - F_x r \right) = \frac{r T_b}{J_w v} + \frac{F_x r^2}{J_w v}$$

**Step 7 — Steady-state analysis:**

At steady state, $\dot{s} = 0$:

$$0 = \frac{r T_b}{J_w v} + \frac{F_x r^2}{J_w v}$$

$$F_x = -\frac{T_b}{r}$$

This simply restates that the brake torque is balanced by the tire force — useful for design sizing.

**Step 8 — The ABS threshold:**

The controller targets $\lambda \in [\lambda_{\text{min}}, \lambda_{\text{max}}]$, typically [0.08, 0.30]. The control law:

$$\text{If } \lambda < \lambda_{\text{min}} \Rightarrow \text{Increase } P_{\text{brake}}$$

$$\text{If } \lambda > \lambda_{\text{max}} \Rightarrow \text{Release } P_{\text{brake}}$$

This forms a **bang-bang** or **sliding-mode** controller.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Bicycle Model Steady-State Yaw Rate (ESC Reference)

### 3.3.1 Steady-State Cornering

For a vehicle in steady cornering at constant radius $R$ and speed $v$, the yaw rate is:

$$\dot{\psi}_{\text{ss}} = \frac{v}{R}$$

For small slip angles, the linear tire model gives:

$$F_{yf} = C_{\alpha f} \alpha_f, \qquad F_{yr} = C_{\alpha r} \alpha_r$$

with slip angles (small-angle approximation):

$$\alpha_f = \delta - \frac{a \dot\psi}{v} - \beta, \qquad \alpha_r = \frac{b \dot\psi}{v} - \beta$$

### 3.3.2 Setting $\ddot\psi = 0$, $\dot\beta = 0$ (steady state)

The lateral force equation reduces to:

$$0 = F_{yf} + F_{yr}$$

The yaw moment equation reduces to:

$$0 = a F_{yf} - b F_{yr}$$

Substituting the linear tire model and solving for $\dot\psi$ yields the classic result:

$$\dot\psi_{\text{ss}} = \frac{v \cdot \delta}{L + K v^2}$$

where:
- $L = a + b$ = wheelbase
- $K = \dfrac{m b}{C_{\alpha r}} - \dfrac{m a}{C_{\alpha f}}$ = understeer gradient

**Step-by-step algebra:**

Starting from the yaw equation:

$$0 = a C_{\alpha f} \left( \delta - \frac{a \dot\psi}{v} - \beta \right) - b C_{\alpha r} \left( \frac{b \dot\psi}{v} - \beta \right)$$

Rearranging and using the lateral force equation $0 = C_{\alpha f}(\delta - \frac{a \dot\psi}{v} - \beta) + C_{\alpha r}(\frac{b \dot\psi}{v} - \beta)$ to eliminate $\beta$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot\psi_{\text{ss}} = \frac{v \delta / L}{1 + K v^2 / L}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Ackermann-like** steady-state formula.

### 3.3.3 ESC Logic

The ESC computes:

$$\dot\psi_{\text{desired}} = \frac{v \cdot \delta}{L + K v^2}$$

and compares with measured yaw rate from the gyroscope. The **yaw rate error** $\Delta\dot\psi$ drives the brake actuator to apply a corrective yaw moment:

$$M_z = K_p \cdot \Delta\dot\psi + K_i \int \Delta\dot\psi \, dt$$

This moment is generated by selectively braking one wheel (typically the outer-front for understeer, or outer-rear for oversteer — a common KTU exam question).

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Subsystem | Control Variable | Actuator | Sensor Inputs | Response Time | Key Limitation |
|-----------|------------------|----------|---------------|---------------|----------------|
| **ABS** | Wheel slip $\lambda$ | Hydraulic modulator | Wheel-speed sensors (4) | ~10–25 ms | Pulsation feel at pedal |
| **TCS** | Wheel slip $\lambda$ (drive) | Engine torque + brake | Wheel speed, throttle | ~50 ms | Power loss during intervention |
| **EBD** | Wheel load $F_z$ | Brake pressure | Wheel speed (decel) | ~50 ms | Depends on ABS hardware |
| **ESC/ESP** | Yaw rate $\dot\psi$, sideslip $\beta$ | Selective brake + engine cut | Yaw gyro, lateral accel, steering angle | ~20–50 ms | Cannot overcome physics |
| **Active Suspension** | $\zeta, k_s$ (variable) | Hydraulic / electromagnetic actuator | Body accel, wheel-height | ~5–20 ms | Cost, energy consumption |
| **EPS** | Steering torque | Electric motor on column | Torque sensor, angle, speed | ~10 ms | Torque feel fidelity |
| **AFS / SbW** | Front-wheel angle $\delta_{fw}$ | Steering actuator | Steering angle, speed, yaw | ~20 ms | Haptic feedback design |
| **AEB** | Time-to-collision | Full brake | Radar, camera, lidar | ~50–100 ms | Sensor false positives |
| **RSC** | Roll angle $\phi$ | Brake + engine | Yaw, lateral accel, steering | ~30 ms | Tall vehicle inertia |
| **TPMS** | Tire pressure $P$ | None (sensor only) | Pressure sensor in tire | ~minute | Battery life (5–10 yr) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examination Use:** This matrix is *gold* for Part B 14-mark questions. Pick any two subsystems and use this table structure to build a 14-mark comparison answer.

## 4.2 Overall Chassis Control System Architecture (System-of-Systems)

flowchart TB
    driver[DRIVER INPUTS STEERING THROTTLE BRAKE PEDAL]

    vdc --> abs

```

## 4.3 ABS Closed-Loop Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    brakePedal[BRAKE PEDAL PRESSURE]
    speedCalc[SLIP RATIO LAMBDA CALCULATOR]

    comparator[SLIP COMPARATOR LAMBDA VS TARGET BAND]
    controller[ABS ECU PRESSURE DECISION LOGIC]

    dumpValve[RELEASE VALVE]

    brakeCyl[BRAKE CYLINDER]
    wheelResponse[WHEEL DECELERATION RESPONSE]

    brakePedal --> controller
    wheelSpeed --> speedCalc
    refSpeed --> speedCalc
    speedCalc --> comparator
    comparator --> controller
    controller --> holdValve
    controller --> dumpValve
    controller --> pump
    holdValve --> brakeCyl
    pump --> brakeCyl
    dumpValve --> brakeCyl
    brakeCyl --> wheelResponse
    wheelResponse --> wheelSpeed
```

## 4.4 Electronic Stability Control (ESC) Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start[START ESC CYCLE 20 MS]
    readSensors[READ STEERING ANGLE YAW RATE LATERAL ACCEL WHEEL SPEEDS]
    calcDesired[CALCULATE DESIRED YAW RATE FROM BICYCLE MODEL]
    calcMeasured[READ MEASURED YAW RATE]
    errorCalc[ERROR EQUALS DESIRED MINUS MEASURED]
    decision{ERROR EXCEEDS THRESHOLD}
    understeer[UNDERSTEER DETECTED ACT BRAKE INNER REAR]
    oversteer[OVERSTEER DETECTED ACT BRAKE OUTER FRONT]
    bothSafe[NO INTERVENTION RETURN]
    applyBrake[APPLY SELECTIVE BRAKE PRESSURE]
    cutTorque[REQUEST ENGINE TORQUE REDUCTION]
    endnode[CYCLE ENDS 20 MS]

    start --> readSensors
    readSensors --> calcDesired
    readSensors --> calcMeasured
    calcDesired --> errorCalc
    calcMeasured --> errorCalc
    errorCalc --> decision
    decision -->|YES UNDERSTEER| understeer
    decision -->|YES OVERSTEER| oversteer
    decision -->|NO| bothSafe
    understeer --> applyBrake
    oversteer --> applyBrake
    understeer --> cutTorque
    oversteer --> cutTorque
    applyBrake --> endnode
    cutTorque --> endnode
    bothSafe --> endnode
```
