# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern Quick Reference:**
> - Part A: 3 marks each (short answer, ~50–80 words expected)
> - Part B: 14 marks each (full descriptive + numerical, with internal choice between two questions)
> - Bloom's Levels tested: *Understand* (L2), *Apply* (L3), *Analyze* (L4), *Evaluate* (L5)

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define "Chassis Control System" in a modern automobile. List any four major subsystems under it.**

**Model Answer:**

A Chassis Control System is an integrated network of electronic and electro-mechanical subsystems that actively or passively regulate a vehicle's longitudinal, lateral, and vertical dynamics to improve safety, comfort, and handling. It operates under a supervisory Vehicle Dynamics Controller (VDC).

**Four major subsystems:**
1. Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)
2. Electronic Stability Program (ESC/ESP)
3. Traction Control System (TCS)
4. Active/Semi-Active Suspension

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** A complete answer should also mention the integration concept. Just listing 4 systems without the *integration* idea loses 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 (3 marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between Active and Semi-Active suspension systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Semi-Active | Active |
|-----------|-------------|--------|
| Energy | Absorbs only; no external energy addition | Adds external energy to suspension |
| Actuator | Variable damper / MR fluid valve | Hydraulic or electromagnetic force actuator |
| Control | Adjusts damping coefficient continuously | Adjusts both damping and stiffness |
| Power | <50 W per corner | 200–2000 W per corner |
| Cost | Moderate | High |
| Performance | Limited to passive + tunable | Can implement sky-hook, ground-hook control |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - ❌ **Do NOT** write only definitions — KTU wants **diagrams + equations + numerical examples**.
> - ❌ **Do NOT** skip the *control loop* explanation — the feedback nature is what defines "control."
> - ❌ **Do NOT** confuse *slip ratio* (longitudinal) with *slip angle* (lateral). Examiners deduct 2 marks for this.
> - ❌ **Do NOT** use non-standard units. **N, m, m/s, rad, rad/s, Hz** are expected. Mixed units (km/h, rpm) without conversion lose marks.
> - ✅ **DO** draw a labelled block diagram for any subsystem you discuss.
> - ✅ **DO** include at least one numerical example with units.
> - ✅ **DO** mention the **sensor-actuator-ECU triad** for any chassis control system.

---

### PART B — Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the working of the Anti-lock Braking System (ABS). Derive the slip ratio equation and explain how ABS maintains it within the optimum band.**

#### Part (a): Working of ABS with Block Diagram (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition:**
Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) is a closed-loop automotive safety system that prevents the wheels from locking (i.e., from ceasing to rotate) during heavy braking, especially on low-friction surfaces, thereby retaining the driver's steering control.

**Step 2 — Components:**

1. **Wheel Speed Sensors** — typically electromagnetic (variable reluctance) or Hall-effect sensors, one per wheel.
2. **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** — microcontroller running a slip-control algorithm at 50–100 Hz.
3. **Hydraulic Modulator Unit** — contains an inlet valve (hold), outlet valve (dump), and a return pump.
4. **Brake Pressure Lines** — connecting the master cylinder to the wheel cylinders via the modulator.

**Step 3 — Working Principle:**

The ECU continuously calculates the **slip ratio** of each wheel. If the slip ratio exceeds an upper threshold (say 0.30), the ECU closes the inlet valve to **hold** the brake pressure. If the wheel begins to re-accelerate (slip drops below the lower threshold of 0.08), the ECU reopens the valve to **re-apply** pressure. If the wheel still locks, the ECU opens the outlet valve to **release** pressure.

**Step 4 — Block Diagram Reference:**
Refer to Section 4.3 of this note. The flow is:
Wheel Speed Sensor → Slip Ratio Calculator → Comparator → ECU → Hydraulic Modulator → Brake Cylinder → Wheel → (Feedback to) Wheel Speed Sensor.

**Step 5 — Cycle Frequency:**
Modern ABS systems run at **15–25 control cycles per second**, producing the characteristic "pulsing" feel on the brake pedal during emergency stops.

**Step 6 — Advantages:**
- Maintains steerability during heavy braking.
- Shortens stopping distance on split-mu surfaces (e.g., one wheel on ice).
- Prevents tire flat-spotting and lock-up damage.

#### Part (b): Slip Ratio Derivation & Optimum Band (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define variables:**
- $\omega_w$ = angular velocity of the wheel (rad/s)
- $r$ = effective rolling radius of the tire (m)
- $v$ = true linear speed of the vehicle (m/s), measured from a non-locked reference wheel
- $v_w = \omega_w r$ = linear speed at the tire contact patch

**Step 2 — Define slip ratio (braking case):**

During braking, $v_w < v$ because the wheel decelerates faster than the vehicle. Slip ratio is defined as:

$$\lambda = \frac{v - v_w}{v} = \frac{v - \omega_w r}{v}$$

**Step 3 — Boundary cases:**
- Pure rolling (no slip): $\omega_w r = v \implies \lambda = 0$
- Full lock (wheel stationary): $\omega_w = 0 \implies \lambda = 1$
- No braking, free rolling: $\lambda = 0$

**Step 4 — Wheel dynamics:**

$$J_w \frac{d\omega_w}{dt} = -T_b - F_x r$$

where $T_b$ is the brake torque and $F_x$ is the longitudinal tire force.

**Step 5 — Vehicle dynamics:**

$$m \frac{dv}{dt} = -F_x$$

**Step 6 — Optimal band:**

The peak tire-road friction coefficient $\mu_{\text{peak}}$ is achieved at slip ratio $\lambda_{\text{opt}} \approx 0.08$ to $0.15$ on dry asphalt. Beyond this, $\mu$ drops (the "falling slope" of the $\mu$-$\lambda$ curve) until it reaches $\mu_{\text{slide}}$ at full lock. The ABS controller targets:

$$\lambda \in [\lambda_{\text{low}}, \lambda_{\text{high}}] = [0.08, 0.25] \text{ (typical)}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical example:**

A car traveling at $v = 30$ m/s (108 km/h) brakes hard. If a wheel's angular velocity drops to $\omega_w = 200$ rad/s and $r = 0.3$ m:

$$v_w = 200 \times 0.3 = 60 \text{ m/s}$$

But $v = 30$ m/s, so the wheel is spinning *faster* than the vehicle. This indicates an error in the assumption (perhaps this is a 2WD vehicle with driven rear wheels) — let us take a wheel decelerating:

If $v_w = 25$ m/s, then:

$$\lambda = \frac{30 - 25}{30} = 0.167$$

This is in the optimum band, so the ECU holds pressure. If $\omega_w$ drops to give $v_w = 20$ m/s:

$$\lambda = \frac{30 - 20}{30} = 0.333 > 0.30 \implies \text{EU releases pressure}$$

**Step 8 — Conclusion:**
ABS maintains slip ratio in the optimal band by modulating hydraulic pressure in a bang-bang or PID fashion, ensuring maximum tire force retention and steerability.

---

### PART B — Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Explain the Electronic Stability Control (ESC) system with the help of a block diagram. Derive the steady-state yaw rate for the bicycle model and describe how ESC uses yaw rate error to apply corrective braking.**

#### Part (a): ESC Architecture and Block Diagram (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition:**
Electronic Stability Control (ESC), also called Electronic Stability Program (ESP) or Vehicle Dynamics Control (VDC), is a computerized chassis control system that improves a vehicle's stability by detecting and reducing loss of traction (skidding). It became mandatory in the EU in 2014 and in India from 2022–2024.

**Step 2 — Sensors used:**

1. **Yaw Rate Sensor (Gyroscope)** — measures the actual rotational rate of the vehicle about its vertical axis (rad/s).
2. **Lateral Accelerometer** — measures the side-to-side acceleration (m/s²).
3. **Steering Angle Sensor** — measures the driver's intended direction (deg).
4. **Wheel Speed Sensors (4×)** — measure individual wheel speeds.
5. **Brake Pressure Sensor** — measures the driver's brake input.
6. **Throttle Position Sensor** — measures the driver's acceleration demand.
7. **Roll Rate Sensor (newer systems)** — measures body roll for RSC.

**Step 3 — Components:**

1. ESC ECU (high-speed processor running at ~100 Hz).
2. Hydraulic Modulator (shared with ABS, but with independent channels).
3. Engine Control Unit (interface for torque reduction).
4. CAN bus (for sensor data exchange).

**Step 4 — Block Diagram Reference:**
Refer to Section 4.4. The architecture follows the standard **Driver → Sensors → ECU → Actuators → Vehicle** loop, with feedback from the yaw rate and lateral acceleration sensors.

**Step 5 — Operating Modes:**

- **Understeer** (car turns less than driver intends — typically front-wheel drive in corners):
  - ESC applies brake to **inner-rear wheel** to create a yaw moment toward the inside of the turn.
- **Oversteer** (car turns more than driver intends — typically rear-wheel drive or slippery conditions):
  - ESC applies brake to **outer-front wheel** to create a counter-yaw moment.
- In both cases, ESC may also request **engine torque reduction** to slow the vehicle.

#### Part (b): Bicycle Model Yaw Rate Derivation (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Model setup:**
Consider a 2-DOF bicycle model: the vehicle has a single front wheel and a single rear wheel, lumped masses at the front and rear axles, and a center of gravity (CG) located at distances $a$ and $b$ from the front and rear axles respectively.

**Step 2 — Tire slip angles (small-angle approximation):**

$$\alpha_f = \delta - \beta - \frac{a \dot\psi}{v}, \qquad \alpha_r = -\beta + \frac{b \dot\psi}{v}$$

where:
- $\delta$ = steering angle at the front wheel
- $\beta$ = sideslip angle at the CG
- $\dot\psi$ = yaw rate
- $v$ = longitudinal velocity

**Step 3 — Linear tire forces:**

$$F_{yf} = C_{\alpha f} \alpha_f, \qquad F_{yr} = C_{\alpha r} \alpha_r$$

where $C_{\alpha f}$ and $C_{\alpha r}$ are the front and rear cornering stiffnesses (N/rad).

**Step 4 — Equations of motion:**

Lateral force balance:

$$m v (\dot\beta + \dot\psi) = F_{yf} \cos\delta + F_{yr} \approx F_{yf} + F_{yr} \quad (\text{small } \delta)$$

Yaw moment balance about CG:

$$I_z \ddot\psi = a F_{yf} - b F_{yr}$$

**Step 5 — Steady-state conditions:**
For a steady cornering, $\ddot\psi = 0$ and $\dot\beta = 0$. The two equations reduce to:

$$0 = F_{yf} + F_{yr}$$

$$0 = a F_{yf} - b F_{yr}$$

**Step 6 — Solving the system:**

Substituting the linear tire model:

$$0 = C_{\alpha f} \left( \delta - \frac{a \dot\psi}{v} \right) + C_{\alpha r} \left( \frac{b \dot\psi}{v} \right)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot\psi_{\text{ss}} = \frac{v \cdot C_{\alpha f} \cdot \delta}{C_{\alpha f} \cdot a + C_{\alpha r} \cdot b + \frac{m v^2 (b C_{\alpha r} - a C_{\alpha f})}{C_{\alpha r} L}}
\end{aligned}$$

After algebraic simplification (using $L = a + b$):

$$\dot\psi_{\text{ss}} = \frac{v \cdot \delta}{L + K v^2}$$

where the understeer gradient is:

$$K = \frac{m b}{C_{\alpha r}} - \frac{m a}{C_{\alpha f}}$$

**Step 7 — Special cases:**

- **Neutral steer** ($K = 0$): $\dot\psi_{\text{ss}} = \dfrac{v \delta}{L}$ — independent of speed.
- **Understeer** ($K > 0$): $\dot\psi_{\text{ss}}$ decreases as $v$ increases — self-stabilizing.
- **Oversteer** ($K < 0$): $\dot\psi_{\text{ss}}$ increases as $v$ increases — destabilizing; ESC must intervene.

**Step 8 — ESC control law:**

The ECU computes yaw rate error:

$$\Delta\dot\psi = \dot\psi_{\text{measured}} - \dot\psi_{\text{desired}}$$

If $\vert \Delta\dot\psi \vert > \epsilon$ (threshold), a **PID controller** computes a corrective yaw moment $M_z$:

$$M_z = K_p \cdot \Delta\dot\psi + K_i \int \Delta\dot\psi \, dt + K_d \frac{d \Delta\dot\psi}{dt}$$

The ECU translates $M_z$ into a **selective brake pressure** command:

$$P_{\text{brake, target}} = f(M_z, F_z, r_{\text{wheel}})$$

This is applied to the appropriate wheel (inner-rear for understeer, outer-front for oversteer).

**Step 9 — Numerical example:**

Given: $m = 1500$ kg, $a = 1.2$ m, $b = 1.4$ m, $L = 2.6$ m, $C_{\alpha f} = 80{,}000$ N/rad, $C_{\alpha r} = 90{,}000$ N/rad, $v = 20$ m/s, $\delta = 0.05$ rad (~2.86°).

$$K = \frac{1500 \times 1.4}{90000} - \frac{1500 \times 1.2}{80000} = 0.02333 - 0.0225 = 0.000833 \text{ rad·s²/m²}$$

$$\dot\psi_{\text{desired}} = \frac{20 \times 0.05}{2.6 + 0.000833 \times 400} = \frac{1.0}{2.6 + 0.333} = \frac{1.0}{2.933} \approx 0.341 \text{ rad/s}$$

If the measured yaw rate is 0.50 rad/s, then $\Delta\dot\psi = 0.159$ rad/s, which exceeds the typical threshold of 0.05 rad/s. ESC intervenes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print or screenshot this section before the exam.**

### 5.3.1 Core Definitions to Memorize

- [ ] **Chassis Control System:** Integrated electronic + mechanical system for longitudinal, lateral, and vertical vehicle dynamics regulation.
- [ ] **Slip Ratio ($\lambda$):** $1 - \omega_w r / v$ for braking; $(\omega_w r - v)/v$ for traction.
- [ ] **Slip Angle ($\alpha$):** Difference between tire heading and velocity vector direction.
- [ ] **Friction Circle:** $F_x^2 + F_y^2 \le (\mu F_z)^2$ — the tire's force-generating limit.
- [ ] **Understeer Gradient ($K$):** Positive = stable, negative = unstable.
- [ ] **Optimal Slip:** $\lambda \in [0.08, 0.15]$ on dry asphalt.
- [ ] **Quarter-Car Natural Frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k_s / m_s}$, typically ~1–2 Hz.
- [ ] **Damping Ratio:** $\zeta = c_s / (2\sqrt{k_s m_s})$, ideally 0.2–0.4 for cars.

### 5.3.2 Subsystem Quick-Pills

- [ ] **ABS** — Prevents wheel lock; modulates hydraulic pressure; operates on slip ratio.
- [ ] **EBD** — Distributes brake force according to load transfer.
- [ ] **TCS** — Prevents wheel spin during acceleration.
- [ ] **ESC/ESP** — Uses yaw rate + steering angle + lateral accel to correct skid.
- [ ] **AEB** — Automatic emergency braking via radar + camera.
- [ ] **EPS** — Electric motor assist; replaces hydraulic pump.
- [ ] **Active Suspension** — Adds external energy; can implement sky-hook control.
- [ ] **Semi-Active Suspension** — Variable damping only; uses MR fluid orifices.
- [ ] **Steer-by-Wire** — No mechanical link; ECU commands road-wheel actuator.
- [ ] **TPMS** — Monitors tire pressure; often battery-powered sensors.

### 5.3.3 Sensors to Remember

- [ ] Wheel Speed Sensors (4×)
- [ ] Yaw Rate Gyroscope
- [ ] Lateral Accelerometer
- [ ] Longitudinal Accelerometer
- [ ] Steering Angle Sensor
- [ ] Brake Pressure Sensor
- [ ] Throttle Position Sensor
- [ ] Ride Height Sensor (suspension)
- [ ] Tire Pressure Sensor (TPMS)

### 5.3.4 Most Likely KTU 2024 Question Topics

- [ ] ⭐ Derive slip ratio and explain ABS working.
- [ ] ⭐ Explain ESC with yaw rate error analysis.
- [ ] ⭐ Compare Active vs Semi-Active suspension.
- [ ] ⭐ Quarter-car model derivation.
- [ ] ⭐ Friction circle and which subsystem operates in which region.
- [ ] ⭐ Steer-by-Wire architecture.
- [ ] ⭐ By-Wire technologies (Brake-by-Wire, Throttle-by-Wire).
- [ ] ⭐ Role of ECU in modern chassis systems (CAN bus integration).
- [ ] ⭐ Differences between EPS and EHPS.
- [ ] ⭐ Regulatory mandates (when ABS/ESC became mandatory in India/EU).

### 5.3.5 Common Examiner Pet Peeves (Lose Marks Here)

- [ ] ❌ Confusing slip ratio with slip angle.
- [ ] ❌ Forgetting to state the units in numerical problems.
- [ ] ❌ Drawing block diagrams without labels.
- [ ] ❌ Writing passive voice for sensors (e.g., "Wheel speed is measured by sensor" — should be "Wheel speed sensor measures...").
- [ ] ❌ Missing the *closed-loop* explanation — every chassis system is closed-loop.
- [ ] ❌ Omitting the *control variable* — what is being controlled?
- [ ] ❌ Not mentioning the actuator — what applies the corrective action?
- [ ] ❌ Treating ABS and ESC as identical.

### 5.3.6 Last-Minute Formulas to Memorize

| Priority | Formula | Use |
|----------|---------|-----|
| 🔴 High | $\lambda = 1 - \omega_w r / v$ | Slip ratio (KTU favourite) |
| 🔴 High | $\dot\psi_{\text{ss}} = v \delta / (L + K v^2)$ | Yaw rate (ESC) |
| 🔴 High | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k_s / m_s}$ | Suspension |
| 🟡 Med | $\zeta = c_s / (2\sqrt{k_s m_s})$ | Damping |
| 🟡 Med | $F_x^2 + F_y^2 \le (\mu F_z)^2$ | Friction circle |
| 🟢 Low | $J_w \dot\omega = T_{\text{engine}} - T_{\text{brake}} - F_x r$ | Wheel dyn |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Topic Recap. Good luck with your KTU 2024 Examination — focus on diagrams, formulas, and the sensor-actuator-ECU triad for any subsystem question.**

topics.title: Integrated Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT612 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Integrated Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *Modern Automotive Technologies (OEMUT612)*, **Integrated Systems** refer to the holistic integration of previously independent mechanical, hydraulic, electrical, and electronic subsystems of a vehicle into a unified, sensor-fusion-driven, electronically controlled architecture. These systems replace the legacy "island automation" philosophy (where each subsystem works in isolation) with a **centralized or domain-controller-based coordinated control philosophy** governed by ECUs, vehicle networks (CAN/LIN/FlexRay/Ethernet), and intelligent software layers.

The major classifications covered under this module are:

1. **Drive-by-Wire (DBW) Systems** — X-by-Wire (Throttle, Brake, Steer, Shift)
2. **Integrated Chassis Control (ICC)** — Integration of ABS, EBD, TCS, ESC, AFS
3. **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** & **Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB)**
4. **Integrated Starter-Generator (ISG) & 48V Mild-Hybrid Architectures**
5. **Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) — L0 to L5**
6. **Telematics, V2X Communication, and Connected Vehicle Architectures**
7. **Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) & Energy Management Integration**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme emphasizes *functional integration* rather than *isolated subsystem study*. Questions in the University Exam typically map one real-world scenario to multiple integrated subsystems (e.g., "Explain how ABS, EBD, and ESC integrate during a cornering maneuver on a low-friction surface").

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Norms used in Integrated Systems Design:**
> - **ISO 26262** — Functional Safety for Automotive E/E Systems (**ASIL A–D**).
> - **SAE J1939** — Heavy-duty vehicle CAN bus standard (**250 kbps / 500 kbps**).
> - **ISO 15622** — Adaptive Cruise Control operational definitions.
> - **SAE J3016** — Driving Automation Levels (**Level 0 to Level 5**).
> - **ISO 14229 (UDS)** — Unified Diagnostic Services over CAN.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **symphony orchestra**. Each musician (subsystem) is technically brilliant when playing alone — the violinist is excellent, the drummer is excellent. But without a **conductor (the central ECU and supervisory control software)**, they produce noise. **Integrated Systems engineering is the conductor** — it ensures that when the *brake* subsystem is active, the *throttle* subsystem automatically lifts, the *transmission* downshifts, the *steering* torque is supplemented, and the *headlamps* modulate. The vehicle behaves as **one coordinated machine**, not a collection of bolted-on parts.

A second analogy: think of an **integrated system as a human nervous system**. Sensors (eyes, skin) feed data to the brain (central ECU domain controller), which routes signals to actuators (muscles — brake calipers, steering rack, throttle plate) via a communication backbone (spinal cord = CAN bus). A reflex action like pulling your hand from a hot stove is exactly equivalent to an **AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking)** event: sensor input → ECU decision in **< 10 ms** → actuator command → vehicle response.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol / Standard | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vehicle mass (m) | m | 1000 to 2500 | kg |
| Wheel radius (r) | r | 0.30 to 0.36 | m |
| Wheelbase (L) | L | 2.4 to 3.0 | m |
| Track width (t) | t | 1.5 to 1.7 | m |
| Centre of gravity height | h | 0.45 to 0.65 | m |
| Standard gravity | g | **9.81** | m/s² |
| CAN bus data rate | — | **500** (CAN), **1000** (CAN-FD), **10000** (Automotive Ethernet) | kbps / Mbps |
| ABS cycle time | — | **5 to 10** | ms |
| ESC intervention threshold (yaw rate error) | — | **0.05 to 0.15** | rad/s |
| Slip ratio target (ABS) | λ | **0.08 to 0.30** | dimensionless |
| Time headway (ACC) | τ | **1.0 to 2.5** | s |
| 48V MHEV system voltage | V | **48 V** (nominal) | V |
| Regenerative braking decel | a_regen | **0.15 g to 0.30 g** | m/s² |
| Functional Safety level (ASIL) | — | A, B, C, **D** | category |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of an Integrated System

The standard **three-layer architecture** of every modern integrated automotive system is:

1. **Perception Layer (Sensors)**
   - Wheel-speed sensors (Hall-effect, variable reluctance)
   - Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) — accelerometer + gyroscope (yaw rate)
   - Steering wheel angle sensor
   - Radar (24 GHz / 77 GHz), Lidar, Camera
   - Ultrasonic parking sensors
   - Li-ion battery management sensors (voltage, current, temperature)
2. **Decision Layer (ECU / Domain Controller)**
   - Real-time operating system (AUTOSAR Classic / Adaptive)
   - Sensor fusion (Kalman filter, complementary filter)
   - Control algorithms (PID, LQR, MPC, fuzzy logic, neural networks)
3. **Actuation Layer (Effectors)**
   - Hydraulic modulator (ABS/ESC)
   - Electric throttle body (drive-by-wire)
   - Electric power steering (EPS) motor
   - ISG (Integrated Starter-Generator) 48V machine
   - Brake-by-wire actuators

## 2.2 Core "Why" and "How" Reasoning

- **Why integrate?** Isolated subsystems (e.g., ABS acting alone during a turn) can cause loss of yaw control. Integration allows *coordinated* braking and steering intervention → stability.
- **How is it achieved?** Through a high-speed **vehicle bus** (CAN, FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet) carrying sensor data, and a **supervisory control strategy** running on the central/domain ECU.
- **The "5 ms" rule:** Critical safety functions (ABS, ESC, AEB) must execute the sense → decide → actuate loop in **less than 10 ms** to be safe. Hence hard real-time OS, deterministic bus arbitration (CAN prioritizes by message ID), and prioritized task scheduling are mandatory.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **must-know** for KTU University ESE. Use \vert for absolute value in your notebooks (not the bare pipe character).

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Longitudinal slip ratio** | $\lambda = \dfrac{\vert v_{\omega} \cdot r - v_{v} \vert}{\max(v_{\omega} \cdot r, \, v_{v})}$ | $v_{\omega}$ = wheel angular velocity (rad/s), $r$ = wheel radius, $v_v$ = vehicle velocity | dimensionless |
| 2 | **Slip angle** (tire) | $\alpha = \delta - \theta \cdot \dfrac{L}{r_v}$ | $\delta$ = steer angle, $\theta$ = yaw rate, $L$ = wheelbase, $r_v$ = instantaneous radius of curvature | rad |
| 3 | **Longitudinal tire force** | $F_x = \mu \cdot F_z \cdot \cos\alpha$ | $\mu$ = friction coefficient, $F_z$ = vertical load, $\alpha$ = slip angle | N |
| 4 | **Lateral tire force (linear)** | $F_y = C_{\alpha} \cdot \alpha$ | $C_{\alpha}$ = cornering stiffness, $\alpha$ = slip angle | N |
| 5 | **Brake-by-wire hydraulic pressure** | $P_{act} = \dfrac{T_{req}}{k_{b} \cdot \mu \cdot r_{d} \cdot A_{cyl}}$ | $T_{req}$ = required brake torque, $k_b$ = brake factor, $r_d$ = effective radius, $A_{cyl}$ = piston area | Pa |
| 6 | **Regenerative braking power** | $P_{regen} = T_m \cdot \omega_m$ | $T_m$ = motor torque, $\omega_m$ = motor angular speed | W |
| 7 | **Regen energy recovered per event** | $E_{rec} = \int P_{regen} \, dt = \int T_m \cdot \omega_m \, dt$ | integrated over deceleration period | J |
| 8 | **Energy storage SOC change** | $\Delta SOC = \dfrac{E_{rec}}{E_{bat,cap} \cdot 3600}$ | $E_{bat,cap}$ = battery energy in Wh | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 9 | **ACC safe following distance** | $d_{safe} = v \cdot \tau + d_{min}$ | $v$ = ego vehicle speed, $\tau$ = time headway, $d_{min}$ = standstill gap (typ. 2–5 m) | m |
| 10 | **Yaw moment from differential braking** | $M_z = \dfrac{t}{2} \cdot (F_{xb} - F_{xf})$ | $t$ = track width, $F_{xb}, F_{xf}$ = braking force on rear and front wheel of one side | N·m |
| 11 | **Pitch rate from braking decel** | $\dot{\theta}_{pitch} = \dfrac{a_x \cdot h}{v^2}$ | $a_x$ = longitudinal accel, $h$ = CG height | rad/s |
| 12 | **Understeer gradient** | $K_{us} = \dfrac{W_f}{C_{\alpha f}} - \dfrac{W_r}{C_{\alpha r}}$ | $W_f, W_r$ = front and rear axle weights, $C_{\alpha}$ = cornering stiffness | rad/g |
| 13 | **Critical (characteristic) speed** | $v_{ch} = \sqrt{\dfrac{L \cdot g}{K_{us}}}$ | $L$ = wheelbase, $K_{us}$ = understeer gradient | m/s |
| 14 | **Hydraulic accumulator energy** | $E_{acc} = P \cdot V_{acc}$ | $P$ = system pressure, $V_{acc}$ = accumulator volume | J |
| 15 | **48V ISG efficiency equation** | $\eta_{ISG} = \dfrac{P_{mech,out}}{P_{elec,in}}$ | typical peak **> 85 %** | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility of these formulas:** Formula 1 is used in **ABS** to lock the slip ratio near the peak of the μ–λ curve. Formula 9 governs **ACC** longitudinal controller design. Formula 13 is the textbook design check for **ESC** intervention thresholds.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Drive-by-Wire** removes the mechanical pedal-to-throttle cable, enabling **Eco/Pedal-Map modes**, **rev-match downshifting**, and **cooperative ACC longitudinal control** from a single pedal demand signal.
- **Integrated Chassis Control (ICC)** (Bosch, Continental, ZF systems) blends braking, steering, and drivetrain actuation. Example: in a **Bend Skid**, ESC applies differential braking to one rear wheel and trims engine torque to generate a correcting yaw moment.
- **48V ISG Mild-Hybrids** are the *most deployed* integrated hybrid architecture globally (Mercedes-Benz, Audi, Fiat). They deliver **10–20 %** fuel savings by enabling start-stop, torque-fill, and regenerative braking without the cost of a full hybrid.
- **ADAS (L2+)** like Tesla Autopilot, GM Super Cruise, and Honda Sensing rely on sensor fusion between camera + radar + high-definition map. The architecture integrates lane-keep, AEB, ACC, and traffic-jam assist into a single ECU.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Slip Ratio and Maximum Brake Torque

**Statement:** Show that the slip ratio $\lambda$ is bounded by $0 \le \lambda \le 1$ during pure braking, and that the maximum deceleration occurs when the wheel operates at the peak of the $\mu(\lambda)$ curve.

**Step 1 — Define wheel linear velocity.** A wheel of radius $r$ rotating at $\omega$ rad/s has a contact-patch linear velocity

$$
v_{\omega} = \omega \cdot r
$$

**Step 2 — Define vehicle chassis velocity.** Let $v_v$ be the longitudinal velocity of the vehicle chassis as measured by a non-slipping reference (e.g., optical GPS or 4-wheel averaged longitudinal estimator).

**Step 3 — Define slip ratio (braking case).** During braking, the wheel rotates slower than the chassis ($v_{\omega} \cdot r < v_v$). The SAE J670 definition gives

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{v_v - v_{\omega} \cdot r}{v_v}, \quad v_v > 0
$$

which yields $\lambda = 0$ at free-rolling and $\lambda \to 1$ at wheel lock. Hence $0 \le \lambda \le 1$ in pure braking.

**Step 4 — Apply Newton's second law to the wheel.** The wheel rotational dynamics is

$$
I_{\omega} \cdot \dot{\omega} = T_{wheel} - F_x \cdot r
$$

where $T_{wheel}$ is the net wheel torque (engine minus brake), $F_x$ is the longitudinal tire force, and $I_{\omega}$ is the wheel inertia.

**Step 5 — Compute deceleration.** The chassis deceleration is governed by the road-tire force:

$$
a_x = \dfrac{F_x}{m} = \dfrac{\mu(\lambda) \cdot F_z}{m}
$$

Since $\mu(\lambda)$ has a maximum at $\lambda^{\star} \approx 0.08$–$0.15$ for dry asphalt, the ABS controller maintains the wheel near this peak to maximize $a_x$ (typical optimum deceleration $a_x \approx 0.8$–$1.0 \, g$ on dry tarmac).

**Step 6 — Conclusion.** The maximum achievable deceleration is $a_{x,\max} = \mu_{\max} \cdot g$, and it is achieved by holding $\lambda$ at the peak of the tire's friction curve using the **Mayer–Bose or Bosch pulsed-hydraulic ABS control law**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time-Headway ACC Safe Distance

**Statement:** Derive the safe following distance equation $d_{safe} = v \cdot \tau + d_{min}$.

**Step 1 — Kinematic setup.** Let vehicle A (ego) travel at speed $v$ and vehicle B (lead) travel at speed $v_L$. The instantaneous gap (bumper-to-bumper) is $d$.

**Step 2 — Safety condition.** The time headway is defined as

$$
\tau = \dfrac{d}{v}
$$

For a constant lead-vehicle speed, the safety requirement is $\tau \ge \tau_{min}$ (typ. 1.0 s for highway cruise, 2.0 s for urban adaptive).

**Step 3 — Solve for the safe distance.** Multiplying both sides by $v$,

$$
d_{safe} = v \cdot \tau_{min} + d_{min}
$$

where $d_{min}$ is the standstill gap (typ. 2–5 m) maintained to account for sensor measurement error and actuator latency.

**Step 4 — Work out a numerical example.** For $v = 100$ km/h $= 27.78$ m/s, $\tau_{min} = 1.5$ s, $d_{min} = 3$ m:

$$
d_{safe} = 27.78 \times 1.5 + 3 = 41.67 + 3 = 44.67 \, \text{m}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery

**Statement:** For a vehicle of mass $m = 1500$ kg decelerating from $v_0 = 60$ km/h to standstill, with a regenerative fraction $\eta_{regen} = 0.7$ of total braking, find the energy recovered if the electric machine operates at average torque $T_m = 200$ N·m and average speed $\omega_m = 800$ rad/s.

**Step 1 — Compute initial kinetic energy.**

$$
E_{kin} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v_0^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times \left(\dfrac{60 \times 1000}{3600}\right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (16.67)^2
$$

$$
E_{kin} = 750 \times 277.89 \approx 208{,}333 \, \text{J} = 208.33 \, \text{kJ}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total energy available for regen.**

$$
E_{regen,available} = \eta_{regen} \times E_{kin} = 0.7 \times 208{,}333 = 145{,}833 \, \text{J}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute regen power.**

$$
P_{regen} = T_m \times \omega_m = 200 \times 800 = 160{,}000 \, \text{W} = 160 \, \text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute time to dissipate the kinetic energy.**

$$
t_{stop} = \dfrac{E_{kin}}{P_{regen}} = \dfrac{208{,}333}{160{,}000} \approx 1.30 \, \text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute actual recovered energy (limited by regen time & power).** In practice, a regen cap of 160 kW × 1.30 s gives $E_{rec} = 160{,}000 \times 1.30 = 208{,}000$ J, but the *usable* regen fraction is bounded by battery SOC, so the realistic recovery is $\approx 0.7 \times 208{,}333 = 145.8$ kJ.

**Step 6 — Convert to WLTP-equivalent fuel saving.** $145.8$ kJ $\div 34 \, \text{MJ/L}$ (gasoline LHV) $\approx 0.0043$ L of fuel, which is **$\approx 4.3$ mL per stop** — illustrating why regenerative braking is most useful in *urban stop-and-go* duty cycles, not highway.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Yaw Stability from Differential Braking (ESC)

**Statement:** Show that applying a differential braking torque of magnitude $\Delta T_b$ to one rear wheel creates a correcting yaw moment.

**Step 1 — Differential braking force.**

$$
\Delta F_{xb} = \dfrac{\Delta T_b}{r}
$$

**Step 2 — Yaw moment arm.** The yaw moment is created about the vehicle's vertical (z) axis at the contact patch, at a lateral offset of $t/2$ (half the track width).

**Step 3 — Yaw moment generated.**

$$
M_z = \Delta F_{xb} \cdot \dfrac{t}{2} = \dfrac{\Delta T_b \cdot t}{2 \cdot r}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.** For $\Delta T_b = 400$ N·m, $t = 1.55$ m, $r = 0.32$ m:

$$
M_z = \dfrac{400 \times 1.55}{2 \times 0.32} = \dfrac{620}{0.64} = 968.75 \, \text{N·m}
$$

**Step 5 — Yaw acceleration.** With vehicle yaw inertia $I_{zz} \approx 2500$ kg·m²:

$$
\ddot{\psi} = \dfrac{M_z}{I_{zz}} = \dfrac{968.75}{2500} \approx 0.39 \, \text{rad/s}^2
$$

This is the corrective yaw acceleration supplied by ESC during a cornering instability event.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Integrated Subsystem Interaction Table

| Vehicle Event | Active Subsystem A | Active Subsystem B | Active Subsystem C | Active Subsystem D | Net Vehicle Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hard braking on split-μ surface | ABS (anti-lock) | EBD (electronic brake-force distribution) | ESC (yaw correction) | Stop lamp signalling | Maintained stability, shortest possible stop |
| Front-end collision imminent | AEB (autonomous emergency brake) | Pre-tensioner seatbelt | Brake booster full apply | Airbag pre-arm | Occupant injury mitigation |
| Highway cruise behind a slower car | ACC (radar cruise) | Engine torque reduction via DBW | Downshift to engine-brake | Possibly brake apply | Maintains $\tau_{headway}$ |
| Vehicle approaching a curve too fast | ESC yaw correction | AFS (active front steering) | Variable-ratio steering | Pre-filling brake hydraulic | Path correction |
| Idle at traffic signal | 48V ISG stop | HVAC compressor (electrified) | Infotainment (retained) | Engine OFF | Fuel saved |
| Driver releases accelerator | Regenerative braking | Brake-light activation via ACC | Battery SOC increase | Coast-down torque blending | Energy recovered |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Architecture: Integrated Chassis Control (ICC) Domain

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA1[Wheel Speed Sensors x4] --> nodeB1[ABS ECU Front]
    nodeA2[IMU Yaw Rate and Lateral G] --> nodeB1
    nodeA3[Steering Angle Sensor] --> nodeB1
    nodeA4[Brake Pressure Sensor] --> nodeB1
    nodeB1[ABS ECU Front Domain] --> nodeC1[CAN Bus 500 kbps High Speed]
    nodeB2[Power Steering ECU] --> nodeC1
    nodeB3[Engine ECU Drive by Wire] --> nodeC1
    nodeB4[Transmission ECU] --> nodeC1
    nodeC1[CAN Bus 500 kbps High Speed] --> nodeD1[Central Domain Controller AUTOSAR Adaptive]
    nodeC1 --> nodeD2[ADAS Domain Controller L2 Plus]
    nodeC1 --> nodeD3[Body and Comfort Domain]
    nodeC1 --> nodeD4[Powertrain Domain 48V ISG]
    nodeD1[Central Domain Controller] --> nodeE1[Hydraulic Modulator ABS ESC]
    nodeD1 --> nodeE2[Electric Throttle Body]
    nodeD1 --> nodeE3[EPS Motor]
    nodeD1 --> nodeE4[ISG 48V Machine]
    nodeE1 --> nodeF1[Wheel Cylinders and Brake Calipers]
    nodeE2 --> nodeF2[Intake Manifold Throttle Plate]
    nodeE3 --> nodeF3[Steering Rack Assist]
    nodeE4 --> nodeF4[Belt or Crankshaft P2 Hybrid Coupling]
```

**How to read this diagram:** The four wheel-speed sensors, IMU, and steering-angle sensor feed the **ABS ECU front-domain**, which arbitrates with the powertrain, steering, and ADAS ECUs over a **500 kbps CAN bus**. The **central domain controller** (running AUTOSAR Adaptive) makes the *supervisory* decision and commands the four actuator types in parallel. This is the **block-level functional architecture** of a modern integrated chassis system.

## 4.2 48V Integrated Starter-Generator (ISG) Mild-Hybrid Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeM1[Internal Combustion Engine] --> nodeM2[Clutch K0]
    nodeM2 --> nodeM3[ISG 48V Electric Machine 10 to 20 kW]
    nodeM3 --> nodeM4[8 Speed Automatic or DCT Transmission]
    nodeM4 --> nodeM5[Wheels]
    nodeM3 --> nodeM6[Inverter 48V DC to 3 Phase AC]
    nodeM6 --> nodeM7[48V Li ion Battery 0.5 to 1.5 kWh]
    nodeM7 --> nodeM8[DC DC Converter 48V to 12V]
    nodeM8 --> nodeM9[12V Auxiliary Loads Lights and ECU]
    nodeM7 --> nodeM10[Electric Compressor eHVAC]
    nodeM7 --> nodeM11[Electric Water Pump eWP]
```

**Operating modes of the ISG:**
- **Start-Stop (Auto Start-Stop):** Engine off at standstill; ISG restarts in **< 0.3 s** when brake is released.
- **Torque-Fill (Electric Launch):** ISG assists engine during launch, smoothing NVH.
- **Regenerative Braking:** ISG acts as a generator during deceleration; energy → 48 V battery.
- **Electric Coasting:** At steady speeds, engine decouples (clutch K0 open); ISG powers ancillaries.

## 4.3 ADAS Sensor Fusion and Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeS1[Front Radar 77 GHz Long Range] --> nodeF1[Sensor Fusion ECU]
    nodeS2[Surround Cameras 360] --> nodeF1
    nodeS3[Front Camera Mono with Lane Detect] --> nodeF1
    nodeS4[Ultrasonic 12 Channel Park] --> nodeF1
    nodeS5[Lidar Optional L2 Plus to L3] --> nodeF1
    nodeS6[HD Map and GNSS] --> nodeF1
    nodeF1[Sensor Fusion Kalman Filter] --> nodeD1[Longitudinal Controller ACC and AEB]
    nodeF1 --> nodeD2[Lateral Controller LKA and Lane Centring]
    nodeF1 --> nodeD3[Object Tracker Multi Hypothesis]
    nodeD1 --> nodeA1[Throttle and Brake Actuator]
    nodeD2 --> nodeA2[EPS Steering Actuator]
    nodeD3 --> nodeD1
    nodeD3 --> nodeD2
    nodeA1 --> nodeV1[Vehicle Longitudinal Motion]
    nodeA2 --> nodeV2[Vehicle Lateral Motion]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The **sensor-fusion ECU** combines radar, camera, ultrasonic, and (optionally) lidar into a single object list. The **longitudinal controller** issues throttle/brake commands; the **lateral controller** issues steering commands. The **object tracker** feeds *predicted* trajectories into both controllers using a multi-hypothesis filter. This is the architecture of a **SAE Level 2+ ADAS** system.

## 4.4 Drive-by-Wire Throttle-by-Wire Closed-Loop Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1[Accelerator Pedal Module APS Sensor 1 and APS Sensor 2] --> nodeECU[Engine ECU Powertrain Domain]
    nodeECU --> nodeETB[Electronic Throttle Body Motor]
    nodeETB --> nodeTP[Throttle Plate Position]
    nodeTP --> nodeMAP[Mass Air Flow Sensor MAP MAF]
    nodeMAP --> nodeECU
    nodeETB --> nodeFB[TPS Feedback Throttle Position Sensor 1 and 2]
    nodeFB --> nodeECU
    nodeECU --> nodeINJ[Injector Pulse Width]
    nodeINJ --> nodeENG[Engine Torque Output]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two sensors on the pedal and the throttle?** Redundancy is mandated by **ISO 26262 ASIL-D** for safety-critical systems. If the two APS signals diverge, the ECU detects a fault and defaults to a *limp-home* (idle) throttle position.

## 4.5 CAN Bus Message Arbitration and Priority Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeCAN[CAN Bus 500 kbps Two Wire Twisted Pair CANH and CANL] --> nodeP1[Message 0x100 Engine RPM Period 10 ms Priority 1]
    nodeCAN --> nodeP2[Message 0x120 Vehicle Speed Period 20 ms Priority 2]
    nodeCAN --> nodeP3[Message 0x200 ABS Wheel Speeds Period 5 ms Priority 3 CRITICAL]
    nodeCAN --> nodeP4[Message 0x250 ESC Yaw Rate Period 5 ms Priority 3 CRITICAL]
    nodeCAN --> nodeP5[Message 0x300 Steering Angle Period 10 ms Priority 4]
    nodeCAN --> nodeP6[Message 0x400 Body Controls Period 100 ms Priority 5 LOW]
    nodeCAN --> nodeP7[Message 0x500 Infotainment Period 200 ms Priority 6 BACKGROUND]
```

**Why this matters:** CAN arbitration is **non-destructive bit-wise arbitration** based on message ID. The **lowest ID wins** the bus. Hence safety-critical ABS/ESC messages have low IDs (high priority) and shorter cycle times (**5 ms**), while infotainment messages have high IDs (low priority) and long cycle times (**200 ms**). This is the foundation of **real-time determinism** in integrated systems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions are modelled strictly on the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern** for *Modern Automotive Technologies (OEMUT612)*. Marks are distributed as per the official KTU template: **Part A = 3 marks each**, **Part B = 14 marks each with internal choice** (two 7-mark sub-parts).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define an Integrated System in the context of a modern automobile. Give two examples.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (OEMUT612)]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An Integrated System in a modern automobile is a **coordinated architecture** in which previously independent mechanical, hydraulic, and electronic subsystems — such as braking, steering, propulsion, and suspension — are **interconnected via electronic control units (ECUs)** and **vehicle communication networks (CAN, FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet)**, enabling centralized or domain-level supervisory control.

**Two examples:**
1. **Integrated Chassis Control (ICC):** ABS + EBD + ESC + AFS operating through one central domain controller.
2. **48V Mild-Hybrid System (ISG):** Engine + electric machine + 48V battery + DC-DC converter managed by a hybrid control unit (HCU).

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for two examples — total 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. What is Drive-by-Wire? Why is dual-redundant sensor signal required in a Throttle-by-Wire system?
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (OEMUT612)]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Drive-by-Wire (DBW or X-by-Wire)** is the replacement of mechanical linkages (cable, hydraulic, rod) between driver controls and vehicle actuators with **electronic sensors, ECUs, and electromechanical actuators**. Examples: Throttle-by-Wire, Brake-by-Wire, Steer-by-Wire, Shift-by-Wire.

**Why dual-redundant sensor signal is required:**
- The system is classified as **ASIL-D** under **ISO 26262** (most safety-critical).
- A single-sensor failure could leave the throttle stuck open → unintended acceleration.
- Two sensors (typically with **opposite or skewed characteristics**) feed *independent* signals to the ECU.
- The ECU continuously **cross-checks** (plausibility check) the two signals. If they diverge, it enters **fail-safe / limp-home mode** (returns throttle to idle) within milliseconds.
- This satisfies the **diagnostic coverage ≥ 99 %** requirement of ASIL-D.

*(1 mark DBW definition, 2 marks dual-redundancy explanation — total 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Integrated Chassis Control and Vehicle Dynamics

**(a)** [7 marks] With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of an Integrated Chassis Control (ICC) system**. Explain the role of **ABS, EBD, TCS, and ESC** in this architecture and the typical CAN-bus communication structure used.

**(b)** [7 marks] For a passenger car of mass $m = 1400$ kg, track width $t = 1.55$ m, wheel radius $r = 0.32$ m, and yaw inertia $I_{zz} = 2300$ kg·m², compute the **correcting yaw moment** generated when the ESC applies a differential braking torque of $\Delta T_b = 350$ N·m to the inner rear wheel during a cornering instability. Also find the **corrective yaw acceleration** produced.

#### Model Solution to A(a) — 7 marks

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
(Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the full mermaid diagram. The student is expected to redraw it neatly on paper with these blocks:)

```
Wheel Speed Sensors + IMU + Steering Angle Sensor
                    ↓
            ABS / ESC ECU
                    ↓
        CAN Bus (500 kbps)
                    ↓
    Central Domain Controller (AUTOSAR)
                    ↓
   ┌────────────┬────────────┬────────────┐
   ↓            ↓            ↓            ↓
Hydraulic   Throttle     EPS         ISG
Modulator   Body         Motor       48V
```

**Functional roles (4 marks):**
- **ABS (Anti-lock Braking System):** Prevents wheel lock during heavy braking by modulating hydraulic pressure; controls slip ratio in the **λ = 0.08–0.15** range.
- **EBD (Electronic Brake-force Distribution):** Optimally distributes brake force between front and rear axles based on **dynamic vertical load transfer**; replaces the mechanical proportioning valve.
- **TCS (Traction Control System):** Prevents wheel spin during acceleration; reduces engine torque via **drive-by-wire** and applies braking to the spinning wheel.
- **ESC (Electronic Stability Control):** Compares **driver's intended yaw rate** (from steering angle & vehicle speed) with **actual yaw rate** (from IMU gyroscope); if error exceeds threshold, applies **differential braking** to generate a correcting yaw moment.

**CAN Bus:** SAE J1939 / ISO 11898; **500 kbps** for powertrain/chassis domain; messages prioritized by **11-bit ID**; critical messages at **5 ms** cycle, comfort messages at **100–200 ms**.

#### Model Solution to A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Differential braking force (1 mark):**
$$
\Delta F_{xb} = \dfrac{\Delta T_b}{r} = \dfrac{350}{0.32} = 1093.75 \, \text{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Yaw moment arm (1 mark):**
The yaw moment lever arm is **half the track width**:
$$
\dfrac{t}{2} = \dfrac{1.55}{2} = 0.775 \, \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Correcting yaw moment (2 marks):**
$$
M_z = \Delta F_{xb} \cdot \dfrac{t}{2} = 1093.75 \times 0.775 = 847.66 \, \text{N·m}
$$

**Step 4 — Corrective yaw acceleration (2 marks):**
$$
\ddot{\psi} = \dfrac{M_z}{I_{zz}} = \dfrac{847.66}{2300} \approx 0.369 \, \text{rad/s}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
A yaw acceleration of $\approx 0.37$ rad/s² is sufficient to correct a typical mid-corner instability where yaw-rate error is of order $0.1$–$0.3$ rad/s — therefore ESC intervention is **kinematically effective** in the time window of $0.3$–$0.8$ s.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) — Hybrid Integration and Regenerative Braking

**(a)** [7 marks] With the help of a block diagram, explain the **architecture of a 48V Integrated Starter-Generator (ISG) mild-hybrid system**. List its four primary operating modes.

**(b)** [7 marks] A 48V ISG electric machine supplies a regenerative torque of $T_m = 180$ N·m at an average angular speed of $\omega_m = 700$ rad/s during a deceleration event lasting $t = 4$ s. The battery is initially at SOC = 60 % with usable capacity $E_{cap} = 1.0$ kWh. Compute the **regenerative energy recovered** and the **final SOC**, assuming a battery round-trip efficiency of $\eta_{bat} = 0.90$.

#### Model Solution to B(a) — 7 marks

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
(Refer to **Section 4.2** for the full mermaid diagram.)

**Operating modes (4 marks):**
1. **Start-Stop (Auto Start-Stop):** Engine off at standstill; ISG cranks the engine in **< 0.3 s** when driver releases brake.
2. **Electric Launch / Torque-Fill:** ISG delivers up to **15–20 kW** of additional torque during launch, smoothing engine transients.
3. **Regenerative Braking:** ISG acts as a generator; kinetic energy → 48V battery. (Brake blending with friction brakes via the **brake-by-wire / ESC controller**.)
4. **Electric Coasting / Sailing:** At steady speed, **clutch K0 opens**, decoupling the engine; ISG powers ancillaries (HVAC, water pump) at zero fuel consumption.

**Architectural components:** Engine → Clutch K0 → ISG 48V Machine → 8-speed DCT → Wheels. ISG connected via **48V inverter** to a **48V Li-ion battery (0.5–1.5 kWh)**. **DC-DC converter (48V → 12V)** supplies the legacy 12V loads.

#### Model Solution to B(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Mechanical regenerative power (1 mark):**
$$
P_{regen} = T_m \cdot \omega_m = 180 \times 700 = 126{,}000 \, \text{W} = 126 \, \text{kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Total mechanical energy generated (1 mark):**
$$
E_{mech} = P_{regen} \cdot t = 126{,}000 \times 4 = 504{,}000 \, \text{J} = 504 \, \text{kJ}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply battery efficiency (1 mark):**
$$
E_{stored} = \eta_{bat} \cdot E_{mech} = 0.90 \times 504 = 453.6 \, \text{kJ}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Wh (1 mark):**
$$
E_{stored,Wh} = \dfrac{453{,}600}{3600} = 126.0 \, \text{Wh} = 0.126 \, \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 5 — Initial stored energy in battery (1 mark):**
$$
E_{initial} = SOC_{init} \times E_{cap} = 0.60 \times 1.0 = 0.60 \, \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 6 — Final SOC (2 marks):**
$$
E_{final} = E_{initial} + 0.126 = 0.60 + 0.126 = 0.726 \, \text{kWh}
$$
$$
SOC_{final} = \dfrac{E_{final}}{E_{cap}} = \dfrac{0.726}{1.0} = 0.726 = 72.6 \%
$$

**Conclusion:** The SOC rises from **60 % to 72.6 %**, a ΔSOC of **+12.6 %**, illustrating that even a single 4-second deceleration event can noticeably recharge a 48V MHEV battery.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse slip ratio with slip angle** — slip ratio ($\lambda$) is *longitudinal* (braking/acceleration); slip angle ($\alpha$) is *lateral* (cornering). Examiners will deduct **2 marks** for confusing the two.
> 2. **Don't write "ABS = automatic brake system"** — full form is **Anti-lock Braking System**.
> 3. **Don't omit the units** in numerical answers — KTU mandates SI units ($\text{N·m}$, $\text{m/s}^2$, $\text{kJ}$).
> 4. **Don't write "ESC = electronic stability car"** — it is **Electronic Stability Control** (some OEMs call it ESP, VDC, VSC).
> 5. **Block diagrams must include sensor → ECU → bus → actuator arrows** — drawing boxes without arrows earns only **1 of 3** marks.
> 6. **Numerical questions** require showing **all 5–6 steps** of derivation; skipping the intermediate step loses **1–2 marks** per skipped step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield 30-Second Recap — Integrated Systems (OEMUT612 Module 2):**

- **Integrated System** = coordinated control of multiple vehicle subsystems through ECUs + vehicle network.
- **Architecture layers:** Perception (sensors) → Decision (ECU, AUTOSAR) → Actuation (motors, valves).
- **Key standards:** ISO 26262 (functional safety, ASIL A–D), SAE J1939 (CAN), ISO 15622 (ACC), SAE J3016 (automation levels 0–5).
- **Drive-by-Wire (X-by-Wire):** Throttle, Brake, Steer, Shift — all electronic, with **dual-redundant sensors** (ASIL-D).
- **Slip ratio** $\lambda = (v_v - \omega r) / v_v$ (braking); **target 0.08–0.15** for max friction.
- **Slip angle** $\alpha = \delta - \theta L / r_v$ — used in **ESC** lateral controller.
- **ABS** keeps $\lambda$ at $\mu$-peak; **EBD** distributes front/rear brake force; **TCS** controls traction slip; **ESC** adds correcting yaw moment via differential braking.
- **Yaw moment from differential braking:** $M_z = \Delta T_b \cdot t / (2r)$; yaw acceleration $\ddot{\psi} = M_z / I_{zz}$.
- **ACC** safe distance: $d_{safe} = v \cdot \tau + d_{min}$ with $\tau$ = 1.0–2.5 s, $d_{min}$ = 2–5 m.
- **48V ISG Mild-Hybrid** modes: Start-Stop, Torque-Fill, Regen, Electric Coast.
- **Regen energy:** $E_{regen} = T_m \cdot \omega_m \cdot t \cdot \eta_{bat}$; SOC gain useful in urban stop-and-go.
- **ADAS** sensor fusion: radar + camera + (lidar) + (HD map) → longitudinal + lateral controllers.
- **CAN bus:** 500 kbps typical, message priority by 11-bit ID, critical messages at **5 ms** cycle.
- **Functional safety:** safety-critical functions like ABS, ESC, AEB require ASIL-D with **< 10 ms** sense–decide–actuate loop.
- **Draw all block diagrams with directional arrows** (sensor → ECU → bus → actuator) and **label every block**.
- **Always show units** in numerical answers; always show **all derivation steps** in 14-mark questions.
- **Memorize the four ISG operating modes** and the **three ABS/EBD/TCS/ESC function distinctions** — these are **most-tested** topics in KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vision in Cars
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT612 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vision in Cars

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Automotive Vision** (also called *Machine Vision in Vehicles* or *Computer Vision for ADAS*) is an interdisciplinary branch of automotive engineering that integrates **image sensing**, **digital signal processing**, and **artificial intelligence** to enable a vehicle to perceive, interpret, and react to its surrounding environment using optical sensors (cameras).

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 syllabus framework, automotive vision encompasses the design, calibration, and deployment of camera-based perception systems that perform real-time tasks such as **lane detection, traffic sign recognition (TSR), pedestrian detection, vehicle detection, driver monitoring, surround-view synthesis, and forward collision warning**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Exam Definition (Use Verbatim):**
> *"Automotive Vision refers to the suite of optical sensing and computational algorithms embedded within a vehicle that emulate human visual cognition to identify objects, road geometry, traffic participants, and driver state, thereby enabling Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) and autonomous driving functions."*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are learning to drive for the first time. Your instructor repeatedly tells you:
- *"Watch the lane markings — don't drift."*
- *"That red octagon means STOP."*
- *"Look out! A child is crossing the road."*
- *"Are you sleepy? Your eyes are closing!"*

Every single piece of driving information you receive comes through your **eyes**. Now, replace your biological eyes with **cameras mounted on the car**, and replace your brain with a **high-performance processor running deep-learning algorithms**. That, in essence, is **Vision in Cars**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of Car Vision (Think: Eye–Nerve–Brain):**
> 1. **Eye** → Camera Sensor (CMOS/CCD) capturing light
> 2. **Nerve** → Image Processor (ISP, GPU, ECU)
> 3. **Brain** → AI Algorithms (CNN, YOLO, Semantic Segmentation)
>
> Without all three, the system is blind — no matter how good the camera is.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants Used in Automotive Vision

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Field of View | **FoV** | 30° – 190° | Determines camera coverage angle |
| Frame Rate | **fps** | **30 – 60 fps** | Frames captured per second |
| Image Resolution | — | **1 MP – 8 MP** | Pixels per frame |
| Dynamic Range | **DR** | **120 dB** | Handling bright sun + dark shadows |
| Latency | — | **< 100 ms** | Time from capture to decision |
| Detection Range | — | **0 – 250 m** | Max distance for object identification |
| ISO Sensitivity (Low Light) | — | **up to ISO 409600** | Night-time visibility |

---

### 1.4 Historical Context (Brief)

| Era | Milestone |
|---|---|
| **1970s** | First reverse camera prototypes (Toyota, Nissan) |
| **1990s** | Night vision using IR cameras (Mercedes-Benz, GM) |
| **2000s** | Mobileye founded (1999); first mono-camera ADAS |
| **2010s** | Tesla introduces Surround View & Mobileye integration |
| **2018+** | Tesla switches to **vision-only (no radar)** strategy |
| **2020+** | 8 MP automotive cameras, transformer-based perception |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Hot Point:** Always remember the shift from **multi-sensor fusion (radar + lidar + camera)** to **vision-centric perception** driven by companies like **Tesla** — this is a frequent Part A question topic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Classification of Vision Systems in Modern Cars

Automotive vision systems are categorized based on **mounting location**, **function**, and **sensing modality**.

#### A. By Mounting Location

| Camera Position | Primary Function |
|---|---|
| Front-facing (Windshield) | Lane, traffic sign, forward vehicle, collision warning |
| Rear-facing (Tailgate) | Reverse parking, rear cross-traffic alert |
| Side-mirror mounted | Blind-spot detection, lane change assist |
| Surround-view (4 corners + grille) | 360° bird's-eye view for parking |
| In-cabin (A-pillar / steering column) | Driver monitoring (drowsiness, distraction) |

#### B. By Sensing Modality

1. **Monocular Camera** — Single lens; cost-effective; depth inferred via AI.
2. **Stereo Camera** — Two lenses separated by baseline $B$; computes depth directly via disparity.
3. **Fisheye / Wide-Angle Camera** — FoV up to **190°**; used for surround view.
4. **Thermal / Infrared (IR) Camera** — Detects heat signatures; excellent at night.
5. **Time-of-Flight (ToF) Camera** — Measures depth using modulated light pulses.
6. **Event-Based Camera** — Reports per-pixel brightness changes; ultra-low latency.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Concept:** Stereo vision provides direct metric depth, while monocular vision estimates depth using learned priors (size, motion parallax, vanishing points). Tesla famously uses monocular cameras + neural depth estimation.

---

### 2.2 The Pinhole Camera Model (Foundational Theory)

Every automotive camera is modeled mathematically by the **pinhole projection equation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} \\[4pt]
y &= f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $(X, Y, Z)$ = 3D world coordinates of a point
- $(x, y)$ = 2D image-plane coordinates (in pixels, after scaling by pixel size)
- $f$ = **focal length** (in mm or pixels)

In homogeneous matrix form (used in all real-world automotive calibration):

$$
s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{\text{Intrinsic Matrix } K} \cdot \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} R & \vert & t \end{bmatrix}}_{\text{Extrinsic } [R \mid t]} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Where:
- $(u, v)$ = pixel coordinates
- $s$ = arbitrary scale (depth)
- $(c_x, c_y)$ = principal point (image center)
- $R, t$ = rotation and translation of camera w.r.t. world frame

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Calibration Matters in Cars:** A mis-calibrated intrinsic matrix $K$ by even **1 pixel** at the principal point can cause lane offsets of **30+ cm at 50 m range** — this is why every OEM performs **end-of-line (EOL) camera calibration** at the factory using checkerboard targets.

---

### 2.3 Depth from Stereo Vision — The Disparity Equation

For a stereo camera pair with baseline $B$ and focal length $f$, the **depth $Z$** of a scene point is:

$$
Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}
$$

Where $d = x_L - x_R$ is the **disparity** (pixel shift of the same point between left and right images).

**Critical Observations:**
- Disparity is **inversely proportional** to depth → nearby objects have large disparity.
- Doubling the baseline $B$ doubles the depth accuracy but reduces the common FoV.
- Sub-pixel disparity resolution (~**0.1 px**) is essential for ranges > **100 m**.

---

### 2.4 Optical Flow for Motion Perception

Optical flow gives the apparent 2D motion of pixels between consecutive frames. The **Lucas–Kanade** sparse optical flow equation is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} I_x \\ I_y \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix} + I_t = 0
$$

Where:
- $I_x, I_y$ = spatial image gradients
- $I_t$ = temporal gradient
- $(u, v)$ = optical flow vector

Solving over a small window yields the velocity of features — used for **Egomotion Estimation**, **Structure from Motion (SfM)**, and **Visual Odometry**.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Used For | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x = f \cdot X / Z$, $y = f \cdot Y / Z$ | Pinhole projection | Camera model | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | $s \tilde{\mathbf{u}} = K [R \mid t] \tilde{\mathbf{X}}$ | Full projection matrix | Calibration | ★★★★ |
| 3 | $Z = f B / d$ | Stereo depth | Distance estimation | ★★★★★ |
| 4 | $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$ | Optical flow constraint | Motion estimation | ★★★★ |
| 5 | $\text{TTC} = \Delta d / (d_{t-1} - d_t)$ | Time-to-Collision | Forward collision warning | ★★★★★ |
| 6 | $\text{FoV} = 2 \arctan(w / 2f)$ | Field of view | Camera selection | ★★★ |
| 7 | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}(V_{signal} / V_{noise})$ | Image quality | Sensor benchmarking | ★★★ |
| 8 | $\text{DR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(I_{max} / I_{min})$ | Dynamic range | HDR imaging | ★★★ |
| 9 | $\text{Pinhole F-number} = f / D$ | Aperture | Low-light performance | ★★ |
| 10 | $E_{pixel} = L \cdot A \cdot \cos\theta \cdot t / N^2$ | Exposure equation | Image exposure | ★★ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Character Trap:** In your answer sheet, if you ever write $Z = fB / \vert d \vert$, ensure you use a clean vertical bar notation. In our KTU answer keys, we use $Z = fB/d$ where $d > 0$ by convention to avoid absolute-value clutter.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Vision Module Used | Industry Example |
|---|---|---|
| Adaptive Cruise Control | Forward stereo / mono + radar fusion | Bosch, Continental |
| Lane Keep Assist | Front mono camera | Mobileye EyeQ |
| Auto Emergency Braking | Front camera + radar | Tesla AEB |
| Traffic Sign Recognition | Front mono + CNN | Audi, BMW |
| Blind Spot Detection | Side fisheye | Volvo BLIS |
| 360° Surround View | 4 fisheye cameras | Nissan AVM |
| Driver Drowsiness Detection | In-cabin IR + eye-tracking | Bosch Driver Drowsiness Detection |
| Autonomous Driving (L4) | Multi-camera + AI | Waymo, Cruise |
| Night Vision | Thermal IR | Mercedes Night View Assist |
| Automatic Parking | 4× fisheye + ultrasonic | Tesla AutoPark, BMW |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Students often forget that **ADAS Level 0–2** uses vision primarily for *warning*, while **Level 3+** uses vision for *control*. This distinction is often asked as a 3-mark question.

## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Time-to-Collision (TTC) from Stereo Vision

**Problem Statement:** A vehicle is approaching a stationary obstacle at a constant velocity $v$. The stereo camera (baseline $B$, focal length $f$) measures disparities at two consecutive frames $t-1$ and $t$ as $d_{t-1}$ and $d_t$. Derive an expression for **Time-to-Collision** $\text{TTC}$.

---

#### Step 1 — Write the Depth at Each Frame

Using the stereo depth formula:
$$
Z_{t-1} = \frac{f B}{d_{t-1}}
$$

After one frame interval $\Delta t$:
$$
Z_t = \frac{f B}{d_t}
$$

---

#### Step 2 — Compute Change in Depth (Closure Rate)

The vehicle closes in by an amount:
$$
\Delta Z = Z_{t-1} - Z_t = f B \left( \frac{1}{d_{t-1}} - \frac{1}{d_t} \right)
$$

---

#### Step 3 — Compute Closing Velocity

The relative velocity $v_{rel}$ of approach is:
$$
v_{rel} = \frac{\Delta Z}{\Delta t} = \frac{f B}{\Delta t} \left( \frac{1}{d_{t-1}} - \frac{1}{d_t} \right)
$$

---

#### Step 4 — Define Time-to-Collision

Time-to-Collision is the time remaining before impact, i.e., current distance divided by closing velocity:
$$
\text{TTC} = \frac{Z_t}{v_{rel}}
$$

---

#### Step 5 — Substitute & Simplify

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TTC} &= \frac{\dfrac{f B}{d_t}}{\dfrac{f B}{\Delta t}\left( \dfrac{1}{d_{t-1}} - \dfrac{1}{d_t} \right)} \\[8pt]
&= \frac{\dfrac{1}{d_t}}{\dfrac{1}{\Delta t} \cdot \dfrac{d_t - d_{t-1}}{d_{t-1} d_t}} \\[8pt]
&= \frac{\dfrac{1}{d_t}}{\dfrac{d_t - d_{t-1}}{\Delta t \, d_{t-1} d_t}} \\[8pt]
&= \frac{d_{t-1}}{d_t - d_{t-1}} \cdot \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Step 6 — Final Compact Form

$$
\boxed{\text{TTC} = \frac{d_{t-1}}{d_t - d_{t-1}} \cdot \Delta t}
$$

Equivalently, using the inverse-depth form:
$$
\text{TTC} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{d_t} - \frac{1}{d_{t-1}}} \cdot \frac{\Delta t}{1} = \frac{\Delta t}{\Delta(1/d)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Notice that **TTC can be computed using only disparity measurements — no explicit speed or distance knowledge is needed.** This is why stereo cameras are so powerful for **Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB)**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Lane Curvature from Inverse Perspective Mapping (IPM)

**Problem Statement:** A forward-facing camera captures lane markings on a flat road. Derive the relationship between image-plane coordinates $(u, v)$ and the road-plane coordinates $(X, Y)$ using IPM, and show how **lane curvature $\kappa$** can be extracted.

---

#### Step 1 — Camera Setup

Place the camera at height $h$ above the road, with pitch angle $\theta$ and focal length $f$. Assume the optical axis is along the $Z$-axis (initially).

The general forward-looking camera transformation is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= h \cdot \cot(\theta + \arctan((v - c_y)/f_y)) \\
Y &= h \cdot \frac{(u - c_x)}{f_y} \cdot \cot(\theta + \arctan((v - c_y)/f_y))
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Step 2 — For Small Pitch (Standard Driving Case)

For typical automotive pitch $\theta \approx 1°$–$3°$, we approximate:
$$
\cot(\theta + \alpha) \approx \frac{1}{\alpha + \theta}
$$

This gives the simplified IPM:
$$
\begin{aligned}
X &\approx \frac{h \cdot f_y}{(v - c_y) + f_y \theta} \\
Y &\approx \frac{(u - c_x) \cdot h}{(v - c_y) + f_y \theta}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Step 3 — Lane Model

Lane markings in road coordinates follow a **2nd-order polynomial** (parabolic lane model):
$$
Y(X) = a X^2 + b X + c
$$

Here, $a$ relates to curvature $\kappa$ as:
$$
\kappa = \frac{2a}{\left(1 + (2aX + b)^2\right)^{3/2}}
$$

For straight-ahead vision ($X$ small), we approximate:
$$
\kappa \approx 2a
$$

---

#### Step 4 — Solve for Lane Coefficients

Using detected image points $(u_i, v_i)$, convert each to $(X_i, Y_i)$ via IPM, then fit the parabola using **least squares**:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} Y_1 \\ Y_2 \\ \vdots \\ Y_n \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} X_1^2 & X_1 & 1 \\ X_2^2 & X_2 & 1 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\ X_n^2 & X_n & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a \\ b \\ c \end{bmatrix}
$$

Solve via:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} a \\ b \\ c \end{bmatrix} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{Y}
$$

---

#### Step 5 — Output

$$
\boxed{\kappa_{\text{lane}} \approx 2a}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This curvature $\kappa$ is fed directly into the **Lane Keeping Assist (LKA)** or **Lane Centering Control (LCC)** algorithm, which then computes the steering correction $\delta = K_p \cdot e_{lat} + K_d \cdot \dot{e}_{lat}$, where $e_{lat}$ is lateral offset.

---

| Lane Detection | Front camera | Edge detection + Hough / CNN segmentation | Lane polynomial + curvature | Worn lane markings, shadows |
| Vehicle Detection | Front/side camera | YOLO, Faster R-CNN | Bounding boxes + class | Occlusion, night-time |
| Pedestrian Detection | Front camera | HOG + SVM (legacy) / CNN (modern) | Pedestrian boxes + distance | Crowded scenes, costumes |
| Traffic Sign Recognition | Front camera | CNN (ResNet, MobileNet) | Sign class + confidence | Motion blur, occlusion |
| Traffic Light Detection | Front camera | Color thresholding + shape | Red/Yellow/Green state | Backlight, faded LEDs |
| Driver Monitoring | In-cabin IR | Eye landmark (MediaPipe) + EAR | Drowsiness score | Glasses, sunglasses |
| Parking Assist | 4 fisheye cameras | Homography warping | Bird's-eye view | Curbs, low contrast |
| Visual Odometry | Front camera | Feature tracking (ORB, SIFT) | Ego-motion (R, t) | Textureless roads |
| Depth Estimation | Mono / stereo camera | SGBM, PSMNet, DPT | Per-pixel depth | Reflective surfaces |

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Board Pattern)

**Q:** A vehicle is fitted with a stereo camera of baseline $B = 120$ mm and focal length $f = 4$ mm. The disparity of a pedestrian detected at a given instant is $d = 12$ pixels. If the pixel pitch is $4.65 \,\mu m$, compute the distance $Z$ to the pedestrian.

**Solution:**

Convert focal length to pixels:
$$
f_{px} = \frac{f}{\text{pixel pitch}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{4.65 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 860 \text{ pixels}
$$

Apply stereo depth formula:
$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= \frac{f_{px} \cdot B}{d} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{860 \times 0.120}{12} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{103.2}{12} \\[4pt]
&= 8.6 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{Z = 8.6 \text{ m}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Note:** Always convert focal length to pixels first when disparity is given in pixels. This is where 70% of students lose 1–2 marks in KTU numericals.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example — Time to Collision (TTC)

**Q:** A stereo camera reports disparities at two consecutive frames (separated by $\Delta t = 33$ ms) as $d_{t-1} = 24$ px and $d_t = 16$ px. Compute the TTC.

**Solution:**

Using the derived formula:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TTC} &= \frac{d_{t-1}}{d_t - d_{t-1}} \cdot \Delta t \\[4pt]
&= \frac{24}{16 - 24} \cdot 0.033 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{24}{-8} \cdot 0.033 \\[4pt]
&= -3 \cdot 0.033 \\[4pt]
&= -0.099 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since disparity is increasing ($d_t > d_{t-1}$ — wait, here $d_t < d_{t-1}$, meaning the object is moving **closer**, but our sign convention depends on whether disparity increases with proximity). For a closing-in scenario where the object is approaching, disparity **increases** → $d_t > d_{t-1}$.

In our example, $d_{t-1} > d_t$, meaning the object is **moving away** → TTC is negative (no collision threat).

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Convention Pitfall:** Always verify whether $d_t - d_{t-1}$ is positive (approaching) or negative (receding) before reporting TTC. AEB systems trigger only when TTC < **2.5 s** AND vehicle is approaching.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

---

**Q1.** Define the term **"Automotive Vision"** and list **any four key applications** of vision systems in modern cars.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Automotive Vision refers to the use of camera-based optical sensors combined with image processing and artificial intelligence algorithms to enable a vehicle to perceive and interpret its surroundings in real time.

**Applications (4 × 0.375 = 1.5 marks):**
1. Lane Detection and Lane Keep Assist
2. Forward Collision Warning / AEB
3. Traffic Sign Recognition (TSR)
4. Pedestrian and Cyclist Detection
5. Driver Drowsiness Monitoring
6. 360° Surround View for Parking

> [!TIP]
> Examiners expect at least **one application paired with its triggering ADAS function** (e.g., "Pedestrian Detection → AEB"). Pairing fetches extra credit.

---

**Q2.** With a neat diagram, explain the working principle of a **stereo vision camera** used in ADAS.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A stereo vision system uses two cameras separated by a known baseline $B$ (typically 120–350 mm in vehicles). Both cameras capture the same scene from slightly different viewpoints.

- (1 mark) The two images are rectified to remove lens distortion.
- (1 mark) A stereo-matching algorithm (e.g., SGBM) computes per-pixel disparity $d$.
- (0.5 marks) Depth is obtained from $Z = fB/d$.
- (0.5 marks) Applications include obstacle detection, AEB, and ACC in cars like **Subaru EyeSight**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme - Module 3]`

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the **pinhole camera projection equations** in matrix form and explain the role of the **intrinsic matrix K** and **extrinsic matrix [R∣t]**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a block diagram the **end-to-end image processing pipeline** in an automotive vision system, from photon capture to actuator response. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Pinhole Projection Derivation (7 marks):**

Consider a 3D world point $\mathbf{X}_w = [X, Y, Z, 1]^T$ in homogeneous coordinates.

**Step 1:** Transform from world frame to camera frame using extrinsic matrix:
$$
\mathbf{X}_c = [R \mid t] \cdot \mathbf{X}_w = \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & r_{13} & t_x \\ r_{22} & r_{22} & r_{23} & t_y \\ r_{31} & r_{32} & r_{33} & t_z \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply pinhole projection to obtain normalized image coordinates:
$$
x_n = \frac{X_c}{Z_c}, \quad y_n = \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply intrinsic matrix $K$ to convert normalized coordinates to pixel coordinates:
$$
s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K \begin{bmatrix} x_n \\ y_n \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_n \\ y_n \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Combined Equation (3 marks):**
$$
s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K \cdot [R \mid t] \cdot \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Role of K (2 marks):**
- $K$ contains the camera's **focal length** $(f_x, f_y)$, **principal point** $(c_x, c_y)$, and **skew**.
- It is determined via **camera calibration** (e.g., Zhang's method using a checkerboard target).
- Maps camera-frame metric units → image pixel units.

**Role of [R∣t] (2 marks):**
- $R$: 3×3 rotation matrix; describes orientation of camera w.r.t. world.
- $t$: 3×1 translation vector; describes position of camera origin in world frame.
- Together, $[R \mid t]$ converts world coordinates to camera coordinates — essential for **multi-camera extrinsic calibration** in surround-view systems.

---

**(b) Image Processing Pipeline (7 marks):**

The complete pipeline consists of the following sequential stages:

**Stage 1 — Optical Path (1 mark):**
Light from the scene enters through a multi-element lens group that focuses rays onto the sensor. A bandpass IR-cut filter removes unwanted infrared.

**Stage 2 — Sensor (1 mark):**
A **CMOS image sensor** (e.g., Sony IMX490, 5 MP, 60 fps) converts photons into electrons via photodiodes. A Bayer color filter array (CFA) is overlaid for color capture.

**Stage 3 — Image Signal Processor (ISP) (2 marks):**
The ISP performs:
- Black-level correction
- Lens shading correction
- Demosaicing (Bayer → RGB)
- Noise reduction (NR)
- HDR tone mapping (merging long/short exposures for **120 dB** dynamic range)
- Auto-exposure and auto-white-balance feedback loops

**Stage 4 — Perception AI (2 marks):**
The clean RGB frames are fed to neural network accelerators (e.g., **Mobileye EyeQ5, NVIDIA Orin**) running:
- Object detection (YOLO, EfficientDet)
- Semantic segmentation (U-Net, DeepLab)
- Lane detection (SCNN, LaneNet)
- Driver monitoring (MediaPipe, EAR-based CNN)

**Stage 5 — Fusion and Decision (1 mark):**
Camera outputs are fused with radar/lidar in the central ECU. A planning module computes trajectory; control module actuates steering, brake, throttle via **drive-by-wire**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **stereo vision depth estimation principle**. Derive the depth equation $Z = fB/d$ and discuss how it is used to compute **Time-to-Collision (TTC)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the working principle of any **three** of the following automotive vision systems: (i) Lane Departure Warning, (ii) Traffic Sign Recognition, (iii) Driver Drowsiness Detection, (iv) 360° Surround View. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Stereo Vision Depth + TTC (7 marks):**

**Geometric Setup (2 marks):**
A stereo camera consists of two lenses with parallel optical axes, separated by baseline $B$. A 3D point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ projects onto the left image at $x_L$ and the right image at $x_R$ (assuming rectified epipolar geometry, so $y_L = y_R$).

**Derivation (3 marks):**
Using similar triangles:
$$
\frac{x_L}{f} = \frac{X}{Z}, \quad \frac{x_R}{f} = \frac{X - B}{Z}
$$
Subtracting:
$$
d = x_L - x_R = \frac{f B}{Z}
$$
Solving for $Z$:
$$
\boxed{Z = \frac{f B}{d}}
$$

**TTC Computation (2 marks):**
Measuring disparity at two consecutive frames gives:
$$
\text{TTC} = \frac{d_{t-1}}{d_t - d_{t-1}} \cdot \Delta t
$$
This is used in AEB systems to trigger braking when TTC falls below a threshold (typically **2.5 s** at 50 km/h).

---

**(b) Three Vision Systems (7 marks = 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 for diagrams):**

**(i) Lane Departure Warning (LDW) — 2 marks:**
- The forward camera captures the road ahead.
- Image is converted to bird's-eye view via IPM.
- Edge detection (Sobel/Canny) finds lane markings.
- A lane model (parabolic fit) estimates lateral offset $e_{lat}$ and departure angle.
- If $e_{lat}$ exceeds a threshold and turn signal is OFF, an **audible/visual alert** is triggered.

**(ii) Traffic Sign Recognition (TSR) — 2 marks:**
- Front camera captures scene.
- **Two-stage detection:** (a) Region proposal using color/shape heuristics → (b) CNN classifier (ResNet-18 or MobileNet) classifies sign into Speed Limit, Stop, Yield, etc.
- Output displayed on dashboard and used for **Speed Limit Assist**.

**(iii) Driver Drowsiness Detection — 2 marks:**
- An IR camera (mounted on steering column) captures driver's face even at night.
- **Eye Aspect Ratio (EAR)** is computed from 6 eye landmarks:
  $$ \text{EAR} = \frac{\vert p_2 - p_6 \vert + \vert p_3 - p_5 \vert}{2 \cdot \vert p_1 - p_4 \vert} $$
- EAR < 0.2 for sustained 0.5 s → **drowsiness alert**.
- Head pose (pitch/yaw) and PERCLOS (Percentage Eye Closure) are also monitored.

**(iv) [Optional] 360° Surround View — 2 marks:**
- Four fisheye cameras (front, rear, left, right) capture surroundings.
- Each image is **dewarpped** using precomputed lens-distortion maps.
- A homography $H_i$ warps each image onto a common ground plane.
- Images are blended using **multiband blending** to produce a seamless bird's-eye view displayed on the infotainment screen.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Vision Questions:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention for Disparity:** Students forget that disparity is **positive when object is in front** and **approaches zero as Z → ∞**. Always state the convention.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Convert Focal Length to Pixels:** If disparity $d$ is in pixels, focal length $f$ in the stereo formula MUST also be in pixels. Mixing units (e.g., $f$ in mm with $d$ in pixels) is the #1 numerical error.
>
> 3. **Conflating Monocular Depth vs Stereo Depth:** Monocular depth is **estimated** (no metric guarantee); stereo depth is **measured**. Examiners check this distinction.
>
> 4. **Skipping the Diagram:** A 14-mark question without a block diagram loses at least 2 marks even if the text is perfect. Always include a labeled flowchart.
>
> 5. **Not Mentioning Real OEMs:** Citing real-world examples (Tesla, Mobileye, Subaru, Bosch) demonstrates industry awareness and earns extra credit.
>
> 6. **Ignoring Failure Modes:** A complete answer must address *what happens in rain, fog, or at night* — this is part of "engineering judgment" assessed in KTU.

---

- [ ] Full projection: $s\tilde{u} = K[R\mid t]\tilde{X}$
- [ ] Stereo depth: $Z = fB/d$
- [ ] TTC: $\text{TTC} = \dfrac{d_{t-1}}{d_t - d_{t-1}}\cdot \Delta t$
- [ ] Optical flow: $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$
- [ ] EAR: ratio of vertical to horizontal eye distances

#### 🏗️ System Components
- [ ] **Lens** (optics) → **CMOS sensor** (photons → electrons) → **ISP** (cleaning) → **AI Accelerator** (perception) → **ECU** (fusion + decision) → **Actuators**
- [ ] Four surround-view cameras → homography warping → bird's-eye view

#### 🏢 Industry Touchpoints (Name-Drop for Marks)
- [ ] **Tesla** — vision-only Autopilot (no radar since 2021)
- [ ] **Mobileye** (Intel) — EyeQ chips, used by BMW, VW, Ford
- [ ] **Subaru EyeSight** — stereo-only ADAS
- [ ] **NVIDIA Drive** — Orin/Thor SoC for autonomy
- [ ] **Bosch, Continental, ZF** — Tier-1 vision module suppliers
- [ ] **Waymo** — L4 robotaxi with multi-camera + lidar

#### ⚠️ Critical Failure Conditions
- [ ] Direct sunlight glare → use HDR (120 dB)
- [ ] Night driving → IR illumination + large aperture
