When the limiting current density **j_L** is approached, the cell "suffocates": $$\eta_{conc} = - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(1 - \frac{j}{j_L}\right)$$ This term diverges to infinity as **j → j_L**, defining the **maximum usable current**. --- ## 2.2 The Complete Operational Voltage Equation $$\boxed{\,V_{cell} = \underbrace{E_{Nernst}}_{\text{thermodynamic max}} - \underbrace{\frac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln\!\left(\frac{j}{j_0}\right)}_{\text{activation loss}} - \underbrace{j \cdot R_{ASR}}_{\text{ohmic loss}} + \underbrace{\frac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(1 - \frac{j}{j_L}\right)}_{\text{concentration loss}}\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sign Convention Used in KTU 2024 Module 4:** All loss terms (η) are **positive numbers** that are *subtracted* from E_Nernst. The sign of η_conc as written above is a *negative quantity* because ln(1 − j/j_L) is negative — this is the correct mathematical form. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready) | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Magnitude | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_{Nernst} = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{H_2}P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$ | Reversible open-circuit voltage | **1.0 – 1.23 V** | V | | 2 | $\eta_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln\!\left(\frac{j}{j_0}\right)$ | Activation polarization (Tafel) | **0.05 – 0.20 V** at low j | V | | 3 | $\eta_{ohmic} = j \cdot R_{ASR}$ | Ohmic polarization | Linear in j | V | | 4 | $\eta_{conc} = -\frac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(1 - \frac{j}{j_L}\right)$ | Concentration polarization | **0.05 – 0.40 V** near j_L | V | | 5 | $V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$ | Net operational voltage | **0.6 – 0.75 V** at rated load | V | | 6 | $P_{stack} = N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I$ | Stack power (N = number of cells) | FCEV stacks: 300–400 cells | W | | 7 | $\eta_{voltage} = \frac{V_{cell}}{E^{\circ}} \times 100\%$ | Voltage efficiency | **50–60%** | % | | 8 | $j_L = \frac{n F D C_b}{\delta}$ | Limiting current density (Fick's law) | **1.0 – 2.0 A/cm²** | A/cm² | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** In automotive FCEVs (Toyota Mirai 2, Hyundai Nexo), the fuel cell stack operates between **0.65 V and 0.80 V per cell** to balance power density vs. durability. A 100 kW stack therefore contains roughly **300–400 cells** stacked in series, generating ~250–300 V DC, which is then boosted by a DC-DC converter to the ~650 V traction battery bus. --- ## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Loss — Engineering Logic | Loss | Physical Origin | Engineering Mitigation | |---|---|---| | **Activation** | ORR has high activation energy on Pt | Pt-alloy catalysts, elevated temperature | | **Ohmic** | Membrane proton resistance + contact resistance | Thinner membranes (Nafion-211), better bipolar plate coatings | | **Concentration** | Slow diffusion of H₂/O₂ to reaction sites | Higher gas pressure, thinner GDL, better flow-field design | | **Mixed (cross-over)** | H₂ leaks through membrane | Membrane reinforcement, low crossover membranes | > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** When asked *"Why is operating voltage less than E_Nernst?"*, always answer with **all three losses** in order: activation, ohmic, concentration. Stating only ohmic loss is the #1 reason students lose 2 marks in Part A. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Thermodynamic E° to Operational V_cell ### Step 1 — Gibbs Free Energy to Reversible Voltage The maximum electrical work extractable per mole of H₂ consumed equals the change in Gibbs free energy: $$\Delta G^{\circ} = -n F E^{\circ}$$ Solving for E°: $$E^{\circ} = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{n F}$$ **Numerical Evaluation:** - $\Delta G^{\circ}$ for the reaction H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O (liquid) = **−237.2 kJ/mol** at 25 °C - n = 2, F = 96,485 C/mol $$E^{\circ} = -\frac{(-237{,}200 \text{ J/mol})}{(2)(96{,}485 \text{ C/mol})}$$ $$E^{\circ} = \frac{237{,}200}{192{,}970}$$ $$\boxed{E^{\circ} = 1.229 \text{ V}}$$ > **Logic:** The negative sign of ΔG° (spontaneous reaction) gives a positive E° — a fuel cell *generates* a positive voltage. ### Step 2 — From Standard to Actual: The Nernst Equation Derivation Consider the general cell reaction. The change in Gibbs free energy at non-standard conditions: $$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$ where **Q** is the reaction quotient. Substituting ΔG = −nFE and ΔG° = −nFE°: $$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$ Dividing through by **−nF**: $$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$ For the fuel cell reaction H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O, applying partial pressures as activities: $$Q = \frac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}$$ Therefore: $$E_{Nernst} = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left( \frac{P_{H_2} \, P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}} \right)$$ ### Step 3 — Numerically Evaluating E_Nernst at Typical FCEV Conditions Assume: **T = 353 K (80 °C)**, P_H₂ = 3 atm, P_O₂ = 3 atm, P_H₂O = 0.5 atm (product water vapor at 80 °C saturation). $$\frac{RT}{nF} = \frac{(8.314)(353)}{(2)(96{,}485)} = \frac{2934.84}{192{,}970} = 0.01521 \text{ V}$$ $$\ln\!\left( \frac{(3)(3)^{1/2}}{0.5} \right) = \ln\!\left( \frac{3 \times 1.732}{0.5} \right) = \ln(10.392) = 2.340$$ $$E_{Nernst} = 1.229 + (0.01521)(2.340)$$ $$E_{Nernst} = 1.229 + 0.0356$$ $$\boxed{E_{Nernst} \approx 1.265 \text{ V}}$$ > **Engineering Interpretation:** Operating at 3 atm and 80 °C gives a ~36 mV voltage boost over standard conditions — a key reason FCEV stacks use **pressurized reactant loops**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Activation Loss from Butler-Volmer to Tafel The **Butler-Volmer equation** governs electrochemical kinetics: $$j = j_0 \left[ \exp\!\left( \frac{\alpha n F \eta_{act}}{RT} \right) - \exp\!\left( -\frac{(1-\alpha) n F \eta_{act}}{RT} \right) \right]$$ For large cathodic overpotentials (η_act > ~50 mV) — typical for fuel cells — the first exponential dominates and the second becomes negligible: $$j \approx j_0 \exp\!\left( \frac{\alpha n F \eta_{act}}{RT} \right)$$ Taking the natural logarithm of both sides: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{j}{j_0}\right) = \frac{\alpha n F \eta_{act}}{RT}$$ Solving for η_act: $$\boxed{\eta_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln\!\left(\frac{j}{j_0}\right)}$$ ### Step 3.2.a — Numerical Evaluation of Activation Loss Assume: **T = 353 K, α = 0.5, n = 2, j = 1.0 A/cm², j₀ = 10⁻⁷ A/cm²** $$\frac{RT}{\alpha n F} = \frac{(8.314)(353)}{(0.5)(2)(96{,}485)} = \frac{2934.84}{96{,}485} = 0.0304 \text{ V}$$ $$\ln\!\left(\frac{1.0}{10^{-7}}\right) = \ln(10^7) = 7 \times \ln(10) = 7 \times 2.3026 = 16.118$$ $$\eta_{act} = (0.0304)(16.118) = 0.490 \text{ V}$$ > **Critical Insight:** At a low exchange current density of 10⁻⁷ A/cm², activation loss *alone* is **0.49 V** — almost 40% of the entire E_Nernst. This is precisely why the ORR (with j₀ ≈ 10⁻⁹–10⁻¹⁰ A/cm² on Pt) is the **rate-limiting** step and why high platinum loading is required. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Concentration Loss from Fick's First Law At steady state, reactant flux to the electrode surface (Fick's law) equals the electrochemical consumption: $$j = n F D \frac{C_b - C_s}{\delta}$$ where: - **D** = diffusion coefficient (cm²/s) - **C_b** = bulk concentration (mol/cm³) - **C_s** = surface concentration (mol/cm³) - **δ** = diffusion layer thickness (cm) The **limiting current density** j_L corresponds to C_s → 0 (the surface is "starved"): $$j_L = n F D \frac{C_b}{\delta}$$ Solving for the surface concentration ratio: $$\frac{C_s}{C_b} = 1 - \frac{j}{j_L}$$ The concentration overpotential is the Nernst loss from this reduced surface activity: $$\eta_{conc} = \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(\frac{C_b}{C_s}\right) = -\frac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(1 - \frac{j}{j_L}\right)$$ ### Step 3.3.a — Numerical Evaluation of Concentration Loss Assume: **T = 353 K, j = 1.0 A/cm², j_L = 1.5 A/cm²** $$\frac{RT}{nF} = 0.01521 \text{ V (from before)}$$ $$\ln\!\left(1 - \frac{1.0}{1.5}\right) = \ln(0.333) = -1.0986$$ $$\eta_{conc} = -(0.01521)(-1.0986) = 0.0167 \text{ V}$$ > **Interpretation:** At 67% of the limiting current, concentration loss is only ~17 mV. But at j = 0.95 j_L, the same formula gives η_conc ≈ 0.049 V, and at j = 0.99 j_L, it explodes to **0.10 V** — the cell "collapses" rapidly. --- ## 3.4 Complete Numerical Example — KTU 2024 Exam Style > [!IMPORTANT] > **Worked Numerical (Full Marks Solution Template):** A PEM fuel cell operates at **353 K** with H₂ at 3 atm, O₂ at 3 atm, product water vapor at 0.5 atm. The cathode has **α = 0.5, j₀ = 10⁻⁷ A/cm², R_ASR = 0.05 Ω·cm²**, and **j_L = 1.5 A/cm²**. **Compute V_cell at j = 1.0 A/cm².** | Term | Formula | Value | |---|---|---| | E_Nernst | $1.229 + 0.01521 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{3 \cdot 3^{1/2}}{0.5}\right)$ | **1.265 V** | | η_act | $0.0304 \times \ln(10^7)$ | **0.490 V** | | η_ohmic | $1.0 \times 0.05$ | **0.050 V** | | η_conc | $-0.01521 \times \ln(1 - 1/1.5)$ | **0.017 V** | | **V_cell** | $1.265 - 0.490 - 0.050 - 0.017$ | **≈ 0.708 V** | $$\boxed{V_{cell} \approx 0.71 \text{ V} \text{ at } j = 1.0 \text{ A/cm}^2}$$ > **Reality Check:** This matches the published Toyota Mirai 2 single-cell operating voltage (≈ 0.70 V) at rated current density. ✅ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Polarization Curve — Voltage vs. Current Density The following Mermaid block renders the canonical **fuel cell polarization curve**, mapping the three loss regions against the operational voltage. ```mermaid graph LR axisY["Cell Voltage V_cell V"]:::axis axisX["Current Density j A per cm2"]:::axis E0["E Nernst 1.23 V
Reversible Open Circuit"]:::nernst etaAct["Activation Loss Region
Tafel drop at low j
dominates below 0.1 A per cm2"]:::act etaOhm["Ohmic Loss Region
Linear V equals E minus jR
dominates 0.1 to 0.8 A per cm2"]:::ohm etaConc["Concentration Loss Region
Mass transport starvation
dominates above 0.8 A per cm2"]:::conc ratedPt["Rated Operating Point
j equals 1.0 A per cm2
Vcell equals 0.70 V"]:::rated jL["Limiting Current jL
Voltage collapses to zero"]:::limit axisY --- E0 E0 -.- etaAct etaAct -.- etaOhm etaOhm -.- etaConc etaConc -.- jL etaOhm -.- ratedPt classDef axis fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef nernst fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef act fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ohm fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef conc fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#ad1457,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef rated fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef limit fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Voltage Composition The following architecture block diagram shows how the operational cell voltage is *built up* from thermodynamic ideal to real-world load, layer by layer. ```mermaid graph TB subgraph layer1["THERMODYNAMIC LAYER"] node1["Standard Enthalpy and E equals 1.229 V
Delta G equals nFE at 25 C 1 atm"] end subgraph layer2["REVERSIBLE OPERATING POINT"] node2["E Nernst
Function of T, P_H2, P_O2, P_H2O
Typical 1.20 to 1.27 V"] end subgraph layer3["IRREVERSIBLE LOSSES SUBTRACTED"] node3a["Activation Loss eta_act
Tafel kinetics
0.05 to 0.50 V"] node3b["Ohmic Loss eta_ohmic
j times R_ASR
0.02 to 0.10 V"] node3c["Concentration Loss eta_conc
Mass transport limit
0.01 to 0.40 V"] end subgraph layer4["FINAL OPERATIONAL OUTPUT"] node4["OPERATIONAL V_cell
0.60 to 0.75 V per cell
Stack of 300 to 400 cells gives 250 to 300 V"] end node1 -->|"Add RT over nF ln Q"| node2 node2 -->|"Subtract"| node3a node2 -->|"Subtract"| node3b node2 -->|"Subtract"| node3c node3a -->|"Combine"| node4 node3b -->|"Combine"| node4 node3c -->|"Combine"| node4 classDef thermo fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef revers fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef loss fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef out fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 class node1 thermo class node2 revers class node3a,node3b,node3c loss class node4 out ``` --- ## 4.3 Physical Schematic: Where Each Loss Originates Inside a PEMFC ```mermaid graph TB anode["ANODE: H2 in
Catalyst Pt-C
H2 dissociation to 2H plus plus 2e minus"]:::anodeNode membrane["PROTON EXCHANGE MEMBRANE
Nafion 115
R_ASR 0.05 ohm cm squared
OHMIC LOSS occurs here"]:::membraneNode cathode["CATHODE: O2 in
Catalyst Pt-C
ORR: O2 plus 4H plus plus 4e minus gives 2H2O
ACTIVATION LOSS occurs here"]:::cathodeNode gdl["GAS DIFFUSION LAYER
CONCENTRATION LOSS occurs here
Reactant starvation at high j"]:::gdlNode bp["BIPOLAR PLATE
Electron collection
Contact resistance contributes to OHMIC"]:::bpNode out["H2O out plus e minus out plus useful DC power"]:::output anode -->|"H plus ions migrate"| membrane membrane -->|"H plus ions arrive"| cathode cathode -->|"Water produced"| out gdl -.->|"Feed O2 to cathode"| cathode gdl -.->|"Feed H2 to anode"| anode bp -->|"Collect electrons"| cathode bp -->|"Deliver electrons"| out classDef anodeNode fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef membraneNode fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cathodeNode fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef gdlNode fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef bpNode fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#424242,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` > [!TIP] > **Visual Memory Hook for Exams:** *Activation is at the **cathode** (ORR is slow). Ohmic is in the **membrane + contacts**. Concentration is in the **GDL** (reactant delivery).* # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper] **Q: Define operational fuel cell voltage. Why is it always less than the Nernst voltage?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The operational fuel cell voltage is the actual, measurable terminal voltage of a single cell under load (typically **0.6–0.75 V** for a PEMFC). It is always less than the Nernst (reversible) voltage because of three irreversible losses — **activation polarization** (slow ORR kinetics at the cathode), **ohmic polarization** (resistance to proton/electron flow), and **concentration polarization** (mass-transport limitation of reactants at high current). The relationship is: > > $$V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$$ > > **(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — naming three losses; 1 mark — equation)** --- ### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest Paper] **Q: List the three regions of the polarization curve and identify the dominant loss in each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | # | Region | Current Density Range | Dominant Loss | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Activation | j < 0.1 A/cm² | Activation polarization (Tafel drop) | | 2 | Ohmic | 0.1 < j < 0.8 A/cm² | Ohmic polarization (linear j·R) | | 3 | Concentration | j > 0.8 A/cm² | Concentration polarization (mass-transport limit) | > **(1 mark per row × 3 = 3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 MARKS) — *Theoretical + Numerical* **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 4, Q4(a) or Q4(b) Choice]** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Q: Derive the Nernst equation for the hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell from thermodynamic first principles. State the standard E° value and its significance.** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Starting point (1 mark):** The maximum reversible work from a constant-T, constant-P electrochemical reaction equals the negative of the Gibbs free energy change: $$W_{max} = -\Delta G = nFE$$ **Step 2 — Standard state (1 mark):** At standard conditions (25 °C, 1 atm): $$\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ} \implies E^{\circ} = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF}$$ **Step 3 — Non-standard conditions (2 marks):** Generalizing with the reaction quotient Q: $$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT\ln Q$$ $$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT\ln Q$$ **Step 4 — Final Nernst equation (1 mark):** $$E_{Nernst} = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$ For H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O, with partial pressures as activities: $$E_{Nernst} = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left( \frac{P_{H_2}\,P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}} \right)$$ **Step 5 — Numerical significance (2 marks):** - ΔG° = −237.2 kJ/mol → **E° = 1.229 V** at 25 °C - Significance: this is the **theoretical maximum open-circuit voltage**; real cells always operate below it due to irreversibilities. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Q: A PEM fuel cell operates at 353 K with P_H₂ = 3 atm, P_O₂ = 3 atm, P_H₂O = 0.5 atm. The Tafel slope is 0.05 V/decade, j₀ = 10⁻⁷ A/cm², R_ASR = 0.05 Ω·cm², and j_L = 1.5 A/cm². Calculate V_cell at j = 1.0 A/cm².** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — E_Nernst (2 marks):** $$\frac{RT}{nF} = \frac{(8.314)(353)}{2(96{,}485)} = 0.01521 \text{ V}$$ $$\ln\!\left(\frac{3 \cdot \sqrt{3}}{0.5}\right) = \ln(10.392) = 2.340$$ $$E_{Nernst} = 1.229 + (0.01521)(2.340) = 1.265 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2 — η_act (2 marks):** $$\eta_{act} = A \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{j}{j_0}\right) = 0.05 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{1.0}{10^{-7}}\right) = 0.05 \times 16.118 = 0.806 \text{ V}$$ > **Note to examiner:** With A = 0.05 V/decade, the activation loss is high. A more realistic Tafel slope of 0.03 V would give 0.49 V. Either is acceptable if the problem states A. **Step 3 — η_ohmic (1 mark):** $$\eta_{ohmic} = (1.0)(0.05) = 0.050 \text{ V}$$ **Step 4 — η_conc (1 mark):** $$\eta_{conc} = -(0.01521)\ln\!\left(1 - \frac{1.0}{1.5}\right) = -(0.01521)(-1.0986) = 0.017 \text{ V}$$ **Step 5 — V_cell (1 mark):** $$V_{cell} = 1.265 - 0.806 - 0.050 - 0.017 = \boxed{0.392 \text{ V}}$$ > **Examiner's Note:** If A = 0.0304 V (theoretical Tafel slope at α=0.5, n=2, T=353K), then η_act = 0.490 V and V_cell = **0.708 V**. Accept any answer between 0.39 and 0.71 V with correct methodology. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 MARKS) — *Qualitative + Comparative* **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 4, Alternative Choice]** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Q: With a neat sketch of the polarization curve, explain the three regions: activation, ohmic, and concentration losses. Compare their magnitudes at low, medium, and high current densities.** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Sketch (2 marks):** V_cell on Y-axis, j on X-axis; curve starting at E_Nernst ≈ 1.23 V at j = 0; sharp initial drop; then linear middle; sharp drop near j_L. **Region 1 — Activation (2 marks):** - Dominant at **low current density** (j < 0.1 A/cm²) - Caused by **sluggish oxygen reduction reaction (ORR)** kinetics at the cathode - Governed by the Tafel equation; produces a **non-linear, exponential-looking** drop - Magnitude: **0.05 – 0.50 V** depending on j₀ and T **Region 2 — Ohmic (2 marks):** - Dominant in the **middle range** (0.1 < j < 0.8 A/cm²) - Caused by **proton resistance in the membrane** (Nafion) + **electronic resistance** in electrodes, GDL, and bipolar plates - Produces a **linear** voltage drop with slope = −R_ASR - Magnitude: **0.02 – 0.10 V** at typical FCEV current densities **Region 3 — Concentration (1 mark):** - Dominant at **high current density** (j > 0.8 A/cm²) - Caused by **reactant starvation** at the catalyst surface — H₂/O₂ cannot diffuse through the GDL fast enough - Produces a **sharp, asymptotic** drop as j → j_L (limiting current) - Magnitude: **0.05 – 0.40 V**, diverging as j approaches j_L --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Q: A 100 kW PEM fuel cell stack for an automotive FCEV operates at an average single-cell voltage of 0.70 V. The stack delivers 250 A. Determine: (i) the number of cells in the stack, (ii) the voltage efficiency, and (iii) the operating current density if the active area of each cell is 250 cm².** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Stack power equation (1 mark):** $$P_{stack} = N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I$$ $$100{,}000 = N \cdot 0.70 \cdot 250$$ **Step 2 — Number of cells (2 marks):** $$N = \frac{100{,}000}{(0.70)(250)} = \frac{100{,}000}{175} = \boxed{571 \text{ cells}}$$ > **Reality check:** Real FCEV stacks use **300–400 cells** at slightly higher current density. The answer is in the right engineering ballpark. **Step 3 — Voltage efficiency (2 marks):** $$\eta_{voltage} = \frac{V_{cell}}{E^{\circ}} = \frac{0.70}{1.229} = 0.569$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{voltage} = 56.9\%}$$ **Step 4 — Current density (2 marks):** $$j = \frac{I}{A_{cell}} = \frac{250 \text{ A}}{250 \text{ cm}^2} = \boxed{1.0 \text{ A/cm}^2}$$ > **Interpretation:** 1.0 A/cm² is the standard rated operating point for modern automotive PEMFC stacks, consistent with the 2024 Toyota Mirai 2 specifications. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Based on Real Valuation Patterns):** > > 1. **Sign Errors in η_conc (–2 marks):** Students forget the negative sign in η_conc = −(RT/nF)ln(1 − j/j_L). Always state that ln(1 − j/j_L) is **negative**, so η_conc is **positive** (a loss). > > 2. **Mixing up ΔG and ΔH (–1 mark):** E° = 1.229 V is derived from **ΔG° (Gibbs free energy)**, not ΔH°. Using ΔH° = −285.8 kJ/mol gives the **thermo-neutral voltage** E_th = 1.481 V (a different quantity used for heat-balance calculations). > > 3. **Forgetting the Tafel approximation validity (–1 mark):** The Tafel equation is valid only when **j ≫ j₀** and overpotential > ~50 mV. Do not apply it at very low j. > > 4. **Not specifying units of j₀ and j (–1 mark):** Exchange current density j₀ and current density j **must be in the same units** (A/cm²) before taking the ratio inside ln. > > 5. **Stacking voltage ≠ cell voltage (–2 marks):** When asked for *stack* voltage, multiply by N. When asked for *cell* voltage, do not. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Print-This-Before-Exam 1-Page Summary:** - ✅ **Operational Voltage Equation:** $V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$ - ✅ **Standard E° = 1.229 V** at 25 °C, 1 atm (from ΔG° = −237.2 kJ/mol) - ✅ **Activation Loss** = Tafel drop = $\frac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln(j/j_0)$ — dominant at low j - ✅ **Ohmic Loss** = j × R_ASR — linear with current, dominant in mid-range - ✅ **Concentration Loss** = $-\frac{RT}{nF}\ln(1 - j/j_L)$ — diverges near j_L - ✅ **FCEV Operating Point:** ~0.70 V per cell at 1.0 A/cm² (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo) - ✅ **Constants to memorize:** F = 96,485 C/mol, R = 8.314 J/mol·K, n = 2, E° = 1.229 V - ✅ **Voltage Efficiency** = V_cell / E° ≈ 50–60% in real stacks - ✅ **Stack voltage** = N × V_cell; **Stack power** = N × V_cell × I - ✅ **Three Regions of Polarization Curve:** Activation (low j) → Ohmic (mid j) → Concentration (high j) - ✅ **Limiting Current j_L** = $nFD C_b / \delta$ — physical maximum current before cell "suffocates" - ✅ **Higher T and P increase E_Nernst** but also accelerate membrane dehydration and degradation (engineering trade-off) - ✅ **Pt-catalyst necessity:** ORR's j₀ is so low (10⁻⁹ A/cm²) that activation loss would be ~0.5 V without platinum > **Final Word:** The operational fuel cell voltage is the *engineering reality* of the thermodynamic ideal. Master the equation, the three loss terms, and the polarization curve shape — these are the three pillars for any Module 4 question.

topics.title: Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMUT612 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cells ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)**, also called a **Polymer Electrolyte Membrane Fuel Cell**, is an electrochemical energy conversion device that directly converts the chemical energy of a fuel (typically pure **hydrogen**, $\text{H}_2$) and an oxidant (typically **oxygen** from ambient air, $\text{O}_2$) into electrical energy, with **water** and **heat** as the only by-products. The defining feature of a PEMFC is its use of a thin, solid, ion-conducting **perfluorinated sulfonic acid polymer membrane** (most commonly **Nafion™** by DuPont) as the electrolyte, which selectively transports only **protons ($\text{H}^+$)** from the anode to the cathode while blocking electrons and gases. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** PEMFCs are the *only* fuel cell chemistry currently mass-deployed in light-duty automotive applications (e.g., **Toyota Mirai**, **Hyundai Nexo**, **Honda Clarity**) due to their low operating temperature (**60–100 °C**), high power density (**0.5–1.0 W/cm²**), rapid start-up, and modular scalability. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hydrogen Sandwich Battery" Imagine a sandwich where: - The **top slice of bread** is the **anode** (negative electrode), where hydrogen gas is piped in and "cracked" into protons and electrons. - The **bottom slice of bread** is the **cathode** (positive electrode), where oxygen from the air is supplied. - The **filling in the middle** is the **proton exchange membrane** — a special plastic sheet that only lets protons squeeze through, like a one-way turnstile at a metro station. Electrons, being blocked by the membrane, are forced to take the long way around through an **external circuit** (the wires of your car), creating a useful electric current that powers the motor. Meanwhile, the protons meet up with the electrons and oxygen at the cathode, combine, and produce **pure water** — which literally drips out of the exhaust pipe. The car "exhales" water vapor instead of carbon dioxide! ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | |-----------|--------|----------------| | Faraday's constant | $F$ | **96,485 C/mol** | | Universal gas constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | | Standard temperature | $T$ | **298.15 K (25 °C)** | | Standard pressure | $P$ | **1 atm (101.325 kPa)** | | Standard cell potential (PEMFC) | $E^0$ | **1.229 V** | | Low Heating Value of H₂ | $\text{LHV}$ | **240 kJ/mol** | | High Heating Value of H₂ | $\text{HHV}$ | **285.8 kJ/mol** | > [!NOTE] > Unlike a battery, a PEMFC is an **open system** — as long as hydrogen and oxygen are continuously supplied, it will keep producing electricity indefinitely without ever "running down" like a rechargeable battery. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Why and the How A PEMFC operates on the principle of **reversed electrolysis of water**. The internal workings can be broken down into four sequential stages: 1. **Fuel Delivery & Adsorption (Anode Side):** Pressurized hydrogen gas is fed into flow channels machined into graphite or metallic **bipolar plates**. The gas diffuses through a porous **gas diffusion layer (GDL)** — typically carbon paper — and reaches the **anode catalyst layer** (a thin coating of **platinum nanoparticles** dispersed on carbon black). 2. **Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction (HOR) at the Anode:** Each $\text{H}_2$ molecule adsorbs onto a Pt site and is split into **2 protons** and **2 electrons**. The protons ($\text{H}^+$) are released into the humidified membrane, while electrons are rejected into the electrically conductive GDL and current collector. 3. **Proton Conduction Through the Membrane:** The Nafion™ membrane conducts protons via the **Grotthuss mechanism** — protons "hop" along a network of water molecules attached to the sulfonic acid groups ($-\text{SO}_3^-$) in the polymer. This requires the membrane to be **fully hydrated**; dehydration causes catastrophic drops in conductivity. 4. **Oxygen Reduction Reaction (ORR) at the Cathode:** Oxygen gas diffuses through the cathode GDL to the cathode catalyst layer. There, $\text{O}_2$ combines with the incoming protons and the electrons arriving via the external circuit to form **water** and **heat**. ### 2.2 Electrochemical Half-Reactions and Overall Reaction **Anode (HOR — Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction):** $$\text{H}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \quad E^0_{anode} = 0.000 \text{ V vs. SHE}$$ **Cathode (ORR — Oxygen Reduction Reaction):** $$\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \;\longrightarrow\; \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad E^0_{cathode} = +1.229 \text{ V vs. SHE}$$ **Overall Cell Reaction:** $$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \quad E^0_{cell} = +1.229 \text{ V}$$ ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — All Key Equations | # | Equation Name | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---------------|---------|---------------| | 1 | Standard Gibbs Free Energy | $\Delta G^0 = -nFE^0$ | J/mol ; $n$ = electrons transferred | | 2 | Theoretical Electrical Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta G^0}{\Delta H}$ | Dimensionless ratio | | 3 | LHV-based Efficiency | $\eta_{LHV} = \dfrac{1.229 \times 2F}{\text{LHV}} = 83\,\%$ | Used for automotive FCs | | 4 | HHV-based Efficiency | $\eta_{HHV} = \dfrac{1.229 \times 2F}{\text{HHV}} = 94.5\,\%$ | Theoretical maximum | | 5 | Nernst Equation (cell voltage) | $E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(P_{\text{H}_2} \, P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}\right)$ | V ; for the overall reaction | | 6 | Tafel Equation (activation loss) | $\eta_{act} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right)$ | V ; $\alpha$ = transfer coefficient | | 7 | Ohmic Loss (Ohm's law in cell) | $\eta_{ohm} = i \cdot R_{ohm}$ | V ; $R_{ohm}$ = ASR in $\Omega\cdot\text{cm}^2$ | | 8 | Concentration Loss | $\eta_{conc} = -\dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(1 - \dfrac{i}{i_L}\right)$ | V ; $i_L$ = limiting current density | | 9 | Actual Cell Voltage | $V_{cell} = E^0 - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$ | V ; polarization losses subtracted | | 10 | Power Density | $P = V_{cell} \times i$ | W/cm² | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Unit Note:** $i$ (current density) is in **A/cm²**, $i_0$ (exchange current density) is typically **10⁻⁹ to 10⁻⁶ A/cm²** for Pt catalysts. The transfer coefficient $\alpha$ for the ORR is approximately **0.5**. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility PEMFCs form the heart of the modern **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV)**. The Toyota Mirai (2nd generation, 2020+) delivers **128 kW** from a PEMFC stack with a peak efficiency of **60%** at the wheel — roughly **2× the efficiency** of a comparable internal combustion engine vehicle. PEMFCs also power forklifts, buses (e.g., New Flyer Xcelsior), backup power for telecom towers, and the **Viking lander** derivatives in space applications. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Reversible Voltage from Thermodynamics **Goal:** Derive the standard cell potential $E^0 = 1.229$ V from first-principles thermodynamics. **Step 1 — Begin with the Gibbs Free Energy change of the overall reaction.** For the reaction $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$, the standard Gibbs free energy change at 25 °C is: $$\Delta G^0 = -237.2 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$ *(Negative sign indicates a spontaneous, exergonic reaction that can produce electrical work.)* **Step 2 — Relate Gibbs free energy to electrical work.** The maximum non-PV (pressure-volume) electrical work obtainable from a galvanic cell equals the negative of $\Delta G$: $$W_{elec} = -\Delta G = nFE^0$$ where $n = 2$ electrons are transferred per molecule of hydrogen, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol is Faraday's constant, and $E^0$ is the standard electromotive force (EMF). **Step 3 — Rearrange to solve for $E^0$.** $$\begin{aligned} E^0 &= -\dfrac{\Delta G^0}{nF} \\ E^0 &= -\dfrac{(-237{,}200 \;\text{J/mol})}{(2)(96{,}485 \;\text{C/mol})} \\ E^0 &= \dfrac{237{,}200}{192{,}970} \\ E^0 &= 1.229 \;\text{V} \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned}$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** This is the *theoretical open-circuit voltage* of a perfect PEMFC under standard conditions. Real cells never reach this due to irreversibilities. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Theoretical (Maximum) Efficiency **Goal:** Derive the maximum thermal-to-electrical efficiency of a PEMFC. **Step 1 — Define the thermodynamic efficiency.** $$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\text{Electrical energy out}}{\text{Chemical energy in (as heat)}}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the relevant energies.** The electrical energy available is the Gibbs free energy (since the cell runs isothermally at constant T, P): $$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{|\Delta G^0|}{\Delta H}$$ **Step 3 — Insert numerical values for hydrogen oxidation to liquid water.** Using $\Delta H^0 = -285.8$ kJ/mol (the High Heating Value, HHV): $$\begin{aligned} \eta_{th} &= \dfrac{237.2}{285.8} \\ \eta_{th} &= 0.830 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 83.0\,\% \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Re-derive using LHV (relevant for PEMFC since product water leaves as vapor at ~80 °C).** Using $\Delta H_{LHV} = -241.8$ kJ/mol: $$\begin{aligned} \eta_{LHV} &= \dfrac{|\Delta G^0|}{\Delta H_{LHV}} = \dfrac{237.2}{241.8} \\ \eta_{LHV} &= 0.981 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 98.1\,\% \end{aligned}$$ **Correction — Why the discrepancy?** In automotive PEMFC evaluation, $\eta_{HHV} = 83\%$ is *traditionally* reported because it uses the electrical work $W = -\Delta G$ relative to the *enthalpy* released assuming *liquid* water product. This is the standard automotive FC efficiency benchmark. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Nernst Equation for Variable Pressure Operation **Goal:** Find the cell voltage when the cell operates at non-standard partial pressures. **Step 1 — Start from the general Gibbs relation.** For the overall reaction with reaction quotient $Q$: $$\Delta G = \Delta G^0 + RT \ln Q$$ **Step 2 — Divide both sides by $-nF$.** $$E = E^0 - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$ **Step 3 — Write the reaction quotient for $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$.** Since $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ is liquid (activity = 1) and gases are expressed as partial pressures: $$Q = \dfrac{1}{P_{\text{H}_2} \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute and simplify.** $$\begin{aligned} E &= E^0 - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{P_{\text{H}_2} \, P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}\right) \\ E &= E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(P_{\text{H}_2} \, P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}\right) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Numerical evaluation at 80 °C ($T = 353$ K), $P_{\text{H}_2} = 1$ atm, $P_{\text{O}_2} = 0.21$ atm (air).** $$\begin{aligned} \dfrac{RT}{nF} &= \dfrac{(8.314)(353)}{(2)(96{,}485)} = 0.01521 \;\text{V} \\ E &= 1.229 + 0.01521 \cdot \ln\!\left(1 \cdot 0.21^{0.5}\right) \\ E &= 1.229 + 0.01521 \cdot \ln(0.4583) \\ E &= 1.229 + 0.01521 \cdot (-0.7803) \\ E &= 1.229 - 0.01187 \\ E &= 1.217 \;\text{V} \end{aligned}$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** Even under realistic air-fed operation, the reversible voltage drops only ~12 mV. This confirms why PEMFCs are designed to operate on **pure $\text{O}_2$ or pressurized air** to minimize concentration losses downstream. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Stack Voltage for Automotive Application **Goal:** Compute the number of cells required in a 300 V automotive stack. **Step 1 — Identify typical operating voltage per cell.** A real PEMFC at $i = 0.5$ A/cm² delivers approximately $V_{cell} \approx 0.65$ V (after all polarization losses). **Step 2 — Solve for $N_{cells}$.** $$\begin{aligned} V_{stack} &= N_{cells} \cdot V_{cell} \\ 300 \;\text{V} &= N_{cells} \times 0.65 \;\text{V} \\ N_{cells} &= \dfrac{300}{0.65} \\ N_{cells} &\approx 462 \;\text{cells} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Translate into physical stack size.** With each cell ~2 mm thick (membrane + 2 GDLs + 2 BPs), total stack length ≈ **92 cm**. This is why automotive PEMFC stacks are typically assembled as **compact, vertically-stacked modules** (~10–15 cm height) and operated at high current density. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block — PEMFC Single Cell Architecture & Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB A[H2 Fuel Inlet
Pressurized Hydrogen] --> B[Anode Bipolar Plate
Graphite or Metal with Flow Channels] B --> C[Anode Gas Diffusion Layer
Porous Carbon Paper] C --> D[Anode Catalyst Layer
Pt Nanoparticles on Carbon Black] D --> E{Proton Exchange Membrane
Nafion Perfluorinated Sulfonic Acid} E --> F[Cathode Catalyst Layer
Pt Nanoparticles] F --> G[Cathode Gas Diffusion Layer
Porous Carbon Paper] G --> H[Cathode Bipolar Plate
with Oxidant Flow Channels] H --> I[Air or O2 Inlet
From Ambient or Compressor] D -. H2 -> 2H+ + 2e- .-> E E -. Proton Hopping via Grotthuss Mechanism .-> F F -. 1/2 O2 + 2H+ + 2e- -> H2O .-> H D == Electrons Forced Through External Circuit ==> J[Electric Load
Traction Motor, Auxiliaries] J == Return Path ==> G H --> K[H2O and Heat Outlet
Water Management System] H --> L[Thermal Loop
Coolant Plates] style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc style I fill:#ffe5cc,stroke:#cc6600 style E fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#6600cc style J fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009900 style K fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000 style L fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Voltage Loss / Polarization Curve Mapping ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Open Circuit Voltage
E0 = 1.229 V] --> B[Activation Losses
Kinetic Barrier at Pt Catalyst] B --> C[Ohmic Losses
Ionic + Electronic Resistance] C --> D[Concentration Losses
Mass Transport Limit] D --> E[Limiting Current Density
iL approx 2.0 A/cm2] A1[Region 1: Activation Dominated
i = 0 to 0.1 A/cm2] -.-> B B1[Region 2: Linear Ohmic Region
i = 0.1 to 1.0 A/cm2] -.-> C C1[Region 3: Concentration Dominated
i = 1.0 to 2.0 A/cm2] -.-> D style A fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006600 style B fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#996600 style C fill:#ff9933,stroke:#993300 style D fill:#ff6666,stroke:#990000 style E fill:#cc0000,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Automotive PEMFC Powertrain Integration ```mermaid flowchart TB A[High Pressure H2 Tank
700 bar Type IV Composite] --> B[Pressure Regulator
Reduces to approx 10 bar] B --> C[PEMFC Stack
300 to 650 V DC, 100 to 130 kW] C --> D[DC-DC Boost Converter
Steps up to 650 V Traction Bus] D --> E[Li-Ion Battery Pack
approx 1 to 2 kWh Buffer] E --> F[Three-Phase Inverter
DC to AC Conversion] F --> G[PMSM Traction Motor
approx 160 kW Peak] G --> H[Single Speed Reduction Gearbox] H --> I[Drive Wheels] J[Air Compressor and Humidifier] --> C K[Cathode Water Recirculation
and Humidification Loop] --> C L[Coolant Pump and Radiator Stack] --> C style A fill:#cce5ff style C fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#6600cc style E fill:#ffff99 style G fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009900 style I fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]** **Define a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell. List any four advantages of PEMFCs over other fuel cell types.** **Model Answer:** A **PEMFC (Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell)** is an electrochemical device that converts the chemical energy of hydrogen and oxygen directly into electrical energy, water, and heat using a **solid polymer membrane** (e.g., Nafion) as the electrolyte that selectively conducts protons. **Four Advantages:** 1. **Low operating temperature** (60–100 °C) enables rapid start-up, making it suitable for automotive applications. 2. **High power density** (0.5–1.0 W/cm²) leads to compact, lightweight stacks. 3. **Solid, leak-proof electrolyte** eliminates corrosive liquid handling issues. 4. **Modular design** allows easy scaling of voltage (by stacking cells) and power (by increasing active area). *(3 marks — 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for any 4 advantages)* --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary Exam]** **Explain the role of platinum in a PEMFC. Why is CO poisoning a concern?** **Model Answer:** **Role of Platinum:** Platinum serves as the **electrocatalyst** at both the anode and cathode. Its function is to lower the **activation energy** of the Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction (HOR) at the anode and the sluggish Oxygen Reduction Reaction (ORR) at the cathode. Because the ORR has intrinsically slow kinetics, Pt loading (~0.1–0.4 mg/cm²) is critical to achieving reasonable current densities at automotive operating voltages. **CO Poisoning Concern:** Carbon monoxide (CO) strongly **chemisorbs** onto Pt active sites at PEMFC operating temperatures (~80 °C), physically blocking them from adsorbing $\text{H}_2$. Even trace CO levels (≥ 10 ppm) in reformate hydrogen can degrade performance by **>50%**. Mitigation strategies include Pt-Ru alloy catalysts, air bleeding at the anode, and using **high-purity (Grade D) hydrogen** from electrolyzers. *(3 marks — 1.5 marks each for role and poisoning)* --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[Internal Choice Pattern: Answer ANY ONE — Question A OR Question B]** --- #### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Main Exam, Question II Module 4]** **(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the construction and working of a PEMFC. State the half-reactions at both electrodes. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A PEMFC stack delivers **$V_{cell} = 0.70$ V** per cell at a current density of **$i = 0.6$ A/cm²**. The active area of each cell is **$A = 300$ cm²**. Compute the **(i)** power output per cell, **(ii)** number of cells needed for a **300 V** traction bus, and **(iii)** total stack power assuming the stack operates at the rated current density. **(7 marks)** ### **Model Solution — Question A** #### **Part (a) — Construction and Working** A PEMFC consists of the following layered components (from anode to cathode): | Layer | Material | Function | |-------|----------|----------| | Anode Bipolar Plate | Graphite / SS / Ti | Distributes $\text{H}_2$, collects current, conducts heat | | Anode GDL | Carbon paper (Toray) | Uniform gas diffusion + electrical contact | | Anode Catalyst Layer | Pt/C (20–40 wt% Pt) | Catalyzes HOR | | **Membrane** | **Nafion 117/115/212** | **Proton conductor + electron insulator + gas separator** | | Cathode Catalyst Layer | Pt/C | Catalyzes ORR | | Cathode GDL | Carbon paper | Uniform $\text{O}_2$ diffusion + water removal | | Cathode Bipolar Plate | Graphite / SS | Distributes air, collects current | **Working Principle:** 1. $\text{H}_2$ enters the anode flow field and diffuses to the Pt catalyst. 2. $\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$ (HOR). 3. Protons migrate through the hydrated Nafion membrane (Grotthuss hopping). 4. Electrons flow through the external circuit (load) — producing **useful work**. 5. At the cathode, $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$ (ORR). 6. Product water is removed via cathode flow channel; excess heat is rejected by coolant plates. *(4 marks for schematic description + 1 mark for half-reactions + 2 marks for working sequence)* #### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution** **(i) Power output per cell:** $$\begin{aligned} P_{cell} &= V_{cell} \times i \times A \\ P_{cell} &= 0.70 \;\text{V} \times 0.6 \;\text{A/cm}^2 \times 300 \;\text{cm}^2 \\ P_{cell} &= 126 \;\text{W} \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Number of cells for 300 V bus:** $$\begin{aligned} N_{cells} &= \dfrac{V_{bus}}{V_{cell}} = \dfrac{300}{0.70} \\ N_{cells} &\approx 429 \;\text{cells (rounded up)} \end{aligned}$$ **(iii) Total stack power:** $$\begin{aligned} P_{stack} &= N_{cells} \times P_{cell} \\ P_{stack} &= 429 \times 126 \;\text{W} \\ P_{stack} &= 54{,}054 \;\text{W} \approx 54 \;\text{kW} \end{aligned}$$ *(1.5 marks each for the three sub-parts — 0.5 marks formula, 1 mark calculation)* --- #### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Supplementary, Question II Module 4]** **(a)** Derive the standard reversible cell potential of a hydrogen–oxygen PEMFC from thermodynamic principles. Use the result to compute the **theoretical efficiency** based on HHV. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the three major polarization losses in a PEMFC with appropriate equations. Sketch the typical polarization curve showing all three regions. **(7 marks)** ### **Model Solution — Question B** #### **Part (a) — Derivation of Standard EMF** *(Full derivation presented in Section 3.1 above)* **Recap of derivation logic:** - Start with $\Delta G^0 = -nFE^0$ (relation between free energy and electrical work). - For $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$: $\Delta G^0 = -237.2$ kJ/mol, $n = 2$. - Solve: $E^0 = \dfrac{237{,}200}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 1.229$ V. **Theoretical Efficiency (HHV basis):** $$\begin{aligned} \eta_{HHV} &= \dfrac{|\Delta G^0|}{|\Delta H^0_{HHV}|} = \dfrac{237.2 \;\text{kJ/mol}}{285.8 \;\text{kJ/mol}} \\ \eta_{HHV} &= 0.830 \;\text{or}\; 83.0\,\% \end{aligned}$$ This 83% value represents the **maximum achievable thermal-to-electrical efficiency** for a hydrogen-fueled PEMFC — far exceeding the **~25–35%** Carnot-limited efficiency of an internal combustion engine. *(3 marks derivation + 2 marks efficiency derivation + 2 marks interpretation)* #### **Part (b) — Three Polarization Losses** **1. Activation Loss ($\eta_{act}$) — Dominant at low current density:** $$\eta_{act} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right) \quad \text{(Tafel Equation)}$$ Caused by the **sluggish ORR kinetics** at the cathode. The transfer coefficient $\alpha \approx 0.5$. **2. Ohmic Loss ($\eta_{ohm}$) — Dominant in the linear mid-region:** $$\eta_{ohm} = i \cdot R_{ohm}$$ $R_{ohm}$ includes the ionic resistance of the Nafion membrane (~0.03 Ω·cm² when fully hydrated) plus contact resistances. **3. Concentration Loss ($\eta_{conc}$) — Dominant at high current density:** $$\eta_{conc} = -\dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(1 - \dfrac{i}{i_L}\right)$$ Occurs when mass transport of reactants to the catalyst sites becomes rate-limiting. **Complete Polarization Curve:** $$V_{cell}(i) = E^0 - \eta_{act}(i) - \eta_{ohm}(i) - \eta_{conc}(i)$$ *(2 marks each for the three losses + 1 mark for combined equation + sketch worth 1 mark)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Zones in PEMFC Questions:** > 1. **Confusing HOR and ORR locations** — students often swap anode/cathode reactions. *Remember:* Fuel (H₂) is always oxidized at the **anode**; oxidant (O₂) is always reduced at the **cathode**. > 2. **Forgetting the factor of ½ in $\text{O}_2$** — the cathode half-reaction is $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2$, NOT $\text{O}_2$. This affects $n$ in Nernst/Tafel equations. > 3. **Using LHV vs HHV inconsistently** — KTU expects you to *state* which one you are using. Default to **HHV = 285.8 kJ/mol** for liquid water product unless specified. > 4. **Ignoring units** — current density must be in **A/cm²**, not Amperes. Always multiply by active area $A$ (cm²) to obtain power. > 5. **Drawing schematics without arrows** — examiners award partial marks for showing **direction of proton flow, electron flow, gas flow, and water flow** clearly. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist - **PEMFC** = Proton (Polymer) Electrolyte Membrane Fuel Cell; operates at **60–100 °C**; uses **Nafion™** membrane. - **Anode reaction:** $\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$ (HOR). - **Cathode reaction:** $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$ (ORR). - **Overall reaction:** $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$ — only emissions are **water and heat**. - **Standard reversible voltage:** $E^0 = 1.229$ V at 25 °C, 1 atm. - **Theoretical max efficiency:** $\eta_{HHV} = 83\%$, $\eta_{LHV} = 98\%$. - **Three polarization losses:** Activation (Tafel), Ohmic (linear), Concentration (mass-transport). - **Real cell voltage** at operating point ($i \approx 0.5$–$0.7$ A/cm²) is typically **0.6–0.7 V**. - **Automotive stacks** consist of **~300–500 cells** connected in series to reach 300–650 V DC bus. - **Catalyst:** Pt nanoparticles on carbon black (~0.1–0.4 mg/cm² loading). - **Key automotive deployments:** Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo, Honda Clarity, Daimler GenH2. - **Critical operational concerns:** Membrane **humidification** (Grotthuss mechanism needs water), **CO poisoning** (< 10 ppm tolerance), **water management** (flooding vs drying tradeoff), **cold start** capability. - **Comparison metric:** PEMFC has the **highest power density** among all fuel cell types but the **lowest operating temperature**, making it ideal for transportation. - **Stack components in order (anode → cathode):** BP → GDL → Catalyst → Membrane → Catalyst → GDL → BP. - **Auxiliaries in automotive FCEV:** 700 bar H₂ tank, air compressor, humidifier, DC-DC converter, Li-ion buffer battery, electric traction motor.

topics.title: Alkaline Electrolyte Fuel Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT612 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Alkaline Electrolyte Fuel Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alkaline Fuel Cell (AFC)** — A low-to-medium temperature electrochemical energy conversion device that directly converts the chemical energy of a fuel (typically hydrogen) and an oxidant (typically pure oxygen) into electrical energy using an **aqueous alkaline electrolyte** (most commonly potassium hydroxide, KOH, in 30–40 wt% concentration) immobilized in a porous matrix between two porous electrodes.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the AFC belongs to the family of **Polymer Electrolyte Membrane / Liquid Electrolyte Fuel Cells** (as opposed to Solid Oxide Fuel Cells, SOFC, or Molten Carbonate Fuel Cells, MCFC) and is studied within Module 4 as a **space-proven, automotive-relevant low-temperature fuel cell technology** with the fastest intrinsic oxygen-reduction reaction (ORR) kinetics of any fuel cell type.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Operating Conditions for KTU Examinations:**
> - **Electrolyte:** 30–40 wt% aqueous KOH (mobile or matrix-immobilized)
> - **Operating Temperature:** 60–90 °C (low-temp) or 200–250 °C (with 85 wt% KOH, space-qualified)
> - **Operating Pressure:** 1–10 atm
> - **Theoretical Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV):** **1.229 V** at 25 °C, **1.182 V** at 80 °C
> - **Practical Operating Cell Voltage:** **0.7–0.9 V**
> - **Electrical Efficiency:** 60–70 % (HHV basis)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Battery of Two Bubbles" Analogy**
>
> Imagine two soap bubbles — one filled with **hydrogen gas**, the other with **oxygen gas** — connected by a wet paper towel soaked in soapy water (the alkaline electrolyte). When you connect wires to each bubble, electrons flow from the hydrogen bubble to the oxygen bubble (producing current), and water forms on the wet towel.
>
> In reality:
> - The "hydrogen bubble" is the **anode** (negative terminal, like the − terminal of a battery).
> - The "oxygen bubble" is the **cathode** (positive terminal, like the + terminal of a battery).
> - The "soapy wet paper towel" is the **alkaline electrolyte** (KOH solution), which carries charge by **OH⁻ ions** (not H⁺ ions as in PEMFC — a *critical difference*).
> - Unlike a battery, **fuel is continuously supplied** and water is continuously removed — the cell never goes "flat."

The **"alkaline"** in the name refers to the pH of the electrolyte (pH ≈ 14 for concentrated KOH), which is in sharp contrast to the acidic membrane in a PEMFC. This basic environment is the reason why **non-precious metal catalysts** (like nickel and silver) can replace expensive platinum in AFCs.

## 1.3 Why AFCs Matter for Modern Automotive Technologies

- **Historical Significance:** AFCs powered the **Apollo Lunar Missions (1960s)** and the **Space Shuttle Orbiter (1981–2011)** — every time NASA launched a crewed vehicle, alkaline fuel cells supplied both electricity and drinking water.
- **Automotive Relevance:** Studied as a **lower-cost alternative to PEMFC** for buses, light-commercial vehicles, and military transport because the alkaline environment permits Ni/Ag catalysts, reducing the platinum-group-metal (PGM) loading by an order of magnitude.
- **Carbon Dioxide Sensitivity:** The Achilles' heel — atmospheric CO₂ reacts with KOH to form K₂CO₃, poisoning the electrolyte (this is why AFCs in space used pure O₂, not air).
- **High Specific Power:** AFC stacks routinely achieve 1 kW/kg, attractive for vehicle powertrains where weight matters.

## 1.4 Fundamental Distinction from PEMFC

| Feature | AFC | PEMFC |
|---|---|---|
| Charge carrier | OH⁻ (hydroxide) | H⁺ (proton) |
| Electrolyte | Liquid KOH (30–85 wt%) | Solid Nafion™ membrane |
| Catalyst | Ni, Ag, Raney-Ni (cheap) | Pt/C (expensive) |
| CO₂ tolerance | **Poor** (forms carbonates) | Good |
| Operating temperature | 60–250 °C | 60–80 °C |
| Water management | Produced at **anode** | Produced at **cathode** |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 4):** The contrast *where water is produced* is a frequently tested concept. In AFC, H₂ + 2OH⁻ → 2H₂O + 2e⁻ produces water at the **anode**; in PEMFC, H₂O is produced at the **cathode**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Electrochemistry — Half-Cell Reactions

The alkaline electrolyte governs the charge carrier. The hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) and the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) proceed through the following pathways:

### 2.1.1 Anode Half-Reaction (Hydrogen Oxidation, HOR)

$$\text{H}_2 + 2\text{OH}^- \longrightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- \qquad E^0_a = -0.828 \text{ V (vs. SHE)}$$

**Logic Step-by-Step:**
1. Molecular hydrogen (H₂) diffuses through the porous anode to the catalyst–electrolyte triple-phase boundary.
2. H₂ dissociates into two adsorbed H atoms (Volmer step) on the nickel surface.
3. Each H atom reacts with an OH⁻ ion from the electrolyte to form adsorbed water and releases 1 electron.
4. Two electrons travel through the external circuit (doing useful work).
5. Liquid water forms inside the anode structure — **must be removed continuously** (anode flooding is a failure mode).

### 2.1.2 Cathode Half-Reaction (Oxygen Reduction, ORR)

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- \longrightarrow 2\text{OH}^- \qquad E^0_c = +0.401 \text{ V (vs. SHE)}$$

**Logic Step-by-Step:**
1. O₂ (or air) diffuses through the porous cathode to the silver/Pt catalyst.
2. O₂ is reduced in a **4-electron transfer** to form peroxide intermediate, then to OH⁻.
3. The OH⁻ ions migrate through the electrolyte toward the anode.
4. This completes the ionic circuit.

### 2.1.3 Overall Cell Reaction

$$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \qquad E^0_{cell} = 1.229 \text{ V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The single overall reaction is **identical** to PEMFC, but the *intermediate species* and the *direction of ionic charge flow* are reversed. The overall free-energy change and the theoretical EMF are the same for any H₂–O₂ fuel cell.

## 2.2 Thermodynamic Foundations

### 2.2.1 Standard Thermodynamic Data (at 25 °C, 1 atm)

- Enthalpy of formation of liquid water: **ΔH°f = −285.8 kJ/mol** (Higher Heating Value, HHV)
- Enthalpy of formation of steam: **ΔH°f = −241.8 kJ/mol** (Lower Heating Value, LHV)
- Gibbs free energy of formation: **ΔG°f = −237.2 kJ/mol** (liquid water)
- Standard entropy change: **ΔS° = −163.2 J/(mol·K)**
- Number of electrons transferred: **n = 2**

### 2.2.2 Cell Voltage and Gibbs Free Energy

$$\Delta G = -nFE_{cell} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad E_{cell} = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF}$$

Substituting the standard value at 25 °C:

$$E^0 = \frac{237200 \text{ J/mol}}{2 \times 96485 \text{ C/mol}} = \mathbf{1.229 \text{ V}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Whenever a fuel-cell problem states "standard conditions" or "25 °C, 1 atm," the answer must start from **1.229 V**. If a temperature other than 25 °C is given, apply the temperature correction derived in §3.1.

## 2.3 Cell Losses (Polarization)

The actual cell voltage under load is always **lower** than the theoretical EMF. The losses are grouped into three main categories — a high-yield topic:

| Loss Type | Cause | Magnitude | Dominant Region |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Activation loss (η_act)** | Slow electrode kinetics, especially ORR | High at low current | < 100 mA/cm² |
| **Ohmic loss (η_ohm)** | Ionic + electronic resistance of electrolyte, electrodes, bipolar plates | Linear in current | All regions |
| **Concentration loss (η_conc)** | Mass-transport limitations of H₂ and O₂ at high current density | Sharp rise near limiting current | > 500 mA/cm² |

The practical operating voltage is:

$$V_{cell} = E_{thermo} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Activation Loss (Tafel Equation):**
> $$\eta_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$$
> where α is the charge-transfer coefficient (typically 0.5 for ORR), i₀ is the exchange current density, and i is the operating current density.
>
> AFCs have **i₀ values 100× higher than PEMFC** for the ORR (≈ 10⁻¹⁰ A/cm² on Ag), which is precisely why their activation losses are smaller.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the consolidated reference for numerical problems on AFCs. Memorise the symbols and units.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E^0 = -\Delta G^0 / nF$ | Standard reversible EMF | $E^0$ in V; $\Delta G^0$ in J/mol; $n$ = 2; $F = 96485$ C/mol |
| 2 | $E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(p_{H_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{0.5}\right)$ | Nernst equation (AFC, 1 atm pure gases) | $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); $T$ in K; $p$ in atm |
| 3 | $E(T) = 1.229 - 0.85\times 10^{-3}(T - 298.15)$ | Temperature-corrected OCV | $T$ in K; valid 25–250 °C |
| 4 | $\eta_{elec} = \Delta G / \Delta H_{HHV}$ | Electrical efficiency (HHV basis) | Dimensionless, ≈ 0.83 |
| 5 | $\eta_{therm} = \Delta G / \Delta H_{LHV}$ | Thermal efficiency (LHV basis) | Dimensionless, ≈ 0.98 |
| 6 | $\eta_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha nF}\ln(i/i_0)$ | Tafel activation loss | $\alpha$ typically 0.5 |
| 7 | $\eta_{ohm} = i \cdot R_{ASR}$ | Ohmic loss (ASR = area-specific resistance) | $R_{ASR}$ in Ω·cm² |
| 8 | $i_{lim} = \frac{nFD_{H_2}C_{H_2}}{\delta}$ | Limiting current density (mass transport) | $D$ = diffusivity, $\delta$ = diffusion layer thickness |
| 9 | $\dot{m} = \frac{M_{H_2} \cdot I}{nF}$ | Faraday's law (H₂ consumption rate) | $\dot{m}$ in g/s, $M_{H_2}=2.016$ g/mol |
| 10 | $\eta_{voltage} = V_{cell}/E_{thermo}$ | Voltage efficiency | Dimensionless |

> **Note on absolute-value notation:** In equations above, vertical bars (e.g., $\vert p_{H_2}\vert$) are rendered using `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

## 2.5 Real-World Automotive & Aerospace Utility

1. **NASA Apollo Program (1960s):** Three Bacon-type AFCs per service module provided 1.4 kW of power and potable drinking water. Each cell operated at 230 °C with 85 % KOH.
2. **Space Shuttle (STS-1 to STS-135):** Three 12 kW AFC stacks using 35 % KOH at 93 °C generated electricity and 25 kg of drinking water per flight day.
3. **AFC Buses (Europe, 1990s–2000s):** The European Union HYSECURE and ECTOS projects demonstrated 100 kW AFC bus fleets using H₂ fuel and ambient air (with scrubbers to remove CO₂).
4. **Modern R&D:** AFCs are being investigated for **forklifts, submarines, military vehicles, and railway locomotives** where the high efficiency and low catalyst cost outweigh the CO₂-sensitivity limitation.
5. **Automotive Future:** While most production fuel-cell vehicles (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo) use PEMFC, AFCs remain the **research benchmark for ORR kinetics** and are studied in KTU automotive curricula for their potential in **hybrid alkaline-membrane systems (AEMFC)** — a 2020s hot research topic.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section is dedicated to **exhaustive, board-style derivations**. Every algebraic transition is explicitly written so that students can reproduce the solution in the KTU examination.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Temperature Correction of Open-Circuit Voltage

**Statement:** Show that the reversible cell potential of an H₂–O₂ AFC decreases by ≈ **0.85 mV per Kelvin** rise in temperature.

### Step 1: Start from the Gibbs–Helmholtz identity
For a reversible cell, the relationship between the Gibbs free energy, enthalpy, and entropy is:

$$\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$$

Differentiate both sides with respect to temperature at constant pressure:

$$\left(\frac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial T}\right)_p = -\Delta S$$

### Step 2: Relate ΔG to cell voltage
Since $E = -\Delta G / nF$, differentiate to obtain:

$$\left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_p = -\frac{1}{nF}\left(\frac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial T}\right)_p = \frac{\Delta S}{nF}$$

### Step 3: Substitute the standard entropy change for the cell reaction
The reaction $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \to \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$ has a standard entropy change of:

$$\Delta S^0 = S^0(\text{H}_2\text{O},l) - S^0(\text{H}_2,g) - \tfrac{1}{2}S^0(\text{O}_2,g) = 69.9 - 130.7 - \tfrac{1}{2}(205.1) = -163.2 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

### Step 4: Compute the temperature coefficient

$$\left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_p = \frac{-163.2}{2 \times 96485} = -8.456 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V/K} \approx \mathbf{-0.85 \text{ mV/K}}$$

### Step 5: Write the linearised temperature-corrected OCV

$$E(T) = E(298.15) + \left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_p (T - 298.15)$$

$$\boxed{E(T) = 1.229 - 0.85 \times 10^{-3}(T - 298.15) \text{ V}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A hotter cell *thermodynamically* wants to deliver a slightly lower voltage, but *kinetically* (faster ORR) it will deliver more current. This is why AFCs are sometimes operated at 80–100 °C to capture the kinetic benefit, accepting a small thermodynamic penalty.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Nernst Equation for an AFC at Non-Standard Pressure

**Statement:** Derive the Nernst equation for an AFC operating at pressure $p_{H_2}$ and $p_{O_2}$ (in atm) and temperature $T$.

### Step 1: Nernst starting relation

$$E = E^0(T) - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$

where the reaction quotient $Q$ is built from the activities of products/reactants.

### Step 2: Build the reaction quotient
For the overall reaction $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \to \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$:

$$Q = \frac{a_{H_2O}}{a_{H_2} \cdot a_{O_2}^{1/2}} = \frac{1}{p_{H_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{1/2}}$$

(The activity of liquid water is unity by convention; gas activities are partial pressures in atm.)

### Step 3: Substitute

$$E = E^0(T) - \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{p_{H_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{1/2}}\right)$$

### Step 4: Apply log identity $\ln(1/x) = -\ln(x)$

$$\boxed{E = E^0(T) + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(p_{H_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{1/2}\right)}$$

**Sanity check (KTU past-pattern question):** If the cell is pressurised to 5 atm of pure H₂ on the anode and 5 atm of pure O₂ on the cathode at 80 °C, what is the reversible EMF?

**Substitute** $E^0(353.15) = 1.229 - 0.85 \times 10^{-3} \times 55 = 1.182$ V:

$$E = 1.182 + \frac{(8.314)(353.15)}{2 \times 96485}\ln(5 \times 5^{0.5}) = 1.182 + 0.0152 \times \ln(11.18) = 1.182 + 0.0152 \times 2.414 = \mathbf{1.219 \text{ V}}$$

Pressurisation *raises* the EMF — this is why high-pressure AFC systems in space used pressure vessels.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Electrical Efficiency (HHV vs LHV)

**Statement:** Compute the maximum electrical efficiency of an H₂–O₂ fuel cell on a higher-heating-value (HHV) and lower-heating-value (LHV) basis.

### Step 1: Definition
Electrical efficiency is the ratio of useful electrical work to the chemical energy released:

$$\eta_{elec} = \frac{\text{Electrical energy out}}{\text{Chemical energy in}} = \frac{-\Delta G}{-\Delta H}$$

### Step 2: Substitute numerical values

**HHV basis (water product is liquid):**
$$\eta_{HHV} = \frac{237.2}{285.8} = 0.830 \text{ or } \mathbf{83.0\%}$$

**LHV basis (water product is vapour, $T > 100$ °C):**
$$\eta_{LHV} = \frac{237.2}{241.8} = 0.981 \text{ or } \mathbf{98.1\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does LHV give 98 %?** Because the HHV includes the latent heat of condensation of water (44 kJ/mol). A high-temperature fuel cell that exhausts water as steam does *not* recover that latent heat as electricity — it would have to use a bottoming Rankine cycle. Thus, when reporting "efficiency" in an exam, **always specify the basis**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Hydrogen Consumption Rate (Faraday's Law)

**Statement:** A 60 kW AFC stack for an electric bus operates at an average cell voltage of 0.8 V. Each cell delivers 200 A. Calculate the H₂ mass flow rate per cell.

### Step 1: Faraday's law of electrolysis

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{M_{H_2} \cdot I}{nF}$$

### Step 2: Substitute values
- $M_{H_2} = 2.016$ g/mol
- $I = 200$ A
- $n = 2$
- $F = 96485$ C/mol

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{2.016 \times 200}{2 \times 96485} = \frac{403.2}{192970} = 2.089 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 2.089 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/s} \times 3600 = \mathbf{7.52 \text{ g/h}}$$

### Step 3: Cross-check with the power requirement
Electrical power per cell: $P_{cell} = V_{cell} \cdot I = 0.8 \times 200 = 160$ W
Hydrogen energy (HHV): $LHV_{H_2} = 241.8$ kJ/mol → 119.9 kJ/g (HHV: 142 MJ/kg)
Hydrogen needed for 160 W: $\dot{m}_{H_2} = P / (LHV_{mass}) = 160 / 119900 = 1.335 \times 10^{-3}$ g/s = **4.81 g/h**

The discrepancy is **expected** — Faraday's law gives the *stoichiometric* minimum, but real systems add a *stoichiometric ratio* of 1.1–1.2 to account for inefficiencies and prevent fuel starvation.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical: KTU-Style Problem

> **Problem (simulated KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4):**
> An alkaline fuel cell operates with H₂ at 1.2 atm and O₂ at 1.5 atm, both at 85 °C. Using a cell geometry with an active area of 250 cm², the cell delivers 0.75 V at 400 mA/cm². Calculate (a) the reversible EMF, (b) the total polarisation loss, (c) the volumetric H₂ consumption rate at NTP, and (d) the electrical efficiency at LHV.

### Part (a): Reversible EMF
Step 1: Temperature-corrected standard EMF:
$E^0(358.15) = 1.229 - 0.85\times 10^{-3}(358.15-298.15) = 1.229 - 0.051 = 1.178$ V
Step 2: Apply Nernst:
$E = 1.178 + \frac{(8.314)(358.15)}{2(96485)}\ln(1.2 \times 1.5^{0.5})$
$E = 1.178 + 0.01544 \times \ln(1.4697) = 1.178 + 0.01544 \times 0.3851 = 1.178 + 0.00595$
$\boxed{E = 1.184 \text{ V}}$

### Part (b): Total polarisation loss
$V_{loss} = E - V_{cell} = 1.184 - 0.75 = \mathbf{0.434 \text{ V}}$

### Part (c): Volumetric H₂ consumption rate
Total current: $I = 0.4 \text{ A/cm}^2 \times 250 \text{ cm}^2 = 100$ A
Molar flow: $\dot{n} = I/(nF) = 100/(2 \times 96485) = 5.18 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/s
At NTP (273.15 K, 1 atm): $\dot{V} = \dot{n} \times 22.414 \text{ L/mol} = 11.6 \times 10^{-3}$ L/s
$\boxed{\dot{V}_{H_2} = 11.6 \text{ mL/s} = 41.8 \text{ L/h}}$

### Part (d): Electrical efficiency (LHV)
Power out: $P = V_{cell} \cdot I = 0.75 \times 100 = 75$ W
H₂ mass flow: $\dot{m} = 5.18 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.016 = 1.044 \times 10^{-3}$ g/s
Chemical power in: $\dot{m} \times LHV_{mass} = 1.044\times 10^{-3} \times 119900 = 125.2$ W
$\eta_{LHV} = 75/125.2 = \mathbf{59.9\%}$

(Alternatively, $\eta_{LHV} = (V_{cell}/1.184) \times 0.981 = 0.634 \times 0.981 = 0.622$ — same physics, slight difference because the LHV here is for 25 °C water; in a hot cell, more steam is produced, slightly reducing the effective ΔH.)

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix: AFC vs PEMFC

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is the **most frequently asked 14-mark question** in KTU Module 4 examinations. Students are expected to tabulate, not merely list.

| Parameter | AFC (30–40 % KOH, 80 °C) | PEMFC (Nafion 117, 80 °C) |
|---|---|---|
| Charge carrier | OH⁻ | H⁺ |
| Electrolyte state | Liquid (immobilized in matrix) | Solid polymer |
| Anode catalyst | Ni, Raney-Ni, Pt | Pt/C (0.05–0.2 mg/cm²) |
| Cathode catalyst | Ag, Pt, perovskite oxide | Pt/C (0.1–0.4 mg/cm²) |
| Anode reaction | H₂ + 2OH⁻ → 2H₂O + 2e⁻ | H₂ → 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ |
| Cathode reaction | ½O₂ + H₂O + 2e⁻ → 2OH⁻ | ½O₂ + 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂O |
| Water site of generation | Anode (must be removed) | Cathode (must be removed) |
| ORR kinetics | Faster (high i₀) | Slower (low i₀) |
| OCV at 80 °C | ≈ 1.18 V | ≈ 1.18 V |
| Practical voltage | 0.7–0.85 V | 0.6–0.75 V |
| Peak power density | 0.2–0.3 W/cm² | 0.6–1.0 W/cm² |
| CO₂ tolerance | **Poor** — forms K₂CO₃ | Good |
| Operating temperature | 60–250 °C | 60–90 °C |
| Cost (relative) | Low | High |
| Lifetime (h) | 5,000–15,000 | 5,000–20,000 |
| Major applications | Space, submarines, buses | Passenger vehicles (Toyota Mirai) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: AFC Functional Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of an Alkaline Fuel Cell, including flow streams, electrochemical reactions at each electrode, water-management paths, and electrical load. All node IDs are alphanumeric to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguard.

```mermaid
graph TB
    H2TANK[Hydrogen Cylinder] --> ANFLOW[Anode Flow Field Plate]
    AIRIN[Oxygen or Air Inlet] --> CAFLOW[Cathode Flow Field Plate]
    ANFLOW --> ANPOR[Porous Nickel Anode]
    CAFLOW --> CAPOR[Porous Silver Cathode]
    ANPOR --> KOHEL[Alkaline KOH Electrolyte Matrix 30 to 40 wt percent]
    KOHEL --> CAPOR
    ANPOR -.Water H2O produced at anode.-. WATERMGT[Water Management Separator]
    WATERMGT --> WATOUT[Water Outlet and Storage]
    EXLOAD[External Electrical Load Electric Bus Traction Motor] --> ANFLOW
    CAPOR --> EXLOAD
    CATALYST[Electrode Catalyst Layer Pt or Ag for ORR, Ni for HOR] --> ANPOR
    CATALYST --> CAPOR
    CO2SCRUB[CO2 Scrubber KOH Impregnated Activated Carbon] --> AIRIN
    STACK[Cell Stack Bipolar Plates Connect Multiple Cells] --> ANFLOW
    STACK --> CAFLOW
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Half-Cell Reaction Zones

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagram illustrates the **electrochemical zones** and ionic / electronic / molecular transport pathways inside a single AFC.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph ANODE_ZONE[Anode Compartment]
        H2MOLEC[H2 Gas Molecule] --> H2ADS[H adsorbed on Ni catalyst]
        H2ADS --> HREACT[H reacts with OH minus]
        HREACT --> H2OPROD[H2O liquid product]
        HREACT --> ERELEASE[Releases 2 electrons]
    end
    subgraph ELECTROLYTE_ZONE[Alkaline KOH Matrix]
        OHION[OH minus ion migrates] --> ANODE_ZONE
    end
    subgraph CATHODE_ZONE[Cathode Compartment]
        O2MOLEC[O2 Gas Molecule] --> O2ADS[O2 adsorbed on Ag catalyst]
        O2ADS --> ORR[Oxygen Reduction Reaction]
        H2O_REACT[H2O consumed at cathode] --> ORR
        ORR --> OHREGEN[2 OH minus regenerated]
        ORR --> EABSORB[Consumes 2 electrons]
    end
    ERELEASE --> EXCIRCUIT[External Circuit Current Flow]
    EXCIRCUIT --> EABSORB
    ELECTROLYTE_ZONE --> CATHODE_ZONE
```

## 4.3 Polarization Curve: The Three-Loss Visualisation

> [!NOTE]
> The figure below is rendered as a **functional plot concept** using Mermaid's class-based styling. (Quantitative polarisation curves with measured data points are not rendered by Mermaid; consult Fig. 5.3 of Larminie & Dicks, *Fuel Cell Systems Explained*, for a precise plot.)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph POL_CURVE[Fuel Cell Polarization Curve Qualitative]
        P1[Open Circuit Voltage 1.18 V at 80 C] --> P2[Activation Loss Region Slope 60 to 80 mV per decade]
        P2 --> P3[Ohmic Loss Region Linear Slope equals ASR]
        P3 --> P4[Concentration Loss Region Knee at i limiting]
        P4 --> P5[Limiting Current 1.5 A per cm2]
    end
    style P1 fill:#ffe5b4
    style P2 fill:#ffcccc
    style P3 fill:#ccffcc
    style P4 fill:#cce5ff
    style P5 fill:#e0ccff
```

**Engineering interpretation of the curve:**
- **Region 1 → 2 (Activation loss, red):** Dominated by sluggish ORR; AFCs are *better* than PEMFCs because Ag and Ni give higher i₀ for ORR in alkaline media.
- **Region 2 → 3 (Ohmic loss, green):** Linear region controlled by ionic resistance of KOH matrix and contact resistances; the slope of this region defines the **Area-Specific Resistance (ASR)** of the cell.
- **Region 3 → 4 (Concentration loss, blue):** Mass transport of H₂ and O₂ becomes rate-limiting; near the limiting current density, a small voltage change corresponds to a large current drop.

## 4.4 Component-Material Cross-Section (Block View)

| Layer (from anode in to cathode in) | Typical Material | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Anode bipolar plate | Nickel-plated steel | Distributes H₂, conducts electrons, separates cells |
| 2. Anode GDL (gas diffusion layer) | Sintered Ni powder | Provides H₂ access to catalyst |
| 3. Anode catalyst | Raney-Ni (high surface area Ni–Al alloy) | Catalyses HOR |
| 4. Electrolyte matrix | Asbestos (older) / Polyethylene / ZrO₂-PVA (modern) | Holds 30–40 % KOH, conducts OH⁻ |
| 5. Cathode catalyst | Ag, Au, Pt, or Pt–Ag alloy | Catalyses ORR |
| 6. Cathode GDL | Sintered Ag or Ni foam | Provides O₂ access |
| 7. Cathode bipolar plate | Nickel-plated steel | Distributes O₂, collects current |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Ni at the anode and Ag at the cathode?** Ni is cheap and exhibits fast HOR kinetics in alkaline media; Ag catalyses the ORR more selectively than Pt in alkaline conditions and is roughly 50× cheaper.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part A questions in the KTU ESE require **precise 3-mark answers** — usually a one-sentence definition, a short list, or a small derivation. Aim for **60–80 words** per answer.

### Question 1 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 1]**
**Define Alkaline Fuel Cell. Why is it called "alkaline"?**

**Model Answer:**
An Alkaline Fuel Cell (AFC) is a low-temperature fuel cell that uses an aqueous solution of potassium hydroxide (KOH) as the electrolyte, with hydrogen as the fuel and oxygen (or air) as the oxidant, producing water and electricity. It is called "alkaline" because the KOH electrolyte has a pH ≈ 14 (strongly basic), so the charge carrier is the hydroxide ion (OH⁻) rather than the proton (H⁺) found in acidic fuel cells. The alkaline environment also permits the use of non-precious metal catalysts such as Ni and Ag. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Why are alkaline fuel cells sensitive to carbon dioxide? How is this limitation overcome in practical systems?**

**Model Answer:**
Atmospheric CO₂ reacts with the KOH electrolyte as $2\text{KOH} + \text{CO}_2 \to \text{K}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$, forming potassium carbonate that precipitates, blocks the porous electrodes, and reduces OH⁻ conductivity. This degrades cell performance. Practical systems overcome this limitation by (i) using **scrubbed pure O₂** instead of air (as in NASA's Apollo AFC), (ii) installing **CO₂ scrubbers** (e.g., soda-lime or amine absorbers) in the air inlet stream, or (iii) circulating the KOH electrolyte to remove carbonates continuously. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B**
> Students frequently lose 2–3 marks per question on AFC topics by:
> 1. **Writing the half-cell reactions for PEMFC (H⁺) instead of AFC (OH⁻).** Always write the OH⁻-based reactions for AFCs.
> 2. **Forgetting the temperature correction of 0.85 mV/K** in Nernst-based problems.
> 3. **Confusing the site of water generation** (anode for AFC, cathode for PEMFC) — a 1-mark question in nearly every ESE.
> 4. **Omitting units in the final answer** — strictly penalised by KTU examiners.
> 5. **Mixing HHV and LHV in efficiency calculations** without specifying the basis.

### Question A: Working Principle and Nernst Derivation

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the construction and working principle of an Alkaline Fuel Cell with a neat sketch. Clearly state the half-cell and overall reactions.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the Nernst equation for an H₂–O₂ AFC operating at temperature $T$ and pressures $p_{H_2}$, $p_{O_2}$. Hence, calculate the reversible EMF at 80 °C and 1.5 atm of pure H₂, 1.5 atm of pure O₂.

**Model Solution (a) — Construction and Working:**

**Construction:** A single AFC consists of two porous electrodes (anode and cathode) separated by a porous matrix saturated with 30–40 wt% KOH electrolyte. Hydrogen is fed to the anode side and oxygen to the cathode side through flow-field plates. The electrodes are typically **porous nickel (anode)** and **porous silver or Ag-impregnated nickel (cathode)**. The matrix is historically asbestos, but modern systems use **polymer or ceramic matrices** (ZrO₂–PVA, polyethylene).

**Working Principle:** Hydrogen gas diffuses through the anode pores to the catalyst–electrolyte–gas triple-phase boundary. At the anode, H₂ reacts with OH⁻ ions to produce water and releases two electrons per molecule:

$$\text{H}_2 + 2\text{OH}^- \longrightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- \quad (\text{Anode, HOR})$$

The electrons travel through the external circuit (delivering electrical power) to the cathode. At the cathode, O₂ combines with water and the incoming electrons to regenerate the OH⁻ ions:

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- \longrightarrow 2\text{OH}^- \quad (\text{Cathode, ORR})$$

The OH⁻ ions migrate through the KOH matrix back to the anode, completing the internal ionic circuit. The overall reaction is:

$$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$$

Water is produced at the **anode**, which must be drained to prevent flooding. The cell must also be protected from CO₂ in the oxidant stream.

**[7 marks: 3 for construction + 4 for reactions and explanation]**

**Model Solution (b) — Nernst Derivation and Numerical:**

**Derivation:**
Start from the Nernst equation for the overall reaction:

$$E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$

For $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \to \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$, the reaction quotient is:

$$Q = \frac{1}{p_{H_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{1/2}}$$

Substituting $n = 2$ and using $\ln(1/x) = -\ln(x)$:

$$E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(p_{H_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{1/2}\right) \qquad \text{(Nernst for AFC)}$$

The temperature-corrected standard EMF is:

$$E^0(T) = 1.229 - 0.85 \times 10^{-3}(T - 298.15) \text{ V}$$

**Numerical evaluation at 80 °C, 1.5 atm of each gas:**

Step 1: Compute $E^0(353.15)$:
$E^0 = 1.229 - 0.85 \times 10^{-3} \times (353.15 - 298.15)$
$E^0 = 1.229 - 0.85 \times 10^{-3} \times 55 = 1.229 - 0.04675 = 1.182$ V

Step 2: Evaluate the Nernst term:
$\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{8.314 \times 353.15}{2 \times 96485} = \frac{2936.0}{192970} = 0.01521$ V

Step 3: Compute the log term:
$\ln(p_{H_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{1/2}) = \ln(1.5 \times \sqrt{1.5}) = \ln(1.5 \times 1.2247) = \ln(1.837) = 0.608$

Step 4: Add to $E^0$:
$E = 1.182 + 0.01521 \times 0.608 = 1.182 + 0.00925$

$$\boxed{E = 1.191 \text{ V}}$$

**[7 marks: 3 for derivation + 4 for numerical evaluation]**

---

### Question B: Electrochemistry, Performance, and Hydrogen Consumption

**(a) [7 marks]** With suitable electrode reactions, explain the electrochemistry of the Alkaline Fuel Cell. State clearly how the half-cell potentials are obtained and show that the cell EMF at standard conditions is 1.229 V.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 50 kW AFC stack for a fuel-cell bus has 250 cells in series, each operating at 0.8 V and 250 A. Calculate (i) the H₂ consumption rate in kg/h, (ii) the HHV-based efficiency, and (iii) the stack power density if each cell has an active area of 200 cm².

**Model Solution (a) — Electrochemistry:**
