## 5.2 Part B Question — **Internal Choice (14 Marks)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** In Part B, Module 4, students must answer ONE full 14-mark question. Two alternative choices (A or B) are provided. Each 14-mark question is subdivided into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. **Solve BOTH sub-parts.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 — Model Question Paper]`

> **(a)** Explain the **classification of automotive fuels** with neat categorization. Discuss the **merits and demerits of at least three alternative fuels** compared to conventional gasoline and diesel. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** A four-cylinder SI engine has a **compression ratio of 10:1**, runs on gasoline with **RON = 95**, and consumes fuel at a rate of **2.5 kg/hr** while producing **30 kW** of brake power. Compute the **(i)** Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC), **(ii)** air mass flow rate (assuming stoichiometric operation), and **(iii)** discuss whether a fuel switch to ethanol (RON 110) would be advantageous. *(7 Marks)*

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Classification of Automotive Fuels:**

**(i) Based on Physical State:**
- *Gaseous:* CNG, LPG, Hydrogen.
- *Liquid:* Gasoline, diesel, ethanol, biodiesel.

**(ii) Based on Source:**
- *Fossil:* Petroleum products, natural gas.
- *Bio-derived:* Bioethanol, biodiesel.
- *Synthetic:* E-fuels, Fischer-Tropsch fuels.

**(iii) Based on Engine Compatibility:**
- *SI fuels:* Gasoline, ethanol, CNG.
- *CI fuels:* Diesel, biodiesel.

**Merits and Demerits — Three Alternative Fuels:**

**1. Compressed Natural Gas (CNG):**
- *Merits:* High octane (130), low CO and HC emissions, lower cost per km, domestically abundant. **(1 mark)**
- *Demerits:* Lower energy density per volume (requires large/high-pressure tanks), limited refuelling network. **(1 mark)**

**2. Bioethanol (E100):**
- *Merits:* Renewable, carbon-neutral cycle, very high octane (110), reduces crude-oil dependence. **(1 mark)**
- *Demerits:* Lower LHV (26.9 MJ/kg → 39% less energy/kg than gasoline), hygroscopic (corrosion risk), engine cold-start difficulty. **(1 mark)**

**3. Hydrogen (H₂):**
- *Merits:* Zero CO₂ emissions, ultra-high LHV (120 MJ/kg), abundant raw source (water). **(1 mark)**
- *Demerits:* Extremely low density (storage challenges at 700 bar or –253 °C), high production cost via electrolysis, infrastructure near-absent. **(1 mark)**

**Conclusion:** No single alternative fuel universally replaces gasoline/diesel; selection is application-specific. **(1 mark)**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Compression Ratio (CR) = 10:1
- Fuel: Gasoline, RON = 95
- Fuel consumption rate ($\dot{m}_f$) = 2.5 kg/hr
- Brake Power ($P_b$) = 30 kW
- Stoichiometric AFR for gasoline = 14.7

### (i) BSFC Calculation — 2 Marks

$$
BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b} \times 3600
$$

Convert fuel flow to g/hr: $\dot{m}_f = 2.5\ kg/hr = 2500\ g/hr$.

$$
BSFC = \dfrac{2500\ g/hr}{30\ kW} = 83.33\ g/kWh
$$

**BSFC = 83.33 g/kWh** (a typical value for an SI engine indicates good fuel economy). **(2 Marks)**

### (ii) Air Mass Flow Rate — 3 Marks

$$
\dot{m}_{air} = AFR_{stoich} \times \dot{m}_{fuel}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{air} = 14.7 \times 2.5 = 36.75\ kg/hr
$$

**Air mass flow rate = 36.75 kg/hr = 0.0102 kg/s.** **(3 Marks)**

### (iii) Ethanol Switch Analysis — 2 Marks

A switch to ethanol (RON 110) **would be advantageous** for the following reasons:
- Ethanol's higher octane permits an **increase in compression ratio** (e.g., 10:1 → 12:1), boosting **thermal efficiency** by ≈ 8–10%.
- Ethanol's higher latent heat (841 vs 350 kJ/kg) provides **intake charge cooling**, further suppressing knock.

**However, disadvantages include:**
- Ethanol has only **61%** of gasoline's energy density → **fuel consumption increases ~40%**.
- Fuel system components (rubber seals, tanks) may need **modification** due to ethanol's solvent properties.
- Cold-start in winter climates becomes problematic without auxiliary gasoline injection.

**Verdict:** Advantageous for high-performance/racing SI engines with proper recalibration; not ideal for economy-oriented urban commuter cars due to range penalty. **(2 Marks)**

**Total Part (b): 7 Marks.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 — Alternative Module 4 Choice]`

> **(a)** With a neat sketch (or block diagram), explain the **Well-to-Wheel (WTW) energy pathway** for a Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) and a conventional gasoline car. Compare their net **drivetrain efficiencies** and discuss the implications for CO₂ emissions. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** A compression-ignition engine runs on a **B20 biodiesel blend** (20% biodiesel + 80% diesel by volume). Given that diesel density = 835 kg/m³, biodiesel density = 880 kg/m³, LHV of diesel = 43 MJ/kg, LHV of biodiesel = 37 MJ/kg, and the engine consumes **5 L/hr** of the blend, compute the **(i)** mass-based blending ratio, **(ii)** average LHV of the blend in MJ/kg, and **(iii)** total energy released per hour. *(7 Marks)*

---

### **Model Answer — Question B**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Well-to-Wheel Pathway Comparison:**

**For Gasoline ICE Vehicle:**
1. Crude oil extraction (Stage 1).
2. Refining to gasoline (Stage 2) — losses ≈ 10–15%.
3. Distribution to petrol pump (Stage 3) — losses ≈ 1–2%.
4. Storage in fuel tank (Stage 4).
5. Engine combustion (Stage 5) — thermal efficiency ≈ 30–35%.
6. Transmission + drivetrain (Stage 6) — mechanical efficiency ≈ 90%.
7. Wheels (Stage 7) — Net efficiency ≈ **20–25%**.

**For BEV:**
1. Primary energy: coal/hydro/solar/wind (Stage 1).
2. Electricity generation (Stage 2) — efficiency ≈ 35–50% (thermal plants) or 90% (renewables).
3. Grid transmission (Stage 3) — losses ≈ 6–8%.
4. Battery charging (Stage 4) — efficiency ≈ 90%.
5. Motor + inverter (Stage 5) — efficiency ≈ 85–95%.
6. Transmission (Stage 6) — efficiency ≈ 95%.
7. Wheels (Stage 7) — Net drivetrain efficiency ≈ **70–80%**.

**Comparative Table:**

| Metric | Gasoline ICE | BEV (with renewable grid) |
|--------|--------------|----------------------------|
| Well-to-Wheel Efficiency | 20–25% | 70–80% |
| Tank-to-Wheel CO₂ | ~2.3 kg CO₂/L | 0 g/km (TTW) |
| Well-to-Wheel CO₂ | ~2.4 kg CO₂/km | 0.05–0.15 kg CO₂/km (depends on grid) |

**Implications:** A BEV charged from a **renewable** grid produces **70–90% less lifecycle CO₂** than an ICE vehicle. Even with India's current coal-heavy grid (~75% coal-based), BEVs typically achieve 30–40% lower WTW CO₂ than gasoline vehicles. **(7 Marks: 2 for ICE pathway + 2 for BEV pathway + 1 for table + 2 for CO₂ discussion)**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Blend: 20% biodiesel + 80% diesel by **volume**.
- $\rho_{diesel} = 835\ kg/m^3$, $\rho_{biodiesel} = 880\ kg/m^3$.
- $LHV_{diesel} = 43\ MJ/kg$, $LHV_{biodiesel} = 37\ MJ/kg$.
- Total blend volume consumed = 5 L/hr.

### (i) Mass-Based Blending Ratio — 3 Marks

Volume of biodiesel in 5 L blend = $0.20 \times 5 = 1.0\ L$.
Volume of diesel in 5 L blend = $0.80 \times 5 = 4.0\ L$.

Convert to mass:
$$
m_{biodiesel} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 880 = 0.880\ kg
$$

$$
m_{diesel} = 4.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 835 = 3.340\ kg
$$

$$
m_{total} = 0.880 + 3.340 = 4.220\ kg
$$

Mass-based ratio:
$$
\%m_{biodiesel} = \dfrac{0.880}{4.220} \times 100 \approx 20.85\%
$$

$$
\%m_{diesel} = \dfrac{3.340}{4.220} \times 100 \approx 79.15\%
$$

**Mass blend = 20.85% biodiesel + 79.15% diesel (by mass).** **(3 Marks)**

### (ii) Average LHV of the Blend — 2 Marks

$$
LHV_{blend} = \%m_{diesel} \times LHV_{diesel} + \%m_{biodiesel} \times LHV_{biodiesel}
$$

$$
LHV_{blend} = 0.7915 \times 43 + 0.2085 \times 37
$$

$$
LHV_{blend} = 34.03 + 7.71 = 41.74\ MJ/kg
$$

**Average LHV ≈ 41.74 MJ/kg.** **(2 Marks)**

### (iii) Total Energy Released Per Hour — 2 Marks

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \times LHV_{blend}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = 4.220\ kg/hr \times 41.74\ MJ/kg
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = 176.14\ MJ/hr
$$

Convert to kW: $\dot{Q}_{in} = 176.14 / 3.6 \approx 48.93\ kW$.

**Total heat input ≈ 176.14 MJ/hr ≈ 48.93 kW.** **(2 Marks)**

**Total Part (b): 7 Marks.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on "Fuel and Fuel Choice" Questions:**
> 1. **Mixing up RON and MON:** Research Octane Number (RON) is measured under less severe conditions than Motor Octane Number (MON). Anti-Knock Index (AKI) used in India = (RON + MON)/2. Confusing these is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 2. **Wrong unit conversions:** LHV in MJ/kg must be converted to kW when paired with time in seconds (1 MJ/hr = 0.2778 kW). Missing this conversion is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Neglecting volumetric vs mass-based blends:** When asked about B20 biodiesel, the volume fraction is NOT the same as mass fraction because densities differ. **Always convert using ρ.**
> 4. **Forgetting λ vs φ:** Equivalence ratio φ = 1/λ. Examiners swap these to test understanding.
> 5. **No diagrams/sketches for descriptive answers:** KTU examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for **neat labeled diagrams**. Even a block diagram of fuel classification fetches full marks.
> 6. **Mis-stating WTW vs TTW:** Examiners frequently demand precise terminology. Saying "EV has zero emissions" without qualifying "TTW only" is technically incorrect.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Fuel and Fuel Choice: Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ⭐ **Calorific Value (LHV)** order (descending MJ/kg): Hydrogen (120) > CNG (50) > LPG (46) > Gasoline (44) > Diesel (43) > Biodiesel (37) > Ethanol (26.9).
- ⭐ **Octane Number (RON)** order (descending): Hydrogen (~130+) > CNG (130) > Ethanol (110) > Premium Gasoline (95–98) > Regular Gasoline (91) > Diesel (20–30).
- ⭐ **Cetane Number (CN)** order (descending): Biodiesel (55–60) > Diesel (45–55) > Gasoline (< 20).
- ⭐ **Stoichiometric AFR** for gasoline = 14.7; for diesel = 14.5; for hydrogen = 34.3 (highest due to oxygen requirement per kg).
- ⭐ **Knocking** in SI engines → solved by higher octane, retarded ignition timing, lower CR, or charge cooling (ethanol).
- ⭐ **Diesel knock (diesel ping)** is opposite of SI knock — caused by *low* cetane → solved by ignition improver additives.
- ⭐ **B20 biodiesel** retains ≈ **97% of diesel's LHV per kg** but has higher viscosity → may require fuel-heating in winter.
- ⭐ **CNG** has high RON (130) but suffers **energy density penalty** → vehicle range drops ~30% vs gasoline.
- ⭐ **Hydrogen** has highest LHV but lowest density → requires **700-bar tanks** (≈ 800 km range achievable).
- ⭐ **BSFC** is the standard metric for fuel economy — lower is better (SI engines: 250–400 g/kWh; CI engines: 200–300 g/kWh).
- ⭐ **BS-VI norms** (India) cap sulphur at 10 ppm in both gasoline and diesel, mandating advanced after-treatment (GPF, DPF, SCR).
- ⭐ **Well-to-Wheel (WTW)** analysis is **mandatory** for any fuel comparison — TTW-only analysis is misleading.
- ⭐ **Lambda (λ)** is the universally used equivalence parameter in ECUs; **φ** (phi) is the academic symbol; **φ = 1/λ**.
- ⭐ For KTU ESE, always include a **classification diagram** and a **comparison table** in 14-mark answers — they consistently fetch 1–2 bonus marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT612 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fuel Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuel Processing (KTU 2024 OEMUT612 Definition):**
> Fuel processing refers to the **integrated thermochemical, catalytic, and physical transformation** of a primary hydrocarbon or alternative fuel into a secondary energy carrier (most commonly hydrogen-rich synthesis gas, or "syngas") that can be efficiently utilized in advanced automotive powertrains such as Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cells (PEMFC), Solid Oxide Fuel Cells (SOFC), or specialized internal combustion engines.

The process encompasses a **chain of unit operations**: desulfurization → reforming → water-gas shift (WGS) → CO cleanup (PROX/methanation) → hydrogen purification/separation.

### 1.2 The Three Principal Reforming Pathways

| Pathway | Oxidant | Reaction Class | Key Character |
|---------|---------|----------------|---------------|
| **Steam Reforming (SR)** | H₂O (steam) | Endothermic | Highest H₂ yield, slow start-up |
| **Partial Oxidation (POX)** | O₂ (limited air) | Exothermic | Fast response, lower H₂ yield |
| **Autothermal Reforming (ATR)** | H₂O + O₂ | Thermally neutral | Balanced, compact, preferred for vehicles |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Kitchen Stove Metaphor":**
> Imagine you bought a car that runs on **flavored sugar water** instead of petrol. The car cannot use sugar water directly — first, a tiny onboard **"kitchen reactor"** must heat the sugar water with controlled amounts of air and steam to break it apart into **hydrogen gas** (the fuel the engine actually wants) and waste byproducts (CO₂, water vapor). The hydrogen is then cleaned of poisonous "impurities" (mainly CO) before being fed to the fuel cell. This entire kitchen — the burners, filters, scrubbers — is **Fuel Processing**.

### 1.4 Strategic Importance in Modern Automotive Technologies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **CO tolerance** of PEM fuel cells: **< 10 ppm** — mandates deep CO cleanup.
> - **Hydrogen yield target**: ≥ **70%** volumetric efficiency for SR, **40–60%** for POX.
> - **Operating temperature windows**:
>   - SR: **700–850 °C**
>   - POX: **600–900 °C**
>   - WGS (HT): **350–450 °C**, WGS (LT): **190–250 °C**
>   - PROX: **80–200 °C**
> - Standard reference metric: **LHV (Lower Heating Value) of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg**.

### 1.5 Why Onboard Fuel Processing?

- **Hydrogen infrastructure is sparse** → carry liquid/gaseous hydrocarbon (gasoline, diesel, methanol, ethanol, natural gas) and convert onboard.
- **Enables fuel-cell vehicles (FCV)** with conventional fuel logistics.
- **Powers APUs (Auxiliary Power Units)** in heavy trucks, military vehicles.
- **Critical for SOFC ranges** (high-temp, CO-tolerant) versus low-temp PEMFC.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Fuel Processing Chain — Step-by-Step Logic

The onboard fuel processor is a **multi-stage catalytic microreactor system**. Each stage has a deterministic chemical role:

**Step 1 → Feedstock Vaporization & Preheating**
- Liquid fuels (gasoline, methanol) are vaporized at **180–350 °C**.
- Heat is recovered from downstream exhaust via **recuperative heat exchangers**.

**Step 2 → Desulfurization (Adsorptive Guard Bed)**
- Sulfur compounds (thiophenes, H₂S) **poison reforming catalysts** irreversibly.
- Adsorbents: **ZnO, Ni-based sorbents, activated carbon, Ag-zeolites**.
- Operating regime: **ambient to 400 °C**.

**Step 3 → Reforming (Core Reaction Zone)**
- Hydrocarbons react with H₂O, O₂, or both to yield **H₂ + CO + CO₂ + unconverted CH₄**.
- Catalyst: **Ni/Al₂O₃, Rh/CeO₂, Pt-based monoliths**.

**Step 4 → High-Temperature Water-Gas Shift (HT-WGS)**
- Converts bulk CO to additional H₂ using **Fe₃O₄–Cr₂O₃** catalyst at **350–450 °C**.

**Step 5 → Low-Temperature Water-Gas Shift (LT-WGS)**
- Further reduces CO from ~3% to ~0.5% using **Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃** at **190–250 °C**.

**Step 6 → Preferential Oxidation of CO (PROX)**
- Selective catalytic oxidation: CO + ½O₂ → CO₂.
- Catalyst: **Pt/Al₂O₃, Au/Fe₂O₃, Cu/CeO₂**.
- Drops CO to **< 10–50 ppm** for PEMFC compatibility.

**Step 7 → Hydrogen Purification (PSA / Membrane)**
- **Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA)** or **Pd-based membrane** separates H₂.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" of Each Step:**
> - Why desulfurize first? → Sulfur permanently deactivates Ni and Pt catalysts (chemisorption poisoning).
> - Why two-stage WGS? → Thermodynamics favors low T; kinetics favors high T. Cascading recovers both yields.
> - Why PROX? → Even trace CO (≥10 ppm) binds Pt anode catalysts in PEMFCs and degrades performance by **>50%**.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Equations for Fuel Processing (KTU Exam Essentials)**

| # | Equation / Quantity | Symbolic Form | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------------------|---------------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Steam Reforming (generic) | $C_nH_m + nH_2O \rightarrow nCO + (n + m/2)H_2$ | — | Stoichiometric H₂ yield |
| 2 | Partial Oxidation | $C_nH_m + \tfrac{n}{2}O_2 \rightarrow nCO + \tfrac{m}{2}H_2$ | — | Fast-response reforming |
| 3 | Water-Gas Shift (WGS) | $CO + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2 \quad \Delta H = -41.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | kJ/mol | CO → H₂ conversion |
| 4 | Methanation (parasitic) | $CO + 3H_2 \rightleftharpoons CH_4 + H_2O$ | — | Reverse PROX (unwanted) |
| 5 | PROX (desired) | $CO + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$ | — | Final CO removal |
| 6 | H₂ yield definition | $Y_{H_2} = \dfrac{\text{moles } H_2 \text{ produced}}{2n + m/2 \text{ (theoretical max)}}$ | dimensionless | Reactor efficiency |
| 7 | Fuel processor efficiency | $\eta_{FP} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot LHV_{H_2}}{\dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot LHV_{fuel} + Q_{aux}}$ | % | System KPI |
| 8 | Steam-to-Carbon Ratio (S/C) | $S/C = \dfrac{\text{moles } H_2O}{\text{moles } C \text{ in fuel}}$ | mol/mol | Coke-prevention metric |
| 9 | Equilibrium constant (WGS) | $K_{eq} = \dfrac{[CO_2][H_2]}{[CO][H_2O]} = \exp\left(-\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{RT}\right)$ | — | Conversion limit |
| 10 | Arrhenius rate law | $k = A \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | 1/s | Catalyst kinetics |
| 11 | Gibbs free energy (WGS) | $\Delta G^\circ = -29.0 + 0.0267\,T \text{ (kJ/mol, 298–1000 K)}$ | kJ/mol | Equilibrium shift |
| 12 | CO tolerance (PEMFC) | $[CO] \leq 10 \text{ ppm}$ | ppm | Design constraint |
| 13 | Steam reforming (methanol) | $CH_3OH + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + 3H_2$ | — | Low-T reforming (250°C) |
| 14 | Auto-thermal balance | $\Delta H_{SR} + \Delta H_{POX} = 0$ | kJ/mol | ATR stoichiometry |
| 15 | LHV of H₂ | $LHV_{H_2} = 120 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | MJ/kg | Energy benchmark |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Substitution Warning:** Use **S/C ≥ 2.5** to suppress carbon deposition (coking) on Ni catalysts. KTU examiners frequently test this value.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Toyota Mirai FCV** (2014 onwards): Uses high-pressure H₂ tanks, not onboard reforming — but extensive research continues on methanol reformers for **range-extender FCV-APUs**.
- **Military / Heavy-duty APU**: Diesel-fueled SOFC systems with onboard ATR + WGS for silent watch operations.
- **Biodiesel processing**: Transesterification of triglycerides with methanol → FAMEs + glycerol (a parallel "fuel processing" pathway for ICE vehicles).
- **Hydrogen refueling stations**: Off-board reformers convert natural gas → H₂ dispensed at **350 bar** or **700 bar**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: H₂ Yield from Steam Reforming of Methane

We derive the **maximum theoretical H₂ yield** and the **equilibrium CO slip** for the SR + WGS cascade.

**Starting assumption:** 1 mol of CH₄ as feedstock, complete conversion.

**Step 1 — Steam Reforming Reaction**
$$
CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2
$$

The stoichiometric table per mole of CH₄ (with S/C = s):

| Species | Initial | Change | Final |
|---------|---------|--------|-------|
| CH₄ | 1 | −1 | 0 |
| H₂O | s | −1 | s − 1 |
| CO | 0 | +1 | 1 |
| H₂ | 0 | +3 | 3 |

**Step 2 — Water-Gas Shift Equilibrium**
$$
CO + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2
$$

Let $x$ = moles of CO converted via WGS.

| Species | After SR | After WGS |
|---------|----------|-----------|
| CO | 1 | $1 - x$ |
| H₂O | $s - 1$ | $s - 1 - x$ |
| CO₂ | 0 | $x$ |
| H₂ | 3 | $3 + x$ |

**Step 3 — Total Hydrogen Yield**

$$
Y_{H_2} = \dfrac{n_{H_2}}{\text{theoretical max}} = \dfrac{3 + x}{4} \quad \text{(since max } H_2 \text{ from 1 CH}_4 = 4 \text{ mol)}
$$

**Step 4 — Equilibrium Constant Expression**

Total moles at equilibrium:
$$
n_{tot} = (1-x) + (s-1-x) + x + (3+x) = s + 3
$$

Note: $n_{tot}$ is independent of $x$ (favorable property). Partial pressures assuming ideal gas at total pressure $P$:

$$
K_{eq} = \dfrac{(x)(3+x)/n_{tot}^2}{[(1-x)(s-1-x)]/n_{tot}^2} = \dfrac{x(3+x)}{(1-x)(s-1-x)}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $x$ at typical conditions**

Take $T = 800$ °C $= 1073$ K, $s = 3$ (typical S/C ratio):

$$
\Delta G^\circ = -29.0 + 0.0267 \times 1073 = -29.0 + 28.65 = -0.35 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

$$
K_{eq} = \exp\left(-\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{RT}\right) = \exp\left(\dfrac{350}{8.314 \times 1073}\right) = \exp(0.0392) = 1.040
$$

Insert into equilibrium expression with $s = 3$:
$$
1.040 = \dfrac{x(3+x)}{(1-x)(2-x)}
$$

Cross-multiply:
$$
1.040(1-x)(2-x) = x(3+x)
$$

Expand left side:
$$
1.040 \left(2 - 3x + x^2\right) = x(3+x)
$$

$$
2.080 - 3.120x + 1.040x^2 = 3x + x^2
$$

Rearrange:
$$
0.040x^2 - 6.120x + 2.080 = 0
$$

Apply quadratic formula $x = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$:
$$
x = \dfrac{6.120 \pm \sqrt{37.45 - 0.333}}{0.080} = \dfrac{6.120 \pm \sqrt{37.12}}{0.080}
$$

$$
x = \dfrac{6.120 \pm 6.092}{0.080}
$$

Two roots: $x = \dfrac{12.21}{0.080} = 152.6$ (reject, physically > 1) and
$$
x = \dfrac{0.028}{0.080} = 0.35
$$

So $x = 0.35$ — physically reasonable ($0 \leq x \leq 1$).

**Step 6 — Final Yield Calculation**

$$
Y_{H_2} = \dfrac{3 + 0.35}{4} = \dfrac{3.35}{4} = 0.8375 = 83.75\%
$$

**Conclusion:** At 800 °C, S/C = 3, **83.75% of theoretical maximum H₂** is recoverable from methane steam reforming with equilibrium WGS — a typical benchmark cited in KTU problems.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Reforming Pathway Comparison

| Performance Metric | Steam Reforming | Partial Oxidation | Autothermal |
|--------------------|-----------------|-------------------|-------------|
| H₂ yield (% of max) | **70–85%** | 40–60% | 60–75% |
| Thermal character | Endothermic (−206 kJ/mol for CH₄) | Exothermic (−36 kJ/mol) | Neutral |
| Start-up time | Slow (heat required) | Fast | Moderate |
| Catalyst coke risk | Medium (low with high S/C) | Low | Low |
| O₂ requirement | None | High | Moderate |
| Compactness | Low (large heat exchangers) | High | **High (preferred)** |
| Preferred for onboard FCV | No (slow start-up) | Auxiliary start | **Yes (steady-state)** |

### 3.3 Mass Balance Verification — Numerical Worked Example

**Problem (KTU-Style):** A fuel processor receives **1 kg/h of methanol** at steady state. Steam reformer operates at 250 °C with conversion **X = 95%**. Calculate H₂ production rate and reformer efficiency.

**Reaction:** $CH_3OH + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + 3H_2$

**Step 1 — Molar feed rate of methanol**
$$
\dot{n}_{CH_3OH} = \dfrac{1000 \text{ g/h}}{32.04 \text{ g/mol}} = 31.21 \text{ mol/h}
$$

**Step 2 — H₂ produced at 95% conversion**
$$
\dot{n}_{H_2} = 3 \times \dot{n}_{CH_3OH} \times X = 3 \times 31.21 \times 0.95 = 88.94 \text{ mol/h}
$$

**Step 3 — H₂ mass flow rate**
$$
\dot{m}_{H_2} = 88.94 \times 2.016 = 179.3 \text{ g/h} = 0.1793 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 4 — Energy out (H₂ LHV basis)**
$$
\dot{E}_{out} = 0.1793 \times 120 = 21.52 \text{ MJ/h}
$$

**Step 5 — Energy in (methanol LHV ≈ 19.9 MJ/kg)**
$$
\dot{E}_{in} = 1.0 \times 19.9 = 19.9 \text{ MJ/h}
$$

**Step 6 — Reformer efficiency**
$$
\eta_{FP} = \dfrac{21.52}{19.9 + Q_{aux}}
$$

Ignoring $Q_{aux}$ (ideal adiabatic reformer):
$$
\eta_{FP} = \dfrac{21.52}{19.9} \times 100\% = 108.1\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical insight:** Efficiency > 100% is possible because the **water shift reaction releases chemical energy** (WGS is exothermic, −41.2 kJ/mol). Real reformers include heat losses, so $\eta_{FP} = 78$–$85\%$ is the practical range cited in KTU answer keys.

### 3.4 Derivation — Minimum Steam-to-Carbon Ratio for Coke Avoidance

Coking reaction (Boudouard): $2CO \rightarrow C + CO_2$.

For thermodynamic coke suppression, the carbon activity $a_C \leq 1$.

Using thermodynamic data for Ni catalyst:
$$
a_C = \dfrac{K_1 \cdot P_{CO}^2 / P_{CO_2}}{1 + K_2 P_{CO} + K_3 P_{H_2} + \dots}
$$

For practical purposes, **KTU answer-key value**:
$$
(S/C)_{\min} \approx 2.5 \text{ to } 3.0
$$

Operation above this threshold guarantees $a_C < 1$ — meaning no solid carbon deposits form on the Ni reforming catalyst.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Onboard Fuel Processor — Functional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A1["Fuel Tank Module"]
        tank["Liquid Fuel Tank"]
        pump["Feed Pump"]
    end

    subgraph A2["Pre-Treatment Unit"]
        vap["Vaporizer"]
        desulf["Desulfurizer ZnO Bed"]
    end

    subgraph A3["Reforming Reactor Core"]
        atr["Autothermal Reformer Ni-Al2O3"]
    end

    subgraph A4["Shift Reactor Cascade"]
        htwgs["HT-WGS Fe3O4-Cr2O3"]
        ltwgs["LT-WGS Cu-ZnO-Al2O3"]
    end

    subgraph A5["CO Cleanup Stage"]
        prox["PROX Reactor Pt-Al2O3"]
    end

    subgraph A6["Hydrogen Delivery"]
        hx["Heat Exchanger"]
        psa["PSA / Membrane Separator"]
        pemfc["PEM Fuel Cell Stack"]
    end

    tank --> pump
    pump --> vap
    vap --> desulf
    desulf --> atr
    atr -->|syngas CO H2 CO2| htwgs
    htwgs -->|CO drop to 3 percent| ltwgs
    ltwgs -->|CO drop to 0.5 percent| prox
    prox -->|CO less than 10 ppm| hx
    hx --> psa
    psa -->|pure H2| pemfc
    atr -.->|heat recuperation| vap
    pemfc -.->|exhaust heat| hx
```

### 4.2 Reforming Pathway Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Hydrocarbon Fuel Input"] --> q1{"Fast Start-Up Required?"}
    q1 -- Yes --> pox["Partial Oxidation POX"]
    q1 -- No --> q2{"Maximum H2 Yield Priority?"}
    q2 -- Yes --> sr["Steam Reforming SR"]
    q2 -- No --> atr["Autothermal Reforming ATR Balanced"]
    pox --> out["Syngas to WGS"]
    sr --> out
    atr --> out
    out --> wgs["WGS Cascade HT then LT"]
    wgs --> prox2["PROX Final Cleanup"]
    prox2 --> pem["PEMFC Ready H2 Stream"]
```

### 4.3 Fuel-Processing Subsystem Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Inputs
        f1["Gasoline Octane C8H18"]
        f2["Methanol CH3OH"]
        f3["Natural Gas CH4"]
        f4["Ethanol C2H5OH"]
    end

    subgraph ProcessUnits
        u1["Vaporizer Unit"]
        u2["Desulfurization Guard"]
        u3["Reformer Ni Catalyst"]
        u4["Shift Reactors"]
        u5["PROX Reactor"]
        u6["PSA Separator"]
    end

    subgraph Outputs
        o1["H2 to PEMFC"]
        o2["Heat Recovery Loop"]
        o3["CO2 Vent"]
        o4["Water Condensate"]
    end

    f1 --> u1
    f2 --> u1
    f3 --> u1
    f4 --> u1
    u1 --> u2 --> u3 --> u4 --> u5 --> u6 --> o1
    u3 --> o2
    u6 --> o3
    u6 --> o4
```

### 4.4 Operational Block — Steam-to-Carbon Ratio Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sc["S/C Sensor"] --> ctrl["PID Controller"]
    ctrl --> valve["Water Injection Valve"]
    valve --> reformer["ATR Reformer"]
    reformer --> sc
    reformer --> outlet["Syngas Outlet to WGS"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, OEMUT612 Module 4]**

**Q1 (3 marks):** *Define fuel processing in the context of modern automotive powertrains. List the three principal reforming methods.*

**Model Answer:**
Fuel processing is the **integrated conversion of a primary hydrocarbon or alcohol fuel into a hydrogen-rich synthesis gas (syngas)** suitable for use in fuel cells or specialized ICE systems, comprising desulfurization, reforming, water-gas shift, and CO cleanup stages.

The three principal reforming methods are:
1. **Steam Reforming (SR)** — endothermic, highest H₂ yield.
2. **Partial Oxidation (POX)** — exothermic, fast response.
3. **Autothermal Reforming (ATR)** — thermally balanced, compact and preferred for vehicles.

---

**Q2 (3 marks):** *Why is CO removal mandatory in a PEMFC fuel processor, and what method is used?*

**Model Answer:**
PEM fuel cell anodes use Pt catalysts that are **severely poisoned by carbon monoxide** at concentrations above **10 ppm**, causing voltage losses exceeding 50%. CO is removed by **Preferential Oxidation (PROX)**, where CO is selectively oxidized to CO₂ using Pt, Au, or Cu-ceria catalysts at 80–200 °C in the presence of excess H₂.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, OEMUT612 Module 4]**

---

#### **Question A (14 marks):** *Reforming Pathways and Fuel Processing Chain*

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of balanced chemical equations, explain **Steam Reforming (SR), Partial Oxidation (POX), and Autothermal Reforming (ATR)** of methane. Compare their H₂ yields, thermal character, and start-up behavior.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Steam Reforming**
$$
CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2 \quad \Delta H = +206 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

- Strongly **endothermic**, requires external heat input.
- H₂ yield: **75–85%** of theoretical maximum.
- Operates at **700–850 °C** over Ni/Al₂O₃ catalyst.

**(ii) Partial Oxidation**
$$
CH_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO + 2H_2 \quad \Delta H = -36 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

- **Exothermic**, no external heat required; ideal for fast start-up.
- H₂ yield: lower at **40–60%**.
- Catalyst: Rh, Pt on ceramic monoliths.

**(iii) Autothermal Reforming**
Combination of SR + POX such that:
$$
\Delta H_{total} = \Delta H_{SR} + \Delta H_{POX} = 0
$$

- **Thermally neutral**, compact, suitable for transient vehicle duty cycles.
- H₂ yield: **60–75%**.
- Best balance for onboard automotive applications.

**Comparison Table**

| Parameter | SR | POX | ATR |
|-----------|----|----|-----|
| ΔH | +206 kJ/mol | −36 kJ/mol | ~0 |
| H₂ yield | 75–85% | 40–60% | 60–75% |
| Start-up | Slow | Fast | Moderate |
| Heat source | External | Internal | Self |

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the complete **onboard fuel processing chain** for a methanol-fueled PEMFC vehicle. Include reaction equations and temperature ranges.

**Model Solution:**

The fuel processing chain consists of **six sequential stages**:

**Stage 1 — Vaporization** (180–250 °C)
Liquid methanol is vaporized using recovered heat.

**Stage 2 — Desulfurization** (ambient–400 °C)
- Even trace sulfur poisons catalysts.
$$
H_2S + ZnO \rightarrow ZnS + H_2O
$$

**Stage 3 — Steam Reforming of Methanol** (250–300 °C, Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst)
$$
CH_3OH + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + 3H_2 \quad \Delta H = +49.4 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

**Stage 4 — Water-Gas Shift** (HT: 350 °C; LT: 210 °C)
$$
CO + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2 \quad \Delta H = -41.2 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

**Stage 5 — PROX** (80–200 °C, Pt/Al₂O₃)
$$
CO + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO_2
$$

**Stage 6 — PSA / Membrane Separation** → pure H₂ to PEMFC.

**Justification:** Methanol is favored for onboard reforming because it reforms at the **lowest temperature** (250 °C) compared to methane (800 °C), enabling fast cold start-up and compact reactor design.

---

#### **Question B (14 marks):** *Water-Gas Shift and Process Efficiency*

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **H₂ yield** for steam reforming of methane with S/C = 3 at 800 °C, given the WGS equilibrium constant is 1.04. Show all steps.

**Model Solution:** (Refer to §3.1 detailed derivation.)

Setting up the stoichiometric table for $CH_4 + 3H_2O$ with WGS conversion variable $x$:

$$
K_{eq} = \dfrac{x(3+x)}{(1-x)(2-x)} = 1.040
$$

Cross-multiplying and expanding:
$$
2.080 - 3.120x + 1.040x^2 = 3x + x^2
$$

$$
0.040x^2 - 6.120x + 2.080 = 0
$$

Applying the quadratic formula:
$$
x = \dfrac{6.120 - \sqrt{37.12}}{0.080} = 0.35
$$

Final H₂ yield:
$$
Y_{H_2} = \dfrac{3 + 0.35}{4} \times 100\% = 83.75\%
$$

**(b) [7 marks]** A fuel processor receives **2 kg/h of methane**. With 95% reformer conversion, S/C = 3, and equilibrium WGS at 800 °C, calculate (i) total H₂ produced in mol/h, and (ii) the reformer thermal efficiency.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Molar flow of CH₄:**
$$
\dot{n}_{CH_4} = \dfrac{2000}{16.04} = 124.7 \text{ mol/h}
$$

After reforming at 95% conversion:
$$
\dot{n}_{CO}^{SR} = 124.7 \times 0.95 = 118.5 \text{ mol/h}
$$

$$
\dot{n}_{H_2}^{SR} = 3 \times 118.5 = 355.5 \text{ mol/h}
$$

WGS conversion with $x = 0.35$ per the previous derivation:
$$
\dot{n}_{H_2}^{WGS} = 0.35 \times 118.5 = 41.5 \text{ mol/h additional}
$$

$$
\dot{n}_{H_2}^{total} = 355.5 + 41.5 = 397.0 \text{ mol/h}
$$

**(ii) Energy balance:**
- LHV of CH₄ = **50.0 MJ/kg**
- LHV of H₂ = **120 MJ/kg**

$$
\dot{E}_{in} = 2 \times 50.0 = 100 \text{ MJ/h}
$$

H₂ mass flow:
$$
\dot{m}_{H_2} = 397.0 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3} = 0.800 \text{ kg/h}
$$

$$
\dot{E}_{out} = 0.800 \times 120 = 96.0 \text{ MJ/h}
$$

Reformer thermal efficiency (ignoring auxiliary heat):
$$
\eta_{FP} = \dfrac{96.0}{100} \times 100\% = 96.0\%
$$

Accounting for realistic heat losses (~10%) and auxiliary heat:
$$
\eta_{FP,\text{realistic}} \approx 78\text{–}85\%
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit confusion in LHV** — examiners expect **MJ/kg**, not kJ/kg. Conversion error = 2 marks lost.
> 2. **Forgetting WGS contribution** — students often compute only reformer H₂ and forget shift reactor adds ≈ 10–15% more H₂. Marks deducted: 3.
> 3. **Improper stoichiometric table** — not tabulating initial/change/final columns leads to incorrect equilibrium equations. Marks deducted: 2.
> 4. **Confusing CO cleanup methods** — PROX vs methanation vs PSA: only PROX is the **selective catalytic oxidation**; methanation consumes H₂ (undesired).
> 5. **Missing S/C threshold** — KTU answer keys penalize omission of the **(S/C) ≥ 2.5** anti-coking condition.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Fuel Processing (KTU Module 4)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Conversion of hydrocarbon fuel → H₂-rich syngas for fuel cells.
- ✅ **Three reforming routes:** SR (endothermic, high yield), POX (exothermic, fast), ATR (balanced, preferred for vehicles).
- ✅ **Reforming chain:** Desulfurize → Reform → HT-WGS → LT-WGS → PROX → PSA.
- ✅ **Critical temperatures:** SR 700–850 °C; POX 600–900 °C; methanol reforming **250 °C**; WGS HT 350–450 °C, LT 190–250 °C; PROX 80–200 °C.
- ✅ **Key equations:** SR: $CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2$; WGS: $CO + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2$ (ΔH = −41.2 kJ/mol); PROX: $CO + ½O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$.
- ✅ **PEMFC CO tolerance: < 10 ppm.**
- ✅ **Steam-to-Carbon ratio (S/C) ≥ 2.5–3.0** prevents catalyst coking.
- ✅ **LHV benchmarks:** H₂ = 120 MJ/kg; CH₄ = 50 MJ/kg; CH₃OH = 19.9 MJ/kg.
- ✅ **Typical H₂ yields:** SR 75–85%, POX 40–60%, ATR 60–75%.
- ✅ **ATR is the preferred automotive reformer** because it combines SR's high yield with POX's thermal self-sufficiency.
- ✅ **Methanol reforming** is favored for low-temperature onboard fuel processors.
- ✅ **PROX catalyst examples:** Pt/Al₂O₃, Au/Fe₂O₃, Cu/CeO₂.
- ✅ **Methanation (CO + 3H₂ → CH₄ + H₂O) is parasitic** — consumes H₂, not used in PEMFC systems.
- ✅ **Boudouard coking:** $2CO \rightarrow C + CO_2$ — suppressed by high S/C and steam presence.
- ✅ **Practical reformer efficiency:** 78–85% (theoretical can exceed 100% due to WGS exothermicity contribution).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Cell Stacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT612 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fuel Cell Stacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fuel Cell Stack** is a series and/or parallel arrangement of multiple individual fuel cells electrically and fluidically interconnected to deliver practically useful voltage, current, and power output for automotive and stationary applications. A single fuel cell (e.g., a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell – PEMFC) typically produces an open-circuit voltage in the range of **0.8 V to 1.2 V** under standard conditions, which is far too low to drive any vehicular electric traction motor. Therefore, $N$ individual cells are mechanically stacked using **bipolar plates** that act as the anode of one cell and the cathode of the adjacent cell, allowing a cumulative stack voltage of:

$$V_{\text{stack}} = N \cdot V_{\text{cell,avg}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):** A fuel cell stack is the modular assembly of membrane-electrode assemblies (MEAs), gas diffusion layers (GDLs), seals, and bipolar plates arranged in series to scale up voltage while meeting the power, thermal, and durability demands of modern hybrid-electric and zero-emission vehicles.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Battery Tower of Pancakes"

Imagine a single AA battery producing **1.5 V**. To light a 12 V car headlamp, you stack **8 AA cells in series** end-to-end (a "tower of pancakes"). A fuel cell stack is the **same idea**, but instead of batteries, each "pancake" is a hydrogen-fed electrochemical cell. Each pancake gives you about **0.6–0.7 V** under load. To get the **300–400 V** required by a typical EV traction inverter, you stack roughly **400–600 such pancakes** in series. The current collector between each pancake is the **bipolar plate** — analogous to the metal tab joining two AA cells in a flashlight.

### 1.3 Key Stack-Level Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Cell active area:** $A_{\text{cell}} = 50 \text{ to } 300 \text{ cm}^2$
- **Single-cell nominal voltage:** $V_{\text{cell,nom}} = 0.6 \text{ V to } 0.7 \text{ V}$
- **Typical automotive stack power:** $P_{\text{stack}} = 80 \text{ kW to } 120 \text{ kW}$ (passenger EV)
- **Operating temperature (PEMFC):** $T = 60 \text{ °C to } 80 \text{ °C}$
- **Operating pressure:** $p = 1 \text{ atm to } 3 \text{ atm}$
- **Stack volumetric power density:** $\rho_V \approx 3 \text{ kW/L to } 5 \text{ kW/L}$ (current state-of-art)
- **Stack gravimetric power density:** $\rho_m \approx 2 \text{ kW/kg to } 3.5 \text{ kW/kg}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Stack at All?** Three engineering reasons:
> 1. **Voltage scalability** — Boost $\sim 0.7 \text{ V}$ to $300\text{–}400 \text{ V}$ for traction inverters.
> 2. **Current sharing** — Multiple parallel cells reduce current per cell, minimizing $I^2R$ losses.
> 3. **Modular serviceability** — A defective cell can be bypassed/replaced without scrapping the entire pack.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Stack Architecture

A fuel cell stack is built by repeating a repeating unit called the **repeat unit** (or cell unit). The sequence of layers in a single PEMFC repeat unit is:

1. **Anode Bipolar Plate (BPP)** — channels $\text{H}_2$ to the anode, conducts electrons, removes product water and heat.
2. **Anode Gas Diffusion Layer (GDL)** — distributes reactant gas and conducts electrons/heat to/from the catalyst.
3. **Anode Catalyst Layer (CL)** — Pt/C catalyst site where Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction (HOR) occurs.
4. **Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM)** — conducts $\text{H}^+$ ions, blocks electrons and $\text{H}_2$ crossover.
5. **Cathode Catalyst Layer (CL)** — Pt/C catalyst site where Oxygen Reduction Reaction (ORR) occurs.
6. **Cathode Gas Diffusion Layer (GDL)** — distributes air/$\text{O}_2$ and removes product water.
7. **Cathode Bipolar Plate (BPP)** — channels air to the cathode, conducts electrons, collects current.

The same cathode BPP becomes the anode BPP of the next cell — this is what makes it **"bipolar."**

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

- **Step 1 — Reactant Distribution:** Dry hydrogen is fed into the anode channels of every cell through a **manifold** (external, internal, or hybrid). Simultaneously, filtered and humidified air is fed into the cathode channels.
- **Step 2 — Electrochemical Reaction at the MEA:** At the anode, $\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$; at the cathode, $\frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$.
- **Step 3 — Charge Transport:** $\text{H}^+$ ions migrate through the hydrated PEM; $e^-$ flow through the external circuit via the BPPs (in series through the stack).
- **Step 4 — Series Summation:** Because cells share a common bipolar plate, the EMFs add: $V_{\text{stack}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_{\text{cell},i}$.
- **Step 5 — Thermal & Water Management:** Heat generated ($Q = I \cdot (E_{\text{th}} - V_{\text{cell}})$) and product water must be removed by coolant loops and airflow.
- **Step 6 — Output Conditioning:** The high-voltage DC output is fed to a DC-DC boost/regulated stage and then to the traction inverter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'Why' Behind Bipolar Plates:** A unipolar (monopolar) design would have one large electrode shared by many cells — this would force all current to travel through long conduction paths, dramatically increasing ohmic losses. The bipolar design forces current to flow through the **shortest path** from cell to cell, which is why modern automotive stacks are exclusively bipolar.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning of Each Symbol | Units | Typical Use |
|---|--------------------|------------------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $V_{\text{stack}} = N \cdot V_{\text{cell,avg}}$ | $N$ = number of cells; $V_{\text{cell,avg}}$ = average cell voltage | V | Required stack voltage sizing |
| 2 | $P_{\text{stack}} = V_{\text{stack}} \cdot I_{\text{stack}} = N \cdot V_{\text{cell}} \cdot I$ | $P$ = power; $I$ = current | W or kW | Power output calculation |
| 3 | $V_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{Nernst}} - \eta_{\text{act}} - \eta_{\text{ohm}} - \eta_{\text{conc}}$ | $E_{\text{Nernst}}$ = thermodynamic EMF; $\eta$ = losses | V | Polarization curve |
| 4 | $E_{\text{Nernst}} = E^0 + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{H}_2}\sqrt{p_{\text{O}_2}}}{p_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}\right)$ | $E^0 = 1.229$ V (25 °C); $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K; $F = 96485$ C/mol | V | Theoretical open-circuit voltage |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{ohm}} = I \cdot (R_{\text{ohm}}) = j \cdot \text{ASR}$ | $R_{\text{ohm}}$ = total ohmic resistance; ASR = area-specific resistance (Ω·cm²); $j$ = current density (A/cm²) | V | Linear region loss |
| 6 | $j = \dfrac{I}{A_{\text{cell}}}$ | Current density | A/cm² | Performance metric |
| 7 | $\rho_V = \dfrac{P_{\text{stack}}}{V_{\text{stack,total}}}$ | Volumetric power density | kW/L | Packaging index |
| 8 | $\rho_m = \dfrac{P_{\text{stack}}}{m_{\text{stack}}}$ | Gravimetric power density | kW/kg | Vehicle range index |
| 9 | $N = \dfrac{V_{\text{required}}}{V_{\text{cell,nom}}}$ | Number of cells to be stacked | dimensionless | Stack design |
| 10 | $\eta_{\text{stack}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{cell,avg}}}{1.482} \times 100\%$ | $1.482$ V = higher heating value $\text{H}_2$ equivalent | % | Stack efficiency |
| 11 | $\dot{Q}_{\text{loss}} = I \cdot (E_{\text{Nernst}} - V_{\text{cell}})$ per cell | Heat generated per cell | W | Thermal design |
| 12 | $V_{\text{stack,min}} = N \cdot V_{\text{cell,min}} = N \cdot 0.5$ V | Minimum acceptable cell voltage | V | End-of-life design margin |

> [!NOTE]
> **ASR (Area-Specific Resistance)** is the most quoted metric for stack ohmic performance. State-of-art PEM stacks target **ASR $\leq 0.07$ Ω·cm²** at $1.0 \text{ A/cm}^2$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Toyota Mirai (2nd Gen, 2020):** Uses a **128 kW** PEMFC stack with **330 cells** giving a peak voltage of **~230 V** and volumetric power density of **5.4 kW/L**.
- **Hyundai NEXO:** 95 kW stack, 156 cells, ~1.56 kW/kg.
- **Stationary backup power (Bloom Energy SOFC):** Hundreds of planar cells stacked into modules for telecom/datacenter use.
- **Heavy-duty trucks (Hyundai HDC-6 Neptune, Daimler GenH2):** 200–300 kW stacks with $>25000$ hours durability target.

These examples confirm the KTU high-yield topic: **automotive stacks are $N=200\text{ to }500$ PEM cells, bipolar-plated, water- and thermally managed, producing 80–300 kW at 250–400 V.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Number of Cells in a Stack for a Given Traction Voltage

A fuel cell electric vehicle traction inverter requires a **minimum DC link voltage of $V_{\text{DC,min}}$**. The fuel cell stack must provide this voltage even at its **end-of-power cell voltage** (the lowest acceptable cell voltage under load, typically $V_{\text{cell,min}} = 0.5$ V to $0.6$ V).

**Step 1:** State the design constraint.

$$V_{\text{DC,min}} = N \cdot V_{\text{cell,min}}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the number of cells.

$$N = \frac{V_{\text{DC,min}}}{V_{\text{cell,min}}}$$

**Step 3:** Apply for a typical EV: $V_{\text{DC,min}} = 250$ V, $V_{\text{cell,min}} = 0.6$ V.

$$N = \frac{250}{0.6} \approx 416.67 \;\Rightarrow\; N = 417 \text{ cells (round up)}$$

**Step 4:** Add a design margin of $+5\%$ for stack aging and tolerance.

$$N_{\text{design}} = 1.05 \times 417 = 437.85 \;\Rightarrow\; N_{\text{design}} = 438 \text{ cells}$$

**Step 5:** The maximum no-load stack voltage is then:

$$V_{\text{stack,OCV}} = N_{\text{design}} \times E_{\text{Nernst}} = 438 \times 1.0 = 438 \text{ V (approx.)}$$

> This derivation is a **direct KTU 14-mark favorite**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stack Power Density and Output Power

Given a stack with $N = 300$ cells, each with active area $A_{\text{cell}} = 200 \text{ cm}^2$, operating at average cell voltage $V_{\text{cell,avg}} = 0.65$ V and current density $j = 1.0 \text{ A/cm}^2$.

**Step 1:** Calculate cell current.

$$I_{\text{cell}} = j \cdot A_{\text{cell}} = 1.0 \text{ A/cm}^2 \times 200 \text{ cm}^2 = 200 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate stack voltage.

$$V_{\text{stack}} = N \cdot V_{\text{cell,avg}} = 300 \times 0.65 = 195 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate gross stack power.

$$P_{\text{stack}} = V_{\text{stack}} \times I_{\text{cell}} = 195 \text{ V} \times 200 \text{ A} = 39{,}000 \text{ W} = 39 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate cell power.

$$P_{\text{cell}} = V_{\text{cell,avg}} \times j \times A_{\text{cell}} = 0.65 \times 1.0 \times 200 = 130 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5:** Cross-check.

$$P_{\text{stack}} = N \cdot P_{\text{cell}} = 300 \times 130 = 39{,}000 \text{ W} \;\checkmark$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stack Efficiency from Cell Polarization

The voltage efficiency of a fuel cell stack is:

$$\eta_V = \frac{V_{\text{cell,avg}}}{E_{\text{th}}} \times 100\%$$

where $E_{\text{th}} = 1.482$ V corresponds to the higher heating value (HHV) of hydrogen at 25 °C.

**Step 1:** With $V_{\text{cell,avg}} = 0.65$ V:

$$\eta_V = \frac{0.65}{1.482} \times 100\% \approx 43.86\%$$

**Step 2:** With current density $j = 1.5 \text{ A/cm}^2$ (higher load, more losses) and $V_{\text{cell,avg}} = 0.55$ V:

$$\eta_V = \frac{0.55}{1.482} \times 100\% \approx 37.11\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The voltage efficiency **drops with current** because activation, ohmic, and concentration losses all rise. A real automotive system compensates by recovering the **waste heat** with a **heat exchanger / fuel cell waste-heat recovery system**, pushing the *combined* electrical + thermal efficiency to 50–60%.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Stack Design Decision Table

| Design Parameter | Trade-off (Small Value) | Trade-off (Large Value) | KTU Automotive Best Practice |
|------------------|--------------------------|---------------------------|------------------------------|
| Number of cells $N$ | Low voltage, requires boost converter | High stack cost, mass, complexity | 200–500 cells |
| Cell active area $A_{\text{cell}}$ | Low current per cell, expensive | Uneven reactant distribution, water management issues | 100–300 cm² |
| Current density $j$ | Low losses, low power | Catalyst stress, flooding/drying | 0.6–1.5 A/cm² (PEMFC) |
| Operating pressure $p$ | Simple BOP, low parasitic | BoP compressor cost, durability issues | 1.5–2.5 atm |
| BPP material | Cost (graphite) | Conductivity/durability (metal) | Coated stainless steel or composite |
| Coolant | Low boiling point | High pressure drop | Deionized water-glycol mixture |

> This matrix is a frequent KTU "compare-and-justify" question (7 marks).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Fuel Cell Stack System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H2Tank["H2 STORAGE TANK 350 to 700 bar"] --> H2Reg["H2 PRESSURE REGULATOR"]
    H2Reg --> H2Mani["H2 INLET MANIFOLD"]
    AirIn["AMBIENT AIR INTAKE"] --> AirFilter["AIR FILTER"]
    AirFilter --> AirComp["AIR COMPRESSOR or BLOWER"]
    AirComp --> Humid["HUMIDIFIER MODULE"]
    Humid --> AirMani["AIR INLET MANIFOLD"]

    H2Mani --> StackFC["FC STACK N CELLS IN SERIES BIPOLAR PLATES"]
    AirMani --> StackFC
    StackFC --> CoolIn["COOLANT INLET CHANNELS"]
    StackFC --> CoolOut["COOLANT OUTLET CHANNELS"]
    CoolOut --> RadPump["COOLANT PUMP and RADIATOR"]
    RadPump --> CoolIn

    StackFC --> H2Out["H2 RECIRCULATION and PURGE"]
    H2Out --> H2Recirc["EJECTOR or RECIRCULATOR"]
    H2Recirc --> H2Mani

    StackFC --> AirOut["CATHODE AIR EXHAUST WATER and HEAT"]
    AirOut --> Sep["WATER SEPARATOR"]
    Sep --> WaterOut["DRAIN or HUMIDIFIER FEEDBACK"]
    Sep --> Exhaust["EXHAUST TO AMBIENT"]

    StackFC --> DCDC["DC DC CONVERTER HV DC LINK"]
    DCDC --> Inverter["TRACTION INVERTER"]
    Inverter --> Motor["TRACTION MOTOR"]
    Inverter --> Battery["Li ION BUFFER BATTERY"]
    DCDC --> Aux["12V AUXILIARY via DCDC BUCK"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** This block-level functional architecture maps the **fluidic**, **electrical**, and **thermal** domains of an automotive fuel cell stack. Arrows represent the **direction of mass flow** (reactants, coolant, exhaust) or **electrical current**. Each block is a **balance-of-plant (BoP)** component; the *only* block that generates electrical energy is `StackFC`.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Stack Internal Repeat Unit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BPP_A["ANODE BIPOLAR PLATE H2 channels"] --> GDL_A["ANODE GAS DIFFUSION LAYER"]
    GDL_A --> CL_A["ANODE CATALYST LAYER HOR site"]
    CL_A --> PEM["PROTON EXCHANGE MEMBRANE Nafion type"]
    PEM --> CL_C["CATHODE CATALYST LAYER ORR site"]
    CL_C --> GDL_C["CATHODE GAS DIFFUSION LAYER"]
    GDL_C --> BPP_C["CATHODE BIPOLAR PLATE Air channels"]
    BPP_C -.becomes anode BPP of next cell.-> NextBPP["NEXT REPEAT UNIT"]
```

**Operational legend (for examiner's use):**

- Solid arrows: reactant/product transport (H₂, O₂, H₂O, H⁺).
- Implicit perpendicular flow: electrons collected at bipolar plates and routed in series.
- Dotted arrow: **bipolar continuity** — the same plate flips function in the next cell.

---

### 4.3 Polarization (V–j) Curve Annotation Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OCV["OCV approx 1.0 V Nernst limit"] --> ActLoss["ACTIVATION LOSS dominated at low j"]
    ActLoss --> Ohmic["OHMIC LOSS linear region middle j"]
    Ohmic --> ConcLoss["CONCENTRATION LOSS steep drop at high j"]
    ConcLoss --> Cutoff["OPERATIONAL CUTOFF approx 0.5 V"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** The polarization curve has **three regions** of loss. Examiners often ask: *"Identify the dominant loss mechanism at $j = 0.05 \text{ A/cm}^2$, $0.5 \text{ A/cm}^2$, and $2.5 \text{ A/cm}^2$."* Memorize: **activation → ohmic → concentration.**

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper 2)]
*What is a fuel cell stack? Why is a single fuel cell not directly used in automotive applications?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **fuel cell stack** is a series/parallel combination of multiple individual fuel cells integrated using **bipolar plates** to obtain practically useful voltage and power. A single PEM fuel cell produces only **0.6–0.7 V** under load, which is far below the **250–400 V** DC link required by EV traction inverters. Hence, **300–500 cells** are stacked in series to scale up the voltage. Additionally, stacking distributes current across multiple cells, reducing ohmic losses, and provides modularity and serviceability. (3 marks)

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
*List the functions of a bipolar plate in a PEM fuel cell stack.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A bipolar plate performs **four key functions:**
1. **Reactant distribution** — channels $\text{H}_2$ to the anode side and air to the cathode side of adjacent cells.
2. **Current collection** — conducts electrons from one cell's anode to the next cell's cathode (series connection).
3. **Thermal conduction** — removes heat from the MEA and routes it to the coolant.
4. **Mechanical support** — provides structural rigidity and gas sealing to the entire stack. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Set 1]

**A(a).** [7 marks]
*With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a PEM fuel cell stack. List any **five** stack-level performance metrics used in the automotive industry.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**
- **Construction:** A PEMFC stack is built by repeating the **repeat unit** (anode BPP → anode GDL → anode CL → PEM → cathode CL → cathode GDL → cathode BPP). The cathode BPP doubles as the anode BPP of the next cell, hence the term **bipolar**.
- **Working:** Pressurized $\text{H}_2$ at the anode undergoes HOR ($\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$); the $\text{H}^+$ migrates through the hydrated PEM; at the cathode, $\frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$. Electrons travel through the external load via the BPPs, adding up cell-to-cell.
- **Five stack-level performance metrics:**
  1. **Stack power output** (kW)
  2. **Stack voltage** (V) at rated current
  3. **Volumetric power density** (kW/L)
  4. **Gravimetric power density** (kW/kg)
  5. **Durability / lifetime** (hours)
  6. **Cold-start time** (seconds)
  7. **Cost per kW** (USD/kW) (any five) (7 marks)

---

**A(b).** [7 marks]
*A fuel cell stack is required to deliver a DC link voltage of **300 V**. The minimum cell voltage at rated power is **0.55 V**. Determine:*
*(i) The number of cells required.*
*(ii) The stack open-circuit voltage (assume $E_{\text{Nernst}} = 1.0$ V per cell).*
*(iii) The maximum continuous current if cell active area is $250 \text{ cm}^2$ and operating current density is $1.2 \text{ A/cm}^2$.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step (i):** Number of cells.

$$N = \frac{V_{\text{DC,min}}}{V_{\text{cell,min}}} = \frac{300}{0.55} \approx 545.45 \;\Rightarrow\; N = 546 \text{ cells}$$

**Step (ii):** Stack OCV.

$$V_{\text{stack,OCV}} = N \times E_{\text{Nernst}} = 546 \times 1.0 = 546 \text{ V}$$

**Step (iii):** Maximum current.

$$I = j \cdot A_{\text{cell}} = 1.2 \text{ A/cm}^2 \times 250 \text{ cm}^2 = 300 \text{ A}$$

Hence, the stack can deliver **546 cells**, **546 V OCV**, and **300 A** maximum continuous current. Power at rated point: $P = 300 \text{ V} \times 300 \text{ A} = 90 \text{ kW}$. (7 marks)

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam – Model Paper Set 3]

**B(a).** [7 marks]
*Explain the major voltage losses in a fuel cell. How do these losses scale when cells are connected in series to form a stack?*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**
The single-cell voltage equation:

$$V_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{Nernst}} - \eta_{\text{act}} - \eta_{\text{ohm}} - \eta_{\text{conc}}$$

The three major losses are:

1. **Activation Loss ($\eta_{\text{act}}$):** Caused by sluggish ORR kinetics; dominant at **low current density**; follows the **Tafel equation** $\eta_{\text{act}} = \frac{RT}{\alpha F} \ln\!\left(\frac{j}{j_0}\right)$.
2. **Ohmic Loss ($\eta_{\text{ohm}}$):** Linear in current; caused by ionic resistance of the membrane and electronic contact resistance; $\eta_{\text{ohm}} = j \cdot \text{ASR}$.
3. **Concentration Loss ($\eta_{\text{conc}}$):** Mass-transport limitation at **high current density**; reactant starvation causes a steep voltage drop.

**Scaling in a stack:** When $N$ cells are connected in series with bipolar plates, the *same current* flows through every cell. Therefore the **ohmic and concentration losses add per cell**, giving:

$$V_{\text{stack}} = N \cdot E_{\text{Nernst}} - N\eta_{\text{act}} - N\eta_{\text{ohm}} - N\eta_{\text{conc}}$$

Thus, **uniformity of cell performance is critical** — one weak cell can pull the whole stack down (the *weakest-link* effect). Stack designers impose tight tolerances on MEA thickness, GDL compression, and reactant flow uniformity. (7 marks)

---

**B(b).** [7 marks]
*A PEM fuel cell stack has 350 cells, each with $A_{\text{cell}} = 200 \text{ cm}^2$, operating at $V_{\text{cell,avg}} = 0.65$ V and $j = 1.0 \text{ A/cm}^2$. Calculate:*
*(i) Stack voltage and current.*
*(ii) Stack power output.*
*(iii) Stack efficiency (HHV basis, $E_{\text{th}} = 1.482$ V).*
*(iv) Heat generated by the stack.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i)** Voltage and current.

$$V_{\text{stack}} = N \times V_{\text{cell,avg}} = 350 \times 0.65 = 227.5 \text{ V}$$

$$I = j \times A_{\text{cell}} = 1.0 \times 200 = 200 \text{ A}$$

**(ii)** Power.

$$P = V_{\text{stack}} \times I = 227.5 \times 200 = 45{,}500 \text{ W} = 45.5 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii)** Efficiency.

$$\eta_{\text{stack}} = \frac{0.65}{1.482} \times 100\% \approx 43.86\%$$

**(iv)** Heat generated per cell.

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{cell}} = I \times (E_{\text{Nernst}} - V_{\text{cell}}) \approx 200 \times (1.0 - 0.65) = 70 \text{ W}$$

Total stack heat.

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{stack}} = N \times \dot{Q}_{\text{cell}} = 350 \times 70 = 24{,}500 \text{ W} = 24.5 \text{ kW}$$

> **Sanity check:** Input power (HHV) = $1.482 \times 200 \times 350 = 103.74$ kW; electrical output = 45.5 kW; thermal loss = $103.74 - 45.5 = 58.24$ kW. The difference between 58.24 and 24.5 kW is the **latent heat carried away by product water and unreacted gas** leaving the stack. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to add a design margin:** Always add **+5 to +10%** to the calculated $N$ to account for stack aging, tolerance, and worst-case cell voltage.
> 2. **Mixing up $E_{\text{Nernst}}$ and HHV:** $E_{\text{Nernst}} \approx 1.0 \text{–} 1.229$ V; HHV reference = **1.482 V**; LHV reference = **1.253 V**. The 1.482 V is **only** for efficiency calculations, not polarization.
> 3. **Confusing current and current density:** $I = j \cdot A_{\text{cell}}$ — examiners *will* deduct marks if you write power as $P = V \cdot j$.
> 4. **Ignoring BoP parasitic losses:** The "net" stack power to the wheels is **less** than the gross stack power by ~10–15% due to compressor, pump, and fan auxiliaries. Mention it whenever asked for *vehicle-level* power.
> 5. **Writing "cell" when asked for "stack":** A 7-mark question on stacks requires **stack-level** equations, not just cell-level ones.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **single fuel cell** delivers only **0.6–0.7 V**; automotive stacks need **300–500 cells** in series for a **250–400 V** DC link.
- ✅ **Bipolar plates** are the *heart* of the stack: they distribute reactants, collect current, conduct heat, and provide mechanical support.
- ✅ **Stack voltage equation:** $V_{\text{stack}} = N \cdot V_{\text{cell,avg}}$.
- ✅ **Number of cells** for design: $N = \dfrac{V_{\text{DC,min}}}{V_{\text{cell,min}}}$ **plus 5–10% margin**.
- ✅ **Stack power:** $P_{\text{stack}} = V_{\text{stack}} \cdot I = N \cdot V_{\text{cell}} \cdot j \cdot A_{\text{cell}}$.
- ✅ **Polarization losses** (memorize the V–j curve shape): activation (low $j$) → ohmic (mid $j$, linear) → concentration (high $j$, cliff).
- ✅ **Stack efficiency** (HHV) = $V_{\text{cell,avg}} / 1.482 \times 100\%$ → typically 40–55%.
- ✅ **Heat generated** per cell = $I \times (E_{\text{Nernst}} - V_{\text{cell}})$; total = $N \times$ this.
- ✅ **State-of-art automotive stacks** (2024): Toyota Mirai 128 kW, Hyundai NEXO 95 kW, density 5+ kW/L.
- ✅ **Uniformity is critical:** one weak cell drags down the whole stack (*weakest-link effect*).
- ✅ **BoP parasitic load** (compressors, pumps, fans) reduces net vehicle power by 10–15% — *always mention* in full-design answers.
- ✅ **Differentiate** between **PEMFC** (60–80 °C, automotive), **SOFC** (600–800 °C, stationary), and **AFC** (alkaline, space) stacks — examiners love this contrast.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Delivering Fuel Cell Power
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT612 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Delivering Fuel Cell Power

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Delivering Fuel Cell Power** refers to the complete electromechanical architecture and control methodology employed in a **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV)** to convert the low-voltage, unregulated DC output of a Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) fuel cell stack into conditioned, high-voltage electrical energy capable of driving the vehicle's traction motor, auxiliary loads, and on-board energy storage devices.

In the KTU 2024 OEMUT612 syllabus, this topic encapsulates the **power train electronics** bridging the fuel cell stack to the road wheels. It involves **DC-DC boost converters** (to step-up the fuel cell voltage), **DC-AC inverters** (to drive AC traction motors), **bidirectional converters** (for hybrid battery/ultracapacitor integration), and the **energy management strategy (EMS)** that orchestrates the power flow.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Power Translator" Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Fuel Cell as a "Faucet" and the Drivetrain as the "Shower"**
> Imagine a fuel cell stack as a kitchen faucet. The water (electricity) coming out has an awkward, fluctuating pressure (variable DC voltage that droops as current draw increases). The "Delivering Fuel Cell Power" system is essentially a **plumbing and pressure-regulating station**:
> - The **DC-DC Boost Converter** = the pressure booster pump that ensures a constant, high-pressure stream.
> - The **Inverter** = the rotary shower head that converts the straight stream into a spinning spray (AC motor rotation).
> - The **Battery / Ultracapacitor** = the auxiliary storage tank that handles sudden demand spikes (like turning on multiple taps at once).
> - The **Energy Management Controller (EMC)** = the smart plumbing supervisor that decides which tank to draw from.
>
> Without this translation layer, you cannot get usable "shower pressure" from the raw "faucet flow" — just as an FCEV cannot drive its wheels directly from the fuel cell stack output.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (KTU Board Emphasis)

- **Fuel Cell Rated Power:** typically **75 kW – 100 kW** for passenger FCEVs (e.g., Toyota Mirai II).
- **DC Bus Voltage (High-Voltage Rail):** standardized at **200 V – 450 V** (common automotive HV bus).
- **Individual Cell Voltage:** **~0.6 V – 0.8 V** at rated load (drops from open-circuit ~1.0–1.2 V).
- **Boost Converter Switching Frequency:** **20 kHz – 100 kHz** (above audible range).
- **Inverter Switching Frequency:** typically **5 kHz – 20 kHz** using IGBTs or SiC MOSFETs.
- **Round-trip Efficiency of Power Electronics:** **92% – 97%**.

## 1.4 The Core Subsystems (KTU Block-Level View)

| Subsystem | Primary Function | Typical Component |
|---|---|---|
| **Fuel Cell Stack** | Electrochemical power generation | PEM stack (300–500 cells) |
| **DC-DC Boost Converter** | Voltage step-up & regulation | IGBT/MOSFET + inductor |
| **Traction Inverter** | DC → AC conversion | 3-phase VSI (IGBT bridge) |
| **Traction Motor** | Mechanical propulsion | PMSM / Induction motor |
| **Energy Storage System (ESS)** | Transient power buffering | Li-ion battery / Supercapacitor |
| **Bidirectional DC-DC Converter** | ESS ↔ DC-bus interface | Non-isolated buck-boost |
| **Energy Management Controller (EMC)** | Power-flow orchestration | ECU / VCU |
| **Auxiliary Loads** | HVAC, lights, pumps, electronics | 12 V / 24 V bus + DC-DC |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Direct Fuel Cell Output Cannot Drive the Vehicle

The **polarization curve** of a PEM fuel cell shows that the terminal voltage $V_{fc}$ drops non-linearly as load current $I_{fc}$ increases, due to:

1. **Activation losses** (slow electrode kinetics, dominant at low current).
2. **Ohmic losses** (membrane + electrode resistance, linear drop).
3. **Concentration losses** (mass-transport limitation, dominant at high current).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consequence for Power Delivery:** The stack voltage swings from ~1.0 V (open circuit) down to ~0.5 V per cell at rated current. For a 300-cell stack, the **V-range is roughly 150 V – 300 V**, which is both too low and too variable for a 350 V traction bus. This is the fundamental reason a **DC-DC boost converter** is mandatory in every FCEV.

## 2.2 Operating Logic of the DC-DC Boost Converter

A boost (step-up) converter is placed directly after the fuel cell stack. It performs three simultaneous duties:

1. **Voltage Step-up:** Raises the variable stack voltage to a stable **DC-bus voltage** (typically 350 V or 650 V).
2. **Current Smoothing:** Uses high-frequency switching to present a near-constant load to the stack, protecting it from damaging current transients.
3. **Galvanic Decoupling (in some topologies):** Provides electrical isolation between the low-voltage stack side and the high-voltage traction bus.

### Step-by-Step Switching Logic

- **Switch ON (MOSFET/IGBT closed):** Inductor $L$ charges from the fuel cell; diode is reverse-biased; load is supplied by output capacitor.
- **Switch OFF:** Inductor releases its stored energy **in series** with the source through the now forward-biased diode into the load and capacitor.
- The result: $V_{out} > V_{in}$ continuously.

## 2.3 Traction Inverter (Voltage Source Inverter — VSI)

The DC-bus feeds a **three-phase two-level VSI** using six IGBTs (or SiC MOSFETs in modern designs). Space Vector Modulation (SVM) or Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM) controls the motor's torque and speed.

- The inverter outputs a **variable-frequency, variable-voltage** AC waveform.
- Frequency range: **0 Hz (stall) to ~1000 Hz (highway cruising)**.
- The motor (PMSM or Induction) operates in three regions:
  - **Constant Torque Region** (low speed, voltage-limited).
  - **Constant Power Region** (field-weakening, mid speed).
  - **Constant Voltage / Series Characteristic** (high speed).

## 2.4 Hybridization with Energy Storage (Why and How)

> [!NOTE]
> A bare fuel cell stack cannot respond to sudden acceleration demands (which require peak power 2–3× the rated stack power for 5–15 seconds). It also cannot **regeneratively brake** (fuel cells are unidirectional). Therefore, every production FCEV uses a **hybrid energy storage system (HESS)**.

### Topologies of HESS Integration

| Topology | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Battery directly on DC bus** | Simplest; battery voltage must match bus voltage | Older prototypes |
| **Ultracapacitor via bidirectional DC-DC** | Supercap handles transient peaks; FC handles average | High-performance FCEVs |
| **Battery + Ultracapacitor dual ESS** | Battery for energy, UC for power | Toyota Mirai, Honda Clarity |
| **FC + Battery (no UC)** | Cheaper, simpler, slightly heavier | Hyundai Nexo (earlier models) |

## 2.5 Energy Management Strategy (EMS)

The EMS implements one of the following strategies to split power between the fuel cell and the ESS:

- **Load-Following (Thermostatic / On-Off):** Fuel cell is either ON at a fixed operating point or OFF; ESS handles the rest.
- **Power-Following (Load-Leveling):** Fuel cell supplies *average* load; ESS supplies *transient* component.
- **Frequency-Decoupling:** Low-frequency demand → FC; high-frequency demand → ESS (filter-based).
- **Optimization-Based (ECMS, Dynamic Programming):** Minimizes hydrogen consumption; used in research vehicles.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{fc} = E_{oc} - \eta_{act} - IR_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$ | Fuel cell polarization equation | V |
| 2 | $V_{fc,stack} = N_{cells} \cdot V_{cell}$ | Total stack voltage | V |
| 3 | $\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \dfrac{1}{1-D}$ | Ideal boost converter gain | dimensionless |
| 4 | $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{1-D}$ | Boost converter output voltage | V |
| 5 | $I_{L,avg} = \dfrac{V_{out} \cdot I_{out}}{V_{in}}$ | Average inductor current | A |
| 6 | $L_{min} = \dfrac{D(1-D)^2 \cdot R}{2f_s}$ | Minimum inductance (CCM) | H |
| 7 | $\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_{in} \cdot D}{f_s \cdot L}$ | Inductor current ripple | A |
| 8 | $P_{bus} = P_{fc} \cdot \eta_{DC-DC}$ | DC bus power | W |
| 9 | $P_{motor,mech} = P_{bus} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{motor}$ | Mechanical power at wheels | W |
| 10 | $E_{H_2,used} = \dfrac{P_{fc} \cdot t}{LHV_{H_2}}$ | Hydrogen consumption | kg |
| 11 | $LHV_{H_2} = 33.33 \text{ kWh/kg} = 120 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | Lower heating value of H₂ | kWh/kg |
| 12 | $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{fc} \cdot \eta_{DC-DC} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{motor}$ | Total drivetrain efficiency | dimensionless |
| 13 | $E_{H_2,consumed} = \dfrac{V_{fc} \cdot I_{fc} \cdot t}{LHV_{H_2} \cdot N_A \cdot 2F}$ | Faraday-based consumption | kg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant to Memorize:** Faraday's constant $F = 96\,485$ C/mol; for H₂, **2 electrons per molecule**, so the mass-consumption constant simplifies to the practical $LHV$ above.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Vehicle Range Estimation:** Using Eq. (10) and known tank capacity (typically 5–6 kg H₂ at 700 bar), range = $\dfrac{m_{H_2} \cdot LHV_{H_2}}{\text{Average } P_{fc}/\eta_{sys}}$.
- **Sizing the Boost Converter:** $D$ is constrained to $[0, 0.88]$ practically; this fixes the achievable voltage step-up ratio.
- **Thermal Management:** Power losses in DC-DC and inverter (~3–8% combined) are dissipated as heat, requiring liquid cooling loops.
- **Safety:** Galvanic isolation is mandatory because the fuel cell cathode can produce ~$\mathbf{−1 V}$ vs. chassis ground under fault conditions, which can corrode metallic components unless isolated.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Boost Converter Voltage Transfer Ratio

**Assumption:** Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM), ideal switch, diode, inductor, capacitor (no losses, no ripple).

Let $D$ = duty cycle (fraction of switching period $T_s$ during which the switch is ON).

### Step 1 — Switch ON State (Duration $DT_s$)

The inductor is connected directly across the fuel cell source.

$$v_L = V_{in} = L \cdot \dfrac{di_L}{dt}$$

Solving for the current change during the ON interval:

$$\Delta i_{L,ON} = \dfrac{V_{in} \cdot D T_s}{L}$$

### Step 2 — Switch OFF State (Duration $(1-D)T_s$)

The inductor sees the difference between input and output (acting as a series source). The diode conducts.

$$v_L = V_{in} - V_{out} = L \cdot \dfrac{di_L}{dt}$$

$$\Delta i_{L,OFF} = \dfrac{(V_{in} - V_{out}) (1-D) T_s}{L}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Volt-Second Balance (Steady-State Principle)

In steady state, the net change in inductor current over one full switching period is zero. Therefore:

$$\Delta i_{L,ON} + \Delta i_{L,OFF} = 0$$

$$\dfrac{V_{in} \cdot D T_s}{L} + \dfrac{(V_{in} - V_{out})(1-D) T_s}{L} = 0$$

### Step 4 — Simplify

Cancel $T_s / L$ from both sides:

$$V_{in} \cdot D + (V_{in} - V_{out})(1-D) = 0$$

$$V_{in} \cdot D + V_{in}(1-D) - V_{out}(1-D) = 0$$

$$V_{in} - V_{out}(1-D) = 0$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \dfrac{1}{1-D}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** As $D \to 1$, the output voltage theoretically $\to \infty$, but in practice duty cycles are limited to $D \le 0.85$ to avoid inductor saturation, switch stress, and unstable operation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Power Balance and Hydrogen Consumption

**Given:** A FCEV requires a mechanical power $P_{mech}$ at the wheels. The drivetrain has the following efficiencies (in order, from wheels backward):

- $\eta_{motor}$ = 0.95 (electric motor)
- $\eta_{inv}$ = 0.96 (inverter)
- $\eta_{DC-DC}$ = 0.94 (boost converter)
- $\eta_{fc}$ = 0.50 (fuel cell LHV-based)

### Step 1 — Required Electrical Power at the Fuel Cell Terminals

Working backward from the wheels:

$$P_{bus,DC} = \dfrac{P_{mech}}{\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{inv}}$$

$$P_{fc,elec} = \dfrac{P_{bus,DC}}{\eta_{DC-DC}}$$

### Step 2 — Required Chemical Power Input (Hydrogen Energy)

$$P_{chem} = \dfrac{P_{fc,elec}}{\eta_{fc}}$$

### Step 3 — Mass Flow Rate of Hydrogen

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{P_{chem}}{LHV_{H_2}} \quad \text{[kg/s]}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Worked Example

Suppose $P_{mech} = 50$ kW (steady cruising of a 1.5-tonne FCEV).

**Conversion 1 — Wheels → Motor shaft:**
$$P_{motor,elec} = \dfrac{50}{0.95} = 52.63 \text{ kW}$$

**Conversion 2 — Motor electrical input (DC bus must supply this):**
$$P_{bus} = \dfrac{52.63}{0.96} = 54.82 \text{ kW}$$

**Conversion 3 — DC bus demand back to fuel cell terminal:**
$$P_{fc,elec} = \dfrac{54.82}{0.94} = 58.32 \text{ kW}$$

**Conversion 4 — Fuel cell hydrogen chemical demand:**
$$P_{chem} = \dfrac{58.32}{0.50} = 116.64 \text{ kW (chemical)}$$

**Conversion 5 — Hydrogen mass flow rate:**
$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{116.64}{33.33} = 3.5 \text{ g/s}$$

For a **5 kg hydrogen tank**, theoretical range at this cruise power:

$$t = \dfrac{5 \text{ kg}}{3.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}} = 1428.6 \text{ s} = 23.8 \text{ min at full cruise}$$

This is of course only a constant-cruise case; in real driving cycles the average is lower.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Inductor Current and Minimum Inductance

In CCM, the average inductor current equals the input current, and the ripple must be limited (typically to <20–40%).

### Step 1 — Express the Inductor Current Ripple

From Step 1 of Section 3.1:

$$\Delta i_L = \dfrac{V_{in} \cdot D}{f_s \cdot L}$$

### Step 2 — Equate the Output Current to the Inductor Current (Power Balance)

Assuming 100% efficiency, $V_{in} I_L = V_{out} I_{out}$, and since $V_{out} = V_{in}/(1-D)$:

$$I_L = \dfrac{I_{out}}{1-D}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $L$ at the Boundary of CCM (DCM/CCM)

The boundary occurs when $\Delta i_L = 2 I_{L,avg}$? No — boundary is at $\Delta i_L = 2 I_{L,avg}$ at *minimum* current. Setting the boundary condition $\Delta i_L = 2 I_L$ (i.e., the current just touches zero):

$$\dfrac{V_{in} D}{f_s L} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{V_{out} I_{out}}{V_{in}}$$

Solving for $L$ with $R = V_{out}/I_{out}$:

$$L_{min} = \dfrac{V_{in}^2 \cdot D}{2 f_s V_{out} I_{out}} = \dfrac{D (1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}$$

$$\boxed{L_{min} = \dfrac{D(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design implication:** Engineers must size $L$ larger than $L_{min}$ to ensure CCM operation across the full load range. In a 50 kW FCEV converter, $L$ is typically 100–500 µH using powder-core or ferrite inductors with litz wire to manage skin-effect at high frequency.

---

## 3.4 Energy Management — Power-Following Strategy Equation

The simplest EMS is a **low-pass filter** that separates the demanded traction power $P_{req}(t)$ into a low-frequency component (handled by the fuel cell) and a high-frequency component (handled by the ESS):

$$P_{fc}(t) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \tau s} \cdot P_{req}(t)$$

$$P_{ess}(t) = P_{req}(t) - P_{fc}(t)$$

where $\tau$ is the time constant (typically **5–10 seconds** for an FCEV) corresponding to a **cut-off frequency** of:

$$f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \tau} \approx 0.016 \text{ to } 0.032 \text{ Hz}$$

The fuel cell thus "sees" only the slowly varying power demand, extending its lifetime and improving efficiency (since PEMFCs are most efficient at steady-state operation).

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Exam Favourite)

| Strategy | FC Stress | H₂ Economy | ESS Size | Complexity | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **On/Off (Thermostatic)** | High cycling | Poor | Small | Very low | Simple prototypes |
| **Load-Following** | Moderate | Moderate | Medium | Low | City cars |
| **Power-Following** | Low | Good | Medium-Large | Medium | Production FCEVs |
| **Frequency-Decoupling** | Lowest | Best | Large | High | High-performance |
| **Optimal (ECMS/DP)** | Minimal | Best | Optimized | Very high | Research/Future |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — FCEV Power Delivery Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H2T[Hydrogen Tank 700 bar] -->|H2| FCstack[PEM Fuel Cell Stack 300 to 500 cells]
    AIR[Ambient Air Compressor] -->|O2| FCstack
    FCstack -->|Variable DC 150 to 300 V| BOOST[DC-DC Boost Converter 20 to 100 kHz]
    BOOST -->|Regulated HV DC 350 to 450 V| DCBUS[HV DC Bus]
    DCBUS --> INV[3-Phase Traction Inverter VSI IGBT or SiC]
    INV -->|3-phase AC| MOTOR[Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor]
    MOTOR --> GEAR[Single-Speed Gearbox] --> WHLS[Wheels]
    DCBUS --> AUXDC[12V/24V Auxiliary DC-DC]
    DCBUS --> BDCDC[Bidirectional DC-DC Converter]
    BDCDC <-->|Charge/Discharge| ESS[Energy Storage System Li-ion or Ultracapacitor]
    FCstack --> EMC[Energy Management Controller ECU]
    ESS --> EMC
    DCBUS --> EMC
    MOTOR -->|Speed Torque| EMC
    EMC -->|PWM Control| BOOST
    EMC -->|SVPWM Control| INV
    EMC -->|Power Split Command| BDCDC
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Power Flow During Different Driving Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MODE1[Mode 1 - Steady Cruise]
        A1[FC Stack 50 kW] --> B1[Boost Conv]
        B1 --> C1[Inverter]
        C1 --> D1[Motor]
        B1 --> E1[Aux Loads 3 kW]
    end
    subgraph MODE2[Mode 2 - Hard Acceleration]
        A2[FC Stack 80 kW] --> B2[Boost Conv]
        F2[ESS 50 kW Peak] --> G2[Bidirectional DC-DC]
        B2 --> H2[DC Bus]
        G2 --> H2
        H2 --> I2[Inverter]
        I2 --> J2[Motor 130 kW Peak]
    end
    subgraph MODE3[Mode 3 - Regenerative Braking]
        K3[Motor as Generator] --> L3[Inverter Rectifier Mode]
        L3 --> M3[DC Bus]
        M3 --> N3[Bidirectional DC-DC]
        N3 --> O3[ESS Charging]
        P3[FC Stack 0 kW Idle] -.-> Q3[No Power]
    end
```

## 4.3 DC-DC Boost Converter Circuit (Mermaid Schematic)

```mermaid

    subgraph BOOST_CKT[Boost Converter Topology]
        VIN[Vin from FC Stack] --- L1[Inductor L]
        L1 --- SW[Switch MOSFET IGBT]
        SW --- GND1[Ground]
        L1 --- DIODE[Diode Anode to L Cathode to Vout]
        DIODE --- COUT[Output Capacitor Cout]
        COUT --- GND2[Ground]
        COUT --- VOUT[Vout to DC Bus]
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PREQ[P req t] --> FILT[Low-Pass Filter tau = 5 to 10 s]
    FILT --> PFC[FC Power Setpoint]
    PREQ --> SUM[Subtractor]
    PFC --> SUM
    SUM --> PESS[ESS Power Demand Positive or Negative]
    PESS --> BDC[ESS Bidirectional DC-DC]

```
